# Section 3: Step-by-Step Sector-Parameter Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Framework — From Sector to Principal Particulars

In Ship Design I, the very first deliverable of a design project is the **Principal Particulars** block. The sector dictates the entry values. The procedure below is what a professional naval architect follows **on paper** before any drawing is started.

### Step 1: Identify the Sector

The first question is binary: **Is the vessel commercial, offshore, naval, fishing, passenger, recreational, or specialised?**

This decides the **applicable rule set** (SOLAS, MARPOL, MODU Code, NSTC, MCA LY2, IGC Code, etc.) and the **expected contract speed / cargo / mission duration**.

### Step 2: Translate Sector to Deadweight (DWT)

For a commercial ship, DWT is the **primary sizing variable**. A canonical first estimate uses the **cargo-to-DWT ratio** for each sector:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DWT}_{\text{total}} &= \text{Cargo Mass} + \text{Fuel (Bunker)} + \text{Lube Oil} + \text{Ballast} + \text{Fresh Water} + \text{Crew + Stores} \\
\end{aligned}$$

For a **Capesize Bulk Carrier** carrying **180,000 t of iron ore** on a Brazil–China voyage:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cargo} &= 180{,}000 \text{ t} \\
\text{Bunker (IFO 380, long voyage)} &\approx 4{,}500 \text{ t} \\
\text{Ballast (return leg)} &\approx 50{,}000 \text{ t} \\
\text{Crew + Stores + FW + LO} &\approx 500 \text{ t} \\
\hline
\text{DWT}_{\text{total}} &\approx 235{,}000 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}$$

For a **VLCC Tanker** of **2 million barrels** cargo:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cargo} &= 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bbl} \times 0.159 \text{ m}^3/\text{bbl} \times 0.86 \text{ t/m}^3 \\
&\approx 273{,}500 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Rule:** Always add a **5–10% design margin** on top of the calculated DWT for unknown weights discovered during detailed design.

### Step 3: Choose the Block Coefficient $C_B$

The sector prescribes a **family of $C_B$ vs. speed curves** (often called the *Ayre–Silver* or *Taylor* curves). For a first iteration:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_B &= K \cdot F_n^{-\alpha}
\end{aligned}$$

where $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ is the **Froude number**, $K$ and $\alpha$ are sector-dependent constants. A simplified sector-wise tabulation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Tanker (slow)} &\Rightarrow C_B \approx 0.83 \cdot F_n^{-0.25} \\
\text{Bulk Carrier} &\Rightarrow C_B \approx 0.78 \cdot F_n^{-0.22} \\
\text{Container} &\Rightarrow C_B \approx 0.55 \cdot F_n^{-0.15} \\
\text{Naval Frigate} &\Rightarrow C_B \approx 0.45 \cdot F_n^{-0.10}
\end{aligned}$$

For a **VLCC at 15.5 kn** with $L_{pp} = 320$ m:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_n &= \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{pp}}} = \frac{15.5 \times 0.5144}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 320}} \approx 0.147 \\
C_B &\approx 0.83 \times (0.147)^{-0.25} \approx 0.83 \times 1.62 \approx 0.83
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Estimate Displacement $\Delta$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho \cdot \nabla = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T
\end{aligned}$$

Using saltwater density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$, and reasonable $L/B$ and $B/T$ ratios for a VLCC:

$$\begin{aligned}
L/B &\approx 6.0 \Rightarrow B \approx 53.3 \text{ m} \\
B/T &\approx 2.5 \Rightarrow T \approx 21.3 \text{ m} \\
\nabla &= C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.83 \times 320 \times 53.3 \times 21.3 \\
&\approx 301{,}800 \text{ m}^3 \\
\Delta &= 1.025 \times 301{,}800 \approx 309{,}300 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}$$

This checks with DWT + lightweight (LW ≈ 45,000 t) ≈ 354,000 t — within the design envelope.

### Step 5: Powering (Holtrop–Mennen Form, Sector-Aware)

The total resistance is sector-dependent because wave-making dominates at high $F_n$ (container, naval) and viscous + form drag dominate at low $F_n$ (tanker, bulk carrier).

$$\begin{aligned}
R_T &= R_F(1+k) + R_{APP} + R_W + R_B + R_{TR} + R_A
\end{aligned}$$

where each component responds differently to sector choice. A naval architect would never reuse a tanker's $R_W$ curve for a frigate.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Sectors Side-by-Side

| Parameter | Commercial Tanker | Container | Offshore AHTS | Naval Frigate | Cruise Liner | Fishing Trawler |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Typical Length (m) | 250–340 | 200–400 | 60–100 | 100–150 | 200–360 | 25–80 |
| Beam (m) | 40–60 | 30–60 | 14–22 | 12–18 | 30–50 | 6–14 |
| Draft (m) | 18–22 | 12–16 | 5–8 | 4–6 | 7–9 | 3–6 |
| $C_B$ | 0.78–0.85 | 0.55–0.65 | 0.65–0.75 | 0.50–0.58 | 0.60–0.70 | 0.55–0.70 |
| Speed (kn) | 14–16 | 20–25 | 14–16 | 28–32 | 18–22 | 10–14 |
| $F_n$ | 0.14–0.16 | 0.22–0.27 | 0.18–0.25 | 0.40–0.55 | 0.20–0.26 | 0.18–0.26 |
| Power plant | Slow 2-stroke | Slow 2-stroke | Diesel-electric | CODOG/COGAG | Diesel-electric | Diesel-mech. |
| Crew | 20–30 | 20–30 | 30–50 | 200–250 | 800–2200 | 10–30 |
| Rule set | SOLAS, MARPOL | SOLAS, MARPOL | Class + IMO MODU | Naval Std. | SOLAS + Flag | Class + FAO |
| Design focus | DWT, EEDI | TEU/hour, EEDI | Bollard pull, deck area | Stealth, survivability | Comfort, stability | Catch hold volume |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Crew column** is the single biggest differentiator between merchant and passenger ships. A cruise liner carries **~6000 souls** (crew + passengers). Damage-stability and life-saving appliances (LSA) drive most of the design choices — unlike a tanker, where DWT drives them.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — "Classify the Sector"

> **Problem:** A vessel of $L_{pp} = 200$ m, $B = 32.26$ m, $T = 13.5$ m, $C_B = 0.65$, service speed = 22 kn, DWT = 60,000 t, payload unitised. Identify the sector and justify using at least three parameters.

**Solution (Step-by-step):**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_n &= \frac{22 \times 0.5144}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 200}} = \frac{11.32}{44.3} \approx 0.256 \\
\end{aligned}$$

1. **Speed + $F_n$** → Too high for a tanker (VLCC runs $F_n \approx 0.14$); consistent with **Container Ship or Fast Naval Vessel**.
2. **$C_B = 0.65$** → Too low for bulk/tanker (≥0.78), too high for a frigate (≤0.58). → **Container Ship**.
3. **DWT = 60,000 t** with "unitised payload" → Implies **≈ 4500–5000 TEU** Post-Panamax feeder.
4. **$L/B \approx 6.2$ and $B/T \approx 2.4$** → typical container ship proportions.

**Conclusion:** Sector = **Container / Merchant Liner Service**. The chosen $C_B$ is at the **upper edge** of container-ship range — indicates a slightly full-form design optimised for fuel economy, possibly a feeder on Asia–Europe routes.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Classify the major sectors of the international marine industry and list two representative vessel types for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The international marine industry can be classified into ten major sectors:

1. **Commercial / Merchant Shipping** – Bulk carriers, Container ships, Tankers (VLCC), LNG carriers.
2. **Offshore Oil & Gas** – FPSO, Drillships, AHTS, PSV.
3. **Offshore Renewable Energy** – CTVs, SOVs, Cable-laying vessels.
4. **Naval & Defence** – Frigates, Corvettes, Submarines, Aircraft carriers.
5. **Passenger & Cruise** – Cruise liners, Ro-Pax ferries, High-speed craft.
6. **Fishing & Aquaculture** – Stern trawlers, Purse seiners, Well-boats.
7. **Inland Waterway** – River-sea ships, Push-tow convoys.
8. **Recreational Marine** – Motor yachts, Sailing yachts, Mega-yachts.
9. **Specialised Service Vessels** – Dredgers, Tugs, Research vessels, Cable-layers, Icebreakers.
10. **Shipbuilding, Repair & Conversion** – Greenfield yards, SME yards, Dry docks.

(Any 4–5 sectors with two vessel types each = full 3 marks.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain how the choice of marine sector affects the block coefficient $C_B$ of a vessel. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The block coefficient $C_B$ is a measure of hull fullness and is strongly sector-dependent because the **mission profile** dictates the trade-off between displacement (volume for cargo) and speed.

- **Slow-steaming full-form vessels** like VLCC tankers and Capesize bulk carriers operate at low Froude number ($F_n \approx 0.14$–$0.16$) and require maximum displacement to carry heavy cargo → **$C_B = 0.78$ to $0.85$**.
- **High-speed fine-form vessels** like naval frigates and container ships operate at high Froude number ($F_n \approx 0.25$–$0.55$) and need slender hulls to limit wave-making resistance → **$C_B = 0.50$ to $0.65$**.

Thus, a tanker's $C_B$ is roughly **1.5 times** that of a frigate of similar length.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Module-Level Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **any ONE full question** from the following two alternatives. Each question carries 14 marks distributed across two sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sector Classification & Vessel Comparison

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Classify the international marine industry into its major sectors. For **each** of the following vessel types, identify the sector, the typical block coefficient $C_B$, the applicable rule set, and the dominant design driver:

1. Suezmax Tanker
2. Neo-Panamax Container Ship
3. Frigate
4. Anchor Handling Tug Supply (AHTS)
5. Cruise Liner
6. Mega-yacht
7. Hopper Dredger

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A bulk carrier of principal particulars $L_{pp} = 290$ m, $B = 45$ m, $T = 18.2$ m, service speed $V = 14.5$ kn, and block coefficient $C_B = 0.82$ is to be re-evaluated for conversion into an oil tanker on the same route.

(i) Calculate the **Froude number** $F_n$.
(ii) Comment on whether the original $C_B = 0.82$ is still appropriate for the converted tanker.
(iii) Suggest two major design changes that the shipyard must implement to comply with **MARPOL Regulation 23** (double-hull requirement).

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** Tabulation (7 marks — 1 mark per vessel type):

| Vessel | Sector | Typical $C_B$ | Applicable Rule Set | Dominant Design Driver |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Suezmax Tanker | Commercial (Wet Bulk) | 0.78–0.82 | SOLAS, MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 23 | Maximise cargo volume |
| Neo-Panamax Container | Commercial (Liner) | 0.60–0.66 | SOLAS, MARPOL Annex VI | Maximise TEU, low EEDI |
| Frigate | Naval & Defence | 0.50–0.58 | Naval Ship Technical Code (NSTC) | Stealth + survivability |
| AHTS | Offshore (O&G) | 0.65–0.75 | Class + IMO MODU Code | High bollard pull, deck load |
| Cruise Liner | Passenger | 0.60–0.70 | SOLAS + RCD | Passenger comfort, stability |
| Mega-yacht | Recreational | 0.30–0.55 | LY2 / MCA Large Yacht Code | Aesthetics, comfort, range |
| Hopper Dredger | Specialised Service | 0.80–0.90 | Class + Port State | Hopper volume, suction power |

**(b)** 

(i) Froude number calculation:

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{pp}}} = \frac{14.5 \times 0.5144}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 290}} = \frac{7.46}{53.36} \approx 0.140$$

(ii) Justification:

The original $C_B = 0.82$ was selected for an iron-ore carrier at low $F_n$. A converted Suezmax tanker on the same trade lane typically operates at $V = 14.5$–$15.5$ kn with $C_B$ in the range **0.78–0.83**. Therefore, $C_B = 0.82$ remains **broadly appropriate** (slight reduction may be possible to fine-tune powering). No major hull-form change is needed; only internal subdivision changes are mandatory.

(iii) Two major design changes for **MARPOL Reg. 23 (double-hull)** compliance:

1. **Construction of double hull (sides + bottom)** — installation of wing tanks and double-bottom tanks of prescribed minimum width ($w$ in metres, per Reg. 23 formula).
2. **Re-routing of ballast piping** — segregated ballast tanks must be reconfigured; pipe tunnels and slop tanks redesigned.
3. *(Bonus)* Installation of **SBT (Segregated Ballast Tank)** or **CBT (Clean Ballast Tank)** system, plus revised **inert gas system (IGS)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Sector-Based Principal Particulars and Design Drivers

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **three pillars** that differentiate vessel design across the various sectors of the international marine industry. Explain each pillar with **one example vessel** drawn from a different sector.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A naval architect is tasked with sizing a **new offshore wind-farm CTV (Crew Transfer Vessel)** for European operations. The vessel must transport 24 technicians, achieve a service speed of 25 kn, and operate in sea state 4.

(i) Identify the sector and applicable rule set.
(ii) Estimate a suitable block coefficient $C_B$ and justify.
(iii) Estimate the principal dimensions $L_{pp}$, $B$, $T$ for a displacement $\Delta = 220$ t.

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** Three pillars (7 marks — ~2 marks per pillar + 1 mark for the introduction):

1. **Mission** – What does the ship *do*? Defines payload and route.
   - Example: A **VLCC** transports 2 million barrels of crude oil from the Persian Gulf to Rotterdam.
2. **Regime** – Which *rules* apply? Defines scantlings, safety, and environmental performance.
   - Example: A **Naval Frigate** is governed by the Indian Navy's Naval Ship Technical Code (NSTC), prioritising survivability over cargo.
3. **Environment** – Where does she *operate*? Defines seakeeping, structural loads, and powering.
   - Example: A **Polar-class Icebreaker** is designed for operation in pack ice, requiring ice-strengthened hull and high installed power.

The combination of (Mission, Regime, Environment) completely determines every downstream design parameter — principal particulars, hull form, structural scantlings, and propulsion.

**(b)**

(i) **Sector:** Offshore Renewable Energy → Sub-sector: Wind Farm Support Vessels.
**Rule Set:** MCA Workboat Code (MGN 612) or Class rules + IMO Resolution A.971; flag State (e.g., UK MCA) + EU/UK HSE regulations.

(ii) **$C_B$ estimation:**
At 25 kn and approximate $L_{pp} \approx 27$ m,

$$F_n \approx \frac{25 \times 0.5144}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 27}} \approx 0.241$$

For a fast catamaran CTV, $C_B$ (mono-hull) is typically **0.45–0.55**. For a **SWATH or catamaran**, effective $C_B$ can drop to **0.30–0.40** per demi-hull.

Selected: **$C_B = 0.50$** (mono-hull) or **$C_B \approx 0.35$ per demi-hull** for catamaran — chosen for low wave-making in steep wind-farm sea states.

(iii) Principal dimensions (mono-hull, $C_B = 0.50$, $\Delta = 220$ t):

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$$

For typical CTV ratios $L/B = 5.0$ and $B/T = 3.0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 1.025 \times 0.50 \times L_{pp} \times (L_{pp}/5) \times (L_{pp}/15) \\
220 &= 1.025 \times 0.50 \times \frac{L_{pp}^3}{75} \\
L_{pp}^3 &= \frac{220 \times 75}{1.025 \times 0.50} \approx 32{,}195 \\
L_{pp} &\approx 31.8 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$\begin{aligned}
L_{pp} &\approx 27 \text{ m} \quad (\text{rounded to standard CTV length}) \\
B &\approx L_{pp}/5 \approx 6.0 \text{ m} \quad (\text{or } 9 \text{ m for catamaran}) \\
T &\approx B/3 \approx 2.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

(Iterative refinement typically yields $L_{pp} = 25$–$27$ m for a 24-PAX CTV.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing *sector* with *vessel type*.** "Tanker" is *not* a sector — it is a vessel type within the *Commercial Shipping* sector. Always state the sector first, then the vessel.
> 2. **Forgetting the rule set.** A 7-mark question that does *not* name the applicable regulation (SOLAS, MARPOL, NSTC, MCA, IGC, MODU Code) typically loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Using wrong units / wrong conversions.** Common error: $V$ (knots) used directly in Froude number without the $0.5144$ m/s per knot conversion. Always write:
>
> $$V_{m/s} = V_{kn} \times 0.5144$$
>
> 4. **Stating $C_B$ without justification.** A bare number with no sector/rule justification attracts **zero** marks for design reasoning.
> 5. **Ignoring the ITTC 1978 or 1957 friction line.** If the question hints at powering, you must mention **ITTC 1978 formulation** ($C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$) for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 1 Revision — Print This Section**

- [ ] The **international marine industry** has **10 recognised sectors** (commercial, offshore O&G, offshore renewables, naval, passenger, fishing, inland, recreational, specialised, shipbuilding/repair), supported by classification, insurance, finance, and IMO.
- [ ] Every sector can be reduced to **three pillars**: Mission, Regime, Environment.
- [ ] **Block coefficient $C_B$** is the single most sector-sensitive parameter:
  - Tanker / Bulk: $C_B \approx 0.78$–$0.85$
  - Container: $C_B \approx 0.55$–$0.65$
  - Naval Frigate: $C_B \approx 0.50$–$0.58$
  - Yacht: $C_B \approx 0.30$–$0.55$
  - Tug / Dredger: $C_B \approx 0.80$–$0.90$
- [ ] **Froude number** $F_n = V / \sqrt{g L_{pp}}$ dictates resistance regime:
  - Low $F_n$ ($< 0.18$): viscous-dominated (tanker, bulk)
  - High $F_n$ ($> 0.30$): wave-dominated (naval, container, fast ferry)
- [ ] **Key rule sets** to memorise:
  - **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea)
  - **MARPOL** (Marine Pollution — Annex I-VI)
  - **IGC Code** (Gas carriers)
  - **MODU Code** (Mobile Offshore Drilling Units)
  - **NSTC** (Naval Ship Technical Code)
  - **MCA LY2** (Large Yacht Code)
  - **IBC / BCH Codes** (Chemical tankers)
- [ ] **Standard metrics** — GT, NT, DWT, TEU, EEDI, EEXI, CII.
- [ ] **Holtrop–Mennen resistance formula** is sector-aware; do **not** reuse a tanker's $R_W$ curve for a frigate.
- [ ] **MARPOL Regulation 23** mandates double hull for tankers > 6000 DWT.
- [ ] **Classification societies** — LR, DNV, BV, ABS, ClassNK, IRS (Indian Register of Shipping) — apply to every sector except purely recreational small craft.
- [ ] **Orderbook dominance** (2024 data, approximate): China ≈ 50%, South Korea ≈ 30%, Japan ≈ 15%, rest of world ≈ 5% by GT.
- [ ] **India's shipbuilding footprint**: Cochin Shipyard, GRSE, MDL, HSL, L&T, Mazagon Dock — strategic for naval and commercial sectors.
- [ ] **Cruise and passenger sector** is governed by **SOLAS Ch. II-1 + II-2** damage-stability rules — passenger ships have the **most stringent subdivision** requirements.
- [ ] **Recreational / Mega-yacht** rules: MCA LY2 (up to 24 m), LY3 (24–60 m), Red Ensign Group variations, MLC for crew.
- [ ] **Ship Design Spiral starts with sector identification**, then mission spec, then principal particulars, then powering, then hull form, then GA, then structural design.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024:** Whenever a long-answer question involves a specific vessel, *always* open your answer with: *"This vessel belongs to the _____ sector. The applicable rule set is _____. The dominant design driver is _____."* — this single opening line often secures **2 of the 14 marks** outright.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Trade and Methods of Employment of Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Trade and Methods of Employment of Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PBNST504 – Ship Design I** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the topic "Types of Trade and Methods of Employment of Ships" addresses the fundamental classification of **maritime commerce** and the **operational patterns** under which a merchant vessel is engaged to transport cargo and passengers. The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and the **United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD)** define ship trade and employment as the strategic framework that governs how shipping capacity is matched to global demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Types of trade* refers to the **geographic and regulatory classification** of shipping operations (Foreign Trade, Coastal Trade, and Inland Water Trade). *Methods of employment* refers to the **commercial/contractual pattern** under which a ship is operated (Tramp, Liner, and Industrial Carrier Services).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you own a **taxi (ship)** in a city. There are three ways you can make money:

1. **Tramp Service** → You drive your taxi wherever a passenger needs to go, picking up jobs from a board at the taxi stand. You carry anyone to any destination at negotiated prices. The **cargo dictates your route**.

2. **Liner Service** → You run your taxi on a **fixed route** (e.g., Airport → City Center → Mall → Airport) on a **strict timetable**, stopping at scheduled stations. Any passenger can hop on at standard fares.

3. **Industrial Carrier Service** → Your taxi is hired **exclusively by one company** (e.g., a corporate shuttle for a tech park). It runs only for them, and they pay you a **monthly retainer**.

Similarly, the **cargo type** and **trading pattern** determine the ship type:
- A ship carrying **liquid bulk (crude oil)** is a **tanker**.
- A ship carrying **dry bulk (coal, grain)** is a **bulker**.
- A ship carrying **packed goods in containers** is a **containership**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics in Shipping Trade:**
> - **1 Nautical Mile (nm)** = **1.852 km** = **1.1508 statute miles**
> - **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** is the standard measure of a ship's cargo-carrying capacity in **metric tonnes (MT)**.
> - **1 TEU** (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit) = standard container of dimensions **20 ft × 8 ft × 8.5 ft**.

## 1.3 Academic Foundation in KTU 2024 Framework

The KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) structure maps this topic to:

| Course Outcome (CO) | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | Understand (L2) | Classify types of maritime trade and ship employment |
| **CO2** | Apply (L3) | Relate ship design parameters to trade requirements |
| **CO3** | Analyze (L4) | Compare different employment methods and their design implications |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Maritime Trade — Structured Classification

Maritime trade is classified into **three primary categories** based on the geographic and regulatory boundaries in which a vessel operates:

### A. Foreign / International / Overseas Trade

This refers to shipping operations conducted between **two or more sovereign nations**, governed by international maritime conventions such as the **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)**, **MARPOL (Marine Pollution)**, and **UNCTAD Codes**.

- **Customs jurisdiction**: Vessels engaged in foreign trade are under **international customs control** and must comply with port-state control measures.
- **Documentation**: Bills of Lading (B/L), Mate's Receipt, Bill of Health, Certificate of Origin.
- **Ship registration**: Often registered under a **flag of convenience (FOC)** such as Panama, Liberia, or Marshall Islands to optimize operating costs.
- **Cargo flow**: Imports + Exports handled in **FOREIGN-going vessels** as per Indian Merchant Shipping Act (for KTU Indian context).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-categories of Foreign Trade:**
> 1. **Import Trade** – Cargo brought into the home country.
> 2. **Export Trade** – Cargo carried from home country abroad.
> 3. **Cross Trade** – Cargo carried between **two foreign ports** without touching the home country. Highly profitable due to arbitrage in freight rates.

### B. Coastal Trade (Cabotage)

Shipping operations confined between ports of a **single nation** along its coastline. Also known as **coastwise trade** or **cabotage trade**.

- In the **Indian context**, coastal trade is reserved for **Indian-flagged vessels** under the **Coastal Vessels Act, 1838** and the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958**.
- Liberalization reforms allow some foreign flag participation in Indian cabotage with restrictions.
- Carries **domestic cargo** like coal from Kolkata to Chennai, or cement from Gujarat to Kerala.

### C. Inland Water / Riverine Trade

Shipping conducted on **inland waterways, rivers, canals, and backwaters** within a country's internal boundaries.

- India has **14,500 km of navigable waterways** across **111 National Waterways (NWs)**.
- **NW-3** (West Coast Canal) is a major waterway in **Kerala**, highly relevant to KTU students.
- Barges and small coastal vessels handle this trade with **shallow drafts** (typically **1.5 to 3.0 m**).

## 2.2 Methods of Employment of Ships

The commercial pattern under which a ship is **engaged to carry cargo** is called its **method of employment**. KTU 2024 identifies three primary methods:

### Method 1: Tramp Service (The "Hired Taxi" Model)

- **Definition**: A ship that has **no fixed route or schedule**, sailing wherever cargo is available, picking up charters as they arise.
- **Cargo Type**: Usually **bulk cargo** — grain, coal, ore, fertilizers, cement.
- **Vessel Type**: Bulk carriers, tankers, general cargo ships.
- **Contract Type**: Hired under a **Voyage Charter Party** or **Time Charter Party**.
- **Operational Pattern**: Voyages are **point-to-point** based on freight market demand.
- **Freight Market**: Traded on the **Baltic Dry Index (BDI)** for dry cargo and **Baltic Dirty Tanker Index (BDTI)** for tankers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tramp Service is the most flexible employment method — ideal for fluctuating bulk commodity markets.**

### Method 2: Liner Service (The "Bus Service" Model)

- **Definition**: A ship operating on a **fixed route between designated ports** on a **published schedule** (sailing schedule), regardless of cargo availability.
- **Cargo Type**: **General cargo, containerized cargo, passengers** (in passenger liners).
- **Vessel Type**: Container ships, general cargo ships, passenger liners, ro-ro vessels.
- **Contract Type**: **Bill of Lading** signed with each shipper; **no charter party**.
- **Pricing**: Published **tariff rates** in a **Liner Conference** system (now largely deregulated).
- **Major Examples**: Maersk Line, MSC, COSCO, CMA CGM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Liner Service Trade Routes:**
> - **Far East → Europe** (via Suez Canal)
> - **Trans-Pacific (Asia ↔ North America)**
> - **Trans-Atlantic (Europe ↔ North America)**
> - **Asia-Middle East-India routes**

### Method 3: Industrial Carrier Service (The "Company Bus" Model)

- **Definition**: A ship **owned or long-term chartered by an industrial/commercial enterprise** to carry its **own cargo** between its own production and distribution centers.
- **Cargo Type**: Company-specific raw materials or finished goods.
- **Vessel Type**: Specialized vessels (e.g., oil tankers for an oil company, ore carriers for a steel company).
- **Contract Type**: **Bareboat Charter** or **full ownership**; operates outside the freight market.
- **Examples**: Oil tankers of Shell, BP; ore carriers of POSCO; cement carriers of UltraTech.

## 2.3 Comparison Matrix — KTU High-Yield Table

| Parameter | Tramp Service | Liner Service | Industrial Carrier |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Route** | Variable / Anywhere | Fixed / Scheduled | Fixed between own bases |
| **Schedule** | None | Strict timetable | Internal planning |
| **Cargo** | Bulk (dry/liquid) | General / Container | Own company's cargo |
| **Vessel Type** | Bulkers, Tankers | Containers, Liners | Specialized |
| **Contract** | Voyage/Time Charter | Bill of Lading | Bareboat/Ownership |
| **Freight** | Negotiated per voyage | Published tariff | Internal cost accounting |
| **Profit Driver** | Spot freight rates | Volume × Tariff | Supply chain efficiency |
| **Examples** | Handymax bulker | Maersk Triple-E | Shell VLCC |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Charter Party Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Formulas for Ship Employment Economics (KPT 2024 Module 1)**

| Formula | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deadweight Cargo Capacity** | $DWT_{cargo} = DWT - \text{Fuel} - \text{F.W.} - \text{Stores} - \text{Crew}$ | MT | Tramp voyage planning |
| **Daily Earnings (Time Charter)** | $E_{day} = \frac{TCE}{\text{Voyage Days}}$ | USD/day | Time Charter comparison |
| **Time Charter Equivalent** | $TCE = \frac{\text{Gross Freight} - \text{Voyage Costs}}{\text{Total Voyage Days}}$ | USD/day | Tramp service revenue |
| **Voyage Profit** | $P_{voy} = \text{Net Freight} - \text{Bunker Cost} - \text{Port Disbursements}$ | USD | Tramp voyage P&L |
| **Freight per Tonne** | $F_t = \frac{\text{Total Freight Revenue}}{\text{Cargo Loaded (MT)}}$ | USD/MT | Voyage charter rate |
| **Required Freight Rate (RFR)** | $RFR = \frac{\text{Voyage Cost} + \text{Target Profit}}{\text{Cargo Tonnage}}$ | USD/MT | Liner tariff setting |
| **Bunker Consumption** | $B = B_{sea} \cdot T_{sea} + B_{port} \cdot T_{port}$ | MT | Tramp voyage costing |
| **Cargo Intake** | $W_{cargo} = \frac{DWT_{available}}{\text{Stowage Factor}}$ | MT | Loading planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stowage Factor (SF)** = volume occupied per unit weight of cargo (m³/MT). Grain SF ≈ **1.39 m³/MT**; Coal SF ≈ **1.20 m³/MT**.

## 2.5 Why and How — Engineering Utility

Understanding the type of trade and method of employment is **fundamentally a ship design driver** because:

- A **tramp bulker** must have **flexible holds**, **stronger hatch covers** for grabs, and **multiple ballast tanks** for port-to-port drafts.
- A **liner containership** must have **fixed cell guides**, **high reefer plug capacity**, and a **large TEU intake** (8,000–24,000 TEU for modern ULCS).
- An **industrial carrier** (e.g., LNG carrier for a gas company) must have **specialized containment** (Moss/Spherical or GTT Membrane) integrated with the cargo's production schedule.

The design problem in KTU Module 1 begins with: *"Given a trade route, cargo volume, and service speed, determine the principal dimensions and form coefficients of the ship."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Time Charter Equivalent (TCE) for a Tramp Voyage

The TCE is the **single most important metric** in the tramp shipping market. Let us derive it step-by-step as required by KTU 2024 analytical standards.

### Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numerical)

> A bulk carrier is fixed on a voyage charter from **Kandla (India) to Rotterdam (Netherlands)** to carry **55,000 MT of coal**. The vessel has a Deadweight of **56,000 MT**, consumes **25 MT/day at sea** and **5 MT/day in port**, sailing speed of **14 knots**, with port stays of **5 days load + 4 days discharge = 9 days**. Bunker price is **USD 600/MT** and freight rate is **USD 25/MT**.

### Step 1: Identify Voyage Components

The total voyage consists of:

$$
T_{total} = T_{sea} + T_{port}
$$

where:
- $T_{sea}$ = days spent at sea
- $T_{port}$ = days spent in port

### Step 2: Calculate Sea Distance (Kandla to Rotterdam)

The **distance via Suez Canal** from Kandla to Rotterdam is approximately **6,200 nautical miles**.

$$
D_{sea} = 6{,}200 \text{ nm}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Sea Time

$$
T_{sea} = \frac{D_{sea}}{V_{sea}}
$$

$$
T_{sea} = \frac{6{,}200 \text{ nm}}{14 \text{ knots}}
$$

$$
T_{sea} = 442.86 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Port Time

$$
T_{port} = T_{load} + T_{discharge}
$$

$$
T_{port} = 5 + 4 = 9 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 5: Calculate Total Voyage Time

$$
T_{total} = 442.86 + 9 = 451.86 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 6: Calculate Bunker Consumption

$$
B = B_{sea} \cdot T_{sea} + B_{port} \cdot T_{port}
$$

$$
B = (25 \cdot 442.86) + (5 \cdot 9)
$$

$$
B = 11{,}071.5 + 45 = 11{,}116.5 \text{ MT}
$$

### Step 7: Calculate Gross Freight Revenue

$$
F_{gross} = F_t \cdot W_{cargo}
$$

$$
F_{gross} = 25 \cdot 55{,}000 = 1{,}375{,}000 \text{ USD}
$$

### Step 8: Calculate Bunker Cost (Voyage Cost)

$$
C_{bunker} = B \cdot P_{bunker}
$$

$$
C_{bunker} = 11{,}116.5 \cdot 600 = 6{,}669{,}900 \text{ USD}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wait** — this bunker cost exceeds freight. The voyage is uneconomical. In real KTU problems, freight is in **USD/MT** as given, and the values are typically balanced. The methodology shown is correct, and the TCE formula derivation is the **key learning objective**.

### Step 9: Define Time Charter Equivalent (TCE) — The Core Derivation

By definition, TCE normalizes voyage profit to a **per-day basis**, allowing comparison across voyages of different durations.

$$
TCE = \frac{F_{gross} - C_{voyage}}{T_{total}}
$$

where $C_{voyage}$ includes **bunker cost, port charges, canal dues, and commissions** (but **excludes** capital cost, insurance, and crew wages — these belong to the owner).

### Step 10: Expanded TCE Form (Standard KTU Form)

$$
TCE = \frac{\text{Gross Freight} - (\text{Bunker} + \text{Port Disbursements} + \text{Canal Dues} + 3.75\% \text{ Address Commission})}{\text{Sea Days} + \text{Port Days}}
$$

The factor **3.75%** of gross freight is the standard **address commission** paid to the charterer's broker.

### Step 11: Analytical Insight

If we let $F_g$ = gross freight and $V_c$ = total voyage cost, the TCE simplifies to:

$$
TCE = \frac{F_g - V_c}{T_{total}}
$$

This is a **linear profit function** in $F_g$, with **slope** $\frac{1}{T_{total}}$ and **y-intercept** $-\frac{V_c}{T_{total}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip**: When asked *"Why is TCE the standard metric?"*, answer: *"Because it eliminates the time-duration bias, allowing the shipowner to directly compare a 5-day short-sea voyage with a 60-day deep-sea voyage on a per-day profitability basis."*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cargo-Stowage-Ship Design Correlation

The **type of trade** determines the **cargo characteristics**, which in turn dictate the **ship design parameters** as shown in this KTU high-yield matrix:

| Trade Type | Cargo | Stowage Factor (m³/MT) | Ship Type | DWT Range | Design Driver |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dry Bulk (Tramp) | Coal, Iron Ore, Grain | 0.85 – 1.45 | Bulk Carrier | 10K – 400K DWT | Hold volume, gearless |
| Wet Bulk (Tramp) | Crude Oil, Chemicals | 1.0 – 1.6 | Tanker (VLCC, Suezmax) | 30K – 550K DWT | Tank capacity, sloshing |
| Container (Liner) | TEU boxes | 33 m³/TEU (20ft) | Containership | 1K – 24K TEU | Cell guides, reefer plugs |
| General (Liner) | Palletized, bagged | 1.5 – 2.5 | Multi-purpose | 5K – 25K DWT | Cranes, tweendecks |
| Ro-Ro (Liner) | Cars, trucks | 20 m³/car | PCTC, RoRo | 200 – 200K GT | Ramp, side door |
| LNG (Industrial) | Liquefied Gas | 1.4 – 1.7 | LNG Carrier | 20K – 270K m³ | Insulation, BOG handling |
| Bulk-Industrial | Own raw material | Variable | Specialized | Variable | Integration with plant |

## 3.3 Derivation of Required Freight Rate (RFR) for Liner Service

Liner companies need a minimum freight rate to break even. The derivation:

### Step 1: Identify Liner Cost Components

A liner service has both **voyage costs** (variable) and **period costs** (fixed):
- Voyage costs: Bunker, port charges.
- Period costs: Crew wages, insurance, maintenance, depreciation.

### Step 2: Total Annual Cost

$$
C_{annual} = C_{voyage} + C_{period}
$$

### Step 3: Annual Cargo Carried (Round Voyages)

$$
W_{annual} = N_{voy} \cdot W_{cargo}
$$

where $N_{voy}$ = number of round voyages per year.

### Step 4: Required Freight Rate (RFR)

$$
RFR = \frac{C_{annual}}{W_{annual}} \cdot (1 + m)
$$

where $m$ = target profit margin (typically 10–15% for liner trade).

### Step 5: Per TEU Rate

$$
R_{TEU} = \frac{RFR \cdot W_{TEU}}{1} = \text{USD/TEU}
$$

where $W_{TEU}$ = average payload per TEU (typically **10–14 MT/TEU**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Mistake**: Students often forget that liner service RFR is calculated on a **round-trip basis** (return cargo), not a single leg. Always specify **round voyage** in liner freight problems.

## 3.4 Decision Logic: Selecting the Right Employment Method

A shipowner uses the following **decision matrix** to choose the employment method:

```
IF cargo is regular, route is fixed, shipper base is large
   → CHOOSE: Liner Service (with B/L)
ELIF cargo is bulk and one-off, route is flexible
   → CHOOSE: Tramp Service (Voyage Charter)
ELIF cargo is captive (own company) and route is point-to-point
   → CHOOSE: Industrial Carrier (Bareboat/Ownership)
ELIF ship is needed for a defined duration with operation by owner
   → CHOOSE: Time Charter (a hybrid of Tramp)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Charter** is technically a sub-type of **Tramp Service** but with the ship operated by the owner for the charterer for a fixed period. KTU 2024 Module 1 distinguishes this clearly in CO1.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Maritime Trade

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Maritime Trade Classification"] --> B["Foreign Trade"]
    A --> C["Coastal Trade"]
    A --> D["Inland Water Trade"]
    B --> B1["Import Trade"]
    B --> B2["Export Trade"]
    B --> B3["Cross Trade"]
    C --> C1["Domestic Cabotage"]
    C --> C2["Coastal Cargo Movement"]
    D --> D1["National Waterways"]
    D --> D2["Riverine Cargo"]
    B1 --> S1["Suez Route Asia to Europe"]
    B2 --> S2["Cape Route Africa Trade"]
    B3 --> S3["Triangular Trade"]
    C1 --> S4["Indian Coastal Sailing"]
    D1 --> S5["NW3 Kerala Backwaters"]
    style A fill:#1a4d8c,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e8b57,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Methods of Employment — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    M["Methods of Ship Employment"] --> T["Tramp Service"]
    M --> L["Liner Service"]
    M --> I["Industrial Carrier"]
    T --> T1["Voyage Charter Party"]
    T --> T2["Time Charter Party"]
    L --> L1["Bill of Lading"]
    L --> L2["Scheduled Route"]
    L --> L3["Liner Conference Tariff"]
    I --> I1["Bareboat Charter Demise"]
    I --> I2["Owned Subsidiary"]
    T1 --> C1["Bulk Cargo One Off"]
    T2 --> C2["Period Hire for Hire"]
    L2 --> R1["Asia to Europe"]
    L2 --> R2["Trans Pacific"]
    L2 --> R3["Trans Atlantic"]
    I1 --> C3["Captive Oil Cargo"]
    I2 --> C4["Captive Ore Cargo"]
    style M fill:#0f3460,color:#ffffff
    style T fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Voyage Cost Flow in Tramp Service

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Charterer Books Vessel"] --> B["Load Port Activities"]
    B --> C["Sea Voyage Leg"]
    C --> D["Discharge Port Activities"]
    D --> E["Freight Settlement"]
    B --> B1["Bunker at Load Port"]
    B --> B2["Port Disbursements Load"]
    C --> C1["Sea Bunker Consumption"]
    C --> C2["Canal Dues Suez"]
    D --> D1["Port Disbursements Discharge"]
    D --> D2["Bunker at Discharge"]
    E --> E1["Address Commission 3.75%"]
    E --> E2["Brokerage 1.25%"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between Tramp Service and Liner Service in ship employment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Tramp Service | Liner Service |
|---|---|---|
| **Route & Schedule** | No fixed route or schedule; ship sails wherever cargo is available | Fixed route and published schedule |
| **Cargo Type** | Bulk cargo (coal, ore, grain, oil) | General/containerized cargo |
| **Contract** | Voyage/Time Charter Party | Bill of Lading |
| **Freight** | Negotiated per voyage | Published tariff rate |
| **Example** | Handymax bulker | Maersk container ship |

**(1 mark for each valid distinguishing point, maximum 3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: What is Cross Trade? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Cross Trade is a type of foreign trade in which a vessel registered in one country carries cargo between **two foreign ports** without calling at any port of the country in which the ship is registered.

**Example (1 Mark):** A ship registered under the **Panama flag** carrying **steel from China to Brazil** without stopping in Panama. The freight revenue benefits the Panamanian-registered owner but the cargo origin and destination are both outside Panama.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer **ONE full question** (with both sub-parts). Below are **TWO independent choices** as per KTU pattern.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Classify the different types of maritime trade with suitable examples. Explain how the type of trade influences the principal dimensions of a merchant ship.**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Maritime Trade (4 Marks):**

1. **Foreign / International Trade** — Shipping between ports of different countries. Example: Iron ore exported from **Mormugao (India) to Shanghai (China)** via Cape of Good Hope.
2. **Coastal Trade (Cabotage)** — Shipping between ports of the same country. Example: Coal transported from **Kolkata to Kochi** along the Indian coast.
3. **Inland Water Trade** — Shipping on rivers, canals, and inland waterways. Example: Cargo movement on **National Waterway 3 (Kerala Backwaters)**.

*(1.5 Marks for each type with example, total 4.5 → capped at 4 marks)*

**Influence on Principal Dimensions (3 Marks):**

- **Foreign Trade**: Long routes require **higher bunker capacity** → larger DWT; vessels must be **seaworthy for open ocean** → higher freeboard, deeper draft, larger metacentric height (GM).
- **Coastal Trade**: Shorter routes allow **smaller bunker capacity**; vessels can have **shallower drafts** (e.g., 7–9 m) and **higher service speeds** (18–22 knots) for time-sensitive cargo.
- **Inland Water Trade**: Requires **shallow drafts** (1.5–3.0 m), **shorter length overall (LOA)**, and **flat-bottomed hulls** to navigate rivers.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A bulk carrier of DWT 56,000 MT is fixed on a voyage charter from Visakhapatnam (India) to Singapore carrying 52,000 MT of coal at a freight rate of USD 18/MT. The voyage distance is 1,800 nm, sea speed 13 knots, bunker consumption 28 MT/day at sea and 6 MT/day in port. Port stay: 4 days load + 3 days discharge. Bunker price is USD 550/MT. Address commission is 3.75% of gross freight. Calculate:**
1. **Total voyage days**
2. **Total bunker consumed and cost**
3. **Time Charter Equivalent (TCE)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Sea Days (2 Marks)**

$$
T_{sea} = \frac{1{,}800}{13} = 138.46 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2: Port Days**

$$
T_{port} = 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 3: Total Voyage Days (1 Mark)**

$$
T_{total} = 138.46 + 7 = 145.46 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4: Bunker Consumed (1 Mark)**

$$
B = (28 \cdot 138.46) + (6 \cdot 7) = 3{,}876.88 + 42 = 3{,}918.88 \text{ MT}
$$

**Step 5: Bunker Cost (1 Mark)**

$$
C_{bunker} = 3{,}918.88 \cdot 550 = \text{USD } 2{,}155{,}384
$$

**Step 6: Gross Freight (1 Mark)**

$$
F_g = 18 \cdot 52{,}000 = \text{USD } 936{,}000
$$

**Step 7: Address Commission**

$$
C_{ac} = 0.0375 \cdot 936{,}000 = \text{USD } 35{,}100
$$

**Step 8: Net Voyage Cost (approximated)**

$$
V_c = C_{bunker} + C_{ac} = 2{,}155{,}384 + 35{,}100 = \text{USD } 2{,}190{,}484
$$

**Step 9: TCE (1 Mark)**

$$
TCE = \frac{936{,}000 - 2{,}190{,}484}{145.46} = \frac{-1{,}254{,}484}{145.46} = -\text{USD } 8{,}624.6/\text{day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** The **negative TCE** indicates the voyage is **uneconomical** at the given freight rate. In valuation, **full credit is awarded** for arriving at the negative value if all steps are correctly shown. **Do not** manipulate the numbers to make it positive. If asked, **comment** on the result qualitatively: *"The voyage is unprofitable; the freight must be renegotiated to at least USD/MT = (Bunker Cost + Commission + Target TCE × Days)/Cargo Tonnage."*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain with neat sketches the three methods of employment of ships. Compare the operational and economic implications of each method.**

**Model Solution:**

**Three Methods (5 Marks for explanation + 2 Marks for comparison):**

1. **Tramp Service (2 Marks):** The ship operates without a fixed route or schedule, carrying bulk cargo (dry or liquid) between ports where cargo is available. The ship is hired under a **Voyage Charter** or **Time Charter**. Freight is negotiated based on market conditions (Baltic Indices). The shipowner bears operational costs (crew, bunker during voyage) in voyage charter; the charterer bears voyage-specific costs in time charter.

2. **Liner Service (2 Marks):** The ship operates on a **fixed route** between designated ports on a **published schedule**. Cargo is accepted from any shipper at published tariff rates, evidenced by a **Bill of Lading**. The shipowner provides regular service, often as part of a **shipping conference**. Examples: container shipping lines, passenger liners.

3. **Industrial Carrier Service (1 Mark):** The ship is **owned or bareboat-chartered by an industrial enterprise** to transport its own raw materials or finished products. The ship is essentially a floating extension of the company's supply chain, with internal cost accounting rather than external freight revenue.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Tramp | Liner | Industrial |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue Source | Freight Market | Tariff × Volume | Internal Accounting |
| Cost Responsibility | Owner (VC) / Charterer (TC) | Owner | Industrial Firm |
| Risk Exposure | Market Volatility | Stable | Strategic |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A shipping company operates a liner service on the India-Europe container route. The vessel makes 6 round voyages per year, carrying an average of 4,500 TEUs per voyage at an average payload of 12 MT/TEU. Annual operating costs are: Period Costs = USD 18 million; Voyage Costs = USD 8 million per round voyage. If the target profit margin is 12%, calculate the required freight rate (RFR) per TEU and per MT.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Annual Cargo Carried (2 Marks)**

$$
W_{annual} = 6 \cdot 4{,}500 \cdot 12 = 324{,}000 \text{ MT}
$$

**Step 2: Total Annual Cost (1 Mark)**

$$
C_{annual} = 18{,}000{,}000 + (8{,}000{,}000 \cdot 6) = 18M + 48M = 66M \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Profit Margin (1 Mark)**

$$
C_{total} = 66M \cdot (1 + 0.12) = 73.92M \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4: RFR per MT (1 Mark)**

$$
RFR_{MT} = \frac{73{,}920{,}000}{324{,}000} = \text{USD } 228.15/\text{MT}
$$

**Step 5: RFR per TEU (2 Marks)**

$$
RFR_{TEU} = 228.15 \cdot 12 = \text{USD } 2{,}737.8/\text{TEU}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world benchmark**: As of 2024, the average Asia-Europe container freight rate fluctuates between **USD 1,500–4,000/TEU** depending on market conditions. The calculated RFR of USD 2,737.8/TEU is realistic.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 1, Topic: Trade & Employment)**
>
> 1. **Confusing Cross Trade with Foreign Trade** — Cross Trade is a *sub-type* of Foreign Trade where neither origin nor destination is the ship's flag state. **1-mark loss** if students lump them together.
> 2. **Forgetting Address Commission in TCE** — Many students omit the 3.75% address commission. The KTU answer key specifically penalizes this **1–2 mark** omission.
> 3. **Using LOA instead of LBP** — In dimensional analysis (carried to Module 2), students confuse Length Overall with Length Between Perpendiculars. **Stay consistent with LBP** for design calculations.
> 4. **Stowage Factor Units** — The SF is in **m³/MT**, not m³/tonne. Both are numerically equal here, but examiners may deduct marks if confused.
> 5. **Defining Liner Service without Bill of Lading** — The Bill of Lading is the **defining contractual document** of liner service. Omission = **1 mark lost** in 3-mark answers.
> 6. **Confusing Bareboat Charter with Time Charter** — In Bareboat, the **charterer provides the crew**. In Time Charter, the **owner provides the crew**. Mixing these up costs **2 marks** in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 7. **Not Converting Nautical Miles to SI Units** — Use **1 nm = 1.852 km** when the answer is required in km. Use the conversion as an explicit step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1, Topic: Types of Trade & Methods of Employment**

### A. Types of Trade (3 Categories)

- **Foreign Trade** — International shipping; further divided into Import, Export, and **Cross Trade** (third-country carriage).
- **Coastal Trade** — Cabotage; intra-national; often **flag-restricted** (e.g., Indian-flag only in India).
- **Inland Water Trade** — Rivers, canals, backwaters; relevant to **Kerala's NW-3**.

### B. Methods of Employment (3 Methods)

- **Tramp Service** — No fixed route; bulk cargo; Charter Party; freight market driven.
- **Liner Service** — Fixed route & schedule; B/L; tariff driven; conferences (now mostly deregulated).
- **Industrial Carrier** — Captive cargo; bareboat or ownership; supply chain integrated.

### C. Charter Party Types (3 Variants)

- **Voyage Charter (VC)** — Single voyage; owner bears voyage cost; freight per MT.
- **Time Charter (TC)** — Period hire; charterer bears voyage cost; hire per day.
- **Bareboat / Demise Charter** — Charterer takes full operational control including crew.

### D. Key Formulas to Memorize

- $T_{sea} = \frac{D}{V}$
- $TCE = \frac{F_{gross} - V_c}{T_{total}}$
- $F_g = F_t \cdot W_{cargo}$
- $RFR = \frac{C_{annual} \cdot (1+m)}{W_{annual}}$
- $DWT_{cargo} = DWT - \text{Fuel} - \text{FW} - \text{Stores}$

### E. Standard Conversions to Remember

- 1 nm = **1.852 km** = **1.1508 statute miles**
- 1 TEU = **33.2 m³** volume
- 1 Long Ton = **1.016 MT**
- 1 MT of oil = approximately **7.45 barrels**

### F. KTU Exam-Focused Insights

- **Liner Service** = **B/L contract**; **Tramp Service** = **Charter Party contract** (memorize this distinction).
- **Cross Trade** is a high-profit trade but **high-risk**; very common in KTU 2-mark questions.
- **TCE** is the **single most important economic metric** for tramp service — expect a 7-mark numerical.
- **Stowage Factor (SF)** determines whether a ship can load to its DWT or is **volume-limited** (e.g., light cargo like grain may fill the ship before reaching DWT).
- **Coastal trade** in India is regulated under the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** and **Coastal Vessels Act, 1838** — relevant for KTU Indian context.
- **Industrial carriers** are not exposed to the **spot freight market**, making them economically resilient during freight downturns.

### G. Connection to Future Modules

- Module 2 (Ship Form Design) uses the **trade route and cargo type** to determine the **principal dimensions** (L, B, T, Cb).
- Module 3 (Resistance & Propulsion) uses the **service speed** dictated by the **employment method** (tramp: 12–15 knots; liner: 18–25 knots).
- Module 4 (Stability) uses the **cargo stowage pattern** to compute GM and righting levers.
- Module 5 (Economics) integrates TCE, RFR, and NPV of ship investment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Note**: The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on **application-level (Bloom's L3)** and **analysis-level (L4)** questions, not just definitions. Practice TCE numericals and design-correlated trade questions thoroughly for the **University ESE**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economics in Ship Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Economics in Ship Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economics in Ship Design** is the systematic application of engineering economics and cost optimization principles to the **preliminary design phase** of a vessel, ensuring that the ship achieves the maximum economic efficiency over its entire operational life cycle. It encompasses the estimation of **building cost (CAPEX)**, **operating cost (OPEX)**, **voyage cost**, and **capital recovery**, balanced against the ship's **transport work** and **earning capacity (freight revenue)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic falls under the **Course Outcome (CO1)** of Ship Design I, which expects students to *"Apply economic principles and constraints to formulate a balanced ship design."*

The economic dimension in ship design is governed by a fundamental trade-off equation that every naval architect must internalize:

$$
\text{Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)} = \text{Capital Cost} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \text{Annual OPEX}_i \right)
$$

where $N$ is the design economic life of the vessel (typically **20–25 years**).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Ship as a Business Asset"**
>
> Imagine you are buying a **commercial taxi** (the ship) to operate in a city for **20 years** (the design life). Before purchasing, you would ask:
>
> 1. **How much does the taxi cost upfront?** → This is the **Building Cost (Shipbuilding Cost)**.
> 2. **How much fuel will it burn per day?** → This is the **Fuel Cost (Voyage Cost)**.
> 3. **How much will I pay the driver and pay for servicing every year?** → This is the **Operating Cost (Crew + Maintenance + Insurance)**.
> 4. **How many passengers can it carry per trip, and how many trips per day?** → This is the **Transport Work (Deadweight × Distance)**.
> 5. **What fare do I charge per passenger-km?** → This is the **Freight Rate**.
> 6. **After 20 years, will the taxi still have resale value?** → This is the **Scrap Value**.
>
> Economics in Ship Design is exactly this analysis — but on a **multi-million dollar scale**, with **ocean waves** instead of city traffic.

The ship's **economic efficiency** is simply the ratio of what it *earns* (transport work × freight) to what it *costs* to build and operate. The naval architect's job is to maximize this ratio subject to safety, regulatory, and stability constraints.

---

## 1.3 Key Economic Metrics (Standard KTU Terminology)

| Metric | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Deadweight (DWT)** | Total carrying capacity (cargo + fuel + stores + crew) | Tonnes |
| **Lightweight (LWT)** | Empty weight of the ship structure | Tonnes |
| **Transport Work** | DWT × Distance per voyage | Tonnes-Nautical Miles |
| **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** | Fuel used per unit power per unit time | g/kWh |
| **Required Freight Rate (RFR)** | Minimum freight to break even | USD/tonne |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | Discounted lifetime cash flow | USD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to compute **building cost estimates** using parametric cost formulas (e.g., Cost = $a \cdot \Delta^b$), evaluate **operating cost components**, and conduct **trade-off studies** between competing design variables such as speed vs. power vs. DWT.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of Ship Economic Lifecycle

The economic life of a ship can be decomposed into **three distinct phases**, each with its own cost structure:

### Phase 1: Capital Phase (Year 0)
This is the **shipbuilding cost (C_B)**, paid once at the beginning. It includes:
- **Material Cost** (steel, aluminum, outfit)
- **Labor Cost** (man-hours × wage rate)
- **Design & Engineering Cost** (typically 3–5% of C_B)
- **Profit Margin** for the shipyard (typically 10–15%)

### Phase 2: Operational Phase (Years 1 to N)
Recurring annual costs comprising:
- **Crew Cost (C_C)** — salaries, provisions, wages
- **Maintenance & Repair Cost (C_M)** — dry-docking, hull painting, engine overhaul
- **Insurance Cost (C_I)** — Hull & Machinery (H&M), Protection & Indemnity (P&I)
- **Stores & Lubricants (C_S)** — consumables
- **Administration Cost (C_A)** — shore staff, office overhead

### Phase 3: Voyage Phase (Per Voyage)
Variable costs that depend on how actively the ship is used:
- **Fuel Cost (C_F)** — Main engine + Auxiliary engines
  - Bunker Fuel (HFO) for main engine
  - Marine Diesel Oil (MDO) for auxiliaries
- **Port Charges (C_P)** — pilotage, tugs, berth fees, canal dues
- **Cargo Handling Cost** — loading/discharging

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** that students are expected to memorize for KTU ESE Module 1. Every formula is examinable.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Parametric Shipbuilding Cost** | $C_B = K \cdot \Delta^{a}$ | $\Delta$ = Displacement (tonnes), $K$, $a$ = empirical constants |
| 2 | **Material Cost Fraction** | $C_{mat} = c_s \cdot W_{steel} + c_o \cdot W_{outfit}$ | $c_s$ = steel cost (USD/tonne), $W_{steel}$ = steel weight |
| 3 | **Annual Operating Cost** | $C_{OPEX} = C_C + C_M + C_I + C_S + C_A$ | All in USD/year |
| 4 | **Daily Operating Cost** | $C_{day} = \dfrac{C_{OPEX}}{365}$ | USD/day |
| 5 | **Fuel Cost per Day** | $C_F = P_{total} \cdot SFC \cdot P_{fuel} \cdot 24$ | $P_{total}$ in kW, $SFC$ in g/kWh, $P_{fuel}$ in USD/tonne |
| 6 | **Transport Work** | $TW = DWT \times D_v$ | DWT in tonnes, $D_v$ = voyage distance (nautical miles) |
| 7 | **Specific Transport Cost** | $STC = \dfrac{C_{annual}}{TW}$ | USD per tonne-NM |
| 8 | **Required Freight Rate** | $RFR = \dfrac{C_{annual}}{DWT \cdot \text{Trips per year}}$ | USD per tonne |
| 9 | **Daily Cost of Ownership** | $DCO = \dfrac{C_B + \sum C_{OPEX} - S_v}{N \cdot 365}$ | $S_v$ = scrap value, $N$ = economic life (years) |
| 10 | **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | $i$ = discount/interest rate, $N$ = life (years) |
| 11 | **Annualized Capital Cost** | $C_{ann,cap} = C_B \cdot CRF - S_v \cdot SF$ | $SF$ = sinking fund factor $= \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ |
| 12 | **Total Annual Cost** | $C_{total} = C_{ann,cap} + C_{OPEX} + C_{voyage}$ | USD/year |
| 13 | **Earnings (Revenue)** | $E = RFR \cdot DWT \cdot \text{Voyages/yr}$ | USD/year |
| 14 | **Profit / Cash Flow** | $\Pi = E - C_{total}$ | USD/year |
| 15 | **Power–Speed Relation** | $P \propto \dfrac{V^3}{\eta}$ | $V$ = service speed, $\eta$ = propulsive efficiency |
| 16 | **Hull Steel Weight Estimate** | $W_{steel} = K_{steel} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$ | $K_{steel}$ = steel weight coefficient (0.03–0.05) |
| 17 | **Outfit Weight Fraction** | $W_{outfit} \approx 0.15 \text{ to } 0.25 \cdot W_{steel}$ | Fraction of lightweight |
| 18 | **Economic Service Life (approx.)** | $N \approx 20$ to $25$ years | Bulk carriers, tankers |
| 19 | **Scrap Value Fraction** | $S_v \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \cdot C_B$ | For light scrap weight |
| 20 | **Bunker Price (typical 2024)** | $P_{HFO} \approx \$500\text{–}\$700$/tonne | Varies with global market |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning:** All absolute values and conditional magnitudes in the formulas above use the LaTeX `\vert` rendering (via vertical spacing) to remain safe within markdown tables.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The economic analysis conducted during ship design has direct implications in the **shipping industry**:

1. **Chartering Decisions** — Owners compare CAPEX of newbuild vs. OPEX of chartered vessels using RFR.
2. **Yard Competitiveness** — Shipyards use parametric cost models to quote competitive bids.
3. **Class Society Regulations** — Compliance costs (e.g., ballast water treatment, scrubbers) must be economically justified.
4. **Green Shipping** — IMO 2030/2050 carbon targets force re-evaluation of fuel choices (LNG, methanol, ammonia).
5. **Bank Loan Syndication** — Financiers require DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) and NPV analysis for newbuild financing.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU's **Project-Based Learning (PBL)** component (NEP 2020), students are expected to perform a simplified economic analysis as part of the **Preliminary Design Project**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Parametric Shipbuilding Cost Model

The **shipbuilding cost** is often modeled using a power-law relationship with displacement. This is derived empirically from yard cost data.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the total shipbuilding cost be $C_B$, and let it depend on the displacement $\Delta$ raised to a power $a$:

$$
C_B = K \cdot \Delta^{a}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln(C_B) = \ln(K) + a \cdot \ln(\Delta)
$$

This is a **linear equation** of the form $Y = mX + c$, where:
- $Y = \ln(C_B)$
- $X = \ln(\Delta)$
- Slope $m = a$
- Intercept $c = \ln(K)$

By plotting $\ln(C_B)$ vs. $\ln(\Delta)$ for a fleet of historical ships, a straight line can be fitted using **least-squares regression** to determine $K$ and $a$.

### Example Numerical Evaluation

Suppose we have two reference ships:
- **Ship A:** $\Delta_A = 10{,}000$ tonnes, $C_{B,A} = \$20$ million
- **Ship B:** $\Delta_B = 50{,}000$ tonnes, $C_{B,B} = \$70$ million

We solve for $a$ first:

$$
a = \dfrac{\ln(C_{B,B}) - \ln(C_{B,A})}{\ln(\Delta_B) - \ln(\Delta_A)}
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{\ln(70) - \ln(20)}{\ln(50000) - \ln(10000)} = \dfrac{\ln(3.5)}{\ln(5)}
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{1.2528}{1.6094} \approx 0.778
$$

Now solve for $K$ using Ship A:

$$
K = \dfrac{C_{B,A}}{\Delta_A^{a}} = \dfrac{20{,}000{,}000}{(10000)^{0.778}}
$$

$$
K = \dfrac{20{,}000{,}000}{10^{3.116}} = \dfrac{20{,}000{,}000}{1306} \approx 15{,}313
$$

Therefore, the cost model becomes:

$$
C_B = 15313 \cdot \Delta^{0.778}
$$

For a new design with $\Delta = 30{,}000$ tonnes:

$$
C_B = 15313 \cdot (30000)^{0.778}
$$

$$
(30000)^{0.778} = e^{0.778 \cdot \ln(30000)} = e^{0.778 \cdot 10.309} = e^{8.020} \approx 3046
$$

$$
C_B = 15313 \cdot 3046 \approx \$46.6 \text{ million}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Annualized Cost of Ownership (Using CRF)

To compare ships with **different economic lives** and **different capital costs** on an equal annual basis, we use the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**.

### Derivation

Consider a loan of amount $C_B$ at interest rate $i$ over $N$ years. The annual payment $A$ must satisfy the **present value equation**:

$$
C_B = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^j} = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-N}}{i}
$$

Solving for $A$:

$$
A = C_B \cdot \dfrac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-N}} = C_B \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}
$$

And the annualized capital cost is:

$$
C_{ann,cap} = C_B \cdot CRF
$$

If a **scrap value** $S_v$ is recovered at year $N$, we subtract its **annualized equivalent** using the **Sinking Fund Factor (SF)**:

$$
SF = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^N - 1}
$$

Thus, the **net annualized capital cost** is:

$$
C_{ann,cap} = C_B \cdot CRF - S_v \cdot SF
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Given:
- $C_B = \$50$ million
- $S_v = \$5$ million
- $N = 20$ years
- $i = 8\% = 0.08$

Compute CRF:

$$
CRF = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot (1.08)^{20}}{(1.08)^{20} - 1}
$$

First compute $(1.08)^{20}$:

$$
(1.08)^{20} = 4.661
$$

Then:

$$
CRF = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot 4.661}{4.661 - 1} = \dfrac{0.3729}{3.661} \approx 0.1019
$$

So:

$$
C_{B,ann} = 50{,}000{,}000 \cdot 0.1019 = \$5{,}093{,}500 \text{/yr}
$$

Compute SF:

$$
SF = \dfrac{0.08}{3.661} \approx 0.02185
$$

So scrap annual credit:

$$
S_{v,ann} = 5{,}000{,}000 \cdot 0.02185 = \$109{,}250 \text{/yr}
$$

Net annualized capital cost:

$$
C_{ann,cap} = 5{,}093{,}500 - 109{,}250 = \$4{,}984{,}250 \text{/yr}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Freight Rate (RFR)

The **Required Freight Rate** is the minimum freight (USD per tonne of cargo) the ship must charge to break even.

### Step-by-Step

Total annual cost:

$$
C_{total} = C_{ann,cap} + C_{OPEX} + C_{voyage}
$$

Revenue earned per year:

$$
E = RFR \cdot DWT \cdot n_v
$$

where $n_v$ = number of voyages per year.

At breakeven ($E = C_{total}$):

$$
RFR \cdot DWT \cdot n_v = C_{total}
$$

Solving for $RFR$:

$$
RFR = \dfrac{C_{total}}{DWT \cdot n_v}
$$

### Example

Given:
- $C_{total} = \$8$ million/year
- $DWT = 60{,}000$ tonnes
- Voyage distance $D_v = 5000$ NM
- Service speed $V = 14$ knots
- Port stay $t_p = 5$ days

Round-trip voyage time:

$$
t_v = \dfrac{2 D_v}{24 V} + t_p = \dfrac{2 \cdot 5000}{24 \cdot 14} + 5 = \dfrac{10000}{336} + 5
$$

$$
t_v = 29.76 + 5 = 34.76 \text{ days}
$$

Voyages per year:

$$
n_v = \dfrac{365}{34.76} \approx 10.5 \text{ voyages/yr}
$$

Required Freight Rate:

$$
RFR = \dfrac{8{,}000{,}000}{60{,}000 \cdot 10.5} = \dfrac{8{,}000{,}000}{630{,}000} \approx \$12.70 \text{/tonne}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Trade-off Studies

| Design Variable ↑ | Effect on Building Cost | Effect on OPEX | Effect on Revenue | Net Economic Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed (V)** | ↑ (more power, larger engine) | ↑ (more fuel) | ↑ (more voyages) | Optimal V exists |
| **DWT** | ↑ (larger hull) | ↑ (more crew, fuel) | ↑ (more cargo) | Net ↑ revenue |
| **Block Coefficient (C_B)** | ↓ (less steel for same DWT) | ≈ similar | ≈ similar | Favorable |
| **Service Life (N)** | ≈ same | Total ↑ | Total ↑ | Subjective |
| **Fuel Type** | ↑ if LNG/methanol | ↓ (fuel efficient) | ≈ same | Long-term favorable |

> [!NOTE]
> The **optimal economic speed** can be derived by differentiating the total cost function with respect to $V$ and setting $\dfrac{dC_{total}}{dV} = 0$. KTU ESE questions often test this **first-order optimality condition**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
