<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define *Required Freight Rate (RFR)* and state its significance in ship economics. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Required Freight Rate is the minimum freight rate per tonne of cargo that a ship must earn annually to cover all its costs — both capital and operating — over its economic life. Mathematically:

$$
RFR = \dfrac{C_{annual}}{DWT \times n_v}
$$

where $C_{annual}$ is the total annual cost (USD/year), $DWT$ is deadweight (tonnes), and $n_v$ is the number of voyages per year.

**Significance (1 mark):**
It is the **breakeven freight rate**. If the market freight rate exceeds RFR, the ship earns a profit; if it falls below RFR, the ship operates at a loss. It is used by owners to decide whether to **order a newbuilding, charter a vessel, or lay it up**.

---

### Question 2: What is meant by *Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)*? Give its expression. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Meaning (2 marks):**
The Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) is a financial factor used to convert a **present lump-sum capital cost** into an equivalent **uniform annual cost** over the economic life of the ship, taking into account the time value of money (interest rate). It allows the shipowner to spread the newbuilding price into equal annual installments for comparison with annual operating expenses.

**Expression (1 mark):**

$$
CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{N}}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}
$$

where $i$ is the annual discount/interest rate (decimal) and $N$ is the economic life in years.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Complete Economic Analysis of a Bulk Carrier

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A bulk carrier has principal dimensions $L = 150$ m, $B = 25$ m, $T = 9$ m, $C_B = 0.78$. The shipbuilding cost follows $C_B = 15{,}000 \cdot \Delta^{0.78}$. The annual operating cost (excluding fuel) is \$1.5 million. Service speed is 14 knots at an installed power of 12,000 kW. Fuel consumption is 180 g/kWh and fuel price is \$600/tonne. Calculate:
- (i) The displacement and shipbuilding cost.
- (ii) The daily fuel cost.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

If the ship makes 10 voyages per year at an average distance of 6,000 NM, calculate:
- (i) The total annual cost including annualized capital (use $i = 8\%$, $N = 20$ years, scrap = \$3 million).
- (ii) The Required Freight Rate.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Compute displacement (3 marks).**

Volume of displacement:

$$
\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B = 150 \cdot 25 \cdot 9 \cdot 0.78
$$

$$
\nabla = 26{,}325 \text{ m}^3
$$

Convert to mass displacement using seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³:

$$
\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho_{sw} = 26325 \cdot 1.025 = 26{,}983 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 2: Shipbuilding cost (2 marks).**

$$
C_B = 15000 \cdot (26983)^{0.78}
$$

Compute exponent:

$$
(26983)^{0.78} = e^{0.78 \cdot \ln(26983)} = e^{0.78 \cdot 10.204} = e^{7.959} \approx 2865
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_B = 15000 \cdot 2865 = \$42{,}975{,}000 \approx \$43 \text{ million}
$$

**Step 3: Daily fuel cost (2 marks).**

Daily fuel energy consumption:

$$
E_{day} = P \cdot SFC \cdot 24 = 12000 \cdot 180 \cdot 24 \text{ g/day}
$$

$$
E_{day} = 51{,}840{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 51.84 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

Daily fuel cost:

$$
C_{F,day} = 51.84 \cdot 600 = \$31{,}104 \text{/day}
$$

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Annualized capital cost (3 marks).**

CRF with $i = 0.08$, $N = 20$:

$$
CRF = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot (1.08)^{20}}{(1.08)^{20} - 1} = \dfrac{0.08 \cdot 4.661}{3.661} = 0.1019
$$

Annual capital:

$$
C_{cap,ann} = 43{,}000{,}000 \cdot 0.1019 = \$4{,}381{,}700 \text{/yr}
$$

Sinking fund:

$$
SF = \dfrac{0.08}{3.661} = 0.02185
$$

Scrap annual credit:

$$
S_{v,ann} = 3{,}000{,}000 \cdot 0.02185 = \$65{,}550 \text{/yr}
$$

Net annualized capital:

$$
C_{ann,cap,net} = 4{,}381{,}700 - 65{,}550 = \$4{,}316{,}150 \text{/yr}
$$

**Step 2: Voyage days per year (1 mark).**

Round-trip time:

$$
t_v = \dfrac{2 \cdot 6000}{24 \cdot 14} + t_p = \dfrac{12000}{336} + 5 = 35.71 + 5 = 40.71 \text{ days}
$$

Annual voyage days:

$$
t_{year} = 10 \cdot 40.71 = 407.1 \text{ days/yr}
$$

**Step 3: Annual fuel cost (1 mark).**

$$
C_{F,ann} = 31{,}104 \cdot 407.1 = \$12{,}662{,}438 \text{/yr}
$$

**Step 4: Total annual cost (1 mark).**

$$
C_{total} = 4{,}316{,}150 + 1{,}500{,}000 + 12{,}662{,}438 = \$18{,}478{,}588 \text{/yr}
$$

**Step 5: Required Freight Rate (1 mark).**

Deadweight estimate: $DWT \approx 0.85 \cdot \Delta \approx 0.85 \cdot 26983 \approx 22{,}936$ tonnes.

Annual cargo transported:

$$
\text{Cargo} = 22{,}936 \cdot 10 = 229{,}360 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

$$
RFR = \dfrac{18{,}478{,}588}{229{,}360} \approx \$80.57 \text{/tonne}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Question — Cost Optimization & Speed Selection

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A tanker operates at a service speed of 15 knots consuming 80 tonnes of fuel per day. For each **1-knot increase** in speed, fuel consumption rises by 8 tonnes/day, but **revenue per voyage** rises by \$20,000 (due to faster turnaround). Voyage time per round trip is 20 days at 15 knots, and the ship makes 15 voyages per year. The annual operating cost (excluding fuel) is \$2 million.

Find:
- (i) The annual fuel cost at 15 knots (fuel price = \$550/tonne).
- (ii) The annual revenue at 15 knots (assume base freight earnings = \$5 million at 15 knots).
- (iii) Whether increasing speed to 16 knots improves profitability.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

Explain the **concept of optimal economic speed** in ship design. With the help of a sketch, show how the total cost curve is U-shaped and how it intersects the revenue line to identify the break-even speed. State **three factors** that influence the optimal service speed.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution to Part (a):**

**(i) Annual fuel cost at 15 knots (2 marks):**

Fuel per day = 80 tonnes. Annual fuel:

$$
\text{Fuel}_{ann} = 80 \cdot 365 = 29{,}200 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

Cost:

$$
C_F = 29{,}200 \cdot 550 = \$16{,}060{,}000 \text{/yr}
$$

**(ii) Annual revenue at 15 knots (2 marks):**

Given base revenue = \$5,000,000/yr.

**(iii) Comparison at 16 knots (3 marks):**

At 16 knots: Fuel per day = $80 + 8 = 88$ tonnes.

Annual fuel:

$$
88 \cdot 365 = 32{,}120 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

Fuel cost:

$$
C_{F,16} = 32{,}120 \cdot 550 = \$17{,}666{,}000 \text{/yr}
$$

Extra fuel cost = $17{,}666{,}000 - 16{,}060{,}000 = \$1{,}606{,}000$ extra.

Additional revenue from speed = 15 voyages × \$20,000 = **\$300,000/year** extra.

Net effect: $\Delta \text{Profit} = 300{,}000 - 1{,}606{,}000 = -\$1{,}306{,}000$.

**Conclusion: Increasing speed to 16 knots REDUCES profitability** — do not increase speed.

---

**Solution to Part (b):** (7 marks — descriptive)

- **Definition (2 marks):** The optimal economic speed is the service speed at which the **total annual cost** (capital + operating + fuel) is minimized OR at which the **net profit** is maximized.
- **U-shaped cost curve (3 marks):** Total cost decreases up to a point (as fixed cost is spread over more voyages) then increases (as fuel grows with $V^3$). Revenue line intersects total cost at two speeds; the optimum is the speed between them giving maximum net profit.
- **Three influencing factors (2 marks):**
  1. **Fuel price** — higher fuel price shifts optimum to lower speeds.
  2. **Voyage distance** — longer voyages favor lower speeds.
  3. **Freight rate** — higher freight rewards faster turnaround.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where Students Lose Marks in Module 1 Economic Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert SFC units** — SFC is in **g/kWh**, but daily fuel must be in **tonnes**. Failing to divide by $10^6$ results in answers off by **1000×**.
> 2. **Using cargo capacity incorrectly** — The freight earnings use **DWT (cargo)**, NOT displacement $\Delta$. Mixing them up is a frequent error.
> 3. **Voyage time calculation** — Always include **port stay time** $t_p$. Pure sailing time $2D_v / (24V)$ alone is incomplete.
> 4. **CRF confusion** — CRF is used for **spreading capital cost**, while SF is used for **recovering scrap value**. Students often invert them.
> 5. **Wrong power exponent** — The cubic relationship $P \propto V^3$ is only for **bare ship resistance**. Including $\eta$ reduces the effective exponent to ~4.5–5 overall fuel cost vs. speed.
> 6. **No units in final answer** — Always state USD/tonne or USD/year.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** = Capital + OPEX + Voyage Costs (over $N$ years).
- ✅ **Shipbuilding cost** is modeled as $C_B = K \cdot \Delta^{a}$, with $a \approx 0.6$–$0.8$.
- ✅ **Annual OPEX** = Crew + Maintenance + Insurance + Stores + Admin (exclude fuel).
- ✅ **Fuel cost/day** = $P \times SFC \times 24 \times P_{fuel}$ (remember unit conversion g→t).
- ✅ **Transport Work** = DWT × Distance per voyage.
- ✅ **CRF** = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{N}}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$ converts CAPEX to annual.
- ✅ **SF** = $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$ annualizes scrap value.
- ✅ **RFR** = Total Annual Cost ÷ (DWT × Voyages/year) — **breakeven freight**.
- ✅ **Profit** = Revenue − Total Annual Cost.
- ✅ **Optimal speed** balances fuel cost ($V^3$ law) against revenue gains.
- ✅ **Block coefficient ↑** reduces steel for same DWT → lowers CAPEX.
- ✅ **Discount rate $i$** typically 5–10% in shipping industry.
- ✅ **Economic life $N$** = 20–25 years for tankers/bulkers.
- ✅ **Scrap value** = 5–10% of $C_B$.
- ✅ **Fuel price** dominates voyage cost (often >50% of total cost).
- ✅ **Power³ law:** Doubling speed requires 8× the power → 8× fuel.
- ✅ **Outfit weight** ≈ 15–25% of steel weight.
- ✅ **Crew cost** scales with ship size but plateaus above ~30 crew.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For Module 1 ESE questions, always begin by **listing the assumed data**, **computing displacement first**, then **capital cost**, then **annual cost**, and finally **RFR**. Examiners reward systematic presentation with full mark allocation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Economic Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economic Criteria in Ship Design** is the systematic engineering methodology used to determine the **principal dimensions and operating parameters** of a vessel such that the **total annual cost of ownership per unit of transport work** is minimized, while the ship remains technically feasible and compliant with statutory regulations.

In formal Naval Architecture terms, the design must satisfy:

$$\text{Objective} = \min \left[ \frac{C_{Annual}}{W_{Annual}} \right]$$

where:
- $C_{Annual}$ = Total Annual Cost (Capital Recovery + Operating Expenses) in USD/year
- $W_{Annual}$ = Annual Transport Work (Deadweight $\times$ Distance $\times$ Voyages) in ton-miles/year

The reciprocal of this ratio is the **Required Freight Rate (RFR)**:

$$\text{RFR} = \frac{C_{Annual}}{W_{Annual}} \quad \left[ \text{USD/ton-mile} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ship with the **lowest RFR** is the **optimum economic design**. RFR is the single most important yardstick in preliminary ship design economics.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Delivery Truck Problem"**
>
> Imagine you run a logistics company and must choose a truck. A **small truck** is cheap to buy but makes many trips (more driver hours, more fuel per ton delivered). A **huge truck** is expensive to buy and burns more fuel per trip but finishes the job faster. The **economic optimum** is the truck that delivers one ton of goods over one mile at the **lowest total cost** — balancing *purchase price* against *fuel and labor cost*.
>
> A ship is identical, except the "truck capacity" is **Deadweight (DWT)** and the "trip time" is governed by **Service Speed (V)**. The economic criteria is precisely the mathematics of finding that sweet spot.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Memorize These)

| Symbol | Metric | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DWT** | Deadweight Tonnage (cargo capacity) | tonnes |
| **V** | Service Speed | knots |
| **BHP** | Brake Horsepower (main engine) | kW or HP |
| **sfc** | Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh or g/HPh |
| **$C_B$** | Building Cost (new-building price) | USD |
| **$C_{op}$** | Annual Operating Cost | USD/year |
| **RFR** | Required Freight Rate | USD/ton-mile |
| **TW** | Transport Work | ton-miles/year |

---

### 1.4 Why Economic Criteria Comes First in Module 1

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, **economic sizing precedes technical sizing**. A naval architect does **not** start by drawing a hull form. The first question is always: *“What size ship, at what speed, makes commercial sense?”* Only then does the design proceed to hydrostatics, stability, and structural design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Ship Economics

The annual cost of a ship splits into two broad families:

> **Pillar 1 — Capital Cost (One-time, spread over the ship's life)**
> The mortgage/loan repayment on the building cost. Spread over the payback period, it becomes an **annual fixed cost**.

> **Pillar 2 — Operating Cost (Recurring, year after year)**
> Fuel, crew wages, lubricating oil, maintenance, insurance, port charges, and stores.

### 2.2 Cost Breakdown Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Step 1 — Annualize the Building Cost**

The capital cost of a new ship is converted into an annual equivalent using the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**:

$$\text{Annual Capital Cost} = C_B \times \text{CRF} = C_B \times \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

where $i$ is the discount/interest rate and $n$ is the economic life of the vessel (typically **20–25 years** for ocean-going ships).

**Step 2 — Express Building Cost as a Function of DWT**

A long-standing empirical rule in naval architecture is the **power-law relationship**:

$$C_B = k \cdot (\text{DWT})^x$$

where:
- $k$ = constant depending on ship type, year, and currency
- $x \approx \mathbf{0.6}$ for tankers and bulk carriers
- $x \approx \mathbf{0.7}$ for container ships and general cargo

> [!NOTE]
> This exponent is **less than 1**, meaning a 2× larger ship costs **less than 2× more** to build. This is called **economy of scale**.

**Step 3 — Express Fuel Cost (the dominant operating cost)**

Fuel cost is the heartbeat of ship economics:

$$C_{Fuel} = \text{sfc} \times \text{BHP} \times T_{voyage} \times P_{fuel} \times N_{voyages}$$

- $\text{sfc}$ = specific fuel consumption (g/kWh)
- $\text{BHP}$ = brake horsepower (function of speed and DWT)
- $T_{voyage}$ = time at sea per voyage (days)
- $P_{fuel}$ = price of fuel (USD/tonne)
- $N_{voyages}$ = number of voyages per year

**Step 4 — Relate BHP to DWT and Speed**

Empirically, for a given ship form:

$$\text{BHP} \propto \frac{(\text{DWT})^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{C_s}$$

where $C_s$ is a hull-form efficiency coefficient and $V$ is service speed in knots. This is the **cubic law of speed** — speed has a brutal effect on power.

**Step 5 — Relate Voyage Time to Speed**

$$T_{voyage} = \frac{D}{24 \cdot V}$$

where $D$ is the round-trip (or one-way) distance in nautical miles.

**Step 6 — Calculate Annual Transport Work**

$$\text{TW} = \text{DWT} \times D \times N_{voyages} = \text{DWT} \times \frac{365}{\frac{D}{24V} + T_{port}} \times D$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exam-ready formula bank**. Master these — KTU board questions on Module 1 typically test 2–3 of these directly.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $C_B = k \cdot (\text{DWT})^{0.6}$ | Building cost vs DWT | Capital cost |
| 2 | $\text{BHP} = \dfrac{c \cdot (\text{DWT})^{2/3} V^3}{\eta}$ | Power requirement | Fuel cost |
| 3 | $C_{Fuel} = \text{sfc} \cdot \text{BHP} \cdot t \cdot P_f$ | Fuel cost per voyage | Operating cost |
| 4 | $N_v = \dfrac{365}{D/(24V) + t_p}$ | Voyages per year | Revenue |
| 5 | $\text{TW} = \text{DWT} \cdot D \cdot N_v$ | Transport work | RFR denominator |
| 6 | $\text{RFR} = \dfrac{C_{Annual}}{\text{TW}}$ | Economic efficiency | **Primary metric** |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d(\text{RFR})}{d(\text{DWT})} = 0$ | Optimum DWT | Design condition |
| 8 | $\text{CRF} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Capital recovery | Annualize $C_B$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In the formula table, never write absolute values with a vertical bar inside the table. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{mid}$ syntax. Examiners often dock marks for broken LaTeX.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Use of Economic Criteria |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Shipyards** | Deciding which vessel size to quote for a tender |
| **Shipowners** | Choosing new-build tonnage vs. chartering |
| **Classification Societies** | Setting design loads from economic life |
| **Maritime Banks** | Evaluating loan viability of new vessels |
| **IMO / Policy Makers** | Assessing cost impact of emissions regulations (e.g., CII, EEXI) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the RFR Curve Has a Minimum

We will rigorously derive the **Economic Criterion Equation** — the condition for optimum DWT.

---

#### Step 1 — Define Total Annual Cost

$$C_{Annual} = \underbrace{C_B \cdot \text{CRF}}_{\text{Capital}} + \underbrace{C_{Fuel}}_{\text{Dominant operating cost}} + C_{Misc}$$

For the optimization, we group capital and operating costs into a single function of DWT and V:

$$C_{Annual} = A_1 (\text{DWT})^x + A_2 \, \frac{(\text{DWT})^{2/3} V^3}{V}$$

> The fuel term carries $V^2$ net (power $\propto V^3$ divided by speed $V$ gives $V^2$ for voyage fuel), and an inverse dependence on DWT (bigger ship = more capacity for fuel).

For a **fixed speed** $V$ optimization:

$$C_{Annual} = A_1 (\text{DWT})^x + A_2 (\text{DWT})^{-1/3} V^2$$

---

#### Step 2 — Define Transport Work

$$\text{TW} = \text{DWT} \cdot D \cdot N_v = \text{DWT} \cdot D \cdot \frac{365}{\frac{D}{24V} + t_p}$$

For long voyages where $t_p \ll D/24V$:

$$N_v \approx \frac{365 \cdot 24 V}{D} = \frac{8760 V}{D}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{TW} \approx \text{DWT} \cdot D \cdot \frac{8760 V}{D} = 8760 \cdot \text{DWT} \cdot V$$

So transport work is **linear in DWT and linear in V**.

---

#### Step 3 — Write RFR Explicitly

$$\text{RFR} = \frac{C_{Annual}}{\text{TW}} = \frac{A_1 (\text{DWT})^x + A_2 (\text{DWT})^{-1/3} V^2}{8760 \cdot \text{DWT} \cdot V}$$

$$\text{RFR} = \frac{A_1}{8760} (\text{DWT})^{x-1} V^{-1} + \frac{A_2}{8760} (\text{DWT})^{-4/3} V$$

> **Key Insight:** The first term (capital) **decreases** as DWT grows. The second term (fuel amortized over cargo) also **decreases** as DWT grows. But DWT appears with **opposite signs in the two exponents**, so a minimum exists.

---

#### Step 4 — Find the Minimum of RFR with Respect to DWT (V held constant)

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial (\text{RFR})}{\partial (\text{DWT})} = 0$$

$$\frac{A_1 (x-1)}{8760} (\text{DWT})^{x-2} V^{-1} - \frac{4A_2}{3 \cdot 8760} (\text{DWT})^{-7/3} V = 0$$

Multiply through by $8760 \cdot \text{DWT}^{2}$:

$$A_1 (x-1) \cdot \text{DWT}^{x} \cdot V^{-1} = \frac{4A_2}{3} \cdot \text{DWT}^{-1/3} \cdot V$$

Collecting terms:

$$A_1 (1-x) \cdot \text{DWT}^{x + 1/3} = \frac{4A_2}{3} \cdot V^2$$

Solving for DWT:

$$\boxed{\;(\text{DWT})_{opt} = \left[ \frac{4 A_2 V^2}{3 A_1 (1-x)} \right]^{\frac{1}{x + 1/3}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **principal economic design equation**. It tells the naval architect: *"For a chosen service speed $V$, the optimum deadweight that minimizes RFR is given by this power-law formula."*

---

#### Step 5 — Verify with a Numerical Example

**Given:**
- $A_1 = 1500$ USD/tonne/year, $x = 0.6$
- $A_2 = 8000$ USD/year constant
- $V = 15$ knots
- $x + 1/3 = 0.6 + 0.333 = 0.933$

**Step 5.1 — Substitute into the formula**

$$\text{DWT}_{opt} = \left[ \frac{4 \times 8000 \times 15^2}{3 \times 1500 \times (1 - 0.6)} \right]^{1/0.933}$$

**Step 5.2 — Compute numerator**

$$4 \times 8000 \times 225 = 7{,}200{,}000$$

**Step 5.3 — Compute denominator**

$$3 \times 1500 \times 0.4 = 1800$$

**Step 5.4 — Compute ratio**

$$\frac{7{,}200{,}000}{1800} = 4000$$

**Step 5.5 — Apply exponent**

$$\text{DWT}_{opt} = (4000)^{1.0718} \approx 4795 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A ship of approximately **4800 DWT** at **15 knots** minimizes the required freight rate for the assumed cost regime. Any ship larger or smaller at the same speed will have a higher RFR.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameter Changes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **exam gold** — KTU frequently asks, *"What happens to the optimum DWT if the fuel price doubles?"*

| Parameter Change | Effect on $(\text{DWT})_{opt}$ | Effect on Optimum Speed $V$ | Physical Intuition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fuel price $\uparrow$ | Decreases (smaller ship) | Decreases (slow steaming) | Big engines become unaffordable |
| Cargo rate $\uparrow$ | Increases (bigger ship) | Increases | More revenue per voyage justifies more capacity |
| Interest rate $i$ $\uparrow$ | Decreases | Decreases | Capital cost dominates; build smaller |
| Cargo density $\uparrow$ | Increases | Marginal | Can pack more tonnes per cubic meter |
| Sea margin $\uparrow$ | Increases | Decreases | More hull volume, more resistance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Economic Design Loop

This block diagram traces the **iterative procedure** a naval architect follows when applying economic criteria in preliminary ship design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Trade Route and Cargo Type Defined] --> B[Set Trial Service Speed V]
    B --> C[Compute Required BHP from Resistance Curves]
    C --> D[Estimate Building Cost CB = k * DWT^0.6]
    D --> E[Compute Annual Capital Cost CB * CRF]
    E --> F[Compute Annual Fuel Cost sfc * BHP * Voyages * Price]
    F --> G[Compute Annual Transport Work TW = DWT * D * Nv]
    G --> H[Calculate RFR = C_Annual / TW]
    H --> I{Is RFR at Minimum?}
    I -- No --> J[Adjust DWT Up or Down]
    J --> B
    I -- Yes --> K[Optimum DWT and V Identified]
    K --> L[Proceed to Technical Design Phase]
    L --> M[End: Principal Dimensions Fixed]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cost-Composition Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAP[CAPITAL COST BLOCK]
        CB[Building Cost CB]
        CRF[Capital Recovery Factor CRF]
        ACC[Annual Capital Cost]
        CB --> ACC
        CRF --> ACC
    end
    subgraph OP[OPERATING COST BLOCK]
        CF[Fuel Cost]
        CM[Manning and Crew Wages]
        CMNT[Maintenance]
        CI[Insurance]
        CP[Port Charges]
        CS[Stores and Lub Oil]
        AOC[Annual Operating Cost]
        CF --> AOC
        CM --> AOC
        CMNT --> AOC
        CI --> AOC
        CP --> AOC
        CS --> AOC
    end
    ACC --> TOT[Total Annual Cost]
    AOC --> TOT
    TOT --> RFR[Required Freight Rate RFR]
    TW[Annual Transport Work] --> RFR
    RFR --> DEC{Decision: Minimum RFR?}
    DEC -- Yes --> OPT[OPTIMUM DESIGN]
    DEC -- No --> LOOP[Iterate DWT and V]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Graph: RFR vs DWT — The Classic U-Curve

The shape of the RFR vs DWT curve is the most important visual in ship economics. Below is a schematic block-graph illustrating the **family of U-curves** for different speeds.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GRAPH[RFR vs Deadweight Family of Curves]
        A1[Low Speed V1: 12 knots] --> P1[Shallow U, minimum at small DWT]
        A2[Medium Speed V2: 15 knots] --> P2[Deep U, minimum at medium DWT]
        A3[High Speed V3: 20 knots] --> P3[Steep U, minimum at large DWT]
        P1 --> ENV[Envelope Curve: Optimum Locus]
        P2 --> ENV
        P3 --> ENV
        ENV --> STAR[Star of Optimum Designs]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each curve corresponds to a fixed service speed. The envelope touching all the curve minima is the **"locus of optimum designs"** — the path a shipowner follows when scaling up the fleet.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Stakeholder Decision Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OWN as Shipowner
    participant NA as Naval Architect
    participant YD as Shipyard
    participant BANK as Financing Bank
    OWN->>NA: Specify trade route and cargo
    NA->>NA: Compute RFR(DWT, V) family
    NA->>OWN: Recommend optimum DWT and V
    OWN->>BANK: Submit project for loan
    BANK->>BANK: Check RFR vs market freight rate
    BANK-->>OWN: Approve / reject financing
    OWN->>YD: Place order
    YD-->>OWN: Deliver vessel
    OWN->>NA: Monitor actual vs predicted RFR
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **outcome-based learning**. Diagram 4.4 directly maps to the Course Outcome *“CO1: Apply economic principles to size a ship”*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **Required Freight Rate (RFR)**. Why is it considered the primary economic yardstick in ship design?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> RFR is the freight rate that a ship must earn per ton-mile of cargo to exactly cover all its annual costs, both capital and operating. Mathematically, $\text{RFR} = C_{Annual} / \text{TW}$.
> It is the primary yardstick because it combines *cost*, *capacity*, and *productivity* into a single number that can be directly compared with the prevailing market freight rate. The design with the lowest RFR is the most economically efficient. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** State the empirical relationship between **building cost** and **deadweight tonnage**. What does the exponent signify?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> $C_B = k \cdot (\text{DWT})^x$ where $x \approx 0.6$ for tankers and bulk carriers.
> The exponent $x < 1$ signifies **economy of scale**: doubling the deadweight increases the building cost by only $2^{0.6} \approx 1.52$ times, not 2 times. This non-linear scaling is the foundation of larger-ship economics. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice]**
> Choose **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the various cost components that contribute to the total annual cost of a ship.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a bulk carrier, the following data are given:
- Building cost coefficient $k = 1200$ USD/tonne, exponent $x = 0.6$
- Annual fuel cost: $C_F = 60{,}000 \cdot (\text{DWT})^{-1/3} \cdot V^2$ USD
- Annual transport work: $\text{TW} = 8760 \cdot \text{DWT} \cdot V$ ton-miles
- Interest rate $i = 8\%$, ship life $n = 20$ years

For a design speed of $V = 14$ knots, determine the **optimum DWT** that minimizes the Required Freight Rate.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram of Cost Components (7 marks)**

[Use Section 4.2 Mermaid block diagram; in written exam, redraw as labeled boxes with arrows]

Key components and their typical share:
- Building cost (annualized) — **50–60%**
- Fuel cost — **20–30%**
- Manning — **8–10%**
- Maintenance, insurance, port charges, stores — **5–10%**

Each block must be **explicitly named** and **connected with arrows** to the total annual cost box. (7 marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for description, 2 for typical percentages)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Optimization (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**

$$\text{CRF} = \frac{0.08 (1.08)^{20}}{(1.08)^{20} - 1} = \frac{0.08 \times 4.661}{4.661 - 1} = \frac{0.3729}{3.661} = 0.1019$$

**Step 2: Annual Capital Cost**

$$C_{Cap} = 0.1019 \times 1200 \times (\text{DWT})^{0.6} = 122.2 \cdot (\text{DWT})^{0.6}$$

**Step 3: RFR Expression**

$$\text{RFR} = \frac{122.2 \cdot (\text{DWT})^{0.6} + 60{,}000 \cdot (\text{DWT})^{-1/3} \cdot 14^2}{8760 \cdot \text{DWT} \cdot 14}$$

$$\text{RFR} = \frac{122.2 \cdot (\text{DWT})^{0.6} + 11{,}760{,}000 \cdot (\text{DWT})^{-1/3}}{122{,}640 \cdot \text{DWT}}$$

**Step 4: Differentiate with respect to DWT and set to zero**

$$\frac{d(\text{RFR})}{d(\text{DWT})} = 0$$

After simplification (using the derivation in Section 3.1):

$$0.1019 \times 1200 \times 0.6 \cdot (\text{DWT})^{1.6} \cdot 122{,}640 = \frac{4}{3} \times 11{,}760{,}000$$

**Step 5: Solve numerically**

$$\text{DWT}_{opt} \approx 5400 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Final Answer:** The optimum deadweight at 14 knots is approximately **5400 tonnes**. (7 marks: 2 for CRF, 2 for RFR expression, 2 for differentiation, 1 for numerical answer)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the **optimum deadweight** of a ship from the Required Freight Rate minimization condition. State clearly all assumptions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A shipping company operates a fleet of 15-knot tankers. The market fuel price rises by 50%. Using the economic design equation, qualitatively explain the impact on:
1. Optimum DWT
2. Optimum service speed
3. Required Freight Rate

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

Follow the **5-step derivation in Section 3.1** above. Key assumptions to state:
- Fixed trade route (constant distance $D$)
- Cubic law of resistance: $\text{BHP} \propto V^3$
- Power law of building cost: $C_B \propto (\text{DWT})^x$ with $x \approx 0.6$
- Voyage time dominates port time
- Discount rate $i$ and ship life $n$ are constants

Final boxed result: $\text{DWT}_{opt} = \left[ \dfrac{4 A_2 V^2}{3 A_1 (1-x)} \right]^{1/(x + 1/3)}$ (7 marks: 1 for assumptions, 4 for derivation, 2 for final expression)

---

**Part (b) — Qualitative Analysis (7 marks)**

1. **Optimum DWT** → **Decreases** (smaller ships are favored because fuel scales with DWT^(2/3) but capital scales with DWT^0.6, and now fuel cost is amplified). Expect ~10–15% reduction.

2. **Optimum Speed** → **Decreases** (slow steaming). Cubic power law means a 10% speed drop yields ~27% fuel saving. New optimum speed is lower.

3. **Required Freight Rate** → **Increases** (every tonne-mile now costs more to transport, even at the new optimum). The U-curve shifts **upward** but the minimum is **flatter**.

(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for integration)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting CRF**: Many students compute the building cost but forget to **annualize it** using the Capital Recovery Factor. The total annual cost requires the **mortgage equivalent**, not the raw building cost. **−3 marks common.**
> 2. **Confusing DWT with Displacement**: In the formulas, **DWT = Deadweight** (cargo + fuel + stores + crew). It is **not** the displacement $\Delta$. Examiners will mark this wrong. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **Ignoring Units in RFR**: RFR must be in **USD/ton-mile** (or similar consistent units). Showing only the formula without unit consistency is a partial-credit loss. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Wrong Exponent in Building Cost Law**: Writing $C_B \propto \text{DWT}^1$ (linear) is a common conceptual error. The correct exponent is **0.6**. **−2 marks.**
> 5. **No Block Diagram in Part (a)**: KTU board explicitly asks for "neat block diagram". Hand-drawn boxes with arrows are mandatory, not optional. **−2 marks if missing.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Exam**

- [x] **RFR is the king metric** — minimum RFR = optimum ship.
- [x] **Building cost law:** $C_B = k \cdot (\text{DWT})^{0.6}$ (tankers/bulk), $0.7$ (containers).
- [x] **Power law:** $\text{BHP} \propto \text{DWT}^{2/3} \cdot V^3$ (cubic in speed!).
- [x] **Fuel cost dominates** operating cost (~20–30% of annual cost).
- [x] **Annualize capital cost** using CRF; never quote raw $C_B$ as annual cost.
- [x] **Transport work** is linear in DWT and linear in speed.
- [x] **Optimum DWT equation:** $\text{DWT}_{opt} = \left[ \dfrac{4 A_2 V^2}{3 A_1 (1-x)} \right]^{1/(x+1/3)}$ — memorize the form.
- [x] **Slow steaming** lowers RFR when fuel prices are high.
- [x] **Economy of scale:** $x < 1 \Rightarrow$ bigger ships are cost-efficient.
- [x] **Number of voyages:** $N_v = 8760 V / D$ for long voyages.
- [x] **Final RFR formula:** $\text{RFR} = C_{Annual} / (8760 \cdot \text{DWT} \cdot V)$.
- [x] **Block diagram of cost components is mandatory** in any 7-mark cost question.
- [x] **DWT $\neq$ Displacement** — never confuse the two.
- [x] **KTU module weightage:** Module 1 carries **~15–20%** of ESE marks; expect one full 14-mark question on this topic.

---

> **End of Notes — Economic Criteria (Module 1, PBNST504)**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education Framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shipbuilding Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shipbuilding Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shipbuilding Cost** is defined as the total monetary expenditure incurred in the design, material procurement, fabrication, assembly, outfitting, launching, and commissioning of a sea-going vessel, expressed as the aggregate of **Material Cost ($C_M$)**, **Labor Cost ($C_L$)**, and **Overhead/Indirect Cost ($C_O$)**, prior to the addition of profit margin and escalation clauses.

Mathematically, the **Newbuilding Contract Price (C)** is expressed as:

$$C = C_M + C_L + C_O + P$$

where **P** is the builder's profit margin, typically **8–15%** of the combined direct and indirect cost base.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **building a custom house** in Kerala. The total bill you pay is not just bricks and cement — it includes:

| Real-World House Building | Equivalent Shipbuilding Concept |
|---|---|
| Cement, steel rods, bricks | Hull steel plates, sections |
| Masons, plumbers, electricians | Welders, fitters, outfitters |
| Architect's fee, permits | Design/engineering office cost |
| Site office, transport, utilities | Yard overheads, facilities |
| Builder's margin | Shipyard profit |

In the **ship domain**, instead of square feet, we measure cost against **tonnes of steel (Lightweight)** or **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)**. A bulk carrier of ~80,000 DWT may cost around **USD 30–35 million** newbuild, while an LNG carrier can exceed **USD 200 million**.

---

### 1.3 Standard Cost Metrics Used Globally

> [!NOTE]
> Three key yardsticks are used in shipping economics:
> - **Cost per Lightweight Ton (LWT)** — measures fabrication efficiency
> - **Cost per Deadweight Ton (DWT)** — measures commercial value delivered
> - **Cost per Compensated Gross Ton (CGT)** — OECD standard for fair-trade comparison

These metrics are essential for **comparative shipbuilding analysis** between yards in Korea, China, Japan, and Europe.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cost Component Breakdown (Cost Breakdown Structure — CBS)

The shipbuilding cost is partitioned into **six engineering work packages**:

1. **Hull Steel Cost ($C_{HS}$)** — Plates, profiles, castings, forgings  
   - Typically **20–25%** of newbuild price
2. **Hull Outfit Cost ($C_{HO}$)** — Hatch covers, masts, funnels, doors, windows  
   - Approximately **8–12%**
3. **Machinery Cost ($C_{Mach}$)** — Main engine, generators, propulsion plant  
   - Approximately **20–28%** (highest for tankers due to pumping systems)
4. **Electrical Cost ($C_{Elec}$)** — Cables, switchboards, navigation electronics  
   - Approximately **4–7%**
5. **Labor Cost ($C_L$)** — Direct wages + indirect wages  
   - Approximately **15–25%** (varies heavily by geography)
6. **Overhead Cost ($C_O$)** — Design, administration, utilities, insurance  
   - Approximately **8–12%**

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these equations — they appear regularly in KTU ESE numericals.

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Typical Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $C = C_M + C_L + C_O + P$ | Total newbuild contract price | USD million |
| $C_{DWT} = \dfrac{C}{\Delta_{DWT}}$ | Specific cost per deadweight ton | USD/ton |
| $C_{LWT} = \dfrac{C}{\Delta_{LWT}}$ | Specific cost per lightweight ton | USD/ton |
| $C_{CGT} = \dfrac{C}{CGT}$ | OECD standard cost metric | USD/CGT |
| $C_L = N \times H \times W$ | Labor cost = workers × hours × wage rate | USD |
| $C_O = k_o \cdot (C_M + C_L)$ | Overhead as fraction of direct cost | USD, $k_o = 0.10$–$0.20$ |
| $C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}$ | Learning curve / progress curve equation | USD, dimensionless $n$ |

---

### 2.3 The Learning Curve (Progress Function)

In shipbuilding, as **cumulative output (n)** doubles, the **man-hours required per ship** decrease by a fixed percentage $b$. This is formalized as:

$$C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}$$

where:
- $C_n$ = cost (or man-hours) for the $n^{th}$ ship
- $C_1$ = cost of the first ship
- $b$ = learning index (typically **0.85–0.95** in series shipbuilding)
- $n$ = ship number in the series

> [!NOTE]
> A learning index $b = 0.85$ means **15% reduction** in labor hours every time cumulative production doubles.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shipbuilding cost estimation is the **financial backbone** of:
- **Shipping company investment decisions** (newbuild vs. second-hand)
- **Shipyard tender pricing** (competitive bidding for contracts)
- **Government policy** (subsidies, OECD trade monitoring via CGT)
- **Loan syndication** (banks financing 60–80% of vessel cost)
- **Ship design optimization** (trade-off studies between hull form and outfit weight)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Specific Cost per Deadweight Ton

The specific cost is a normalised indicator allowing comparison between vessels of different sizes.

**Step 1:** Start with the total newbuilding price.

$$C_{total} = C_M + C_L + C_O + P$$

**Step 2:** Identify the deadweight tonnage of the vessel at design draft.

$$\Delta_{DWT} = \Delta_{Displacement} - \Delta_{Lightweight}$$

**Step 3:** Define the cost per deadweight ton as the ratio of total cost to cargo-carrying capacity.

$$C_{DWT} = \frac{C_{total}}{\Delta_{DWT}}$$

**Step 4:** For a VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier), let $C_{total} = \$100 \text{ million}$ and $\Delta_{DWT} = 300{,}000$ tonnes.

$$C_{DWT} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{300 \times 10^3} = 333.33 \text{ USD/ton}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Learning Curve Equation

**Step 1:** Define cumulative production doubling. After producing 1 ship, 2 ships, 4 ships, 8 ships… the production count $n$ represents the *ship number* in a series.

**Step 2:** Assume man-hours per ship follow an exponential decay with experience.

$$H_n = H_1 \cdot n^{-b}$$

where $H_n$ is the man-hours for the $n^{th}$ ship.

**Step 3:** Take logarithms to linearize the relation.

$$\log H_n = \log H_1 - b \cdot \log n$$

This is a **straight line** on a log-log plot with slope $-b$ and intercept $\log H_1$.

**Step 4:** Numerical example — let $H_1 = 1{,}000{,}000$ man-hours, $b = 0.90$, and $n = 4$ (fourth ship).

$$H_4 = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 4^{-0.90}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the exponent.

$$4^{-0.90} = e^{-0.90 \cdot \ln 4} = e^{-0.90 \times 1.3863} = e^{-1.2477} = 0.2872$$

**Step 6:** Final result.

$$H_4 = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.2872 = 287{,}200 \text{ man-hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This represents a **~71.3% reduction** in labor hours from ship #1 to ship #4 — typical of Japanese/Korean series production.

---

### 3.3 Overhead Cost Calculation

**Step 1:** Overhead is generally computed as a fraction of direct cost (material + labor).

$$C_O = k_o \cdot (C_M + C_L)$$

**Step 2:** Total direct + indirect cost becomes.

$$C_{D+I} = (C_M + C_L)(1 + k_o)$$

**Step 3:** Add profit margin $k_p$ (typically 10%).

$$C = C_{D+I} \cdot (1 + k_p) = (C_M + C_L)(1 + k_o)(1 + k_p)$$

**Step 4:** Numerical example — $C_M = 20$ M USD, $C_L = 15$ M USD, $k_o = 0.12$, $k_p = 0.10$.

$$C = (20 + 15)(1.12)(1.10) = 35 \times 1.232 = 43.12 \text{ M USD}$$

---

### 3.4 Cost per CGT (Compensated Gross Ton) — OECD Method

**Step 1:** OECD defines a coefficient for each ship type $i$ and each size bracket.

$$CGT = GT \times C_i$$

where $C_i$ is the compensation factor (e.g., 1.0 for small bulk carriers, up to ~2.5 for LNG carriers).

**Step 2:** The specific newbuilding price in USD per CGT is then.

$$C_{CGT} = \frac{C_{total}}{CGT}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The OECD uses **CGT-based market price benchmarks** to detect unfair subsidization. Korea and China are monitored closely under this framework.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
