<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define the term **"Shipbuilding Cost"** and list its **four major components**.

**Model Answer:**

Shipbuilding cost is the total monetary expenditure required to construct a sea-going vessel from design through delivery. Its four major components are:

1. **Material Cost ($C_M$)** — Hull steel, outfit, machinery, electrical
2. **Labor Cost ($C_L$)** — Direct and indirect wages
3. **Overhead Cost ($C_O$)** — Design, administration, utilities
4. **Profit Margin (P)** — Builder's commercial return

> *(3 marks: definition + 4 components listed correctly)*

---

**Q2.** What is meant by **"Cost per Compensated Gross Ton (CGT)"**? Why is it used?

**Model Answer:**

CGT is the OECD-standardized cost metric computed by multiplying the Gross Tonnage (GT) of a vessel by a compensation factor $C_i$ that reflects the **technical complexity** of the ship type.

$$CGT = GT \times C_i$$

It is used because **simple GT** understates the construction effort for complex vessels (LNG, cruise, container ships). CGT provides a **fair trade benchmark** for comparing shipbuilding prices across nations and is used by the **OECD Working Party on Shipbuilding (WP6)** to monitor subsidies.

> *(3 marks: definition + formula + OECD purpose)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Style]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **total shipbuilding contract price** in terms of material cost, labor cost, overhead ratio, and profit margin. Compute the newbuilding price for a vessel with $C_M = \$25$ M, $C_L = \$18$ M, $k_o = 0.15$, $k_p = 0.10$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **learning curve** in shipbuilding. A shipyard produces a series of identical bulk carriers. The first ship required **500,000 man-hours**. If the learning index is **0.90**, estimate the man-hours required for the **8th ship**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Contract Price Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the basic components.

$$C_{direct} = C_M + C_L$$

**Step 2:** Apply overhead ratio $k_o$ to direct cost.

$$C_O = k_o (C_M + C_L)$$

**Step 3:** Add overhead to direct cost.

$$C_{D+O} = (C_M + C_L)(1 + k_o)$$

**Step 4:** Apply profit margin $k_p$.

$$C = (C_M + C_L)(1 + k_o)(1 + k_p)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute numerical values.

$$C = (25 + 18)(1.15)(1.10) = 43 \times 1.265 = 54.395 \text{ M USD}$$

> **Answer: Newbuilding price ≈ USD 54.40 million** ✅

---

#### Part (b) — Learning Curve Calculation

**Step 1:** Recall the progress function.

$$H_n = H_1 \cdot n^{-b}$$

**Step 2:** Identify parameters: $H_1 = 500{,}000$ man-hours, $n = 8$, $b = 0.90$.

**Step 3:** Compute the exponent.

$$8^{-0.90} = e^{-0.90 \cdot \ln 8} = e^{-0.90 \times 2.0794} = e^{-1.8715} = 0.1539$$

**Step 4:** Final calculation.

$$H_8 = 500{,}000 \times 0.1539 = 76{,}950 \text{ man-hours}$$

> **Answer: Man-hours for 8th ship ≈ 76,950 hours (≈85% reduction)** ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS)** of a merchant ship with typical percentage ranges. Discuss why **machinery cost** is highest for **LNG carriers** and **product tankers**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Panamax bulk carrier has a **deadweight of 75,000 tonnes** and a **lightweight of 12,000 tonnes**. The newbuilding price is **USD 38 million**. Compute the **cost per DWT** and **cost per LWT**. State which metric is more appropriate for **shipyard productivity benchmarking**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Cost Breakdown Structure

The CBS partitions newbuild price as follows:

| **Component** | **Range (% of total)** |
|---|---|
| Hull Steel | 20–25% |
| Hull Outfit | 8–12% |
| Machinery | 20–28% |
| Electrical | 4–7% |
| Labor | 15–25% |
| Overhead + Profit | 8–12% |

**Why machinery dominates LNG/product tankers:**
- LNG carriers require **reliquefaction plants, cryogenic cargo tanks, BOG handling systems** and specialized **dual-fuel engines** (ME-GI, X-DF).
- Product tankers require **extensive pumping/piping systems**, **inert gas plants**, **coating systems** (epoxy for clean petroleum product carriage), and **IG/IGS** for safety.
- These add significantly to machinery and outfit weight, pushing the machinery share to the **upper end of the 20–28% band** (sometimes exceeding **35%** for sophisticated vessels).

---

#### Part (b) — Specific Cost Calculations

**Step 1:** Cost per deadweight ton.

$$C_{DWT} = \frac{38 \times 10^6}{75 \times 10^3} = 506.67 \text{ USD/ton}$$

**Step 2:** Cost per lightweight ton.

$$C_{LWT} = \frac{38 \times 10^6}{12 \times 10^3} = 3166.67 \text{ USD/ton}$$

**Step 3:** Appropriate metric for **shipyard productivity benchmarking**.

> **Cost per LWT** is more appropriate for yard productivity benchmarking because:
> - LWT is directly linked to **steel fabrication, welding hours, and block erection effort**.
> - DWT varies with **cargo density** and design draft — not a fair measure of construction complexity.
> - Korean and Japanese yards benchmark **man-hours per LWT** as the true productivity index.

> **Answers: $C_{DWT} \approx 507$ USD/ton, $C_{LWT} \approx 3167$ USD/ton** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Confusing GT vs CGT**: GT is gross tonnage; CGT applies a complexity multiplier. Don't interchange them.
> - **Forgetting the $1 + k_p$ factor**: Many students compute $C = (C_M + C_L)(1 + k_o)$ and forget profit. You lose 2 marks easily.
> - **Wrong learning curve base**: Use $n$ as the *ship number*, not doubled count, unless the formula is explicitly expressed that way.
> - **Mixing DWT and LWT**: DWT = cargo + stores + crew; LWT = empty ship weight. They are not interchangeable.
> - **Units inconsistency**: Always express final cost in consistent USD units; KTU penalises sloppy unit conversions.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Shipbuilding Cost** = Material + Labor + Overhead + Profit
- ✅ The **Cost Breakdown Structure** follows the engineering work package structure
- ✅ **Cost per DWT** measures commercial value; **Cost per LWT** measures yard productivity
- ✅ **CGT (Compensated Gross Ton)** is the OECD standard for international comparison
- ✅ **Learning curve**: $H_n = H_1 \cdot n^{-b}$, with $b \approx 0.85$–$0.95$
- ✅ **Overhead** is usually 10–20% of (Material + Labor)
- ✅ **Profit margin** is typically 8–15%
- ✅ **Economies of scale**: cost per ton decreases with ship size
- ✅ **LNG carriers** and **tankers** have highest machinery share due to cryogenic/pumping systems
- ✅ Master the four formulas: $C = (C_M + C_L)(1 + k_o)(1 + k_p)$, $C_{DWT}$, $C_{LWT}$, $H_n = H_1 n^{-b}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operating Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Operating Cost** in ship design economics refers to the total recurring expenditure incurred by a shipowner to keep a vessel in operational service over a defined period (typically expressed per day, per annum, or per nautical ton-mile). It encompasses all voyage-related and vessel-related disbursements necessary for the ship to perform its intended commercial function, **excluding** capital cost recovery (depreciation) and financing charges, which are treated separately under Total Cost of Ownership.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Operating Cost (also termed **Daily Running Cost** or **VOC – Voyage Operating Cost** when voyage-specific) is one of the three pillars of ship economic analysis, alongside **Capital Cost** and **Voyage Cost**.

$$C_{Operating} = C_{Manning} + C_{Stores} + C_{Lub.Oil} + C_{M\&R} + C_{Insurance} + C_{Administration}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Operating cost is a fundamental input in the **Required Freight Rate (RFR)** and **Net Present Value (NPV)** calculations for ship acquisition decisions. The cost components are standardized by organizations like **IMO**, **OECD**, and classification societies for benchmarking.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a **commercial taxi car** that you rent out daily:
- **Manning Cost** = the salary you pay the driver
- **Stores Cost** = food, water, fuel for the driver
- **Lubricating Oil Cost** = engine oil top-ups between services
- **Maintenance & Repair (M&R)** = garage servicing and part replacements
- **Insurance** = the third-party and comprehensive car insurance
- **Administration** = the office rent, phone bills, accountant's fees

A ship is essentially a **"floating factory"** that operates 24/7/365. The Operating Cost is the recurring price tag of keeping that factory running smoothly. A bulk carrier burning cheap fuel has low operating cost per day, while a cruise ship with 1,000+ crew members has very high operating cost per day.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Capital Cost* is the price you paid to **buy** the ship (one-time, like the taxi's purchase price). *Operating Cost* is the price you pay **every day** to **use** the ship. The two are kept separate because shipowners use different financial models to manage them.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized units are used universally in ship economics:

| Metric Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **$/day** | Cost per operating day | US Dollars per day |
| **$/annum** | Annual operating cost | US Dollars per year |
| **$/DWT** | Cost per Deadweight Tonne | $/tonne |
| **¢/tonne-mile** | Cost per cargo ton-mile | US cents |
| **OPEX** | Operational Expenditure (industry jargon) | $/year |

The conversion factor universally used in KTU examinations is:

$$1 \text{ year} = 350 \text{ operating days}$$

This **350 days/year** assumption accounts for approximately **15 days** of annual port stay, dry-docking, and idle period — a standard OECD/UNCTAD convention.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Operating Cost — Detailed Breakdown

Operating cost is conventionally divided into **six** sub-components. Each has its own deterministic driver variables that the naval architect must quantify during preliminary design.

#### 2.1.1 Manning Cost (Crew Cost) — $C_{Manning}$

The single largest operating cost for most merchant vessels. It includes:
- **Basic wages** of all crew (Master, Officers, Ratings, Cadets)
- **Overtime and leave pay**
- **Social security contributions** (employer's share)
- **Travel to/from ship** (joining/leave expenses)
- **Uniform and victualling allowance**

**Driver Variables:**
- Crew Complement (CR) — total number of seafarers
- Flag State (nationality of registration — affects minimum wage via ILO conventions)
- Ship type (tankers ~22 crew, bulk carriers ~22, container ships ~25, cruise ships 800–1500)

$$C_{Manning} = CR \times \bar{W}_{annual}$$

Where $\bar{W}_{annual}$ = average annual cost per crew member (including all benefits).

#### 2.1.2 Stores Cost — $C_{Stores}$

Daily consumables required for the crew and vessel operation:
- **Provisions** (food, fresh water, bonded stores)
- **Engine stores** (filters, gaskets, small spares)
- **Deck stores** (ropes, paint, cleaning materials)
- **Cabin stores** (linen, toiletries)

Typical KTU benchmark: **$15–$30 per crew member per day** (varies with ship type and route).

#### 2.1.3 Lubricating Oil Cost — $C_{Lub.Oil}$

Cost of cylinder oil, system oil, and turbocharger oil consumed by the main engine and auxiliary engines.

The main equation governing lubricating oil consumption is:

$$C_{Lub.Oil} = Q_{LO} \times P_{LO} \times 350 \text{ days}$$

Where:
- $Q_{LO}$ = daily lubricating oil consumption (litres/day) — typically **1.5–3.0 g/kWh** for main engine cylinder oil
- $P_{LO}$ = price of lubricating oil (USD/litre) — typically **$2.5–$4.0/litre** for marine cylinder oil

#### 2.1.4 Maintenance and Repair (M&R) Cost — $C_{M\&R}$

This is the **most volatile** operating cost component. It includes:
- **Routine maintenance** (preventive servicing, class surveys)
- **Spares and consumables**
- **Dry-docking** (every 5 years — major cost spike)
- **Hull and machinery repair** (corrosion, wear)

The empirical KTU/industry formula widely used:

$$C_{M\&R,\,annual} = K_{M\&R} \times NRT \times P_{sh}$$

Where:
- $K_{M\&R}$ = empirical coefficient (typically **0.005–0.015** for ocean-going vessels)
- $NRT$ = Net Registered Tonnage (tonnes)
- $P_{sh}$ = ship newbuilding price ($/tonne) — usually a fraction (~0.3–0.6) of the original newbuilding price

#### 2.1.5 Insurance Cost — $C_{Insurance}$

- **Hull and Machinery (H&M) Insurance** — covers physical damage to the ship
- **Protection & Indemnity (P&I) Insurance** — covers third-party liability, crew injury, pollution
- **War Risk Insurance** — additional premium for high-risk zones

Empirical KTU relation:

$$C_{Insurance} = (0.7\% \text{ to } 1.5\%) \times C_{NB}$$

Where $C_{NB}$ = newbuilding cost of the ship, and the percentage varies with ship age, type, and trading area.

#### 2.1.6 Administration Cost — $C_{Administration}$

Overhead costs of the shipowner's office, allocated to the vessel:
- Management fees
- Shore-based staff salaries (proportional allocation)
- Communication, audit, legal fees
- Flag state fees, classification society fees

Typically estimated as:

$$C_{Admin} = 10\% \text{ to } 20\% \text{ of } (C_{Manning} + C_{M\&R})$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU 2024 examinations.** All units must be consistent; **always convert** to US dollars per day before final reporting.

| # | Component | Formula | Key Variables | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Manning Cost | $C_M = CR \times \bar{W}$ | CR = crew number; $\bar{W}$ = annual wage/cost per crew | $CR$ = 20–25 (merchant); $\bar{W}$ = \$30,000–\$60,000/yr |
| 2 | Stores Cost | $C_S = 350 \times CR \times p_s$ | $p_s$ = daily stores per crew | $p_s$ = \$15–\$30/day |
| 3 | Lub. Oil Cost | $C_{LO} = 350 \times Q_{LO} \times P_{LO}$ | $Q_{LO}$ in L/day; $P_{LO}$ in \$/L | $Q_{LO}$ = 100–300 L/day; $P_{LO}$ = \$2.5–\$4.0/L |
| 4 | M&R Cost | $C_{M\&R} = K_{M\&R} \times NRT \times P_{sh}$ | $K_{M\&R}$ = 0.005–0.015 | Empirical |
| 5 | Insurance Cost | $C_{Ins} = (0.007 \text{ to } 0.015) \times C_{NB}$ | $C_{NB}$ = newbuilding price | 0.7%–1.5% of $C_{NB}$ |
| 6 | Administration | $C_{Admin} = (0.1 \text{ to } 0.2) \times (C_M + C_{M\&R})$ | — | 10%–20% overhead |
| 7 | **Total OPEX** | $C_{OPEX} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} C_i$ | Sum of all six | Quoted in \$/day |
| 8 | Per DWT OPEX | $C_{OPEX,\,DWT} = C_{OPEX,\,annual} / DWT$ | Useful for fleet comparison | \$/DWT/yr |
| 9 | Operating Days | $t_{op} = 350 \text{ days/year}$ | OECD/UNCTAD standard | Universal |

**Critical:** Avoid using vertical pipes inside table cells; absolute value $\vert x \vert$ and conditional $\mid$ notations are safe.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Operating cost analysis is the **lifeblood of maritime commerce**:
- **Shipowners** use it to decide whether to operate, lay up, or scrap a vessel.
- **Charterers** use it to negotiate Time Charter rates (daily rate must exceed OPEX to be profitable).
- **Naval architects** use it in the design spiral — a ship with 10% lower fuel consumption may have 5% higher OPEX due to premium machinery, and the trade-off must be optimized.
- **Banks and financial institutions** use OPEX projections to model loan repayment schedules and DSCR (Debt Service Coverage Ratio).
- **Regulators (IMO)** use fleet-wide OPEX data to evaluate the economic impact of environmental regulations (e.g., EEXI, CII).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Daily Cost to Annual Cost to Cost-Per-Ton-Mile

The standard chain of conversions in KTU ship economics problems is:

**Step 1 — Daily Operating Cost** ($C_{day}$ in \$/day)

Each component is naturally computed per day; for annually-based components (e.g., insurance, M&R), divide by 350:

$$C_{M\&R,\,day} = \frac{K_{M\&R} \times NRT \times P_{sh}}{350}$$

**Step 2 — Annual Operating Cost** ($C_{year}$ in \$/year)

$$C_{year} = C_{day} \times 350$$

**Step 3 — Cost Per Deadweight Tonne Per Year** (Benchmark Metric)

$$C_{DWT} = \frac{C_{year}}{DWT}$$

This is the **fleet comparison metric** used in KTU questions.

**Step 4 — Cost Per Ton-Mile** (Transport Efficiency Metric)

For a ship carrying $W$ tonnes over distance $D$ nautical miles in time $t$ days:

$$C_{ton\text{-}mile} = \frac{C_{day} \times t}{W \times D} \times 100 \text{ cents/\$}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example (Full KTU-Style Derivation)

> **Problem:** A bulk carrier has the following data:
> - Crew Complement $CR = 22$
> - Average annual cost per crew member $\bar{W} = \$45{,}000$
> - Daily stores per crew $p_s = \$18$/day
> - Daily lubricating oil $Q_{LO} = 180$ L/day at $P_{LO} = \$3.20$/L
> - $K_{M\&R} = 0.010$, $NRT = 28{,}000$ tonnes, $P_{sh} = \$0.45$ of $C_{NB}$ where $C_{NB} = \$30$ million
> - Insurance rate = $1.2\%$ of $C_{NB}$
> - Administration = $15\%$ of $(C_M + C_{M\&R})$
> 
> **Calculate:** Total annual operating cost and cost per DWT per year (assume $DWT = 50{,}000$ tonnes).

#### Solution (with all algebraic steps shown):

**Component 1: Manning Cost (Annual)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Manning,\,annual} &= CR \times \bar{W} \\
&= 22 \times 45{,}000 \\
&= \$990{,}000 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Component 2: Stores Cost (Annual)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Stores,\,annual} &= 350 \times CR \times p_s \\
&= 350 \times 22 \times 18 \\
&= 350 \times 396 \\
&= \$138{,}600 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Component 3: Lubricating Oil Cost (Annual)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{LO,\,annual} &= 350 \times Q_{LO} \times P_{LO} \\
&= 350 \times 180 \times 3.20 \\
&= 350 \times 576 \\
&= \$201{,}600 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Component 4: Maintenance & Repair (Annual)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M\&R,\,annual} &= K_{M\&R} \times NRT \times P_{sh} \\
&= 0.010 \times 28{,}000 \times (0.45 \times 30{,}000{,}000) \\
&= 0.010 \times 28{,}000 \times 13{,}500{,}000 \\
&= 280 \times 13{,}500{,}000 \\
&= \$3{,}780{,}000 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Component 5: Insurance Cost (Annual)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Insurance,\,annual} &= 0.012 \times C_{NB} \\
&= 0.012 \times 30{,}000{,}000 \\
&= \$360{,}000 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Component 6: Administration Cost (Annual)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Admin,\,annual} &= 0.15 \times (C_{Manning} + C_{M\&R}) \\
&= 0.15 \times (990{,}000 + 3{,}780{,}000) \\
&= 0.15 \times 4{,}770{,}000 \\
&= \$715{,}500 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Annual Operating Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{OPEX,\,annual} &= 990{,}000 + 138{,}600 + 201{,}600 + 3{,}780{,}000 + 360{,}000 + 715{,}500 \\
&= \$6{,}185{,}700 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost Per DWT Per Year**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{OPEX,\,DWT} &= \frac{6{,}185{,}700}{50{,}000} \\
&= \$123.71 \text{ /DWT/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Daily Operating Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{OPEX,\,day} &= \frac{6{,}185{,}700}{350} \\
&= \$17{,}673.43 \text{ /day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The largest cost in this example is **Maintenance & Repair (61%)**, which is unusual but reflects the empirical formula's strong sensitivity to the factor $K_{M\&R}$. In KTU problems, always check if the given $K_{M\&R}$ is realistic before trusting the magnitude.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Effect of Design Variables on OPEX

| Design Variable | Effect on $C_{Manning}$ | Effect on $C_{Stores}$ | Effect on $C_{LO}$ | Effect on $C_{M\&R}$ | Effect on $C_{Insurance}$ | Net Effect on OPEX |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Increase Crew** | ↑↑↑ | ↑ | — | — | — | **↑ Strongly** |
| **Increase Speed** | — | — | ↑↑ (more L.O.) | ↑ (more wear) | ↑ (higher value) | **↑ Strongly** |
| **Use Higher Quality Lub. Oil** | — | — | ↑ (price) | ↓ (less wear) | — | **↓ Net** |
| **Increase DWT** | ↑ (slightly) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑↑ | ↑ | **↑** |
| **Use Premium Coatings** | — | — | — | ↓↓ (less corrosion) | — | **↓ Strongly** |
| **Older Ship** | ↑ | — | ↑ | ↑↑↑ | ↑↑ | **↑↑ Strongly** |
| **Register in Low-Cost Flag** | ↓↓↓ | — | — | — | — | **↓ Strongly** |
| **Higher Automation** | ↓↓ | — | — | ↑ (electronics) | — | **↓ Net** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU question type** — examiners often ask: *"If the speed is increased from 14 to 16 knots, how does each component of OPEX change?"* Use this matrix as a mental checklist.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Six Components of Operating Cost

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Operating Cost OPEX] --> B1[Manning Cost]
    A --> B2[Stores Cost]
    A --> B3[Lubricating Oil Cost]
    A --> B4[Maintenance and Repair]
    A --> B5[Insurance Cost]
    A --> B6[Administration Cost]

    B1 --> C1[Basic Wages]
    B1 --> C2[Overtime and Leave]
    B1 --> C3[Social Security]
    B1 --> C4[Travel to and from Ship]

    B2 --> D1[Provisions and Food]
    B2 --> D2[Engine Stores]
    B2 --> D3[Deck Stores]
    B2 --> D4[Cabin Stores]

    B3 --> E1[Cylinder Oil]
    B3 --> E2[System Oil]
    B3 --> E3[Turbocharger Oil]

    B4 --> F1[Routine Maintenance]
    B4 --> F2[Spare Parts]
    B4 --> F3[Dry Docking Every 5 Years]
    B4 --> F4[Hull and Machinery Repair]

    B5 --> G1[Hull and Machinery H and M]
    B5 --> G2[Protection and Indemnity P and I]
    B5 --> G3[War Risk Insurance]

    B6 --> H1[Shore Office Overhead]
    B6 --> H2[Management Fees]
    B6 --> H3[Communication and Audit]
    B6 --> H4[Classification Society Fees]

    A --> I[Output USD per day]
    A --> J[Output USD per year]
    A --> K[Output USD per DWT per year]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cost Computation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1[Ship Particulars DWT NRT CR] --> Compute
    Input2[Empirical Coefficients K_MR Insurance Rate] --> Compute
    Input3[Market Prices Wages Lub Oil Newbuilding Price] --> Compute

    Compute[Cost Computation Engine] --> Out1[Manning Cost per year]
    Compute --> Out2[Stores Cost per year]
    Compute --> Out3[Lub Oil Cost per year]
    Compute --> Out4[M and R Cost per year]
    Compute --> Out5[Insurance Cost per year]
    Compute --> Out6[Administration Cost per year]

    Out1 --> Sum[Aggregator]
    Out2 --> Sum
    Out3 --> Sum
    Out4 --> Sum
    Out5 --> Sum
    Out6 --> Sum

    Sum --> Final1[Annual OPEX in USD]
    Sum --> Final2[Daily OPEX divide by 350]
    Sum --> Final3[Specific OPEX per DWT per year]

    Final1 --> Decision[Shipowner Decision]
    Final2 --> Decision
    Final3 --> Decision

    Decision --> D1[Operate the Ship]
    Decision --> D2[Charter Out]
    Decision --> D3[Lay Up]
    Decision --> D4[Sell for Scrap]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Bar-Chart Schematic: Typical OPEX Share for a Bulk Carrier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph pie["Typical OPEX Share Bulk Carrier Merchant Fleet"]
        direction TB
        note1[Manning Cost 40 percent - typically the largest]
        note2[Maintenance and Repair 25 percent - second largest and most volatile]
        note3[Insurance 12 percent]
        note4[Lubricating Oil 10 percent]
        note5[Stores 6 percent]
        note6[Administration 7 percent]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Pie Charts** have rendering inconsistencies across platforms. The textual schematic above is more portable. Share percentages are typical industry averages for a 20-year-old bulk carrier. **Manning** dominates when crew is large; **M&R** dominates for older vessels; **Insurance** is the most stable component.

### 4.4 Hierarchical Cost-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Ship Total Cost of Ownership] --> L1A[Capital Cost]
    L0 --> L1B[Operating Cost OPEX]
    L0 --> L1C[Voyage Cost]

    L1B --> L2A[Fixed Annual Costs]
    L1B --> L2B[Variable Daily Costs]

    L2A --> L3A[Manning]
    L2A --> L3B[Insurance]
    L2A --> L3C[Administration]
    L2A --> L3D[Special Survey M and R]

    L2B --> L3E[Stores]
    L2B --> L3F[Lubricating Oil]
    L2B --> L3G[Daily M and R]

    L3A --> L4A[Crew Wages]
    L3A --> L4B[Social Charges]
    L3A --> L4C[Travel Allowances]
```

This diagram emphasizes the **classification of OPEX into Fixed and Variable categories** — a frequently tested distinction in KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Operating Cost" of a ship. List its main components.*

**Model Answer:**
Operating cost is the total recurring expenditure incurred by a shipowner to keep a vessel in operational service, expressed per day or per year. It is the cost of running the ship once it has been acquired, **excluding** capital cost recovery.

The six main components are:
1. Manning Cost (Crew Wages)
2. Stores Cost (Provisions & consumables)
3. Lubricating Oil Cost
4. Maintenance and Repair Cost
5. Insurance Cost (H&M, P&I)
6. Administration Cost (Shore overhead)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the empirical formula used to estimate the annual Maintenance and Repair (M&R) cost of a ship. Identify each variable.*

**Model Answer:**

$$C_{M\&R} = K_{M\&R} \times NRT \times P_{sh}$$

Where:
- $C_{M\&R}$ = Annual M&R cost (\$/year)
- $K_{M\&R}$ = Empirical coefficient (dimensionless, typically 0.005 to 0.015)
- $NRT$ = Net Registered Tonnage (tonnes)
- $P_{sh}$ = Ship newbuilding price per tonne (\$/tonne)

---

### Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

A Handysize bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- Deadweight Tonnage, $DWT = 28{,}000$ tonnes
- Net Registered Tonnage, $NRT = 14{,}000$ tonnes
- Crew Complement, $CR = 21$
- Average annual cost per crew member, $\bar{W} = \$42{,}000$
- Daily stores per crew, $p_s = \$20$/day
- Daily lubricating oil consumption, $Q_{LO} = 150$ L/day
- Price of lubricating oil, $P_{LO} = \$3.00$/L
- M&R coefficient, $K_{M\&R} = 0.008$
- Newbuilding price, $C_{NB} = \$24$ million; assume $P_{sh} = 0.5 \times C_{NB}/NRT$
- Insurance rate = $1.1\%$ of $C_{NB}$
- Administration = $12\%$ of $(C_{Manning} + C_{M\&R})$
- Operating days/year = 350

**(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the annual cost of each of the six components of operating cost.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Determine the total annual OPEX, daily OPEX, and OPEX per DWT per year. Comment on which component dominates the operating cost.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Component-wise Annual Cost:**

**(i) Manning Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Manning} &= 21 \times 42{,}000 = \$882{,}000 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Stores Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Stores} &= 350 \times 21 \times 20 = 350 \times 420 = \$147{,}000 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Lubricating Oil Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{LO} &= 350 \times 150 \times 3.00 = 350 \times 450 = \$157{,}500 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) M&R Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sh} &= \frac{0.5 \times 24{,}000{,}000}{14{,}000} = \$857.14 \text{ /tonne} \\
C_{M\&R} &= 0.008 \times 14{,}000 \times 857.14 \\
&= 112 \times 857.14 = \$96{,}000 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Insurance Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Insurance} &= 0.011 \times 24{,}000{,}000 = \$264{,}000 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(vi) Administration Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Admin} &= 0.12 \times (882{,}000 + 96{,}000) \\
&= 0.12 \times 978{,}000 = \$117{,}360 \text{ /year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Aggregated OPEX Metrics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{OPEX,\,annual} &= 882{,}000 + 147{,}000 + 157{,}500 + 96{,}000 + 264{,}000 + 117{,}360 \\
&= \$1{,}663{,}860 \text{ /year} \\[4pt]
C_{OPEX,\,day} &= \frac{1{,}663{,}860}{350} = \$4{,}753.89 \text{ /day} \\[4pt]
C_{OPEX,\,DWT} &= \frac{1{,}663{,}860}{28{,}000} = \$59.42 \text{ /DWT/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** In this case, **Manning Cost (53% of OPEX)** dominates, followed by Insurance (16%) and Stores/Lub. Oil. This is typical for a young vessel where M&R is low; for an older vessel, M&R would dominate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the six components of operating cost of a merchant ship. State the empirical formula for each cost component and identify the dominant variable in each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A ship is being considered for a 15-year economic life. The annual operating cost in year $n$ is given by:

$$C_{OPEX}(n) = C_0 \times (1 + g)^n$$

where $C_0 = \$1.5$ million and $g = 3\%$ per annum (inflation factor). Calculate the **Present Worth** of the total operating cost over 15 years at a discount rate of $i = 8\%$. Use the standard present worth factor.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Empirical Formulas:**

*(See Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the block diagram.)*

| Component | Empirical Formula | Dominant Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Manning | $C_M = CR \times \bar{W}$ | Crew size $CR$ |
| Stores | $C_S = 350 \times CR \times p_s$ | Crew size $CR$ |
| Lub. Oil | $C_{LO} = 350 \times Q_{LO} \times P_{LO}$ | Oil consumption $Q_{LO}$ |
| M&R | $C_{M\&R} = K_{M\&R} \times NRT \times P_{sh}$ | Tonnage $NRT$ |
| Insurance | $C_{Ins} = r_{ins} \times C_{NB}$ | Newbuilding price $C_{NB}$ |
| Admin | $C_{Admin} = r_a \times (C_M + C_{M\&R})$ | Manning + M&R |

**Part (b) — Present Worth Calculation:**

The Present Worth of a geometric gradient series is given by:

$$PW = C_0 \times \left[\frac{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^N}{i - g}\right]$$

Here $C_0 = 1.5 \times 10^6$, $g = 0.03$, $i = 0.08$, $N = 15$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1+g}{1+i} &= \frac{1.03}{1.08} = 0.95370 \\
\left(\frac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^{15} &= 0.95370^{15} = 0.49413 \\
\text{Numerator} &= 1 - 0.49413 = 0.50587 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 0.08 - 0.03 = 0.05 \\
\text{PW Factor} &= \frac{0.50587}{0.05} = 10.1174 \\
PW &= 1{,}500{,}000 \times 10.1174 = \$15{,}176{,}100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Present Worth of OPEX over 15 years ≈ \$15.18 million.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Operating Cost Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** The most common mistake is mixing dollars and cents, or forgetting to convert annual to daily by dividing by 350. Always check units **first** in the problem.
> 2. **M&R Formula Trap:** Students frequently write $P_{sh}$ = newbuilding price per **gross** tonne instead of per **net** tonne, or forget to multiply $C_{NB}$ by the fraction (0.4–0.6) before using $P_{sh} = (f \times C_{NB})/NRT$. Read the problem wording carefully.
> 3. **Insurance Percentage:** If the problem says "1.2% of newbuilding price," do **not** apply it to the current market value — only to $C_{NB}$ unless specified otherwise.
> 4. **Sign Convention in NPV:** Operating cost is a **cash outflow** (negative in NPV). If the question asks for NPV, subtract OPEX, do not add it.
> 5. **Geometric Gradient:** When OPEX increases year-on-year (inflation), use the **geometric gradient present worth factor** as in Question B(b), not the simple uniform series factor.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] Operating cost has **6 components**: Manning, Stores, Lub. Oil, M&R, Insurance, Administration.
- [ ] Standard operating days = **350 days/year** (OECD/UNCTAD convention).
- [ ] Manning cost is typically the **largest** component for young vessels; M&R dominates for old vessels.
- [ ] $C_{M\&R} = K_{M\&R} \times NRT \times P_{sh}$, with $K_{M\&R} \in [0.005, 0.015]$.
- [ ] $C_{Insurance} = (0.7\% \text{ to } 1.5\%) \times C_{NB}$.
- [ ] Lub. Oil cost = $350 \times Q_{LO} \times P_{LO}$; $Q_{LO} \approx 1.5$–$3.0$ g/kWh for main engine cylinder oil.
- [ ] Cost per DWT per year is the **fleet comparison benchmark**.
- [ ] For NPV problems with **escalating OPEX**, use the **geometric gradient** present worth factor.
- [ ] OPEX is a **cash outflow** — subtract, never add, in NPV.
- [ ] Always keep units consistent; prefer **USD/year** as the base unit.
- [ ] The empirical formulas in this module are **first-order estimates**; actual costs vary ±25% in real operations.
- [ ] The Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 is the **canonical visualization** to draw in KTU exams for 14-mark derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Required Freight Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Required Freight Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Required Freight Rate (RFR)** is defined as the minimum freight rate (expressed in monetary units per unit of cargo transported, e.g., **USD per tonne** or **USD per tonne-mile**) that a ship must earn annually to recover **all annualised capital costs, operating costs, and voyage costs**, while also providing the required return on the owner's investment.

Mathematically, the governing identity of ship economics is:

$$
RFR \times Q_{annual} = C_{capital} + C_{operating} + C_{voyage}
$$

where $Q_{annual}$ is the **annual cargo transport work** performed by the vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** RFR is the foundational economic metric that bridges *technical ship design* (DWT, speed, power) with *commercial ship operation* (revenue, profit). It directly determines the minimum viable charter rate the owner must charge.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Taxi Driver's Fare

Imagine you buy a taxi (the ship) for **₹20 lakh** to run a shuttle service. Before you can quote a "per-passenger fare", you must add up:

| Taxi (Analogy) | Ship (Real World) |
|---|---|
| EMI on car loan | **Capital Recovery Cost** |
| Driver's salary, garage rent | **Manning & Operating Costs** |
| Diesel, tolls | **Voyage & Bunker Costs** |
| Passengers transported per year | **Annual Cargo Transported (DWT × Voyages × Load Factor)** |

The **per-passenger fare** that exactly covers all costs (and gives you a small profit) is the **Required Freight Rate**. Just as a taxi driver who quotes too low goes bankrupt, a ship designed with an RFR above the prevailing market freight rate will never be commercially viable.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations

- **Freight Rate unit:** **USD per tonne** (common for tramp trades) or **USD per tonne-mile** (common for tankers and liners).
- **Cost unit:** **USD per year** (annualised).
- **Time unit:** **Year (365 days)** — assumes continuous operation, i.e., **365-day commercial year**.
- **Reference speed:** **Service speed $V_S$ in knots**, used with a sea margin (typically +15%) to determine the trial speed.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners expect you to clearly state the unit of RFR in your final answer. Always write *"RFR = X USD/tonne"* — never leave it dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Annual Ship Cost

The annualised total cost $C_{total}$ of owning and operating a ship is decomposed into three structural pillars.

### Pillar 1 — Capital Cost ($C_{cap}$)
The cost of buying the ship and financing it, spread evenly across its economic life.

- **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF):** Converts a present lump-sum into a uniform annual series.

$$
CRF = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

- **Annual Capital Cost (with scrap value recovery):**

$$
C_{cap} = (P - S) \cdot CRF + S \cdot i
$$

where:
- $P$ = new-building price (USD)
- $S$ = scrap value at end of life (USD)
- $i$ = annual discount/interest rate (decimal)
- $n$ = economic life in years (typically **20–25 years**)

> Why two terms? $(P-S)\cdot CRF$ returns the principal progressively, while $S \cdot i$ pays interest perpetually on the scrap value (a perpetuity compensation to the investor).

### Pillar 2 — Operating Cost ($C_{op}$)
Fixed costs incurred regardless of whether the ship sails or stays in port.

| Sub-cost | Typical KTU Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Manning | $C_{manning}$ | Wages, benefits, travel of crew |
| Stores & Lubricants | $C_{stores}$ | Engine consumables, cabin stores |
| Maintenance & Repair | $C_{M\&R}$ | Dry-docking, hull, machinery upkeep |
| Insurance (H&M) | $C_{ins}$ | Hull and machinery premium |
| Administration | $C_{admin}$ | Office overheads |

$$
C_{op} = C_{manning} + C_{stores} + C_{M\&R} + C_{ins} + C_{admin}
$$

### Pillar 3 — Voyage Cost ($C_{voy}$)
Variable costs that scale with sailing activity.

- **Bunker cost** dominates. Fuel consumption $F$ (tonnes/day) is split into *at-sea* ($F_s$) and *in-port* ($F_p$) consumption.

$$
C_{voy} = 365 \cdot \left[ F_s \cdot t_{sea} + F_p \cdot t_{port} \right] \cdot C_{fuel} \cdot \frac{1}{T_{cycle}}
$$

Equivalently, per voyage:
$$
C_{voy,voyage} = \left( F_s \cdot t_{sea} + F_p \cdot t_{port} \right) \cdot C_{fuel}
$$

where $T_{cycle} = t_{sea} + t_{port}$ is the round-voyage cycle time in days.

## 2.2 Annual Cargo Transport Work ($Q_{annual}$)

$$
Q_{annual} = DWT \cdot N_v \cdot L_f
$$

where:
- $DWT$ = Deadweight Tonnage (tonnes)
- $N_v$ = Number of voyages per year
- $L_f$ = Load factor (0 to 1; typically **0.8 – 1.0** for bulk trades)

The number of voyages per year:

$$
N_v = \frac{365}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{365}{\dfrac{R}{24 \cdot V_S} + t_{port}}
$$

where $R$ = round-voyage distance (nautical miles) and $V_S$ = service speed (knots).

## 2.3 The Master RFR Equation

$$
\boxed{\,RFR = \frac{C_{cap} + C_{op} + C_{voy}}{DWT \cdot N_v \cdot L_f} \;\; \left[ \frac{\text{USD}}{\text{tonne}} \right]\,}
$$

For tonne-mile based freight markets (liners, tankers):

$$
\boxed{\,RFR_{tm} = \frac{C_{cap} + C_{op} + C_{voy}}{DWT \cdot N_v \cdot L_f \cdot R} \;\; \left[ \frac{\text{USD}}{\text{tonne-mile}} \right]\,}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n-1}$ | Converts principal to annual payment | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{cap} = (P-S)\cdot CRF + S\cdot i$ | Annual capital cost | USD/yr |
| 3 | $C_{op} = \sum(\text{Manning, Stores, M\&R, Ins, Admin})$ | Annual operating cost | USD/yr |
| 4 | $t_{sea} = \dfrac{R}{24 \cdot V_S}$ | Time at sea per voyage | days |
| 5 | $T_{cycle} = t_{sea} + t_{port}$ | Total round-voyage time | days |
| 6 | $N_v = \dfrac{365}{T_{cycle}}$ | Voyages per year | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{voy,voy} = (F_s t_{sea} + F_p t_{port}) C_{fuel}$ | Voyage fuel cost | USD/voyage |
| 8 | $C_{voy,ann} = N_v \cdot C_{voy,voy}$ | Annual voyage cost | USD/yr |
| 9 | $Q_{ann} = DWT \cdot N_v \cdot L_f$ | Annual cargo carried | tonnes/yr |
| 10 | $RFR = \dfrac{C_{cap}+C_{op}+C_{voy,ann}}{Q_{ann}}$ | Required Freight Rate | USD/tonne |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Shipping banks, classification societies, and chartering desks compute RFR during the **preliminary design stage** to decide whether a new-building project is bankable. If the projected market freight rate is below the RFR, the loan is denied. Thus, RFR is the single most important *design constraint* linking hull form, propulsion, and deadweight to **commercial viability**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)

Consider a loan amount $P$ taken at interest rate $i$ for $n$ years, with an annual payment $A$ (the annuity). The future value of $A$ paid at the end of each year for $n$ years must equal the future value of $P$ compounded forward:

$$
P(1+i)^n = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

Rearranging for $A$:

$$
A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = P \cdot CRF
$$

When a scrap value $S$ is recovered at year $n$, we subtract its present equivalent. The annual equivalent of $S$ (treated as a future value) is $S \cdot CRF$. Net annual cost is therefore:

$$
C_{cap} = P \cdot CRF - S \cdot CRF + S \cdot i
$$

$$
C_{cap} = (P - S) \cdot CRF + S \cdot i
$$

> The term $S \cdot i$ arises because once the owner recovers $S$ at year $n$, they could perpetually earn interest on it — this lost opportunity cost is added back as a continuous annual charge.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Annual Voyage Cost

Let $R$ be the round-voyage distance in nautical miles and $V_S$ the service speed in knots.

- Time at sea:
$$
t_{sea} = \frac{R}{24 \cdot V_S} \;\; [\text{days}]
$$

- Bunker consumed at sea per voyage: $F_s \cdot t_{sea}$ tonnes.
- Bunker consumed in port per voyage: $F_p \cdot t_{port}$ tonnes.
- Cost per voyage:
$$
C_{voy,voy} = \left( F_s \cdot t_{sea} + F_p \cdot t_{port} \right) \cdot C_{fuel}
$$

- Number of voyages per year:
$$
N_v = \frac{365}{t_{sea} + t_{port}}
$$

- Total annual voyage cost:
$$
C_{voy,ann} = N_v \cdot C_{voy,voy} = 365 \cdot \frac{F_s \cdot t_{sea} + F_p \cdot t_{port}}{t_{sea} + t_{port}} \cdot C_{fuel}
$$

## 3.3 Master RFR Derivation

By definition, RFR is the freight income per tonne of cargo that breaks even with the total annual cost:

$$
RFR \cdot Q_{annual} = C_{total} = C_{cap} + C_{op} + C_{voy,ann}
$$

Substituting $Q_{annual} = DWT \cdot N_v \cdot L_f$:

$$
RFR = \frac{C_{cap} + C_{op} + C_{voy,ann}}{DWT \cdot N_v \cdot L_f}
$$

This is the **master equation** evaluated in every KTU numerical problem on RFR.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Worked Example (Model Problem — 14 Mark Standard)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
> - New-building price $P$ = **20 million USD**
> - Scrap value $S$ = **1 million USD**, Life $n$ = **20 years**, Interest $i$ = **10%**
> - Deadweight $DWT$ = **40,000 tonnes**
> - Service speed $V_S$ = **14 knots**
> - Round-voyage distance $R$ = **6,000 nm**
> - Port time $t_{port}$ = **8 days**
> - Fuel at sea $F_s$ = **70 t/day**, Fuel in port $F_p$ = **5 t/day**
> - Fuel price $C_{fuel}$ = **250 USD/tonne**
> - Manning = 1.2 M USD/yr, Stores = 0.3 M USD/yr, M&R = 0.5 M USD/yr, Insurance = 0.2 M USD/yr, Admin = 0.1 M USD/yr
> - Load factor $L_f$ = **0.8**
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Annual Capital Cost, (b) Voyage cost and annual cargo carried, (c) Required Freight Rate in USD/tonne and USD/tonne-mile.

### Solution — Part (a): Annual Capital Cost

**Step 1 — Compute $(1+i)^n$:**

$$
(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CRF:**

$$
CRF = \frac{0.10 \times 6.7275}{6.7275 - 1} = \frac{0.67275}{5.7275} = 0.11746
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the capital cost formula:**

$$
C_{cap} = (20 - 1) \times 0.11746 + 1 \times 0.10
$$

$$
C_{cap} = 19 \times 0.11746 + 0.10 = 2.2317 + 0.10 = 2.3317 \;\; \text{M USD/yr}
$$

### Solution — Part (b): Voyage Cost and Annual Cargo

**Step 4 — Time at sea:**

$$
t_{sea} = \frac{6000}{24 \times 14} = \frac{6000}{336} = 17.857 \;\; \text{days}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cycle time:**

$$
T_{cycle} = 17.857 + 8 = 25.857 \;\; \text{days}
$$

**Step 6 — Voyages per year:**

$$
N_v = \frac{365}{25.857} = 14.115
$$

**Step 7 — Bunker per voyage:**

$$
Bunker_{voy} = (70 \times 17.857) + (5 \times 8) = 1250.0 + 40.0 = 1290.0 \;\; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 8 — Voyage cost (fuel) per voyage:**

$$
C_{voy,voy} = 1290.0 \times 250 = 322{,}500 \;\; \text{USD/voyage}
$$

**Step 9 — Annual voyage cost:**

$$
C_{voy,ann} = 14.115 \times 322{,}500 = 4.5521 \;\; \text{M USD/yr}
$$

**Step 10 — Annual cargo carried:**

$$
Q_{annual} = 40{,}000 \times 14.115 \times 0.8 = 451{,}680 \;\; \text{tonnes/yr}
$$

### Solution — Part (c): RFR Computation

**Step 11 — Total Operating Cost:**

$$
C_{op} = 1.2 + 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 2.3 \;\; \text{M USD/yr}
$$

**Step 12 — Total Annual Cost:**

$$
C_{total} = C_{cap} + C_{op} + C_{voy,ann} = 2.3317 + 2.3 + 4.5521 = 9.1838 \;\; \text{M USD/yr}
$$

**Step 13 — RFR per tonne:**

$$
RFR = \frac{9.1838 \times 10^6}{451{,}680} = 20.33 \;\; \text{USD/tonne}
$$

**Step 14 — RFR per tonne-mile:**

$$
RFR_{tm} = \frac{20.33}{6000} = 0.00339 \;\; \text{USD/tonne-mile}
$$

> **Final Answer:** RFR ≈ **20.33 USD/tonne** or **0.00339 USD/tonne-mile**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of RFR to Design Variables

| Design Variable | Effect on RFR | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $\uparrow$ Service speed $V_S$ | $\downarrow$ voyages, $\uparrow$ fuel, ambiguous net | Optimise via **speed-fuel trade-off** studies |
| $\uparrow$ Deadweight $DWT$ | $\downarrow$ RFR (economies of scale) | Larger ships favoured in stable trades |
| $\uparrow$ Fuel price $C_{fuel}$ | $\uparrow$ RFR | Favours slow steaming & dual-fuel designs |
| $\uparrow$ New-building price $P$ | $\uparrow$ RFR linearly | Justifies series production discounts |
| $\uparrow$ Load factor $L_f$ | $\downarrow$ RFR | Calls for **cargo-handling optimisation** |
| $\uparrow$ Port time $t_{port}$ | $\downarrow N_v$, $\uparrow$ RFR | Justifies faster cargo gear, just-in-time arrival |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RFR Cost-Component Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Required Freight Rate Computation] --> B[Total Annual Cost]
    A --> C[Annual Cargo Transport Work]
    B --> D[Capital Cost C_cap]
    B --> E[Operating Cost C_op]
    B --> F[Voyage Cost C_voy]
    D --> D1[New-building Price P]
    D --> D2[Scrap Value S]
    D --> D3[Interest Rate i]
    D --> D4[Economic Life n]
    E --> E1[Manning Cost]
    E --> E2[Stores and Lubricants]
    E --> E3[Maintenance and Repair]
    E --> E4[Insurance H and M]
    E --> E5[Administration]
    F --> F1[Fuel at Sea Fs]
    F --> F2[Fuel in Port Fp]
    F --> F3[Fuel Price Cfuel]
    F --> F4[Round Voyage Distance R]
    F --> F5[Service Speed Vs]
    C --> G1[Deadweight DWT]
    C --> G2[Voyages per Year Nv]
    C --> G3[Load Factor Lf]
    B --> H[Sum of all costs in USD per year]
    C --> I[Product of DWT Nv and Lf in tonnes per year]
    H --> J[RFR equals C_total divided by Q_annual USD per tonne]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compare with Market Freight Rate]
    K --> L{Market Rate greater than RFR}
    L -->|Yes| M[Project is BANKABLE - proceed]
    L -->|No| N[REDESIGN - reduce costs or increase cargo]
```

## 4.2 Voyage Cycle Time Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Departure from Load Port] --> B[Ballast Leg]
    B --> C[Load Port Stay]
    C --> D[Loaded Leg]
    D --> E[Discharge Port Stay]
    E --> F[Return to Load Port]
    F --> A
    B -.time.-> B1[t_ballast equals R_ballast divided by 24 Vs]
    D -.time.-> D1[t_loaded equals R_loaded divided by 24 Vs]
    C -.time.-> C1[t_load_port days]
    E -.time.-> E1[t_discharge_port days]
    B1 --> G[T_cycle total]
    D1 --> G
    C1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    G --> H[N_v equals 365 divided by T_cycle]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — From Design Parameters to RFR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Principal Dimensions L B D T] --> S1[Ship Particulars]
    P2[Machinery Selection] --> S1
    P3[Tonnage and Deadweight] --> S1
    S1 --> Q1[Service Speed Vs]
    S1 --> Q2[Daily Fuel Consumption Fs and Fp]
    S1 --> Q3[New-building Price P]
    S1 --> Q4[Deadweight DWT]
    Q1 --> R1[Voyage Time Calculation]
    Q2 --> R2[Voyage Cost Calculation]
    Q3 --> R3[Capital Cost via CRF]
    Q4 --> R4[Annual Cargo Work]
    R1 --> R5[Annual Operating Profile]
    R2 --> R5
    R3 --> R6[Total Annual Cost]
    R4 --> R6
    R5 --> R6
    R6 --> END1[RFR Output in USD per tonne]
    END1 --> DEC{Is RFR less than Market Rate}
    DEC -->|Yes| OK[Design Accepted]
    DEC -->|No| REV[Return to Principal Dimensions and Iterate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The three Mermaid schematics collectively map the *information flow* of ship economic design. Block 1 decomposes cost components; Block 2 shows the operational time-loop; Block 3 highlights the iterative optimisation loop inherent in preliminary ship design. KTU examiners often award partial credit for well-labelled flowcharts in design-oriented 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1(a)]**
>
> **Q1.** Define *Required Freight Rate*. Why is it considered a critical metric in ship design economics?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Required Freight Rate (RFR) is the minimum freight rate (USD/tonne) a vessel must earn annually to recover its **annualised capital cost**, **operating cost**, and **voyage cost**, including the required return on investment. It is the *break-even freight rate* of the ship.
>
> (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming the three cost components, 1 mark for the phrase "minimum/break-even rate required for commercial viability".)
>
> **Significance:** RFR directly links the technical design (DWT, speed, power) to the **commercial feasibility** of a new-building project. If RFR exceeds the prevailing market freight rate, the project is non-bankable. Hence, RFR is used by shipowners, banks, and classification societies during the **preliminary design stage** to validate the principal dimensions and machinery selection.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1(b)]**
>
> **Q2.** Distinguish between **Capital Cost** and **Operating Cost** of a ship, with one example of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Capital Cost | Operating Cost |
> |---|---|---|
> | Nature | One-time, recovered over ship's life | Recurring, annual |
> | Occurrence | Independent of trading pattern | Independent of voyage activity |
> | Example | New-building price, financed via CRF | Manning wages, insurance, stores |
>
> (1 mark each for correct distinction + 1 mark for valid examples.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Q2]

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for **Annual Capital Cost** of a ship, including the role of scrap value. Clearly state the meaning of the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** and list **five** components of annual operating cost.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A general cargo ship is purchased for **$25 million** with an expected life of **20 years** and a scrap value of **$1.5 million**. The annual interest rate is **8%**. Compute the **annual capital cost**.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define CRF:**
The Capital Recovery Factor is the uniform annual payment $A$ required to recover a present principal $P$ over $n$ years at interest rate $i$:

$$
CRF = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Derive annual capital cost with scrap value:**
The annual payment must return the principal net of scrap and provide interest on outstanding capital. Considering the future scrap value $S$ received at year $n$:

$$
C_{cap} = (P - S) \cdot CRF + S \cdot i
$$

**Step 3 — Justify both terms:**
- $(P-S)\cdot CRF$ spreads the *depreciable* amount over the life.
- $S \cdot i$ compensates for the *lost interest* on the perpetual scrap value.

**Step 4 — Five Operating Cost Components (1.5 marks):**
1. Manning (crew wages)
2. Stores and lubricants
3. Maintenance and repair
4. Insurance (Hull & Machinery)
5. Administration

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $P = 25$ M USD, $S = 1.5$ M USD, $n = 20$ yr, $i = 0.08$

**Step 1 — Compute $(1.08)^{20}$:**

$$
(1.08)^{20} = 4.6610
$$

**Step 2 — CRF:**

$$
CRF = \frac{0.08 \times 4.6610}{4.6610 - 1} = \frac{0.37288}{3.6610} = 0.10185
$$

**Step 3 — Annual Capital Cost:**

$$
C_{cap} = (25 - 1.5) \times 0.10185 + 1.5 \times 0.08
$$

$$
C_{cap} = 23.5 \times 0.10185 + 0.12 = 2.3935 + 0.12 = 2.5135 \;\; \text{M USD/yr}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Annual capital cost ≈ **$2.51 million per year**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q2 — Alternative]

> **(a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a clear schematic, explain the **complete procedure** to compute the Required Freight Rate of a ship. List all the input data required.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A bulk carrier has $DWT$ = **55,000 t**, $V_S$ = **15 knots**, round-voyage distance $R$ = **7,500 nm**, port time = **10 days**, fuel at sea = **80 t/day**, fuel in port = **6 t/day**, fuel price = **$280/t**, total annual cost = **$11 million**, load factor = **0.85**. Calculate RFR in **USD/tonne** and **USD/tonne-mile**.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Collect ship particulars: $P$, $S$, $n$, $i$, $DWT$, $V_S$, $R$, $t_{port}$, $F_s$, $F_p$, $C_{fuel}$, operating cost sub-items, $L_f$.

**Step 2:** Compute $CRF$ and hence $C_{cap}$ using:

$$
C_{cap} = (P - S) \cdot CRF + S \cdot i
$$

**Step 3:** Sum operating costs: $C_{op} = \text{Manning} + \text{Stores} + \text{M\&R} + \text{Insurance} + \text{Admin}$.

**Step 4:** Compute voyage cost per voyage:

$$
C_{voy,voy} = (F_s \cdot t_{sea} + F_p \cdot t_{port}) \cdot C_{fuel}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $N_v$ and $C_{voy,ann}$:

$$
N_v = \frac{365}{t_{sea} + t_{port}}, \quad C_{voy,ann} = N_v \cdot C_{voy,voy}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute $Q_{annual} = DWT \cdot N_v \cdot L_f$.

**Step 7:** Compute RFR:

$$
RFR = \frac{C_{cap} + C_{op} + C_{voy,ann}}{Q_{annual}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Time at sea:**

$$
t_{sea} = \frac{7500}{24 \times 15} = \frac{7500}{360} = 20.833 \;\; \text{days}
$$

**Step 2 — Total cycle:**

$$
T_{cycle} = 20.833 + 10 = 30.833 \;\; \text{days}
$$

**Step 3 — Voyages per year:**

$$
N_v = \frac{365}{30.833} = 11.838
$$

**Step 4 — Annual cargo:**

$$
Q_{annual} = 55{,}000 \times 11.838 \times 0.85 = 553{,}418 \;\; \text{tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — RFR per tonne:**

$$
RFR = \frac{11 \times 10^6}{553{,}418} = 19.876 \;\; \text{USD/tonne}
$$

**Step 6 — RFR per tonne-mile:**

$$
RFR_{tm} = \frac{19.876}{7500} = 0.00265 \;\; \text{USD/tonne-mile}
$$

> **Final Answer:** RFR ≈ **19.88 USD/tonne** or **0.00265 USD/tonne-mile**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Cost You 2–3 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the scrap interest term $S \cdot i$** in the capital cost formula. Many students write only $(P-S) \cdot CRF$ — this loses **1 mark** in capital cost questions.
> 2. **Mixing up trial speed and service speed.** Use $V_S$ (service speed) for voyage time, never the trial speed.
> 3. **Using 360 days instead of 365 days** for $N_v$. KTU strictly uses **365 days** unless otherwise stated.
> 4. **Forgetting the load factor** in $Q_{annual} = DWT \cdot N_v \cdot L_f$. The default is $L_f = 1.0$ only if the problem explicitly says "fully loaded each voyage".
> 5. **Forgetting port-time bunker consumption** $F_p \cdot t_{port}$. KTU problems frequently test this — omitting it costs **1 mark**.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Reporting RFR as USD/tonne when the problem asks for USD/tonne-mile (or vice versa) — **lose 0.5 to 1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **RFR** = (Annual Capital + Operating + Voyage Cost) ÷ Annual Cargo Transported.
- [x] **CRF formula** must be memorised: $CRF = i(1+i)^n / [(1+i)^n - 1]$.
- [x] **Capital Cost** = $(P-S)\cdot CRF + S \cdot i$ — **never omit** the $S\cdot i$ term.
- [x] **Voyage time** uses **service speed** in knots, distance in nautical miles, time in days: $t_{sea} = R/(24 V_S)$.
- [x] **Voyages per year** = $365 / T_{cycle}$ — always use **365** unless specified.
- [x] **Bunker per voyage** = $(F_s \cdot t_{sea} + F_p \cdot t_{port})$ — include **port consumption**.
- [x] **Annual cargo** = $DWT \cdot N_v \cdot L_f$ — multiply by **load factor**.
- [x] Always state the **unit of RFR** explicitly: USD/tonne OR USD/tonne-mile.
- [x] For **tonne-mile** markets: divide the per-tonne RFR by the round-voyage distance $R$.
- [x] **RFR < Market Freight Rate** ⇒ project is **bankable**; otherwise, **redesign**.
- [x] KTU numericals often test the **speed–fuel trade-off**: higher $V_S$ → fewer voyages but more fuel.
- [x] Larger $DWT$ lowers RFR (economies of scale); higher $C_{fuel}$ raises it.
- [x] **Sensitivity analysis** of RFR to design variables is a high-yield topic for 14-mark design questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Owner's Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Owner's Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Owner's Requirements (OR)** is a formal, comprehensive design brief prepared by the prospective shipowner (or operator) that defines the **mission profile, operational envelope, commercial constraints, regulatory boundaries, and performance expectations** of the vessel to be designed. It serves as the **primary input document** to the preliminary ship design process and forms the contractual and technical foundation upon which the naval architect develops the **Ship Design Specification (SDS)**.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, formulation of Owner's Requirements maps directly to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Formulate owner's requirements and mission profile for a given ship operational scenario."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Owner's Requirements like building a custom house:**
> When you commission a house, you don't hand the architect a blueprint — you hand them a **wish-list**: how many bedrooms, budget, location, style, future family growth, parking needs, and local building rules. The architect then translates this into technical drawings.
> 
> Similarly, in ship design, the **owner** does not provide a GA (General Arrangement) drawing. The owner states: *"I want a ship that carries 45,000 tonnes of bulk coal from Visakhapatnam to Singapore, at 14 knots service speed, with 5 crew, and must be ready by 2027."* The **naval architect** then converts these business needs into displacement, power, dimensions, and stability criteria.

### 1.3 Position in the Ship Design Spiral

The Owner's Requirements sit at **Step 0 / Phase 1** of Watson's *Practical Ship Design* spiral (or Biran's design methodology). It is the **upstream input** that drives:

- Mission profile definition
- Deadweight / payload estimation
- Principal dimensions estimation
- Form coefficient selection
- Powering requirement calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The output of the Owner's Requirements phase is typically documented as a **Ship Design Specification Sheet** containing approximately **40–60 quantified parameters**, often cited in KTU board examinations under the 14-mark design question category.

### 1.4 Classification of Owner's Requirements

Owner's Requirements are conventionally grouped into **five major categories** as per established ship design literature:

| # | Category | Nature | Example |
|---|----------|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Commercial Requirements** | Economic | ROI, freight rate, charter type |
| 2 | **Operational Requirements** | Functional | Route, cargo, speed, port restrictions |
| 3 | **Technical Requirements** | Engineering | Type of propulsion, automation level |
| 4 | **Regulatory Requirements** | Statutory | Class rules, SOLAS, MARPOL, flag state |
| 5 | **Human & Environmental** | Ergonomic / Ecological | Crew comfort, emission limits |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Owner's Requirement Categories

#### 2.1.1 Commercial Requirements (The Business Driver)

These define **why the ship is being built** and the **economic boundaries** of the project.

- **Type of Trade:** Tramp (spot market), Liner (scheduled), Industrial carrier (dedicated), or Specialized (e.g., cable layer).
- **Charter Arrangement:** Voyage charter, time charter, or bareboat charter — each implies different sensitivity to fuel consumption vs. capital cost.
- **Required Return on Investment (ROI):** Typically **8–15 % IRR** for merchant vessels.
- **Expected Service Life:** Usually **20–25 years** for merchant ships; 30+ years for naval auxiliaries.
- **Newbuilding Budget Ceiling:** Drives choice between conventional vs. advanced (and expensive) solutions.
- **Resale Value Considerations:** Standard hull forms retain value better than highly customized ones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Commercial requirements are often the *most underestimated* parameters in student design projects. KTU board examiners frequently test whether students understand that **two ships with identical operational requirements can have different designs** purely due to differing commercial constraints (e.g., lower fuel cost vs. lower build cost optimization).

#### 2.1.2 Operational Requirements (The Mission Profile)

This is the **core of the Owner's Requirements** and most heavily tested.

- **Type of Cargo & Quantity:** Deadweight (DWT), cargo density ($\rho_c$), stowage factor (SF).
- **Route & Distance:** Specific ports, e.g., *Kochi → Hamburg*, distance ~$6{,}000$ nautical miles.
- **Service Speed ($V_S$):** Contractual speed, typically at **85–90 % MCR** with **15 % sea margin**.
- **Required Number of Voyages per Year:** Directly determines annual transport work.
- **Port & Waterway Restrictions:** Maximum length overall (LOA), beam ($B$), air draft — e.g., **Panamax** ($L \leq 294$ m, $B \leq 32.3$ m), **Suezmax** ($L \leq \leq 400$ m, draught $\leq 20.1$ m).
- **Cargo Handling Rate:** Determines number of holds, hatch sizes, crane capacity.
- **Manning Scale:** Crew number and accommodation grade (drives $C_B$ via block coefficient accommodation volume).

#### 2.1.3 Technical Requirements (Engineering Preferences)

- **Propulsion Type:** Diesel, diesel-electric, steam turbine, LNG, or nuclear.
- **Machinery Layout:** Aft, amidships, or COGAS / COGOG arrangements.
- **Automation Level:** UMS (Unmanned Machinery Space) — affects manning cost and electronics budget.
- **Hull Form Preference:** Single skin, double skin, double hull (mandatory for tankers under MARPOL Reg. 19).
- **Materials of Construction:** Mild steel vs. high-tensile steel (e.g., **AH36, DH36**) — affects lightweight and cost.
- **Special Features:** Bow thrusters, stabilizers, ice-strengthening (e.g., **Finnish-Swedish Ice Class 1A**).

#### 2.1.4 Regulatory Requirements (Statutory Compliance)

- **Classification Society:** Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas, ABS — drives scantling rules.
- **Flag State:** Country of registry — defines applicable national regulations.
- **International Conventions:** SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines (1966), COLREG, STCW, MLC 2006.
- **Local Rules:** Coastal state requirements (e.g., sulphur cap ECA — **0.10 % S** in emission control areas).
- **Damage Stability Standards:** Probabilistic (SOLAS 2009) vs. deterministic (older rules).

#### 2.1.5 Human & Environmental Requirements

- **Crew Comfort:** Noise limits (typically **\leq 75 dB(A)** in accommodation), vibration standards (ISO 6954).
- **Ergonomic Standards:** ILO 133 / MLC 2006 crew spaces.
- **Environmental Compliance:** IMO Tier II / Tier III NOx limits, ballast water management (BWMS), SOx scrubbers.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Owner's Requirements Phase)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following parameters are *outputs* derived from the Owner's Requirements and are required for subsequent design stages. KTU frequently tests these derivations.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Source / Meaning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Annual Cargo Transport (Deadweight) | $DWT_{req} = \dfrac{W_{annual}}{N_{voy} \cdot \eta_{stow}}$ | tonnes | $W_{annual}$ = annual cargo tonnage, $N_{voy}$ = voyages/yr |
| 2 | Stowage Factor | $SF = \dfrac{V_{cargo}}{W_{cargo}}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{t}$ | Determines hold volume |
| 3 | Service Speed with Sea Margin | $V_S = V_{trial} \cdot f_{sea}$ (sea margin $f_{sea} = 1.15$) | knots | Trial $\rightarrow$ service conversion |
| 4 | EHP Required (Preliminary) | $EHP = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot V_S^3 \cdot C_F}{C_{prop} \cdot \eta_H \cdot 75}$ | metric HP | Holtrop-style preliminary |
| 5 | Range / Endurance | $Range = V_S \cdot t_{voy}$ | nautical miles | Used for fuel capacity |
| 6 | Required Fuel Bunker | $B = \dfrac{EHP \cdot SFOC \cdot t_{voy}}{10^6}$ | tonnes | $SFOC$ in g/kWh |
| 7 | Block Coefficient Estimate (Bulker) | $C_B = 0.7 + 0.0025 \cdot V_S$ | dimensionless | Empirical Ayre's |
| 8 | Length-to-Beam Ratio | $L/B$ | — | $5.5$–$7.5$ typical |

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, examiners often give an *Owner's Requirement* problem (e.g., "Design a bulk carrier to carry 50,000 t of iron ore from Goa to China"). Students must *first extract* the OR data, then *compute* the principal dimensions using these formulas. Marks are split: 4 marks for OR extraction, 10 marks for derived design.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

In industrial practice, the Owner's Requirements document is used to:
- Solicit bids from multiple shipyards.
- Form the basis of the **Shipbuilding Contract**.
- Establish a **Technical Specification Annex**.
- Provide inputs for **Risk Assessment** (Hazard Identification - HAZID).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Practice:** Leading shipowners like **Maersk, MSC, and BW** maintain standardized Owner's Requirement templates that can run to 200+ pages. The naval architect must first parse this document into a 1-page **Design Brief** for feasibility study.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: From Owner's Requirements to Principal Dimensions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU-style problem (14-mark weightage):**
> *An owner requires a bulk carrier to transport **55,000 tonnes of iron ore** per voyage from **Mormugao to Qingdao** (distance = **4,200 nm**). Service speed = **14 knots**, with **9 voyages/year** desired. The vessel must comply with **IACS Common Structural Rules** and operate with a crew of **24**. Estimate: (a) Annual transport work and required deadweight, (b) Block coefficient and principal dimensions, (c) EHP and SHP.*

---

#### 3.1.1 Part (a): Annual Transport Work & Deadweight Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify Owner's Requirements (Extraction)**

From the problem statement, the following OR data are extracted:

- Cargo per voyage: $W_c = 55{,}000$ tonnes
- Voyages per year: $N_{voy} = 9$
- Route distance (one way): $D = 4{,}200$ nm
- Service speed: $V_S = 14$ knots
- Crew: $N_{crew} = 24$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Transport Work**

$$
W_{annual} = W_c \cdot N_{voy} = 55{,}000 \cdot 9 = 495{,}000 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for Round Trip and Port Stay**

Round trip distance:
$$
D_{RT} = 2 \cdot D = 2 \cdot 4{,}200 = 8{,}400 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 4 — Add Crew, Stores, Consumables to Cargo for DWT**

$$
DWT = W_c + W_{crew} + W_{stores} + W_{fuel} + W_{fw}
$$

Assuming typical allowances:
- Crew + effects: $W_{crew} = 0.4 \cdot N_{crew} = 0.4 \cdot 24 = 9.6$ tonnes
- Stores for round trip ($\sim$ 30 days): $W_{stores} = 30 \cdot 0.05 \cdot N_{crew} = 36$ tonnes
- Fresh water: $W_{fw} = 30 \cdot 0.15 \cdot N_{crew} = 108$ tonnes
- Fuel (to be estimated in part c): $W_{fuel} \approx 1{,}800$ tonnes (assumed initially)

Summing the constant terms:
$$
W_{const} = 9.6 + 36 + 108 = 153.6 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Therefore:
$$
DWT_{req} = 55{,}000 + 153.6 + W_{fuel} \approx 57{,}000 \text{ tonnes (round figure)}
$$

---

#### 3.1.2 Part (b): Block Coefficient & Principal Dimensions

**Step 5 — Empirical Block Coefficient (Ayre's Formula for Bulk Carriers)**

$$
C_B = 0.7 + 0.0025 \cdot V_S
$$

Substituting $V_S = 14$ knots:
$$
C_B = 0.7 + 0.0025 \cdot 14 = 0.7 + 0.035 = 0.735
$$

**Step 6 — Assume Standard $L/B$ and $B/T$ Ratios for Bulk Carrier**

For a Handysize/Supramax bulk carrier:
$$
L/B = 6.0, \qquad B/T = 2.5
$$

**Step 7 — Volume-Displacement Equation**

The displacement volume is:
$$
\nabla = \frac{DWT + LWT}{\rho_{sw}}
$$

For a 57,000 DWT bulker, lightweight is approximately:
$$
LWT \approx 0.18 \cdot DWT = 0.18 \cdot 57{,}000 = 10{,}260 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Therefore displacement:
$$
\Delta = DWT + LWT = 57{,}000 + 10{,}260 = 67{,}260 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Displacement volume (seawater $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³):
$$
\nabla = \frac{67{,}260}{1.025} = 65{,}620 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 8 — Use Volume Coefficient Relations**

The principal dimensions satisfy:
$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T
$$

And from $L/B = 6.0$:
$$
L = 6B
$$

From $B/T = 2.5$:
$$
T = \frac{B}{2.5} = 0.4B
$$

Substituting into the volume equation:
$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot (6B) \cdot B \cdot (0.4B)
$$
$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot 2.4 \cdot B^3
$$

Therefore:
$$
B^3 = \frac{\nabla}{C_B \cdot 2.4}
$$
$$
B^3 = \frac{65{,}620}{0.735 \cdot 2.4}
$$
$$
B^3 = \frac{65{,}620}{1.764}
$$
$$
B^3 = 37{,}210
$$

Solving:
$$
B = \sqrt[3]{37{,}210} \approx 33.43 \text{ m}
$$

Rounding to standard:
$$
B \approx 32.30 \text{ m (Panamax beam)}
$$

Wait — Panamax constraint from OR! Let us **revise** using Panamax $B = 32.30$ m:

Recompute $B^3$:
$$
B^3 = 32.30^3 = 33{,}696.8 \text{ m}^3
$$

Recompute $\nabla$:
$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot 2.4 \cdot B^3 = 0.735 \cdot 2.4 \cdot 33{,}696.8 = 59{,}437 \text{ m}^3
$$

Revised displacement:
$$
\Delta = 59{,}437 \cdot 1.025 = 60{,}923 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Therefore:
$$
L = 6B = 6 \cdot 32.30 = 193.8 \text{ m}
$$
$$
T = B / 2.5 = 32.30 / 2.5 = 12.92 \text{ m}
$$

**Principal Dimensions:**
$$
\boxed{L_{pp} \approx 190 \text{ m}, \quad B = 32.3 \text{ m}, \quad T = 12.9 \text{ m}}
$$

---

#### 3.1.3 Part (c): EHP and SHP Estimation

**Step 9 — Estimate Total Resistance Coefficient (Holtrop-Mennen style, simplified)**

For a bulk carrier, the ITTC 1978 friction coefficient is:
$$
C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}
$$

where $R_n = V \cdot L / \nu$.

At $V_S = 14$ knots = **7.20 m/s** (using $1$ knot = $0.5144$ m/s):
$$
V = 14 \cdot 0.5144 = 7.20 \text{ m/s}
$$

Reynolds number:
$$
R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu} = \frac{7.20 \cdot 193.8}{1.19 \cdot 10^{-6}} = 1.17 \cdot 10^9
$$

Friction coefficient:
$$
C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.17 \cdot 10^9) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(9.068 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{49.96} = 1.501 \cdot 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 10 — Effective Power (EHP)**

Assuming $C_T \approx 1.15 \cdot C_F$ (form + wave + correlation):
$$
C_T = 1.15 \cdot 1.501 \cdot 10^{-3} = 1.726 \cdot 10^{-3}
$$

EHP formula:
$$
EHP = \frac{\Delta \cdot V^3 \cdot C_T}{75} \quad \text{(metric units, with V in m/s, Δ in tonnes-force)}
$$

Wait — this is a common student error. Let me use the **dimensional EHP equation**:

$$
EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} = \frac{\left(0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_T\right) \cdot V}{75}
$$

Wetted surface for bulker (Trawler formula approximation):
$$
S = 1.7 \cdot L \cdot T + \nabla/T \approx 1.7 \cdot 193.8 \cdot 12.9 + 59{,}437/12.9
$$
$$
S = 4{,}250 + 4{,}608 = 8{,}858 \text{ m}^2
$$

Total resistance:
$$
R_T = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 7.20^2 \cdot 8{,}858 \cdot 1.726 \cdot 10^{-3}
$$
$$
R_T = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 51.84 \cdot 8{,}858 \cdot 1.726 \cdot 10^{-3}
$$
$$
R_T = 3.974 \cdot 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 397.4 \text{ kN}
$$

Effective Horsepower:
$$
EHP = \frac{397.4 \cdot 10^3 \cdot 7.20}{75} = 38{,}150 \text{ metric HP}
$$

Wait, conversion check: $1$ HP = $745.7$ W, so:
$$
EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{745.7} = \frac{397{,}400 \cdot 7.20}{745.7} = 3{,}837 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 11 — Shaft Horsepower (SHP)**

Assume propulsive efficiency:
- Hull efficiency: $\eta_H = 1.05$
- Propeller efficiency: $\eta_0 \cdot \eta_R = 0.65$
- Rotational efficiency: $\eta_{rot} = 1.0$
- Mechanical transmission: $\eta_M = 0.98$

Total propulsive coefficient:
$$
\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_{rot} \cdot \eta_M = 1.05 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.98 = 0.669
$$

SHP:
$$
SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{3{,}837}{0.669} = 5{,}735 \text{ kW}
$$

**Adding Sea Margin (15 %):**
$$
SHP_{MCR} = 1.15 \cdot 5{,}735 = 6{,}595 \text{ kW} \approx 6{,}600 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 12 — Recompute Fuel Capacity for DWT Revision**

Specific fuel oil consumption: $SFOC = 200$ g/kWh.
Round trip time: $t_{RT} = D_{RT} / V_S = 8{,}400 / 14 = 600$ hours.

Fuel for propulsion:
$$
W_{fuel,p} = \frac{SHP \cdot SFOC \cdot t_{RT}}{10^6} = \frac{5{,}735 \cdot 200 \cdot 600}{10^6} = 688.2 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Add auxiliary boiler fuel (~$3$ t/day for 30 days): $W_{fuel,a} = 90$ tonnes.
Reserve: $W_{res} = 0.1 \cdot (688.2 + 90) = 78$ tonnes.

Total fuel:
$$
W_{fuel} \approx 856 \text{ tonnes}
$$

DWT revised:
$$
DWT = 55{,}000 + 9.6 + 36 + 108 + 856 = 56{,}010 \text{ tonnes}
$$

This is consistent with the assumed DWT — design converges.

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Phase | OR Input | Output | Tool / Method |
|-------|----------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Cargo, route, speed | Mission profile | Tabular extraction |
| 2 | DWT, range | Principal dimensions | Ayre's $C_B$, volume eqn. |
| 3 | $V_S$, dimensions | EHP | Holtrop-Mennen / ITTC |
| 4 | EHP, $\eta_D$ | SHP | Propulsive coefficient |
| 5 | SHP, voyage time | Bunker capacity | $W_{fuel} = SFOC \cdot SHP \cdot t$ |
| 6 | All above | LWT, $\Delta$ | Empirical LWT ratio |

### 4.3 OR-to-Design Flow Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

### 4.4 Feedback Loop: OR Refinement During Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial OR] --> B[Feasibility Check]
    B -->|Feasible| C[Accept OR Proceed]
    B -->|Infeasible| D[OR Negotiation]
    D --> A
    C --> E[Design Iteration]
    E --> F[Final SDS]
    F --> G[Owner Approval]
    G -->|Approved| H[Build]
    G -->|Changes Requested| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note (KTU):** The above diagrams emphasize that OR is **iterative**, not static. This is a common board question: *"Why is owner's requirements formulation iterative and not a one-time activity?"* — Answer: because design feasibility, budget reality, and regulatory updates force renegotiation.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define "Owner's Requirements" in the context of ship design. List any four categories.**

**Model Answer:**
Owner's Requirements (OR) is the formal design brief prepared by the prospective shipowner specifying the mission, operational envelope, commercial limits, and regulatory constraints of the vessel. It is the primary input to the ship design process, which the naval architect converts into a Ship Design Specification.

Four categories:
1. Commercial requirements (trade type, budget, ROI)
2. Operational requirements (cargo, route, speed, crew)
3. Technical requirements (propulsion, materials, automation)
4. Regulatory requirements (class, flag state, conventions)

---

**Q2. Why is the formulation of Owner's Requirements considered the most critical step in ship design? [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
The Owner's Requirements phase is critical because:
1. **All subsequent design decisions** (principal dimensions, powering, stability, cost) are derived from OR.
2. **Cost of change is minimum at OR stage** but increases exponentially as design progresses.
3. **Contractual binding** — the OR becomes the basis of the shipbuilding contract.
4. **Scope creep prevention** — a well-defined OR prevents later disputes between owner and yard.
5. It determines the **design spiral's starting point**; errors in OR propagate through the entire design.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 1 Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1, Set A]**

*An owner requires a **Handysize bulk carrier** to transport **38,000 tonnes of coal** per voyage from **Kolkata to Haldia (and onwards to international ports)**, with **service speed of 13.5 knots**, **10 voyages per year**, and a **maximum crew of 22**. The vessel must comply with **IACS CSR** and operate under **Indian flag** (Class: IRS). The maximum allowable length is constrained by **Hooghly River bar** ($L \leq 180$ m, $B \leq 30$ m).*

*Estimate:*
*(a) The required deadweight and principal dimensions using Ayre's block coefficient formula. (7 marks)*
*(b) The effective horsepower and service shaft horsepower, with full derivation of all intermediate coefficients. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Required Deadweight and Principal Dimensions [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Extract Owner's Requirements** [1 mark]
- Cargo per voyage $W_c$ = 38,000 t
- Voyages/year $N_{voy}$ = 10
- Service speed $V_S$ = 13.5 knots
- Crew $N_{crew}$ = 22
- Length constraint $L \leq 180$ m
- Beam constraint $B \leq 30$ m

**Step 2: Compute Lightweight (Empirical)** [1 mark]
$$
DWT \approx 38{,}000 \text{ t (initial estimate)}
$$
$$
LWT \approx 0.18 \cdot DWT = 0.18 \cdot 38{,}000 = 6{,}840 \text{ t}
$$
$$
\Delta = 38{,}000 + 6{,}840 = 44{,}840 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Block Coefficient** [1 mark]
$$
C_B = 0.7 + 0.0025 \cdot V_S = 0.7 + 0.0025 \cdot 13.5 = 0.7338
$$

**Step 4: Volume-Displacement** [1 mark]
$$
\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{44{,}840}{1.025} = 43{,}746 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Apply $L/B$ and $B/T$ Ratios** [1 mark]
For Handysize: $L/B = 5.8$, $B/T = 2.4$

$$
L = 5.8 B, \quad T = B/2.4
$$

**Step 6: Solve for B** [1 mark]
$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T = C_B \cdot (5.8B) \cdot B \cdot (B/2.4)
$$
$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot \frac{5.8}{2.4} \cdot B^3 = 0.7338 \cdot 2.4167 \cdot B^3 = 1.7734 \cdot B^3
$$
$$
B^3 = \frac{43{,}746}{1.7734} = 24{,}670 \text{ m}^3
$$
$$
B = \sqrt[3]{24{,}670} = 29.05 \text{ m}
$$

Apply beam constraint $B \leq 30$ m: OK. Set $B = 30.0$ m (round to standard).

**Step 7: Compute L and T** [1 mark]
$$
L = 5.8 \cdot 30 = 174 \text{ m} \quad (\leq 180 \text{ m, OK})
$$
$$
T = 30/2.4 = 12.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Principal Dimensions:**
$$
\boxed{L_{pp} = 174 \text{ m}, \quad B = 30 \text{ m}, \quad T = 12.5 \text{ m}}
$$

---

**(b) EHP and SHP [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Velocity in m/s** [0.5 mark]
$$
V = 13.5 \cdot 0.5144 = 6.944 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Reynolds Number** [1 mark]
$$
R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu} = \frac{6.944 \cdot 174}{1.19 \cdot 10^{-6}} = 1.015 \cdot 10^9
$$

**Step 3: Friction Coefficient (ITTC 1978)** [1 mark]
$$
C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(9.0066 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{49.1} = 1.527 \cdot 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4: Wetted Surface (Trawler approximation)** [1 mark]
$$
S = 1.7 \cdot L \cdot T + \nabla/T = 1.7 \cdot 174 \cdot 12.5 + 43{,}746/12.5
$$
$$
S = 3{,}697.5 + 3{,}499.7 = 7{,}197 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5: Total Resistance Coefficient** [0.5 mark]
$$
C_T = (1 + k) \cdot C_F + C_R + C_A
$$
Assuming $1+k = 1.4$, $C_R = 0.4 \cdot 10^{-3}$, $C_A = 0.5 \cdot 10^{-3}$:
$$
C_T = 1.4 \cdot 1.527 \cdot 10^{-3} + 0.4 \cdot 10^{-3} + 0.5 \cdot 10^{-3} = 2.138 \cdot 10^{-3} + 0.9 \cdot 10^{-3} = 3.038 \cdot 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 6: Total Resistance** [1 mark]
$$
R_T = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_T
$$
$$
