R_T = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 6.944^2 \cdot 7{,}197 \cdot 3.038 \cdot 10^{-3}
$$
$$
R_T = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 48.22 \cdot 7{,}197 \cdot 3.038 \cdot 10^{-3}
$$
$$
R_T = 5.41 \cdot 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 541 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7: Effective Horsepower** [1 mark]
$$
EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{745.7} = \frac{541{,}000 \cdot 6.944}{745.7} = 5{,}036 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 8: SHP Calculation** [1 mark]
$$
\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \eta_{rot} \cdot \eta_M = 1.05 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 0.98 = 0.669
$$
$$
SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{5{,}036}{0.669} = 7{,}527 \text{ kW}
$$

**With 15% Sea Margin:**
$$
\boxed{SHP_{MCR} \approx 8{,}656 \text{ kW} \approx 11{,}605 \text{ BHP}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 1 Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1, Set B]**

*A prospective shipowner wishes to procure a **container feeder vessel** for **coastal Indian trade** (Mumbai–Kochi–Tuticorin triangle). The vessel must carry **750 TEU** with a **refrigerated plug capacity of 80 reefer plugs**, operate at **16 knots**, with **crew of 18**, and meet **IMO Tier II NOx** standards. The vessel must call at **Mormugao port** (max LOA = 200 m, max beam = 32 m).*

*(a) Formulate the **Owner's Requirements** as a structured design brief with at least **ten quantified parameters**. (7 marks)*
*(b) Discuss the **iterative nature** of OR formulation and explain how **regulatory and operational constraints** can force OR renegotiation. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structured Owner's Requirements Brief [7 marks]**

| # | Parameter | Value | Source/Justification |
|---|-----------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | Vessel type | Container feeder (coastal) | Trade profile |
| 2 | TEU capacity | 750 TEU | Cargo requirement |
| 3 | Reefer plugs | 80 units | Perishable cargo share |
| 4 | Service speed | 16 knots | Coastal schedule |
| 5 | Range | 1,800 nm | Triangle route ~ 1,500 nm + 20% margin |
| 6 | Crew complement | 18 persons | Indian manning norms |
| 7 | Max LOA | 200 m | Mormugao port limit |
| 8 | Max beam | 32 m | Mormugao port limit |
| 9 | Max draught | 9.0 m | Indian coastal draught |
| 10 | NOx emission | Tier II (130 g/kWh) | IMO Marpol Annex VI |
| 11 | Flag state | Indian (DG Shipping) | Coastal trade license |
| 12 | Class | IRS (Indian Register of Shipping) | IACS member |
| 13 | Automation | UMS (Unmanned Machinery Space) | Modern feeder norm |
| 14 | Annual voyages | 100 (bi-weekly) | Coastal trade schedule |
| 15 | Service life | 25 years | Standard coastal vessel |

[+ 2 marks for proper OR structuring, +5 marks for adequate parameter coverage]

---

**(b) Iterative Nature of OR Formulation [7 marks]**

**The OR Formulation Cycle [3.5 marks]:**

1. **Initial OR Drafting** — Owner states broad needs (cargo, speed, route).
2. **Feasibility Check** — Naval architect evaluates if requirements are physically and economically achievable.
3. **Trade-off Analysis** — e.g., 16 knots + 750 TEU may require larger engine → higher fuel cost → lower ROI.
4. **OR Renegotiation** — Owner and designer iterate: e.g., reducing speed to 15 knots may save 8% fuel cost.
5. **Final OR Lock** — Once a balanced design brief is signed off.

**Regulatory Constraints Triggering OR Change [2 marks]:**
- A new IMO regulation (e.g., **EEXI**, **CII**) coming into effect may force modification of speed, propulsion, or hull form — even after design is frozen.
- Example: From **Jan 2023**, EEXI forced many existing ships to limit engine power, effectively reducing service speed.

**Operational Constraints Triggering OR Change [1.5 marks]:**
- A new port restriction (e.g., deepening of a channel may suddenly allow longer vessels).
- Change in trade pattern (e.g., shift to a larger triangle) increases range requirement.
- Charter party demands (e.g., need for shaft generator) push technical OR update.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do not** mix up "Owner's Requirements" with "Ship Design Specification" — they are *different documents* (OR is input; SDS is output).
> 2. **Do not** forget to add **port/waterway restrictions** to OR — this is the most commonly missed parameter.
> 3. **Do not** present $C_B$ without justifying the formula (state it's Ayre's empirical for bulkers).
> 4. **Always** include the 15% sea margin in SHP — missing it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Always** quote units — examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Avoid** using the word "should" when stating OR — use "shall" for binding requirements.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Owner's Requirements):**

- ☐ **OR is the FIRST step** of the ship design spiral, not a derivative.
- ☐ **5 Categories:** Commercial, Operational, Technical, Regulatory, Environmental.
- ☐ **Output of OR phase = Design Brief / SDS**, NOT a drawing.
- ☐ **Ayre's $C_B$ formula** valid for bulkers only: $C_B = 0.7 + 0.0025 V_S$.
- ☐ **Standard $L/B$ for bulkers:** 5.5–7.0; **for tankers:** 5.5–6.5; **for containers:** 6.0–8.0.
- ☐ **Sea margin:** always **15%** (or as specified by class).
- ☐ **Port constraints** (Panamax, Suezmax, Hooghly bar) **always override** free-design dimensions.
- ☐ **LWT ≈ 0.18 × DWT** for bulkers; ≈ 0.22 × DWT for tankers; ≈ 0.30 × DWT for containers.
- ☐ **EHP formula:** $EHP = R_T \cdot V / 745.7$ (kW), with $R_T$ in Newtons and $V$ in m/s.
- ☐ **SHP = EHP / $\eta_D$**, where $\eta_D \approx 0.65$–$0.70$ for single-screw merchant ships.
- ☐ **Service speed** is at **85–90% MCR**, not full MCR.
- ☐ **Always** state **flag state, class society, and statutory basis** in OR.
- ☐ **OR must be iterative** — initial OR is rarely final OR.
- ☐ **Two ships with same OR can have different designs** if their *commercial* OR differs.
- ☐ **MCR** (Maximum Continuous Rating) is the contractual engine power; **CSR** is Continuous Service Rating.
- ☐ **Round-trip fuel** must be carried as part of DWT — drives main engine selection.
- ☐ **Reefer capacity** (e.g., 80 plugs) is part of **operational OR** for reefer ships.
- ☐ **Always** distinguish **trial speed** vs. **service speed** (sea margin adjustment).
- ☐ **SFOC** values: 170 g/kWh (modern 2-stroke MAN/B&W), 200 g/kWh (typical assumption).
- ☐ **Panamax:** $L \leq 294$ m, $B \leq 32.31$ m, $T \leq 12.04$ m.
- ☐ **Suezmax:** $DWT \leq 200{,}000$ t, $L \leq \leq 400$ m, $B \leq 50$ m, $T \leq 20.1$ m.
- ☐ **Aframax:** $DWT \approx 80{,}000$–$120{,}000$ t.
- ☐ **Handysize bulker:** $DWT \leq 35{,}000$–$40{,}000$ t.
- ☐ **Supramax bulker:** $DWT \approx 50{,}000$–$60{,}000$ t.
- ☐ **Capesize bulker:** $DWT \geq 100{,}000$ t (cannot pass Suez/Panama).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phases of Ship Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phases of Ship Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ MODULE 1 — PHASES OF SHIP DESIGN

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **Naval Architecture & Ship Design**, the **Phases of Ship Design** refer to the systematic, iterative, and progressively detailed sequence of engineering activities that transform an **Owner's operational requirement** into a fully dimensioned, buildable, and seaworthy vessel. Each phase refines the design from broad mission goals to specific structural scantlings, taking into account hydrostatics, hydrodynamics, structural strength, stability, propulsion, and regulatory compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"The design of a ship is not a single linear process but a series of well-defined phases — Concept Design, Preliminary (or Contract) Design, Detailed (Functional) Design, and Production (Working) Design — each characterized by a progressive narrowing of the design space, increasing data accuracy, and decreasing degrees of freedom in decision-making."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of designing a ship like planning and constructing a custom-built house, but with the house that must float, survive storms, cross oceans, and carry cargo efficiently.**

- **Concept Phase ≈ "Dreaming & Sketching on Paper":** You decide *what* kind of house you want — a 3-bedroom cottage, a beach villa, or a 50-floor apartment. For a ship, this means: a **Panamax bulk carrier**, a **VLCC tanker**, or a **military frigate**.
- **Preliminary Phase ≈ "Architect's Blueprint":** You lock in the layout, number of rooms, plot area, and approximate cost. For a ship, this means: the **general arrangement (GA)**, **principal dimensions (L, B, T, D)**, **block coefficient (C_B)**, and **propulsion plant selection**.
- **Detailed Phase ≈ "Engineer's Structural Drawings":** The structural engineer calculates beam sizes, column widths, and reinforcement. For a ship, this means: **scantlings** (plate thicknesses, frame spacing), **local structural details**, and **system designs (piping, electrical)**.
- **Production Phase ≈ "Contractor's Workshop Drawings":** Every nut, bolt, and weld is detailed for the builder. For a ship, this means: **shop-floor drawings**, **welding procedures**, and **assembly sequences**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Each phase is a *filter*. Early phases have many unknowns and broad decisions; later phases have fewer unknowns but demand much higher precision. Going backward (i.e., discovering in production that the ship cannot meet the contract speed) is **extremely expensive** — that's why KTU emphasizes the **design spiral** concept.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard KTU/Naval Architecture metrics govern the design phases:

- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** $g_f \approx \mathbf{170 \text{ g/kWh}}$ (for modern low-speed marine diesel engines).
- **Propulsive Coefficient (η_D):** Typically **0.55 to 0.65** for single-screw merchant ships.
- **Service Life:** Generally **25 to 30 years** for commercial vessels.
- **Block Coefficient (C_B):** Ranges from **0.55 to 0.85** depending on vessel type.
- **Design Margin:** A **5% to 15% safety margin** is retained on contract speed and deadweight.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔍 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Four (Five) Classical Phases

The design of a ship is governed by the principle of **progressive iteration**, often visualized as the famous **"Design Spiral"** introduced by Prof. J.H. Evans (1959). Each phase answers a specific set of questions and provides data for the next.

## 2.1 Phase I — Concept Design (Feasibility Study)

This is the **broadest and most creative phase**, where the naval architect works closely with the owner to translate a **mission requirement** into a **feasible ship concept**.

### Key Activities:
1. **Mission Profile Definition** — route, cargo, speed, endurance.
2. **Initial Sizing** — based on statistical regressions (e.g., **Ayre's method**).
3. **Concept General Arrangement (GA)** — block diagram of compartments.
4. **Cost Estimation** — very approximate, ±25% accuracy.
5. **Feasibility Check** — does the concept meet owner's requirements at an acceptable cost?

### Key Output:
A **Concept Design Report** containing 2–3 alternative designs with principal dimensions, estimated displacement, power, and cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** During Concept Design, the naval architect uses **parametric equations** (e.g., $L = k_1 \cdot V^{0.3} \cdot DWT^{0.3}$) derived from similar existing ships, called the **"parent ship"** or **"reference vessel"** approach.

## 2.2 Phase II — Preliminary Design (Contract Design)

This is the phase where the **owner and shipyard sign the contract**. It is therefore the most critical phase in commercial shipbuilding.

### Key Activities:
1. **Optimization of Principal Dimensions** — minimize required power or maximize deadweight for a given displacement.
2. **Lines Plan / Body Plan Development** — defines hull form.
3. **Resistance and Powering Calculations** — using **ITTC-78** or **Holtrop-Mennen** methods.
4. **Stability and Trim Analysis** — intact and damage stability per **SOLAS**.
5. **General Arrangement (GA) finalization** — tank capacities, machinery spaces, accommodations.
6. **Preliminary Cost Estimate** — ±10% accuracy.

### Key Output:
A **Specification Booklet** (the "Spec"), **Lines Plan**, **General Arrangement**, and **Midship Section** drawings. These are **contractually binding**.

## 2.3 Phase III — Detailed (Functional) Design

Once the contract is signed, the shipyard expands the preliminary design into a **fully engineered** package.

### Key Activities:
1. **Structural Scantling Calculations** — per **Classification Society Rules** (e.g., IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register).
2. **Local Strength Analysis** — finite element analysis (FEA) of openings, brackets, and transitions.
3. **System Design** — piping (HVAC, ballast, fuel), electrical (one-line diagram), and automation.
4. **Outfitting Details** — joiner work, insulation, coatings.
5. **Safety and Environmental Compliance** — SOLAS, MARPOL, IACS Unified Requirements.

### Key Output:
**Structural drawings**, **system schematics**, **piping diagrams**, and **equipment specifications**.

## 2.4 Phase IV — Production (Working) Design

This is the **workshop-ready** phase where every detail is provided to the fabrication shop.

### Key Activities:
1. **Workshop Drawings** — exact plate nesting, profile cutting plans.
2. **Assembly Sequences** — block construction strategy.
3. **Welding Procedures** — WPS (Welding Procedure Specifications).
4. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** — for procurement and inventory.

### Key Output:
**Cutting plans**, **assembly drawings**, and **manufacturing instructions**.

## 2.5 Phase V — Trials, Delivery, and Feedback

- **Builder's Trials** — measure actual speed, power, and maneuvering.
- **Sea Trials** — confirm contractual performance.
- **Delivery** — handover to owner.
- **Feedback Loop** — performance data feeds into the next ship design.

---

## 📊 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Meaning / Use** |
|---|---|---|
| **Displacement** | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Mass of water displaced; $\rho$ = 1.025 t/m³ (seawater) |
| **Block Coefficient** | $C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Hull fullness indicator |
| **Prismatic Coefficient** | $C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | Resistance optimization parameter |
| **Waterplane Coefficient** | $C_WP = \frac{A_W}{L \cdot B}$ | Stability parameter |
| **Midship Coefficient** | $C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Cross-section fullness |
| **Froude Number** | $F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$ | Wave resistance scaling |
| **Powering (Effective Power)** | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | $R_T$ = Total resistance (kN), $V$ in m/s |
| **Delivered Power** | $P_D = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S}$ | Includes propulsive and shaft efficiency |
| **Brake Power** | $P_B = \frac{P_D}{\eta_G}$ | Engine output power |
| **SFC-Based Fuel Rate** | $\dot{m}_f = SFC \cdot P_B$ | Tonnes/day fuel consumption |
| **Endurance Fuel** | $W_f = \dot{m}_f \cdot t_{endurance}$ | Total fuel for voyage |
| **Deadweight Ratio** | $DWR = \frac{DWT}{\Delta}$ | Cargo capacity efficiency |
| **Design Spiral Iteration** | $x_{i+1} = x_i + \Delta x$ | Each loop refines design variables |
| **Parametric Length (Ayre)** | $L_{WL} = k_1 \cdot V^{0.3} \cdot DWT^{0.3}$ | Initial sizing in Concept Phase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** In all phases, the **nondimensional coefficients** ($C_B, C_P, C_WP, C_M$) and **Froude Number ($F_n$)** are the primary tools for transferring knowledge from a parent ship to a new design.

---

## ⚙️ 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The phased design process is **not merely academic** — it is a **risk management and cost-control framework** used by shipyards worldwide (Mazagon Dock, Cochin Shipyard, Hyundai Heavy Industries, etc.). For example:
- A mistake discovered in **Concept Design** costs **\$1** to fix.
- The same mistake in **Preliminary Design** costs **\$10**.
- In **Detailed Design** it costs **\$100**.
- During **Production** it costs **\$1,000**.
- After **Delivery** (recall) it can cost **\$10,000+**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention the **cost-of-change factor** when explaining why design phasing matters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Parametric Sizing in Concept Phase (Ayre's Method)

In the **Concept Design phase**, principal dimensions are estimated using **statistical regressions** from a parent (reference) ship.

### Given:
- Desired **service speed** $V = 14$ knots
- Required **deadweight** $DWT = 35,000$ tonnes
- Parent ship: $L_{parent} = 170$ m, $V_{parent} = 15$ knots, $DWT_{parent} = 40,000$ t

### Ayre's Equation (statistical):
$$L_{WL} = L_{parent} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{V_{parent}}\right)^{0.3} \cdot \left(\frac{DWT}{DWT_{parent}}\right)^{0.3}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the speed ratio term:**

$$\left(\frac{V}{V_{parent}}\right)^{0.3} = \left(\frac{14}{15}\right)^{0.3}$$

$$\frac{14}{15} = 0.9333$$

$$0.9333^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \cdot \ln(0.9333)}$$

$$\ln(0.9333) = -0.0690$$

$$0.3 \cdot (-0.0690) = -0.0207$$

$$e^{-0.0207} = 0.9795$$

**Step 2 — Compute the deadweight ratio term:**

$$\left(\frac{DWT}{DWT_{parent}}\right)^{0.3} = \left(\frac{35,000}{40,000}\right)^{0.3}$$

$$\frac{35,000}{40,000} = 0.875$$

$$0.875^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \cdot \ln(0.875)}$$

$$\ln(0.875) = -0.1335$$

$$0.3 \cdot (-0.1335) = -0.0401$$

$$e^{-0.0401} = 0.9607$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both factors:**

$$0.9795 \times 0.9607 = 0.9410$$

**Step 4 — Apply to parent ship length:**

$$L_{WL} = 170 \times 0.9410$$

$$L_{WL} = 159.97 \text{ m} \approx 160 \text{ m}$$

### Result:
$$\boxed{L_{WL} \approx 160 \text{ m}}$$

This is the **waterline length** for the new ship, derived in the **Concept Phase** without any hydrodynamic calculations — purely from the parent ship analogy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Powering in Preliminary Phase (Effective Power → Brake Power)

### Given:
- Calculated resistance: $R_T = 800$ kN at service speed $V = 14$ knots
- Propulsive coefficient: $\eta_D = 0.60$
- Shafting efficiency: $\eta_S = 0.98$
- Gearbox efficiency: $\eta_G = 0.97$

### Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s:
$$V = 14 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 7.20 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Effective Power ($P_E$):
$$P_E = R_T \cdot V$$

$$P_E = 800 \text{ kN} \times 7.20 \text{ m/s}$$

$$P_E = 5760 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Delivered Power ($P_D$):
$$P_D = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S}$$

$$P_D = \frac{5760}{0.60 \times 0.98}$$

$$P_D = \frac{5760}{0.588}$$

$$P_D = 9795.9 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Brake Power ($P_B$):
$$P_B = \frac{P_D}{\eta_G}$$

$$P_B = \frac{9795.9}{0.97}$$

$$P_B = 10098.9 \text{ kW} \approx 10.1 \text{ MW}$$

$$\boxed{P_B \approx 10,100 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is the power the **engine** must deliver. A safety margin of **10–15%** is added to specify the engine rating. So the actual MCR (Maximum Continuous Rating) = ~11,500 kW. This engine selection happens in the **Preliminary Design phase**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Inputs → Outputs of Each Design Phase

| **Design Phase** | **Key Inputs** | **Key Outputs** | **Accuracy of Cost Estimate** | **Major Tools / Codes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Concept Design** | Owner requirements, mission profile, cargo, route, speed | Principal dimensions, displacement, power, cost range | ±25% | Parametric regressions, parent ship analogy |
| **Preliminary (Contract) Design** | Concept report, owner's spec, classification rules | Lines Plan, GA, Spec, midship section, contract price | ±10% | Holtrop-Mennen, ITTC-78, SOLAS, MARPOL |
| **Detailed (Functional) Design** | Contract drawings, rules, owner approvals | Structural drawings, system diagrams, FEA, FDS | ±5% | FEA (ANSYS, NASTRAN), IACS UR, Class rules |
| **Production (Working) Design** | Detailed drawings, yard standards | Workshop drawings, cutting plans, WPS, BOM | ±2% | CAD/CAM nesting software, welding standards |
| **Trials & Delivery** | Completed vessel | Trial data, performance curves, as-built drawings | Final actual cost | Sea trial instruments, inclining test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The matrix above is a **favorite exam question** in Module 1. Students are often asked to *"Compare the design phases in terms of accuracy, output, and the kind of decisions made."*

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Deadweight Ratio and Displacement Check

### Given:
- Deadweight $DWT = 35,000$ t
- Lightweight $LWT = 8,000$ t

### Displacement:
$$\Delta = DWT + LWT$$

$$\Delta = 35,000 + 8,000 = 43,000 \text{ t}$$

### Deadweight Ratio:
$$DWR = \frac{DWT}{\Delta}$$

$$DWR = \frac{35,000}{43,000}$$

$$DWR = 0.814$$

$$\boxed{DWR = 0.814 \text{ (or 81.4\%)}}$$

This is a typical DWR for a **bulk carrier** (0.75–0.85), confirming the design is in the correct range. This check is done in the **Preliminary Phase**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Design Spiral (Evans, 1959) — Iterative Concept Visualization

The classical representation of how a ship's design evolves is the **Design Spiral**, where each "loop" represents a refinement of the principal parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Owner Mission Requirement] --> B[Phase 1: Concept Design]
    B --> C[Define Principal Dimensions L B T D]
    C --> D[Estimate Displacement and Power]
    D --> E[Phase 2: Preliminary Design]
    E --> F[Resistance and Powering Calculation]
    F --> G[Stability and Trim Analysis]
    G --> H{Design Meets Contract?}
    H -- No --> C
    H -- Yes --> I[Phase 3: Detailed Design]
    I --> J[Structural Scantlings per Class Rules]
    J --> K[System Design Piping Electrical]
    K --> L[Phase 4: Production Design]
    L --> M[Workshop Drawings and BOM]
    M --> N[Phase 5: Construction and Trials]
    N --> O[Feedback to Next Ship Design]
    O --> A
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **red diamond node** in the middle is the **decision gate** — if the design fails any criterion (speed, stability, cost), the loop goes back to the **Concept Phase** for refinement. This is the essence of the **iterative nature** of ship design.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Phased Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Block]
        I1[Owner Mission Requirements]
        I2[Route and Cargo Data]
        I3[Regulatory Constraints SOLAS MARPOL]
        I4[Classification Society Rules]
    end
    subgraph PHASE1[Phase 1 Concept]
        P1A[Parametric Sizing]
        P1B[Concept GA]
        P1C[Cost Range]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2[Phase 2 Preliminary]
        P2A[Lines Plan]
        P2B[Resistance and Powering]
        P2C[Stability and Trim]
        P2D[Contract Specification]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3[Phase 3 Detailed]
        P3A[Structural Scantlings]
        P3B[Piping and Electrical]
        P3C[FEA and Local Strength]
    end
    subgraph PHASE4[Phase 4 Production]
        P4A[Workshop Drawings]
        P4B[Nesting and Cutting]
        P4C[Assembly Plan]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Block]
        O1[As Built Ship]
        O2[Trial Data]
        O3[Feedback to Next Design]
    end
    I1 --> P1A
    I2 --> P1A
    I3 --> P1B
    I4 --> P3A
    P1A --> P1B
    P1B --> P1C
    P1C --> P2A
    P2A --> P2B
    P2A --> P2C
    P2B --> P2D
    P2C --> P2D
    P2D --> P3A
    P3A --> P3B
    P3A --> P3C
    P3B --> P4A
    P3C --> P4A
    P4A --> P4B
    P4A --> P4C
    P4B --> O1
    P4C --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> P1A
    style INPUT fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px
    style PHASE1 fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style PHASE2 fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    style PHASE3 fill:#FFE6F0,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px
    style PHASE4 fill:#F0E6FF,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    style OUTPUT fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** The arrows going **left to right** show the **flow of information** between phases. The arrow from `O3 → P1A` (red curved arrow in the conceptual sense) represents the **feedback loop** — real-world data from one ship becomes the **parent ship** for the next design. This is the **heart of the Design Spiral**.

---

## 4.3 Cost-of-Change vs. Design Phase (Bar-Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Phase 1 Concept] -->|Cost 1x| B[Phase 2 Preliminary]
    B -->|Cost 10x| C[Phase 3 Detailed]
    C -->|Cost 100x| D[Phase 4 Production]
    D -->|Cost 1000x| E[Phase 5 Trials Delivery]
    E -->|Cost 10000x| F[Post Delivery Recall]
    style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#8B0000,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#000000,stroke:#FF0000,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pearl:** This exponential cost-of-change curve is a **classic viva question**. Be ready to explain why early design decisions dominate the lifecycle cost of a ship.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 🅰️ PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Briefly explain the Concept Design phase of a ship. List its key outputs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Concept Design is the **first and broadest phase** of ship design where the naval architect works with the owner to translate the mission requirement into a feasible ship concept.

**Key Activities (1 mark):**
- Mission profile definition (route, cargo, speed).
- Initial sizing using parent ship analogy or parametric equations.
- Concept General Arrangement (block diagram).
- Preliminary cost estimation.

**Key Outputs (1 mark):**
- Concept Design Report with **2–3 alternative designs**.
- Principal dimensions (L, B, T, D).
- Estimated displacement, power, and cost range (accuracy ±25%).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the "Design Spiral"? Why is ship design considered iterative?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **Design Spiral**, introduced by Prof. J.H. Evans in 1959, is a graphical representation of the iterative process where each loop represents a refinement of principal parameters such as length, beam, draft, displacement, and power.

**Why iterative (1.5 marks):**
- The design variables (L, B, T, C_B, P_E) are **interdependent** — changing one affects the others.
- Hydrodynamic, structural, stability, and economic constraints must all be satisfied **simultaneously**.
- A closed-form solution does not exist; we approximate iteratively.

**Key takeaway (0.5 mark):** The spiral converges to an **optimal design** satisfying all constraints.

---

## 🅱️ PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **Preliminary (Contract) Design phase** of a ship. List its activities, outputs, and the role of the **General Arrangement (GA)** drawing.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With a suitable example, explain the **parent ship analogy** method used in the Concept Design phase to estimate the principal dimensions of a new ship.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Preliminary Design (also called **Contract Design**) is the phase in which the **concept is refined to a level sufficient for signing a binding shipbuilding contract** between the owner and the shipyard.

**2. Key Activities (3 marks):**
- Optimization of **principal dimensions** ($L_{WL}, B, T, D$) for minimum powering or maximum deadweight.
- Development of the **Lines Plan / Body Plan** (offsets table).
- **Resistance and powering prediction** using ITTC-78 or Holtrop-Mennen methods.
- **Intact and damage stability** analysis per IMO SOLAS regulations.
- Finalization of **machinery selection** (main engine, gearbox, propeller).
- Preparation of the **Midship Section** drawing.
- Cost estimation with **±10% accuracy**.

**3. Role of the General Arrangement (GA) (2 marks):**
The **GA drawing** is a top-view (and side/end views) showing the **location and size of every compartment** — cargo holds, tanks, engine room, accommodations, navigation bridge, etc. It ensures:
- Adequate **subdivision** for damage stability.
- Efficient **cargo flow** and **crew movement**.
- Compliance with **class rules and statutory requirements**.

**4. Key Outputs (1 mark):**
- Lines Plan
- General Arrangement (GA)
- Midship Section
- **Specification Booklet** (the contract document)
- Preliminary cost estimate

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**1. Concept of Parent Ship (2 marks):**
The **parent ship** is an **existing, similar vessel** whose performance, dimensions, and coefficients are well-documented. The new design starts by **scaling the parent ship** using statistical relationships.

**2. Parametric Equation (2 marks) — Ayre's Method:**

$$L_{WL,new} = L_{WL,parent} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{new}}{V_{parent}}\right)^{0.3} \cdot \left(\frac{DWT_{new}}{DWT_{parent}}\right)^{0.3}$$

$$B_{new} = B_{parent} \cdot \left(\frac{DWT_{new}}{DWT_{parent}}\right)^{0.15}$$

**3. Worked Example (2 marks):**
A new bulk carrier with $V = 14$ knots, $DWT = 35{,}000$ t is to be designed. The parent ship has $L = 170$ m, $V = 15$ knots, $DWT = 40{,}000$ t.

$$\left(\frac{14}{15}\right)^{0.3} = 0.9795$$

$$\left(\frac{35{,}000}{40{,}000}\right)^{0.3} = 0.9607$$

$$L_{WL,new} = 170 \times 0.9795 \times 0.9607 \approx 160 \text{ m}$$

**4. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Only valid for **similar ship types** (e.g., bulk carrier to bulk carrier).
- Cannot be used for **radically new designs** (e.g., SWATH, air-cushion vehicles).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Detailed Design phase** of a ship. Discuss the role of **Classification Society Rules** (e.g., IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register) in this phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bulk carrier has a calculated total resistance $R_T = 800$ kN at service speed $V = 14$ knots. The propulsive coefficient is $\eta_D = 0.60$, shaft efficiency $\eta_S = 0.98$, and gearbox efficiency $\eta_G = 0.97$. Calculate the **Effective Power, Delivered Power, and Brake Power** required.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Detailed (Functional) Design is the phase in which the **preliminary design is expanded into a fully engineered package** ready for production. Every plate, frame, pipe, and cable is dimensioned and specified.

**2. Key Activities (3 marks):**
- **Structural scantlings** — plate thickness, frame spacing, girder sizes (per class rules).
- **Local strength analysis** using **FEA** (e.g., ANSYS, NASTRAN) for openings, brackets, and superstructure transitions.
- **System design** — piping (ballast, fuel, HVAC), electrical (one-line diagram), automation.
- **Outfitting** — joiner work, insulation, cathodic protection.
- **Safety and environmental compliance** — SOLAS, MARPOL, IACS Unified Requirements.

**3. Role of Classification Society Rules (2 marks):**
Classification Societies (e.g., **IRS — Indian Register of Shipping, DNV, Lloyd's Register, BV, ABS, KR**) are independent bodies that:
- Provide **rules for structural scantlings** based on empirical formulas and theoretical analysis.
- Conduct **surveys during construction** to ensure compliance.
- Issue the **Classification Certificate**, which is **mandatory for insurance and port entry**.
- For example, **IRS Rules for Bulk Carriers** specify minimum shell plate thickness, double-bottom height, hopper tank dimensions, etc.

**4. Outputs (1 mark):**
Structural drawings, system schematics, FEA reports, and Class-approved drawings.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
$R_T = 800$ kN, $V = 14$ knots, $\eta_D = 0.60$, $\eta_S = 0.98$, $\eta_G = 0.97$.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s:**
$$V = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.20 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Power:**
$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 800 \times 7.20 = 5760 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Delivered Power:**
$$P_D = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S} = \frac{5760}{0.60 \times 0.98} = \frac{5760}{0.588} = 9795.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Brake Power:**
$$P_B = \frac{P_D}{\eta_G} = \frac{9795.9}{0.97} = 10{,}098.9 \text{ kW} \approx 10.1 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5 — Engine Selection (with margin):**
Adding 15% sea margin: $MCR = 10{,}099 \times 1.15 \approx 11{,}614$ kW.

**Result:** $\boxed{P_E = 5760 \text{ kW}, \quad P_D = 9796 \text{ kW}, \quad P_B = 10{,}099 \text{ kW}}$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Module 1 — Phases of Ship Design):**
> 
> **1. Confusing "Contract Design" with "Detailed Design":** Contract Design = Preliminary Design (the phase where the contract is signed). Detailed Design comes **after** the contract. Many students lose **2–3 marks** by mixing these up.
> 
> **2. Forgetting Units in Power Calculations:** Power must be in **kW or MW** and speed in **m/s** (not knots) when using $P = R \cdot V$. Forgetting the **0.5144 conversion factor** for knots → m/s is a very common error.
> 
> **3. Not Mentioning the Design Spiral:** When asked *"Why is ship design iterative?"*, students often give a vague answer. You **must** mention the **Design Spiral (Evans, 1959)** by name and explain the **interdependence of variables**.
> 
> **4. Confusing LWT and DWT:** **Lightweight (LWT)** = empty ship weight (steel + outfit). **Deadweight (DWT)** = cargo + fuel + crew + stores. **Displacement** $\Delta = LWT + DWT$. Mixing these up loses marks.
> 
> **5. Skipping the Cost-of-Change Argument:** When explaining *why* phased design is used, the **exponential cost growth** with design phase is a **bonus point** answer. Don't miss it.
> 
> **6. Forgetting Classification Societies:** Detailed Design is **incomplete** without mentioning **Class Rules (IRS, DNV, LR)**. Always include them.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ⚓ **Five Phases:** Concept → Preliminary (Contract) → Detailed (Functional) → Production (Working) → Trials & Delivery.
- ⚓ **Design Spiral (Evans, 1959):** The iterative nature — variables are interdependent; no closed-form solution.
- ⚓ **Parent Ship Analogy (Ayre's Method):** $L_{new} = L_{parent} \cdot (V_{new}/V_{parent})^{0.3} \cdot (DWT_{new}/DWT_{parent})^{0.3}$ — used in **Concept Phase**.
- ⚓ **Key Outputs by Phase:**
  - *Concept:* Dimensions, displacement, cost range (±25%).
  - *Preliminary:* Lines Plan, GA, Spec, Midship Section (±10%).
  - *Detailed:* Structural scantlings, FEA, system diagrams.
  - *Production:* Workshop drawings, BOM, WPS.
- ⚓ **Cost-of-Change Factor:** Multiplies by **10x per phase** — early design decisions dominate lifecycle cost.
- ⚓ **Key Formulas:**
  - $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ (seawater: $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³)
  - $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$
  - $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$
  - $P_E = R_T \cdot V$, $P_D = P_E / (\eta_D \eta_S)$, $P_B = P_D / \eta_G$
- ⚓ **Classification Societies:** IRS, DNV, LR, ABS, BV, KR — provide rules for scantlings and certify the vessel.
- ⚓ **Regulatory Bodies:** **SOLAS** (safety), **MARPOL** (pollution), **IACS** (classification society umbrella).
- ⚓ **DWR** = $DWT / \Delta$ → typically **0.75–0.85** for bulk carriers, **0.60–0.70** for tankers.
- ⚓ **Speed Conversion:** **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s** — memorize this!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Spiral
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Spiral

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Spiral Definition (Woodward, 1880):**
> *"The Design Spiral is an iterative, non-linear decision-making framework that visually and conceptually represents the sequential and recursive process by which a naval architect transforms an owner's operational requirement into a buildable, economically viable, and seaworthy vessel. Each revolution around the spiral represents a cycle of refinement where every major design decision influences, and is influenced by, every other decision."*

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Ship Design I**, the Design Spiral is treated as the foundational philosophical model of naval architecture. It was originally proposed by **Professor John B. Woodward** in 1880 and later refined by leading ship design authorities such as **David G. M. Watson**, **Eyres (Ship Construction)**, and **Lewis (Principles of Naval Architecture)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cake Baking" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine you are baking a layered cake for a customer. The customer says: *"I want a cake that serves 100 people, looks beautiful, costs under ₹2000, and tastes like chocolate."*
>
> You don't just mix, bake, and decorate in one straight line. Instead:
> - You pick the **pan size** (similar to choosing the ship's principal dimensions).
> - Then you check if the **ingredient cost** fits the budget.
> - Then you check if your **oven** (the shipyard) can handle that size.
> - Then you reconsider the **number of layers** (structural arrangement).
> - And you keep **going round and round**, refining each choice until the cake is perfect.
>
> Each "round" of the spiral is one full revolution — and by the time you finish the cake, you have probably gone around the spiral **3 to 5 times**, with each loop producing a more refined, more realistic, and more detailed design.

In ship design, every "loop" of the spiral brings the design closer to reality:
- **Loop 1:** Rough concept (Lines plan, principal dimensions, deadweight).
- **Loop 2:** Feasibility check (Stability, powering, structural strength).
- **Loop 3:** Refinement (Hull form optimization, general arrangement).
- **Loop 4 & 5:** Detailed engineering and production planning.

### 1.3 Why the Spiral is NOT a Straight Line

The fundamental reason the design process is depicted as a **spiral** rather than a **flowchart** is that:

$$\boxed{\text{Every design decision is coupled to every other decision.}}$$

For example, increasing the **ship's length (L)** to improve speed reduces **resistance (R)** but increases **structural weight (W)** and **harbor maneuvering problems**, which in turn affects the **engine power required (BHP)**, which affects the **fuel capacity**, which affects the **displacement (Δ)**, which brings us back to reconsider **length and breadth**.

This **non-linear, cyclic dependency** is what creates the spiral.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in the Spiral

- **Displacement (Δ):** measured in **tonnes (t)** or **m³**
- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP):** measured in **metres (m)**
- **Service Speed (Vs):** measured in **knots (kn)**
- **Block Coefficient (Cb):** dimensionless, typically **0.55 – 0.85**
- **Froude Number (Fn):** dimensionless, **0.10 – 0.40** for displacement hulls
- **Reynolds Number (Rn):** typically **10⁸ – 10⁹** for ships
- **Sea Water Density (ρ):** **1.025 t/m³** (standard KTU reference value)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect students to recognize that the Design Spiral is **not a literal physical spiral drawn on paper** — it is a **conceptual model** used to teach the iterative nature of design decision-making.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Philosophical Foundation of the Design Spiral

The Design Spiral rests on three core naval-architecture principles:

1. **Multi-Variable Coupling** — No ship parameter is independent. There are at least **40–60 primary design variables** that interact simultaneously.
2. **Iteration Over Convergence** — A ship design is never "finished" in one pass; it converges asymptotically toward an optimal solution.
3. **Cost-Performance Trade-off** — Every gain in performance, capacity, or comfort has a corresponding penalty in cost, weight, or resistance.

### 2.2 The Twelve Major Design Activities (Woodward's Original Steps)

The classic **Woodward Design Spiral** identifies the following twelve interlinked design activities (sometimes grouped into 8–10 in modern texts):

| Sl. No. | Design Activity | Output / Decision |
|:-------:|:----------------|:------------------|
| 1 | Mission Requirements | Mission profile, route, cargo type |
| 2 | Principal Dimensions | LBP, B, D, T |
| 3 | Hull Form (Lines Plan) | Cb, Cm, Cw, Cp |
| 4 | General Arrangement |舱室布局, deck plan |
| 5 | Resistance & Propulsion | EHP, SHP, propeller selection |
| 6 | Powering & Fuel | Main engine, fuel capacity |
| 7 | Stability | GM, GZ curve, freeboard |
| 8 | Structural Design | Plate thickness, frame spacing |
| 9 | Weight Estimate | Lightweight vs. Deadweight split |
| 10 | Cost Estimate | Newbuilding price, CAPEX, OPEX |
| 11 | Statutory & Class | SOLAS, MARPOL, IACS rules |
| 12 | Production Planning | Build strategy, shipyard facilities |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **logical sequence** is shown above, but in reality, the designer jumps between activities in a non-sequential, iterative fashion — hence the **spiral**.

### 2.3 The Modern "Hybrid" / Parallel Design Spiral

Modern ship design practice (post-1990s, per Watson's *Practical Ship Design*) uses a **hybrid spiral** where certain parallel activities (e.g., hull form and structural design) are progressed simultaneously, then merged.

```
Traditional Sequential (1880)  →  Spiral (Woodward)  →  Hybrid Parallel (Modern)
        ↓                              ↓                          ↓
   One-way flow               Iterative refinement       Concurrent + Iterative
```

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Equations Used During Spiral Iterations

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are repeatedly applied **inside** each spiral revolution to check feasibility at that stage of design.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \rho \cdot \nabla \\
\nabla &= L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B \\
C_B &= \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} \\
F_n &= \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}} \\
R_T &= R_F + R_R + R_A + R_W \\
EHP &= \frac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75} \quad \text{(metric HP)} \\
SHP &= \frac{EHP}{\eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R} \\
GM &= KM - KG \\
P_{B} &= 0.65 \cdot \frac{\Delta \cdot L_{BP}}{100} \quad \text{(Bennett's Bending Moment proxy)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.5 Boundary Conditions / Acceptable Design Ranges (KTU Reference Table)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Units |
|:----------|:------:|:--------------|:------|
| Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | **0.55 – 0.85** | dimensionless |
| Midship Coefficient | $C_M$ | **0.90 – 0.99** | dimensionless |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P$ | **0.55 – 0.85** | dimensionless |
| Waterplane Coefficient | $C_W$ | **0.65 – 0.90** | dimensionless |
| Length-to-Beam Ratio | L/B | **5.0 – 8.0** | dimensionless |
| Beam-to-Draft Ratio | B/T | **2.0 – 3.5** | dimensionless |
| Speed-Length Ratio | $V_s / \sqrt{L}$ | **0.40 – 1.40** | dimensionless |
| Froude Number | $F_n$ | **0.10 – 0.40** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students know **which range is acceptable for which ship type** (e.g., tankers → high Cb ≈ 0.80–0.85; container ships → moderate Cb ≈ 0.60–0.70; naval combatants → low Cb ≈ 0.45–0.55).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Design Spiral is not just academic — it directly influences:

1. **Shipyard Production Planning** — Build strategy is locked in early spiral loops; mistakes are very expensive to fix later.
2. **Classification Society Approvals** — Class surveyors (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require iterative proof of safety at each design stage.
3. **Cost Estimation Accuracy** — Each spiral loop progressively refines cost estimates from ±30% (concept) to ±5% (contract design).
4. **Risk Management** — Identifying design risks early in the spiral avoids late-stage redesign penalties.
5. **Digital Twin Development** — Modern CAD/CAE tools essentially automate the spiral, running thousands of iterations computationally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Logical Order of the Design Spiral

The classical spiral order is derived from the **dependency hierarchy** of design variables. We proceed as follows:

**Step 1 — Start from the Mission:**
The mission defines the **Required Deadweight (DWT)**, **Service Speed (Vs)**, and **Range (R)**.

$$
\text{Mission} \rightarrow \{DWT, V_s, R\}
$$

**Step 2 — Translate Mission into Principal Dimensions:**
Using historical data (Rechnitzer, Schneekluth) and statistical regression:

$$
L_{BP} = f_1(DWT, V_s)
$$
$$
B = f_2(L_{BP}, C_B)
$$
$$
D = f_3(\text{freeboard, deck house})
$$
$$
T = f_4(\text{draft constraints, displacement})
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Displacement:**
$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$
This is **Step 3 of every spiral revolution**, because once dimensions are estimated, displacement follows.

**Step 4 — Compute Hydrostatic & Resistance Parameters:**
$$
R_T(V_s) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V_s^2 \cdot S \cdot C_T
$$
where $S$ is the wetted surface area and $C_T$ is the total resistance coefficient.

**Step 5 — Compute Powering:**
$$
EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75} \quad \text{(in metric HP)}
$$
$$
SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_{Total}}
$$
where the total propulsive coefficient is:
$$
\eta_{Total} = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R
$$

**Step 6 — Stability Check (In each spiral revolution):**
$$
GM = KM - KG
$$
where:
- $KM = T/2 + (B^2)/(11.4 \cdot T)$ — approximated for box-shaped vessels
- $KG$ is found from weight distribution

If $GM < GM_{min}$, the spiral **loops back** to revise B or T.

**Step 7 — Structural & Cost Iteration:**
$$
\text{Steel Weight} = k \cdot \Delta^{2/3} \cdot L_{BP}
$$
where $k$ is a ship-type coefficient. The cost per tonne of steel is then applied.

**Step 8 — Convergence Check:**
If all major criteria (stability, power, cost, deadweight) are satisfied within tolerance, the spiral is exited. Otherwise, return to Step 2.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The Spiral as a Mathematical Iteration

We can express one revolution of the spiral as a vector mapping:

$$
\vec{X}_{n+1} = \mathbf{F}(\vec{X}_n)
$$

where:
- $\vec{X}_n = [L_{BP}, B, T, D, C_B, \Delta, V_s, BHP, GM, W_t]^T$ is the design state vector at iteration $n$.
- $\mathbf{F}$ is the **design operator** that maps one design state to the next.

**Convergence criterion:**

$$
\| \vec{X}_{n+1} - \vec{X}_n \| < \epsilon
$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small design tolerance vector. Typical tolerance values are:

| Variable | Typical Tolerance $\epsilon$ |
|:---------|:------------------------------|
| $L_{BP}$ | ±0.5 m |
| $B$ | ±0.2 m |
| $T$ | ±0.1 m |
| $C_B$ | ±0.005 |
| $\Delta$ | ±2% |
| $EHP$ | ±3% |
| $GM$ | ±0.05 m |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — First Revolution of the Spiral

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A bulk carrier is to carry 50,000 DWT at 14 knots. Perform the **first revolution** of the design spiral.

**Step 1: Estimate LBP using Schneekluth's formula for bulk carriers:**
$$
L_{BP} = 5.43 \cdot (DWT)^{1/3} \cdot V_s^{0.3}
$$

Let us substitute the values:
$$
L_{BP} = 5.43 \cdot (50000)^{1/3} \cdot (14)^{0.3}
$$

We evaluate the cube root first:
$$
(50000)^{1/3} \approx 36.84
$$

Then the speed term:
$$
(14)^{0.3} \approx 2.20
$$

Therefore:
$$
L_{BP} \approx 5.43 \cdot 36.84 \cdot 2.20
$$

Computing the product:
$$
L_{BP} \approx 5.43 \cdot 81.05 \approx 440.1 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Estimate B using L/B ratio for bulk carriers (≈ 6.0):**
$$
B = \frac{L_{BP}}{L/B} = \frac{440.1}{6.0} \approx 73.4 \text{ m}
$$

But B must also satisfy **panama-x fitting** for bulk carriers (B ≤ 32.3 m is wrong for bulk; for Capesize B is large). Using real data for Capesize bulkers:
$$
B \approx 65 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Estimate T (draft) using DWT-based regression:**
$$
T = 0.0135 \cdot L_{BP}^{0.5} + 3.5
$$
$$
T = 0.0135 \cdot \sqrt{440.1} + 3.5
$$
$$
T = 0.0135 \cdot 20.98 + 3.5 \approx 0.283 + 3.5 \approx 3.78 \text{ m?}
$$

This is unrealistically low — indicating the empirical formula chosen is for smaller vessels. For a 50,000 DWT bulker, the realistic loaded draft is **T ≈ 12.5 m**, and the design draft is **T_d ≈ 11.0 m**.

**Step 4: Set C_B ≈ 0.78 (typical for bulk carrier).**

**Step 5: Compute displacement:**
$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$
$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 440 \cdot 65 \cdot 11 \cdot 0.78
$$

Compute incrementally:
$$
440 \cdot 65 = 28,600
$$
$$
28,600 \cdot 11 = 314,600
$$
$$
314,600 \cdot 0.78 = 245,388
$$
$$
245,388 \cdot 1.025 = 251,523 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 6: Verify DWT:**
$$
DWT = \Delta - LWT \approx 251,523 - 25,000 \approx 226,523 \text{ t}
$$

This is **much higher** than the required 50,000 t — meaning the spiral needs to **loop back** to Step 1 and **reduce dimensions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lesson:** This numerical example shows exactly why the design is a **spiral** — the first loop almost never gives a correct answer. The designer must iterate, typically 3–5 times, before dimensions converge.

### 3.4 The Three Phases of Modern Spiral Design

| Phase | Loop Range | Key Output | KTU Typical Mark Allocation |
|:------|:----------:|:-----------|:---------------------------|
| **Concept / Preliminary Design** | Loops 1–2 | Principal dimensions, hull form, general arrangement | 7 marks |
| **Contract Design** | Loops 3–4 | Final lines plan, structural design, cost estimate, stability book | 14 marks |
| **Detail / Production Design** | Loops 5+ | Workshop drawings, machining data, classification-approved plans | Not in KTU Module 1 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Classical Woodward Design Spiral

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Mission Requirements] --> B[Principal Dimensions L B T D]
    B --> C[Displacement Calculation]
    C --> D[Weight Estimate DWT and LWT]
    D --> E[Hull Form Lines Plan Cb Cm Cp]
    E --> F[Resistance and Powering EHP SHP]
    F --> G[Stability Check GM GZ Curve]
    G --> H[Structural Design Plate Frames]
    H --> I[General Arrangement Decks Tanks]
    I --> J[Cost Estimation]
    J --> K{Converged?}
    K -- No --> L[Loop back revise L B T]
    L --> B
    K -- Yes --> M[Final Design Output: Lines GA Structure Cost]
    M --> N[End]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Interaction Matrix of Major Design Variables

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Length LBP] --> P3[Displacement Delta]
    P2[Beam B] --> P3
    P3 --> P4[Cb]
    P4 --> P5[Resistance RT]
    P2 --> P6[GM Stability]
    P5 --> P7[EHP]
    P7 --> P8[SHP Engine Selection]
    P8 --> P9[Fuel Capacity]
    P9 --> P3
    P6 --> P10[Freeboard Check]
    P3 --> P11[Steel Weight]
    P11 --> P12[Cost Estimate]
    P12 --> P13{Budget OK}
    P13 -- No --> P1
    P13 -- Yes --> P14[Class Approval]
    P14 --> P15[Production Plan]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Three Phases of Spiral Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PH1[Phase 1: Concept Preliminary] --> PH1A[Loops 1 to 2]
    PH1A --> PH1B[Output: Principal Dimensions and Hull Form]
    PH1B --> PH2[Phase 2: Contract Design]
    PH2 --> PH2A[Loops 3 to 4]
    PH2A --> PH2B[Output: Final Lines GA Structure Cost]
    PH2B --> PH3[Phase 3: Detail Design]
    PH3 --> PH3A[Loops 5 and beyond]
    PH3A --> PH3B[Output: Workshop Drawings Class Approved Plans]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Modern Hybrid Parallel Spiral

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stream 1 Hull Form Hydrostatics] --> M[Merge Point]
    S2[Stream 2 Structural Strength] --> M
    S3[Stream 3 Machinery and Outfitting] --> M
    S4[Stream 4 Cost and Risk] --> M
    M --> CV{Convergence Check}
    CV -- Not converged --> S1
    CV -- Not converged --> S2
    CV -- Not converged --> S3
    CV -- Not converged --> S4
    CV -- Converged --> OUT[Final Integrated Design]
```

### 4.5 Schematic Explanation of Each Loop

Each complete revolution of the spiral represents the **simultaneous re-evaluation** of all twelve design activities. The most important activities that dominate each loop are:

- **Loop 1 (Mission → Dimensions):** Mainly activity 1, 2.
- **Loop 2 (Hull Form → Resistance):** Mainly activities 3, 5.
- **Loop 3 (Stability & Structure):** Mainly activities 7, 8.
- **Loop 4 (Cost & Class):** Mainly activities 10, 11.
- **Loop 5 (Production):** Activity 12 — workshop detail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **spiral** is drawn as widening loops because **Loop 1 is quick and rough**, while **Loop 5 is slow and detailed** — the loops grow in depth as the design matures.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the term "Design Spiral" as used in Ship Design. Who originally proposed it and in which year?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Design Spiral is an iterative, non-linear decision-making model used in ship design to represent the sequential and recursive process of transforming the owner's mission requirements into a buildable, safe, and economical vessel. It was originally proposed by **Professor John B. Woodward** of the United States Naval Academy in the year **1880**. The spiral visually conveys that every major design decision influences, and is in turn influenced by, every other design decision, requiring multiple revolutions (loops) before the design converges.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List any FOUR design activities that are repeatedly revised during each revolution of the Design Spiral.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four design activities repeatedly revised in each spiral revolution are:
1. **Hull Form (Lines Plan)** — block coefficient, prismatic coefficient, waterplane coefficient.
2. **Resistance and Powering** — total resistance, EHP, SHP, propeller selection.
3. **Stability** — GM, GZ curve, freeboard compliance.
4. **Cost Estimate** — newbuilding cost, operational cost (OPEX).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Woodward Design Spiral** with a neat sketch. Discuss why the design process cannot be represented by a simple linear flowchart. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small cargo vessel is required to carry **5,000 DWT** at a **service speed of 12 knots**. Using empirical formulas, estimate the **principal dimensions** for the first loop of the Design Spiral. Take $C_B = 0.68$, $L/B = 6.0$, and $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**(a) — Detailed Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Woodward Design Spiral** is a conceptual model proposed by **Prof. J.B. Woodward in 1880** that captures the iterative, non-linear nature of ship design. Each complete revolution of the spiral represents one full cycle in which the designer revisits **all major design activities**: principal dimensions, hull form, hydrostatics, resistance and propulsion, stability, structural design, general arrangement, weight and cost estimates, and statutory/class compliance.

**Why a linear flowchart fails:**

A linear flowchart represents decisions as **sequential, one-directional steps with no feedback loops**. This is fundamentally inappropriate for ship design because:

1. **Every parameter is coupled.** Increasing the ship's beam (B) to improve stability also increases resistance, which raises SHP, which increases fuel weight, which increases displacement, which again forces a revision of B. This creates a **cyclic dependency** that a linear flowchart cannot capture.

2. **Decisions are not made once.** In real ship design, the designer does not finalize the hull form and then move permanently to structure. Instead, hull form, structure, and general arrangement must be **revisited iteratively** as new information emerges from cost analysis, class rules, or production constraints.

3. **Iteration depth varies.** Some decisions (e.g., principal dimensions) need 2–3 iterations. Others (e.g., structural scantlings) may need 5–7. A linear chart cannot represent variable iteration depth.

4. **Convergence, not termination.** The spiral represents **convergence toward an acceptable solution**, not termination at a fixed endpoint.

5. **Cost of late changes grows exponentially.** Each loop allows the designer to catch problems earlier, when the cost of correction is low. A linear chart encourages "finishing" each phase prematurely.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- DWT = 5,000 t
- Vs = 12 knots
- C_B = 0.68
- L/B = 6.0
- ρ = 1.025 t/m³

**Step 1 — Estimate LBP using Schneekluth-type regression for small cargo:**
$$
L_{BP} = 4.5 \cdot (DWT)^{1/3} \cdot V_s^{0.25}
$$

Compute $(DWT)^{1/3}$:
$$
(5000)^{1/3} \approx 17.10
$$

Compute $V_s^{0.25}$:
$$
(12)^{0.25} \approx 1.861
$$

Substitute:
$$
L_{BP} \approx 4.5 \cdot 17.10 \cdot 1.861
$$
$$
L_{BP} \approx 143.2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Estimate Beam (B):**
$$
B = \frac{L_{BP}}{L/B} = \frac{143.2}{6.0} \approx 23.87 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate Draft (T) using B/T = 2.5:**
$$
T = \frac{B}{2.5} = \frac{23.87}{2.5} \approx 9.55 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Displacement:**
$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$
$$
\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 143.2 \cdot 23.87 \cdot 9.55 \cdot 0.68
$$

Compute step by step:
- $143.2 \cdot 23.87 = 3,418.18$
- $3,418.18 \cdot 9.55 = 32,643.6$
- $32,643.6 \cdot 0.68 = 22,197.6$
- $22,197.6 \cdot 1.025 = 22,752.6 \text{ t}$

**Step 5 — Verify DWT:**
$$
LWT \approx 0.20 \cdot \Delta = 0.20 \cdot 22,752.6 \approx 4,550 \text{ t}
$$
$$
DWT_{computed} = \Delta - LWT \approx 22,752.6 - 4,550 \approx 18,202 \text{ t}
$$

Since **18,202 t >> 5,000 t** (required), the design is **over-sized**. The spiral must loop back:

**Revised LBP** (proportional scaling):
$$
L_{BP,new} = 143.2 \cdot \left(\frac{5000}{18202}\right)^{1/3} \approx 143.2 \cdot 0.647 \approx 92.6 \text{ m}
$$

Recompute:
- $B_{new} = 92.6 / 6.0 \approx 15.4$ m
- $T_{new} = 15.4 / 2.5 \approx 6.18$ m
- $\Delta_{new} \approx 1.025 \cdot 92.6 \cdot 15.4 \cdot 6.18 \cdot 0.68 \approx 6,140$ t

This is closer to the target, confirming that **two spiral loops** are needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert knots to m/s** when computing EHP from resistance. 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.
> - Students often **mix up LBP and LOA** — KTU expects **LBP** unless stated otherwise.
> - **Do not skip the convergence check** — KTU marks are explicitly awarded for showing that the design has **not yet converged** in the first loop, justifying a loop-back.
> - Forgetting the **LWT (Lightweight)** estimate before computing DWT leads to a 2-mark deduction.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **modern hybrid parallel design spiral** and compare it with the classical Woodward spiral. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier must satisfy: **DWT = 75,000 t**, **Vs = 15 knots**. Compute the **Froude number and EHP** for the first spiral loop using: $L_{BP} = 215$ m, $B = 32.3$ m, $T = 13.5$ m, $C_B = 0.82$, total resistance coefficient $C_T = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$, wetted surface area $S = 9{,}500$ m², total propulsive efficiency $\eta_{Total} = 0.62$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**(a) — Hybrid Parallel Spiral (7 marks):**

The **modern hybrid parallel design spiral** (post-1990s) retains the iterative principle of Woodward's spiral but executes **multiple design streams in parallel**, then merges them at a synchronization point. This dramatically reduces design cycle time.

| Feature | Classical Woodward Spiral | Modern Hybrid Parallel Spiral |
|:--------|:--------------------------|:------------------------------|
| **Process flow** | Sequential with loops | Parallel streams merged at checkpoints |
| **Iteration depth** | 5–7 loops typical | 3–4 loops due to parallel refinement |
| **Design cycle time** | 6–12 months (concept to contract) | 2–4 months |
| **Tool support** | Manual calculation + early CAD | Integrated CAD/CAE, CFD, FEA, PDM |
| **Risk management** | Reactive (catch errors late) | Proactive (concurrent analysis) |
| **Stakeholder involvement** | Mostly internal design team | Owner, class, shipyard, vendors in parallel |
| **Convergence** | Sequential convergence | Concurrent convergence with shared KPIs |
| **Cost estimate accuracy** | ±15–30% (concept) → ±5% (contract) | ±10–15% (concept) → ±3% (contract) |
| **Examples** | Pre-1980 naval design | Modern container ships, LNG carriers, naval combatants |

The hybrid spiral creates **4 parallel streams**: (1) Hull form & hydrostatics, (2) Structural design, (3) Machinery & outfitting, (4) Cost & risk analysis. Each stream runs independently but shares data via a **common product model**. Streams converge at **integration milestones** (e.g., 30%, 60%, 90% design completion). Discrepancies trigger **stream-level loops** rather than full-spiral restarts.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L_{BP} = 215$ m
- $B = 32.3$ m
- $T = 13.5$ m
- $C_B = 0.82$
- $C_T = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$
- $S = 9{,}500$ m²
- $\eta_{Total} = 0.62$
- $V_s = 15$ knots

**Step 1 — Convert Vs to m/s:**
$$
V_s = 15 \cdot 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Froude Number:**
$$
F_n = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}
$$
Taking $L_{WL} \approx L_{BP} = 215$ m and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:
$$
F_n = \frac{7.716}{\sqrt{9.81 \cdot 215}}
$$

Compute denominator:
$$
\sqrt{9.81 \cdot 215} = \sqrt{2109.15} \approx 45.92
$$

Therefore:
$$
F_n = \frac{7.716}{45.92} \approx 0.168
$$

This is **typical for a bulk carrier** (Fn ≈ 0.15–0.20), confirming we are on track.

**Step 3 — Compute Total Resistance:**
$$
R_T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V_s^2 \cdot S \cdot C_T
$$

Substitute values:
$$
R_T = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot (7.716)^2 \cdot 9500 \cdot 0.0045
$$

Compute $(7.716)^2$:
$$
(7.716)^2 \approx 59.54
$$

Continue:
$$
R_T = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 59.54 \cdot 9500 \cdot 0.0045
$$

Multiply step by step:
- $0.5 \cdot 1025 = 512.5$
- $512.5 \cdot 59.54 = 30,514.25$
- $30,514.25 \cdot 9500 = 289,885,375$
- $289,885,375 \cdot 0.0045 \approx 1,304,484$ N

So:
$$
R_T \approx 1.304 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1,304 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute EHP:**
$$
EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75}
$$
$$
EHP = \frac{1,304,484 \cdot 7.716}{75}
$$

Numerator:
$$
1,304,484 \cdot 7.716 \approx 10,065,398
$$

Divide by 75:
$$
EHP \approx 134,205 \text{ HP (metric)} \approx 134.2 \text{ metric HP} \times 1000? 
$$

**Recheck:** $1,304,484 \cdot 7.716 = 10,065,398$ J/s = 10,065,398 W (since 1 N · 1 m/s = 1 W).

Convert to metric HP (1 metric HP = 735.5 W):
$$
EHP = \frac{10,065,398}{735.5} \approx 13,684 \text{ metric HP}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute SHP:**
$$
SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_{Total}} = \frac{13,684}{0.62} \approx 22,071 \text{ metric HP}
$$

Convert to kW:
$$
SHP \approx 22,071 \cdot 0.7355 \approx 16,233 \text{ kW} \approx 16.2 \text{ MW}
$$

This corresponds to a main engine of about **16,000 kW**, which is realistic for a 75,000 DWT bulk carrier.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - Students often use **Vs in knots directly** in the Froude formula, getting **wrong Fn values**. Always convert to m/s.
> - In the resistance formula, **forgetting to multiply by ½** or **using fresh water density (1000) instead of seawater (1025)** leads to a 1–2 mark deduction.
> - Students sometimes **forget the conversion factor 75** (metric) or **75/0.7457 = 100.6** (imperial confusion).
> - **The units of EHP and SHP must be stated explicitly** to get full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Design Spiral**

- ✅ The **Design Spiral** was proposed by **Prof. J.B. Woodward in 1880** and represents the iterative, non-linear nature of ship design.
- ✅ Each **loop (revolution)** of the spiral revisits **all 12 major design activities**: dimensions, hull form, hydrostatics, resistance, propulsion, stability, structure, general arrangement, weight, cost, statutory/class, production.
- ✅ The **ship design process is iterative**, not linear — a flowchart cannot capture the cyclic dependencies.
- ✅ **Three phases**: (1) Concept/Preliminary, (2) Contract, (3) Detail/Production.
- ✅ **Modern hybrid parallel spiral** runs 4 streams concurrently (hull, structure, machinery, cost/risk) and merges at integration checkpoints.
- ✅ Key empirical formulas used in **Loop 1**: $L_{BP} = k \cdot (DWT)^{1/3} \cdot V_s^{n}$, $B = L_{BP} / (L/B)$, $T = B / (B/T)$, $\Delta = \rho \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$.
- ✅ Typical **Cb ranges**: Tanker 0.80–0.85, Bulk carrier 0.75–0.82, Container 0.60–0.70, Naval 0.45–0.55.
- ✅ **Froude number** $F_n = V_s / \sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}$ is the most important dimensionless number in ship design — every spiral loop checks it.
- ✅ **Convergence criterion**: $\|X_{n+1} - X_n\| < \epsilon$, with $\epsilon$ tolerances per parameter (e.g., L ±0.5 m, Cb ±0.005).
- ✅ The spiral **widens** with each loop because later loops involve **greater detail and more stakeholders** (class, shipyard, owner).
- ✅ **First loop almost never converges** — KTU expects students to demonstrate that iteration is required.
- ✅ Cost estimate accuracy improves with each loop: ±30% → ±15% → ±5% as the design progresses.
- ✅ **Remember unit conversions**: 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; 1 metric HP = 735.5 W; seawater density ρ = **1.025 t/m³**.
- ✅ KTU Module 1 focuses on the **conceptual and first-loop aspects** of the spiral — detailed structural scantlings are deferred to higher semesters.

---

> **End of Notes — Design Spiral | PBNST504 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Ship Parameters # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Ship Parameters"? In the context of naval architecture and ship design, **Ship Parameters** refer to the fundamental set of geometric, hydrodynamic, and load-carrying descriptors that uniquely define a vessel's physical form, capacity, and performance envelope. These parameters form the **first input layer** of every design spiral, structural calculation, stability analysis, and resistance estimation performed on a ship. Formally, ship parameters can be classified into four broad families: 1. **Principal Dimensions** (Length, Breadth, Depth, Draft) 2. **Displacement & Tonnage** (Volume of water displaced, weight carried) 3. **Form Coefficients** (Block, Midship, Waterplane, Prismatic, Vertical Prismatic) 4. **Performance Parameters** (Speed, Power, Endurance, Range) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > The 2024 Scheme places heavy weightage on **Principal Dimensions**, **Displacement**, **Tonnage**, and **Form Coefficients**. Expect a guaranteed numerical problem in Part B (14 marks) where students are asked to compute displacement, form coefficients, or related quantities. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Ship as a "Floating Rectangular Box" Imagine you take a large rectangular cardboard box and push it into a bathtub full of water. What happens? - The box **pushes water aside**, and that water rises to compensate. The amount of water displaced weighs exactly as much as the box (Archimedes' Principle). That volume of water is the **Displacement**. - The box has a **length**, a **breadth (width)**, and a **depth (height)**. These are the **Principal Dimensions**. - A box is not streamlined — water flowing around it has to "squeeze" through a smaller cross-section than the maximum body cross-section. This "fullness" or "slenderness" is captured by **Form Coefficients**. A real ship is essentially this box, but with curved, streamlined surfaces. The shape deviates from the box in a controlled way, and the parameters we study **quantify exactly how much it deviates** from that simple box. > [!NOTE] > **Archimedes' Principle (foundational axiom):** > A floating body displaces a volume of fluid whose weight equals the total weight of the body. > $$W_{ship} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$ > where $\nabla$ is the **displaced volume** and $\rho_{sw}$ is the density of seawater (typically **$\rho_{sw} = 1.025\ \text{tonnes/m}^3$**). --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Constants to Memorize) | Constant | Symbol | Value | Use | |---|---|---|---| | Seawater density | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1.025 t/m³** | Displacement to weight | | Freshwater density | $\rho_{fw}$ | **1.000 t/m³** | River/inland vessels | | Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | SI conversions | | 1 Nautical Mile | NM | **1852 m** | Speed (knots) | | 1 Knot | kn | **1 NM/hr** | Speed unit | --- ## 1.4 Why Are Ship Parameters So Important? Every downstream calculation in ship design begins with these numbers: - **Hull Resistance** (Holtrop-Mennen, Hollenbach methods) → needs $L$, $B$, $T$, $C_B$, $S$ - **Propulsion Power** (EHP, BHP, SHP) → needs displacement and speed - **Stability** (GM, GZ curve) → needs $B$, $T$, $C_B$, KG - **Structural Design** (plate thickness, scantlings) → needs $L$, $B$, $D$, $T$ - **Cost & Deadweight** estimation → needs DWT and lightweight > [!TIP] > If you master these parameters, the rest of naval architecture becomes a sequence of **plug-and-chug** operations on these fundamental numbers. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principal Dimensions — The "Big Four" These are the four dimensions that define the **outer envelope** of a ship. ### (a) Length Over All (LOA) The **maximum** distance between the foremost and aftmost points of the ship, including any projections (bulbous bow, stern overhangs). ### (b) Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP or $L_{pp}$) The distance between the **forward perpendicular (FP)** and the **aft perpendicular (AP)**. - FP is typically where the **design waterline** meets the **stem**. - AP is typically through the **rudder post** (or rudder axis on modern ships). > [!IMPORTANT] > **For hydrodynamics and resistance**, almost always use $L_{pp}$, NOT LOA. KTU problems that ask "length" without qualification usually imply $L_{pp}$. ### (c) Breadth (B or $B_{WL}$) The **maximum** width of the ship measured at the **design waterline** (or sometimes at midship). Often denoted $B_{WL}$. ### (d) Depth (D) The **vertical distance** from the **keel** (or baseline) to the **strength deck** (usually the main deck) measured at **midship**. This is the structural depth. ### (e) Draft (T or $T_{d}$) The **vertical distance** from the **keel** to the **design waterline**. This determines how deep the ship sits in the water. > [!NOTE] > **Freeboard** $FB = D - T$. This is the visible hull above water. --- ## 2.2 Displacement — The Floating Weight **Displacement ($\Delta$ or $W$)** is the **weight of water displaced** by the ship's hull. By Archimedes, this equals the **total weight of the ship** at that draft. $$\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla \quad \text{(in Newtons, SI)}$$ In naval architecture, we typically use the **metric tonne (1000 kg)** as the unit and absorb $g$ into the density (expressed as **mass density**, $\rho$ in t/m³): $$\boxed{\Delta \ (\text{tonnes}) = \rho \ (\text{t/m}^3) \times \nabla \ (\text{m}^3)}$$ ### Types of Displacement | Type | Symbol | Definition | |---|---|---| | Lightweight | $\Delta_L$ | Weight of empty ship (hull, machinery, outfit) | | Deadweight | DWT | Variable load (cargo, fuel, crew, stores) | | Load Displacement | $\Delta_L + \text{DWT}$ | Ship fully loaded | | Trial Displacement | — | Ship at design trial condition | --- ## 2.3 Tonnage — The "Volume" Cousin of Displacement **Tonnage is NOT weight** — it is a **volume measure** used primarily for **regulation, registration, and fee calculation**. | Tonnage | Symbol | What it Measures | |---|---|---| | Gross Tonnage | GT | Total enclosed volume of ship | | Net Tonnage | NT | Revenue-earning volume (cargo + passenger) | | Displacement Tonnage | — | Old term = $\Delta$ in long tons (2240 lb) | Modern GT/NT follows the **IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention** (complex formula with correction factors). For KTU problems, the basic geometric volume is usually sufficient. --- ## 2.4 Form Coefficients — Quantifying "Slenderness" These are **dimensionless ratios** that describe how "fat" or "thin" a ship is in different cross-sections. ### (a) Block Coefficient ($C_B$) The most important form coefficient. It compares the actual underwater volume to a rectangular box of $L \times B \times T$. $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$ - Typical values: **0.55 (slender, fast container ship) to 0.85+ (full, slow tanker)**. ### (b) Midship Area Coefficient ($C_M$ or $A_M / A_M^{\text{box}}$) $$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$ where $A_M$ is the **midship section area** underwater. For most ships, $C_M \approx 0.90$ to $0.99$. ### (c) Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$) $$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$ - $C_P$ describes the **longitudinal fullness**. Low $C_P$ = fine ends, high $C_P$ = full ends. ### (d) Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_{WP}$) $$C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$$ where $A_{WP}$ is the waterplane area. ### (e) Vertical Prismatic Coefficient ($C_{VP}$) $$C_{VP} = \frac{\nabla}{A_{WP} \cdot T} = \frac{C_B}{C_{WP}}$$ --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Do not memorize blindly — understand the geometry. Every coefficient is a ratio of "actual shape area/volume" to "bounding box area/volume".** | Parameter | Formula | Typical Range | |---|---|---| | Displacement (weight) | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | — | | Block coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | 0.55 – 0.85 | | Midship coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | 0.90 – 0.99 | | Prismatic coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M} = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | 0.55 – 0.85 | | Waterplane coeff. | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$ | 0.65 – 0.90 | | Vertical prismatic | $C_{VP} = \dfrac{C_B}{C_{WP}} = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_{WP} \cdot T}$ | 0.60 – 0.85 | | Length-to-beam ratio | $L/B$ | 5 – 8 (cargo), 9+ (fast) | | Beam-to-draft ratio | $B/T$ | 2.0 – 3.5 | | Length-to-depth ratio | $L/D$ | 10 – 16 | | Displacement-Length | $\Delta / (L/100)^3$ | 50 – 600 | | Deadweight ratio | DWR = DWT / $\Delta$ | 0.5 – 0.85 | | Froude number | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | 0.10 – 0.40 | | Speed-Length ratio | $SLR = V / \sqrt{L}$ | 0.5 – 1.6 | | Powering | $EHP = R_T \cdot V$ | — | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule Reminder:** All vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert A \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Concept Design Stage**: A naval architect specifies $L$, $B$, $T$, $C_B$, speed, and DWT — these **six numbers** define ~90% of the ship. - **Resistance Estimation**: Froude's Law of Comparison and modern Holtrop methods all require $C_B$, $L$, $B$, $T$, $S$ (wetted surface). - **Stability**: $C_B$ directly affects $BM$ and metacentre location. - **Structural Weight Estimation**: $L$, $B$, $D$ feed into the Steel Weight Equation (Muckle's curves). - **Classification Society Rules** (IRS, DNV, Lloyd's): All scantlings begin with $L$, $B$, $D$. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Block Coefficient from First Principles Consider a ship of length $L$, breadth $B$, and draft $T$. The **bounding box** (a rectangular prism enclosing the underwater hull) has volume: $$\nabla_{\text{box}} = L \cdot B \cdot T$$ The **actual underwater volume** of the ship is $\nabla$. The block coefficient is defined as the **ratio** of the actual volume to the bounding box volume: $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{\nabla_{\text{box}}} = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$ Since the ship is always "inside" the bounding box, $0 < C_B < 1$. Specifically: - $C_B = 1.0$ would be a perfect rectangular barge. - $C_B \approx 0.5$ would be a highly streamlined, slender hull (submarine-like). **Displacement from $C_B$:** Starting from $C_B$: $$\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$ Applying Archimedes' Principle: $$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **single most useful formula** in early-stage ship design — it lets you compute weight directly from principal dimensions and block coefficient. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Form Coefficients Starting from the three fundamental volume equations: $$\begin{aligned} \nabla &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \quad &\text{(Block definition)} \\ \nabla &= C_P \cdot A_M \cdot L \quad &\text{(Prismatic definition)} \\ \nabla &= C_{VP} \cdot A_{WP} \cdot T \quad &\text{(Vertical prismatic definition)} \\ A_M &= C_M \cdot B \cdot T \quad &\text{(Midship definition)} \\ A_{WP} &= C_{WP} \cdot L \cdot B \quad &\text{(Waterplane definition)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 1:** Equate the first two expressions for $\nabla$: $$C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T = C_P \cdot A_M \cdot L$$ **Step 2:** Cancel $L$ on both sides: $$C_B \cdot B \cdot T = C_P \cdot A_M$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T$: $$C_B \cdot B \cdot T = C_P \cdot C_M \cdot B \cdot T$$ **Step 4:** Cancel $B \cdot T$: $$\boxed{C_B = C_P \cdot C_M}$$ Similarly: $$\boxed{C_B = C_{WP} \cdot C_{VP}}$$ These are **mandatory relations** that KTU expects you to remember. --- ## 3.3 Full Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-style 14-mark format) **Problem Statement:** A bulk carrier has the following particulars: - Length between perpendiculars, $L_{pp} = 150\ \text{m}$ - Breadth, $B = 25\ \text{m}$ - Draft, $T = 9.5\ \text{m}$ - Midship coefficient, $C_M = 0.98$ - Waterplane coefficient, $C_{WP} = 0.78$ - Density of seawater, $\rho = 1.025\ \text{t/m}^3$ **Compute:** (a) The block coefficient $C_B$ and prismatic coefficient $C_P$. (b) The displacement $\Delta$ in tonnes. (c) If the lightweight is 6,500 t and the deadweight carried is 15,000 t, find the loaded draft. --- ### Solution: **Part (a): Finding $C_B$ and $C_P$** **Step 1:** Use the relation $C_B = C_{WP} \cdot C_{VP}$ — but $C_{VP}$ is unknown. Instead, use the relationship between coefficients directly from the bounding-box approach. We are not given $C_B$ directly. But notice — without more information (like actual underwater volume or $A_M$), we must use the **displacement-based approach** if additional data is given. In this problem, we are missing the direct value of $C_B$. **Correction / Standard KTU pattern:** Usually $C_B$ is **directly given** in Part (a).
