Let me reframe with the standard given data: **Revised Problem:** Suppose additionally the block coefficient $C_B = 0.78$. **Step 1:** Compute $C_P$ using $C_B = C_M \cdot C_P$: $$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.78}{0.98} = 0.7959 \approx 0.796$$ **Step 2:** Compute $C_{VP}$ using $C_B = C_{WP} \cdot C_{VP}$: $$C_{VP} = \frac{C_B}{C_{WP}} = \frac{0.78}{0.78} = 1.00$$ This means the waterplane area $A_{WP} = 1.00 \cdot L \cdot B$ — i.e., the waterplane is essentially rectangular (typical for full-form bulk carriers). --- **Part (b): Compute Displacement $\Delta$** **Step 1:** Compute the displaced volume: $$\begin{aligned} \nabla &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\ &= 0.78 \times 150 \times 25 \times 9.5 \\ &= 0.78 \times 35{,}625 \\ &= 27{,}787.5\ \text{m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Archimedes' Principle: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta &= \rho \cdot \nabla \\ &= 1.025\ \text{t/m}^3 \times 27{,}787.5\ \text{m}^3 \\ &= 28{,}482.19\ \text{tonnes} \end{aligned}$$ --- **Part (c): Loaded Draft** **Step 1:** Total weight when loaded: $$W_{\text{loaded}} = \Delta_L + \text{DWT} = 6{,}500 + 15{,}000 = 21{,}500\ \text{t}$$ **Step 2:** Note that 21,500 t < 28,482 t, so the ship is NOT fully submerged at 9.5 m draft. The **load displacement** corresponds to a **different draft** than 9.5 m. **Step 3:** Assuming $C_B$ remains approximately constant with small draft changes (a common first-order assumption), solve for the new draft $T_{\text{new}}$: $$\begin{aligned} \nabla_{\text{loaded}} &= \frac{W_{\text{loaded}}}{\rho} = \frac{21{,}500}{1.025} = 20{,}975.6\ \text{m}^3 \\ \nabla_{\text{loaded}} &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_{\text{new}} \\ T_{\text{new}} &= \frac{\nabla_{\text{loaded}}}{C_B \cdot L \cdot B} \\ &= \frac{20{,}975.6}{0.78 \times 150 \times 25} \\ &= \frac{20{,}975.6}{2{,}925} \\ &= 7.17\ \text{m} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The ship actually rides higher (draft 7.17 m) than the design draft (9.5 m) because the **actual deadweight carried** (15,000 t) is **less than the design deadweight**. The 9.5 m draft is the **design maximum draft** for this vessel. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Which Parameter Affects What? | Parameter | Resistance | Stability | Strength | Cost | Cargo Capacity | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | $L$ (length) | **★★★** | ★ | **★★★** | **★★★** | ★★ | | $B$ (breadth) | ★★ | **★★★** | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ | | $T$ (draft) | **★★★** | **★★★** | ★ | ★ | **★★★** | | $C_B$ | **★★★** | ★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | | $C_M$ | ★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★ | ★ | | $C_P$ | **★★★** | ★ | — | ★ | ★ | | DWT | ★ | ★ | ★★ | **★★★** | **★★★** | (★★★ = dominant influence, ★ = minor influence, — = negligible) # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Principal Dimensions of a Ship ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ProfileView["SHIP PROFILE - PRINCIPAL DIMENSIONS"] direction LR Bow["BOW
Foremost Point"] Stern["STERN
Aftmost Point"] LBP["Lpp
Length Between
Perpendiculars"] LOA["LOA
Length Over All
GREATER THAN Lpp"] Depth["D - DEPTH
Keel to Main Deck"] Draft["T - DRAFT
Keel to Waterline"] Freeboard["FREEBOARD FB
FB equals D minus T"] end Bow ---|"FP Forward
Perpendicular"| LBP LBP ---|"AP Aft
Perpendicular"| Stern Bow -.->|"Includes Bow
Overhang"| LOA LOA -.->|"Includes Stern
Overhang"| Stern Depth ---|"Vertical"| Draft Draft ---|"Vertical"| Freeboard style Bow fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style Stern fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style LBP fill:#059669,color:#ffffff style LOA fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style Depth fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style Draft fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff style Freeboard fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff style ProfileView fill:#f1f5f9,color:#0f172a ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Form Coefficients — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Inputs["INPUT PARAMETERS"] L["Lpp - Length"] B["B - Breadth"] T["T - Draft"] Vdis["Nabla - Displaced Volume"] AMid["AM - Midship Area"] AWP["AWP - Waterplane Area"] end subgraph Box["VIRTUAL BOUNDING BOX"] BoxVol["L times B times T"] end subgraph Coefficients["FORM COEFFICIENTS"] CB["CB Block Coefficient
Nabla divided by LBT"] CM["CM Midship Coefficient
AM divided by BT"] CP["CP Prismatic Coefficient
Nabla divided by AM times L"] CWP["CWP Waterplane Coefficient
AWP divided by LB"] CVP["CVP Vertical Prismatic
Nabla divided by AWP times T"] end subgraph Relations["MANDATORY RELATIONS"] Rel1["CB equals CM times CP"] Rel2["CB equals CWP times CVP"] end subgraph Outputs["ENGINEERING OUTPUTS"] Res["Resistance Estimation"] Stab["Stability GM"] Str["Structural Weight"] Pow["Propulsion Power"] end L --> BoxVol B --> BoxVol T --> BoxVol BoxVol --> CB Vdis --> CB Vdis --> CP AMid --> CP Vdis --> CVP AWP --> CVP AMid --> CM B --> CM T --> CM AWP --> CWP L --> CWP B --> CWP T --> CWP CB --> Rel1 CM --> Rel1 CP --> Rel1 CB --> Rel2 CWP --> Rel2 CVP --> Rel2 CB --> Res CB --> Pow CM --> Stab CWP --> Stab CP --> Res CVP --> Stab Res --> Str Pow --> Str style L fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff style B fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff style T fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff style Vdis fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff style AMid fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff style AWP fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff style BoxVol fill:#94a3b8,color:#0f172a style CB fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style CM fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style CP fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style CWP fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style CVP fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style Rel1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style Rel2 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style Res fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff style Stab fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff style Str fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff style Pow fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff style Inputs fill:#dbeafe,color:#0f172a style Box fill:#e2e8f0,color:#0f172a style Coefficients fill:#dcfce7,color:#0f172a style Relations fill:#fee2e2,color:#0f172a style Outputs fill:#f3e8ff,color:#0f172a ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Displacement and Tonnage Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Hierarchy["WEIGHT AND VOLUME HIERARCHY"] direction TB Light["LIGHTWEIGHT DeltaL
Hull plus Machinery plus Outfit
Approximately 50 to 80 percent of Load"] DWT["DEADWEIGHT DWT
Cargo plus Fuel plus Stores plus Crew"] Load["LOAD DISPLACEMENT
DeltaL plus DWT"] end subgraph Volumes["VOLUME EQUIVALENTS"] Box["LBT Bounding Box"] CBVolume["CB times LBT equals Nabla"] DispVol["Nabla plus Appendage Volume"] end subgraph Tonnages["TONNAGE MEASURES"] GT["GT Gross Tonnage
Total Enclosed Volume
REGULATION"] NT["NT Net Tonnage
Revenue Earning Volume

This mirrors the standard "input → processing → output" structure expected in naval architecture design workflows. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the following terms with reference to ship design:** (a) Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP) (b) Block Coefficient ($C_B$) (c) Freeboard **Model Answer:** **(a) Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP or $L_{pp}$):** It is the horizontal distance between the **forward perpendicular (FP)** and the **aft perpendicular (AP)** of a ship. The forward perpendicular is the line passing through the point where the design waterline meets the **stem** (forward side of the hull). The aft perpendicular is the line passing through the **rudder post** (or the centreline of the rudder on modern ships). LBP is the dimension used in **almost all resistance, powering, and stability calculations**, as it excludes the appendages (bulbous bow, stern overhang) that complicate hydrodynamic modelling. **(b) Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** The block coefficient is the ratio of the **displaced volume of the ship's underwater hull** ($\nabla$) to the volume of a **rectangular bounding box** of length $L$, breadth $B$, and draft $T$: $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$ It is a measure of the **fullness of form**. Typical values range from 0.55 for slender, fast vessels to 0.85 for full, slow cargo ships. $C_B$ is the **single most important form coefficient** in early-stage ship design. **(c) Freeboard:** Freeboard is the **vertical distance** between the **design waterline** and the **top of the weather deck** (strength deck) at the ship's side. It is given by: $$FB = D - T$$ where $D$ is the moulded depth and $T$ is the draft. Freeboard is regulated by international load line conventions to ensure adequate **reserve buoyancy** and seaworthiness. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is meant by displacement of a ship? Distinguish between lightweight, deadweight, and load displacement.** **Model Answer:** **Displacement:** The displacement of a ship is the **weight of the volume of water displaced by the ship's underwater hull**. By Archimedes' Principle, this equals the **total weight of the ship** at the corresponding waterline. It is measured in tonnes and given by: $$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla \quad \text{(in tonnes)}$$ **Distinction:** | Term | Definition | Typical Proportion | |---|---|---| | **Lightweight Displacement ($\Delta_L$)** | Weight of the empty ship, i.e., hull structure, machinery, propulsion system, outfit, and permanent ballast | ~20% to 50% of load displacement | | **Deadweight (DWT)** | Weight of all **variable loads** — cargo, fuel, lubricating oil, freshwater, crew, passengers, stores, and cargo gear | ~50% to 80% of load displacement | | **Load Displacement** | Total weight = $\Delta_L$ + DWT | 100% (the fully loaded condition) | Mathematically: $$\Delta_{\text{load}} = \Delta_L + \text{DWT}$$ --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each**. Both sub-parts are compulsory. Internal choice (OR option) is offered at the **module level** — students pick ONE of two full questions. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **A bulk carrier has the following principal particulars:** - Length between perpendiculars: $L_{pp} = 180\ \text{m}$ - Breadth: $B = 28\ \text{m}$ - Design draft: $T = 10.5\ \text{m}$ - Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.80$ - Midship coefficient: $C_M = 0.97$ - Density of seawater: $\rho = 1.025\ \text{t/m}^3$ **(a)** Determine: - (i) The displaced volume $\nabla$ - (ii) The displacement $\Delta$ in tonnes - (iii) The prismatic coefficient $C_P$ - (iv) The waterplane area coefficient $C_{WP}$ (assume vertical prismatic coefficient $C_{VP} = 0.95$) **(b)** If the ship proceeds to a freshwater port ($\rho = 1.000\ \text{t/m}^3$) at the same displacement, calculate the **change in draft**. Comment on the practical significance. --- ### Model Solution: **Part (a):** **(i) Displaced Volume $\nabla$:** $$\begin{aligned} \nabla &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\ &= 0.80 \times 180 \times 28 \times 10.5 \\ &= 0.80 \times 52{,}920 \\ &= 42{,}336\ \text{m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Displacement $\Delta$:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta &= \rho \cdot \nabla \\ &= 1.025 \times 42{,}336 \\ &= 43{,}394.4\ \text{tonnes} \approx 43{,}394\ \text{t} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$:** Using $C_B = C_M \cdot C_P$: $$\begin{aligned} C_P &= \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.80}{0.97} \\ &= 0.8247 \approx 0.825 \end{aligned}$$ **(iv) Waterplane Area Coefficient $C_{WP}$:** Using $C_B = C_{WP} \cdot C_{VP}$: $$\begin{aligned} C_{WP} &= \frac{C_B}{C_{VP}} = \frac{0.80}{0.95} \\ &= 0.8421 \approx 0.842 \end{aligned}$$ --- **Part (b): Change in Draft in Freshwater** **Step 1:** The weight (displacement) remains constant: $\Delta = 43{,}394.4\ \text{t}$. **Step 2:** In freshwater, the displaced volume must increase to compensate for lower density: $$\begin{aligned} \nabla_{fw} &= \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{fw}} = \frac{43{,}394.4}{1.000} \\ &= 43{,}394.4\ \text{m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Difference in volume (extra water displaced): $$\Delta \nabla = 43{,}394.4 - 42{,}336 = 1{,}058.4\ \text{m}^3$$ **Step 4:** Assuming block coefficient remains approximately constant (a standard first-order approximation), the draft increases as: $$\begin{aligned} T_{fw} &= \frac{\nabla_{fw}}{C_B \cdot L \cdot B} = \frac{43{,}394.4}{0.80 \times 180 \times 28} \\ &= \frac{43{,}394.4}{4{,}032} \\ &= 10.762\ \text{m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Change in draft: $$\Delta T = T_{fw} - T_{sw} = 10.762 - 10.5 = 0.262\ \text{m} \approx 26\ \text{cm}$$ **Practical Significance:** > [!IMPORTANT] > When a ship moves from seawater to freshwater, it sinks **deeper** by approximately **2% to 3% of its draft**. For this bulk carrier, ~26 cm extra draft. This is critical for vessels operating in **river mouths, estuaries, and ports with freshwater**, where over-drafting could lead to **grounding**. Loading officers must account for this **"Fresh Water Allowance (FWA)"**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** Derive the relationship between **block coefficient $C_B$**, **midship coefficient $C_M$**, and **prismatic coefficient $C_P$**, stating clearly any assumptions. **(b)** A container ship has the following particulars: - $L_{pp} = 220\ \text{m}$, $B = 32\ \text{m}$, $T = 12\ \text{m}$ - $C_B = 0.62$, $C_M = 0.94$ Compute: - (i) The prismatic coefficient $C_P$ - (ii) The midship area $A_M$ - (iii) The displacement $\Delta$ in tonnes (seawater) - (iv) The speed in knots corresponding to a **Froude number of 0.25** --- ### Model Solution: **Part (a): Derivation of $C_B = C_M \cdot C_P$** **Assumption:** The ship's underwater hull is treated as a series of contiguous transverse cross-sections whose areas vary smoothly along the length. The mean of these cross-sectional areas equals the **midship area multiplied by the prismatic coefficient** — this is the defining assumption of $C_P$. **Step 1:** Define the block coefficient from first principles. The displaced volume is the integral of the cross-sectional area $A(x)$ along the length: $$\nabla = \int_0^L A(x) \, dx$$ **Step 2:** By definition of $C_P$, the **mean cross-sectional area** is: $$\bar{A} = \frac{\nabla}{L} = C_P \cdot A_M$$ That is, the prismatic coefficient expresses the ratio of the **average cross-sectional area** to the **maximum (midship) cross-sectional area**. **Step 3:** The block coefficient is the ratio of the displaced volume to the bounding box volume: $$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$ **Step 4:** Rewrite $\nabla$: $$\nabla = \bar{A} \cdot L = C_P \cdot A_M \cdot L$$ **Step 5:** Substitute into $C_B$: $$C_B = \frac{C_P \cdot A_M \cdot L}{L \cdot B \cdot T} = C_P \cdot \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$ **Step 6:** Recognise that the term $\dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ is exactly the **midship coefficient $C_M$**: $$\boxed{C_B = C_M \cdot C_P}$$ This is the required relationship. **Q.E.D.** --- **Part (b): Numerical Computations** **(i) Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$:** $$\begin{aligned} C_P &= \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.62}{0.94} \\ &= 0.6596 \approx 0.660 \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Midship Area $A_M$:** $$\begin{aligned} A_M &= C_M \cdot B \cdot T = 0.94 \times 32 \times 12 \\ &= 360.96\ \text{m}^2 \approx 361\ \text{m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Displacement $\Delta$:** $$\begin{aligned} \nabla &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\ &= 0.62 \times 220 \times 32 \times 12 \\ &= 0.62 \times 84{,}480 \\ &= 52{,}377.6\ \text{m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \Delta &= \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 52{,}377.6 \\ &= 53{,}687.04\ \text{tonnes} \approx 53{,}687\ \text{t} \end{aligned}$$ **(iv) Speed from Froude Number:** The Froude number is defined as: $$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$ where $V$ is speed in m/s and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. Solving for $V$: $$\begin{aligned} V &= F_n \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot L} \\ &= 0.25 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 220} \\ &= 0.25 \times \sqrt{2{,}158.2} \\ &= 0.25 \times 46.457 \\ &= 11.614\ \text{m/s} \end{aligned}$$ Converting to knots (1 m/s = 1.9438 knots): $$V = 11.614 \times 1.9438 = 22.57\ \text{knots}$$ > [!NOTE] > A Froude number of 0.25 corresponds to **medium-to-high speed** for a container ship (typical service speeds are 20–25 knots). The high $F_n$ regime is dominated by **wave-making resistance**, which is why container ships adopt slender hull forms ($C_B \approx 0.60$). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (based on KTU valuation patterns):** > > 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students often write $\Delta$ in Newtons instead of tonnes, or forget to include the density. Always remember: **nautical architects use tonnes and t/m³, not kg and kg/m³**, because $g$ is absorbed into mass density. > > 2. **LBP vs LOA (–1 to –2 marks):** Resistance, powering, and Froude number calculations **always use $L_{pp}$**, not LOA. Mixing these up is the #1 error. > > 3. **Forgetting Coefficient Relations (–2 marks):** KTU often provides 3 coefficients and asks for the 4th. You **must** remember: > - $C_B = C_M \cdot C_P$ > - $C_B = C_{WP} \cdot C_{VP}$ > > 4. **Mixing Density (Seawater vs Freshwater) (–2 marks):** Use $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ for open-ocean problems. Switch to $\rho_{fw} = 1.000$ only when explicitly stated. > > 5. **Dimensional Homogeneity (–1 mark):** All lengths in metres, areas in m², volumes in m³, weight in tonnes. Mixing cm and m is a frequent killer. > > 6. **Not Stating Assumptions (–1 to –2 marks):** For derivation-type questions, students lose marks for failing to state the **definition of $C_P$** or the assumption that the mean cross-section area relates to $A_M$ via $C_P$. > > 7. **Approximation Errors (–1 mark):** Don't round prematurely. Carry at least 4 significant digits throughout the calculation. Round only the **final answer** to 2–3 significant digits. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **Archimedes' Principle** is the foundation of all displacement calculations: $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$. - [x] **Seawater density** = 1.025 t/m³; **Freshwater** = 1.000 t/m³. - [x] **Principal dimensions:** Always use $L_{pp}$, $B$, $T$, $D$. Freeboard $FB = D - T$. - [x] **Five form coefficients** to know cold: $C_B$, $C_M$, $C_P$, $C_{WP}$, $C_{VP}$. - [x] **Two mandatory relations:** $C_B = C_M \cdot C_P$ AND $C_B = C_{WP} \cdot C_{VP}$. - [x] **Block coefficient** is the **most important** form coefficient in early design. - [x] **Typical $C_B$ ranges:** Tanker ~0.80–0.85, Bulk Carrier ~0.75–0.80, Container ~0.55–0.65, Warship ~0.40–0.55. - [x] **Displacement is weight**, **Tonnage (GT/NT) is volume** — never confuse. - [x] **Froude number** $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ governs **wave-making resistance**. - [x] **Speed-Length Ratio** $SLR = V / \sqrt{L}$ (with $V$ in knots, $L$ in feet) is a quick check: values > 1.3 indicate planing/very fast craft; values < 0.5 indicate slow displacement craft. - [x] **Slender ships** (high $L/B$, low $C_B$) → less resistance, higher speed. - [x] **Full ships** (low $L/B$, high $C_B$) → more cargo capacity, lower speed. - [x] **Freshwater Allowance (FWA):** Always ~2–3% increase in draft. - [x] **Deadweight Ratio (DWR)** = DWT / $\Delta_{load}$ — high DWR → efficient cargo ship. > [!TIP] > **Final Tip for KTU Exam Day:** When a numerical problem looks intimidating, always start by writing down **what is given** and **what is to be found**. Then write the **relevant formula**. This 30-second habit alone will save you from the most common silly mistakes and ensure you **grab every available mark**.

topics.title: Deadweight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deadweight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 : Deadweight — Foundational Concepts in Ship Design

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a cornerstone of Naval Architecture and directly supports **Course Outcome CO1** — *Apply the principles of ship form, hydrostatics, and weight estimation to develop preliminary ship design specifications.* It is a guaranteed high-weightage topic in both Continuous Assessment and End Semester Examinations.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Naval Architecture and KTU 2024 Ship Design I curriculum, **Deadweight (DWT)** is defined as the **total weight a vessel must carry to achieve her load draft**, expressed in metric tonnes (t). Mathematically, it is the **difference between the displacement of the ship at her load (or design) waterline and the lightweight of the ship**.

$$\boxed{\text{Deadweight (DWT)} = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_{L}}$$

Where:
- $\Delta_{\text{load}}$ = Displacement at Load Draft (in **tonnes** or **long tons**)
- $W_{L}$ = Lightweight / Lightship Weight (in **tonnes** or **long tons**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Terminology Note:** Always use the phrase *"Deadweight at Load Draft"* in your ESE answers. Many students lose marks by writing only "DWT" without specifying the reference waterline. The deadweight *changes* with the draft — it is **not a fixed ship constant** like the lightweight.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cargo Truck" Intuition

Imagine a large, empty cargo truck standing on a weighbridge.

- The **empty weight** of the truck (chassis, body, engine, fuel tank empty) is analogous to the **Lightweight** of a ship — it is the permanent, unchangeable structure of the vehicle.
- Now imagine fully loading the truck with **goods, fuel in the tank, the driver, the spare tyre, water bottles, and luggage**. The total weight the truck can legally carry before becoming overloaded is the **Deadweight** of the ship.
- The weighbridge reading when fully loaded is the **Displacement (Loaded)**.

**Mapping the analogy:**

| Truck Concept | Ship Concept |
|---|---|
| Empty truck weight | Lightweight ($W_L$) |
| Driver + luggage + fuel | Crew + Stores + Fuel |
| Goods in the cargo hold | Deadweight Cargo |
| Fully loaded weighbridge reading | Displacement at Load Draft ($\Delta_{\text{load}}$) |
| **Load – Empty** | **= Deadweight (DWT)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy works:** A ship is fundamentally a *floating cargo carrier*, and deadweight is the designer's answer to *"How much useful stuff can this floating truck legally carry?"* — the answer depends on the hull volume, structural strength, and stability limits.

---

## 1.3 The Five Major Components of Deadweight

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes deadweight into the following **five mandatory components** (memorize this list — it is a 3-mark direct question):

1. **Deadweight Cargo ($DWT_C$)** — The actual paying revenue-earning cargo (containers, bulk grain, oil, ore, vehicles, etc.)
2. **Fuel Oil, Diesel Oil & Lubricating Oil ($FO + DO + LO$)** — Bunker fuel for main engine, diesel for generators, lube oil for machinery.
3. **Fresh Water ($FW$)** — Potable water for crew, plus boiler feed water and domestic use.
4. **Ballast Water ($BW$)** — Seawater taken into dedicated tanks to maintain draft, trim, and stability on ballast legs.
5. **Stores, Provisions, Crew & Passengers ($S+C+P$)** — Food, spares, crew effects, baggage, and (if applicable) passenger weights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Weight Memory Trick — "CFFBS"**: **C**argo, **F**uel, **F**resh water, **B**allast, **S**tores+Crew. Use this acronym in your ESE answer for the **components of deadweight** question to demonstrate structured recall.

---

## 1.4 The Fundamental Displacement Equation

By **Archimedes' Principle**, a floating ship displaces a volume of water whose mass equals the total mass of the ship. This gives the foundational identity:

$$\Delta = W_{L} + DWT$$

This equation is the **single most important relationship** in Module 1 of Ship Design I. Every KTU question on deadweight reduces to manipulating this identity in some form.

The corresponding volumetric form is:

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}}$$

Where:
- $\nabla$ = Volume of displacement (in $\text{m}^3$)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = Density of seawater (standard value $\mathbf{1.025 \, t/m^3}$)
- $\Delta$ = Displacement mass (in tonnes)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** In freshwater (e.g., a river or a freshwater port like the Great Lakes), $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \, t/m^3$. A ship drafts *more* in freshwater than in seawater for the same displacement. KTU questions often test whether you adjust the density correctly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of Deadweight

The complete weight breakdown of a loaded merchant ship is hierarchically structured as follows (this is a standard KTU 14-mark question pattern):

### Step 1 — Identify the Top-Level Split

$$\Delta_{\text{load}} = \underbrace{W_{L}}_{\text{Constant for ship}} + \underbrace{DWT_{\text{total}}}_{\text{Varies with voyage}}$$

- $W_L$ is **fixed** for a given ship (changes only when major modifications occur).
- $DWT_{\text{total}}$ is **variable** — it changes every voyage depending on cargo, fuel burned, water consumed, etc.

### Step 2 — Subdivide the Lightweight

$$W_{L} = W_{\text{steel}} + W_{\text{outfit}} + W_{\text{machinery}}$$

- $W_{\text{steel}}$ — Hull structure (plating, frames, decks, superstructure)
- $W_{\text{outfit}}$ — Equipment (hatches, winches, pumps, electrical, furnishings)
- $W_{\text{machinery}}$ — Main engine, generators, shaft line, propellers, bow thruster

> [!NOTE]
> The **Lightweight** is determined by the designer's preliminary calculation methods (e.g., Watson-Gilfillan equations, Mumford's formulas, or regression analysis from similar ships). It is NOT the student's job to derive it from first principles in ESE — but the student MUST know the three sub-components.

### Step 3 — Subdivide the Deadweight

$$DWT = DWT_C + DWT_{FO} + DWT_{DO} + DWT_{LO} + DWT_{FW} + DWT_{BW} + DWT_{S} + DWT_{C} + DWT_{P}$$

A more compact, examiner-friendly grouping:

$$DWT = DWT_{\text{Cargo}} + W_{\text{consumables}} + W_{\text{ballast}} + W_{\text{crew+stores}}$$

Where $W_{\text{consumables}} = W_{FO} + W_{DO} + W_{LO} + W_{FW}$ (these decrease as the voyage progresses).

### Step 4 — Why Consumables Matter

Consumables (fuel, lube, fresh water) are **time-dependent**. The ship sails with the *maximum* amount of consumables (heaviest condition) and burns/consumes them during the voyage. KTU problems often ask for the **deadweight at departure** vs. **deadweight at arrival** — this is exactly the difference!

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Ship chartering contracts (e.g., the GENCON form) and freight rate calculations depend on deadweight. A charterer pays for carrying cargo; the *owner* pays for fuel, port charges, and crew. This is why the **deadweight vs. lightweight split is the financial backbone of the shipping industry.**

---

## 2.2 The Deadweight Coefficient ($C_D$)

The **Deadweight Coefficient** is a dimensionless form coefficient that compares deadweight to the volume of displacement:

$$C_{D} = \frac{DWT}{\nabla \cdot \rho_{sw}}$$

Or equivalently (since $\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho_{sw}$ and $\Delta = W_L + DWT$):

$$C_{D} = \frac{DWT}{\Delta} = 1 - \frac{W_{L}}{\Delta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Values of $C_D$ in Merchant Ships (memorize for KTU viva & ESE):**
> - **Tankers:** $C_D \approx 0.85 - 0.90$ (heavy cargo, lightweight structure optimized)
> - **Bulk Carriers:** $C_D \approx 0.80 - 0.87$
> - **Container Ships:** $C_D \approx 0.60 - 0.75$ (light cargo, large volume)
> - **General Cargo Ships:** $C_D \approx 0.65 - 0.75$
> - **Naval Warships:** $C_D \approx 0.45 - 0.60$ (heavy weapons, small cargo)

A **higher $C_D$** means the ship is more *cargo-efficient* — it carries a large deadweight relative to its size. A **lower $C_D$** means the ship has a heavy lightweight (warships) or carries a lot of volume-based cargo (container ships).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is your KTU Module 1 Deadweight "cheat sheet." Memorize all eight rows.** In ESE, the valuation key awards marks for each correct formula and unit.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units | Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DWT = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_{L}$ | Deadweight identity | tonnes (t) | Foundation — write first |
| 2 | $\Delta = W_{L} + DWT$ | Total displacement at load draft | tonnes (t) | Often tested as derivation |
| 3 | $\nabla = \Delta \, / \, \rho$ | Volumetric displacement | $\text{m}^3$ | Use $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \, t/m^3$ |
| 4 | $C_{D} = DWT \, / \, \Delta$ | Deadweight coefficient | dimensionless | Range 0.45 (warships) – 0.90 (tankers) |
| 5 | $C_{D} = 1 - W_{L} \, / \, \Delta$ | Alternate form | dimensionless | Useful when $W_L$ is given |
| 6 | $DWT_{\text{arrival}} = DWT_{\text{dep}} - W_{\text{consumed}}$ | Consumables accounting | tonnes (t) | Voyage-by-voyage calculation |
| 7 | $DWT_{\text{cargo}} = DWT - (W_{FO} + W_{DO} + W_{LO} + W_{FW} + W_{BW} + W_{S+C+P})$ | Net payload | tonnes (t) | This is the *revenue-earning* cargo |
| 8 | $D_{L} = f(W_{L}, \Delta)$ via $\nabla$ and $C_{B}$ | Load draft from displacement | metres (m) | Links deadweight to ship form |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Ship Design Phase:** Deadweight is a *design input* — the owner specifies the required DWT, and the naval architect sizes the hull.
2. **Classification Society Rules:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, and IRS use deadweight categories (e.g., DWT < 500 = "Small ship," DWT > 150,000 = "VLCC" — Very Large Crude Carrier) for rule application.
3. **Port & Canal Dues:** Suez Canal and Panama Canal fees are calculated based on DWT or PC/UMS net tonnage.
4. **Stability & Strength Booklets:** Every ship has a "Stability Booklet" with curves of **displacement vs. draft** and **deadweight vs. draft** for operational use.
5. **Insurance:** Hull insurance premiums are partly indexed to DWT.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Connection:** A common 7-mark question in ESE is: *"A bulk carrier of lightweight 8,200 t has a load draft of 10.5 m in seawater ($C_B = 0.78$). Find the deadweight."* This is exactly the workflow a naval architect follows in the preliminary design stage.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Archimedes' Principle to the DWT Identity

This is the foundational derivation. KTU often awards 2 marks in Part A for stating the starting principle and 2 marks in Part B for completing the derivation.

**Starting Principle — Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy:**

A floating body displaces a volume of fluid whose weight equals the total weight of the body.

$$W_{\text{body}} = W_{\text{fluid displaced}}$$

**Step 1 — Express fluid weight in terms of volume and density:**

The weight of displaced seawater is:

$$W_{\text{fluid}} = \nabla \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g$$

For ship design, we use the **tonne as a mass unit** (where $1 \, t = 1000 \, kg$) and absorb $g$ into the mass-density definition. The **mass displacement** is:

$$\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho_{sw}$$

**Step 2 — Set this equal to the ship's total weight at load draft:**

The ship's total weight at load draft consists of two parts: the *permanent* lightweight $W_L$ and the *variable* deadweight $DWT$.

$$\nabla \cdot \rho_{sw} = W_{L} + DWT$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that displacement equals mass of displaced water:**

By definition, $\Delta_{\text{load}} = \nabla \cdot \rho_{sw}$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\Delta_{\text{load}} = W_{L} + DWT \quad \Rightarrow \quad DWT = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_{L}}$$

**Result:** We have derived the deadweight identity from first principles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expressing DWT in Terms of Form Coefficients

This is a critical "Problem-Solving" derivation used in KTU 14-mark numericals.

**Step 1 — Volume of displacement in terms of principal dimensions and block coefficient:**

The volume of displacement for a ship of length $L$, breadth $B$, draft $D$, and block coefficient $C_B$ is:

$$\nabla = C_{B} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$$

**Step 2 — Mass displacement in seawater:**

$$\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho_{sw} = C_{B} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D \cdot \rho_{sw}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the DWT identity:**

$$DWT = C_{B} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D \cdot \rho_{sw} - W_{L}$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the load draft $D$:**

$$D = \frac{DWT + W_{L}}{C_{B} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot \rho_{sw}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** This is the **inverse design formula** — given a required deadweight, the naval architect solves for the load draft. If the computed draft exceeds the design constraint, the ship is too small for the required deadweight.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Deadweight Coefficient Identity

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of $C_D$:**

$$C_{D} = \frac{DWT}{\Delta}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $DWT = \Delta - W_L$ (from the displacement identity):**

$$C_{D} = \frac{\Delta - W_{L}}{\Delta}$$

**Step 3 — Split the fraction:**

$$C_{D} = \frac{\Delta}{\Delta} - \frac{W_{L}}{\Delta}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{C_{D} = 1 - \frac{W_{L}}{\Delta}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $W_L$ in terms of $\Delta$ and $C_D$:**

$$W_{L} = \Delta \cdot (1 - C_{D})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is **extremely useful** in KTU problems. If the problem gives you $C_D$ and $\Delta$ (or $DWT$), you can find $W_L$ in one step without needing the lightweight breakdown.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU ESE Pattern):**
> *"A Panamax bulk carrier has the following particulars: Length between perpendiculars $L_{BP} = 180 \, m$, breadth $B = 32.2 \, m$, block coefficient $C_B = 0.82$. The lightweight is $9{,}500 \, t$ and the load draft is $12.20 \, m$. Calculate: (a) the displacement at load draft, (b) the deadweight, and (c) the deadweight coefficient. Use $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \, t/m^3$."*

### Part (a) — Displacement at Load Draft

**Step 1 — Write the volume of displacement formula:**

$$\nabla = C_{B} \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot D_{L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\nabla = 0.82 \times 180 \times 32.2 \times 12.20$$

**Step 3 — Compute step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
180 \times 32.2 &= 5796.0 \, m^2 \\
5796.0 \times 12.20 &= 70711.2 \, m^3 \\
\nabla = 0.82 \times 70711.2 &= 57983.184 \, m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by seawater density to get mass displacement:**

$$\Delta_{\text{load}} = 57983.184 \times 1.025 = 59432.76 \, t$$

> **Final Answer (a):** $\Delta_{\text{load}} \approx \mathbf{59{,}432.76 \, t}$

### Part (b) — Deadweight

**Step 1 — Apply the DWT identity:**

$$DWT = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_{L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$DWT = 59432.76 - 9500 = 49932.76 \, t$$

> **Final Answer (b):** $DWT \approx \mathbf{49{,}932.76 \, t}$

### Part (c) — Deadweight Coefficient

**Step 1 — Apply the $C_D$ formula:**

$$C_{D} = \frac{DWT}{\Delta_{\text{load}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$C_{D} = \frac{49932.76}{59432.76}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$C_{D} = 0.8402 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

> **Final Answer (c):** $C_D \approx \mathbf{0.840}$ (dimensionless)

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** The computed $C_D = 0.840$ falls within the typical range for bulk carriers ($0.80$ to $0.87$ per our formula sheet). The answer is *physically plausible*. Always perform this check in your ESE answers — it earns a "validity" mark from the examiner.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Setups

| # | Given Quantities | To Find | Key Formula | Mark Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta_{\text{load}}$, $W_L$ | $DWT$ | $DWT = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_L$ | 3 marks (Part A) |
| 2 | $DWT$, $W_L$ | $\Delta_{\text{load}}$ | $\Delta_{\text{load}} = DWT + W_L$ | 3 marks (Part A) |
| 3 | $C_D$, $\Delta$ | $W_L$ | $W_L = \Delta \cdot (1 - C_D)$ | 7 marks (Part B sub) |
| 4 | $L, B, D, C_B, W_L$ | $DWT$ | Combine $\nabla = C_B LBD$ with $DWT = \Delta - W_L$ | 14 marks (Full B) |
| 5 | $DWT_{\text{dep}}$, $W_{\text{consumables}}$ | $DWT_{\text{arrival}}$ | $DWT_{\text{arr}} = DWT_{\text{dep}} - W_{\text{cons}}$ | 7 marks (Part B sub) |
| 6 | $DWT_{\text{cargo}}$, $W_{FO}, W_{DO}, W_{LO}, W_{FW}, W_{BW}, W_{S+C+P}$ | Verification: $DWT$ total | Sum all components and compare to DWT identity | 7 marks (Part B sub) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Weight Breakdown of a Loaded Ship

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete weight hierarchy** of a merchant ship at load draft. This is the standard structure that KTU examiners expect students to draw in a 7-mark or 14-mark answer on deadweight.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["TOTAL DISPLACEMENT AT LOAD DRAFT Δ_Load"]:::root
    node2["LIGHTWEIGHT W_L (Constant)"]:::left
    node3["DEADWEIGHT DWT (Variable)"]:::right
    node4["Hull Steel Weight W_steel"]:::leftleaf
    node5["Outfit Weight W_outfit"]:::leftleaf
    node6["Machinery Weight W_machinery"]:::leftleaf
    node7["Deadweight Cargo DWT_C"]:::rightleaf
    node8["Fuel Oil W_FO"]:::rightleaf
    node9["Diesel and Lube Oil W_DO plus W_LO"]:::rightleaf
    node10["Fresh Water W_FW"]:::rightleaf
    node11["Ballast Water W_BW"]:::rightleaf
    node12["Stores Crew and Passengers W_S plus W_Cr plus W_P"]:::rightleaf

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node2 --> node6
    node3 --> node7
    node3 --> node8
    node3 --> node9
    node3 --> node10
    node3 --> node11
    node3 --> node12

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef left fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef right fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef leftleaf fill:#E0F6FF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef rightleaf fill:#FFE4D4,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

**How to read this diagram in your ESE answer:**
- **Top (yellow)** is the total — displacement at load draft.
- **Middle left (blue)** is the **permanent** weight (lightweight), which does not change voyage to voyage.
- **Middle right (orange)** is the **variable** weight (deadweight), which changes with every voyage.
- **Leaves** are the individual sub-components. A complete KTU 14-mark answer must mention *all* leaf-level components.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Voyage-Based Deadweight Evolution

This diagram shows how the deadweight **evolves during a single voyage** as consumables are burned and water is consumed. This is a frequent KTU 7-mark sub-question.

```mermaid
graph LR
    dep["DEPARTURE PORT
    DWT_max
    Full Bunkers + FW + Stores"]:::start
    midsea["MID-VOYAGE
    DWT decreases linearly
    FO and FW consumed"]:::middle
    arr["ARRIVAL PORT
    DWT_min
    Bunkers low + FW low"]:::end
    unload["CARGO DISCHARGED
    Ship becomes LIGHTSHIP
    plus minimal consumables"]:::final

    dep -->|"Consumables burn"| midsea
    midsea -->|"More consumables burn"| arr
    arr -->|"Cargo off-loaded"| unload
    unload -->|"Re-load cargo + re-bunker"| dep

    classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef middle fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef end fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef final fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- At **departure**, the ship is at her **heaviest** condition (DWT is maximum).
- During the voyage, **fuel is burned** and **fresh water is consumed**, so DWT gradually decreases.
- At **arrival**, just before unloading, the ship is at her **lightest loaded** condition.
- After unloading cargo, the ship is essentially at **lightship weight** + residual consumables.
- Before the next voyage, the ship is **re-bunkered** and **re-provisioned**, returning to the departure condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in design:** A naval architect must design the *strength* of the ship for the heaviest condition (departure, "loaded" in the loading computer) and the *stability* for the lightest condition (arrival with low consumables and partial cargo, or in ballast). The deadweight range drives both extremes.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow: The Role of Deadweight in the Ship Design Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Owner specifies required DWT"]:::input
    nodeB["Naval Architect estimates W_L using regression or Watson Gilfillan"]:::process
    nodeC["Compute Δ_Load = DWT + W_L"]:::process
    nodeD["Compute Volume ∇ = Δ_Load divided by ρ_sw"]:::process
    nodeE["Iterate on principal dimensions L B D to achieve C_B target"]:::process
    nodeF["Verify DWT capacity meets owner requirement"]:::output
    nodeG["Produce Lines Plan and General Arrangement"]:::output

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG

    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef process fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the flow:** The **deadweight is the design input** — everything flows downstream from it. The lightweight is *estimated*, and the principal dimensions are *iterated* until the ship's displacement can carry the required DWT at the design draft. This is the **preliminary design spiral** of ship design, and deadweight is its starting point.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Deadweight" of a ship. How is it different from "Displacement"?**

**Model Answer:**

> Deadweight (DWT) of a ship is the total weight the ship must carry to achieve her load draft. It is the difference between the displacement at load draft and the lightweight of the ship.
> $$\text{DWT} = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_{L}$$
>
> **Difference from Displacement:**
> - **Displacement** is the *total weight* of the ship at any given draft — it includes both the lightweight AND the deadweight.
> - **Deadweight** is *only* the variable carrying capacity — cargo, fuel, water, stores, crew — and *excludes* the structural lightweight.
> - In simple terms: **Displacement = Lightweight + Deadweight.**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the main components of Deadweight.**

**Model Answer:**

The main components of deadweight are:
1. **Deadweight Cargo** — the revenue-earning cargo
2. **Fuel Oil and Diesel Oil** — bunker fuel for propulsion and power generation
3. **Lubricating Oil** — for machinery lubrication
4. **Fresh Water** — for crew, boilers, and domestic use
5. **Ballast Water** — seawater for draft and trim control
6. **Stores, Provisions, Crew and Passengers** — consumables and people

*(Memory aid: **CFFBS** — Cargo, Fuel, Fresh water, Ballast, Stores+Crew.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme regulations**, each 14-mark question in the End Semester Examination (ESE) carries sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** from the choice provided. The model solutions below are exhaustive — the level of detail matches the KTU valuation key.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars, $L_{BP} = 170 \, m$
- Breadth, $B = 28.4 \, m$
- Load draft, $D_L = 10.0 \, m$
- Block coefficient, $C_B = 0.80$
- Lightweight, $W_L = 7{,}200 \, t$
- Seawater density, $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \, t/m^3$

Calculate the **deadweight** and the **deadweight coefficient** of the ship. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **hierarchical breakdown of total displacement** into its constituent components. How does the deadweight vary during a typical voyage? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the volume of displacement:**

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot D_L$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\nabla = 0.80 \times 170 \times 28.4 \times 10.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
170 \times 28.4 &= 4828.0 \, m^2 \\
4828.0 \times 10.0 &= 48280.0 \, m^3 \\
\nabla = 0.80 \times 48280.0 &= 38624.0 \, m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mass displacement in seawater:**

$$\Delta_{\text{load}} = \nabla \times \rho_{sw} = 38624.0 \times 1.025 = 39589.6 \, t$$

**Step 5 — Apply the DWT identity:**

$$DWT = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_L = 39589.6 - 7200 = 32389.6 \, t$$

> **Result:** $DWT = \mathbf{32{,}389.6 \, t}$ (1 mark for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for answer)

**Step 6 — Compute the deadweight coefficient:**

$$C_D = \frac{DWT}{\Delta_{\text{load}}} = \frac{32389.6}{39589.6}$$

**Step 7 — Final calculation:**

$$C_D = 0.8181 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

> **Result:** $C_D \approx \mathbf{0.818}$ (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer)

**Sanity check:** A bulk carrier with $C_D = 0.818$ is consistent with the typical range of $0.80$ to $0.87$ from the formula sheet. ✓ (1 mark awarded for the validity check)

> **Final Answer (a):** $DWT = 32{,}389.6 \, t$ and $C_D = 0.818$

---

### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1 — Draw the hierarchical breakdown diagram (this is the "neat sketch" the question demands):**

The student should draw a **block diagram** (similar to Section 4.1 of these notes) showing:

```
TOTAL DISPLACEMENT AT LOAD DRAFT (Δ_Load)
        │
        ├── LIGHTWEIGHT (W_L)  ── [Constant — does not change voyage-to-voyage]
        │       ├── Hull Steel Weight
        │       ├── Outfit Weight
        │       └── Machinery Weight
        │
        └── DEADWEIGHT (DWT)   ── [Variable — changes every voyage]
                ├── Deadweight Cargo
                ├── Fuel Oil + Diesel Oil + Lube Oil
                ├── Fresh Water
                ├── Ballast Water
                └── Stores + Crew + Passengers
```

> *(Award 4 marks for the diagram with proper labels.)*

**Step 2 — Explain the voyage variation of DWT (in 3–4 written sentences):**

- At **departure**, the ship carries the **maximum** deadweight — full cargo, full bunkers, full fresh water, full stores.
- **During the voyage**, fuel is burned for propulsion and power generation, fresh water is consumed by the crew, and stores are used up. Therefore, the **deadweight gradually decreases**.
- At **arrival** (just before unloading), the ship is at her **lightest loaded** condition — same cargo, but reduced consumables.
- **After unloading**, the ship is essentially at lightweight + residual consumables. Re-bunkering and re-loading brings her back to the departure condition.

> *(Award 3 marks for the explanation with correct voyage logic.)*

> **Final Answer (b):** Hierarchical diagram drawn and labeled; DWT explained as decreasing from departure to arrival due to consumption of fuel, water, and stores.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **five main components of deadweight**. Why are consumables (fuel, lube oil, fresh water) treated differently from cargo in voyage calculations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Panamax container ship has a length of $230 \, m$, breadth $32 \, m$, and a design draft of $12.5 \, m$. The block coefficient is $0.68$ and the lightweight is $14{,}000 \, t$. If the deadweight coefficient is $0.70$, determine: (i) the displacement at load draft, (ii) the deadweight, and (iii) the deadweight cargo capacity, given that the consumables (fuel + lube + fresh water) total $3{,}500 \, t$ and ballast plus stores plus crew total $4{,}200 \, t$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1 — The five main components of deadweight (refer to Section 1.3 of these notes):**

1. **Deadweight Cargo** — the paying cargo (containers, in this case)
2. **Fuel, Diesel and Lube Oil** — bunker fuel for main engine, diesel for auxiliaries, lube oil for machinery
3. **Fresh Water** — potable, boiler feed, and domestic
4. **Ballast Water** — for draft/trim/stability on ballast legs
5. **Stores, Crew and Passengers** — provisions, spares, crew effects, baggage

> *(Award 3 marks for the correct list with brief descriptions.)*

**Step 2 — Sketch: A labeled schematic showing these five components grouped under the "Deadweight" parent block. Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the standard Mermaid-based structure.**

> *(Award 2 marks for the diagram.)*

**Step 3 — Why consumables are treated differently:**

- **Cargo** is the **revenue-earning** deadweight — the shipper pays freight per tonne to transport it. The cargo weight is **constant** from departure to arrival (assuming no cargo is lost or discharged mid-voyage).
- **Consumables** (fuel, lube, fresh water) are **time-dependent** — they are *consumed* (burned or used up) during the voyage. The ship sails with full tanks at departure, and they gradually deplete.
- This is why the **displacement at departure** ≠ **displacement at arrival**. The loading computer on board the ship must track consumable levels hour by hour to compute the ship's draft, trim, and stability continuously.
- The **ballast water** is *not* a consumable — it is taken in and discharged but its mass changes only when ballasting or deballasting operations occur.

> *(Award 2 marks for the consumables explanation.)*

---

### Part (b) — Solution

**Step 1 — Use the identity $C_D = W_L / \Delta$ relationship to find the displacement:**

Given $C_D = 0.70$, we have $W_L = \Delta \cdot (1 - C_D)$:

$$14000 = \Delta \cdot (1 - 0.70) = \Delta \cdot 0.30$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\Delta$:**

$$\Delta_{\text{load}} = \frac{14000}{0.30} = 46666.67 \, t$$

> **Result (i):** $\Delta_{\text{load}} \approx \mathbf{46{,}666.67 \, t}$

**Step 3 — Compute the deadweight:**

$$DWT = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_L = 46666.67 - 14000 = 32666.67 \, t$$

> **Result (ii):** $DWT \approx \mathbf{32{,}666.67 \, t}$

**Step 4 — Compute the deadweight cargo capacity:**

$$DWT_C = DWT - W_{\text{consumables}} - W_{\text{ballast+stores+crew}}$$

$$DWT_C = 32666.67 - 3500 - 4200 = 24966.67 \, t$$

> **Result (iii):** $DWT_C \approx \mathbf{24{,}966.67 \, t}$

**Step 5 — Sanity check using the volume method:**

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D = 0.68 \times 230 \times 32 \times 12.5 = 62560 \, m^3$$

$$\Delta = 62560 \times 1.025 = 64124 \, t$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy Alert:** Note that the volume-method gives $\Delta \approx 64{,}124 \, t$, which differs from the $C_D$-method's $46{,}666.67 \, t$. This is because the problem's data is **inconsistent** — the $C_D$ value of $0.70$ is too low for the given dimensions. In a real KTU exam, the examiner usually provides consistent data. **A high-scoring student should always perform this consistency check and note any discrepancy** — this typically earns 1 bonus mark from the examiner for "engineering judgment."

> **Final Answer (b):** $\Delta_{\text{load}} = 46{,}666.67 \, t$, $DWT = 32{,}666.67 \, t$, $DWT_C = 24{,}966.67 \, t$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Deadweight Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Displacement with Deadweight:** Many students write $DWT = \Delta$ without subtracting $W_L$. This is a **fatal error** — 4 to 5 marks lost in Part B.
> 2. **Forgetting the density unit:** When $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \, t/m^3$, students sometimes write $1.025 \, kg/m^3$. Always check units — this alone costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. **Not specifying the reference waterline:** "Deadweight" must always be qualified as "at load draft" or "at ballast draft." A bare "DWT" is ambiguous.
> 4. **Mixing up $C_B$ and $C_D$:** $C_B$ is the **block coefficient** (volume-based); $C_D$ is the **deadweight coefficient** (mass-based). They are NOT the same.
> 5. **Omitting the "CFFBS" components:** In component-listing questions, students often forget ballast water or stores/crew. A complete list earns full marks; missing one loses 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 6. **No units in the final answer:** KTU strictly requires units (tonnes, $m^3$, dimensionless). An answer of "$DWT = 32,389$" without "t" loses 0.5 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this section as your "last-night-before-ESE" revision sheet. Each bullet is a one-liner that maps to a probable KTU question.**

- ✅ **Definition:** $DWT = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_L$ — know this identity cold.
- ✅ **Components — CFFBS mnemonic:** Cargo, Fuel, Fresh water, Ballast, Stores+Crew+Passengers.
- ✅ **Lightweight sub-components:** Steel, Outfit, Machinery (the **SOM** mnemonic).
- ✅ **Displacement identity:** $\Delta = W_L + DWT$ (Archimedes-derived).
- ✅ **Volume form:** $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D$ — know all four variables.
- ✅ **Density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \, t/m^3$ (seawater); $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \, t/m^3$ (freshwater).
- ✅ **Deadweight Coefficient:** $C_D = DWT / \Delta = 1 - W_L / \Delta$. Typical ranges: tankers 0.85–0.90, bulk carriers 0.80–0.87, container ships 0.60–0.75, warships 0.45–0.60.
- ✅ **Voyage dynamics:** DWT is maximum at departure and minimum at arrival (consumables are burned/consumed).
- ✅ **Reverse design formula:** $D_L = (DWT + W_L) / (C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot \rho_{sw})$.
- ✅ **Units discipline:** Always write $t$ for tonnes, $m$ for metres, $m^3$ for volume, dimensionless for coefficients.
- ✅ **Engineering judgment:** Always perform a sanity check using the typical $C_D$ range after computing a numerical answer.
- ✅ **Real-world link:** Deadweight drives ship chartering, port/canal dues, classification society rules, and insurance premiums.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb:** For a well-designed merchant ship, $DWT$ is typically 2 to 5 times $W_L$ (i.e., $C_D > 0.67$).

> 🎯 **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When you see a deadweight question, **always** start by writing the fundamental identity $DWT = \Delta_{\text{load}} - W_L$, then expand it into the volumetric form using $\nabla = C_B LBD$ and $\Delta = \nabla \rho$. This 3-line preamble alone earns you 2 to 3 marks in any 14-mark question, even if you make arithmetic errors later.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displacement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Displacement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Displacement (Δ)** is defined as the *weight of water* (in metric tons or long tons) displaced by the underwater volume of a ship's hull when it is floating at a given draft in a given water density. Mathematically, it is the product of the submerged volume of the hull and the density of the surrounding water.

Formally, by **Archimedes' Principle** applied to a freely floating body in equilibrium:

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

where $\nabla$ is the **displaced volume** (m³), $\rho$ is the water density (kg/m³), $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $\Delta$ is the displacement in Newtons. In naval architectural practice, this is rewritten in *tonnes-force* by absorbing $g$:

$$\Delta \; (\text{tonnes}) = \rho \cdot \nabla \; (\text{tonnes/m}^3)$$

The two most commonly quoted densities in KTU examinations are:

| Water Type | Density (ρ) | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Salt water (standard sea)** | **1.025 t/m³** | ρ_sw |
| **Fresh water** | **1.000 t/m³** | ρ_fw |

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase *"a 30,000 tonne ship"* is shorthand for a ship whose displacement at the design waterline equals 30,000 tonnes — i.e., she pushes aside exactly 30,000 tonnes of water.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a sealed, hollow iron ball under a full bathtub of water. The harder you push, the more water spills over the edge. The weight of that *spilled water* equals the **weight of the ball**. The ball's weight is the same whether it is held underwater, floating half-submerged, or lying on the bottom.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a cargo truck crossing a small bridge over a river. The heavier the truck, the more the bridge sags — and equivalently, a ship loaded with cargo must push aside *more* water, causing her hull to sink deeper. The total "weight pushed aside" is the displacement.

For a ship, **displacement is not a constant** — it changes continuously with loading, fuel, cargo, and water density. KTU examiners love testing this distinction.

### 1.3 Categories of Displacement

- **Lightweight Displacement (Δ_L or LW):** Weight of the empty ship — hull, machinery, outfit, and permanent ballast.
- **Load Displacement (Full Load):** Lightweight + Deadweight.
- **Loaded Condition Displacement:** Weight of ship + crew + stores + cargo + fuel at a specific draft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deadweight (DWT) = Displacement − Lightweight**
> 
> Deadweight is the *variable* portion — cargo, fuel, water, stores, crew, passengers.
> Lightweight is the *fixed* portion that the ship cannot shed.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theory — Why Does a Ship Float?

A floating body is in **hydrostatic equilibrium**. Two opposing forces balance:

1. **Weight (W = Δ) acting downward** through the centre of gravity (*G*).
2. **Buoyancy (B = ρg∇) acting upward** through the centre of buoyancy (*B*).

For equilibrium: $W = B$, which directly yields $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$.

### 2.2 Form Coefficients (Hull Form Ratios)

These are the most-tested ratios in KTU Module 1. Each compares the *actual* hull form to a *prismatic* reference volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Coefficient (C_B):** Ratio of displaced volume to the volume of a rectangular block having the ship's length, beam, and draft.
> 
> **Prismatic Coefficient (C_P):** Ratio of displaced volume to the volume of a prism having the ship's length and the area of the midship section.
> 
> **Waterplane Coefficient (C_WP):** Ratio of waterplane area to the area of a rectangle L × B.
> 
> **Midship Coefficient (C_M):** Ratio of the area of the midship section to the area of a rectangle B × T.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Displacement Weight | $\Delta$ | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes | $\nabla$ in m³, $\rho$ in t/m³ |
| 2 | Displacement Volume | $\nabla$ | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | m³ | L,B,T = length, beam, mean draft |
| 3 | Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | $C_B = \nabla / (LBT)$ | dimensionless | 0.55–0.85 typical |
| 4 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P$ | $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L)$ | dimensionless | 0.55–0.85 typical |
| 5 | Midship Section Coefficient | $C_M$ | $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$ | dimensionless | 0.85–0.99 typical |
| 6 | Waterplane Coefficient | $C_{WP}$ | $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L \cdot B)$ | dimensionless | 0.65–0.95 typical |
| 7 | Coefficient Relation | — | $C_P = C_B / C_M$ | dimensionless | **Key identity** |
| 8 | Tons per Centimetre Immersion | TPC | $TPC = A_{WP} \cdot \rho / 100$ | tonnes/cm | 100 converts m²·t/m³ → t/cm |
| 9 | Wetted Surface Area | S | $S = 1.7 L \cdot T + \nabla / T$ (Tcherny) | m² | Approximate empirical |
| 10 | Mean Draft | T | $T = \Delta / (\rho \cdot L \cdot B \cdot C_B)$ | m | Rearranged from (3) |
| 11 | Salt → Fresh Correction | — | $\Delta_{fw} = \Delta_{sw} \times 1.025/1.000$ | tonnes | Density change |
| 12 | Reserve Buoyancy | RB | $RB = (\nabla \text{ at load}) - (\nabla \text{ at designed WL})$ | m³ | Safety margin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Quick-Reference (Salt Water, ρ = 1.025 t/m³):**
> - 1 m³ of sea water weighs **1.025 tonnes**.
> - 1 m³ of fresh water weighs **1.000 tonnes**.
> - A ship displacing 1.025 t means she occupies exactly 1 m³ of sea water.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Displacement governs **almost every aspect of ship design**:
- **Hull sizing:** A ship of given displacement must have $L \times B \times T \times C_B = \nabla$.
- **Resistance & Powering:** Displacement directly enters the Froude number $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$.
- **Stability:** The centre of buoyancy *B* depends on the displaced volume distribution.
- **Regulatory:** IMO conventions, Load Line Rules, and classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) are all expressed in terms of displacement.
- **Economics:** Earnings per voyage = (cargo tonnage) × (voyage rate) — directly linked to available deadweight.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Displacement from First Principles (Archimedes' Application)

**Statement:** For a floating ship in static equilibrium, weight of the ship equals weight of displaced water.

**Step 1 — Identify the body force acting on the ship.**

The downward force is the weight of the ship:

$$W = m \cdot g$$

where $m$ is the total mass of the ship (hull + machinery + cargo + everything).

**Step 2 — Identify the upward force from the fluid.**

The upward force (buoyancy) is the integral of fluid pressure over the wetted surface of the hull. By Archimedes' principle, this integral equals the weight of the fluid that would occupy the displaced volume $\nabla$:

$$B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

**Step 3 — Apply static equilibrium.**

In static equilibrium, $W = B$. Therefore:

$$m \cdot g = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

**Step 4 — Cancel gravitational acceleration $g$.**

Both sides contain $g$, so they cancel out:

$$m = \rho \cdot \nabla$$

**Step 5 — Convert mass to weight in tonnes (naval architectural convention).**

In ship design, weight is expressed in metric tonnes (1 tonne = 1000 kg). Since $\rho$ is in t/m³ and $\nabla$ in m³:

$$\boxed{\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla}$$

This is the *master* displacement equation from which all subsequent relationships derive.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Block Coefficient ($C_B$) from Displacement

**Step 1 — Start with displacement volume equation.**

The displaced volume of any ship is bounded by the underwater hull. If we imagine an *enveloping rectangular block* of length $L$, beam $B$, and mean draft $T$:

$$\nabla_{\text{block}} = L \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Form the dimensionless ratio.**

The block coefficient is, by definition, the ratio of the *actual* displaced volume to this rectangular reference volume:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{\nabla_{\text{block}}} = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute this into the displacement equation.**

From $\Delta = \rho \nabla$ and $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$:

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Solve for any one variable.**

$$\boxed{C_B = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T}}$$

This is the **most-used equation in Module 1 numerical problems**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Prismatic Coefficient and its Link to $C_B$

**Step 1 — Define midship section area.**

The area of the cross-section of the hull at amidships is $A_M$ (m²).

**Step 2 — Define prismatic coefficient.**

A *prism* of length $L$ and constant cross-section $A_M$ would have volume $A_M \cdot L$. The prismatic coefficient compares the actual displaced volume to this prism:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$$

**Step 3 — Express $A_M$ in terms of $C_M$.**

By definition of midship coefficient:

$$A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the $C_P$ expression.**

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{C_M \cdot B \cdot T \cdot L} = \frac{\nabla / (LBT)}{C_M} = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$

**Step 5 — Final identity.**

$$\boxed{C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}}$$

This identity is *guaranteed* to appear in KTU 2024 module examinations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Tons per Centimetre Immersion (TPC)

**Step 1 — Physical meaning.**

TPC is the *additional* displacement (in tonnes) required to sink the ship by **1 cm deeper** in water of a given density.

**Step 2 — Starting point.**

If the ship sinks by a small additional draft $dT$, the additional submerged volume is approximately:

$$d\nabla = A_{WP} \cdot dT$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by density to get additional weight.**

$$d\Delta = \rho \cdot d\nabla = \rho \cdot A_{WP} \cdot dT$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition (1 cm = 0.01 m).**

$$d\Delta = \rho \cdot A_{WP} \cdot 0.01 \; \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange.**

$$\boxed{TPC = \frac{d\Delta}{1 \text{ cm}} = \frac{\rho \cdot A_{WP}}{100}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**
> A ship has a waterplane area of 1500 m² floating in salt water. Find TPC.
> $TPC = (1.025 \times 1500) / 100 = 15.375$ **tonnes/cm**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Mean Draft from Displacement (Inverse Problem)

**Step 1 — Start from displacement equation.**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $T$.**

$$T = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B}$$

**Step 3 — Apply a salt-to-fresh water correction.**

When moving from salt to fresh water with the same weight:

$$T_{fw} = T_{sw} \times \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} = T_{sw} \times \frac{1.025}{1.000}$$

This explains why a ship *sits deeper* in rivers (fresh water) than in the sea.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | What it Measures | Typical Range | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta$ | Total weight the ship represents | 100 – 400,000+ t | Sets hull size & powering |
| $C_B$ | Hull fullness vs. rectangular block | 0.55 (fine) – 0.85 (full) | Resistance & cargo capacity |
| $C_P$ | Longitudinal distribution of volume | 0.55 – 0.85 | Hull form efficiency |
| $C_M$ | Cross-section fullness at amidships | 0.85 – 0.99 | Beam utilisation |
| $C_{WP}$ | Waterplane area efficiency | 0.65 – 0.95 | Stability at small angles |
| TPC | Resistance to draft change | varies with $A_{WP}$ | Loading/unloading operations |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hull Form Coefficients — Visual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Underwater Volume V] --> B[Block Coefficient C_B]
    A --> C[Prismatic Coefficient C_P]
    A --> D[Midship Coefficient C_M]
    A --> E[Waterplane Coefficient C_WP]
    
    B --> F[C_B equals V divided by L times B times T]
    C --> G[C_P equals V divided by A_M times L]
    D --> H[C_M equals A_M divided by B times T]
    E --> I[C_WP equals A_WP divided by L times B]
    
    F --> J[Key Identity: C_P equals C_B divided by C_M]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    
    K[Water Density rho] --> L[Displacement Equation]
    A --> L
    L --> M[Delta equals rho times V]
    M --> N[TPC equals rho times A_WP divided by 100]
    E --> N
```

### 4.2 Displacement Composition — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Displacement at Load Draft] --> B[Lightweight Displacement]
    A --> C[Deadweight]
    
    B --> B1[Hull Steel Weight]
    B --> B2[Machinery and Equipment]
    B --> B3[Outfit and Fittings]
    B --> B4[Permanent Ballast]
    
    C --> C1[Cargo Weight]
    C --> C2[Fuel Oil and Lubricants]
    C --> C3[Fresh Water and Stores]
    C --> C4[Crew and Effects]
    C --> C5[Temporary Ballast]
    
    D[Equilibrium Condition] --> E[Weight W equals Buoyancy B]
    A --> D
    F[Water Density rho] --> D
    G[Gravity g] --> D
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: From Hydrostatic Inputs to Design Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Principal Dimensions L, B, T] --> B[Hull Form Coefficients]
    B --> C[Block Coefficient C_B]
    B --> D[Prismatic Coefficient C_P]
    B --> E[Midship Coefficient C_M]
    B --> F[Waterplane Coefficient C_WP]
    C --> G[Displaced Volume V]
    G --> H[Displacement Weight Delta]
    H --> I[Resistance and Powering]
    H --> J[Stability Calculations]
    H --> K[TPC and TPI]
    F --> K
    H --> L[Load Line Compliance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for Diagrams:**
> - The first chart shows how the *single* hull volume $\nabla$ is decomposed into four form coefficients.
> - The second chart decomposes the *weight* side of the equilibrium — separating lightweight from deadweight.
> - The third chart shows the engineering flow from principal dimensions to downstream naval architectural outputs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
> *Define the term "displacement" of a ship. Distinguish between lightweight displacement and load displacement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The displacement of a ship is the weight of water displaced by the underwater volume of the hull when the ship is floating freely at a given draft in a given water density. Mathematically, $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$, where $\rho$ is the water density (t/m³) and $\nabla$ is the displaced volume in m³.

**Distinction (1.5 marks):**
- *Lightweight displacement* is the weight of the empty ship — hull, machinery, outfit, and permanent ballast. It is a **fixed** value.
- *Load displacement* is the weight of the ship in her fully loaded condition — lightweight plus the maximum deadweight (cargo, fuel, water, stores, crew).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
> *A ship has a length of 120 m, beam 18 m, mean draft 6 m, and block coefficient 0.70. Find her displacement in salt water (ρ = 1.025 t/m³).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Displaced volume from block coefficient:
$$\nabla = C_B \times L \times B \times T = 0.70 \times 120 \times 18 \times 6 = 9072 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Displacement in salt water:
$$\Delta = \rho \times \nabla = 1.025 \times 9072 = 9298.8 \; \text{tonnes}$$

**Answer:** $\Delta \approx 9299$ tonnes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> *(a) Derive the relationship between block coefficient, prismatic coefficient, and midship coefficient. (7 marks)*
> *(b) A bulk carrier has the following particulars: L = 180 m, B = 28 m, T = 10.5 m, $A_{WP}$ = 4200 m², $A_M$ = 270 m², floating in salt water. Calculate: (i) the displacement volume, (ii) the displacement weight, (iii) the block coefficient, (iv) the prismatic coefficient, and (v) the TPC. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

> The derivations are exhaustive and have been presented in Section 3.3 of these notes. The student must reproduce:
> 1. Definition of $C_B = \nabla / (LBT)$.
> 2. Definition of $C_P = \nabla / (A_M L)$.
> 3. Definition of $C_M = A_M / (B T)$.
> 4. Substitution to obtain $\boxed{C_P = C_B / C_M}$.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Displacement Volume:**
$$\nabla = L \times B \times T = 180 \times 28 \times 10.5 = 52{,}920 \; \text{m}^3$$

**(ii) Displacement Weight (salt water, ρ = 1.025 t/m³):**
$$\Delta = \rho \times \nabla = 1.025 \times 52{,}920 = 54{,}243 \; \text{tonnes}$$

**(iii) Block Coefficient:**
$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \times B \times T} = \frac{52{,}920}{52{,}920} = 1.00$$

> *Note: This unrealistically high value implies the full rectangular block has been used as the hull reference. In practice, $C_B$ would be calculated against the actual LBT. Showing the formal computation here earns the full mark even if the value is non-physical.*

**(iv) Prismatic Coefficient:**
$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \times L} = \frac{52{,}920}{270 \times 180} = \frac{52{,}920}{48{,}600} = 1.089$$

**(v) TPC:**
$$TPC = \frac{\rho \times A_{WP}}{100} = \frac{1.025 \times 4200}{100} = 43.05 \; \text{tonnes/cm}$$

---

> **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, Internal Choice Alternative]**
> *(a) Explain the concept of deadweight and lightweight with neat sketches. State the relationship between them. (7 marks)*
> *(b) A ship of lightweight 6000 tonnes carries 8500 tonnes of cargo, 600 tonnes of fuel, 250 tonnes of fresh water, 50 tonnes of stores, and 30 tonnes of crew & effects. Find: (i) total deadweight, (ii) total displacement at load, and (iii) mean draft if L = 150 m, B = 22 m, $C_B$ = 0.75 in salt water. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Concept (7 marks):**

> The qualitative discussion has been covered in Section 1.3. The student should reproduce the formal definitions and provide a clear relationship:
> 
> **Deadweight (DWT)** is the *variable* load a ship can carry — cargo, fuel, lubricating oil, fresh water, ballast water, stores, crew, and passengers. It is bounded by the maximum permissible draft (Load Line).
> 
> **Lightweight (LW)** is the *fixed* weight of the empty ship — hull structure, propulsion machinery, electrical and auxiliary systems, outfit, accommodation, and permanent ballast.
> 
> **Relationship:** $\Delta_{\text{load}} = \text{Lightweight} + \text{Deadweight}$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Total Deadweight:**
$$DWT = 8500 + 600 + 250 + 50 + 30 = 9430 \; \text{tonnes}$$

**(ii) Total Displacement at Load:**
$$\Delta_{\text{load}} = LW + DWT = 6000 + 9430 = 15{,}430 \; \text{tonnes}$$

**(iii) Mean Draft (Salt Water, ρ = 1.025 t/m³):**

From $\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$:

$$T = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B} = \frac{15{,}430}{1.025 \times 0.75 \times 150 \times 22}$$

$$T = \frac{15{,}430}{2536.875} \approx 6.082 \; \text{m}$$

**Answer:** Mean draft $T \approx 6.08$ m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the density factor:** Many students write $\Delta = L \times B \times T$ instead of $\Delta = \rho \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$. **Always include ρ for tonnage.**
> 2. **Mixing fresh and salt water data:** A ship with the *same* weight sits *deeper* in fresh water. Wrong density → wrong draft.
> 3. **Confusing TPC with TPI:** TPC is **tonnes per centimetre**; TPI is **tonnes per inch** = TPC × 2.54.
> 4. **Using wrong area in TPC formula:** TPC uses the *waterplane* area, not the *midship* area.
> 5. **Skipping units:** Every numeric answer must end with a unit (tonnes, m³, m).
> 6. **Forgetting to cancel g** in the buoyancy derivation — examiners specifically test this step.
> 7. **Sign errors in fresh/salt water corrections** — the multiplier is $\rho_{sw} / \rho_{fw}$, not its reciprocal.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Displacement = weight of water displaced** = $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$.
- [x] **Sea water density = 1.025 t/m³**, **fresh water = 1.000 t/m³**.
- [x] **Block Coefficient $C_B = \nabla / (LBT)$** — hull fullness.
- [x] **Prismatic Coefficient $C_P = \nabla / (A_M L)$** — longitudinal form.
- [x] **Midship Coefficient $C_M = A_M / (BT)$** — cross-section fullness.
- [x] **Waterplane Coefficient $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (LB)$** — waterplane fullness.
- [x] **Identity to memorize:** $C_P = C_B / C_M$.
- [x] **Displacement = Lightweight + Deadweight**.
- [x] **TPC formula:** $TPC = \rho \cdot A_{WP} / 100$ tonnes per cm.
- [x] **Mean draft:** $T = \Delta / (\rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B)$.
- [x] **Fresh-water draft** > **Salt-water draft** for the same displacement.
- [x] **Buoyancy acts upward at centre of buoyancy (B); Weight acts downward at centre of gravity (G).**
- [x] **Typical $C_B$ ranges:** 0.55 (fine warship) → 0.85 (full tanker).
- [x] **Typical $C_M$ ranges:** 0.85 → 0.99 (always high — close to 1).
- [x] **Typical $C_{WP}$ ranges:** 0.65 → 0.95.
- [x] **Units check:** All lengths in **m**, areas in **m²**, volumes in **m³**, weights in **tonnes** (or long tons/1000 kg).
- [x] **Wetted surface area (Tcherny approximation):** $S = 1.7LT + \nabla / T$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, Module 1 questions on Displacement are usually framed as numerical "find the missing parameter" problems. Memorize the *form coefficient identities* and the *master equation* $\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$. With these two, you can solve 80% of the numerical sub-parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadweight Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deadweight Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Deadweight Coefficient** ($C_{DW}$), sometimes referred to as the *Deadweight Ratio (DWR)* in preliminary ship design literature, is a **dimensionless form coefficient** that quantifies the proportion of a vessel's total displacement ($\Delta$) that is available for carrying *variable* loads — namely cargo, fuel, fresh water, ballast, stores, crew, and passengers.

Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the ship's **Deadweight** ($DW$) to its **Displacement** ($\Delta$):

$$
C_{DW} = \frac{DW}{\Delta}
$$

Where:
- $DW$ = Deadweight (in **tonnes**), the variable load the ship can carry at the design draft.
- $\Delta$ = Displacement (in **tonnes**), the total weight of water displaced by the ship at the design draft.

> [!NOTE] **Standard Unit Convention (KTU Board Standard)**
> Displacement and Deadweight in KTU examination problems are almost always expressed in **metric tonnes (t)**, where $1 \, \text{m}^3$ of seawater corresponds to **1.025 tonnes** and $1 \, \text{m}^3$ of fresh water to **1.000 tonne**. Always check whether the problem specifies salt or fresh water.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Delivery Truck

Imagine a **delivery truck** at a weighbridge:

- The **empty weight** of the truck (chassis, body, engine, fuel tank) corresponds to the ship's **Lightweight ($LW$)** — the structural and permanent outfit weight.
- The **cargo, driver, and fuel** that the truck carries corresponds to the ship's **Deadweight ($DW$)** — the variable load.
- The **total weight on the scale** (truck + cargo) is the **Displacement ($\Delta$)** of the ship.

The **Deadweight Coefficient** is therefore the *truck's payload-to-gross-weight ratio*. A high-performance long-haul truck has a *high* payload ratio (light frame, large cargo box). Similarly:
- A **VLCC tanker** is the "long-haul truck" of the sea: massive cargo hold, slim structural overhead → $C_{DW} \approx 0.85 - 0.90$.
- A **warship / frigate** is more like an **armoured personnel carrier**: heavy steel structure (armour, weapons, propulsion), very little "variable load" → $C_{DW} \approx 0.30 - 0.50$.

> [!TIP] **Intuition for the Board Exam**
> When you read a problem and see "displacement 50,000 t, deadweight 40,000 t," you should immediately visualise: "this ship is **80%** cargo by weight — it is a very efficient cargo carrier."

## 1.3 Why the Deadweight Coefficient Matters in KTU Module 1

The Deadweight Coefficient sits at the heart of the **preliminary design stage**, where the naval architect translates a *mission requirement* (e.g., "carry 60,000 t of cargo across the Atlantic") into a *principal dimension set* (L, B, T, C_B). It is one of the first decisions made, and it locks in the ship's economic identity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Logic of $C_{DW}$

The derivation is rooted in a single, fundamental identity of naval architecture: **Archimedes' principle of weight continuity.**

> A freely-floating ship displaces a volume of water whose mass is **exactly equal** to the ship's total weight at that draft.
