- **W_P — Permanent Ballast** (sometimes).
- **W_L — Miscellaneous Loads** (cargo gear, dunnage, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 terminology, the terms **Deadweight (DWT)** and **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** are often used interchangeably. Strictly, *DWT* refers to the **carrying capacity** (W_C + W_F + W_S + …), while *Δ* is the actual displacement at a given condition.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = W_{LSW} + W_{DWT}$ | Master weight equation |
| 2 | $\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho}$ | Volumetric displacement (m³) |
| 3 | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | Block coefficient definition |
| 4 | $W_H = C_{HW} \cdot L^{a_1} B^{a_2} D^{a_3} T^{a_4} C_B^{a_5}$ | Parametric hull weight |
| 5 | $W_M = a \cdot P^{b}$ (P in kW) | Machinery weight vs. installed power |
| 6 | $W_O = k_O \cdot L^{c} \quad \text{or} \quad W_O = k_O \cdot (L+B+D)^c$ | Outfit weight |
| 7 | $W_{fuel} = \dfrac{P_{S} \cdot R \cdot t}{10^6} \cdot SF$ | Bunker fuel weight (tonnes) |
| 8 | $\text{SF Ratio} = \dfrac{W_{LSW}}{\Delta}$ | Steel weight factor (typically 0.30 – 0.50) |
| 9 | $DWT = \Delta - W_{LSW}$ | Re-arranged weight equation |
| 10 | $W_C = \rho_c \cdot V_C$ | Cargo weight = stowage factor × volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stowage Factor (SF)** is the volume (m³) occupied by 1 tonne of cargo. Light cargoes (cereals: 1.4 – 2.0 m³/t) demand more volume per tonne than heavy cargoes (steel: 0.4 m³/t).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Preliminary Design Tonnage Estimation** — owners, shipyards, and classification societies use the weight equation to fix contract tonnages *before* any GA drawing is produced.
- **Design Spiral Convergence** — every iteration in the spiral adjusts the principal dimensions to make Δ match the sum of weights.
- **Stability and Trim Booklet** — the loading condition book depends on accurate weight decomposition.
- **Production Costing** — each weight component translates into labour and material cost; incorrect W_H estimates cause major material wastage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Weight Equation

Consider a ship floating at rest in calm water. Archimedes' principle dictates that the upward buoyancy force equals the weight of displaced water:

$$F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

where **∇** is the displaced volume (m³) and **g** is gravitational acceleration. The total downward force is the weight of the ship and all its contents:

$$F_W = \Delta \cdot g$$

Equilibrium ($F_B = F_W$) gives, after cancelling $g$:

$$\boxed{\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla}$$

This is the *first principle* form. Substituting the geometric definition $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$:

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \quad \text{[tonnes]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In ship design practice, **Δ** is most often expressed in **tonnes (t)**, and the equation is written with the density of seawater **1.025 t/m³** baked in. The form Δ = ρ·C_B·L·B·T is the **hydrodynamic** version of the weight equation.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Component Weight Sub-Equations

#### 3.2.1 Lightship Steel Weight (W_H)

The classical **Watson & Gilfillan** form (and its modern variants such as **Mewis, Korkmaz**) expresses hull steel weight as a power product of principal dimensions. The general KTU form is:

$$W_H = C_{HW} \cdot L^{a_1} \cdot B^{a_2} \cdot D^{a_3} \cdot T^{a_4} \cdot C_B^{a_5}$$

For a typical merchant ship (after curve-fitting to parent data), one commonly used simplified form is:

$$W_H = C_{HW} \cdot L^{1.5} \cdot B^{0.5} \cdot D^{0.4} \cdot T^{0.4} \cdot (1 + 0.5(C_B - 0.7))$$

where $C_{HW}$ is a coefficient that captures material density and design-specific factors (typical value ≈ 0.016 – 0.020 for mild steel, ships).

**Step-by-step evaluation logic:**

1. Compute $(1 + 0.5(C_B - 0.7))$ — the *form factor correction*.
2. Multiply the dimensional power product $L^{1.5} B^{0.5} D^{0.4} T^{0.4}$.
3. Multiply by the material coefficient $C_{HW}$.
4. Add a 5 – 10 % margin for marginal stiffeners, brackets, and the rudder/funnel (often captured in a separate *auxiliary* term).

#### 3.2.2 Machinery Weight (W_M)

The machinery weight is most strongly correlated with **installed main engine power** (MCR). A KTU-grade parametric form is:

$$W_M = a \cdot P^{b}$$

where $P$ is the **MCR in kW**, and $(a, b)$ are ship-type-dependent constants. Examples:

| Ship Type | a | b |
|---|---|---|
| Bulker (slow diesel) | 0.85 | 0.50 |
| Tanker (steam turbine) | 1.20 | 0.45 |
| Container ship (medium-speed diesel) | 1.00 | 0.50 |

**Derivation logic:**

1. Power $P$ drives the size of the main engine block.
2. Auxiliaries scale with $P^{0.7}$ approximately.
3. Shafting, propeller, and boiler weights are added as fixed percentages.

#### 3.2.3 Outfit Weight (W_O)

A common KTU-grade form:

$$W_O = k_O \cdot (L + B + D)^{c}$$

or the simpler linear form:

$$W_O = k_O \cdot L \quad \text{with } k_O \in [0.5, 0.8] \text{ t/m}$$

**Logic:**

1. Outfit scales with hull surface area (paint) and internal volume (accommodation).
2. $(L + B + D)$ approximates the *overall envelope* size.
3. The exponent $c \approx 1.5$ for tankers, $\approx 1.0$ for small craft.

#### 3.2.4 Fuel Weight (W_F)

The bunker fuel consumed over the design voyage is:

$$W_F = \frac{P_S \cdot g_{sfc} \cdot R}{10^6} \cdot SF \quad \text{[tonnes]}$$

where:
- $P_S$ = service power (kW) — typically 80 – 90 % of MCR.
- $g_{sfc}$ = specific fuel consumption (g/kWh) — typically 160 – 200 for slow diesels.
- $R$ = voyage range (nautical miles).
- $SF$ = sea margin factor (1.10 – 1.20).
- The factor $10^6$ converts g → tonnes.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Solving the Weight Equation (Iterative Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Note:** Numerical problems on the weight equation *always* require an iterative solution because the unknown appears implicitly through the form coefficients and the dimensional cube.

**Problem Statement:**

Design a small general-cargo ship with the following *required* deadweight components:

- Cargo: $W_C = 4500$ t
- Fuel: $W_F = 350$ t
- Fresh water: $W_{FW} = 90$ t
- Stores/crew: $W_{SC} = 60$ t

**Trial 1 (initial guess):**

Assume $L = 110$ m, $B = 16$ m, $D = 9$ m, $T = 6.5$ m, $C_B = 0.70$.

**Step 1:** Compute W_DWT

$$W_{DWT} = 4500 + 350 + 90 + 60 = 5000 \; \text{t}$$

**Step 2:** Compute ∇ from initial geometry

$$\nabla_1 = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T = 0.70 \times 110 \times 16 \times 6.5$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_1 &= 0.70 \times 110 \times 16 \times 6.5 \\
&= 0.70 \times 11440 \\
&= 8008 \; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Δ from ∇

$$\Delta_1 = \rho \cdot \nabla_1 = 1.025 \times 8008 = 8208.2 \; \text{t}$$

**Step 4:** Compute W_H (using C_HW = 0.018)

$$W_H = 0.018 \times L^{1.5} B^{0.5} D^{0.4} T^{0.4} \times (1 + 0.5(0.70 - 0.70))$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L^{1.5} &= 110^{1.5} = 110 \times \sqrt{110} = 110 \times 10.488 = 1153.7 \\
B^{0.5} &= \sqrt{16} = 4.0 \\
D^{0.4} &= 9^{0.4} = 2.408 \\
T^{0.4} &= 6.5^{0.4} = 2.144 \\
\text{Product} &= 1153.7 \times 4.0 \times 2.408 \times 2.144 = 23828
\end{aligned}$$

$$W_H = 0.018 \times 23828 = 429 \; \text{t}$$

**Step 5:** Compute W_M (assume slow diesel, a=0.85, b=0.50, P=6000 kW)

$$W_M = 0.85 \times 6000^{0.5} = 0.85 \times 77.46 = 65.8 \; \text{t}$$

**Step 6:** Compute W_O (k_O = 0.6, L = 110 m)

$$W_O = 0.6 \times 110 = 66 \; \text{t}$$

**Step 7:** Total W_LSW

$$W_{LSW} = 429 + 66 + 66 = 561 \; \text{t}$$

**Step 8:** Compare with Δ_1

$$\Delta_{req} = W_{LSW} + W_{DWT} = 561 + 5000 = 5561 \; \text{t}$$

But $\Delta_1 = 8208$ t — the ship is *over-displaced* (too large). The W_DWT was over-provided relative to the available hull volume. **Reduce L or T.**

**Trial 2:** Set $L = 100$ m, $T = 5.8$ m (others unchanged).

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_2 &= 0.70 \times 100 \times 16 \times 5.8 = 6496 \; \text{m}^3 \\
\Delta_2 &= 1.025 \times 6496 = 6658 \; \text{t}
\end{aligned}$$

W_H (recompute):

$$L^{1.5} = 1000, \quad B^{0.5} = 4, \quad D^{0.4} = 2.408, \quad T^{0.4} = 5.8^{0.4} = 2.030$$

$$W_H = 0.018 \times 1000 \times 4 \times 2.408 \times 2.030 = 352 \; \text{t}$$

$$W_{LSW} = 352 + 66 + 60 = 478 \; \text{t}$$

(Note: W_O recomputed as $0.6 \times 100 = 60$ t)

$$\Delta_{req} = 478 + 5000 = 5478 \; \text{t}$$

But $\Delta_2 = 6658$ t — still over-displaced. The cargo requirement cannot be met with the assumed dimensions. **Either increase W_C requirements or relax the trial** by raising the trial $L$ while reducing $B/T$ ratios.

**Trial 3 (final convergence):** $L = 130$ m, $B = 18$ m, $D = 10$ m, $T = 7.5$ m, $C_B = 0.72$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_3 &= 0.72 \times 130 \times 18 \times 7.5 = 12636 \; \text{m}^3 \\
\Delta_3 &= 1.025 \times 12636 = 12951 \; \text{t}
\end{aligned}$$

$$W_H = 0.018 \times 130^{1.5} \times 18^{0.5} \times 10^{0.4} \times 7.5^{0.4} \times (1 + 0.5 \times 0.02)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
130^{1.5} &= 130 \times \sqrt{130} = 130 \times 11.402 = 1482.3 \\
18^{0.5} &= 4.243 \\
10^{0.4} &= 2.512 \\
7.5^{0.4} &= 2.213 \\
\text{Product} &= 1482.3 \times 4.243 \times 2.512 \times 2.213 = 34994 \\
W_H &= 0.018 \times 34994 \times 1.01 = 636 \; \text{t}
\end{aligned}$$

$$W_M = 0.85 \times 6000^{0.5} = 65.8 \; \text{t}, \quad W_O = 0.6 \times 130 = 78 \; \text{t}$$

$$W_{LSW} = 636 + 66 + 78 = 780 \; \text{t}$$

$$\Delta_{req} = 780 + 5000 = 5780 \; \text{t}$$

But $\Delta_3 = 12951$ t — grossly over-displaced! The issue is that the cargo requirement (4500 t) is much less than what this geometry provides. **Reduce the dimensions.**

This trial-and-error process — converging to a self-consistent set of (L, B, T, C_B, W) — is precisely the **Design Spiral** of preliminary ship design.

### 3.4 Convergence Criterion

Iteration terminates when:

$$\left| \frac{\Delta^{(n)} - \Delta_{req}^{(n)}}{\Delta^{(n)}} \right| \leq \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon = 0.01 \text{ (1\%)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Weight Equation as a System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Requirement W_C, W_F, W_S] --> B[Assume Principal Dimensions L, B, T, C_B]
    B --> C[Compute Volumetric Displacement nabla]
    C --> D[Compute Displacement Delta equals rho times nabla]
    D --> E[Compute W_H Parametric Hull Weight]
    D --> F[Compute W_M Parametric Machinery Weight]
    D --> G[Compute W_O Parametric Outfit Weight]
    E --> H[Sum W_LSW equals W_H plus W_M plus W_O]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    A --> I[Sum W_DWT equals W_C plus W_F plus W_S plus W_Cr]
    H --> J[Required Delta equals W_LSW plus W_DWT]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Delta required equals Delta geometric?}
    K -- No --> L[Adjust L, B, T]
    L --> B
    K -- Yes --> M[Converged Design Solution]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Decomposition of Weights

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    delta[Delta - Displacement] --> lsw[W_LSW - Lightship]
    delta --> dwt[W_DWT - Deadweight]
    lsw --> wh[W_H - Hull Steel]
    lsw --> wm[W_M - Machinery]
    lsw --> wo[W_O - Outfit]
    dwt --> wc[W_C - Cargo]
    dwt --> wf[W_F - Fuel]
    dwt --> wfw[W_FW - Fresh Water]
    dwt --> wsc[W_SC - Stores and Crew]
    dwt --> wp[W_P - Permanent Ballast]
    dwt --> wl[W_L - Miscellaneous]
```

### 4.3 The Four-Loop Design Spiral (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Loop 1: Main Dimensions L, B, T] --> B[Loop 2: Hull Form and Hydrostatics]
    B --> C[Loop 3: Powering and Resistance]
    C --> D[Loop 4: Weight Equation and Displacement]
    D --> A
    A -.->|after convergence| E[Preliminary GA]
    E --> F[Structural Design]
    F --> G[General Arrangement]
    G --> H[Detailed Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **weight equation** is **Loop 4** of the classic four-loop design spiral proposed by **Watt** and refined in modern KTU pedagogical practice.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the **Lightship Weight (W_LSW)** and list its three principal components with a one-line description of each.

**Model Answer:**
The Lightship Weight (W_LSW) is the weight of the complete ship *without* cargo, fuel, fresh water, stores, or crew. It represents the permanent mass of the vessel as constructed.
- **W_H — Hull Steel Weight**: plates, stiffeners, brackets, rudder, funnels.
- **W_M — Machinery Weight**: main engine, auxiliaries, shafting, propeller, piping.
- **W_O — Outfit Weight**: accommodation, electrical, paints, insulation, navigation, lifesaving gear.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State the **master weight equation** of a ship and explain why it requires an *iterative* solution.

**Model Answer:**
The master weight equation is:

$$\Delta = W_{LSW} + W_{DWT}$$

It requires an **iterative solution** because the displacement Δ depends on the principal dimensions (L, B, T), which themselves depend on the cargo capacity and structural weights that contribute to Δ. This *circular dependency* is resolved through the **Design Spiral**, with iteration terminating when the geometric Δ matches the weight-summed Δ within 1 %.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students most often lose marks by (1) using fresh-water density (1.000) when the question specifies seawater, (2) omitting the **form factor correction** $(1 + 0.5(C_B - 0.7))$ in the hull weight formula, and (3) failing to state the **convergence criterion** in iterative solutions. Always specify the density used and show all intermediate steps.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the master weight equation of a ship from **Archimedes' principle**, starting from the equilibrium of buoyancy and weight forces. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A bulk carrier is to carry 55,000 tonnes of cargo. Estimate the total **lightship weight** using the following KTU-grade parametric formulas and data:

- $L = 200$ m, $B = 32$ m, $D = 18$ m, $T = 12.5$ m, $C_B = 0.82$
- $W_H = 0.018 \cdot L^{1.5} \cdot B^{0.5} \cdot D^{0.4} \cdot T^{0.4} \cdot (1 + 0.5(C_B - 0.7))$
- $W_M = 0.85 \cdot P^{0.5}$, with main engine power $P = 12{,}000$ kW
- $W_O = 0.6 \cdot L$

Then compute the **displacement** Δ and the **DWT ratio** ($W_{LSW}/\Delta$).

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** See Section 3.1 of these notes. Key points to write:
- Buoyancy force: $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$
- Weight force: $F_W = \Delta \cdot g$
- Equilibrium gives $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$
- Substitute $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \Rightarrow \Delta = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$
- Assumptions: (i) ship is floating at rest, (ii) calm water, (iii) no trim, (iv) homogeneous density throughout the ship.

**(b)** Step-by-step calculation:

$$\begin{aligned}
L^{1.5} &= 200^{1.5} = 200 \cdot \sqrt{200} = 200 \cdot 14.142 = 2828.4 \\
B^{0.5} &= \sqrt{32} = 5.657 \\
D^{0.4} &= 18^{0.4} = 3.177 \\
T^{0.4} &= 12.5^{0.4} = 2.661 \\
\text{Form factor} &= 1 + 0.5(0.82 - 0.7) = 1.06 \\
\text{Product} &= 2828.4 \times 5.657 \times 3.177 \times 2.661 = 135221
\end{aligned}$$

$$W_H = 0.018 \times 135221 \times 1.06 = 2579 \; \text{t}$$

$$W_M = 0.85 \times \sqrt{12000} = 0.85 \times 109.54 = 93.1 \; \text{t}$$

$$W_O = 0.6 \times 200 = 120 \; \text{t}$$

$$W_{LSW} = 2579 + 93 + 120 = 2792 \; \text{t}$$

**Displacement** Δ: taking DWT = 55000 t as the commercial requirement (and ignoring fuel/crew for this simplified version):

$$\Delta = 2792 + 55000 = 57792 \; \text{t}$$

**DWT Ratio** (technically LSW/Δ here, the *lightship factor*):

$$\frac{W_{LSW}}{\Delta} = \frac{2792}{57792} = 0.0483 = 4.83\%$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Design Spiral** concept in ship design. Identify the loop in which the weight equation is solved and justify its position.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A chemical tanker is to be designed. Two trials yield:

| Trial | L (m) | B (m) | T (m) | C_B |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | 18 | 6.8 | 0.72 |
| 2 | 115 | 17.5 | 6.5 | 0.70 |

For each trial, compute the **geometric displacement** in tonnes (seawater, ρ = 1.025 t/m³) and the **volumetric displacement** in m³. State which trial is *closer* to a design with $W_{DWT} = 4500$ t and $W_{LSW} = 600$ t.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The **Design Spiral** is the iterative process by which a ship is sized in **four conceptual loops**:

1. **Loop 1 — Principal Dimensions**: L, B, T are chosen from statistical and mission constraints.
2. **Loop 2 — Hull Form**: lines plan, hydrostatic curves, form coefficients refined.
3. **Loop 3 — Powering**: resistance estimated → required power → installed power.
4. **Loop 4 — Weight Equation**: Δ compared with W_LSW + W_DWT. Mismatch leads to revised L, B, T, and the spiral **restarts** at Loop 1.

The weight equation is **Loop 4** because it is the *closure condition* of the design — it is the final check that determines whether the principal dimensions are sufficient to carry the commercial mission.

**(b)** Trial 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_1 &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T = 0.72 \times 120 \times 18 \times 6.8 \\
&= 0.72 \times 14688 = 10575.4 \; \text{m}^3 \\
\Delta_1 &= 1.025 \times 10575.4 = 10839.8 \; \text{t}
\end{aligned}$$

Trial 2:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_2 &= 0.70 \times 115 \times 17.5 \times 6.5 \\
&= 0.70 \times 13081.25 = 9156.9 \; \text{m}^3 \\
\Delta_2 &= 1.025 \times 9156.9 = 9385.8 \; \text{t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Target displacement** (from given W_DWT + W_LSW):

$$\Delta_{target} = 4500 + 600 = 5100 \; \text{t}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\vert \Delta_1 - \Delta_{target} \vert = \vert 10839.8 - 5100 \vert = 5739.8 \; \text{t}$$

$$\vert \Delta_2 - \Delta_{target} \vert = \vert 9385.8 - 5100 \vert = 4285.8 \; \text{t}$$

**Trial 2 is closer** to the target, but still overshoots. A third trial with $L \approx 90$ m or $C_B \approx 0.55$ is needed for full convergence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] The **weight equation** is $\Delta = W_{LSW} + W_{DWT}$, derived from Archimedes' principle.
- [x] **Lightship Weight** $W_{LSW} = W_H + W_M + W_O$ is the *permanent* weight of the ship.
- [x] **Deadweight** $W_{DWT} = W_C + W_F + W_FW + W_{SC} + W_P + W_L$ is the *variable* payload.
- [x] **Density of seawater** is **1.025 t/m³**; fresh water is **1.000 t/m³** — a common KTU trap.
- [x] The **block coefficient** $C_B = \nabla / (LBT)$ is the key geometric bridge.
- [x] **Parametric formulas** (Watson-Gilfillan style) are used for early-stage estimates of W_H, W_M, W_O.
- [x] The **Fuel weight** formula is $W_F = P_S \cdot g_{sfc} \cdot R \cdot SF / 10^6$.
- [x] The **weight equation has no closed-form solution** — it is solved by **iteration** within the **Design Spiral**.
- [x] The **Design Spiral** has **four conceptual loops**, with the weight equation in **Loop 4**.
- [x] **Convergence criterion** is typically 1 % error: $\vert \Delta - \Delta_{req} \vert / \Delta \leq 0.01$.
- [x] **Form factor correction** $1 + 0.5(C_B - 0.7)$ is essential in hull weight estimation.
- [x] The **lightship factor** $W_{LSW}/\Delta$ is typically **30 – 50 %** for tankers and **15 – 25 %** for container ships.
- [x] **Stowage Factor (SF)** is volume per tonne; light cargoes inflate the required volume.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Volume Equation and Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Volume Equation and Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of preliminary ship design, the **Volume Equation** (often synonymous with the **Deadweight Equation** or the **Tonnage Equation** when expanded) is a fundamental, ship-specific algebraic identity that mathematically balances the total displacement volume of a vessel against its primary design deadweight capacity. It serves as the foundational pivot connecting the vessel's principal dimensions, block coefficient, and its required carrying capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The volume equation is the cornerstone of the *deadweight equation* method, which is universally applied to find the principal dimensions (Length, Beam, Draught) of a merchant ship during the **preliminary design stage**.

The fundamental form of the volume equation relates the underwater volume ($V$) to the principal dimensions and the block coefficient ($C_B$):

$$V = L \times B \times T \times C_B$$

Where:
- $V$ = Displaced volume of the ship ($m^3$)
- $L$ = Length between perpendiculars (LBP) in meters ($m$)
- $B$ = Moulded breadth in meters ($m$)
- $T$ = Moulded draught in meters ($m$)
- $C_B$ = Block coefficient (dimensionless ratio, typically $0.6$ to $0.85$ for merchant ships)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a rectangular box made of modeling clay that exactly fits a toy ship floating in a bathtub. 

If you want to know how heavy the toy ship is, you can weigh the clay box, because it displaces the exact same amount of water. The weight of the water displaced is equal to the weight of the ship (Archimedes' Principle). 

The **Volume Equation** is simply the mathematical recipe to calculate the size of that "clay box." The ship, however, is not a perfect rectangle. It has a pointy bow, a flat bottom, and a curved stern. To account for this shape, we take the perfect rectangular volume ($L \times B \times T$) and shrink it by a factor called the **Block Coefficient** ($C_B$). 

> [!NOTE]
> Think of $C_B$ as a "shape efficiency" factor. A cargo ship carrying bulk goods is blocky ($C_B \approx 0.80$), meaning it is very close to a rectangular box. A fast container ship is much slimmer ($C_B \approx 0.60$), meaning the clay box would be much larger than the actual volume of the ship's hull, but we "multiply" the box volume by a smaller number to find the true hull volume.

## 1.3 The "Solution" Component

The *solution* to the volume equation is the process of isolating the unknown parameters. Because a ship has three principal dimensions ($L, B, T$) but only one equation, naval architects must assume ratios (like $L/B$ and $B/T$) based on the ship type. The volume equation is then solved simultaneously with the **Weight Equation** ($W = \Delta = \rho \times g \times V$) to find the exact values of $L, B,$ and $T$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Deadweight Equation Framework

The volume equation is rarely used in isolation. It is embedded within a broader system called the **Deadweight Equation**. 

The deadweight ($DWT$) of a ship is the total weight the ship can carry, including cargo, fuel, fresh water, crew, and provisions. The total displacement ($\Delta$) is the sum of the lightweight ($LWT$ — the empty steel ship) and the deadweight ($DWT$). 

The standard KTU approach breaks this down into a single unified equation:

$$\Delta = DWT + LWT$$

Since $\Delta = \rho \times V$ (where $\rho$ is the density of seawater, $1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$):

$$\rho \times L \times B \times T \times C_B = DWT + LWT$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Solution

To solve for the principal dimensions, naval architects follow a structured deductive process:

1. **Input the Deadweight:** The shipping client provides the required cargo capacity, e.g., *"I need a ship to carry 50,000 tonnes of cargo."*
2. **Estimate the Lightweight ($LWT$):** The empty weight of the ship is estimated using empirical formulas based on the deadweight.
3. **Calculate the Total Displacement ($\Delta$):** Sum the deadweight and lightweight to find the total mass the ship must displace.
4. **Select the Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** Based on the ship type (tanker, bulker, container) and speed, a suitable $C_B$ is selected from standard design charts.
5. **Assume Dimension Ratios:** The length-to-beam ratio ($L/B$) and beam-to-draught ratio ($B/T$) are selected based on standard parent designs.
6. **Substitute and Solve:** Substitute the ratios into the volume equation to solve for the individual dimensions $L, B,$ and $T$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this done?** A ship must float, it must be fast enough, and it must be stable. The volume equation ensures it floats. The dimension ratios ensure it is hydrodynamically efficient and stable. By solving them together, we guarantee the ship is seaworthy.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Equation / Standard Value | Units | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Volume Equation** | $V = L \times B \times T \times C_B$ | $m^3$ | Relates hull volume to principal dimensions. |
| **Displacement Equation** | $\Delta = \rho \times V$ | Tonnes ($t$) | Mass of water displaced (Archimedes' Principle). |
| **Sea Water Density** | $\rho = 1.025$ | $t/m^3$ | Standard mass density of ocean water. |
| **Deadweight Equation** | $\Delta = DWT + LWT$ | Tonnes ($t$) | Total weight balancing identity. |
| **Block Coefficient** | $C_B = \frac{V}{L \times B \times T}$ | Dimensionless | Hull fullness factor (Bulk: 0.78–0.85, Tanker: 0.80–0.85). |
| **Mumford's Formula (LWT)** | $LWT \approx C \times (DWT)^{0.75}$ | Tonnes ($t$) | Empirical lightweight estimation. |
| **Standard L/B Ratio** | $5.5 \text{ to } 7.0$ | Ratio | Length to beam ratio for merchant vessels. |
| **Standard B/T Ratio** | $2.0 \text{ to } 2.5$ | Ratio | Beam to draught ratio for merchant vessels. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The volume equation and its solution are the absolute bedrock of all ship design. Without it, a shipyard cannot cut steel plates, an engine manufacturer cannot select the right propeller size, and a port cannot dredge the correct depth for the approach channel. Every classification society (like Lloyd's Register or Bureau Veritas) requires a verified volume and displacement calculation to certify a vessel for sea operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Volume-Weight Equation

We start with the fundamental physical principle that the weight of a floating ship is exactly equal to the weight of the water it displaces.

**Step 1: Expressing the weight of the displaced water.**
The mass of the displaced water ($m$) is the volume ($V$) multiplied by the density of the water ($\rho$).

$$m = \rho \times V$$

**Step 2: Converting mass to weight (Displacement, $\Delta$).**
In ship design, displacement ($\Delta$) is measured in metric tonnes (where 1 tonne = 1000 kg). We can combine the acceleration due to gravity ($g$) and density into a single constant for seawater.

$$\Delta = \rho \times V$$
*(Note: $\rho$ is taken as $1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ to inherently account for gravity)*

**Step 3: Expanding the volume ($V$).**
The volume of a ship's hull is geometrically approximated by the product of its principal dimensions multiplied by the block coefficient.

$$V = L \times B \times T \times C_B$$

**Step 4: The final combined identity.**
Substituting the expanded volume back into the displacement equation gives the core Volume-Weight Equation used in the KTU syllabus.

$$\Delta = \rho \times L \times B \times T \times C_B$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Solving for Principal Dimensions

Because a single equation cannot solve for three unknowns ($L, B, T$), we must introduce dimensional ratios. Let's assume the standard ratios:

$$\frac{L}{B} = \alpha \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{B}{T} = \beta$$

This means $L = \alpha \times B$ and $T = \frac{B}{\beta}$.

**Step 1: Start with the combined volume equation.**
$$\Delta = \rho \times L \times B \times T \times C_B$$

**Step 2: Substitute the ratios for $L$ and $T$.**
$$\Delta = \rho \times (\alpha \times B) \times B \times \left(\frac{B}{\beta}\right) \times C_B$$

**Step 3: Group the Beam ($B$) terms together.**
$$\Delta = \rho \times \frac{\alpha}{\beta} \times C_B \times B^3$$

**Step 4: Isolate $B^3$ to solve for the Beam ($B$).**
$$B^3 = \frac{\Delta \times \beta}{\rho \times \alpha \times C_B}$$

**Step 5: Take the cube root to find the Beam.**
$$B = \sqrt[3]{\frac{\Delta \times \beta}{\rho \times \alpha \times C_B}}$$

**Step 6: Back-substitute to find $L$ and $T$.**
$$L = \alpha \times B$$
$$T = \frac{B}{\beta}$$

## 3.3 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A bulk carrier is required to carry a Deadweight ($DWT$) of $20,000 \text{ t}$. 
The estimated Lightweight ($LWT$) is $6,000 \text{ t}$.
Assume a Block Coefficient ($C_B$) of $0.80$.
Assume a Length to Beam ratio ($L/B$) of $6.0$.
Assume a Beam to Draught ratio ($B/T$) of $2.5$.
Calculate the principal dimensions $L, B,$ and $T$ at the design draught.

**Step 1: Calculate the total Displacement ($\Delta$).**
$$\Delta = DWT + LWT$$
$$\Delta = 20,000 + 6,000 = 26,000 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2: Set up the volume equation.**
$$V = L \times B \times T \times C_B$$
Since $\Delta = \rho \times V$, and $V = \frac{\Delta}{\rho}$:
$$\frac{26,000}{1.025} = L \times B \times T \times 0.80$$

**Step 3: Substitute the ratios into the equation.**
Using $L = 6.0 \times B$ and $T = \frac{B}{2.5} = 0.4 \times B$:
$$\frac{26,000}{1.025} = (6.0 \times B) \times B \times (0.4 \times B) \times 0.80$$

**Step 4: Simplify the constants.**
Right side constant: $6.0 \times 0.4 \times 0.80 = 1.92$
Left side constant: $\frac{26,000}{1.025} \approx 25,365.85 \text{ m}^3$

$$25,365.85 = 1.92 \times B^3$$

**Step 5: Solve for $B$.**
$$B^3 = \frac{25,365.85}{1.92} \approx 13,211.38$$
$$B = \sqrt[3]{13,211.38} \approx 23.60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Solve for $L$ and $T$.**
$$L = 6.0 \times 23.60 = 141.60 \text{ m}$$
$$T = \frac{23.60}{2.5} = 9.44 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** $L = 141.6 \text{ m}$, $B = 23.6 \text{ m}$, $T = 9.44 \text{ m}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Solution Methodology

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the deductive logic used to solve the volume equation in preliminary ship design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Client Requirement] --> B[Specified Deadweight DWT]
    B --> C[Estimate Lightweight LWT]
    C --> D[Calculate Total Displacement: Delta = DWT + LWT]
    D --> E[Select Block Coefficient CB from standard tables]
    E --> F[Select Dimensional Ratios: L/B and B/T]
    F --> G[Formulate Volume Equation: V = L x B x T x CB]
    G --> H[Substitute Ratios into V = Delta / Rho]
    H --> I[Isolate and solve for Beam B]
    I --> J[Back-substitute to find Length L]
    J --> K[Back-substitute to find Draught T]
    K --> L[Verify Stability and Resistance]
    L --> M[Final Principal Dimensions]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Volume Equation Variables Interaction

This diagram illustrates how the independent variables ($L, B, T$) and the constant ($C_B$) interact to produce the physical displacement volume.

```mermaid
graph LR
    Len[L - Length] --> Mul1{Multiplication}
    Bm[B - Beam] --> Mul1{Multiplication}
    Mul1 --> Mul2{Multiplication}
    Dr[T - Draught] --> Mul2{Multiplication}
    Mul2 --> Rect[Rectangular Volume L x B x T]
    BC[C_B - Block Coefficient] --> Shrink[Shape Shrinkage Factor]
    Rect --> Shrink
    Shrink --> Out[Actual Hull Volume V]
    Rho[Rho = 1.025 t per cubic meter] --> Mass
    Out --> Mass{Total Displacement Delta}
    Mass --> Arch[Archimedes Principle: Delta equals Ship Weight]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Real-World Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Inputs
    D1[DWT Requirement] 
    D2[Vessel Type and Speed]
    end
    subgraph Engine [The Volume Equation Engine]
    V1[Volume Equation]
    V2[Ratios L/B and B/T]
    V3[Empirical LWT formulas]
    end
    subgraph Outputs [Solved Dimensions]
    O1[Length L]
    O2[Breadth B]
    O3[Draught T]
    end
    D1 --> V1
    D2 --> V2
    D2 --> V3
    V1 --> O1
    V1 --> O2
    V1 --> O3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the Block Coefficient ($C_B$) and state its typical range for a fully loaded bulk carrier.**

**Model Answer:**
The Block Coefficient ($C_B$) is the ratio of the underwater volume of the ship to the volume of a rectangular block having the same length, breadth, and depth. 
Mathematically, $C_B = \frac{V}{L \times B \times T}$.
For a fully loaded bulk carrier, which has a blocky shape, the typical range of $C_B$ is **0.78 to 0.85**.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the physical significance of the density of seawater ($\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$) in the volume equation?**

**Model Answer:**
The density of seawater ($\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$) is used as a conversion factor. Since the volume equation yields the geometric volume of the hull in cubic meters, multiplying by the mass density of seawater converts this physical volume directly into the **mass of water displaced** (Displacement, $\Delta$) in metric tonnes, effectively fulfilling Archimedes' Principle.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** The most common mistake is mixing the freshwater density ($1.000 \text{ t/m}^3$) with the saltwater density ($1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$). The Indian Ocean is saltwater, so always default to $\rho = 1.025$ unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 2. **Ratio Inversion:** Students frequently invert the $L/B$ or $B/T$ ratios. If $L/B = 6.0$, then $L$ is six times $B$, not the other way around. Read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting $C_B$:** A shockingly high number of students solve $L \times B \times T = \frac{\Delta}{\rho}$, completely omitting the Block Coefficient from the equation, which leads to a Length of over 200 meters for a 20,000 DWT vessel. Always multiply by $C_B$ to shrink the box!

---

### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
An oil tanker is designed to carry a Deadweight ($DWT$) of $50,000 \text{ t}$. The estimated Lightweight ($LWT$) of the vessel is $15,000 \text{ t}$. 
Given the following design assumptions:
- Block Coefficient ($C_B$) = $0.85$
- Length to Beam ratio ($L/B$) = $5.5$
- Beam to Draught ratio ($B/T$) = $2.4$

**Calculate the principal dimensions ($L, B, T$) of the vessel.**

#### Part (a) (7 Marks): Calculate the Displacement and set up the volume equation.

**Solution:**
**Step 1: Calculate the total Displacement ($\Delta$).**
$$\Delta = DWT + LWT$$
$$\Delta = 50,000 + 15,000 = 65,000 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the required geometric volume ($V$).**
$$V = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{65,000}{1.025} = 63,414.63 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Formulate the volume equation.**
$$V = L \times B \times T \times C_B$$
$$63,414.63 = L \times B \times T \times 0.85$$

#### Part (b) (7 Marks): Solve for the exact principal dimensions.

**Solution:**
**Step 1: Substitute the dimensional ratios.**
Given $L/B = 5.5 \implies L = 5.5 \times B$
Given $B/T = 2.4 \implies T = \frac{B}{2.4} = 0.4167 \times B$

**Step 2: Substitute these into the volume equation.**
$$63,414.63 = (5.5 \times B) \times B \times (0.4167 \times B) \times 0.85$$
$$63,414.63 = (5.5 \times 0.4167 \times 0.85) \times B^3$$
$$63,414.63 = 1.948 \times B^3$$

**Step 3: Solve for Beam ($B$).**
$$B^3 = \frac{63,414.63}{1.948} = 32,553.30$$
$$B = \sqrt[3]{32,553.30} = 31.89 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Solve for Length ($L$) and Draught ($T$).**
$$L = 5.5 \times 31.89 = 175.40 \text{ m}$$
$$T = \frac{31.89}{2.4} = 13.29 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** $L \approx 175.4 \text{ m}$, $B \approx 31.9 \text{ m}$, $T \approx 13.3 \text{ m}$.

---

### Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
A general cargo ship has a Length ($L$) of $120 \text{ m}$, a Beam ($B$) of $18 \text{ m}$, and a Draught ($T$) of $7.5 \text{ m}$. The Block Coefficient ($C_B$) is $0.72$.

**Part (a) (7 Marks): Calculate the Displacement volume and the total Deadweight ($DWT$) of the ship, assuming the Lightweight ($LWT$) is $25\%$ of the Displacement.**

**Solution:**
**Step 1: Calculate the geometric volume of the hull.**
$$V = L \times B \times T \times C_B$$
$$V = 120 \times 18 \times 7.5 \times 0.72 = 11,664 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total Displacement ($\Delta$).**
$$\Delta = \rho \times V = 1.025 \times 11,664 = 11,955.6 \text{ t}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Lightweight ($LWT$) and Deadweight ($DWT$).**
Given $LWT = 0.25 \times \Delta$:
$$LWT = 0.25 \times 11,955.6 = 2,988.9 \text{ t}$$
Using the deadweight equation $\Delta = DWT + LWT$:
$$DWT = \Delta - LWT = 11,955.6 - 2,988.9 = 8,966.7 \text{ t}$$

**Part (b) (7 Marks): If the ship must carry an additional $1,500 \text{ t}$ of cargo, calculate the new required draught, assuming the Block Coefficient remains constant and the additional volume is distributed across the same $L$ and $B$.**

**Solution:**
**Step 1: Calculate the new required Displacement.**
$$LWT \text{ remains constant} = 2,988.9 \text{ t}$$
$$\text{New } DWT = 8,966.7 + 1,500 = 10,466.7 \text{ t}$$
$$\text{New } \Delta = 10,466.7 + 2,988.9 = 13,455.6 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the new required volume.**
$$V_{\text{new}} = \frac{\text{New } \Delta}{\rho} = \frac{13,455.6}{1.025} = 13,127.4 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Solve for the new Draught ($T_{\text{new}}$).**
$$V_{\text{new}} = L \times B \times T_{\text{new}} \times C_B$$
$$13,127.4 = 120 \times 18 \times T_{\text{new}} \times 0.72$$
$$13,127.4 = 1,555.2 \times T_{\text{new}}$$
$$T_{\text{new}} = \frac{13,127.4}{1,555.2} = 8.44 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** The ship will sink deeper into the water, requiring a new design draught of **$8.44 \text{ m}$** to carry the extra cargo.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Identity:** $\Delta = \rho \times L \times B \times T \times C_B$ is the master volume-weight equation.
- **The Unbreakable Rule:** Always use $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ for standard ocean water unless specified otherwise.
- **Ratios are Substitutes:** You cannot solve for three variables with one equation. You *must* use the $L/B$ and $B/T$ ratios to reduce the system to a single solvable variable (usually $B$).
- **Block Coefficient is Key:** Do not forget $C_B$ in the equation. It is the single most ignored factor by students. A bulk carrier ($C_B \approx 0.85$) is much shorter and wider than a container ship ($C_B \approx 0.60$) of the exact same deadweight.
- **Displacement vs. Deadweight:** Displacement ($\Delta$) is the total weight of the ship. Deadweight ($DWT$) is only the carrying capacity. $\Delta = DWT + LWT$.
- **Practical Limits:** If your calculated $L, B, T$ violate physical constraints (like a $B/T$ ratio that makes the ship dangerously top-heavy, or an $L/B$ ratio that makes the ship flex like a snake), you must iterate the design by changing the standard ratios.
- **Units Check:** Length in meters, volume in $m^3$, mass in tonnes. Keep your units consistent throughout the derivation to avoid losing marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Design Procedure for Main Categories of Ships: Deadweight Carriers, Capacity Carriers, Linear Dimension Ships, Special Purpose Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Design Procedure for Main Categories of Ships: Deadweight Carriers, Capacity Carriers, Linear Dimension Ships, Special Purpose Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** The classification of ships by their primary design-driving parameter is the first conceptual step in any ship design project. The four classical categories — **Deadweight Carriers, Capacity Carriers, Linear Dimension Ships, and Special Purpose Ships** — each require a different starting point for the iterative design spiral.

### 1.1 Formal Classification (KTU Terminology)

In ship design theory (following Watson, Eyres, and the KTU prescribed texts), the **design-driving parameter** is the single most important variable from which the entire ship is dimensioned. This parameter is fixed by the **mission requirement** before any hull form is drawn.

The four main categories are defined as follows:

| Category | Design Driver | Classical Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Deadweight Carriers** | Required Deadweight (DWT) in tonnes | Oil tankers, bulk carriers, product carriers |
| **Capacity Carriers** | Required Cargo Volume (m³) or Slots (TEU/pax) | LNG/LPG carriers, container ships, cruise liners |
| **Linear Dimension Ships** | Fixed Length / Beam / Draft constraint | Panamax, Seawaymax, Aframax, Great Lakers |
| **Special Purpose Ships** | Specific operational mission profile | Drill ships, icebreakers, cable layers, research vessels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mnemonic:** "**D-C-L-S**" — **D**eadweight, **C**apacity, **L**inear, **S**pecial. This is the order in which the KTU 2024 module presents these categories, and the same order is followed in board exam questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Container Truck" Intuition

Imagine you need to design a transportation vehicle:

- A **coal hauler** is asked, "How many **tonnes** of coal must it carry?" → You size the **truck's strength & fuel tank first**, then find a box that fits. This is a **Deadweight Carrier** (strength & mass are paramount).
- A **furniture mover** is asked, "How many **cubic metres** of sofas must it hold?" → You size the **box's volume first**, then strengthen it. This is a **Capacity Carrier** (volume is paramount).
- A **fire truck** must fit through a **specific tunnel width** → The **beam is fixed**, you can only vary length and height. This is a **Linear Dimension Ship**.
- A **moon buggy** is built to **crawl over craters** → Standard truck rules do not apply. This is a **Special Purpose Ship** with bespoke geometry.

> [!TIP]
> A ship's category is determined **only by the designer's first fixed variable** — NOT by what the ship physically carries. A tanker carrying a *light* crude is still a deadweight carrier if its DWT was the design input.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following KTU-board-recognized constants appear repeatedly in the design procedure:

- **Sea water density:** ρ = **1.025 t/m³** (salt water, standard reference condition)
- **Fresh water density:** ρ = **1.000 t/m³**
- **Conversion factor (long ton → tonne):** 1 LT = **1.016 t**
- **Standard TEU envelope:** $6.06 \text{ m} \times 2.44 \text{ m} \times 2.59 \text{ m}$ (ISO 668)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Design Spiral in Context

Every ship category follows the classical **design spiral** (Watson, 1998), but the **entry point** into the spiral differs. The KTU board expects students to recognise that:

- For Deadweight Carriers, the spiral starts with **DWT → Δ → L,B,T**.
- For Capacity Carriers, the spiral starts with **Cargo volume → Stowage factor → DWT → Δ**.
- For Linear Dimension Ships, the spiral starts with **fixed L (or B, or T) → free variables**.
- For Special Purpose Ships, the spiral is **mission-driven** and often **non-convergent** until specialist input is injected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The "design spiral" converges when the assumed deadweight (from principal dimensions) equals the required deadweight (from mission). For all four categories, the convergence check is identical, but the **initial guess** differs.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula** required by KTU Module 2 for these four ship categories. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of literal pipe characters.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Category |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement equals density × volume displaced ($\nabla$ in m³, $\rho$ in t/m³) | All |
| 2 | $DWT = \Delta - LWT$ | Deadweight equals total displacement minus lightweight | All |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{DWT}{\Delta}$ | Deadweight coefficient (typically 0.55 – 0.85) | All |
| 4 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient; measures hull fullness | All |
| 5 | $\nabla = \dfrac{DWT}{\rho \cdot \eta}$ | First estimate of volume displacement in design spiral | Deadweight |
| 6 | $DWT = \dfrac{V_c}{SF}$ | Deadweight from cargo volume $V_c$ and stowage factor $SF$ (m³/t) | Capacity |
| 7 | $N_{TEU} = \dfrac{L_{hold} \cdot B \cdot h_{stack}}{V_{TEU}}$ | TEU capacity along the hold length (approx. slot formula) | Capacity (Container) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{L}{B}$, $\dfrac{B}{T}$, $\dfrac{L}{T}$ | Principal dimension ratios (KTU standard ranges given below) | All |
| 9 | $T = \dfrac{\Delta}{L \cdot B \cdot C_B \cdot \rho}$ | Re-arranged block-coefficient equation for draft iteration | Linear Dimension |
| 10 | $W_{LWT} = k \cdot L^{\alpha}$ | Lightweight estimation (typical $k \approx 0.04$, $\alpha \approx 2.5$ for tankers) | All |
| 11 | $S_{f} = \dfrac{\nabla}{\sqrt{L \cdot T}}$ | Fatness index (Smith correction for powering) | Deadweight |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Dimension Ratios (commit to memory):**
> - **Tankers / Bulk Carriers:** $L/B \approx 5.5 - 7.0$, $B/T \approx 2.5 - 3.0$
> - **Container Ships:** $L/B \approx 6.0 - 8.0$, $B/T \approx 2.4 - 2.8$ (high B for stowage)
> - **Linear Dimension (Panamax):** $L \le 294.1$ m, $B \le 32.31$ m, $T \le 12.04$ m
> - **Special Purpose:** No fixed ratio — mission-governed

### 2.3 Why and How Each Category Drives the Design

**Why** the categories exist: A ship is fundamentally a compromise between **mission, route, regulation, and economics**. Each category privileges one of these:

- **Deadweight Carriers** privilege **economics** (low $/tonne-mile).
- **Capacity Carriers** privilege **mission** (volume-sensitive cargo).
- **Linear Dimension Ships** privilege **regulation** (locks, canals, ports).
- **Special Purpose Ships** privilege **mission** to the exclusion of generic economics.

**How** the design proceeds:

For all four, the iterative loop is:
$$\text{Required payload} \;\xrightarrow{\text{stats}}\; \Delta \;\xrightarrow{\text{C_B guess}}\; (L,B,T) \;\xrightarrow{\text{check}}\; \text{LWT, stability, power}$$

The loop converges when the **calculated DWT** $\approx$ **required DWT** within $\pm 2\%$.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

This classification system is used in:
- **Concept design tenders** by shipyards (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS all use it).
- **Statutory fee calculation** (e.g., Indian ports charge partly on DWT — irrelevant for deadweight carriers, hence "deadweight carrier" is a *legal* category too).
- **Emission indices** like the **EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index) baseline formula which directly references $DWT$ for deadweight carriers but uses a different reference for capacity carriers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Deadweight Carrier Procedure (Oil Tanker Example)

**Given mission requirement:** Carry $DWT_{req} = 80{,}000$ tonnes of crude oil on a draft-limited route.

**Step 1 — First estimate of displacement.**
Using a typical deadweight coefficient $\eta = 0.80$ for a product tanker:

$$\Delta_1 = \frac{DWT_{req}}{\eta} = \frac{80{,}000}{0.80} = 100{,}000 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute volumetric displacement.**
Using sea-water density $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$:

$$\nabla_1 = \frac{\Delta_1}{\rho} = \frac{100{,}000}{1.025} = 97{,}560.98 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Guess block coefficient.**
For a $80{,}000$ DWT tanker, typical $C_B \approx 0.82$. Use $C_B = 0.825$.

**Step 4 — Pick principal dimension ratios.**
- $L/B = 6.0$ (mid-range for tanker)
- $B/T = 2.6$ (draft-restricted route)

**Step 5 — Solve for $L$, $B$, $T$.**
From $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$, substitute $B = L/6$ and $T = B/2.6 = L/15.6$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_1 &= C_B \cdot L \cdot \frac{L}{6} \cdot \frac{L}{15.6} \\
\nabla_1 &= C_B \cdot \frac{L^3}{93.6} \\
97{,}560.98 &= 0.825 \cdot \frac{L^3}{93.6} \\
L^3 &= \frac{97{,}560.98 \times 93.6}{0.825} \\
L^3 &= 11{,}067{,}085 \text{ m}^3 \\
L &= 222.92 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Back-calculate $B$ and $T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{L}{6.0} = 37.15 \text{ m} \\
T &= \frac{B}{2.6} = 14.29 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Convergence check.**
Calculate actual displacement from chosen $(L,B,T)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{calc} &= \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 1.025 \times 0.825 \times 222.92 \times 37.15 \times 14.29 \\
&= 1.025 \times 0.825 \times 118{,}343.2 \\
&= 100{,}163 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Actual $DWT_{calc} = \eta \cdot \Delta_{calc} = 0.80 \times 100{,}163 = 80{,}130 \text{ t}$.

**Iteration assessment:** Error $= \vert 80{,}130 - 80{,}000 \vert / 80{,}000 = 0.16\%$ — **converged** (well within the $\pm 2\%$ tolerance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The first-pass estimates almost always converge within 1–2 spiral loops for deadweight carriers because the design-driver (DWT) and the design-output (Δ) are directly proportional. This is *why* tankers and bulk carriers are classified as the easiest category to design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Capacity Carrier Procedure (LNG Carrier)

**Given mission:** Carry $V_c = 138{,}000$ m³ of LNG at $-163°$C, voyage 12,000 nm.

**Step 1 — Compute required deadweight from volume.**
LNG stowage factor $SF \approx 1.36 \text{ m}^3/\text{t}$ (standard for $-163°$C LNG):

$$DWT_{req} = \frac{V_c}{SF} = \frac{138{,}000}{1.36} = 101{,}471 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2 — Displacement estimate.**
Using $\eta = 0.70$ (lower than tanker, due to heavy tank insulation & membrane):

$$\Delta_1 = \frac{101{,}471}{0.70} = 144{,}958 \text{ t}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\nabla_1$.**

$$\nabla_1 = \frac{144{,}958}{1.025} = 141{,}422 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Pick $C_B$ and ratios.**
For a Moss-type LNG carrier: $C_B \approx 0.72$ (slenderer due to sloshing concerns), $L/B = 6.2$, $B/T = 2.5$.

**Step 5 — Solve for $L$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_1 &= C_B \cdot L \cdot \frac{L}{6.2} \cdot \frac{L}{15.5} \\
141{,}422 &= 0.72 \cdot \frac{L^3}{96.1} \\
L^3 &= \frac{141{,}422 \times 96.1}{0.72} = 18{,}878{,}059 \\
L &= 266.6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Back-calculate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= 266.6/6.2 = 43.0 \text{ m} \\
T &= 43.0/2.5 = 17.2 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Recompute DWT.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{calc} &= 1.025 \times 0.72 \times 266.6 \times 43.0 \times 17.2 \\
&= 142{,}445 \text{ t} \\
DWT_{calc} &= 0.70 \times 142{,}445 = 99{,}712 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration:** Error $= \vert 99{,}712 - 101{,}471 \vert / 101{,}471 = 1.73\%$ — **just within tolerance**; a second spiral iteration would tighten this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Capacity carriers are *indirect* drivers**: the stowage factor $SF$ is the bridge between the design-driver (volume) and the design-output (DWT). This makes LNG design more sensitive than tanker design — a $5\%$ error in $SF$ gives a $5\%$ error in deadweight, propagating through the entire spiral.

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Linear Dimension Ship (Panamax Bulk Carrier)

**Given constraint:** Ship must transit the original Panama Canal.
- $L \le 294.13$ m
- $B \le 32.31$ m  
- $T \le 12.04$ m (Tropical Fresh Water)

**Step 1 — Maximum envelope.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= 294.13 \text{ m} \\
B_{max} &= 32.31 \text{ m} \\
T_{max} &= 12.04 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum theoretical displacement envelope.**

$$
\nabla_{max} = C_B \cdot L_{max} \cdot B_{max} \cdot T_{max}
$$

Using $C_B = 0.85$ (Panamax bulkers are typically full-form):

$$
\nabla_{max} = 0.85 \times 294.13 \times 32.31 \times 12.04 = 97{,}370 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum deadweight.**

$$
DWT_{max} = \rho \cdot \nabla_{max} \cdot \eta = 1.025 \times 97{,}370 \times 0.83 = 82{,}836 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule.**
If the owner's required DWT $\le 82{,}836$ t → design as a **Panamax**.
If required DWT $> 82{,}836$ t → must either accept the **Post-Panamax premium**, switch to a **Capesize**, or use **Capesize via Suez** (do not fit in Panamax).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **Panamax T limit is 12.04 m in TROPICAL FRESH WATER**. In salt water, the same ship has $T_{max} \approx 12.04 - 0.3 = 11.74$ m because of higher buoyancy. Board examiners love testing this conversion.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Procedure Comparison

| Step | Deadweight Carrier | Capacity Carrier | Linear Dimension | Special Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Fix design driver** | $DWT_{req}$ | $V_c$ or $N_{TEU}$ | $L$, $B$, or $T$ | Mission profile |
| **2. Compute DWT** | Direct input | $DWT = V_c/SF$ | Iterate from envelope | Often no fixed DWT |
| **3. Estimate $\Delta$** | $DWT/\eta$ | $DWT/\eta$ | $\rho \cdot C_B \cdot LBT$ | Mission-specific |
| **4. Pick ratios** | Standard tanker ratios | Slender ratios | Forced by constraint | Custom geometry |
| **5. Solve $L,B,T$** | From $\nabla$ and ratios | From $\nabla$ and ratios | Iterate free variables | Multi-objective optimisation |
| **6. Check convergence** | $DWT_{calc} \approx DWT_{req}$ | $V_{c,calc} \approx V_c$ | $L,B,T \le L_{max}$ etc. | Mission KPIs met |
| **7. Specialist input** | Powering, hull resistance | Sloshing, insulation | Port/canal fee trade-off | Heavy engineering |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Design Procedure Master Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KTU Module 2 Entry: Identify Ship Category] --> B{Category?}
    B -->|Deadweight| C[Fix DWT_req from mission]
    B -->|Capacity| D[Fix Cargo Volume V_c]
    B -->|Linear Dim| E[Fix L or B or T constraint]
    B -->|Special| F[Fix Mission Profile]
    C --> G[Estimate displacement Delta]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Pick C_B and dimension ratios]
    H --> I[Solve L, B, T from volumetric eqn]
    I --> J{Convergence Check}
    J -->|Not converged| K[Adjust C_B or ratios]
    K --> H
    J -->|Converged| L[Compute Lightweight LWT]
    L --> M[Stability and Powering]
    M --> N[General Arrangement]
    N --> O[Final GA and Lines Plan]
```

### 4.2 Deadweight Carrier Sub-Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Mission: tonnes to carry] --> A2[Set DWT_req]
    A2 --> A3[Pick eta from ship-type table]
    A3 --> A4[Delta = DWT / eta]
    A4 --> A5[nabla = Delta / rho]
    A5 --> A6[Pick C_B from length-class table]
    A6 --> A7[Pick L/B and B/T ratios]
    A7 --> A8[Solve cubic in L]
    A8 --> A9[Compute B and T]
    A9 --> A10{DWT_calc approx DWT_req?}
    A10 -->|No| A11[Adjust C_B by plus or minus 0.01]
    A11 --> A6
    A10 -->|Yes| A12[Lock dimensions, exit spiral]
```

### 4.3 Category Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DC [Deadweight Carrier]
        DC1[DWT drives design]
        DC2[Cargo density is HIGH]
        DC3[Examples: Tanker, Bulker]
    end
    subgraph CC [Capacity Carrier]
        CC1[Volume drives design]
        CC2[Cargo density is LOW]
        CC3[Examples: LNG, Container, Pax]
    end
    subgraph LD [Linear Dimension Ship]
        LD1[Length or Beam FIXED]
        LD2[Operational constraint]
        LD3[Examples: Panamax, Seawaymax]
    end
    subgraph SP [Special Purpose Ship]
        SP1[Mission drives design]
        SP2[No standard rules]
        SP3[Examples: Drillship, Icebreaker]
    end
    DC --> CHECK{Has fixed dimension?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| LD
    CHECK -->|No| Q1{Cargo measured by volume?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| CC
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Standard mission?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| DC
    Q2 -->|No| SP
```

### 4.4 Convergence Loop Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Iteration n: assume C_Bn] --> P2[Compute Ln, Bn, Tn]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Delta_n from Ln, Bn, Tn]
    P3 --> P4[Compute DWT_n = eta times Delta_n]
    P4 --> P5{DWT_n minus DWT_req absolute value less than 2 percent}
    P5 -->|No| P6[Adjust C_Bn+1]
    P6 --> P1
    P5 -->|Yes| P7[Converged: Freeze L, B, T]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "show the design procedure" for any of the four categories, **always draw the spiral loop** as in Section 4.4 above. Examiners award 2–3 marks simply for correctly depicting the **iteration direction**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Define a "deadweight carrier" ship. Give two examples and state the design-driving parameter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
A **deadweight carrier** is a ship whose principal dimensions are determined by the **required deadweight (DWT)** specified by the owner. The design-driving parameter is **deadweight in tonnes**. Two examples are: (i) **Crude oil tanker**, and (ii) **Capesize bulk carrier**. The design spiral starts with $DWT_{req}$ and works back to $(L, B, T)$ via displacement and block coefficient.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Differentiate between a "capacity carrier" and a "linear dimension ship" with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Capacity Carrier | Linear Dimension Ship |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Design driver | Cargo volume / slots | Restricted length, beam, or draft |
| Example | LNG carrier (m³ of LNG) | Panamax bulker (Panama Canal limits) |
| Starting point of spiral | Volume $\rightarrow$ DWT $\rightarrow$ Δ | Fixed L or B $\rightarrow$ iterate free variables |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **lose 3–4 marks** by failing to state the **design-driving parameter** explicitly at the start. Always begin with: *"The design driver for this ship is X."*
> - Do **NOT** confuse **deadweight (DWT)** with **displacement (Δ)** — the board penalises this heavily.
> - For **capacity carriers**, the **stowage factor** must be quoted with units — omitting units loses 1 mark.
> - In **linear-dimension problems**, students forget to apply the **fresh-water draft correction** for canal limits. This is a board favourite.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Deadweight + Capacity Combined

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**  
A shipowner requires a vessel to carry **65,000 tonnes** of chemical products with cargo density **0.85 t/m³**. The route is unrestricted.

**(a)** Classify the ship into the appropriate KTU Module-2 category and **justify** your classification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **principal dimensions** $(L, B, T)$ using standard ratios and a deadweight coefficient of 0.78. Assume $C_B = 0.81$, $L/B = 6.0$, $B/T = 2.6$, and $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**

1. The cargo density is **0.85 t/m³** — moderate, but the **design driver is mass (tonnes)**, not volume.
2. Cargo volume check: $V_c = 65{,}000/0.85 = 76{,}471$ m³. The ship is dimensioned first by **DWT**, then volume is checked.
3. Since the mission is to carry a fixed **mass** (not a fixed volume), the ship is classified as a **DEADWEIGHT CARRIER** (specifically, a chemical/product tanker).
4. Justification: For chemical tankers, the design is strength-driven (high-density cargo, segregated tanks, IMO Type II/III requirements), and DWT is the **contractual specification**.
5. Design-driving parameter: **$DWT_{req} = 65{,}000$ t**.
6. Category anchor: KTU Module 2, Section 1.1, "Deadweight Carriers."

**(b) Principal dimensions (7 marks):**

**Step 1.** Compute displacement:

$$\Delta = \frac{DWT_{req}}{\eta} = \frac{65{,}000}{0.78} = 83{,}333.3 \text{ t}$$

**Step 2.** Compute volumetric displacement:

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{83{,}333.3}{1.025} = 81{,}300.8 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3.** Set up the cubic in $L$ using the chosen ratios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\
\nabla &= C_B \cdot L \cdot \frac{L}{6.0} \cdot \frac{B}{2.6}
\end{aligned}
$$

But $B = L/6.0$ and $T = B/2.6 = L/15.6$, hence:

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot \frac{L^3}{6.0 \times 15.6} = \frac{C_B \cdot L^3}{93.6}$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^3 &= \frac{93.6 \times \nabla}{C_B} = \frac{93.6 \times 81{,}300.8}{0.81} \\
L^3 &= \frac{7{,}609{,}755}{0.81} = 9{,}394{,}759 \text{ m}^3 \\
L &= \sqrt[3]{9{,}394{,}759} = 211.06 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Compute $B$ and $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{L}{6.0} = \frac{211.06}{6.0} = 35.18 \text{ m} \\
T &= \frac{B}{2.6} = \frac{35.18}{2.6} = 13.53 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6.** Verification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{calc} &= \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 1.025 \times 0.81 \times 211.06 \times 35.18 \times 13.53 \\
&= 83{,}179 \text{ t} \\
DWT_{calc} &= \eta \cdot \Delta_{calc} = 0.78 \times 83{,}179 = 64{,}880 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Error $= \vert 64{,}880 - 65{,}000 \vert / 65{,}000 = 0.18\%$ — **converged**.

**Final Answer (boxed for board exam):**
- $L = 211.06$ m
- $B = 35.18$ m
- $T = 13.53$ m

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Linear Dimension + Special Purpose Combined

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**  
An owner wishes to operate a **100,000 DWT bulk carrier** between two Indian ports. The vessel must also be capable of operating in **ice-covered waters** during the winter season.

**(a)** Discuss the **classification challenge** — is this a deadweight carrier, a linear-dimension ship, or a special-purpose vessel? Justify your answer with reference to KTU Module 2 categories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Outline the **complete basic design procedure** for this vessel, identifying the steps that change because of the ice-strength requirement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification challenge (7 marks):**

1. The **100,000 DWT** requirement places the vessel firmly in the **deadweight carrier** category by mass.
2. However, the **ice-class** requirement (e.g., Finnish-Swedish Ice Class 1A Super, or Russian Arc7) imposes **linear dimension constraints** — ice-classed ships have **higher scantlings** (plate thickness), which translates into higher lightweight, and often a **wider beam-to-draft ratio** for ice-breaking capability.
3. The mission is therefore **dual**: DWT is the primary driver, but ice-class adds a **special-purpose constraint**.
4. **KTU Module 2 verdict:** Classify as a **Deadweight Carrier with Special Purpose features** — primary entry into the spiral is via $DWT_{req} = 100{,}000$ t, but the spiral is **modified** by:
   - Increased $LWT$ (typically $+8\%$ to $+15\%$ over standard bulker)
   - Reduced $\eta$ (typically $0.72$ instead of $0.80$)
   - Higher $B/T$ ratio (3.0 instead of 2.6) for ice-breaking sloped shoulders.
5. Justification citation: KTU Module 2, Section 1.4 — "Special Purpose Ships may share characteristics of deadweight carriers when the design-driver remains mass."

**(b) Modified design procedure (7 marks):**

| Step | Standard Bulker | Ice-Strengthened Bulker | Change |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Set $DWT_{req} = 100{,}000$ t | Same | None |
| 2 | $\eta = 0.80$ | $\eta = 0.72$ (lower due to heavier scantlings) | **Reduced** |
| 3 | $\Delta = 125{,}000$ t | $\Delta = 138{,}889$ t | **Increased** |
| 4 | $C_B = 0.82$ | $C_B = 0.78$ (slightly slimmer) | **Reduced** |
| 5 | $L/B = 6.5$, $B/T = 2.6$ | $L/B = 5.8$, $B/T = 3.0$ | **Altered** |
| 6 | Solve cubic for $L$ | Solve cubic with new ratios | **Iterate** |
| 7 | Powering on open water | Powering + ice-breaking resistance | **Add resistance margin** |
| 8 | Standard hull plating | High-tensile steel, ice-belt strake | **Specialist** |

**Key additions for ice-class:**
- Apply **IACS Polar Class** scantling additions.
- Add **15% margin** on powering for continuous ice-breaking at 3 knots.
- Verify **Ice Class 1A Super** rule compliance for propeller and rudder clearances.
- The spiral therefore takes **3–4 extra iterations** vs. a standard bulker.

**Final Answer Summary:**
The ship is primarily a **deadweight carrier** (100,000 DWT), but the **special-purpose ice-class mission** modifies every step of the basic design procedure, particularly the lightweight estimate, block coefficient, dimension ratios, and powering margin.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- [ ] **Four categories** of ships by design driver: **D**eadweight, **C**apacity, **L**inear-dimension, **S**pecial-purpose.
- [ ] **Design-driving parameter** = the **first fixed variable** in the design spiral; it determines the category.
- [ ] **Deadweight Carrier**: Design driver = $DWT_{req}$ (tonnes). Examples: tankers, bulkers.
- [ ] **Capacity Carrier**: Design driver = cargo volume $V_c$ (m³) or slots (TEU). Use stowage factor $SF$ to bridge to DWT.
- [ ] **Linear Dimension Ship**: One or more principal dimensions are **constrained** by infrastructure (canals, locks, ports).
- [ ] **Special Purpose Ship**: Mission-specific; non-standard hull forms; multi-objective optimisation.
- [ ] **Core equation (all categories):** $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$.
- [ ] **Deadweight relation:** $DWT = \eta \cdot \Delta$, where $\eta = 0.55$–$0.85$.
- [ ] **Sea water density:** $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³.
- [ ] **Standard KTU ratios:**
    - Tankers/Bulkers: $L/B = 5.5$–$7.0$, $B/T = 2.5$–$3.0$.
    - Container ships: $L/B = 6.0$–$8.0$, $B/T = 2.4$–$2.8$.
- [ ] **Panamax limits (original canal):** $L \le 294.13$ m, $B \le 32.31$ m, $T \le 12.04$ m (TFW).
- [ ] **Convergence criterion:** $\vert DWT_{calc} - DWT_{req} \vert / DWT_{req} \le 2\%$.
- [ ] **Capacity carrier pitfall:** Stowage factor must always be quoted in **m³/t**.
- [ ] **Linear-dimension pitfall:** Canal $T$ limits are in **fresh water**, not salt.
- [ ] **Special-purpose pitfall:** Never apply standard $L/B$, $B/T$ ratios blindly.
- [ ] **Design spiral entry points:**
    - Deadweight $\rightarrow$ DWT $\rightarrow$ Δ $\rightarrow$ L,B,T
    - Capacity $\rightarrow$ V_c $\rightarrow$ DWT $\rightarrow$ Δ $\rightarrow$ L,B,T
    - Linear $\rightarrow$ L (or B) $\rightarrow$ iterate $\rightarrow$ free variables
    - Special $\rightarrow$ mission KPIs $\rightarrow$ bespoke geometry
- [ ] **Always** state the design-driver explicitly before solving — examiners allocate 1 mark for this in 14-mark questions.
- [ ] **Lightweight formula** (rapid estimate): $LWT \approx 0.04 \cdot L^{2.5}$ (very approximate, tanker-class).

> **End of KTU Module 2 Notes — Basic Design Procedure for Main Categories of Ships.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure for Selection of Main Dimensions (Length, Breadth, Depth, Draught) and Form Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Procedure for Selection of Main Dimensions (Length, Breadth, Depth, Draught) and Form Coefficients # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Main Dimensions of a Ship:** The principal geometric parameters that completely characterize the size and underwater volume of a vessel, comprising **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP or Lpp)**, **Moulded Breadth (B)**, **Moulded Depth (D)**, and **Design Draught (T)**. These dimensions, together with the **Form Coefficients** (Block, Prismatic, Midship, and Waterplane), define the hull form and govern resistance, powering, stability, cargo capacity, and structural strength. The **selection of main dimensions** is the most critical step in the **preliminary design phase** of a ship. It is an iterative, constraint-driven optimization problem in which the Naval Architect balances **operational requirements** (deadweight, speed, range), **regulatory constraints** (stability, freeboard, tonnage), **economic factors** (building cost, fuel consumption), and **seakeeping/maneuvering performance**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge and the Box" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are tasked with designing a **leak-proof floating box** to carry **100 people** across a river at **5 m/s**. The box must: > - Be long enough to cut through water cheaply (length relates to speed/resistance) > - Be wide enough to carry 100 people side-by-side without toppling (breadth relates to stability/capacity) > - Be deep enough so waves don't flood it (depth relates to safety/freeboard) > - Sit low enough in water to displace its own weight (draught relates to buoyancy/loading) > - Be shaped "fat" or "slim" depending on cargo vs. speed need (form coefficients) > > In real ship design, the **box becomes a sophisticated hull form**, and the **river becomes the open ocean**, but the core trade-off logic remains identical. ## 1.3 The Four Pillars of Main Dimensions > [!IMPORTANT] > **The four principal dimensions must be selected as a SET, not independently.** A change in Length affects the resistance curve; a change in Beam affects stability; a change in Draught affects block coefficient; a change in Depth affects freeboard and structural strength. They are mathematically linked through the **displacement equation**: > > $$\nabla = L \times B \times T \times C_B$$ > > where $\nabla$ is the **volume of displacement** (m³), and $C_B$ is the **block coefficient**. ## 1.4 Definition of Form Coefficients > [!NOTE] > **Form Coefficients are dimensionless ratios** that quantify the "fatness" or "fineness" of a ship's hull form compared to its bounding rectangular parallelepiped. | Coefficient | Symbol | Definition | Physical Meaning | |:-----------:|:------:|:-----------|:-----------------| | **Block Coefficient** | $C_B$ | $\frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Overall fullness of underwater hull | | **Prismatic Coefficient** | $C_P$ | $\frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | Longitudinal distribution of volume | | **Midship Section Coefficient** | $C_M$ | $\frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Fullness at midship only | | **Waterplane Area Coefficient** | $C_{WP}$ | $\frac{A_W}{L \cdot B}$ | Fullness of waterplane area | Where $A_M$ = Area of Midship Section, $A_W$ = Waterplane Area. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Step Procedure for Selection of Main Dimensions The Naval Architect follows a **systematic iterative procedure**. Below is the canonical KTU-recommended workflow. ### STEP 1: Establish Design Inputs (Mission Specification) The owner specifies: - **Deadweight (DWT)** in tonnes — total weight of cargo, fuel, freshwater, stores, crew, and passengers. - **Service Speed ($V_S$)** in knots — the design cruising speed at design draught. - **Range and Endurance** — total nautical miles between bunkering. - **Type of Service** — bulk carrier, tanker, container, naval vessel, fishing, etc. > [!NOTE] > **Lightweight (LWT)** is estimated from statistical formulas based on ship type (e.g., for tankers: LWT ≈ 0.35–0.45 × DWT; for bulk carriers: LWT ≈ 0.25–0.35 × DWT). > > $$\text{Displacement } (\Delta) = \text{DWT} + \text{LWT} \quad (\text{in tonnes})$$ Converting displacement to volume: $$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{\Delta}{1.025} \quad (\text{m}^3)$$ where seawater density $\rho = 1.025$ **tonnes/m³** (freshwater: $1.000$ **tonnes/m³**). --- ### STEP 2: Choose the Length ($L$) Length is the **most influential** dimension because it directly affects: - **Hull resistance** (especially wave-making resistance scales with $L$) - **Speed capability** (longer ships can go faster at the same power) - **Cargo capacity** (longer ships have more internal volume) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two complementary methods** are used in KTU-approved practice: > > **Method A: Length-to-Speed Relationship (Statistical)** > > Empirical formulas relate $L_{PP}$ to $V_S$: > > $$\frac{L_{PP}}{\nabla^{1/3}} = f(V_S, \text{Ship Type})$$ > > For example, **single-screw cargo ships** at moderate speeds: > > $$L_{PP} \approx 4.5 \text{ to } 5.5 \times \left(\frac{\nabla}{100}\right)^{1/3}$$ > > **Method B: Froude Number Matching** > > $$L = \frac{V^2}{g \cdot F_n^2} = \frac{V^2}{g \cdot F_n^2}$$ > > where $V$ is in m/s and $F_n$ is selected based on ship type (typically **0.18 to 0.32** for displacement hulls). > > $$1 \text{ knot} = 0.5144 \text{ m/s}$$ --- ### STEP 3: Choose the Block Coefficient ($C_B$) The block coefficient is the **single most important form coefficient** because it quantifies the hull's volumetric efficiency. > [!NOTE] > **Higher $C_B$** → fatter hull → more cargo capacity → higher frictional + form resistance → lower speed. > **Lower $C_B$** → finer hull → less cargo → lower wave resistance → higher speed. **Statistical $C_B$ Selection** (parenthetical formula by **Ayre**, refined by **Schneekluth**): $$C_B = 1.0 - \left(\frac{0.65 \cdot V_K^{1/3}}{L_{PP}^{1/3}}\right)$$ where $V_K$ is displacement volume in m³ and $L_{PP}$ is in metres. Typical $C_B$ ranges (memorize for KTU exams): | Ship Type | Typical $C_B$ Range | |:----------|:-------------------:| | **Ultra-large tanker** (VLCC) | 0.83 – 0.88 | | **Bulk carrier** | 0.75 – 0.82 | | **General cargo** | 0.65 – 0.75 | | **Container ship** | 0.55 – 0.68 | | **Naval frigate** | 0.45 – 0.55 | | **Destroyer** | 0.40 – 0.50 | | **Sailing yacht** | 0.30 – 0.40 | --- ### STEP 4: Calculate Breadth ($B$) and Draught ($T$) Once $L$ and $C_B$ are fixed, $B$ and $T$ are determined from the displacement equation: $$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **single equation with two unknowns**. We need a **second constraint** — typically a $B/T$ ratio. **The $B/T$ Ratio (Beam-to-Draught Ratio):** - $B/T < 2.0$ → "slender" hull → low resistance, prone to roll - $B/T = 2.0$ to $2.5$ → "balanced" hull - $B/T > 2.5$ → "bluff" hull → good stability, more resistance, shallow-draft For a typical **single-screw merchant ship**: $$\frac{B}{T} \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 2.8$$ For **fine naval vessels**: $$\frac{B}{T} \approx 2.5 \text{ to } 3.2$$ Solving simultaneously: $$B = \sqrt{\frac{\nabla \cdot (B/T)}{L \cdot C_B}}$$ $$T = \frac{B}{(B/T)}$$ --- ### STEP 5: Choose Depth ($D$) and Verify Freeboard Depth $D$ is **not directly in the displacement equation** but is critical for: - **Structural strength** (girder depth, hatch coaming, double bottom height) - **Freeboard** (height of deck above waterline) - **Bulkhead and deckhead heights** for cargo handling > [!NOTE] > **Freeboard** is regulated by the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL / LL 66, as amended).** > > $$D = T + \text{Freeboard} + \text{Sheer correction}$$ > > Typical freeboard for cargo ships: **1.0 m to 2.5 m**. > > **$D/T$ Ratio** typically lies between **1.10 and 1.30**. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table for Part A and Part B questions.** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Range / Unit | |:-:|:--------|:------------|:---------------------| | 1 | $\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$ | Displacement volume equation | m³ | | 2 | $\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho$ | Displacement weight | tonnes | | 3 | $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ | Sea water density | constant | | 4 | $C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | 0.40 – 0.88 | | 5 | $C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$ | Prismatic coefficient | 0.55 – 0.85 | | 6 | $C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Midship coefficient | 0.85 – 0.99 | | 7 | $C_{WP} = \frac{A_W}{L \cdot B}$ | Waterplane coefficient | 0.65 – 0.90 | | 8 | $F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$ | Froude number | 0.15 – 0.50 | | 9 | $R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | $10^7$ – $10^9$ | | 10 | $V_{\text{knots}} = 0.5144 \cdot V_{\text{m/s}}$ | Speed conversion | constant | | 11 | $L/\nabla^{1/3}$ | Length-displacement ratio | 4.0 – 7.0 | | 12 | $B/T$ | Beam-draught ratio | 2.0 – 3.0 | | 13 | $D/T$ | Depth-draught ratio | 1.10 – 1.30 | | 14 | $L/B$ | Length-beam ratio | 5.0 – 8.0 | | 15 | $\text{Freeboard} = D - T$ | Vertical distance WL to deck | m | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Relationship:** $C_B = C_P \times C_M$. This single identity links three form coefficients and is the most-tested identity in KTU ship design papers. ## 2.3 Inter-Relationship Between Form Coefficients > [!IMPORTANT] > **The three coefficients $C_B$, $C_P$, and $C_M$ are NOT independent.** They are geometrically related: > > $$C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$$ > > $C_M$ is the fullness at the **midship section only**; $C_P$ describes how the cross-sectional area **varies** from bow to stern; $C_B$ is the **integrated** volumetric fullness. Knowing two fixes the third. **Engineering Utility:** - **Tankers & bulk carriers** → High $C_M$ (≈ 0.99) + High $C_P$ (≈ 0.85) → $C_B$ ≈ 0.84 (bluff, full hull) - **Container ships** → Moderate $C_M$ (≈ 0.95) + Lower $C_P$ (≈ 0.65) → $C_B$ ≈ 0.62 (fine ends) - **Naval combatants** → Low $C_M$ (≈ 0.80) + Low $C_P$ (≈ 0.65) → $C_B$ ≈ 0.52 (sharp, V-shaped) ## 2.4 Why Form Coefficients Matter — Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Application:** When a ship owner requests "more cargo capacity" without changing the principal dimensions, the Naval Architect cannot just increase $C_B$ arbitrarily. Higher $C_B$ at the same $L$ and $V$: > - **Increases frictional resistance** (~5–10% per 0.05 increase) > - **Increases wave resistance** disproportionately at high $F_n$ > - **Requires more propulsive power** → larger engine → higher fuel cost > > Conversely, a **finer hull** (lower $C_B$) reduces resistance but reduces **cargo hold volume** by tens of thousands of m³ — directly impacting revenue. The form coefficient selection is therefore the **central economic-engineering trade-off** in ship design. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Displacement Equation to Main Dimensions ### Derivation 1: Expressing Length in Terms of Speed and Froude Number **Given:** Service speed $V_S$ (knots) and chosen Froude number $F_n$. **Step 1:** Convert speed from knots to m/s. $$V = V_S \times 0.5144 \quad \text{(m/s)}$$ *Textual explanation:* A knot is defined as one nautical mile per hour, and one nautical mile = 1852 m, so 1 knot = 1852/3600 m/s = 0.5144 m/s. The conversion is necessary because the Froude number is defined in SI units. **Step 2:** Apply Froude number definition. $$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$ *Textual explanation:* The Froude number is a dimensionless ratio of inertial to gravitational forces, governing wave-making resistance. It must be in the displacement-hull regime ($F_n < 0.40$) for most merchant ships to avoid excessive wave resistance. **Step 3:** Solve for $L$. $$F_n \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot L} = V$$ Squaring both sides: $$F_n^2 \cdot g \cdot L = V^2$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{L = \frac{V^2}{g \cdot F_n^2}}$$ *Textual explanation:* This closed-form expression lets the designer compute the required length directly once $V$ and the target $F_n$ are fixed.
