- A defining element in damage stability *R*-index calculation.
- The space forward of CB (forepeak) is reserved as a non-access void or anchor chain locker only.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **$0.05L$ with $0.05L_{pp}$**. Always use **$L$ (rule length)**, not $L_{pp}$, for SOLAS bulkhead offsets. Marks are deducted if the wrong length basis is used.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with the aid of a neat sketch the **General Arrangement Plan** of a general cargo ship, identifying the following: collision bulkhead, engine room bulkheads, cargo holds, double bottom tanks, and after peak. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has rule length $L = 120$ m, breadth $B = 20$ m, draught $T = 7.5$ m, block coefficient $C_B = 0.80$. The GA divides the ship into 5 equal cargo holds separated by 6 transverse bulkheads. The collision bulkhead is at 0.05L from FP. Calculate:
- (i) The length of each cargo hold,
- (ii) Total cargo volume assuming a uniform hold cross-section of $B \times 6$ m height, and
- (iii) Displacement at design draught using sea water density $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

A General Arrangement Plan is a **top-down (plan) view** of the ship showing all decks, bulkheads, compartments, and major features. The student should draw a representative sketch (similar to the ASCII layout in Section 4.5) and clearly mark:

| Element | Location on Plan | Sketch Annotation |
|---|---|---|
| **Collision Bulkhead (CB)** | At 0.05L from FP, athwartships line | Solid line marked "W" (watertight) |
| **Cargo Holds 1–5** | Between transverse bulkheads, amidships | Rectangles labelled "HOLD 1" to "HOLD 5" |
| **Engine Room (ER)** | Aft, between two engine room bulkheads | Boxed area labelled "ER" |
| **Engine Room Forward Bulkhead (ERFB)** | Forward boundary of ER, watertight | Solid "W" line |
| **Engine Room Aft Bulkhead (ERAB)** | Aft boundary of ER, watertight | Solid "W" line |
| **Double Bottom Tanks** | Below cargo holds, between keel and tank top | Shaded strip below holds |
| **After Peak (AP) Bulkhead** | Aft of ER, near AP | Solid "W" line, beyond which lies AP tank |
| **Centerline Bulkhead** | Optional, in some cargo ships; mandatory in tankers | Dashed line CL |

**Key features to mention:**

- All transverse bulkheads shown as continuous lines from port to starboard shell.
- Double bottom shown shaded; depth typically **$0.05L$ to $0.10L$** (here 6 to 12 m — design choice of 1.2 m or higher per class).
- Hatchways, mast, funnel, and accommodation block shown on upper deck.
- Scale notation, ship name, and rule particulars in title block.

**Examiner's Expectation:** A neat sketch with at least 5 of the 6 elements clearly labelled and a one-line description of each.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
$$
L = 120 \text{ m}, \quad B = 20 \text{ m}, \quad T = 7.5 \text{ m}, \quad C_B = 0.80
$$

**(i) Length of each cargo hold:**

The ship is divided into 5 equal holds. Total cargo length = ship length minus the sum of spaces for: forepeak, machinery, and afterpeak.

- Forepeak (FP to CB): $0.05L = 0.05 \times 120 = 6$ m
- Afterpeak (APB to AP): typically $0.02L = 2.4$ m (assume 3 m for round numbers)
- Engine room length: assume $0.20L = 0.20 \times 120 = 24$ m

Sum of non-cargo length = $6 + 24 + 3 = 33$ m

Available cargo length = $120 - 33 = 87$ m

**Each cargo hold length:**
$$
L_{hold} = \frac{87}{5} = 17.4 \text{ m}
$$

> **Alternative simpler assumption (if KTU 2024 paper says "equally divides 5 holds in the cargo length L"):**
> $L_{hold} = L/5 = 24$ m each (this is a frequent textbook assumption).

We will demonstrate the **rigorous version** above and present the alternative for completeness.

**(ii) Total cargo volume (uniform cross-section $B \times 6$ m height):**

$$
V_{cargo} = N \cdot L_{hold} \cdot B \cdot H_{hold} = 5 \times 17.4 \times 20 \times 6
$$

Computing step-by-step:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cargo} &= 5 \times 17.4 \times 20 \times 6 \\
&= 5 \times 17.4 \times 120 \\
&= 5 \times 2088 \\
&= 10{,}440 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Displacement at design draught:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B \\
&= 120 \times 20 \times 7.5 \times 0.80 \\
&= 120 \times 20 \times 6.0 \\
&= 120 \times 120 \\
&= 14{,}400 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mass displacement:**
$$
\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho = 14{,}400 \times 1.025 = 14{,}760 \text{ t}
$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $L_{hold}$ | $17.4$ m (rigorous) or $24$ m (simplified) |
| $V_{cargo}$ | $10{,}440$ m³ |
| $\Delta$ | $14{,}760$ t |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting the forepeak, engine room, and afterpeak deductions. If you write $L_{hold} = 24$ m without comment, you lose 2 marks. The rigorous deduction wins full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Discuss the **subdivision and damage stability** requirements as applicable to a cargo ship, explaining the role of the *attainable subdivision length* and the *factor of subdivision* $K$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The GA of a small chemical tanker shows a pump room of length 8 m located amidships, with 4 cargo tanks of equal length 12 m each on either side. The rule length is 100 m, and the collision bulkhead is at 0.05L. Compute:
- (i) Total cargo tank volume assuming tank cross-section as $4$ m $\times 4$ m,
- (ii) Pump room volume (length $\times$ breadth $\times$ depth = $8 \times 6 \times 5$),
- (iii) Free surface moment of inertia for an 80% filled cargo tank, and its effect on GM if displacement $\Delta = 4000$ t. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Subdivision Concept:**

A cargo ship is subdivided into a number of watertight compartments such that, after assumed damage causing flooding of one or more compartments, the residual stability and buoyancy remain sufficient to keep the ship afloat in a stable condition.

**Key Definitions:**

1. **Factor of Subdivision ($K$):** Defined as $K = N / (N + 1.2)$, where $N$ is the *permitted number of compartments* that may be assumed flooded. Higher $K$ means tighter subdivision (more bulkheads).

2. **Floodable Length ($\ell$):** The maximum length of a compartment (or group of compartments) that, when assumed flooded, leaves the ship with sufficient residual stability as per the margin line criterion.

3. **Permissible Length ($L_p$):** The length derived from the floodable length curve multiplied by the factor of subdivision: $L_p = \ell \cdot K$.

4. **Attainable Subdivision Length ($L_s$):** The actual maximum length of the ship that can be obtained by arranging bulkheads consistent with the permissible length criteria at every station.

5. **Required Subdivision Index ($R$):** A weighted sum that combines contributions from all possible damage cases. For cargo ships per ICLL Reg. 27, $R$ must be $\geq 1 - 0.5K$.

**Role of GA:** The GA is the source of *all* compartment lengths. Damage stability is computed *from* the GA, not the other way around. Iteration is necessary because initial GA lengths may not satisfy the criterion.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- 4 cargo tanks, each $12$ m long, cross-section $4 \times 4$ m
- Pump room: $8 \times 6 \times 5$ m
- $L = 100$ m, CB at $0.05L$
- $\Delta = 4000$ t, tank filling = 80%

**(i) Total cargo tank volume:**

Volume of one tank:
$$
V_1 = 12 \times 4 \times 4 = 192 \text{ m}^3
$$

Total for 4 tanks:
$$
V_{cargo, total} = 4 \times 192 = 768 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(ii) Pump room volume:**
$$
V_{PR} = 8 \times 6 \times 5 = 240 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(iii) Free surface moment of inertia:**

For a rectangular free surface of length $a$ and breadth $b$:
$$
i = \frac{a \cdot b^3}{12}
$$

For a cargo tank: $a = 12$ m, $b = 4$ m:
$$
i = \frac{12 \times 4^3}{12} = \frac{12 \times 64}{12} = 64 \text{ m}^4
$$

For 80% filling (assume free surface is 80% of full area — true only for rectangular tanks; for full rectangular tank at any filling between 5% and 95%, the FS correction uses *full* area). Standard practice:
$$
i_{eff} = \mu \cdot i \quad \text{with } \mu = 0.8
$$

So $i_{eff} = 0.8 \times 64 = 51.2$ m$^4$.

**Effect on GM (Free Surface Correction):**
$$
GG' = \frac{\rho_{cargo} \cdot i_{eff}}{\Delta}
$$

For chemical cargo (assume $\rho = 1.0$ t/m³):
$$
GG' = \frac{1.0 \times 51.2}{4000} = 0.0128 \text{ m} = 12.8 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Interpretation:** GM reduces by 12.8 mm per partially filled tank. With 4 tanks partially filled, the cumulative effect is $4 \times 12.8 = 51.2$ mm reduction. This is a *significant* loss of stability and is precisely why chemical tankers are required to have low filling levels or full filling for sailing.

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $V_{cargo,total}$ | $768$ m³ |
| $V_{PR}$ | $240$ m³ |
| $i$ per tank | $64$ m$^4$ |
| $GG'$ per tank | $12.8$ mm |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in GA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong length basis:** Mixing up $L$, $L_{pp}$, $L_s$, $L_T$. SOLAS uses $L$ (rule length); powering uses $L_{pp}$; tonnage uses $L_T$.
> 2. **Bulkhead offsets:** Forgetting to express them as fractions of $L$ (e.g., 0.05L, 0.08L) instead of absolute values.
> 3. **Missing compartments:** A "GA" that doesn't show all 5 mandatory bulkheads (CB, APB, ERFB, ERAB, cofferdam where applicable) is incomplete — deduct 2 marks.
> 4. **No scale annotation:** Always state the scale (1:100 or 1:50) on a GA sketch.
> 5. **No title block:** Every GA in KTU 2024 must have ship name, principal dimensions, owner, yard, date, scale, and revision number.
> 6. **Damage stability formula misapplication:** Using $L_{pp}$ in place of $L$ in SOLAS formulae.
> 7. **Free surface correction with full tank:** No FSE if tank is 100% full (no free surface) or 0% (empty). Marks lost if applied blindly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GA = Integrating drawing** of all ship systems in one place.
- **Three views:** Plan, Profile, Sections — all mutually consistent.
- **Five mandatory bulkheads:** Collision (CB), Engine Room Forward (ERFB), Engine Room Aft (ERAB), After Peak (APB), Cofferdam (where applicable).
- **Rule length $L$** is the basis for all SOLAS and ICLL bulkhead offsets.
- **Collision bulkhead at $0.05L$ to $0.08L$ from FP.**
- **Subdivision index $R \geq 1 - 0.5K$ for cargo ships per ICLL.**
- **Factor of subdivision** $K = N / (N + 1.2)$ where $N$ = permitted flooded compartments.
- **Floodable length** is *not* a fixed number — it depends on residual stability after flooding.
- **Free surface correction** $GG' = \rho \cdot i / \Delta$; $i$ depends on free-surface area shape (rectangular: $a \cdot b^3 / 12$).
- **GA scale = 1:100 (plan/profile), 1:50 (sections).**
- **Title block is mandatory** — ship name, L, B, T, owner, yard, scale, date, revision.
- **Compartment sizes** are limited by the *minimum* of floodable, operational, and structural constraints.
- **Double bottom height** typically $\geq 1.0$ m or $0.05L$ per class rules.
- **Engine room length** typically $0.20L$ to $0.35L$.
- **Tank cross-sections** must be rectangular-prism-approximated for capacity tables.
- **GA feeds every other discipline** — hull form, structure, machinery, outfitting, stability, regulation.
- **The *R*-index and freeboard calculations both consume GA data** — they are *not* independent.
- **Always convert all GA dimensions to a common unit** (metres) before any calculation.
- **SOLAS / MARPOL / ICLL conventions are the legal backbone** of every GA — ignoring them is a mark-loser.
- **Iterate, iterate, iterate:** The first GA is never final. Expect 3 to 5 major iterations.
- **A neat GA sketch** (with all required annotations) is worth at least 3 marks in any Part B answer.
- **The GA is a contract document** — it carries legal, financial, and operational weight.
- **A well-developed GA demonstrates mastery** of *integration* — the heart of naval architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing GA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing GA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of General Arrangement (GA)

In the context of naval architecture, **General Arrangement (GA)** refers to the comprehensive two- and three-dimensional layout of all internal volumes, compartments, structural members, machinery, equipment, accommodations, and external features of a vessel, as represented through key plans such as the *Profile (Sheer) Plan*, *Deck Plans*, *Cross-Section Plan*, and *Tank Capacity Plan*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** The GA is the *functional blueprint* that resolves the spatial conflict between **mission requirements** (cargo, crew, propulsion), **safety obligations** (SOLAS damage stability), and **economic constraints** (payload, build cost). It is the single most important drawing produced in preliminary ship design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine designing the **floor plan of a multi-storey apartment building on a floating platform** that must:
- Carry thousands of tonnes of heavy goods in its lower levels,
- House 25 families in the middle levels,
- House a power plant (engines + generators + fuel tanks) at the bottom,
- Survive a flood where any one compartment is ruptured,
- Allow every person to escape to the roof in under 3 minutes,
- Cost as little as possible per square metre built.

Every wall placement, door position, window, staircase, elevator, and fuel tank is constrained simultaneously by **function, safety, regulation, and cost**. The General Arrangement is the engineering solution to this multi-variable optimization problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** A "good" GA is not necessarily one with the *largest* cargo capacity. It is the one that satisfies the **highest number of conflicting constraints with the lowest compromise on commercial viability**.

### 1.3 Standard KTU Terminology

| Term | Definition | Significance |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **LBP** | Length Between Perpendiculars | Governs compartmentation and subdivision |
| **Breadth (B)** | Moulded breadth at midship | Sets limits on transverse layout |
| **Depth (D)** | Moulded depth to main deck | Defines deck count and headroom |
| **Cb** | Block coefficient = ∇ / (LBP × B × T) | Indicates hull fullness |
| **DWT** | Deadweight Tonnage | Payload vs. lightweight trade-off |
| **E/R** | Engine Room | Below-deck machinery zone |
| **WT** | Watertight | Critical for subdivision |

> [!TIP]
> The "GA" abbreviation used in this module **always refers to General Arrangement**, NOT to "Gauss" (mathematics) or "Graphic Art." This is a common KTU exam ambiguity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Influencing Factors

The factors influencing the General Arrangement of a ship are broadly classified into **six functional categories**. Each category imposes mandatory or preferred constraints on the placement of hull volumes.

#### Category 1 — Functional / Mission Factors
These derive directly from the **purpose** the ship must serve.

1. **Ship Type and Trade Route**
   - Tanker → large central cargo block, double hull (MARPOL Annex I).
   - Bulk Carrier → large unobstructed cargo holds with hatch covers.
   - Container Ship → cellular guides, wide hatches, no tweendecks.
   - Passenger Ship → large open decks, multiple public spaces.
   - Naval Vessel → weapons magazines forward, command spaces aft.

2. **Cargo / Payload Requirements**
   - Homogeneous cargo → single large tank/hold.
   - Segregated cargo → multiple smaller compartments with separate pipelines.
   - Refrigerated cargo (reefer) → insulation, separate plant rooms.

3. **Speed, Range, and Endurance**
   - High speed → larger engine room, more bunker capacity.
   - Long range → distributed fuel/ballast tanks.

4. **Manning Scale**
   - Crew size (e.g., 25 in a bulker, 1500+ in a cruise ship) directly drives the **accommodation block** size.

#### Category 2 — Safety & Regulatory Factors
5. **SOLAS Damage Stability Subdivision** — number and location of watertight bulkheads.
6. **Fire Safety (SOLAS Ch. II-2)** — separation of high-risk zones, fire-rated boundaries.
7. **Life-Saving Appliances (LSA)** — muster stations, embarkation decks.
8. **MARPOL Pollution Prevention** — segregated ballast, double-hull requirements, oily-water separator location.
9. **Load Line Convention (1966)** — defines freeboard, deck line, superstructure limits.
10. **Classification Society Rules** (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS, BV) — scantlings, tank testing, redundancy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students often confuse *statutory* (mandatory, e.g., SOLAS) with *class* (recommended, e.g., LR rules) requirements. Always clarify in the answer.

#### Category 3 — Hydrostatic & Stability Factors
11. **Intact Stability** — metacentric height (GM), centre of gravity (KG) limits.
12. **Damage Stability** — residual GM after flooding one or more compartments.
13. **Sheer, Camber, and Freeboard** — affects external deck profile and bridge visibility.
14. **Trim and List Limits** — longitudinal and transverse balance.

#### Category 4 — Construction & Economic Factors
15. **Building Strategy** — block construction implies smaller, modular compartments.
16. **Material & Welding** — affects plate sizes, stiffener spacing.
17. **Outfit Density** — equipment-heavy ships (e.g., FPSOs) require more access trunks.
18. **Cost of Steel and Outfit** — drives the lightweight-to-payload ratio.

#### Category 5 — Operational / Human Factors
19. **Working Environment** — noise, vibration isolation (E/R suspension).
20. **Ergonomics and Human Factors** — escape routes ≤ 30 m, signage, lighting.
21. **Port and Terminal Interface** — manifold height, hatch reach for shore cranes.
22. **Maintenance Access** — cofferdams, manholes, ducting.

#### Category 6 — Environmental & Geographic Factors
23. **Operating Sea Area** — ice-classed ships need thicker shell, smaller hatch openings.
24. **Climate Zone** — ventilation, insulation, air-conditioning capacity.
25. **Flag State & Owner Preferences** — flag-specific manning, owner brand layout.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (GA-Relevant Ratios)

> [!NOTE]
> Ship GA is governed more by **constraints and decision logic** than by explicit formulas. However, the following *dimensional ratios* are tested in KTU exams and **must be memorised**.

| # | Formula / Ratio | Expression | Typical Value | Application to GA |
|---|------------------|------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Block Coefficient (Cb)** | $C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \times B \times T}$ | 0.60 – 0.85 | Defines hull fullness → hold volume |
| 2 | **Prismatic Coefficient (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \times L_{BP}}$ | 0.55 – 0.85 | Indicates run form → engine room length |
| 3 | **Midship Coefficient (Cm)** | $C_m = \dfrac{A_M}{B \times T}$ | 0.90 – 0.99 | Hull cross-section fullness |
| 4 | **DWT Ratio** | $DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{light}$ | 10⁴ – 10⁵ t | Cargo capacity allocation |
| 5 | **Lightweight Fraction** | $f_{L} = \dfrac{\Delta_L}{\Delta_{loaded}}$ | 0.30 – 0.55 | Steel weight per compartment |
| 6 | **Number of Watertight Bulkheads** | $N_{wt} \geq \dfrac{L_{BP}}{30\text{ m}} + 1$ | Rule of thumb | Mandatory subdivision |
| 7 | **Minimum Freeboard (F)** | $F = f(C_b, L_{BP})$ from LL Tables | 0.5 – 3.0 m | Sets deck line position |
| 8 | **Accommodation Deck Headroom** | $h_{acc} \geq 2.0$ m (SOLAS) | ≥ 2.0 m | Defines D + number of decks |
| 9 | **Engine Room Length (LER)** | $L_{ER} \approx (0.15 - 0.25) \times L_{BP}$ | 15% – 25% LBP | Aft/central placement |
| 10 | **Bunker Tank Volume** | $V_{bunker} = \dfrac{R \times BHP \times SF}{c \times \rho_f}$ | varies | Range × specific fuel oil consumption |

Where:
- $\nabla$ = displaced volume, $A_M$ = midship area
- $\Delta_{loaded}, \Delta_L$ = loaded and lightweight displacement
- $R$ = range (nautical miles), $SF$ = specific fuel consumption (g/kWh)
- $c$ = calorific value, $\rho_f$ = fuel density

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When writing ratios in KTU answer sheets, **always cite the regulation source** (e.g., "as per SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 4"). This fetches a half-mark bonus in valuation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In commercial shipyards, the GA drawing is the **contractual document** between the Owner, the Yard, and the Classification Society. Any change to the GA after steel cutting incurs **exponential cost penalties** (typically doubling per stage of construction). Therefore, the *systematic listing* of influencing factors, as above, is the **first step of the spiral design process** (Evans, 1959 spiral).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Logical Decision Sequence for GA Development

The General Arrangement is **not drawn freehand**; it is derived through a deterministic sequence. The following 10-step derivation outlines the KTU-expected logical chain.

> [!NOTE]
> Although GA is largely a "decision" rather than a "derivation" topic, KTU examiners award marks for **structured logical reasoning**. The sequence below is the KTU-marking-scheme answer template.

**Step 1 — Mission Statement Definition**
The owner issues a "Particulars" or "Specification" sheet specifying:
- Cargo type, quantity, route, speed.
- Number of crew, accommodations, flag.

**Step 2 — Principal Dimensions Estimation**
Using empirical formulas:

$$L_{BP} = k_1 \cdot (DWT)^{1/3}$$

$$B = k_2 \cdot L_{BP}$$

$$D = k_3 \cdot B$$

$$T = k_4 \cdot D$$

Where $k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$ are type-specific coefficients. For a Panamax bulk carrier ($DWT = 75{,}000$ t), typical values are $k_1 \approx 4.5$, $k_2 \approx 0.143$, $k_3 \approx 1.45$, $k_4 \approx 0.62$.

**Step 3 — Block Coefficient Selection**
Use Cargo Density Charts. The cargo stowage density $\rho_c$ (tonnes/m³) and payload $W_p$ define the required hold volume:

$$V_{hold} = \dfrac{W_p}{\rho_c \cdot stowage\ factor}$$

The block coefficient is then chosen to make the displaced volume $\nabla$ large enough to accommodate $V_{hold}$:

$$C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \times B \times T} \approx \dfrac{V_{hold} + V_{tanks} + V_{E/R} + V_{acc}}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Step 4 — Compartmentation (Subdivision)**
Apply SOLAS subdivision index $R \geq 0.5$ (or 1.0 for passenger ships). The number of watertight bulkheads is calculated as:

$$N_{wt} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{BP}}{30\text{ m}} \right\rceil + 1$$

This defines the **transverse bulkhead positions**.

**Step 5 — Engine Room Sizing**
Use the Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC) and Total BHP:

$$L_{ER} = \dfrac{W_{machinery} \times \rho_{steel}^{-1}}{A_{ER,plan}}$$

In practice, a thumb rule is:

$$L_{ER} \approx (0.18 \pm 0.03) \cdot L_{BP}$$

This places the E/R either aft (most modern merchant ships) or amidships (some naval and LNG carriers).

**Step 6 — Accommodation Block Placement**
Crew quarters are placed **above the engine room casing** (for vibration isolation) and **above the freeboard deck** (for habitability and safety). For a 25-crew bulker, the block occupies a 3-deck module $\approx 0.20 \times L_{BP}$ long, $0.85 \times B$ wide, and $3 \times 2.2$ m = 6.6 m high.

**Step 7 — Tank Layout (Bunker, Ballast, Fresh Water, Sludge)**
Tanks are distributed **double-bottom** (mandatory for oil tankers > 5000 DWT, MARPOL) and in **wing/upper wing** voids in bulk carriers. Volumes are calculated from:

$$V_{tank,i} = \dfrac{M_i}{\rho_i}$$

**Step 8 — Cargo Access Definition**
Define hatch size, number, position, and reach of cranes/derricks. Container ships require a cellular guide structure with cell pitch equal to the standard ISO container width (8 ft × 8 ft = 2.438 m).

**Step 9 — Safety Systems Integration**
- Lifeboat stations on each side of the embarkation deck.
- Fire zones: max 40 m separation, 4-deck limit (SOLAS).
- Cofferdams around machinery casings.

**Step 10 — Sheer, Freeboard, and Profile**
Apply the Load Line regulations: the sheer line is parabolic with sheer at FP $\approx 1.5 \times$ standard sheer, at midships $\approx$ standard, and zero at AP.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Factor → GA Decision

| # | Influencing Factor | Primary GA Decision Affected | Typical Constraint Value |
|---|---------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Ship Type | Overall compartmentalisation pattern | Type-specific |
| 2 | DWT / Cargo Volume | Length and depth of cargo block | $V_{cargo} \leq 0.6 \cdot \nabla$ |
| 3 | SOLAS Damage Stability | Number of WT bulkheads | $R \geq 0.5$ |
| 4 | Engine Power / SFOC | Length of engine room | $L_{ER} \approx 0.18 L_{BP}$ |
| 5 | Crew Size | Accommodation block volume | $V_{acc} \geq 7$ m³ per crew |
| 6 | Freeboard (LL) | Position of main deck | $F = f(C_b, L)$ |
| 7 | MARPOL | Double hull, segregated ballast | $h_{DH} \geq (B/15) \text{ or } 2$ m |
| 8 | Fire Safety | Fire zone spacing | $\leq 40$ m |
| 9 | Speed | E/R size, fuel tank volume | $P \propto V^3$ |
| 10 | Range | Bunker capacity | $V_{fuel} \propto R$ |
| 11 | Building Strategy | Block size, panel sizes | $\leq 300$ t per block |
| 12 | Ice Class | Shell thickness, hatch size | Plate $\geq 25$ mm |
| 13 | Owner / Flag | Manning layout, decor | Variable |
| 14 | Port Interface | Manifold height, hatch reach | Shore crane limits |
| 15 | Maintenance | Cofferdams, access trunks | $\geq 600 \times 600$ mm |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** A Handymax bulk carrier has $DWT = 45{,}000$ t, $L_{BP} = 180$ m, $B = 30$ m, $T = 11$ m, $C_b = 0.78$. Determine: (a) the displaced volume, (b) the approximate engine room length, and (c) the minimum number of watertight bulkheads.

**Solution:**

**(a) Displaced volume:**

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$$

Salt water density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³. The displacement $\Delta$ includes lightweight + DWT. For a typical Handymax:

$$\Delta_{light} \approx 0.35 \cdot DWT \approx 15{,}750 \text{ t}$$

$$\Delta_{loaded} = DWT + \Delta_{light} = 45{,}000 + 15{,}750 = 60{,}750 \text{ t}$$

$$\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta_{loaded}}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{60{,}750}{1.025} \approx 59{,}268 \text{ m}^3$$

Cross-check with $C_b$:

$$\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.78 \times 180 \times 30 \times 11 = 46{,}332 \text{ m}^3$$

The mismatch indicates that the **rule-of-thumb lightweight fraction** is conservative. The actual lightweight should be:

$$\Delta_{L} = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho_{sw} - DWT = 46{,}332 \times 1.025 - 45{,}000 \approx 2{,}490 \text{ t}$$

So the actual lightweight is $\approx 2{,}490$ t, which is unrealistically low (real Handymax lightweight is $\approx 11{,}000$ t). This implies $C_b = 0.78$ is too low for the given dimensions — a **discrepancy worth noting in valuation**.

**(b) Engine room length:**

$$L_{ER} = 0.18 \times L_{BP} = 0.18 \times 180 = 32.4 \text{ m}$$

**(c) Number of watertight bulkheads:**

$$N_{wt} = \left\lceil \dfrac{180}{30} \right\rceil + 1 = \lceil 6 \rceil + 1 = 7$$

So the vessel should have **7 watertight bulkheads**, dividing the hull into **6 main compartments**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — GA Design Logic

The following Mermaid block diagram maps how each factor cascades into specific GA design decisions. It is the **highest-yield visual summary** for the KTU module-2 exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Mission and Particulars] --> B[Principal Dimension Estimation]
    B --> C[Block Coefficient and Form Selection]
    C --> D[Compartmentation and Subdivision]
    D --> E[Machinery Space Allocation]
    D --> F[Accommodation Block Layout]
    D --> G[Cargo and Tank Volume Distribution]
    E --> H[Integrated GA Profile and Plan]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Safety System Overlay]
    I --> J[Final General Arrangement]
    J --> K[Classification Society Approval]

    P1[SOLAS Damage Stability] -.-> D
    P2[MARPOL Pollution Rules] -.-> G
    P3[Load Line Convention] -.-> H
    P4[Classification Rules LR DNV ABS] -.-> K
    P5[Owner Particulars and Flag State] -.-> A
    P6[Speed Power and Range] -.-> E
    P7[Port and Terminal Interface] -.-> H
    P8[Building Strategy] -.-> H
    P9[Environmental Sea Area] -.-> H
    P10[Economic Cost Constraints] -.-> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows (A → B → C …)** show the deterministic design sequence.
- **Dotted arrows (P1, P2, …)** show the *parallel regulatory overlays* applied at every stage. The labels are deliberately written as `P1`, `P2`, … (alphanumeric, no special characters) per the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Interaction Between Factor Categories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Functional Factors] --> X[GA Design Process]
    A2[Regulatory Factors] --> X
    A3[Hydrostatic Factors] --> X
    A4[Construction Factors] --> X
    A5[Operational Factors] --> X
    A6[Environmental Factors] --> X
    X --> Y1[Profile Sheer Plan]
    X --> Y2[Deck Plans]
    X --> Y3[Tank Capacity Plan]
    X --> Y4[Cross Section Plan]
    X --> Z[Approved GA Drawing Set]
```

### 4.3 Typical Bulk Carrier GA — Schematic Block Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BowRegion[Bow Region]
        BW[Forepeak Tank]
        CH1[Cargo Hold 1]
        CH2[Cargo Hold 2]
        CH3[Cargo Hold 3]
        CH4[Cargo Hold 4]
        CH5[Cargo Hold 5]
    end
    subgraph MidRegion[Midship Region]
        DB[Double Bottom Tank]
        WT1[Fresh Water Tank]
        WT2[Sludge Tank]
    end
    subgraph SternRegion[Stern Region]
        ER[Engine Room]
        ERB[Engine Room Below]
        BUNK[Bunker Tank]
        APT[Afterpeak Tank]
        ACC[Accommodation Block Above ER]
    end
    BowRegion --> MidRegion
    MidRegion --> SternRegion
    ACC -. Lifeboats .-> ACC
    ER -. Shaft Tunnel .-> APT
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level architecture shows the **bow-to-stern flow** of a typical single-island bulk carrier. The accommodation block is positioned aft of the engine room casing, in line with SOLAS muster deck requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any six major factors that influence the General Arrangement of a merchant ship.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Ship type and trade route** — determines the primary compartmentalisation pattern.
2. **Cargo characteristics** — defines hold size, hatches, and pumping systems.
3. **Statutory regulations** — SOLAS damage stability, MARPOL pollution rules.
4. **Manning scale** — drives the accommodation block volume and layout.
5. **Speed and range** — sizes the engine room and bunker tanks.
6. **Freeboard and Load Line Convention** — sets the position of the main deck and superstructure limits.
*(Any six, properly named, earn full marks. Examiner's note: avoid generic "cost" or "safety" — name the regulation.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the role of SOLAS damage-stability criteria in determining the number of watertight bulkheads.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SOLAS Chapter II-1 requires that a merchant ship with $L_{BP} \geq 100$ m remain afloat with residual positive metacentric height after flooding any one compartment. This criterion, expressed as the **attainment subdivision index $R \geq 0.5$** for cargo ships and $R = 1.0$ for passenger ships, **directly fixes the number of watertight bulkheads**. As a rule of thumb, $N_{wt} \approx L_{BP}/30 + 1$. More stringent damage stability → more bulkheads → smaller compartments → reduced GA freedom.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write *list* answers (1 mark per bullet) and skip the *interrelationship* between factors. The 14-mark question expects a **structured essay** with (a) definition, (b) classification into 3-4 categories with examples, and (c) a concluding statement on design optimisation. Avoid "wall of text" without headings.

---

**Question A — (14 Marks)**
*With the aid of suitable sketches, discuss in detail the various factors that influence the General Arrangement of a ship. How are these factors prioritised when developing a preliminary GA?*

#### Part (a) — Classification of Factors (7 marks)

The factors influencing GA are grouped into **six categories**:

1. **Functional / Mission Factors** — ship type, cargo type, payload volume, speed, range, crew size.
2. **Statutory & Regulatory Factors** — SOLAS damage stability, MARPOL pollution, Load Line Convention, Classification Society rules.
3. **Hydrostatic & Stability Factors** — intact stability (GM), damage stability (residual GM), freeboard, sheer, trim.
4. **Construction & Economic Factors** — block size limits, steel weight, outfit density, build cost.
5. **Operational / Human Factors** — crew ergonomics, maintenance access, port interface, noise/vibration.
6. **Environmental & Geographic Factors** — operating sea area, ice class, climate, flag-state preferences.

Each factor imposes either a *hard* (regulatory) or *soft* (commercial) constraint. For example:
- *Hard:* Watertight subdivision is mandatory → bulkhead positions are fixed first.
- *Soft:* Accommodation decor is owner-driven → can be moved within the block.

#### Part (b) — Prioritisation Strategy (7 marks)

The **prioritisation sequence** in a preliminary GA design is:

1. **Step 1 — Fix the principal dimensions** ($L_{BP}, B, D, T$) from DWT and speed using empirical formulas.
2. **Step 2 — Apply mandatory regulations** first — SOLAS subdivision, MARPOL double hull, Load Line freeboard. These define the *non-negotiable* bulkhead and tank boundaries.
3. **Step 3 — Size the engine room** using BHP and SFOC ($L_{ER} \approx 0.18 L_{BP}$). Place it aft or amidships.
4. **Step 4 — Distribute tank volumes** (bunker, ballast, fresh water, sludge) in double-bottom and wing voids.
5. **Step 5 — Position the cargo block** — central, with hatch openings sized to shore-crane reach.
6. **Step 6 — Place the accommodation block** above the freeboard deck, aft of the E/R casing.
7. **Step 7 — Overlay safety systems** — lifeboat stations, fire zones, escape routes, cofferdams.
8. **Step 8 — Apply owner preferences** — decor, layout, branding, all within the constrained volume.

The output is a **profile plan, deck plans, and tank capacity plan** submitted to the Classification Society for approval.

**Diagrammatic Sketch Required:** A side elevation showing a single-island bulk carrier with bow → cargo holds → engine room → accommodation block → stern, and a midship cross-section with double bottom, hopper tanks, and wing voids. *(3 marks reserved for the sketch in the valuation key.)*

---

**Question B — (14 Marks)**
*Explain the influence of statutory regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line) on the General Arrangement of a tanker. How do these regulations affect compartmentation, freeboard, and tank layout?*

#### Part (a) — SOLAS and Compartmentation (7 marks)

For an oil tanker, SOLAS Chapter II-1 mandates a **probabilistic damage-stability criterion** (Reg. 27-28), with attained subdivision index $R \geq 0.5$. This is calculated as:

$$R = 0.5 \cdot \sum \left( s_i \cdot p_i \right)$$

where $s_i$ is the survival probability and $p_i$ is the probability of flooding in zone $i$.

**Effect on GA:** The tanker is divided into numerous **cargo tank segments** separated by watertight bulkheads. For a VLCC ($L_{BP} \approx 330$ m), this yields typically **6 to 8 wing tanks + 5 to 6 centre tanks** per side, separated longitudinally by OWS-compatible bulkheads.

Additionally, SOLAS Ch. II-1 Reg. 25 requires a **collision bulkhead** at $0.05 L_{BP}$ from FP and an **aft peak bulkhead**.

#### Part (b) — MARPOL and Load Line Influence (7 marks)

**MARPOL Annex I (Oil Pollution):**
- For tankers > 5000 DWT, **double hull** is mandatory (effective from 1996, accelerated by OPA-90 for US trades).
- Double-hull height $h_{DH} \geq B/15$ or 2.0 m, whichever is greater.
- **Segregated ballast tanks (SBT)** of capacity $\geq 30\%$ of DWT must be provided in dedicated spaces that do not carry cargo.
- **Slop tank** of $\geq 3\%$ of cargo capacity for OWS residue.

**Effect on GA:** The double-hull consumes $\sim 10\%$ of the cargo block volume, which must be compensated by a slightly longer or fuller hull. The pump rooms are placed **forward** of the engine room (aft of the cargo block) in modern tankers.

**Load Line Convention (1966):**
- The minimum freeboard $F$ is a function of $L_{BP}$ and $C_b$, tabulated in the LL Regulations.
- For a tanker with $C_b = 0.83$ and $L = 250$ m, the tabular freeboard is approximately 4.0 m.
- The **sheer profile** is parabolic: $1.5 \times$ standard at FP, $1.0 \times$ at midships, $0.5 \times$ at AP.
- The **superstructure** is limited to $\leq 0.4 L_{BP}$ cumulative length without correction.

**Effect on GA:** Freeboard sets the **main deck line** — the upper boundary of cargo tanks. The superstructure (bridge deck) is positioned such that the line of sight from the navigation bridge is unobstructed over the entire deck length, including ahead of any cargo obstruction (SOLAS Reg. V/22). This is a **15–20 m horizontal visibility** requirement that affects bridge wing position.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls (Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing Cb with Cp** in the formula sheet — 1 mark deducted.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in dimensional calculations — 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Writing "SOLAS" without chapter** — examiners expect "SOLAS Ch. II-1" or "MARPOL Annex I" for full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch** in 14-mark questions — 3 marks reserved for a labelled diagram.
> 5. **Mixing up freeboard and sheer** — freeboard is vertical, sheer is the curvature of the deck line.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **GA = General Arrangement**, the *layout drawing set* of a ship (profile + decks + sections + tank plan).
- **Six factor categories:** Functional, Regulatory, Hydrostatic, Construction, Operational, Environmental.
- **Block coefficient** $C_b$ sets hull fullness and indirectly the available internal volume.
- **Prismatic coefficient** $C_p$ governs the run shape and engine room length.
- **SOLAS damage stability** is the **dominant** regulatory factor in compartmentation → $R \geq 0.5$.
- **MARPOL** drives the **double-hull** geometry and **segregated ballast** in tankers.
- **Load Line** sets the **main deck** elevation through the freeboard tables.
- **Engine room** occupies $\approx 0.18 L_{BP}$ and is placed aft in most modern merchant ships.
- **Accommodation block** sits above the freeboard deck, aft of the E/R casing, with muster deck at embarkation level.
- **Watertight bulkhead rule of thumb:** $N_{wt} \approx L_{BP}/30 + 1$.
- **Design sequence** is *Mission → Dimensions → Form → Compartmentation → E/R → Tanks → Cargo → Accommodation → Safety overlay → Approval*.
- **Always cite the regulation source** (SOLAS/MARPOL/LL/Class) — earns valuation credit.
- **Sketch always:** A labelled profile (side elevation) is worth 3 marks in a 14-mark answer.
- **Mnemonic for the 6 factor categories:** **F-R-H-C-O-E** → *Friends Remember Hygiene, Cleanliness Over Everything* (Functional, Regulatory, Hydrostatic, Construction, Operational, Environmental).
- **GA changes after steel cutting are exponentially costly** — therefore, the *factor prioritisation* is performed before any drawing is committed.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subdivision of Ship's Hull
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Subdivision of Ship's Hull

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subdivision of the Ship's Hull** is the deliberate division of the hull into a series of watertight compartments by means of transverse and/or longitudinal watertight bulkheads, intended to limit flooding to a controllable volume following hull damage, thereby preserving buoyancy, stability, residual freeboard, and structural integrity as mandated by **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Chapter II-1** and classification society rules.

The subdivision process is governed by the concept of the **Floodable Length Curve** (also called the Permissible Length Curve), which establishes the maximum length of compartment that may be flooded without sinking the ship below the **Margin Line** — an imaginary line drawn parallel to the **bulkhead deck (or freeboard deck) line** at a vertical distance of **76 mm (3 inches)** below this deck at its lowest point.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey apartment building** with concrete partition walls between rooms. If a fire or burst pipe floods one room, the water does not seep through the walls into other rooms — the entire building remains safe and stable. The ship's hull works on the same principle: by erecting **watertight steel bulkheads**, we create independent "rooms" within the hull. If the outer shell is breached due to collision or grounding, water rushes in only until it reaches the next watertight bulkhead. The rest of the ship remains dry, buoyant, and safe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Box — The Watermelon Principle:** Picture a watermelon sliced vertically into segments. Even if the outer rind cracks in one segment, the seeds and juice don't leak across the slice. That is exactly what bulkhead subdivision does for a ship — confines damage locally.

## Key Physical Constants and Standards

- **Margin Line Offset:** **76 mm (3 inches)** below the bulkhead deck line (Rule 2, SOLAS Chapter II-1).
- **Standard Permeability (μ) values (SOLAS Regulation 27):**
  - Machinery spaces: **μ = 0.85**
  - Accommodations: **μ = 0.95**
  - Dry cargo holds: **μ = 0.70**
  - Empty holds/voids: **μ = 0.95**
  - Tanks intended for liquids: **μ = 0 or 0.95** depending on use
- **Permeability Formula:**
$$\mu = \dfrac{V_{\text{water accommodated}}}{V_{\text{total compartment}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Subdivision is mandatory under SOLAS for passenger ships of 100 GT+ and applicable (with modifications) to cargo ships. The **Factor of Subdivision (F)** is the cornerstone numerical index regulating ship survivability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Objectives of Hull Subdivision

The decision to subdivide a hull is driven by **three overlapping engineering motives**:

1. **Safety / Survivability** — Prevent total loss after collision or grounding.
2. **Damage Control** — Localize flooding; allow continued operation or evacuation.
3. **Structural Strength** — Transverse bulkheads act as ring stiffeners, increasing hull girder rigidity and reducing bending stress.

## Types of Watertight Bulkheads

| Bulkhead Type | Location | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Collision Bulkhead** | Forward, distance ≤ 5% of L (or 10 m, whichever is less) from FP | Prevents flooding from bow damage |
| **Engine Room Bulkhead** | Aft end of machinery space | Protects machinery; fire/flood barrier |
| **After-peak Bulkhead** | Forward end of stern tube tunnel / rudder trunk | Protects shaft tunnel area |
| **Peak Bulkhead (Fwd)** | Just behind collision bulkhead | Forepeak void |
| **Cargo Hold Bulkheads** | Between cargo holds | Compartmentalization of cargo |
| **Watertight Flats** | Horizontal WT floors at e.g., tweendeck level | Dual-purpose: subdivision + strength |
| **Partial Bulkheads** | Partial height WT walls | Local restriction of flooding |

## The Factor of Subdivision (F)

The **Factor of Subdivision** is the single most important quantitative parameter in SOLAS damage stability calculations. It represents the **average probability of survival after flooding**:

$$F = \dfrac{N}{N + 1}$$

where **N = the number of compartments into which the ship is divided**, assuming all compartments are identical and symmetric about amidships (a simplification for conceptual calculation).

### Alternative Definition (SOLAS Reg. 7):

$$F = \dfrac{A_{\text{max}}}{A_{\text{actual}}}$$

where:
- $A_{\text{max}}$ = total floodable length based on required margin line
- $A_{\text{actual}}$ = sum of actual lengths in the subdivision case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Required Criterion:** The *actual* factor of subdivision ($F_\text{req}$) prescribed by SOLAS for a passenger ship depends on its length and number of persons aboard. The *attained* factor ($F_\text{att}$) must be ≥ $F_\text{req}$.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Permeability $\mu = \dfrac{V_w}{V_{\text{comp}}}$ | dimensionless | Volume fraction available for water |
| 2 | Factor of Subdivision $F = \dfrac{N}{N+1}$ | dimensionless | Survivability index (uniform subdivision) |
| 3 | Margin Line = Bulkhead Deck $-$ **76 mm** | mm / inches | Reference for residual freeboard |
| 4 | Permissible Length (graphical method) | metres (m) | Read from Floodable Length Curve at given depth |
| 5 | Floodable Length Curve | Plot of L vs draft | Graphical tool of SOLAS Regulation 26 |
| 6 | Required Freeboard $f = d_{\text{MD}} - d_{\text{damaged}}$ | mm | Residual freeboard above WL after flooding |
| 7 | Permissible Length Rule $l_p$ from $\int dL = \int \mu L\, dL$ | m | Calculus form of SOLAS curve |
| 8 | Volume of Flooding $V_f = \mu \cdot L \cdot B \cdot H$ | m³ | Approximate flooding volume per compartment |
| 9 | Collision Bulkhead Location $\leq 0.05 L_{pp}$ or $10\,\text{m}$ | m | Whichever is lesser (SOLAS) |
| 10 | Probability of Survival $S = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i)$ | — | s-factor for each compartment |

> [!WARNING]
> **Calculus Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** When asked to derive the Floodable Length Curve, students often skip the **integral form**:
> $$\int dx \cdot f(x) = \int \mu \cdot dx \cdot x$$
> and lose **3 marks** immediately.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation I — Factor of Subdivision for a Uniformly Subdivided Ship

### Step 1: Define the Subdivision
Consider a ship of length $L$ divided into $N$ equal compartments of length $\dfrac{L}{N}$ each, symmetric about amidships.

### Step 2: Define Damage Assumption (SOLAS Probabilistic Basis)
For a passenger ship, the probability of damage extending over any single compartment is:

$$p = \dfrac{\ell_{\text{compartment}}}{L_{\text{ship}}} = \dfrac{1}{N}$$

### Step 3: Probability of No Damage in a Given Compartment
$$p_{\text{safe,1}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{N}$$

### Step 4: Probability of Survival Across All $N$ Compartments
Assuming independence (worst-case statistical bound):

$$P_s = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{N}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Limit for Large $N$
Using the well-known limit $\lim_{N \to \infty}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{N} = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$.

### Step 6: Define the Factor of Subdivision
Following SOLAS convention:

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= 1 - P_s \\
&= 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7: Simplify Using Binomial Approximation
For engineering estimation, SOLAS uses the practical approximation:

$$\boxed{\,F \;\approx\; \dfrac{N}{N + 1}\,}$$

### Step 8: Numerical Verification

| N (Compartments) | F = N/(N+1) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.500 | No subdivision |
| 2 | 0.667 | Two compartments |
| 3 | 0.750 | Moderate subdivision |
| 4 | 0.800 | Good subdivision |
| 6 | 0.857 | High survivability |
| ∞ | 1.000 | Theoretically perfect |

## Derivation II — Floodable Length Curve (Permissible Length as a Function of Draft)

The **floodable length** is the maximum length of compartment that may be flooded at a given draft such that the waterline does not rise above the margin line.

### Step 1: Equilibrium Condition After Flooding
A compartment of length $\ell$ floods, with permeability $\mu$. The ship must maintain floatation such that:

$$\text{(Residual buoyancy)} = \text{(Lost buoyancy)}$$

### Step 2: Submerged Volume of Flooded Compartment
$$V_{\text{flooded}} = \mu \cdot \ell \cdot A$$

where $A$ is the midship sectional area (approximated as $B \cdot \bar{H}$ in simplified analyses).

### Step 3: Buoyancy Restored by Draft Increase
When the ship sinks deeper by a depth $dz$, the additional displaced volume is approximately:

$$dV_{\text{extra}} = A_{\text{wl}} \cdot dz$$

where $A_{\text{wl}}$ is the waterplane area at the damaged condition.

### Step 4: Equate and Integrate

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu \cdot \ell \cdot A &= \int_{0}^{\Delta d} A_{\text{wl}}(z)\, dz \\
\Rightarrow \ell_{\text{perm}} &= \dfrac{\int_{0}^{\Delta d} A_{\text{wl}}(z)\, dz}{\mu \cdot A}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Practical Form Using Moment Coefficients
By the **Loss of Buoyancy Method**, this reduces to the SOLAS standard integral:

$$\boxed{\;\int_{0}^{\ell_p} \mu \cdot x \cdot dx \;=\; \int_{0}^{\ell_p} x \cdot dx\;\;\text{(simplified unit)}\;}$$

For a more precise SOLAS-based result, the **permissible length** is read off a chart at any required draft using:

$$\ell_p = \dfrac{A_{\text{below margin}}}{A_{\text{mid}} \cdot \mu}$$

### Step 6: Construction of the Floodable Length Curve
For each draft $d$ (up to bulkhead deck level):

1. Compute the volume of the hull *below* the margin line at that draft: $V(d)$.
2. Assume the compartment is flooded with permeability $\mu$.
3. The required volume of water to sink the ship exactly to the margin line = $V(d) - V_{\text{safety}}$.
4. Divide this volume by $\mu \cdot A_{\text{midship}}$ to obtain the **maximum allowable compartment length** $\ell_p$.
5. Plot $\ell_p$ versus $d$: this is the **Floodable Length Curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why It Matters in Real Ships:** Naval architects compare the **actual bulkhead spacing** against the **floodable length curve** at every draft. If a bulkhead spacing exceeds $\ell_p$ at the deepest operating draft, the subdivision fails SOLAS — the design must be revised.

## Derivation III — Residual Freeboard Criterion

After flooding, residual freeboard $f_r$ above the new waterline is:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_r &= d_{\text{margin}} - d_{\text{damaged}} \\
&= (d_{\text{initial}} + \Delta d) - d_{\text{damaged}}
\end{aligned}$$

For SOLAS compliance: $f_r \geq 0$ (margin line must not be submerged).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block Diagram: Subdivision Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Design Stage] --> B{Type and Length?}
    B -->|Passenger Ship| C[Apply SOLAS Reg 6-9]
    B -->|Cargo Ship| D[Apply SOLAS Reg 25-28]
    C --> E[Calculate N - Persons Aboard]
    E --> F[Determine Required F sub]
    D --> G[Determine Permeability Values]
    F --> H[Generate Floodable Length Curve]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Plot Permissible Length vs Draft]
    I --> J{Bulkhead Spacing OK?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Compliance Achieved]
    J -->|No| L[Add Additional Bulkheads]
    L --> H
    H --> M[Calculate Factor of Subdivision Attained]
    M --> N{F attained F required?}
    N -->|Yes| K
    N -->|No| O[Modify Layout Increase N]
    O --> H
```

## Schematic Diagram: Floodable Length Curve (Block Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Draft Axis - d] --> B[Minimum Draft d1]
    B --> C[Operating Draft d2]
    C --> D[Maximum Draft d3]
    D --> E[Margin Line Reference]
    A2[Floodable Length Axis - lp] --> B2[lp at d1 - Max]
    B2 --> C2[lp at d2 - Mid]
    C2 --> D2[lp at d3 - Min]
    D2 --> E2[lp = 0 at Margin Line]
    A --- A2
    B --- B2
    C --- C2
    D --- D2
    E --- E2
```

## Schematic Diagram: Hull Compartmentalization (Cross-Section Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Ship Hull] --> CB[Collision Bulkhead at 0.05L]
    CB --> H1[Hold 1]
    H1 --> B1[Watertight Bulkhead 1]
    B1 --> H2[Hold 2]
    H2 --> B2[Watertight Bulkhead 2]
    B2 --> ER[Engine Room]
    ER --> EB[Engine Room Bulkhead]
    EB --> AP[After Peak]
    AP --> STB[Stern Tube Bulkhead]
    style CB fill:#ffcc00
    style EB fill:#ffcc00
    style STB fill:#ffcc00
    style B1 fill:#90ee90
    style B2 fill:#90ee90
```

## Block Diagram: Damage Stability Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA[Assume Compartment Flooded] --> StepB[Compute Volume of Flooding]
    StepB --> StepC[Apply Density 1025 kg per m3 for Seawater]
    StepC --> StepD[Calculate New Draft and Trim]
    StepD --> StepE[Compare with Margin Line]
    StepE --> StepF{Margin Line Submerged?}
    StepF -->|Yes| StepG[Subdivision Fails]
    StepF -->|No| StepH[Check Residual Stability GM]
    StepH --> StepI{GM Positive?}
    StepI -->|Yes| StepJ[Compliance Achieved]
    StepI -->|No| StepG
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Margin Line" as used in SOLAS damage stability calculations. State its vertical offset from the bulkhead deck line.**

**Model Answer:**
> The **Margin Line** is an imaginary reference line drawn on the side plating of a ship, used in damage stability assessment. It is located **76 mm (3 inches)** below the **bulkhead deck line at its lowest point**. After flooding of any compartment(s), the residual waterline must not submerge below this margin line; if it does, the subdivision fails SOLAS compliance. *[3 marks]*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the "Factor of Subdivision"? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**
> The **Factor of Subdivision (F)** is a numerical index prescribed by SOLAS that quantifies the survivability of a ship after hull damage. It is defined as:
> $$F = \dfrac{N}{N + 1}$$
> where N is the effective number of watertight compartments. It is important because it must be **attained ≥ required** (as per SOLAS Regulation 6 / 7 depending on passenger/cargo), ensuring that flooding is localized and the ship remains afloat under damage conditions. *[3 marks]*

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure to construct the **Floodable Length Curve** as per SOLAS. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A passenger ship of length $L = 180\,\text{m}$ is divided into **6 equal watertight compartments**. Compute the **Factor of Subdivision (F)** and comment on its adequacy if the required value is **0.75**. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Plot the ship's **profile and body plan**.
**Step 2:** For each operating draft $d_1, d_2, d_3, \ldots, d_n$, draw the corresponding waterline.
**Step 3:** Mark the **Margin Line** — 76 mm below the bulkhead deck line at its lowest point.
**Step 4:** For each draft, the **permissible length** $\ell_p$ is the length of a compartment of permeability $\mu$ that, when flooded, would just submerge the ship to the margin line.
**Step 5:** Mathematically:
$$\ell_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{below margin}}(d) - V_{\text{void}}}{\mu \cdot A_{\text{midship}}}$$
**Step 6:** Plot $\ell_p$ on the y-axis versus $d$ on the x-axis. The result is the Floodable Length Curve.
**Step 7:** Compare actual bulkhead spacing against $\ell_p$ — actual spacing must not exceed $\ell_p$ at any operating draft.

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given: $L = 180\,\text{m}$, $N = 6$, $F_{\text{required}} = 0.75$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{attained}} &= \dfrac{N}{N+1} \\
&= \dfrac{6}{6+1} \\
&= \dfrac{6}{7} \\
&\approx 0.857
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison:**
$$F_{\text{attained}} = 0.857 \;>\; F_{\text{required}} = 0.75 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The subdivision is **adequate and complies with SOLAS**, since $F_\text{att} > F_\text{req}$. The ship has a substantial margin (~0.107) of safety.

### Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **objectives, types, and SOLAS location requirements** of watertight bulkheads in a ship's hull. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A compartment of length 18 m, beam 22 m, and depth 12 m is damaged and flooded with seawater. The compartment is fitted as a dry cargo hold. Find the **volume of water entering** and the **permeability if the residual freeboard after flooding must not exceed margin line (76 mm)**. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Objectives of watertight bulkheads:**

1. Limit flooding after hull breach — *safety*.
2. Provide transverse strength to hull girder — *structural*.
3. Enable progressive flooding during damage control.
4. Separate functional zones (cargo, machinery, accommodation).
5. Reduce sloshing in tanks — *operational*.

**Types and SOLAS Locations:**

| Bulkhead | Location Rule |
|---|---|
| Collision Bulkhead | $\leq 0.05 L_{pp}$ or $10\,\text{m}$ from FP, whichever is smaller |
| After-peak Bulkhead | Watertight, mandatory |
| Engine Room Bulkhead | Watertight, separate from cargo spaces |
| Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead | Required for shaft installations |
| Cargo Hold Bulkheads | Spacing per floodable length curve |
| Bulkheads in Tween Decks | As per load line considerations |

#### Model Solution to (b):

Given: $\ell = 18\,\text{m}$, $B = 22\,\text{m}$, $H = 12\,\text{m}$, **Dry Cargo Hold** → $\mu = 0.70$ (SOLAS standard).

**Volume of compartment:**
$$V_{\text{comp}} = \ell \cdot B \cdot H = 18 \times 22 \times 12 = 4752\,\text{m}^3$$

**Volume of water entering:**
$$V_w = \mu \cdot V_{\text{comp}} = 0.70 \times 4752 = 3326.4\,\text{m}^3$$

**Mass of seawater:**
$$m_w = V_w \cdot \rho_{\text{sw}} = 3326.4 \times 1025 = 3.41 \times 10^{6}\,\text{kg}$$

**Residual freeboard check:** Given the question states the margin line is **76 mm**, and the problem states residual freeboard must not exceed this — if after flooding the ship's draft rises above this, additional subdivision is needed. The numerical residual freeboard check requires the **waterplane area** $A_{wl}$, which is not provided, hence we accept that the calculation of $V_w$ is sufficient to determine the **buoyancy loss**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **multiply by permeability μ** and write the gross volume as the flooded volume. This loses **2 marks instantly**. Always write:
> $$V_{\text{water}} = \mu \cdot V_{\text{compartment}}$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Subdivision** = hull divided into watertight compartments by WT bulkheads.
- ✅ Primary purposes: **Safety, Damage Control, Structural Strength**.
- ✅ **Margin Line** is **76 mm below bulkhead deck line** at its lowest point.
- ✅ **SOLAS Chapter II-1** governs passenger ships; **SOLAS Reg. 25–28** governs cargo ships.
- ✅ **Factor of Subdivision**: $F = \dfrac{N}{N+1}$ (must have $F_\text{att} \geq F_\text{req}$).
- ✅ **Permeability** (μ): cargo hold = 0.70, machinery = 0.85, accommodation = 0.95.
- ✅ **Collision Bulkhead** location: $\leq 0.05 L_{pp}$ or 10 m, whichever is **smaller**.
- ✅ **Floodable Length Curve** — graphical tool; permissible length $\ell_p$ must be ≥ actual bulkhead spacing.
- ✅ **Volume of Flooding**: $V_w = \mu \cdot V_{\text{compartment}}$.
- ✅ Always check residual freeboard ≥ 0 above margin line.
- ✅ Multiple bulkhead types: Collision, Engine Room, After-peak, Cargo Hold, Peak, Partial.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memorization Trick:** **"76 mm + 3 inches = Margin Line"** and **"70, 85, 95 — Cargo, Machinery, Accommodation"** are the two most-forgotten KTU values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Superstructure and Deckhouses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Superstructure and Deckhouses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Superstructure — Formal Definition

In ship design and naval architecture, a **superstructure** is defined as a decked structure located on the **freeboard deck** (or above it) that extends from side to side of the ship. According to the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL) 1966/1988**, a superstructure is:

> "A decked structure on the freeboard deck, extending from side to side of the ship, with proper side scuttles or windows in its sides, and with plating on its freeboard deck exposed weather deck."

A **deckhouse** is a smaller, localized decked structure situated on the freeboard deck or on a superstructure deck, which **does not extend from side to side** of the ship. It is bounded on its open sides by bulwarks or rails and is used to house crew, navigation equipment, machinery, or stores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:**
> The distinction between *full superstructure* and *partial superstructure* (e.g., raised quarterdeck, forecastle) is a frequent **ESE Part A (3 marks)** question, directly tied to the ICLL definitions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **multi-storey house** sitting on top of a single-storey flat-roofed warehouse. The warehouse roof is the **freeboard deck**. Anything built entirely across the width of the warehouse is the **superstructure** (it acts like a second full floor). Anything built only over a portion — say, just the back half — is a **partial superstructure** (like a *raised quarterdeck*). A small cabin or a glass control room perched anywhere on the roof, not spanning the full width, is a **deckhouse** — much like a rooftop watchtower in a fort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember:**
> - **Superstructure** → Spans **side-to-side** (transverse direction), so it is a "**full-width**" structure.
> - **Deckhouse** → A **localized, non-full-width** structure sitting on the freeboard deck or superstructure deck.

## 1.3 Classification of Superstructures (per ICLL 1966)

The Load Line Convention defines three principal types based on longitudinal extent:

| # | Type | Longitudinal Position | Practical Use |
|---|------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Forecastle** | Forward end of the ship | Protects forward hatch, anchor machinery |
| 2 | **Poop** | Aft end of the ship | Houses steering gear, provides reserve buoyancy aft |
| 3 | **Bridge / Mid-ship superstructure** | Centrally located | Houses accommodation, navigation bridge, machinery casing |

These can also appear in **combined** form: *poop + bridge*, *forecastle + bridge + raised quarterdeck*, etc.

## 1.4 Definition of a "Raised Quarterdeck"

A **raised quarterdeck** is a partial superstructure of **minimum standard height** located aft, whose forward bulkhead is located **0.05L abaft the aft perpendicular (AP)** (per ICLL Reg. 36(2)). It provides effective freeboard reduction to a vessel without reducing the cargo volume excessively.

## 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Standard superstructure height (h):** For ships under **100 m in length**, $h = 1.8 \text{ m}$ (for ordinary vessels). Reduced by $0.6\text{ m}$ for type "A" ships beyond 100 m.
- **Effective length of superstructure ($E$):** The portion of superstructure length that actually contributes to the **minimum bow height** and freeboard calculation.
- **Standard height rules** ensure that the superstructure is tall enough to provide adequate reserve buoyancy in heavy weather.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the ICLL, only the **effective length** of a superstructure (calculated using tabular factors based on position and length) counts toward freeboard reduction — not the full geometric length.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Roles of Superstructures and Deckhouses

Superstructures and deckhouses serve **structural, operational, and regulatory** purposes:

1. **Reserve Buoyancy & Buoyancy Compensation:** A full superstructure traps air, contributing additional buoyancy if the ship is flooded partially — a critical safety feature.
2. **Habitability:** Crew accommodation (cabins, mess rooms, galley) is placed in accommodation blocks to keep it **away from machinery noise and heat**.
3. **Navigation:** The **wheelhouse/bridge** is placed at the topmost level for maximum $360°$ visibility.
4. **Weather Protection:** They shield hatches, machinery, and working decks from green seas and spray.
5. **Freeboard Reduction (Regulatory):** Per ICLL, a properly designed full superstructure allows a ship to carry a deeper cargo (more deadweight) for the same hull form.

## 2.2 The Effective Length ($E$) of a Superstructure

The ICLL does not credit the **entire** geometric length of a superstructure. Instead, it uses a **weighted effective length**:

$$E = l_1 \cdot f_1 + l_2 \cdot f_2 + l_3 \cdot f_3$$

Where:
- $l_1, l_2, l_3$ = lengths of the three superstructures (poop, bridge, forecastle respectively), in meters.
- $f_1, f_2, f_3$ = effective length factors (read from ICLL Table, depending on ship length $L$ and the superstructure position).

| Superstructure Position | Effective Length Factor (approx., for $L = 100$ m, type B ship) |
|-------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Poop** $f_1$ | **1.0** (if standard height) |
| **Bridge** $f_2$ | **0.5** (less effective — mid-ships waterlines rise high) |
| **Forecastle** $f_3$ | **1.0** (forward ends are most effective) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the bridge factor lower?** Because when a ship pitches (bows up/down), the amidships region of the sheer curve doesn't rise as much. Only a fraction of bridge length contributes to raising the **minimum bow height** — hence $f_2 < 1$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|----------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = l_1 f_1 + l_2 f_2 + l_3 f_3$ | Effective length of superstructure (ICLL) | m |
| 2 | $h_{std} = 1.8$ (for $L < 100$ m) | Standard superstructure height (ordinary) | m |
| 3 | $h_{std} = 1.2$ (for $L \ge 100$ m, Type A) | Standard height for type A ships, $L \ge 100$ m | m |
| 4 | Reduction in freeboard $\Delta F = R_f \cdot E$ | $R_f$ = reduction factor (0.34 for type B at $L=100$m) | m |
| 5 | Minimum bow height $H_b = F_b + R + 0.027 L$ | Freeboard + sheer correction + length term | m |
| 6 | Freeboard reduction (single superstructure) $\Delta f = C_s \cdot h$ | $C_s$ = superstructure coefficient | m |
| 7 | Forward bulkhead of raised QD ≥ $0.05 L$ from AP | ICLL Reg. 36(2) regulation | m |
| 8 | Door height / sill height $\ge 380$ mm above deck | Watertight integrity of superstructure doors | mm |
| 9 | Scuttle lower edge $\ge 2.5\%$ of breadth above freeboard deck | Side scuttle height rule | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical exam point:** The forward bulkhead of a **raised quarterdeck** must be placed at **$0.05L$** abaft the after perpendicular. If the forward bulkhead is *forward* of this, it doesn't qualify as a "raised QD" under the ICLL and gets only a smaller freeboard credit.

## 2.4 Structural Arrangement of Deckhouses

A typical merchant ship deckhouse is built up of:

1. **Deckhouse bulkheads (side and end):** Stiffened plated structures; end bulkheads are normally watertight (WT) up to freeboard deck.
2. **Deckhouse deck (top plate / wheelhouse top):** Highly stressed; stiffened with longitudinals/girders.
3. **Tier structure:** Multi-deck deckhouses (3–5 tiers) require vertical pillars and transverse web frames.
4. **Openings:** Doors, windows, vents — classified as **weathertight (WT)** or **watertight (W)** depending on location.
5. **Scuttles & Deadlights:** Round/rectangular side openings in the superstructure.

## 2.5 Practical Engineering Utility

- A well-designed **superstructure layout** affects: hull strength, vibration characteristics, and cargo handling efficiency.
- A poorly designed deckhouse causes **slamming** (green seas over the bow), excessive **windage** (lateral wind area in berthing), and impairs **visibility from the bridge**.
- The **bridge front windows** must provide a clear view forward within a $240°$ arc — IMO Resolution A.601(15).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Length of Superstructure ($E$)

We start from the ICLL principle that a superstructure only contributes to the **minimum bow height** to the extent of its **effective length** — not its physical length.

### Step 1 — Define the components

A vessel may have up to **three** superstructures contributing to freeboard:

$$E = \text{(Poop contribution)} + \text{(Bridge contribution)} + \text{(Forecastle contribution)}$$

### Step 2 — Express each contribution as length × factor

Let $l_1$ = length of poop, $l_2$ = length of bridge, $l_3$ = length of forecastle (in m).

Each has a factor depending on its longitudinal position along the ship.

### Step 3 — Combine the contributions

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= l_1 \cdot f_1 \;+\; l_2 \cdot f_2 \;+\; l_3 \cdot f_3
\end{aligned}
$$

The values of $f_1, f_2, f_3$ are tabulated in **ICLL 1966 Annex II (Reg. 36, Table)** as a function of ship length $L$ (in m).

> The factors account for the **relative vertical motion** of the waterline in pitch — a point near amidships moves up and down *less* than the bow.

### Step 4 — Freeboard Reduction

The freeboard reduction granted by the superstructure is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta F &= R_f \cdot E
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R_f$ is the **freeboard reduction coefficient** (tabulated for each type and length of ship).

> **Example:** For a Type B ship, $L = 100$ m, $R_f = 0.34$. If $E = 30$ m, then $\Delta F = 0.34 \times 30 = 10.2$ mm? No — units are metres:
> $$\Delta F = 0.34 \times 30 = 10.2 \text{ mm} \rightarrow 0.0102 \text{ m}$$
> *(Wait — correction: $R_f$ is dimensionless and $E$ is in m, so $\Delta F$ is in m.)*

**Re-evaluation:**

$$
\Delta F = 0.34 \times 30 \text{ m} = 10.2 \text{ m}
$$

That is clearly wrong physically. The correct interpretation: $R_f$ in ICLL tables is actually applied to freeboard in a more nuanced way (the reduction is **not** simply $R_f \times E$); it is a tabular deduction.

### Step 3 (Corrected) — ICLL Rule 38

The actual reduction in tabular freeboard is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction} &= (C_s \cdot h_1) + (C_s \cdot h_2) + (C_s \cdot h_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C_s$ is the superstructure coefficient and $h_i$ are heights above the freeboard deck. The *full* reduction is capped at a maximum of:

$$
\Delta F_{max} = 0.34 \text{ m} \quad (\text{for } L = 100 \text{ m, Type B})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ICLL uses a **tabular cap** on superstructure credit, not a simple linear formula. The derivation above gives a *qualitative* understanding; **the cap values must be referenced from the ICLL tables** in any numerical problem.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Effective Length Calculation

**Problem:** A Type B vessel of $L = 120$ m has:
- Poop length $l_1 = 20$ m, $f_1 = 1.0$
- Bridge length $l_2 = 35$ m, $f_2 = 0.5$
- Forecastle length $l_3 = 15$ m, $f_3 = 1.0$

**Find the effective length $E$.**

### Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= l_1 f_1 + l_2 f_2 + l_3 f_3 \\
&= (20 \times 1.0) + (35 \times 0.5) + (15 \times 1.0) \\
&= 20 + 17.5 + 15 \\
&= 52.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Although the total geometric superstructure length is $20 + 35 + 15 = 70$ m, only $52.5$ m is "effective" in raising the minimum bow height.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Superstructure vs. Deckhouse Comparison

| Parameter | Superstructure | Deckhouse |
|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| Lateral extent | **Full breadth** (side-to-side) | **Partial** (less than full breadth) |
| ICLL credit for freeboard | **Yes** (subject to limits) | **No** |
| Required plating (per Reg. 11) | **Strength deck plating** standards | Lighter scantlings allowed |
| Side scuttles permitted | Yes, below a certain height | Yes, but stricter limits |
| Typical location | Aft (poop), mid (bridge), fwd (forecastle) | Anywhere on freeboard deck |
| Primary use | Habitability, navigation, machinery casing | Smaller utility spaces |
| Forward bulkhead (raised QD) | $\ge 0.05L$ from AP | N/A |
| Height | Standard superstructure height $h$ | Any practical height |

## 3.4 Derivation: Standard Superstructure Height ($h$)

The ICLL defines standard heights for ordinary and Type A ships:

For $L \le 100$ m (Ordinary ships):

$$
h_{std} = 1.8 \text{ m}
$$

For $L \le 100$ m (Type A ships):

$$
h_{std} = 1.8 - 0.6 = 1.2 \text{ m}
$$

For $L > 100$ m, the standard height reduces linearly with length. For $L = 200$ m:

$$
h_{std} = 1.8 - 0.005 \times (L - 100) = 1.8 - 0.5 = 1.3 \text{ m (Ordinary)}
$$

The derivation is based on equating the **minimum bow height requirement** for the vessel with the **vertical clearance** provided by the superstructure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Superstructure Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FS[Freeboard Deck Level]
        A1[Forecastle: Anchor + Mooring]
        A2[Bridge: Navigation + Accommodation]
        A3[Poop: Steering Gear + Reserve Buoyancy]
    end

    subgraph DS1[Deckhouse Level 1 - Boat Deck]
        B1[Lifeboat Stations]
        B2[Compass Platform]
        B3[Ventilation Uptakes]
    end

    subgraph DS2[Deckhouse Level 2 - Bridge Deck]
        C1[Wheelhouse - Bridge]
        C2[Chart Room]
        C3[Captain's Cabin]
    end

    subgraph DS3[Deckhouse Level 3 - Top]
        D1[Radars and Antenna Masts]
        D2[Signal Lights]
        D3[Exhaust Stacks]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> B3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — ICLL Superstructure Classification

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Superstructure - ICLL Definition] --> B[Full Superstructure]
    A --> C[Partial Superstructure]
    B --> B1[Bridge - Midships]
    B --> B2[Forecastle - Forward]
    B --> B3[Poop - Aft]
    C --> C1[Raised Quarterdeck]
    C --> C2[Open Superstructure]
    A --> D[Deckhouse - Localized]
    D --> D1[Accommodation Block]
    D --> D2[Funnel Casing]
    D --> D3[Machinery Casing]
```

## 4.3 Profile Schematic — Ship Cross-Section Indicating Superstructure Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Z1[Top - Boat Deck]
        Z1A[Boats and Davits]
    end
    subgraph Z2[A Deck - Bridge Deck]
        Z2A[Wheelhouse / Bridge]
        Z2B[Chart Room]
    end
    subgraph Z3[B Deck - Accommodation]
        Z3A[Crew Cabins]
        Z3B[Galley and Mess]
    end
    subgraph Z4[C Deck - Freeboard Deck]
        Z4A[Cargo Hatches]
        Z4B[Deck Machinery]
    end
    subgraph Z5[Lower - Hold and Tank Top]
        Z5A[Cargo Holds]
    end

    Z1A --> Z2A
    Z2A --> Z3A
    Z3A --> Z4A
    Z4A --> Z5A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above represents a typical **general cargo / container ship profile**. The "C Deck" is normally the **freeboard deck**, and any full-width structure on or above it qualifies as a superstructure under the ICLL.

## 4.4 Flow Diagram — Design Considerations for Deckhouses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Identify Functional Requirements] --> P2[Step 2: Choose Location on Freeboard Deck]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Apply ICLL Reg. 36 - Scantlings and Height]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Design Side Scuttles and Doors]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Apply ICLL Reg. 11 - Plating Standards]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Structural Analysis - Bending and Shear]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Stability Check - Wind Heel and GM]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Approval and Classification]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a superstructure as per the ICLL. Differentiate between a full superstructure and a partial superstructure with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **superstructure**, as per the International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL) 1966, is a decked structure on the freeboard deck extending from **side to side** of the ship, with proper side scuttles or windows in its sides, and with plating on the freeboard deck that is an exposed weather deck.

- A **full superstructure** spans the full breadth *and* a substantial longitudinal length, such as a **central bridge** running amidships from approximately $0.15L$ to $0.55L$.
- A **partial superstructure** is shorter in longitudinal extent, e.g., a **poop** (aft, ~ $0.15L$ long) or a **raised quarterdeck**.

> The key difference lies in the *longitudinal extent* and the *amount of freeboard credit* allowed.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**What is a "raised quarterdeck"? State the ICLL regulation governing its position.**

**Model Answer:**

A **raised quarterdeck** is a partial superstructure of minimum standard height located aft, with its **forward bulkhead** positioned **at least $0.05L$** abaft the after perpendicular (AP).

**ICLL Regulation:** **Reg. 36(2)** governs the position and design of the raised quarterdeck. The minimum height, side plating, and bulkhead strength are all specified. The raised QD allows a vessel to gain partial freeboard credit for a shorter aft superstructure, helping it achieve a more useful deadweight capacity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Set 1]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **classification of superstructures** under the ICLL. Mention at least **three types** and their relative positions on the vessel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Type B vessel of $L = 120$ m has a poop of length $20$ m, a bridge of length $35$ m, and a forecastle of length $18$ m. Using ICLL factors $f_1 = 1.0$, $f_2 = 0.5$, $f_3 = 1.0$, compute the **effective length of the superstructure** and discuss the impact on freeboard reduction. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The ICLL classifies superstructures into three principal types based on longitudinal location:

1. **Forecastle** — Located at the **forward** end, typically $0.05L$ to $0.15L$ from the forward perpendicular (FP). Houses anchor winches, mooring equipment, and chain lockers.

2. **Bridge (Central) Superstructure** — Located **amidships**, generally $0.15L$ to $0.55L$ from FP. Provides accommodation and navigation spaces. Effective length factor is **lowest** ($f_2 \approx 0.5$).

3. **Poop** — Located at the **aft** end, from approximately $0.85L$ to $0.95L$ from FP. Houses steering gear, navigation lights, and acts as a reserve buoyancy chamber aft.

4. **Raised Quarterdeck** — A special *partial* superstructure at the aft, with forward bulkhead $\ge 0.05L$ from AP.

5. **Combined Superstructure** — Two or more of the above combined (e.g., forecastle + bridge + poop).
