A labelled sketch of the ship profile would show the three regions, with the freeboard deck as the reference line and the superstructure positioned above it.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 120$ m
- Poop length $l_1 = 20$ m, $f_1 = 1.0$
- Bridge length $l_2 = 35$ m, $f_2 = 0.5$
- Forecastle length $l_3 = 18$ m, $f_3 = 1.0$

**Step 1 — Effective Length Formula:**

$$
E = l_1 f_1 + l_2 f_2 + l_3 f_3
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
E = (20 \times 1.0) + (35 \times 0.5) + (18 \times 1.0)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
E = 20 + 17.5 + 18 = 55.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Discussion:**

- The total geometric length of superstructure is $20 + 35 + 18 = 73$ m.
- However, only $55.5$ m is **effective** in raising the minimum bow height.
- The bridge, being midships, contributes only half its geometric length to effective length — this is because amidships waterlines move vertically less than the bow in a seaway.
- This effective length is then used (along with ICLL tabular caps) to determine the **reduction in tabular freeboard** that the ship can claim.

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{E = 55.5 \text{ m}}$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2 Set 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **structural arrangement of a typical deckhouse** in a merchant ship. Mention the role of side bulkheads, end bulkheads, and the deckhouse top. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State the ICLL requirements for **side scuttles, doors, and windows** in a superstructure. Mention the sill height and weathertightness requirements. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

A deckhouse is constructed as a **stiffened plated assembly**, comprising:

1. **Side Bulkheads (Shell Plating of Deckhouse):**
   - Form the outer skin of the deckhouse.
   - Plating thickness governed by ICLL Reg. 11 and classification society rules (IRS, DNV, LR, etc.).
   - Stiffened by **longitudinals** (T-bars, flat bars) supported by **transverse web frames** at every 2–3 frame spaces.
   - Subjected to **local bending** due to wind, sea pressure, and cargo load transfer.

2. **End Bulkheads (Transverse Bulkheads):**
   - Close the deckhouse fore and aft.
   - Must be **watertight** up to the freeboard deck if it forms part of the watertight subdivision.
   - Carry shear and bending loads transferred from the deckhouse sides to the main hull.

3. **Deckhouse Top (Upper Deck):**
   - Often the **strength deck** of the ship (when deckhouse is full-width).
   - Subjected to **largest longitudinal bending stresses** (hogging/sagging).
   - Stiffened with **longitudinals** and transversely by **girders** (heavy transverse webs).

4. **Intermediate Decks (in multi-tier deckhouses):**
   - Connected by **pillars** and **transverse web frames**.
   - Provide lateral strength and reduce buckling.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**ICLL Reg. 23 and Reg. 12** govern openings in superstructures and deckhouses:

1. **Side Scuttles (Windows):**
   - Must be of **weathertight** construction.
   - Lower edge must be located at a height $\ge 2.5\%$ of the breadth $B$ of the ship above the freeboard deck (but minimum $h_{min}$ varies).
   - For Type B ships, the lower edge of side scuttles in a poop or forecastle is set at a height of $\ge 1.4$ m above the freeboard deck.
   - Fitted with **deadlights** (inboard covers) that can be closed watertight.

2. **Doors in Superstructure:**
   - All access doors in superstructure ends (forward end of poop, after end of forecastle, etc.) must be **permanently attached** to the bulkhead and **weathertight**.
   - Sill height $\ge 380$ mm (i.e., $380$ mm) above the freeboard deck (ICLL Reg. 12).
   - Sills of doors in raised quarterdecks must be $\ge 600$ mm above the freeboard deck.

3. **Windows:**
   - Must have **inboard deadlights** (storm covers).
   - Lower edge must be above a line drawn parallel to the freeboard deck at a height $= v/2$, where $v$ is the sheer.

> **Key takeaway:** ICLL applies the principle of **progressive flooding containment** — openings at lower levels are subject to stricter rules, ensuring the ship remains intact even with the lower tier of scuttles broken.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing superstructure and deckhouse** — Remember, the difference is *lateral* (full-width vs. partial).
> 2. **Wrong ICLL Regulation citation** — Reg. 36 (superstructures), Reg. 12 (doors/openings), Reg. 23 (scuttles). Don't mix them up.
> 3. **Treating effective length as geometric length** — Always multiply by factors $f_1, f_2, f_3$ (this is a top cause of full-mark loss in Part B).
> 4. **Forgetting units** — Heights are in **metres**, lengths in **metres**, sill heights in **mm**. Mixing units is a common 1–2 mark loss.
> 5. **Missing the forward bulkhead rule** for raised quarterdeck ($\ge 0.05L$ from AP) — this is a favourite Part A question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Superstructure and Deckhouses:**

- A **superstructure** is a **full-width, decked** structure on the freeboard deck (ICLL Reg. 36).
- A **deckhouse** is a **localized, partial** structure — it does **not** span side-to-side.
- Three principal types: **Forecastle** (forward), **Bridge** (amidships), **Poop** (aft).
- A **raised quarterdeck** is a partial superstructure with forward bulkhead $\ge 0.05L$ from AP.
- **Effective length** $E = l_1 f_1 + l_2 f_2 + l_3 f_3$ — not the geometric length.
- Bridge superstructure factor $f_2 \approx 0.5$ (lower effectiveness at midships).
- Poop and forecastle factors $f_1, f_3 \approx 1.0$ (higher effectiveness at ends).
- **Standard superstructure height:** $h_{std} = 1.8$ m (ordinary, $L < 100$ m); $h_{std} = 1.2$ m (Type A, $L \ge 100$ m).
- Side scuttle lower edge: $\ge 2.5\% \cdot B$ above freeboard deck; for poop/forecastle Type B $\ge 1.4$ m.
- Door sill height: $\ge 380$ mm above freeboard deck (raised QD: $\ge 600$ mm).
- Side scuttles and doors must be **weathertight** with **inboard deadlights** for storm conditions.
- Superstructure gives **freeboard reduction** — increasing **deadweight capacity** (a key commercial benefit).
- Deckhouse **does not** give freeboard credit, but it still affects stability, windage, and visibility.
- IMO Resolution A.601(15): Bridge front windows must allow $240°$ clear forward view.
- Side bulkheads of deckhouse: stiffened with longitudinals and transverse webs; plating thickness per ICLL Reg. 11.
- End bulkheads of superstructure: must be watertight up to the freeboard deck.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrangement of Cargo Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arrangement of Cargo Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cargo Space Arrangement** is the systematic planning, longitudinal/transverse subdivision, and geometric layout of holds, tanks, 'tween decks, and auxiliary compartments within a ship's hull envelope to optimize **stowage efficiency, structural integrity, loading/discharging speed, stability, and seaworthiness**, while complying with statutory subdivision rules (SOLAS, MARPOL, ICLL).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the cargo space arrangement is a **design synthesis** output that integrates:
- **Hull form geometry** (L, B, T, Cb)
- **Cargo type & density** (liquid, dry bulk, unitized, refrigerated)
- **Operational profile** (port turn-around, gear, route)
- **Statutory regulations** (IACS, Class, Load Line)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a cargo ship as a floating multi-storey warehouse with a watertight skin.** The *arrangement* is the architectural blueprint of that warehouse — where the elevators (hatchways) go, how the floors (decks) are stacked, which rooms (holds) hold what goods, and where the fire-doors (bulkheads) sit.

A simple analogy:
- **Cargo Hold = Apartment** → has fixed volume, access door, and a use-case (bedroom = heavy bulk, kitchen = reefer)
- **Hatch = Elevator Shaft** → vertical access for moving goods in/out
- **Bulkhead = Wall** → structural + safety division
- **Double Bottom = Basement** → structural safety + slop/ballast storage
- **Side Tank = Service Shaft** → protects against sloshing + structural redundancy

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Stowage Factor (SF)** = **Volume per unit mass of cargo**, expressed in **m³/tonne**.
- **Cargo Density (ρ)** = **Mass per unit volume**, expressed in **tonnes/m³**.
- **Permeability (μ)** = **Ratio of cargo volume to hold volume** (always ≤ 1).
- **Block Coefficient (Cb)** = **∇ / (L × B × T)** — measure of hull fullness.
- **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** = Total variable load the ship can carry.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memorize:** A ship is "volume-limited" when SF is large (light cargo like cotton), and "weight-limited" when SF is small (heavy cargo like ore). Designers must check **both** limits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Drivers Influencing Cargo Space Arrangement

The arrangement is governed by a hierarchy of constraints:

### A. Commercial / Operational Drivers
1. **Type of cargo carried** — homogeneous vs. heterogeneous.
2. **Stowage factor spectrum** — requires both volume AND weight balance.
3. **Loading & discharging rate** (tonnes/hour) — dictates hatch size, gear type, hold shape.
4. **Port turn-around time** — favors large hatches & self-trimming holds.
5. **Voyage pattern** — coastal vs. ocean, frequency of port calls.

### B. Statutory & Class Drivers
1. **SOLAS subdivision** — floodable length, factor of subdivision.
2. **MARPOL Annex I** — double hull, segregated ballast for tankers.
3. **ILLC 1966** — freeboard based on sheer, length, block coefficient.
4. **IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR)** — hull girder strength → hold length limits.

### C. Structural Drivers
1. **Hull girder bending moment** → limits unsupported hold length.
2. **Shear forces & local loading** → bulkhead spacing.
3. **Double bottom height** — minimum for access & tank volume.
4. **Web frame / stringer spacing** in cargo hold region.

### D. Stability & Safety Drivers
1. **Grain stability** — surface shift angles & trimming moments.
2. **Free surface effect** in partially filled tanks.
3. **Damage stability** — probabilistic or deterministic subdivision index.
4. **Lashing requirements** for deck cargo / containers.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $SF = \dfrac{V_{cargo}}{W_{cargo}}$ | Stowage Factor | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{tonne}$ | Cargo volume planning |
| 2 | $\rho_{cargo} = \dfrac{1}{SF}$ | Cargo Density | $\text{tonne}/\text{m}^{3}$ | Heavy vs. light cargo |
| 3 | $V_{hold} = L_{hold} \cdot B \cdot D_{cargo}$ | Hold geometric volume | $\text{m}^{3}$ | Available cargo volume |
| 4 | $V_{cargo,eff} = \mu \cdot V_{hold}$ | Effective cargo volume | $\text{m}^{3}$ | Stowage loss due to shape |
| 5 | $W_{cargo} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot V_{hold}}{SF}$ | Mass of cargo stowed | $\text{tonnes}$ | Deadweight allocation |
| 6 | $DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - LW$ | Deadweight = Disp. - Lightweight | $\text{tonnes}$ | Total payload capacity |
| 7 | $C_{b} = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | — | Hull fullness / volume |
| 8 | $N_{holds} \approx \dfrac{L_{pp}}{25 \text{ to } 30}$ | Empirical hold count | — | General cargo guideline |
| 9 | $BM_{FS} = \dfrac{I_{T}}{\nabla \cdot (1 - \mu_{FS})}$ | Free surface correction | $\text{m}$ | Tank filling penalty |
| 10 | $h_{DB,min} = B/20$ or class rule | Minimum double bottom | $\text{m}$ | Structural / regulatory |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol "$\vert$" is replaced with the inline notation (e.g., $\nabla$, $I_T$) to preserve KTU markdown table compatibility. Where absolute value bars are required elsewhere in derivations, **$\vert x \vert$ is written as $\lvert x \rvert$**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The arrangement dictates:
- **Ship's earning capacity** — every cubic metre wasted is lost revenue.
- **Hull insurance premium** — better subdivision = lower risk = lower P&I.
- **Port productivity** — holds tuned to crane outreach & hatch cover size.
- **Environmental compliance** — double-hull tankers are mandatory post-MARPOL.
- **Crew safety** — proper segregation, access, escape routes (SOLAS Reg. II-2).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state **three** factors when asked "what influences cargo space arrangement?" — pick from: (1) cargo type, (2) statutory rules, (3) structural strength, (4) stability, (5) operational speed of cargo handling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stowage Factor and Cargo Density Reciprocity

**Given:** A bag of rice occupies $V = 1.8 \text{ m}^{3}$ and weighs $W = 1.2 \text{ tonnes}$.

**Find:** Stowage factor and density.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SF &= \frac{V}{W} \\
&= \frac{1.8 \text{ m}^{3}}{1.2 \text{ tonne}} \\
SF &= 1.5 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Volume (numerator) divided by mass (denominator) yields the space required per unit mass — the canonical stowage factor definition.

Density is the reciprocal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{W}{V} = \frac{1}{SF} = \frac{1}{1.5} \\
\rho &\approx 0.667 \text{ tonne}/\text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Reciprocity is dimensional, since SF has units $\text{m}^{3}/\text{tonne}$ and density has units $\text{tonne}/\text{m}^{3}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Holds (Empirical + Rational)

**Step 1 — Empirical starting point.** A general cargo ship's rule-of-thumb:

$$
N_{holds} \approx \frac{L_{pp}}{k}, \quad k \in [25,\, 30]\text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Rational bound from hull girder strength.** Maximum permissible hold length is set by bending moment allowable on a hatchless or single-island mid-body:

$$
L_{hold,max} \approx \frac{\sigma_{allow} \cdot Z}{M_{sw} / L_{pp}}
$$

where $Z$ is the section modulus, $\sigma_{allow}$ the allowable stress, and $M_{sw}$ the still-water bending moment.

**Step 3 — Number of holds required:**

$$
N_{holds} \geq \left\lceil \frac{L_{pp}}{L_{hold,max}} \right\rceil
$$

**Conversion logic:** The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ returns the next integer — you cannot have 2.3 holds, so 3 holds must be built.

### Worked Example
For $L_{pp} = 150 \text{ m}$:

$$
N_{holds} = \left\lceil \frac{150}{28} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.36 \rceil = 6 \text{ holds}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hold Volume & Effective Cargo Capacity

**Given:** Hold of length $L_{hold} = 24 \text{ m}$, ship breadth $B = 32 \text{ m}$, cargo height $D_{cargo} = 12 \text{ m}$, permeability $\mu = 0.85$.

**Find:** Hold geometric volume, effective cargo volume, and stowed mass for cargo with $SF = 1.4 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{tonne}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{hold} &= L_{hold} \cdot B \cdot D_{cargo} \\
&= 24 \cdot 32 \cdot 12 \\
V_{hold} &= 9216 \text{ m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Rectangular volume formula, treating hold as an ideal prism.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cargo,eff} &= \mu \cdot V_{hold} \\
&= 0.85 \cdot 9216 \\
V_{cargo,eff} &= 7833.6 \text{ m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Permeability < 1 because of hopper tanks, bilges, frames, and stowage losses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cargo} &= \frac{V_{cargo,eff}}{SF} \\
&= \frac{7833.6}{1.4} \\
W_{cargo} &= 5595.4 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Inverse of stowage factor — divides effective volume by the volume-per-tonne to recover mass.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Block Coefficient and Displaced Volume

**Given:** $L = 180 \text{ m}$, $B = 32 \text{ m}$, $T = 12.5 \text{ m}$, $C_b = 0.78$.

**Find:** Displaced volume $\nabla$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_b \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\
&= 0.78 \cdot 180 \cdot 32 \cdot 12.5 \\
\nabla &= 56160 \text{ m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Block coefficient is the volumetric fullness ratio — multiplying by the bounding rectangular prism gives the actual displaced volume.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cargo Space Arrangement by Ship Type

| Ship Type | Holds / Tanks | Decking | Subdivision | Special Features |
|-----------|---------------|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| **General Cargo** | 4–6 holds | 1–2 decks | Transverse bulkheads | 'Tween decks, hatch coamings |
| **Bulk Carrier** | 5–9 holds | Single deck | Transverse + hopper | Side tanks, double bottom, self-trimming |
| **Oil Tanker** | 5–12 tanks | None (deck) | Longitudinal + transverse | Double hull, COW, IGS, slop tank |
| **Container Ship** | Cellular holds | Hatch covers | Cellular guides | Twist-locks, lashing bridges |
| **Reefer** | Insulated holds | Multiple decks | Air-tight | Cooling coils, ducting |
| **LNG Carrier** | 4–5 tanks | Independent | Membrane / Moss / SPB | Secondary barrier, reliquefaction |
| **Ro-Ro** | Single ramp deck | Multiple decks | Movable bulkheads | Internal ramps, liftable decks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Block Coefficient (Cb)** with **Prismatic Coefficient (Cp)**. $C_b$ uses $L \cdot B \cdot T$, while $C_p$ uses $A_m \cdot L$ (midship area × length).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cargo Spaces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Hull Envelope] --> B[Hull Form Geometry]
    A --> C[Structural Framework]
    A --> D[Compartmentalization System]

    B --> B1[Length Lpp]
    B --> B2[Breadth B]
    B --> B3[Depth D]
    B --> B4[Draught T]

    C --> C1[Double Bottom]
    C --> C2[Side Shell]
    C --> C3[Decks]
    C --> C4[Bulkheads]

    D --> D1[Cargo Spaces]
    D --> D2[Auxiliary Spaces]
    D --> D3[Machinery Spaces]

    D1 --> E1[Dry Cargo Holds]
    D1 --> E2[Tanks Liquid Cargo]
    D1 --> E3[Container Cells]
    D1 --> E4[Reefer Compartments]

    E1 --> F1[Hopper Tanks]
    E1 --> F2[Upper 'Tween Deck]
    E1 --> F3[Lower Hold]

    E2 --> G1[Centerline Tanks]
    E2 --> G2[Side Wing Tanks]
    E2 --> G3[Slop Tanks]

    E3 --> H1[Cell Guides]
    E3 --> H2[Stack Rails]
    E3 --> H3[Twist Lock Pockets]

    D2 --> I1[Bunkers]
    D2 --> I2[Ballast Tanks]
    D2 --> I3[Fresh Water]
    D2 --> I4[Stores]

    D3 --> J1[Engine Room]
    D3 --> J2[Pump Room]
    D3 --> J3[Boiler Room]
```

## 4.2 Cargo Space Arrangement Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Cargo Type] --> K1[Dry Bulk]
    K --> K2[Liquid Bulk]
    K --> K3[Unitized Container]
    K --> K4[General Mixed]

    K1 --> L1[Single Deck]
    K1 --> L2[Hopper Side Tanks]
    K1 --> L3[5 to 9 Holds]

    K2 --> M1[Double Hull Mandated]
    K2 --> M2[Longitudinal Subdivision]
    K2 --> M3[Pumping System]

    K3 --> N1[Cellular Guides]
    K3 --> N2[Hatch Cover Strength]
    K3 --> N3[Lashing System]

    K4 --> O1[Multi-Deck 'Tween]
    K4 --> O2[Transverse Bulkheads]
    K4 --> O3[Variable Permeability]
```

## 4.3 Transverse Section Schematic of a Bulk Carrier Hold

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Deck] --> P2[Upper Hold]
    P2 --> P3[Cargo Space]
    P3 --> P4[Hopper Tank]
    P4 --> P5[Double Bottom Tank]

    P3 -.-> P6[Side Frame]
    P3 -.-> P7[Transverse Bulkhead]

    P4 --> P8[Sloped Hopper 45 deg]
    P5 --> P9[Floor Plate]
    P5 --> P10[Inner Bottom]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Top-down the bulk carrier's cross-section reveals the layered arrangement — upper hold for cargo, sloped hopper tank below for self-trimming grain/ore, and double bottom tank for ballast or fuel. The side frames and transverse bulkheads provide structural continuity.

## 4.4 Tanker's Longitudinal Subdivision Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Cargo Tank 1 P] --> Q2[Cargo Tank 2 P]
    Q2 --> Q3[Cargo Tank 3 P]
    Q3 --> Q4[Slop Tank]
    Q4 --> Q5[Cargo Tank 4 S]
    Q5 --> Q6[Cargo Tank 5 S]

    Q1 -.-> R1[Port Side Double Hull]
    Q5 -.-> R2[Starboard Side Double Hull]

    Q2 -.-> R3[Centerline Bulkhead]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Define **stowage factor**. Why is it a critical parameter in arranging cargo spaces?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Stowage factor (SF) is the volume occupied by one tonne of cargo, expressed in **m³/tonne**.
$$ SF = \frac{V_{cargo}}{W_{cargo}} $$
It is critical because it determines whether the ship is **volume-limited** (light cargo, large SF) or **weight-limited** (heavy cargo, small SF). The cargo space dimensions (height, breadth, length) must be designed so that both volume and weight capacity are utilized efficiently.

---

**Q2.** List any **three factors** that influence the arrangement of cargo spaces in a general cargo ship.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Type and stowage factor of cargo** (determines hold volume vs. hold mass).
2. **Statutory requirements** (SOLAS subdivision, Load Line zones).
3. **Structural strength** (hold length limited by hull girder bending moment).
4. *(Any one of: stability, port turn-around, gear type.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Design of a General Cargo Ship Hold**

**(a)** For a single-deck general cargo ship of $L_{pp} = 140 \text{ m}$, $B = 22 \text{ m}$, depth $D = 12 \text{ m}$, design draught $T = 8.5 \text{ m}$, and block coefficient $C_b = 0.72$:
- Determine the number of holds.
- Calculate the geometric volume of one hold if cargo is stowed to a height of $9 \text{ m}$.
- If permeability $\mu = 0.88$ and stowage factor $SF = 1.6 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{tonne}$, find the cargo mass per hold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **longitudinal and transverse subdivision** of a general cargo ship. Illustrate your answer with a labelled diagram of the cargo region. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Number of holds** (using empirical rule $k = 28 \text{ m}$):

$$
N_{holds} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{pp}}{k} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{140}{28} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.0 \rceil = 5 \text{ holds}
$$

**Step 2 — Hold geometric volume** (rectangular prism, hold length $= 140/5 = 28 \text{ m}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{hold} &= L_{hold} \cdot B \cdot D_{cargo} \\
&= 28 \cdot 22 \cdot 9 \\
&= 5544 \text{ m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective cargo volume:**

$$
V_{cargo,eff} = \mu \cdot V_{hold} = 0.88 \cdot 5544 = 4878.72 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Cargo mass:**

$$
W_{cargo} = \frac{V_{cargo,eff}}{SF} = \frac{4878.72}{1.6} = 3049.2 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Conclusion:** ~3049 tonnes per hold, total cargo = $5 \cdot 3049.2 \approx 15246$ tonnes.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Longitudinal Subdivision** divides the hull along its length using **transverse watertight bulkheads** (typically 4–6 in the cargo region). These limit the **floodable length** and ensure damage stability per SOLAS. They also serve as structural pillars transferring shear between decks and double bottom.

**Transverse Subdivision** divides the hull athwartships using **longitudinal bulkheads** (centerline, side) and **partial decks / platforms**. This:
- Reduces free-surface effect in partially filled tanks.
- Distributes torsional loads.
- Provides passageways and duct keels.

Refer to the schematic below (Mermaid block representation):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Hold 1] --> S2[Transverse Bulkhead 1] --> S3[Hold 2] --> S4[Transverse Bulkhead 2] --> S5[Hold 3]

    S1 -.-> T1[Centerline Bulkhead]
    S3 -.-> T1
    S5 -.-> T1

    S1 -.-> T2[Side Stringer]
    S3 -.-> T2
    S5 -.-> T2
```

A **labelled sketch** in the exam should show: forward bulkhead, after bulkhead, centerline bulkhead, side stringers, 'tween deck, hatch coaming, and double bottom.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Bulk Carrier Hold Design**

**(a)** Explain the **specific features of cargo space arrangement in a bulk carrier**, with emphasis on the hopper tank and double bottom. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has 7 holds, each with $L_{hold} = 25 \text{ m}$, $B = 32 \text{ m}$, and stows iron ore to a height of $15 \text{ m}$. Iron ore has $SF = 0.45 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{tonne}$ and $\mu = 0.95$. Calculate the total deadweight of cargo carried. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

Bulk carriers are **single-deck, transversely subdivided** ships optimized for homogeneous dry bulk. Key features:

1. **Hopper Tanks:** Sloped (typically 45°) tanks at the lower corners of each hold, promoting **self-trimming** of bulk cargo towards the centerline, reducing the need for manual trimming.

2. **Double Bottom:** Mandatory for structural strength and used for **ballast water** in ballast leg, reducing corrosion in cargo holds.

3. **Side Wing Tanks:** Reduce free-surface effect and provide passage for crew/ducting.

4. **Transverse Bulkheads:** Corrugated, vertically stiffened, with manholes and ventilation.

5. **Hatch Coamings & Covers:** Large, hydraulically operated for fast loading.

6. **No 'Tween Decks:** To maximize hold volume for high-SF cargoes (grain) and to allow grabs/excavators.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Hold volume per hold:**

$$
V_{hold} = 25 \cdot 32 \cdot 15 = 12000 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Effective cargo volume per hold:**

$$
V_{eff} = 0.95 \cdot 12000 = 11400 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Mass per hold:**

$$
W_{hold} = \frac{11400}{0.45} = 25333.33 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Total cargo deadweight across 7 holds:**

$$
DW_{total} = 7 \cdot 25333.33 = 177333.33 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Cargo Space Arrangement**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** when computing the number of holds. Always show $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_b$ and $C_p$** — both can appear in a question; $C_b$ uses $\nabla/(L \cdot B \cdot T)$, $C_p$ uses $\nabla/(A_m \cdot L)$.
> 3. **Ignoring permeability** — a common 1-mark loss. Always state $\mu < 1$ and apply it.
> 4. **Treating stowage factor as the same for all cargoes** — it varies widely: coal $\approx 1.2$, grain $\approx 1.4$, cotton $\approx 2.5$, iron ore $\approx 0.45$.
> 5. **Not specifying units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing $\text{m}^{3}$ or $\text{tonne}$.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in (b)** — full 2 marks can be lost if a labelled cargo hold sketch is missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Arrangement of Cargo Spaces"**

- [ ] **Stowage Factor** = $V/W$, expressed in **m³/tonne**; reciprocal gives density.
- [ ] **Permeability** $\mu < 1$ accounts for volume lost to frames, hopper, bilges.
- [ ] **Number of holds** uses empirical rule $N \approx L_{pp}/(25 \text{ to } 30)$, refined by structural limits.
- [ ] **Block Coefficient** $C_b = \nabla/(L \cdot B \cdot T)$ — must be checked for volume-limited cargoes.
- [ ] **General cargo ships** → multi-deck, 'tween decks, transverse subdivision.
- [ ] **Bulk carriers** → single deck, hopper tanks, double bottom, self-trimming.
- [ ] **Tankers** → double hull (MARPOL), longitudinal + transverse subdivision, slop tank.
- [ ] **Container ships** → cellular guides, hatch covers as decks, twist locks.
- [ ] **LNG carriers** → independent tanks (Moss / Membrane / SPB), secondary barrier.
- [ ] **Always** state 3+ design drivers: cargo type, statutory rules, structural strength, stability, port operations.
- [ ] **Always** show $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ when computing discrete hold counts.
- [ ] **Always** include a labelled diagram in 14-mark questions (worth 2–3 marks).
- [ ] **Remember** free-surface correction $BM_{FS} = I_T/(\nabla \cdot (1 - \mu_{FS}))$ in partially filled tanks.
- [ ] **Key mnemonic for ship-type hold features:** **"B**ulk **H**opper **D**ouble, **C**ontainer **C**ells, **T**anker **D**ouble-hull **S**lop, **G**eneral **T**ween-deck".

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Arrangement of Cargo Spaces.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargo Handling Arrangement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cargo Handling Arrangement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cargo Handling Arrangement** refers to the integrated system of mechanical equipment, structural supports, and control mechanisms installed on a merchant ship to enable the safe, efficient, and rapid loading, transfer, and discharge of cargo between the ship and shore-based handling facilities (quays, barges, or other vessels). In KTU 2024 Scheme Naval Architecture parlance, it encompasses the entire suite of **cargo gear** (derricks, cranes, winches, booms, topping lifts, cargo runners, and block-and-tackle systems), the supporting **masts and king posts**, the **hatch covers and coamings**, plus the **cargo securing fittings** (lashing bridges, D-rings, padeyes, and container guides).

The arrangement is designed in accordance with classification society rules (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV), **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Convention Chapter VI and Chapter VII**, and the **Code of Safe Practice for Cargo Stowage and Securing (CSS Code)** issued by the International Maritime Organization (IMO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2, Ship Design I**
> The cargo handling arrangement is the *operational heart* of a cargo ship's design. It dictates hold dimensions, deck layout, structural scantlings of masts/king posts, deck openings, and the ship's **Service Speed vs. Port Turnaround Time** trade-off curve. A poorly conceived arrangement inflates port days, demurrage charges, and charter rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Safe Working Load (SWL)**
> The maximum permissible load that a piece of cargo handling equipment may hoist, lower, or sustain under specified environmental conditions without exceeding the allowable stress in any component. Expressed in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**, where 1 tonne-force ≈ **9.81 kN**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a ship's cargo handling arrangement as the **"shoulder and arm muscles" of a human body**. The **holds** are the storage compartments (like a backpack), the **hatch covers** are the lid flaps, and the **derricks/cranes** are the actual arms that lift heavy weights. Just as you wouldn't use a child's arm to lift a 50 kg suitcase, an engineer doesn't put a 5-tonne SWL derrick on a Capesize bulk carrier. The selection of the gear is *task-matched*:

- A **breakbulk ship** with a dozen small parcels uses multiple **light derricks** operated in **union purchase** (two booms working together like a zipper).
- A **container ship** uses **gantry-type deck cranes** because every lift is identical and heavy (20 ft / 40 ft ISO boxes).
- A **tanker** has *no* cargo gear at all — it relies on shoreside **marine loading arms** because liquid cargo cannot be slung on wires.
- A **pure car carrier (PCC)** uses **internal ramps and liftable decks** — no external cranes.

This *gear-vs-cargo* matching principle is the **conceptual anchor** for every design choice in this module.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Cargo Handling Systems

| Ship Type | Primary Cargo Gear | Typical SWL Range | Hatch Cover Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| General Cargo (Breakbulk) | Heavy-lift derricks, union purchase, deck cranes | 5 t – 30 t per gear | Steel pontoon / folding steel |
| Multipurpose | Electro-hydraulic deck cranes (often twin) | 20 t – 120 t (single), up to 240 t (tandem) | Side-rolling / piggy-back |
| Bulk Carrier | Shore-based cranes only (occasionally shipboard gear) | Nil (gearless) | MacGregor, steel folding |
| Container Ship | Shipboard gantry cranes (rare) or shore gantry | 30 t – 65 t per crane | Folding / pontoon |
| Reefer | Side ports with conveyors; pedestal cranes | 3 t – 8 t | Insulated steel |
| Tanker | None (marine loading arms shoreside) | 0 | None — manifold valves only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Standards Used in This Module**
> - Forces in **tonnes-force (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**: $1 \text{ t} = 9.81 \text{ kN}$
> - Boom length in **metres (m)**
> - Hoist speed in **metres per minute (m/min)**
> - Wire rope diameter in **millimetres (mm)**
> - Safe factor on **Minimum Breaking Load (MBL)** of wire rope: typically **5.0** for running ropes, **4.0** for standing ropes, per **ISO 2408** and classification society rules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Cargo Handling System — Logical Breakdown

A complete cargo handling arrangement consists of **seven interlinked subsystems**:

1. **Lifting Element** — boom, jib, or crane arm
2. **Support Element** — mast, king post, derrick post, A-frame, or pedestal
3. **Hoisting Element** — cargo winch, topping lift winch, slewing winch
4. **Transmission Element** — wire ropes, blocks, sheaves, shackles, and hooks
5. **Securing Element** — lashings, turnbuckles, pad-eyes
6. **Access Element** — hatches, coamings, ramps, doors
7. **Auxiliary Element** — limit switches, load indicators, anti-two-block devices, emergency release

---

## 2.2 Fundamental Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 Single Derrick (Single Boom Operation)

A single derrick is pivoted at the **heel** (on a mast or king post) and supported by the **topping lift** running over a **span block** at the masthead to a winch. A separate **cargo runner** (the hoisting wire) passes through a **boom-head sheave** and back down to the cargo winch. The derrick can **slew** (rotate in the horizontal plane) using a slewing pendant and winch.

- The topping lift **suspends the boom tip** — controls the boom angle.
- The cargo runner **hoists the load** — controls the hook height.
- The slewing line **rotates the boom** — controls outreach direction.

### 2.2.2 Union Purchase (Two-Derrick System)

Two derricks are rigged in opposing fashion — one ship's side derrick and one shoreside derrick — sharing a common **cargo runner** that passes through both boom-head blocks. This gives a **mechanical advantage (MA) of 4** and a working radius equal to roughly **half the distance between the two mastheads** at the working plane. Historically the workhorse of breakbulk shipping, but increasingly replaced by hydraulic cranes due to its slow cycle time (~ 90 seconds per lift) and labour intensity.

### 2.2.3 Shipboard Deck Cranes (Electro-Hydraulic)

Modern standard. A slewing column, a luffing jib, a hoist drum, and an operator's cabin. Crane capacity is governed by:

- **Load Moment (M)**: $M = SWL \times R$ where R is the radius from the slewing axis.
- The crane's structure must withstand the worst-case moment — typically at **maximum radius with full SWL** OR **minimum radius with overload** (e.g., dynamic snatch).
- Cranes are **rated in tonne-metres (t·m)** — e.g., a 30 t × 20 m crane = **600 t·m**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard formula set used in KTU Board Examinations for Cargo Handling Arrangement**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{crane} = \dfrac{SWL \times 9.81}{\eta_{m} \times \eta_{b} \times \eta_{p}}$ | Power required at winch drum, where $\eta_m$ = motor efficiency, $\eta_b$ = block efficiency, $\eta_p$ = pulley efficiency | kW |
| 2 | $v_{hoist} = \dfrac{\pi \times D \times N}{60}$ | Hoist speed, $D$ = drum diameter, $N$ = drum rpm | m/min |
| 3 | $F_{topping} = SWL \times \dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}$ | Force in topping lift, $\theta$ = boom angle from horizontal, $\alpha$ = topping lift angle, $\phi$ = friction angle (~ 2° per sheave) | kN |
| 4 | $F_{slewing} = \dfrac{M_{slew}}{R_{slew}} = \dfrac{SWL \times R_{reach} \times \mu_{wind}}{R_{slew}}$ | Slewing force from wind torque | kN |
| 5 | $MA = n_{lines} = 2^n$ where $n$ = number of blocks | Mechanical advantage of block-and-tackle | dimensionless |
| 6 | $v_{load} = \dfrac{v_{hoist}}{MA}$ | Effective load speed | m/min |
| 7 | $d_{rope} = \sqrt{\dfrac{SWL \times SF}{\sigma_{t} \times 10^6 \times 0.38}}$ | Wire rope diameter — **Fleet Angle** limit: $\alpha \leq 1.5°$ for smooth drums, $4°$ for grooved | mm |
| 8 | $t_{coaming} = \dfrac{p \times s \times k}{2 \times \sigma \times \eta_{weld}}$ | Hatch coaming plate thickness under cargo pressure, $p$ = pressure (kN/m²), $s$ = stiffener spacing (mm) | mm |
| 9 | $P_{cycle} = \dfrac{m \times g \times v_{hoist}}{\eta_{total}}$ | Power per lift cycle, $m$ = load mass, $g = 9.81$ m/s² | W |
| 10 | $T_{crane} = \dfrac{SWL \times R_{max}}{g}$ | Maximum load moment | t·m |
| 11 | $N_{crane} = \dfrac{\sum (W_i \times t_i)}{T_{cycle}}$ | Number of cranes required for given port stay | dimensionless |
| 12 | $h_{mast} = h_{boom} \times \sin\theta_{min} + R_{max} \times \cos\theta_{min} + h_{clear}$ | Minimum masthead height to clear hatch coaming at minimum boom angle | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall**: In formula 7, the constant **0.38** is the **fill factor** for 6×37 IWRC (Independent Wire Rope Core) construction. For 6×19 it is **0.38**; for 6×36 it is **0.40**; for 19×7 rotation-resistant rope it drops to **0.30**. Using the wrong fill factor is a guaranteed mark-loser in KTU valuation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The cargo handling arrangement determines the **TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit) handling rate** of a container ship, the **loading rate (tonnes/hour)** of a bulk carrier's hatch sequence, and the **demurrage exposure** of a charterer. A Capesize bulk carrier with 9 holds and 5 cranes must complete a 180,000 t iron-ore load in ~ 36 hours to avoid demurrage — the cargo gear (or lack thereof) is the **bottleneck**. In offshore construction, shipboard cranes of 5,000 t SWL exist for module installation — these follow the same fundamental physics but at extreme scale.

The cargo handling arrangement also governs **structural design loads** transmitted into the hull. A 30-tonne derrick transmits a dynamic snatch force of up to **1.8 × SWL** (i.e., **54 tonnes**) into the mast and through the mast into the deckhouse. This is one of the highest localized deck loads a ship experiences — comparable to helicopter landing reactions on a frigate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Topping Lift Tension in a Single Derrick

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
A single derrick with **SWL = 10 t** is to be set at a boom angle of **θ = 45°** from the horizontal. The topping lift runs from a winch, over a span block at the masthead, down to the boom tip. The angle between the topping lift and the boom is **α = 60°**. Determine the tension in the topping lift wire, ignoring friction.

### 3.1.2 Free Body Diagram Description
The boom tip is in equilibrium under three forces:
- The vertical load **W = SWL = 10 t × 9.81 = 98.1 kN** acting downward.
- The compression **F_b** along the boom (acting from tip toward heel).
- The tension **T** in the topping lift, at angle **α** above the boom.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the load in engineering units.
$$W = 10 \text{ t} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 98.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2:** Resolve forces perpendicular and parallel to the boom axis.
The boom makes angle **θ = 45°** with the horizontal. The topping lift makes angle **α = 60°** with the boom. Therefore the topping lift makes angle **(θ + α) = 105°** with the horizontal.

**Step 3:** Apply equilibrium of forces perpendicular to the boom.
$$\sum F_{\perp} = 0$$
$$T \times \sin(\alpha) - W \times \cos(\theta) = 0$$
$$T \times \sin(60°) = 98.1 \times \cos(45°)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute trigonometric values.
$$T \times 0.8660 = 98.1 \times 0.7071$$
$$T \times 0.8660 = 69.37 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for T.
$$T = \dfrac{69.37}{0.8660} = 80.10 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6:** Convert back to tonnes-force.
$$T = \dfrac{80.10}{9.81} = 8.16 \text{ t-force}$$

### 3.1.4 Result & Interpretation
The topping lift tension is **8.16 tonnes**, which is **less than the SWL** because the boom carries part of the load in compression. The compression in the boom itself is:
$$F_b = W \times \sin(\theta) + T \times \cos(\alpha) = 98.1 \times 0.7071 + 80.10 \times 0.5 = 69.37 + 40.05 = 109.42 \text{ kN} \approx 11.15 \text{ t}$$

The boom is therefore **more heavily loaded than the topping lift** — a critical insight for **boom scantling design** (which is the subject of classification society rules like **Lloyd's Register Pt 3, Ch 9**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wire Rope Diameter Selection for a Cargo Runner

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
Select a 6×37 IWRC wire rope for a cargo runner carrying a **SWL of 15 t**. The classification society requires a **safety factor of 5.0** on the minimum breaking load (MBL). The ultimate tensile strength of the wire is **σ_t = 1,800 MPa**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert SWL to working load force.
$$W = 15 \text{ t} \times 9.81 = 147.15 \text{ kN} = 147,150 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the safety factor to get the required minimum breaking load.
$$MBL_{req} = SF \times W = 5.0 \times 147,150 = 735,750 \text{ N} = 735.75 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3:** Use the metallic cross-sectional area formula. For a 6×37 IWRC rope, the **fill factor (k_f) = 0.38**.
$$A_{metal} = \dfrac{MBL_{req}}{\sigma_t} = \dfrac{735,750}{1,800 \times 10^6} = 4.0875 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 408.75 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4:** Relate metallic area to nominal rope diameter.
$$A_{metal} = k_f \times \dfrac{\pi \times d^2}{4}$$
$$408.75 = 0.38 \times \dfrac{\pi \times d^2}{4}$$
$$408.75 = 0.2984 \times d^2$$
$$d^2 = \dfrac{408.75}{0.2984} = 1,369.94 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$d = \sqrt{1,369.94} = 37.01 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Round up to next standard rope size.
The nearest standard 6×37 IWRC diameter is **38 mm** (standard increments: 36, 38, 40, 42…).

**Step 6:** Verify the actual MBL of a 38 mm rope.
Typical MBL for 38 mm 6×37 IWRC at 1,800 MPa wire is **~ 850 kN**.
$$SF_{actual} = \dfrac{850}{147.15} = 5.78$$
This satisfies the minimum of 5.0 ✓

### 3.2.3 Result
**Use a 38 mm 6×37 IWRC wire rope** with a verified breaking load of at least **735.75 kN**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Number of Cranes for a Given Port Stay

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement
A general cargo ship of **8,000 DWT** must load **10,000 tonnes** of bagged cement in **72 hours**. Each crane cycle (pickup, hoist, slew, lower, return) averages **90 seconds**, and the average lift is **5 tonnes**. The ship has 4 hatches. Find the number of cranes required assuming **75% crane efficiency** and **20 hours/day effective working time**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Total lifts required.
$$N_{lifts} = \dfrac{10,000}{5} = 2,000 \text{ lifts}$$

**Step 2:** Effective working time available.
$$T_{eff} = 72 \text{ hr} \times 3 \text{ days} = 216 \text{ hr, but with } 20 \text{ hr/day effective} = 72 \times (20/24) = 60 \text{ hr}$$

> [Note from problem: use 72 hours direct for safety; this is conservatively a full working period]

**Step 3:** Effective time per crane.
$$T_{one\_crane} = 60 \text{ hr} \times 3,600 \text{ s/hr} \times 0.75 = 162,000 \text{ s of effective operation}$$

**Step 4:** Lifts per crane.
$$N_{per\_crane} = \dfrac{162,000}{90} = 1,800 \text{ lifts per crane}$$

**Step 5:** Total cranes required.
$$N_{cranes} = \dfrac{2,000}{1,800} = 1.11 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 2 \text{ cranes}$$

### 3.3.3 Result
**At least 2 cranes** are required. The ship has 4 hatches; pairing 2 cranes for union-purchase operation (doubling capacity) is the optimal arrangement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cargo Handling Methods

| Method | SWL Range | Cycle Time | Crew Required | Cost (Index) | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single derrick (manual) | 3 – 30 t | 3 min | 6 sailors | 1.0× | Small breakbulk ships |
| Union purchase | 5 – 60 t (combined) | 2 min | 8 sailors | 1.4× | Mid-size breakbulk |
| Electro-hydraulic deck crane | 20 – 250 t | 45 s | 1 operator | 4.0× | Multipurpose heavylift |
| Shipboard gantry (container) | 30 – 65 t | 30 s | 1 operator | 8.0× | Feeder container ships |
| Helideck crane (offshore) | 5 – 30 t | 60 s | 2 operators | 5.0× | OSV / supply ships |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters in KTU valuation**: A 14-mark problem may ask you to *select* and *justify* a cargo handling system. The matrix lets you argue on cycle time, manning cost, and SWL — the three pillars of port productivity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cargo Handling Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Hull Deck Plate] --> B[Hatch Coaming Frame]
    B --> C[Hatch Cover Pontoon or Folding Steel]
    C --> D[Hydraulic or Chain Drive Actuation]
    D --> E[Cleats and Compression Bars]
    
    F[Deckhouse Foundation] --> G[King Post or Mast]
    G --> H[Span Block and Sheaves at Masthead]
    G --> I[Topping Lift Winch]
    I --> J[Topping Lift Wire Rope]
    J --> K[Boom Head Sheave]
    
    K --> L[Dermick Boom]
    L --> M[Cargo Block and Hook]
    M --> N[Shipped Cargo Unit]
    
    H --> O[Slewing Winch and Pendant]
    O --> L
    
    P[Cargo Winch on Deck] --> Q[Cargo Runner Wire Rope]
    Q --> K
    Q --> N
    
    R[Lashing Bridge or D Ring Foundation] --> S[Container Lashing Rods and Turnbuckles]
    
    T[Operator Cabin with Load Moment Indicator] --> U[Crane Slewing Motor]
    U --> L
    T --> V[Hoist Motor]
    V --> P
    T --> W[Anti Two Block Sensor]
    W --> M
    
    X[Emergency Release Coupling] --> M
```

**Description of Flow:** The block diagram maps the **information and load path** of a typical shipboard electro-hydraulic deck crane on a multipurpose vessel. The **cargo runner** (Q) transmits hoist force from the cargo winch (P) through the boom-head sheave (K) to the hook block (M). The **topping lift** (J) controls the boom angle via the winch (I) on the mast (G). The **slewing pendant** (O) rotates the boom (L). The **operator cabin** (T) commands all motors and is monitored by the **anti-two-block** safety device (W) and the **load moment indicator** (T, self-loop). The **emergency release** (X) allows jettison of the load in catastrophic failure.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Cargo Lift Cycle in Union Purchase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Load Slung at Hold Bottom] --> S2[Both Winches Take Up Slack]
    S2 --> S3[Topping Lift on Shipside Derrick Lowers Boom to 30 deg]
    S3 --> S4[Topping Lift on Shoreside Derrick Lifts Boom to 60 deg]
    S4 --> S5[Load Travels on Catenary Curve from Hold to Quay]
    S5 --> S6[Shoreside Boom Lowered Landward]
    S6 --> S7[Load Lands on Quay]
    S7 --> S8[Hook Released and Slings Recovered]
    S8 --> S9[Return Cycle to Fetch Next Lift]
```

**Description:** This flowchart captures the **timed sequence** of a single union-purchase lift. The horizontal transfer of the load is achieved not by slewing but by **coordinated re-luffing** of the two booms — a graceful, efficient motion familiar to any port worker of the 1950s–1990s era.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison Tree of Cargo Gear by Ship Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Cargo Handling Arrangement Selection]
    Root --> A1[Breakbulk and General Cargo]
    Root --> A2[Multipurpose Heavy Lift]
    Root --> A3[Container Ship]
    Root --> A4[Bulk Carrier]
    Root --> A5[Tanker]
    
    A1 --> B1[Derricks Single or Union Purchase]
    A1 --> B2[Light Hydraulic Cranes SWL 5 to 30 t]
    
    A2 --> C1[Electro Hydraulic Twin Crane SWL 30 to 120 t]
    A2 --> C2[Tandem Lift for 240 t Class]
    
    A3 --> D1[Shipboard Gantry Crane Feeder]
    A3 --> D2[Shoreside STS Gantry only]
    
    A4 --> E1[Gearless, Shore Cranes Only]
    A4 --> E2[Optional Deck Crane 30 t for Stores]
    
    A5 --> F1[No Cargo Gear, Marine Loading Arms on Quay]
    A5 --> F2[Manifold and ESD System]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Safe Working Load" (SWL) of a cargo gear. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Model Answer:**
**Safe Working Load (SWL)** of a cargo gear is the maximum load that the equipment is certified to lift, lower, or sustain under normal service conditions without exceeding the allowable stress limits prescribed by the relevant classification society. It is determined by the weakest link in the load path — typically the wire rope's **Minimum Breaking Load (MBL) divided by a safety factor** (usually 5.0 for running ropes and 4.0 for standing ropes). The SWL must be conspicuously marked on every piece of cargo handling equipment, and exceeding it constitutes a safety violation under SOLAS Chapter VI Regulation 5.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint (3-mark scoring):**
> - Definition (1 mark)
> - Mention of MBL / Safety Factor (1 mark)
> - SOLAS regulation reference (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: What is "Union Purchase" in cargo handling? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**
**Union Purchase** is a traditional cargo handling rigging arrangement in which **two derricks** (one ship's side and one shoreside) share a common **cargo runner** that passes through both boom-head blocks. The load is transferred between the ship's hold and the quay by coordinated **luffing** of the two booms — one lowers while the other raises, producing a catenary motion of the load. The system provides a **mechanical advantage of 4** (i.e., 4-part reeving through the blocks) and was the workhorse method of breakbulk cargo handling before the widespread adoption of electro-hydraulic deck cranes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint (3-mark scoring):**
> - Two derricks with common runner (1 mark)
> - Coordinated luffing motion (1 mark)
> - Mechanical advantage of 4 (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Design of a Cargo Derrick and Hatch Coaming [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

A single cargo derrick is to be fitted on a general cargo ship with the following particulars:
- **Safe Working Load:** 12 tonnes
- **Boom length:** 12 m
- **Boom angle from horizontal:** 30°
- **Topping lift angle from boom:** 50°
- **Cargo winch efficiency:** 85%
- **Block efficiency:** 95%
- **Wire rope efficiency (4-part reeving):** 90%

**(a)** Calculate the following: (7 marks)
1. Tension in the topping lift wire
2. Compression in the boom
3. Tension in the cargo runner
4. Power required at the cargo winch drum (assume hoist speed of 30 m/min)

**(b)** Design a suitable **6×37 IWRC wire rope** for the cargo runner if the wire ultimate tensile strength is **1,770 MPa** and the **safety factor is 5.0**. Also check the **hatch coaming thickness** if the coaming height is 1.2 m and cargo pressure is 5 kN/m² with stiffener spacing of 600 mm and weld efficiency of 0.85. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Forces in the Derrick System (7 marks)

**Step 1: Convert SWL to SI force.**
$$W = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Topping Lift Tension T.**
The topping lift makes angle (θ + α) = 30° + 50° = **80°** with the horizontal.
Resolving perpendicular to the boom:
$$T \sin(\alpha) = W \cos(\theta)$$
$$T \sin(50°) = 117.72 \cos(30°)$$
$$T \times 0.7660 = 117.72 \times 0.8660$$
$$T \times 0.7660 = 101.95 \text{ kN}$$
$$\boxed{T = 133.09 \text{ kN} = 13.57 \text{ t}}$$

**Step 3: Boom Compression F_b.**
Resolving along the boom axis:
$$F_b = W \sin(\theta) + T \cos(\alpha)$$
$$F_b = 117.72 \times \sin(30°) + 133.09 \times \cos(50°)$$
$$F_b = 117.72 \times 0.5 + 133.09 \times 0.6428$$
$$F_b = 58.86 + 85.55 = 144.41 \text{ kN}$$
$$\boxed{F_b = 14.72 \text{ t}}$$

**Step 4: Cargo Runner Tension F_r.**
With 4-part reeving and block efficiency η_b:
$$F_r = \dfrac{W}{n \times \eta_b} = \dfrac{117.72}{4 \times 0.95} = 30.98 \text{ kN} = 3.16 \text{ t}$$

**Step 5: Power at the winch drum.**
$$P = \dfrac{F_r \times v_{hoist}}{60 \times \eta_{winch}}$$
$$P = \dfrac{30.98 \times 30}{60 \times 0.85} = 18.22 \text{ kW}$$
$$\boxed{P \approx 18.22 \text{ kW}}$$

##### Part (b) — Wire Rope and Coaming Design (7 marks)

**Step 1: Required MBL of the wire rope.**
$$MBL_{req} = SF \times F_r = 5.0 \times 30.98 = 154.9 \text{ kN}$$

> Note: Some texts apply the safety factor to SWL, not runner tension. Using SWL for completeness:
> $$MBL_{req (SWL)} = 5.0 \times 117.72 = 588.6 \text{ kN}$$
> We will use the conservative **SWL-based** design.

**Step 2: Required metallic cross-sectional area.**
$$A_{metal} = \dfrac{MBL_{req}}{\sigma_t} = \dfrac{588.6 \times 10^3}{1770 \times 10^6} = 3.326 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 332.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Solve for nominal diameter (fill factor k_f = 0.38 for 6×37 IWRC).**
$$A_{metal} = k_f \times \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$$
$$332.6 = 0.38 \times 0.7854 \times d^2$$
$$332.6 = 0.2985 \times d^2$$
$$d^2 = 1114.6$$
$$d = 33.38 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{use standard 34 mm}$$

**Step 4: Hatch Coaming Thickness.**
Using the formula:
$$t_{coaming} = \dfrac{p \times s \times k}{2 \times \sigma \times \eta_{weld}}$$
Assume permissible bending stress σ = 165 MPa (mild steel), k = 1.0 (stiffener factor):
$$t_{coaming} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^3 \times 600 \times 1.0}{2 \times 165 \times 0.85}$$
$$t_{coaming} = \dfrac{3,000,000}{280.5} = 10,696 \text{ mm}$$

> **Wait — the units must be reconciled.** p is in N/m², s in mm, σ in N/mm². Convert: 5 kN/m² = 5 × 10⁻³ N/mm².
> $$t = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 600}{2 \times 165 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{3.0}{280.5} = 0.01070 \text{ m} = 10.70 \text{ mm}$$
> $$\boxed{t_{coaming} \approx 11 \text{ mm, adopt standard 12 mm plate}}$$

**Step 5: Final Recommendations:**
- Wire rope: **34 mm 6×37 IWRC** (verified MBL ≥ 590 kN)
- Hatch coaming plate: **12 mm mild steel**
- Stiffener: **L 75 × 50 × 6 mm** at **600 mm spacing**

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Selection and Justification of Cargo Handling System [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A **8,000 DWT multipurpose cargo ship** is being designed to carry project cargo, containers, and bagged goods. The owner requires:
- Single lift capacity up to **80 t** at **18 m radius**
- Container handling capability of **30 t per lift** at **30 m radius**
- Total port stay for **15,000 t** of mixed cargo must not exceed **5 days**

**(a)** Recommend a suitable cargo handling arrangement with **number of cranes, type, and SWL**. Justify the selection using **load moment calculations** and **cycle time analysis**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Sketch a **schematic arrangement** of the selected gear on a ship general arrangement plan, showing positions of masts, king posts, hatches, and crane pedestals. Discuss the **structural reinforcements** required at the pedestal foundations. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Crane Selection & Justification (7 marks)

**Step 1: Load Moment Requirement.**
Heavy-lift capacity: 80 t × 18 m = **1,440 t·m**
Container lift: 30 t × 30 m = **900 t·m**
Adopt higher value → Crane must be **rated at least 1,500 t·m**.

**Step 2: Recommended Configuration.**
- **Two electro-hydraulic deck cranes**, each rated at **1,500 t·m** (e.g., 80 t SWL at 18 m radius; 30 t at 30 m radius when used in container mode; can be **tandem-lifted** for **120 t at 18 m** using both cranes together).
- Manufacturer reference type: **Liebherr CBG 350** or **MacGregor GLB 300** (generic spec — no specific endorsement implied).

**Step 3: Cycle Time Analysis.**
- Single-lift cycle: 60 s (hoist + slew + lower)
- Effective time: 5 days × 20 hr = 100 hr = 360,000 s
- With 75% efficiency: 270,000 s of working time
- Lifts per crane: 270,000 / 60 = **4,500 lifts per crane**
- Two cranes in parallel: 9,000 lifts
- Average lift size at 15,000 t total: ~ 5 t (after initial heavy lifts)
- Number of lifts at 5 t: 15,000 / 5 = **3,000 lifts**
- Easily handled by **one crane alone in 50 hours** → **second crane provides redundancy and tandem capability**.

**Step 4: Justification Summary.**
- Two 1,500 t·m cranes satisfy all SWL and radius conditions.
- Two cranes enable tandem lift (120 t × 18 m).
- Redundancy ensures schedule integrity if one crane fails.
- Compact electro-hydraulic design minimises manning (1 operator per crane).

##### Part (b) — General Arrangement and Structural Reinforcement (7 marks)

**Step 5: Schematic Placement.**
- **Forward crane pedestal** on port side, just aft of hatch 1.
- **Aft crane pedestal** on starboard side, just forward of hatch 4.
- This **diagonal placement** allows each crane to serve **all five hatches** at the required radius.
- Twin king posts removed in favour of integrated crane pedestals (eliminates rigging).

**Step 6: Pedestal Foundation Reinforcement.**
- Pedestal transmits up to **1.8 × SWL dynamic snatch** = 1.8 × 80 = **144 t** vertical + slewing reactions.
- **Doubler plate:** 20 mm × 2 m × 2 m under each pedestal, welded to deck plate.
- **Pedestal bulkhead below deck:** Two **transverse ring-stiffened bulkheads** with thickness **16 mm** and **T-section stiffeners** at 500 mm spacing.
- **Web frame in way of pedestal:** Insert a **deep web frame** with **200 × 12 HP section** connecting pedestal base to tank top and side shell via **brackets** (gussets).
- **Hatch coaming reinforcement** at pedestal-side: Upgrade to **16 mm** for **4 m run** each side of pedestal.
- All welds to be **full penetration** in way of high-stress zones, classified society inspected (100% MPI/UT).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit confusion in wire rope formula:** Students frequently mix N and kN, or use σ_t in GPa instead of MPa. Always write the **unit dimension check** explicitly: $[N] = [N/mm^2] \times [mm^2]$.
> 2. **Sign convention error in boom compression:** The boom is in **compression**, not tension. A common mistake is to subtract instead of add the topping-lift component. Markers deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. **Forgetting block efficiency:** When a 4-part reeving is used, students often forget that block efficiency η_b = 0.95 is **per block**, and the total efficiency is η_b^n. For 2 blocks in series, it's 0.95² = 0.9025.
> 4. **Fill factor confusion:** 0.38 for 6×37, 0.40 for 6×36, 0.30 for rotation-resistant. Examiners test this deliberately.
> 5. **Hatch coaming unit mismatch:** Pressure p in kN/m² must be converted to N/mm² (divide by 10⁶) when used with mm and N/mm² outputs. Many students omit this and get answers off by a factor of 10⁶.
> 6. **Failing to round up to standard sizes:** Always round up wire rope and plate thicknesses to next standard size. A 33.38 mm diameter rope becomes 34 mm, not 33 mm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cargo Handling Arrangement**

- **Cargo handling** = integrated system of gear, structure, and access for loading/discharging.
- **SWL** is determined by the weakest component, using safety factor on MBL.
- **Derricks** are traditional; **electro-hydraulic cranes** are modern; **union purchase** is the classic two-derrick method with MA = 4.
- **Topping lift** controls boom angle; **cargo runner** hoists load; **slewing pendant** rotates boom.
- **Boom compression** $F_b = W \sin(\theta) + T \cos(\alpha)$ — always **greater than SWL** for θ < 90°.
- **Wire rope selection:** use $d = \sqrt{MBL_{req} \times 4 / (\pi \times k_f \times \sigma_t)}$; fill factors: 0.38 (6×37), 0.40 (6×36), 0.30 (19×7 rotation-resistant).
- **Safety factor:** 5.0 (running rope), 4.0 (standing rope), per ISO 2408 and classification rules.
- **Hatch coaming thickness:** $t = p \times s / (2 \sigma \eta_{weld})$ — always convert units carefully (N/mm²).
- **Crane rating in tonne-metres (t·m):** load moment = SWL × radius.
- **Fleet angle** of wire rope on drum ≤ 1.5° (smooth) or 4° (grooved).
- **Dynamic snatch factor** = 1.5 to 1.8 × SWL — used for mast/pedestal scantlings.
- **Hatch cover types:** pontoon, folding steel, side-rolling, piggy-back, MacGregor.
- **Code standards:** SOLAS Ch VI/VII, CSS Code, ISO 2408 (wire ropes), ISO 1161 (container corners), classification society rules.
- **Structural reinforcement at pedestals:** doubler plates, deep web frames, ring-stiffened bulkheads, full-penetration welds.
- **Selection criteria:** cycle time, manning, SWL, radius, redundancy, lifecycle cost.
- **Always round up** to standard wire rope and plate sizes; examiners will check this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrangement of Machinery Spaces, Accommodation and Associated Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Arrangement of Machinery Spaces, Accommodation and Associated Spaces # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of ship design, **Machinery Spaces**, **Accommodation Spaces**, and **Associated Spaces** refer to the systematic zoning of a vessel's internal volume into functionally distinct compartments, as strictly defined by **SOLAS (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea) Chapter II-1, II-2**, and major Classification Society Rules (Lloyd's Register, DNV, IRS, ABS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **SOLAS Definition of "Machinery Space of Category A":** > *"A space, and trunks to such spaces, containing internal combustion machinery used either for main propulsion or for other purposes where such machinery has in the aggregate a total power output of not less than 375 kW, or containing any oil-fired boiler or oil-fired internal combustion machinery, or any oil-fired equipment other than boilers (e.g., inert gas generator, incinerator, etc.)."* > [!NOTE] > **Key Spatial Definitions (SOLAS Chapter II-2):** > - **Accommodation Spaces**: Those used for public spaces, corridors, lavatories, cabins, offices, hospitals, cinemas, game and hobby rooms, barber shops, pantries containing no cooking appliances, and similar spaces. > - **Service Spaces**: Galleys, pantries containing cooking appliances, lockers, mail and specie rooms, storerooms, workshops (other than those forming part of machinery spaces), and similar spaces, plus trunks to such spaces. > - **Cargo Spaces**: All spaces used for cargo (including cargo oil tanks) and trunks to such spaces. > - **Control Stations**: Spaces in which the ship's radio or main navigating equipment or the emergency source of power is located, or where the fire recording or fire control equipment is centralized. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating City" Model Imagine a ship as a **vertical floating city** stacked on top of a multi-storey building: - The **Engine Room** is the city's **power plant** — it is loud, hot, dangerous, and must be isolated. - The **Accommodation Block** is the **residential district** — quiet, comfortable, well-ventilated, and safe. - The **Associated Spaces** (cargo holds, tanks, stores) are the **industrial warehouses and storage depots** — functional and accessible, but separate from living areas. Just as a city planner would never place a nuclear reactor next to a kindergarten, a ship designer must ensure **structural separation**, **fire integrity**, **noise isolation**, and **escape routes** between these zones. > [!TIP] > **Designer's Golden Rule**: A well-arranged ship must balance **operational efficiency** (short pipe/cable runs, easy maintenance access) with **safety and habitability** (fire separation, noise control, escape routes). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants - **Maximum permissible length of machinery space of Category A without fixed fire-extinguishing system**: **N/A — system mandatory**. - **Standard fire integrity of bulkhead between machinery space and accommodation**: **A-60 Class** (60 minutes of standard fire test exposure). - **Minimum breadth of escape route in machinery spaces**: **600 mm** (clear passage). - **Standard double bottom depth in machinery spaces**: **B/20** or **2 m**, whichever is less (where B = moulded breadth). - **SOLAS Regulation II-1/26** minimum bilge pumping capacity per compartment: Sufficient to pump the flooded volume in **~3 hours**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Ship Spaces A modern ship is divided into **functional zones** governed by fire safety, structural, and operational logic. The hierarchical structure is: 1. **Category A Machinery Spaces** — Highest fire risk (oil-fired boilers, internal combustion engines > 375 kW). 2. **Other Machinery Spaces** — Lower risk (electric motors, generators, steering gear rooms). 3. **Pump Rooms** — Special category for cargo oil pump rooms on tankers. 4. **Accommodation Spaces** — Habitability zone. 5. **Service Spaces** — Support functions (galleys, pantries, laundries). 6. **Cargo Spaces** — Holds, tanks. 7. **Control Stations** — Bridge, ECR (Engine Control Room), emergency switchboard room. 8. **Tunnels, Trunks, Ducts, Shaft Passages** — Connecting systems. ## 2.2 Design Logic for Machinery Space Arrangement ### 2.2.1 Location of Main Engine Room The main engine room (ER) is typically located **aft of midship** for the following reasons: - **Propeller shaft alignment**: Reduces shaft length, bearings, and alignment problems. - **Vibration isolation**: Distance from forward accommodation block. - **Draft and trim**: Heaviest machinery concentrated aft balances cargo forward. - **Reserve buoyancy**: Aft machinery ships usually have better damage stability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modern Container Ships & Naval Vessels** often adopt a **mid-ship or split arrangement** (e.g., CODAG, CODLAG plants) for redundancy. ### 2.2.2 Vertical Zoning of Engine Room Within the engine room, equipment is arranged on multiple levels: | Level | Typical Equipment | |---|---| | **Top Platform (Casing)** | Main engine controls, funnels, uptakes, silencers | | **Upper Platform (Cylinder Top)** | Cylinders, exhaust manifold, turbochargers, starting air receivers | | **Main Engine Room Floor** | Main engine crankshaft, bedplate, thrust bearing | | **Lower Platform (Pit)** | Oil coolers, lube oil purifiers, bilge wells | | **Double Bottom (Tank Top)** | Oil fuel settling/service tanks, lube oil storage, fresh water | | **Shaft Tunnel** | Propeller shaft, lubricating oil tanks, escape trunk | ### 2.2.3 Longitudinal vs. Transverse Arrangement - **Longitudinal (In-line) Arrangement**: Main engine, gearbox, and shaft on the centerline. Cleaner, shorter shaft, but wider engine room. - **Transverse Arrangement**: Used in multi-engine installations (e.g., twin-screw ships) where engines sit side-by-side. ## 2.3 Accommodation Block Arrangement ### 2.3.1 SOLAS Required Separation Per **SOLAS Chapter II-2 Regulation 4.2.2**, the following boundary standards apply: | Boundary Type | Fire Integrity Class | |---|---| | Machinery Space (Cat A) ↔ Accommodation | **A-60** | | Machinery Space (Cat A) ↔ Corridor | **A-30** | | Service Space ↔ Accommodation | **A-0 to A-30** (depending on use) | | Cargo Space ↔ Accommodation (tankers) | **A-60** | | Stairway Lift Trunk | **A-30 to A-60** | ### 2.3.2 Habitability Zones (MLC 2006 & ILO Conventions) - **Officer cabins**: Single occupancy, ~12–15 m². - **Junior officer cabins**: ~10–12 m². - **Crew cabins**: 2–4 person occupancy, ~4–5 m²/person. - **Mess rooms**: Minimum 1 m²/person seated. - **Recreation deck**: Open-air, aft or forward of accommodation. ### 2.3.3 Noise and Vibration Limits (ISO 6954 / MSC.337(91)) | Space | Max Noise Level (dB(A)) | |---|---| | Wheelhouse | **65** | | Cabins | **60** | | Mess rooms | **65** | | Machinery control rooms | **75** | | Engine room (manned) | **90** | | Engine room (UMS) | **110** (with PPE) | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Units | Source / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Double bottom depth in machinery spaces | $h_{db} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{B}{20},\ 2.0\right)$ | m | SOLAS Reg II-1/11 | | 2 | Bilge pipe diameter | $d = 1.68\sqrt{A}\ +\ 25$ | mm | ISO 7604 (approx) | | 3 | Pumping rate (emergency bilge) | $Q_{bilge} \ge \dfrac{V_{flooded}}{3}$ | m³/h | SOLAS Reg II-1/21 | | 4 | Ventilation duct fire damper activation | $t_{close} \le 30$ | s | SOLAS Reg II-2/9.7 | | 5 | A-class fire integrity | A-0, A-15, A-30, A-60 | minutes | FTP Code | | 6 | B-class fire integrity | B-0, B-15 | minutes | FTP Code | | 7 | Min escape route width (machinery) | $w_{esc} \ge 600$ | mm | SOLAS Reg II-2/13 | | 8 | Min cofferdam width (oil fuel tank vs hull) | $w_{cof} \ge 600$ | mm | SOLAS Reg II-1/26 | | 9 | Mean height of accommodation deck | $H_{deck} \ge 2.0$ | m | MLC 2006 | | 10 | Rule for volume of an A-class compartment | $V = L \times B \times H$ | m³ | Geometric | | 11 | Aspect ratio of machinery space | $AR = L_{ER} / B_{ER}$ | — | Design check (typ. 1.0–1.5) | | 12 | Manning density (habitability) | $\rho_m = N_{crew} / A_{accom}$ | persons/m² | ILO/MLC check | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams**: The most frequently tested boundary values are **A-60** (Cat A machinery ↔ accommodation), **A-30** (corridors), and the **600 mm escape route / cofferdam width**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Classification Society Plan Approval**: All general arrangement (GA) drawings showing machinery, accommodation, and associated spaces must be approved by the Classification Society and the **Maritime Administration (Flag State)** before construction. - **Damage Stability**: The arrangement directly affects subdivision index (SOLAS II-1/4-1, Probabilistic Damage Stability). - **Lifecycle Operating Cost (LOC)**: Poor arrangement leads to increased maintenance hours, fuel consumption (longer pipe runs), and crew fatigue. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Double Bottom Height in Machinery Spaces **Statement (SOLAS Reg II-1/11.2):** The double bottom in a machinery space of Category A shall have a height of not less than **B/20** or **2.0 m**, whichever is less. ### Derivation Logic (Why "B/20"?) The B/20 rule emerges from empirical damage stability studies relating bilge flooding volume to residual freeboard. We can express the **double bottom volume** as: $$ V_{db} = L_{db} \times B \times h_{db} $$ where $L_{db}$ is the length of the double bottom region. The flooding volume upon tank-top penetration is: $$ V_{flood} = L_{db} \times B \times h_{db} $$ This volume must be limited to prevent excessive free surface effect. Setting $h_{db} = B/20$ gives: $$ \begin{aligned} h_{db} &= \dfrac{B}{20} \\ \Rightarrow V_{flood} &= L_{db} \times B \times \dfrac{B}{20} \\ &= \dfrac{L_{db} \times B^{2}}{20} \end{aligned} $$ > The 2 m cap is introduced to ensure **headroom** for maintenance access in the tank top region (a person can crouch and work). Hence the final rule: $$ \boxed{\,h_{db} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{B}{20},\ 2.0\ \text{m}\right)\,} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bilge Pipe Diameter (Hakason's Approximation) For a self-priming bilge pump, the **discharge velocity** must exceed a critical value to clear air-locks. The Hakason-type empirical formula for the internal diameter of a bilge main is: $$ d = 1.68\sqrt{A}\ +\ 25 $$ where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the compartment served (in m²), and $d$ is in mm. **Example**: For a compartment of $A = 25$ m²: $$ \begin{aligned} d &= 1.68 \times \sqrt{25} + 25 \\ &= 1.68 \times 5.0 + 25 \\ &= 8.4 + 25 \\ &= 33.4\ \text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ > Practically, the next standard pipe size (e.g., **40 mm** or **50 mm** depending on the rule set) is selected. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Manning Density for Accommodation The minimum cabin area for a single-berth crew cabin per **MLC 2006 / ILO Convention**: $$ A_{cabin} \ge 4.5\ \text{m}^{2} \quad \text{(single occupancy)} $$ For a multi-berth cabin (max 4 persons): $$ A_{cabin} \ge 4.5\ \text{m}^{2} + 1.5\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{extra person} $$ Thus for 4 berths: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{cabin,4} &\ge 4.5 + 3 \times 1.5 \\ &= 4.5 + 4.5 \\ &= 9.0\ \text{m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Manning density check:** $$ \rho_{m} = \dfrac{N_{crew}}{A_{accom}} $$ For $N_{crew} = 30$ and $A_{accom} = 450$ m²: $$ \rho_m = \dfrac{30}{450} = 0.067\ \text{persons/m}^{2} $$ > This is well above the lower habitability limit (typically $0.04$ persons/m²), indicating adequate space. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Class Selection Use the following decision matrix to select the required fire integrity class for any bulkhead or deck: | Zone A ↓ / Zone B → | Cat A ER | Other Mach. | Accommodation | Service | Cargo | Control Station | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Cat A Engine Room** | A-0 | A-0 | **A-60** | A-30 | A-60 | A-60 | | **Other Machinery** | A-0 | A-0 | A-30 | A-0 | A-0 | A-30 | | **Accommodation** | A-60 | A-30 | C (low risk) | A-0/B-0 | A-60 | A-30 | | **Service** | A-30 | A-0 | B-0 | C | A-30 | A-0 | | **Cargo** | A-60 | A-0 | A-60 | A-30 | A-0 | A-60 | | **Control Station** | A-60 | A-30 | A-30 | A-0 | A-60 | A-30 | > [!TIP] > **Read the matrix as: "If Zone A and Zone B share a boundary, what class of division is required?"** ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Ventilation Duct Sizing (Machinery Space) The ventilation flow requirement is typically based on **air changes per hour**: $$ Q_{vent} = V_{ER} \times n $$ where $Q_{vent}$ = volumetric flow (m³/h), $V_{ER}$ = machinery space volume (m³), $n$ = air changes per hour (typically **15–30** for ER). Duct cross-sectional area: $$ A_{duct} = \dfrac{Q_{vent}}{3600 \times v_{air}} $$ with $v_{air} \approx 8$–$10$ m/s in main ducts. **Example**: $V_{ER} = 1200$ m³, $n = 20$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{vent} &= 1200 \times 20 = 24{,}000\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{h} \\ A_{duct} &= \dfrac{24{,}000}{3600 \times 9} = 0.74\ \text{m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ Equivalent circular duct diameter: $$ D_{duct} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{duct}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.74}{\pi}} \approx 0.97\ \text{m} \approx 1000\ \text{mm} $$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Zoning of Ship Spaces ```mermaid graph TD A0[Ship Internal Volume] --> A1[Machinery Spaces] A0 --> A2[Accommodation Spaces] A0 --> A3[Service Spaces] A0 --> A4[Cargo Spaces] A0 --> A5[Control Stations] A0 --> A6[Tunnels and Trunks] A1 --> B1[Category A Machinery Spaces] A1 --> B2[Other Machinery Spaces] A1 --> B3[Pump Rooms] B1 --> C1[Main Engine Room] B1 --> C2[Boiler Room] B1 --> C3[Auxiliary Engine Room] B1 --> C4[Inert Gas Generator Room] A2 --> D1[Crew Cabins] A2 --> D2[Officer Cabins] A2 --> D3[Public Spaces] A2 --> D4[Hospital] A2 --> D5[Wheelhouse] A3 --> E1[Galley and Pantry] A3 --> E2[Laundry] A3 --> E3[Stores] A3 --> E4[Workshops] A5 --> F1[Navigation Bridge] A5 --> F2[Engine Control Room ECR] A5 --> F3[Emergency Switchboard Room] A5 --> F4[Fire Control Station] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Functional Architecture of Machinery Spaces ```mermaid graph LR Intake[Sea Intake Chest] --> S1[Sea Chest Strainer] S1 --> S2[Main Sea Water Pump] S2 --> S3[Main Engine Coolers] S3 --> S4[Outlet Discharge] Fuel[Oil Fuel Bunker Tank] --> P1[Oil Fuel Purifier] P1 --> P2[Service Tank] P2 --> P3[Main Engine] P3 --> GE[Gearbox] GE --> SH[Shaft Line] SH --> PR[Propeller] Aux[Auxiliary Generator] --> SW[Switchboard] SW --> ECR[Engine Control Room] ECR --> BM[Bridge] Fire[Fire Detection Panel] --> F1[CO2 System] Fire --> F2[Water Mist System] Fire --> F3[FFFA System] Esc[Escape Trunk] --> ST[Safety Stairway] ST --> Deck[Open Deck] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cross-Sectional View (ER Vertical Levels) ```mermaid graph TD L1[Upper Platform - Casing Level
Controls, Up-take, Silencers] L2[Cylinder Top Level
Main Engine Cylinders, Turbochargers] L3[Main Floor Level
Crankshaft, Bedplate, Thrust Bearing] L4[Lower Platform / Pit
Oil Coolers, LO Purifiers, Bilges] L5[Double Bottom
Oil Fuel Tanks, LO Storage, FW Tanks] L6[Shaft Tunnel
Propeller Shaft, Tunnel Tanks, Escape Trunk] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L3 --> L6 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fire Integrity Zoning Around ER ```mermaid graph TD A[Main Engine Room
Category A]:::mach B[Accommodation Block]:::accom C[Galley]:::service D[Cargo Hold]:::cargo E[Steering Gear Room]:::othermach F[Emergency Generator Room]:::control A ---|A-60 Bulkhead| B A ---|A-30 Bulkhead| C A ---|A-60 Bulkhead| D A ---|A-0 Bulkhead| E A ---|A-60 Bulkhead| F classDef mach fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px classDef accom fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px classDef service fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px classDef cargo fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px classDef othermach fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:2px classDef control fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** ### Question 1 **Define "Machinery Space of Category A" as per SOLAS. Why is it treated as the highest fire-risk zone on a ship?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** As per **SOLAS Chapter II-1 Regulation 3.21**, a Machinery Space of Category A is a space and trunks to such spaces containing: 1. Internal combustion machinery used for main propulsion OR other purposes with aggregate power output of **not less than 375 kW**, OR 2. Any **oil-fired boiler** or **oil-fired internal combustion machinery**, OR 3. Any **oil-fired equipment** (e.g., inert gas generator, incinerator) other than boilers.
