It is treated as the **highest fire-risk zone** because of the simultaneous presence of: - **High-temperature surfaces** (boiler flues, exhaust manifolds), - **Ignitable liquid fuels** (HFO, MDO, lube oil), - **Possible oil leakages** from pumps and pipes, - **Confined space** with limited escape routes. > **[Mark Distribution]: 1 mark — definition, 1 mark — examples, 1 mark — justification of risk.** --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** ### Question 2 **State any three SOLAS requirements for separating machinery spaces from accommodation spaces.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Fire Integrity**: The bulkhead or deck separating a Category A machinery space from accommodation must be of class **A-60** (SOLAS Reg II-2/4.2.2). 2. **No Direct Openings**: Openings in A-class divisions shall be fitted with **self-closing fire doors** (SOLAS Reg II-2/9.4). 3. **Ventilation Ducts**: All ventilation ducts passing through the boundary must be fitted with **fire dampers** that close automatically within **30 seconds** of fire detection (SOLAS Reg II-2/9.7). 4. **Means of Escape**: At least **two escape routes** must be provided, widely separated, with one leading to a stairway enclosure of A-class integrity (SOLAS Reg II-2/13). > **[Mark Distribution]: 1 mark per requirement, 3 of the above required.** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 2 Internal Choice]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Describe the **vertical zoning of a typical main engine room** of a motor ship. List the equipment located on each level with justification. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* With a neat sketch (block diagram), explain the **fire integrity zoning** of spaces around a Category A machinery space. State the SOLAS rule references. #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] The main engine room of a motor ship (diesel-driven) is typically arranged on **five vertical levels**, each dedicated to specific functions: | Level | Equipment | Justification | |---|---|---| | **1. Top Platform (Casing Level)** | Main engine controls, exhaust uptakes, funnels, silencers | Easy access for operation, observation of exhaust smoke, ventilation exhaust | | **2. Cylinder Top Level (Upper Platform)** | Engine cylinders, exhaust manifold, turbochargers, starting air receivers, governor | Operational access for adjustment, valve maintenance, gauging | | **3. Main Floor (Centerline Level)** | Crankshaft, bedplate, thrust bearing, turning gear, control console | Central spine of the engine; convenient access for major overhaul | | **4. Lower Platform (Pit Level)** | Lube oil coolers, lube oil purifiers, fuel oil purifiers, bilge wells | Gravity feed of oil to engine; service at lower elevation | | **5. Double Bottom (Tank Top)** | HFO settling tank, HFO service tank, lube oil storage, fresh water tank, sewage tank | Storage of consumables below working floor for stability and centre of gravity | > **[Mark Distribution]: 7 × 1 mark for correct identification of levels + equipment; 2 marks for coherent justification.** #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] A **block diagram** of fire integrity zoning around a Category A machinery space is shown below: ``` ┌──────────────────────────────┐ │ Wheelhouse (Control Station)│ ← A-60 from ER └──────────────┬───────────────┘ │ ┌──────────────┴───────────────┐ │ Accommodation Block │ ← A-60 from ER │ (Cabins, Mess, Hospital) │ └──────────────┬───────────────┘ │ ┌──────────────┴───────────────┐ ← A-30 bulkhead │ Service Spaces (Galley, etc.)│ └──────────────┬───────────────┘ │ ┌───────────────────┴───────────────────┐ │ MAIN ENGINE ROOM (Category A) │ ← Highest Risk │ + Auxiliary Engine Room │ └─┬─────────────────┬─────────────┬──────┘ │ │ │ ↓ ↓ ↓ Shaft Cargo Steering Tunnel Hold Gear Room (A-0) (A-60) (A-0) ``` **SOLAS Rule References:** - **SOLAS Reg II-2/4.2.2** — Fire integrity of bulkheads and decks. - **SOLAS Reg II-2/9** — Ventilation systems and fire dampers. - **SOLAS Reg II-2/13** — Means of escape from machinery spaces. - **SOLAS Reg II-1/26** — Bilge pumping in machinery spaces. | Boundary | Fire Class | |---|---| | ER ↔ Accommodation | A-60 | | ER ↔ Service Space | A-30 | | ER ↔ Cargo (tanker) | A-60 | | ER ↔ Other Machinery | A-0 | | ER ↔ Control Station | A-60 | > **[Mark Distribution]: 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for boundary class table, 2 marks for SOLAS references.** --- ### Question B (Alternative) (14 Marks) **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **design considerations for locating the main machinery space** in a ship. Why is it placed aft in most modern vessels? **(b)** *(7 Marks)* Discuss the **arrangement of accommodation spaces** in a cargo ship with reference to SOLAS fire integrity, MLC 2006 habitability, and noise control. #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] The location of the main machinery space (MMS) is a strategic decision governed by: 1. **Propeller Shaft Alignment**: - A short, direct shaft reduces alignment problems, vibration, and weight. - **Aft placement** minimizes shaft length and number of intermediate bearings. 2. **Vibration and Noise**: - The accommodation block is placed **forward**, away from the noise and vibration of the engine. - The **forward engine room** layout (common in naval vessels and some LNG carriers) sacrifices shaft length for better damage survivability. 3. **Trim and Stability**: - Heavy machinery concentrated aft **balances cargo** placed forward. - Reduces pitching moment in heavy seas. 4. **Reserve Buoyancy**: - Aft machinery ships usually retain the **forward collision bulkhead** intact, improving damage stability. 5. **Cargo Flexibility**: - With machinery aft, the **cargo hold length** is maximized, allowing flexible stowage of varying cargo volumes. 6. **Maintenance Access**: - Aft engine rooms have a clear hull side for sea chest and overboard discharges. - Forward engine rooms often suffer from cramped access. > **Justification for Aft Placement (Summary)**: 1 mark each for 4–5 of the above reasons; 1 mark for the **summary statement** "Aft placement optimizes shaft alignment, cargo volume, and crew habitability." #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] The **accommodation block** is arranged on a single superstructure or multiple decks, typically **forward** (cargo ships) or **midship** (passenger ships). **SOLAS Fire Integrity (Reg II-2/4.2.2):** - Bulkheads and decks within the accommodation block must be of **B-class or C-class** divisions. - The boundary to the engine room must be **A-60**. - Stairway enclosures must be **A-30** at minimum. **MLC 2006 Habitability Requirements:** - **Single-occupancy cabins**: ≥ 4.5 m² floor area. - **Recreation space**: Open deck or enclosed lounge. - **Mess rooms**: 1 m² per seat, minimum 16 seats for a 30-person crew. - **Hospital**: 1 bed per 100 crew (minimum 1 bed for < 100 crew). - **Sanitary facilities**: 1 WC, 1 wash basin, 1 shower per 6 persons (max). **Noise Control (ISO 6954 / MSC.337(91)):** - Cabins: ≤ 60 dB(A). - Mess rooms: ≤ 65 dB(A). - Achieved by **floating floor** (resilient mounts), **acoustic insulation** in bulkheads, and **resilient mounts** on machinery foundations. > **[Mark Distribution]: 2 marks — fire integrity, 3 marks — MLC habitability (with calculations possible), 2 marks — noise control.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**: > - **Common Mistake 1**: Students often write "**A-30**" for the engine room ↔ accommodation boundary. The correct value is **A-60** (highest class). Marks deducted: 1–2. > - **Common Mistake 2**: Confusing "**Machinery Space of Category A**" with "**Other Machinery Spaces**" (e.g., steering gear room). Only the former contains oil-fired boilers or large IC engines. > - **Common Mistake 3**: Forgetting to mention **escape trunks / stairway integrity** (A-class stairways) when discussing accommodation. > - **Common Mistake 4**: Writing "**double bottom in ER is 2 m**" without quoting the full rule: $h_{db} = \min(B/20, 2.0\ \text{m})$. > - **Common Mistake 5**: Failing to differentiate between **means of escape** (SOLAS II-2/13) and **fire integrity** (SOLAS II-2/4). These are different regulations — marks are awarded separately. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Reg 3.21** defines **Machinery Space of Category A** (375 kW threshold, oil-fired boilers). - [ ] **A-60 fire integrity** is the standard between Cat A machinery and accommodation. - [ ] **600 mm minimum** for escape routes and cofferdams. - [ ] **Double bottom height** in ER: $h_{db} = \min(B/20, 2.0\ \text{m})$. - [ ] **Vertical levels** of ER: Top casing → Cylinder top → Main floor → Pit → Double bottom → Shaft tunnel. - [ ] **Aft engine room** for cargo ships; forward for tankers and some naval vessels. - [ ] **B-class and C-class** divisions for internal accommodation; A-class only for hazardous boundaries. - [ ] **Two escape routes** from any machinery space, widely separated. - [ ] **Ventilation fire dampers** close within **30 s** of detection. - [ ] **MLC 2006 cabin area**: ≥ 4.5 m² single occupancy; +1.5 m² per additional berth. - [ ] **ISO 6954 noise limits**: 60 dB(A) cabins, 65 dB(A) mess, 90 dB(A) manned ER. - [ ] **Bilge pump capacity** must clear a flooded compartment in 3 hours. - [ ] **Pump rooms** are treated as **special category** on tankers. - [ ] **Cofferdams** are required between oil fuel tanks and heated surfaces (boilers, exhaust uptakes). - [ ] **UMS (Unmanned Machinery Space)** requirements mandate **automatic fire detection**, **remote shutdown**, and **machinery alarm systems**. - [ ] **Classification society approval** of GA drawings is mandatory before steel cutting. - [ ] **Sliding watertight doors** in subdivision bulkheads must have **local + remote + hydraulic** operation per SOLAS Reg II-1/15. - [ ] **Steering gear compartment** is classified as **"Other Machinery Space"** (not Category A). - [ ] **Emergency generator room** is a **control station**; A-60 boundary from ER. - [ ] **Wheelhouse/bridge** is a control station and requires **unobstructed 360° visibility**. > **Final Examiner's Note**: For KTU 2024 Module 2, the **most expected 14-mark question pattern** is the "**General Arrangement of ER + Fire Integrity Zoning**" combo. Memorize the **boundary matrix** and the **five ER levels** — these are guaranteed scoring topics. ---

topics.title: Tanks and Other Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tanks and Other Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Tanks and Other Spaces (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In ship design, **tanks** are enclosed, watertight or oiltight compartments constructed within the hull structure of a vessel for the purpose of storing liquids (water, fuel, oil) or, occasionally, dry cargo. **Other spaces** refer to auxiliary compartments that are neither intended for human habitation nor cargo stowage, such as cofferdams, voids, chain lockers, and bosun's stores.

According to SOLAS and Classification Society Rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS), tanks are broadly categorized as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tank Classification (KTU High-Yield Concept)**
>
> 1. **Structural (Integral) Tanks** — Formed by the ship's main structural plating (shell, deck, bulkheads, double bottom) without using separate independent liners.
> 2. **Independent Tanks** — Self-supporting tanks that do not form part of the ship's hull structure (used primarily for liquefied gas carriers).
>
> For a Naval Architect designing conventional vessels (bulk carriers, tankers, container ships), **structural/integral tanks** are the predominant type.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ship as a Layered Building"

Imagine a modern multi-storey building where every floor, every wall cavity, and every basement is *engineered to hold water, fuel, or air on demand*. A ship is exactly this — a hollow steel "building" floating on water, where almost every empty corner is intentionally designed to store a specific liquid at a specific pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Why so many tanks?**
>
> Just as a hospital has separate sterile rooms for blood, oxygen, and waste — *never mixed*, never cross-contaminated — a ship segregates:
> - **Salt water** (ballast — to balance the ship when empty)
> - **Fresh water** (drinking & wash water)
> - **Fuel oil** (engine feed)
> - **Lubricating oil** (machinery lubrication)
> - **Sewage & sludge** (waste)
>
> Cross-contamination between, say, fuel oil and ballast water is **illegal under MARPOL** (the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships). Hence, **cofferdams** (empty spaces) act as the "buffer walls" between hazardous zones.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- **Minimum sounding pipe diameter:** **50 mm** (DNV / Lloyd's standard)
- **Minimum tank venting cross-sectional area:** Generally the largest filling/emptying pipe diameter, but never less than the area of a pipe whose diameter is **~50–65 mm**.
- **Standard sounding scale calibration:** Based on **trim correction tables** and **ullage measurement**.
- **Minimum tank height for double bottom:** **750 mm** for new bulk carriers (IACS S21) — though ballast double bottom heights vary **600 mm to 1500 mm** typically.
- **Cofferdam minimum width:** **760 mm** (SOLAS Reg. II-1/11.3) to allow access and inspection.

## 1.4 Types of Tanks Encountered in Ship Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Master List — Tank Types (Memorize for KTU Exams)**
>
> | Category | Tank Type | Typical Use |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Ballast** | Double Bottom (DB) Ballast Tank | Trim/list correction, stability when unladen |
> | | Forepeak Tank | Trim adjustment, slamming protection |
> | | Aftpeak Tank | Stern trim, propeller immersion |
> | | Wing/Side Ballast Tank | Reducing GM, rolling period control |
> | | Topside Tank | Reducing free surface effect of DB tanks |
> | **Fuel/Oil** | Fuel Oil (FO) Bunker Tank | Main engine fuel storage |
> | | Diesel Oil (DO) Tank | Generator/DG fuel |
> | | Lubricating Oil Tank | Engine LO supply |
> | | Settling/Service Tank | Fuel purification |
> | | Sludge Tank | Waste oil residue storage |
> | **Water** | Fresh Water (FW) Tank | Domestic/technical water |
> | | Potable Water Tank | Drinking water |
> | | Boiler Feed Water Tank | Steam plant supply |
> | | Bilge Tank | Waste water collection |
> | **Cargo (Tankers)** | Cargo Tank (Slop/Independent) | Oil/chemical cargo |
> | **Other Spaces** | Cofferdam | Buffer separation |
> | | Void Space | Empty space, buoyancy reserve |
> | | Chain Locker | Anchor chain stowage |
> | | Bosun's Store | Deck equipment storage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural (Integral) Tanks — Design Logic

Structural tanks are formed by the ship's existing structural members. When a tank boundary coincides with the shell plating, deck, or bulkhead, those plating elements serve as the tank walls.

### Design Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Identify the structural boundary** — the shell plate, deck plate, or transverse bulkhead forms the wall.
2. **Apply internal framing** — additional stiffeners (floors, webs, brackets) are fitted to resist **hydraulic pressure** from the contained liquid.
3. **Calculate the design head** — usually taken from the tank top or sounding level to the **top of the vent**, or **2.4 m above the tank top** for DB tanks (whichever is greater).
4. **Apply classification society scantlings** — Plate thickness, stiffener spacing and modulus are determined per DNV Pt.3 Ch.4 or Lloyd's Rules.
5. **Check slamming/wheel impact loads** for tanks at forward regions.
6. **Verify tightness** via hydrostatic or air tests (KTU practical: **tank testing at 1.25 × design head for 30 minutes**).

## 2.2 Cofferdams — The Critical "Empty Wall"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cofferdams?**
>
> - Required by **SOLAS Reg. II-1/11** between oil fuel tanks and machinery spaces, or between cargo oil tanks and freshwater tanks.
> - Ensures that **leakage in one tank cannot migrate to another hazardous tank**.
> - Minimum width of **760 mm** for access; must be **vented independently to the open deck** with no valves.
> - Often provided with a **leakage detection system** (drain to bilge well with alarm).

### Why the "How" Behind Cofferdams

Without cofferdams, a slow leak from a fuel oil tank could:
- Contaminate fresh water (health hazard to crew),
- Mix with cargo oil (MARPOL violation),
- Reach a heated surface (fire hazard — **the "hot zone" / "cold zone" concept in oil tanker design**).

A cofferdam acts as a **physical and atmospheric barrier**, vented separately so that any vapor cannot accumulate.

## 2.3 Double Bottom Tanks — The Most Volumetric Tank

The double bottom extends across the full breadth and length of the ship (between the **inner bottom plating** and the **bottom shell plating**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Pressure Head for DB Tanks**
>
> $$ h_{design} = h_{ullage} + h_{freeboard} $$
>
> where the absolute minimum is **2.4 m above the tank top** for vessels under IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR).

### Advantages of DB Tanks
- Provide **distributed ballast** across the ship's length (reduces concentrated stress).
- Offer **collision protection** — acts as a buffer before flooding reaches cargo holds.
- Supply **reserve buoyancy** in damaged condition.

### Disadvantages
- **Free Surface Effect (FSE)** — large horizontal liquid surface significantly reduces **GM**.
- **Sludge accumulation** in the corners (maintenance issue).

## 2.4 Forepeak & Aftpeak Tanks

| Feature | Forepeak Tank | Aftpeak Tank |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Forward of collision bulkhead | Aft of stern tube / engine room |
| Primary Use | Trim adjustment, head seawater | Trim adjustment, propeller immersion |
| Secondary Use | Ballast, stores, emergency | Steering gear compartment, freshwater |
| Sounding | Sounding pipe in chain locker area or via electronic sensor | Sounding pipe near engine room bulkhead |

> [!WARNING]
> **Forepeak tank heads must include slamming load allowance** — wave impact at the bow can produce dynamic pressures up to **3× the static hydrostatic head**. KTU examiners frequently test this.

## 2.5 Deep Tanks

Deep tanks are tanks that extend from the inner bottom (or shell) up to a deck or tank top, often **two or more decks in height**. They are used when large capacity is required in a small footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Surface Effect Reduction in Deep Tanks**
>
> Deep tanks have **small free surface area relative to volume** compared to a flat DB tank — making them **less damaging to GM** when partially filled.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Tank Capacity & Calibration

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAMS**
>
> | # | Formula | Description | Units |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | $V = \int A(h) \, dh$ | Continuous tank volume by integration of horizontal cross-section vs height | m³ |
> | 2 | $V = \dfrac{h}{3}\left[A_1 + 4(A_2+A_4+\dots) + 2(A_3+A_5+\dots) + A_n\right]$ | **Simpson's 1st Rule** for equally-spaced ordinates (even number of strips) | m³ |
> | 3 | $V = \dfrac{h}{3}\left[(A_1+A_n) + 4\sum A_{even} + 2\sum A_{odd}\right]$ | **Simpson's 1st Rule** (compact notation) | m³ |
> | 4 | $V = \left[\dfrac{2h}{3}\right]\left[2A_1 + A_2 + A_3\right]$ | **Simpson's 2nd Rule (3 ordinates)** | m³ |
> | 5 | $V = h \cdot \left[\dfrac{A_1 + A_n}{2} + A_2 + A_3 + \dots + A_{n-1}\right]$ | **Trapezoidal Rule** (simpler but less accurate) | m³ |
> | 6 | $V_{corrected} = V_{sounding} + \Delta V_{trim}$ | Trim correction for sounding volume | m³ |
> | 7 | $p = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa (N/m²) |
> | 8 | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot k}{2 \sigma_{allow}}$ | Minimum plate thickness for tank boundary (Rule formulation) | mm |
> | 9 | $L_{eff} = L - 0.5 l$ | Effective span between supports (stiffener length) | m |
> | 10 | $I_{min} = \dfrac{L_{eff} \cdot s \cdot p \cdot k}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Minimum section modulus of stiffener | cm³ |

### Where these are used in real engineering
- **Formulas 2, 3, 4, 5** → Used in **tank calibration tables (Capacity Plan / Soundings)** for every commercial ship — mandatory for MARPOL compliance and bunker delivery notes.
- **Formulas 7, 8, 9, 10** → Used in **structural drawing offices** to derive scantlings submitted to classification societies.

## 2.7 Tank Sounding & Venting — Operational Logic

### Sounding
The sounding system measures the **depth of liquid** in a tank. Two main methods:
- **Manual sounding** — A graduated rod or sounding tape lowered through a sounding pipe.
- **Electronic sounding** — Capacitive or float-type sensors giving real-time digital readout.

### Venting
Each tank must be vented to atmosphere to prevent **vacuum collapse** during discharge and **overpressure** during filling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Venting Design Rule of Thumb**
>
> Cross-sectional area of vent $\geq$ Cross-sectional area of largest filling pipe.

For fuel oil tanks, vents must terminate **atmosphere-side above the weather deck**, fitted with **goosenecks and flame arrestors**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Tank Volume Using Simpson's 1st Rule

### Scenario
A rectangular double-bottom tank has horizontal cross-sectional areas measured at **5 evenly-spaced water levels** above the bottom. The water level is sampled at ordinates $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4, h_5$, each spaced a vertical distance $h$ apart. Calculate the total volume of the tank up to water level at the 5th ordinate.

The cross-sectional areas at each level are $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, A_5$.

### Step 1 — Establish the Integration Foundation

The fundamental definition of volume between two parallel cross-sections separated by an infinitesimal height $dh$ is:

$$
dV = A(h) \, dh
$$

This means the total volume from level 1 to level $n$ is:

$$
V = \int_{h_1}^{h_n} A(h) \, dh
$$

### Step 2 — Approximate $A(h)$ with a Quadratic Polynomial

Simpson's 1st Rule approximates the area function by a **parabolic curve** passing through three consecutive ordinates. For $n$ ordinates (where $n$ is odd), there are $\dfrac{n-1}{2}$ parabolic segments.

For any one such parabolic segment between ordinates $A_0, A_1, A_2$ spaced by $h$:

$$
A(x) = A_0 + \frac{A_1 - A_0}{h}x + \frac{A_2 - 2A_1 + A_0}{2h^2}x(x - h)
$$

### Step 3 — Integrate the Parabolic Segment

$$
V_{segment} = \int_{0}^{2h} A(x) \, dx
$$

Evaluating the integral term by term:

$$
V_{segment} = \int_{0}^{2h} A_0 \, dx + \frac{A_1 - A_0}{h}\int_{0}^{2h} x \, dx + \frac{A_2 - 2A_1 + A_0}{2h^2}\int_{0}^{2h} x(x-h) \, dx
$$

Computing each integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{2h} A_0 \, dx = 2h \cdot A_0
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{2h} x \, dx = \frac{(2h)^2}{2} = 2h^2
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{2h} x(x-h) \, dx = \int_{0}^{2h} (x^2 - hx) \, dx = \frac{8h^3}{3} - \frac{4h^3}{2} = \frac{8h^3 - 6h^3}{3} = \frac{2h^3}{3}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$
V_{segment} = 2h A_0 + \frac{A_1 - A_0}{h} \cdot 2h^2 + \frac{A_2 - 2A_1 + A_0}{2h^2} \cdot \frac{2h^3}{3}
$$

$$
V_{segment} = 2h A_0 + 2h(A_1 - A_0) + \frac{h}{3}(A_2 - 2A_1 + A_0)
$$

$$
V_{segment} = 2h A_0 + 2h A_1 - 2h A_0 + \frac{h}{3} A_2 - \frac{2h}{3} A_1 + \frac{h}{3} A_0
$$

Cancelling $2h A_0$ terms:

$$
V_{segment} = 2h A_1 + \frac{h}{3} A_2 - \frac{2h}{3} A_1 + \frac{h}{3} A_0
$$

Combining $A_1$ coefficients: $2h - \dfrac{2h}{3} = \dfrac{4h}{3}$

$$
V_{segment} = \frac{h}{3}A_0 + \frac{4h}{3}A_1 + \frac{h}{3}A_2
$$

$$
V_{segment} = \frac{h}{3}(A_0 + 4A_1 + A_2)
$$

### Step 5 — Sum All Segments

For $n$ ordinates with even number of strips, total volume:

$$
V = \frac{h}{3}\left[A_1 + 4A_2 + 2A_3 + 4A_4 + 2A_5 + \dots + 4A_{n-1} + A_n\right]
$$

This is **Simpson's 1st Rule**, which has a **truncation error of order $h^4$** — extremely accurate for smoothly varying tank cross-sections.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Tank Capacity Calculation

**Given:**
A freshwater tank in a small vessel has cross-sectional areas at equal vertical intervals of $h = 0.4 \, \text{m}$ as follows:

| Ordinate (i) | 1 (Bottom) | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 (Top) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A_i$ (m²) | 8.0 | 9.5 | 10.2 | 9.8 | 8.4 |

**Find:** Tank volume.

### Step-by-Step Application

Using Simpson's 1st Rule (here, $n=5$, an odd number — valid):

$$
V = \frac{h}{3}\left[A_1 + 4(A_2 + A_4) + 2(A_3) + A_5\right]
$$

Substituting:

$$
V = \frac{0.4}{3}\left[8.0 + 4(9.5 + 9.8) + 2(10.2) + 8.4\right]
$$

$$
V = \frac{0.4}{3}\left[8.0 + 4(19.3) + 20.4 + 8.4\right]
$$

$$
V = \frac{0.4}{3}\left[8.0 + 77.2 + 20.4 + 8.4\right]
$$

$$
V = \frac{0.4}{3}\left[114.0\right]
$$

$$
V = \frac{45.6}{3} = 15.2 \, \text{m}^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU:** For volumes expressed in **litres** (often required for bunker tanks), multiply by 1000: $V = 15{,}200 \, \text{litres}$.

## 3.3 Trim Correction (Ullage Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why trim matters:** When the ship is not level (trim), the liquid surface is not horizontal in the tank's reference frame. Sounding readings must therefore be corrected using the trim correction table supplied in the **Capacity Plan / Trim and Stability Booklet**.

The trim correction $\Delta V_{trim}$ is added to (or subtracted from) the volume calculated at level trim:

$$
V_{actual} = V_{level} + \Delta V_{trim}
$$

For a tank of length $L_{tk}$, trim $\Delta T$ (forward or aft), and cross-sectional area $A$ near the sounding point:

$$
\Delta V_{trim} \approx \pm \frac{A \cdot \Delta T \cdot x}{2 L_{tk}}
$$

where $x$ is the longitudinal distance of the sounding pipe from midships.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Tank & Space Classification (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Vessels Internal Volume] --> B[Tanks]
    A --> C[Other Spaces]
    A --> D[Accommodation]
    A --> E[Cargo Holds]

    B --> F[Ballast Tanks]
    B --> G[Fuel Oil Tanks]
    B --> H[Fresh Water Tanks]
    B --> I[Sludge Bilge Tanks]

    F --> F1[Double Bottom]
    F --> F2[Forepeak]
    F --> F3[Aftpeak]
    F --> F4[Wing Side]
    F --> F5[Topside]

    G --> G1[FO Bunker Tank]
    G --> G2[DO Service Tank]
    G --> G3[LO Tank]
    G --> G4[Settling Tank]

    H --> H1[Domestic FW Tank]
    H --> H2[Technical FW Tank]
    H --> H3[Boiler Feed Tank]

    I --> I1[Bilge Tank]
    I --> I2[Sludge Tank]
    I --> I3[Sewage Tank]

    C --> J[Cofferdam]
    C --> K[Void Space]
    C --> L[Chain Locker]
    C --> M[Bosun Store]
    C --> N[Steering Gear Room]

    J --> J1[Between Oil and FW]
    J --> J2[Between Cargo and Machinery]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Tank Sounding & Venting System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TankCompartment[Tank Compartment]
        T1[Liquid Stored]
    end

    T1 --> SP[Sounding Pipe 50 mm dia]
    T1 --> VT[Vent Pipe with Flame Arrestor]
    T1 --> FP[Filling Pipe]
    T1 --> DP[Discharge Pump and Pipe]

    SP --> SG[Sounding Gauge Manual]
    VT --> AD[Atmosphere Above Weather Deck]
    FP --> MN[Manifold at Bunker Station]
    DP --> ST[Service Tank or Sea]

    SP -.-> ES[Electronic Sensor Optional]
    ES --> DC[Digital Display on Bridge]
```

## 4.3 Cofferdam Cross-Sectional Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EngineRoomSide[Engine Room Side]
        ER[Engine Room Hot Zone]
    end

    subgraph CofferdamZone[Cofferdam]
        CD1[Plate Boundary 1]
        AIR[Air Gap Min 760 mm]
        LV[Leakage Detection Drain]
        CD2[Plate Boundary 2]
    end

    subgraph OilFuelSide[Oil Fuel Tank Side]
        FT[FO Tank Cold Zone]
    end

    ER --- CD1
    CD1 --- AIR
    AIR --- LV
    LV --- CD2
    CD2 --- FT

    AIR -.-> V1[Independent Vent to Open Deck]
    LV -.-> V2[Drain to Bilge Well with Alarm]
```

## 4.4 Tank Capacity Calculation Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST[Step 1: Measure Cross Sectional Areas at Equal Spacings] --> SP[Step 2: Identify Number of Ordinates]
    SP --> CH{Count is Odd}
    CH -->|Yes| SR[Step 3: Apply Simpson 1st Rule]
    CH -->|No| TR[Step 4: Apply Trapezoidal Rule or Add One Ordinate]
    SR --> CAL[Step 5: Compute Volume in Cubic Metres]
    TR --> CAL
    CAL --> CO[Step 6: Apply Trim Correction if Ship is Trimmed]
    CO --> OUT[Step 7: Final Corrected Tank Volume in Capacity Plan]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a cofferdam and state its purpose on a ship. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A **cofferdam** is a narrow, empty, watertight and oiltight space formed by two parallel watertight steel bulkheads, located between two adjacent tanks or compartments carrying different fluids.

**Purposes:**
1. **Prevents cross-contamination** of liquids (e.g., fuel oil into freshwater tank).
2. **Acts as a thermal barrier** between heated machinery spaces and oil fuel tanks.
3. **Serves as a fire/explosion buffer**, since it is independently vented to the open deck and contains no valves.
4. **Provides an inspection zone** with leakage detection to alert the crew of any tank boundary failure.

> [!NOTE]
> Per SOLAS Reg. II-1/11, the minimum width of a cofferdam is **760 mm** when provided with access, and it must be **independently vented to the open deck with no valve fitted**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between structural (integral) tanks and independent tanks. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Structural (Integral) Tank | Independent Tank |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Formed by the ship's own hull plating | Self-supporting, separate from hull |
| **Structural Role** | Boundary plating contributes to hull strength | Does not contribute to hull strength |
| **Application** | Conventional cargo ships, tankers, bulk carriers | Liquefied gas carriers (LNG/LPG) |
| **Classification Pressure** | Hydrostatic head of contained liquid | Based on service (low temperature, etc.) |
| **Examples** | Double bottom ballast, FO bunker | Moss-type LNG tanks, Type A/B/C independent tanks |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [Option 1]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the various **types of ballast tanks** fitted on a bulk carrier with the aid of a sketch showing their location. Discuss the relative advantages and disadvantages of double bottom tanks versus wing ballast tanks with respect to stability, structural integrity, and free surface effect. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A bulk carrier typically incorporates the following **ballast tanks**:

1. **Double Bottom (DB) Ballast Tanks** — Span the full breadth and length between the inner bottom and bottom shell plating.
2. **Forepeak Tank** — Forward of the collision bulkhead.
3. **Aftpeak Tank** — Aft of the engine room bulkhead or shaft tunnel.
4. **Wing (Side) Ballast Tanks** — Located in the upper wing regions, typically above the turn of the bilge.
5. **Topside Ballast Tanks** — Located in the upper corners of cargo holds (on Capesize/Ore carriers).
6. **Hopper Side Tanks** — Sloped tanks adjacent to the lower hopper tanks in bulk carriers.

**Comparison: DB vs Wing Ballast Tanks**

| Aspect | DB Tank | Wing Tank |
|---|---|---|
| **Stability Impact** | Large free surface effect reduces GM significantly | Smaller free surface effect per unit volume |
| **Structural Integrity** | Distributes stress uniformly; provides collision protection | Highly stressed at the bilge; prone to cracking if not designed well |
| **Volume per Meter** | High (full breadth × length) | Moderate (limited to wing region) |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult to clean (sludge accumulation) | Easier to inspect from ballast hold adjacent |
| **Damage Stability** | Critical — flooding can collapse inner bottom if poorly designed | Critical — flooding can affect transverse stability |

**Conclusion:** Double bottom tanks are volumetrically superior but cause greater free surface effect. Wing tanks are preferred for **fine-tuning trim** when DB capacity is too sensitive. Modern Capesize bulk carriers often pair **DB + Wing + Topside tanks** to optimize both volume and stability margins.

---

**(b)** A freshwater tank in a ship has cross-sectional areas at six equal vertical ordinates spaced $h = 0.5 \, \text{m}$ apart. The areas (in m²) are:

$A_1 = 12.0,\ A_2 = 14.5,\ A_3 = 15.2,\ A_4 = 14.8,\ A_5 = 13.6,\ A_6 = 11.0$

Calculate the volume of the tank using **Simpson's 1st Rule**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 6$ (even number of ordinates — Simpson's 1st Rule applicable since $n-1 = 5$ strips is odd ✓)

**Formula:**

$$
V = \frac{h}{3}\left[A_1 + A_6 + 4(A_2 + A_4) + 2(A_3 + A_5)\right]
$$

**Step 1: Identify odd and even ordinates**

- Odd-indexed (1, 3, 5): $A_1, A_3, A_5$
- Even-indexed (2, 4): $A_2, A_4$
- End ordinates: $A_1, A_6$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$
V = \frac{0.5}{3}\left[12.0 + 11.0 + 4(14.5 + 14.8) + 2(15.2 + 13.6)\right]
$$

**Step 3: Simplify inside the brackets**

$$
= \frac{0.5}{3}\left[23.0 + 4(29.3) + 2(28.8)\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{0.5}{3}\left[23.0 + 117.2 + 57.6\right]
$$

$$
= \frac{0.5}{3}\left[197.8\right]
$$

**Step 4: Final multiplication**

$$
V = \frac{0.5 \times 197.8}{3} = \frac{98.9}{3} \approx 32.97 \, \text{m}^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** Tank volume $V \approx 32.97 \, \text{m}^3 = 32{,}970 \, \text{litres}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Option 2]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **design considerations for fuel oil bunker tanks** on a commercial vessel. Include mention of SOLAS regulations, structural layout, sounding, venting, and segregation requirements. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Fuel Oil Bunker Tanks — Design Considerations:**

1. **Location and Layout**
   - Traditionally located in **double bottom** or **forward/aft of engine room** in segregated spaces.
   - **SOLAS Reg. II-1/11.2** prohibits the carriage of fuel oil in the forepeak tank to protect against pollution from collision damage.
   - Modern vessels prefer **engine room-side tanks** to minimize pipe runs.

2. **Structural Scantlings**
   - Plate thickness derived from the design pressure head (sounding pipe top + ullage).
   - Reinforced by floors, web frames, and brackets.
   - All internal stiffeners aligned to ensure **complete drainage** during cleaning.

3. **Cofferdam Separation**
   - Any oil fuel tank adjacent to a freshwater tank must have a **cofferdam (min 760 mm)** in between (SOLAS Reg. II-1/11.3).
   - The cofferdam is independently vented and fitted with a leakage detector.

4. **Sounding Arrangements**
   - **Manual sounding pipe** of **50 mm minimum diameter** to a safe position on the weather deck.
   - **Electronic level sensor** for remote read-out at the engine control room.
   - **High-level alarm** at 95% filling to prevent overflow.

5. **Venting Arrangements**
   - **Gooseneck vent pipe** terminating on the weather deck with a **flame arrestor** (brass or stainless steel gauze).
   - Cross-sectional area $\geq$ largest filling pipe diameter.

6. **Piping and Valves**
   - **Quick-closing valve** (solenoid-operated) on all outlet pipes (SOLAS Reg. II-2).
   - **Self-priming pump** for transfer to service/settling tank.
   - All penetrations to be made through **doubled plates** or approved sleeves.

7. **Testing Requirements**
   - **Hydrostatic test at 1.25 × design head** for 30 minutes — no leakage, no permanent deflection.
   - **Air test** for tightness of welded seams.

---

**(b)** With the help of a **functional block diagram**, describe the **tank sounding and venting system** of a typical double bottom ballast tank. Explain why sounding pipes must extend to open decks. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Functional Block Diagram (described):**

```
[BALLAST TANK]  ──► Sounding Pipe (50 mm Ø, marked gauge rod)  ──► Open Deck Sounding Station
        │
        ├──► Vent Pipe with Flame Arrestor  ──► Atmosphere (above weather deck)
        │
        ├──► Filling Pipe from Sea Inlet (sea chest)  ──►  Ballast Pump ──► Discharge to Sea
        │
        └──► Connecting Pipe to Topside Tank (optional cross-over valve)
```

**Functions:**

- **Sounding pipe** carries the dipstick/rod from the open deck down to the tank bottom; allows crew to **measure the depth of liquid** without entering the tank.
- **Vent pipe** equalizes pressure during filling and emptying; prevents **vacuum collapse** (inward buckling of tank top) and **overpressure rupture**.

**Why sounding pipes must extend to open decks:**

1. **Operator safety** — The crew never needs to enter a confined space; all measurements are taken at the deck level.
2. **Vapor release** — Any vapor emerging from the sounding opening is safely discharged to atmosphere, away from ignition sources.
3. **Access for inspection** — Easy to clean blockages; can also serve as an **emergency vent** in case of overpressure.
4. **Compliance with SOLAS** — Regulation requires all sounding arrangements for oil fuel and ballast tanks to be accessible at the open deck for safety and pollution prevention.

> [!NOTE]
> Sounding pipes for fuel oil tanks must terminate at a **safe position** (away from sources of ignition, at least **1.5 m** from any opening into accommodation spaces per SOLAS).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Applying Simpson's 1st Rule to an even number of ordinates** — This is mathematically invalid; the rule requires an **odd number of ordinates** (even number of strips).
> 2. **Confusing cofferdams with void spaces** — A void space is an *empty* structural compartment for buoyancy/access; a cofferdam has a *specific safety segregation function*.
> 3. **Forgetting the 2.4 m minimum head rule** for DB tanks under IACS CSR — many students use only the sounding head.
> 4. **Mixing up sounding vs ullage** — **Sounding** = measured *depth* of liquid; **Ullage** = empty *space* above the liquid.
> 5. **Ignoring trim correction** in sounding tables — examiners explicitly test this in Part B numericals.
> 6. **Sounding pipe diameter** — Students often write 25 mm or 38 mm; the correct minimum is **50 mm** per IACS.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tanks and Other Spaces**
>
> - ☐ **Structural vs Independent tanks** — Structural = formed by hull plating; Independent = self-supporting (LNG/LPG carriers).
> - ☐ **Double bottom tanks** — Design head minimum **2.4 m** above tank top; highest free surface effect among ballast tanks.
> - ☐ **Forepeak tanks** — Must withstand **slamming loads** at the bow.
> - ☐ **Cofferdams** — Minimum **760 mm** width, independently vented, with leakage detection; purpose = segregation + thermal + safety barrier.
> - ☐ **SOLAS Reg. II-1/11** governs tank segregation; **MARPOL Annex I** governs oil pollution prevention.
> - ☐ **Sounding pipe** — Minimum diameter **50 mm**, terminating on open deck.
> - ☐ **Vent pipe** — Cross-section $\geq$ largest filling pipe; flame arrestor mandatory for oil tanks.
> - ☐ **Simpson's 1st Rule** — Use only with **odd number of ordinates** ($n = 3, 5, 7, ...$).
> - ☐ **Trapezoidal Rule** — Fallback when Simpson's rule cannot be applied (e.g., 2 or 4 ordinates).
> - ☐ **Trim correction** — Must be applied to sounding readings when ship is not level; correction tables provided in the Capacity Plan.
> - ☐ **Tank testing** — Hydrostatic test at **1.25 × design head** for **30 minutes**.
> - ☐ **Quick-closing valves** required on all oil fuel tank outlet pipes (SOLAS).
> - ☐ **Sludge tanks** — Used for waste oil residue; segregated from other oil tanks.
> - ☐ **Chain lockers** — Watertight compartments for anchor chain; usually in the forepeak region; separate drainage to bilge.
> - ☐ **Bilge tanks** collect waste water from machinery spaces before transfer to shore facility.
> - ☐ **Free surface effect** is the **most critical stability penalty** of partially-filled DB ballast tanks — design strategy: keep them either nearly full or nearly empty during voyage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship's Lines Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ship's Lines Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Ship's Lines Plan** (also called the **General Arrangement of Form**, or simply the **"Lines"**) is the definitive two-dimensional graphical representation of a three-dimensional ship hull form. It is constructed by projecting the three-dimensional hull onto three mutually perpendicular reference planes, producing a set of intersecting curved lines that uniquely and unambiguously define the entire geometry of the ship's external envelope below the design waterline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Lines Plan is the foundational engineering document of Naval Architecture. Every subsequent calculation — hydrostatics, stability, resistance, powering, and hull construction — originates from the offsets read directly off this plan.

In rigorous terminology, the Lines Plan consists of **three orthographic projections**:

1. **Sheer Plan (Profile View)** – Projection onto the **centerline vertical longitudinal plane** (X–Z plane).
2. **Body Plan (Transverse View)** – Projection onto the **transverse vertical plane** (Y–Z plane), with forward sections on one side of the centerline and aft sections on the other.
3. **Half-Breadth Plan (Plan View)** – Projection onto the **horizontal plane** (X–Y plane), showing only the port or starboard side (typically the port side, with centerline as reference).

The Lines Plan is supplemented by a numerical **Table of Offsets**, which tabulates the (X, Y, Z) coordinates of every intersection point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hull as a 3D Wireframe"

Imagine a sculptor carves a perfect ship-shaped model out of wood. To send the design to a shipyard in another country, the sculptor cannot ship the wooden model. Instead, the sculptor slices the model with three sets of parallel imaginary planes:

- A set of **horizontal slices** (like cutting a loaf of bread lengthwise) → these give the **Waterlines**.
- A set of **vertical slices from bow to stern** (like cutting the loaf along its length) → these give the **Buttocks** (longitudinal) and **Profile** (Sheer).
- A set of **vertical crosswise slices** (like cutting a salami into coins) → these give the **Stations** (transverse sections).

The **Lines Plan is the photograph of every slice, laid flat on paper**, with a coordinate table giving the exact position of every point where the slices intersect. A shipyard anywhere in the world can faithfully reconstruct the hull by reading this plan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Lines Plan is the **legal contract** of hull form. It is the master document from which the mould loft templates are laid out for steel plate cutting. Any error in fairing propagates to every downstream calculation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics

The following conventions are standardized by **ISO 7464** and adopted by KTU naval architecture practice:

- **Principal Particulars Reference Point:** Origin at the **after perpendicular (AP)** on the **design waterline**, OR at the **midship section (mid.Station = 0)**.
- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP):** **LBP** — the longitudinal reference for station numbering.
- **Standard Number of Stations:** Typically **20 stations**, numbered as **Station 0 (AP)**, **Station 20 (FP)**, with **Station 10 = Midship**.
- **Standard Waterlines:** **WL0 (Baseline / Keel)**, **WL1, WL2, … up to the Design Waterline (DWL)**. The number of waterlines is usually **half the number of stations**, i.e., **10 WLs** for 20 stations.
- **Lines Spacing:** **Evenly spaced stations** (preferred for modern computer-aided design) OR **Run-and-Check** spacing for aft sections.
- **Breadth (B)** and **Depth (D)** are referenced from the **centerline** and **baseline** respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Run-and-Check Spacing:** A traditional method where station spacing is NOT uniform — stations are crowded near the stern where the hull curvature changes rapidly (the "counter" and "run"), and spread out where the hull is faired (parallel midbody). This captures form detail with fewer stations. Modern KTU coursework typically uses **evenly spaced stations** for ease of computation.

## 1.4 The Three Reference Planes — Establishing the Coordinate System

| Plane | Also Called | Orientation | What it Shows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Centerline Vertical Longitudinal Plane | **Sheer Plane** | Vertical, runs bow-to-stern | Profile view: sheer, camber, rake, stern shape |
| Horizontal Plane | **Design Waterline Plane** (or Half-Breadth Plane) | Horizontal, level with DWL | Plan view: waterlines, buttock intersections, half-breadths |
| Transverse Vertical Plane | **Station Plane** | Vertical, perpendicular to ship length | Body Plan: transverse sections (stations) |

> [!TIP]
> **Coordinate Notation Convention:** Offsets are universally written as **(X, Y, Z)** or **(Length, Half-Breadth, Height)**.
> - **X** = longitudinal distance (from AP or midship)
> - **Y** = half-breadth (out from centerline)
> - **Z** = vertical height (above baseline / keel)

The Lines Plan is therefore the **graphical inverse** of a 3D point cloud — the set of all (X, Y, Z) triples that lie on the hull surface.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of the Lines Plan — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Sheer Plan (Profile View)

The Sheer Plan is the side elevation of the ship projected onto the centerline plane. It contains:

- **Sheer Line:** The intersection of the hull surface with the centerline plane above the design waterline. Shows the longitudinal curvature of the deck edge.
- **Camber:** The athwartships curvature of the deck (visible only as a line in profile — the actual curvature is in the body plan).
- **Knuckle Line:** The intersection of the deck with the upper sheer strake or bulwark, where the deck has a transverse bend.
- **Rake of Stem:** The forward inclination of the stem from the vertical.
- **Rake of Sternpost / Transom Shape:** Aft profile, whether cruiser stern, transom stern, or counter stern.
- **Load Waterline (LWL) and Design Waterline (DWL):** Horizontal reference lines.

> [!NOTE]
> The Sheer Plan **does NOT** show hull breadth, because the projection collapses the entire half-breadth onto the centerline. It only shows the **keel-to-deck profile** at the centerline.

### 2.1.2 The Body Plan (Transverse View)

The Body Plan is the projection onto the transverse vertical plane. It is unique in that:

- **Forward stations** (Stations 0 to 10) are drawn on the **right side** of the centerline.
- **Aft stations** (Stations 10 to 20) are drawn on the **left side** of the centerline.
- The **centerline (A = 0)** acts as the line of symmetry.

Each station is a transverse cross-section showing:
- The **flat of bottom** (parallel to baseline).
- The **bilge** (rounded turn from bottom to side).
- The **side** (nearly vertical hull plating).
- The **sheer strake** (top strake at the deck edge).
- The **chine** (if present — for hard-chine hulls like planing craft or SWATH).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moulded vs. Shell Lines:** The Lines Plan typically shows **moulded lines** — the *inside* of the plating. The **plating thickness** is added outward to obtain the **shell lines**. The difference is critical in structural scantling but invisible in fairing.

### 2.1.3 The Half-Breadth Plan (Plan View)

The Half-Breadth Plan shows only the **port side** of the ship (starboard is mirror-implied) viewed from above. It contains:

- **Waterlines (WLs):** Curves showing the hull outline at each successive height above the keel.
- **Buttocks:** Longitudinal vertical sections parallel to the centerline, at successive half-breadths (e.g., Buttock 0, B1, B2, …).
- **Diagonals:** Oblique sections used as an additional check on fairness, especially in the bilge.

The **outermost waterline (DWL)** is the **ship's waterline plan form**.

## 2.2 Lines, Sections, and Intersections — The Logic Tree

The Lines Plan is generated from the **intersection of three families of planes** with the hull surface:

| Family of Planes | Resulting Curves | Visible In |
|---|---|---|
| **21 transverse vertical planes** (stations) | 21 transverse section curves | Body Plan |
| **11 horizontal planes** (waterlines) | 11 waterline curves | Half-Breadth Plan |
| **~9 longitudinal vertical planes** (buttocks) | 9 buttock curves | Half-Breadth Plan |

Each curve is named by the parameter that defines its plane:
- **Stations:** numbered **0 (AP), 1, 2, …, 20 (FP)**.
- **Waterlines:** numbered **0 (baseline), 1, 2, …, 10 (DWL)**.
- **Buttocks:** labeled by their **half-breadth Y-value** (e.g., B0.0, B0.5, B1.0, …).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-View Consistency Rule:** A point on the hull lies at the intersection of ONE station, ONE waterline, and ONE buttock. Its coordinates (X, Y, Z) appear once on the Body Plan, once on the Sheer Plan (X,Z only), and once on the Half-Breadth Plan (X,Y only). The Lines Plan is **internally consistent** when these three projections agree.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes the core equations, geometric relations, and offset conventions used in Lines Plan problems. **This is exam-critical material.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coordinate of a hull point** | $P = (X,\ Y,\ Z)$ | X: m from midship or AP; Y: m from CL; Z: m from baseline |
| 2 | **Station spacing (even)** | $\Delta L = \dfrac{L_{BP}}{N_{stations} - 1}$ | m. For LBP=100 m, 21 stations: ΔL = 5.0 m |
| 3 | **Waterline spacing (even)** | $\Delta Z = \dfrac{D}{N_{WL}}$ | m. For D=10 m, 11 WLs (WL0…WL10): ΔZ = 1.0 m |
| 4 | **Offset for body plan section** | $(X_i,\ Y_{ij},\ Z_j)$ | Read at station *i*, waterline *j* |
| 5 | **Breadth at waterline j** | $B_j = 2 \cdot Y_{j,max}$ | m. The *i* at midship gives half-breadth |
| 6 | **Depth of waterline j** | $Z_j = j \cdot \Delta Z$ | m above baseline |
| 7 | **Position of center of buoyancy (LCB, VCB)** | $LCB = \dfrac{\int X \, dV}{\int dV} = \dfrac{\sum X_i \cdot A_i \cdot \Delta L}{\sum A_i \cdot \Delta L}$ | m. Computed by Simpson's 1st or 2nd rule over stations |
| 8 | **Sectional area at station i** | $A_i = \int_{WL0}^{WL_{DWL}} 2Y_{i}(Z)\, dZ$ | m². Computed by Simpson's rule over waterlines |
| 9 | **Volume of displacement (Simpson's 1st Rule)** | $\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta L}{3}\left[A_0 + A_N + 4\sum A_{odd} + 2\sum A_{even}\right]$ | m³. N must be **even number of intervals** (odd number of stations) |
| 10 | **Simpson's multipliers for 20 intervals (21 stations)** | 1, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 1 | Sum = 20 + 1 = 21 ⇒ check: 1+4+2+4+2+4+2+4+2+4+2+4+2+4+2+4+2+4+2+4+1 |
| 11 | **Curvature check — Slope smoothness** | $\dfrac{dY}{dX},\ \dfrac{dY}{dZ}$ must be **continuous** | Used in fairing; sudden kinks = unfair line |
| 12 | **Hull form coefficients (from offsets)** | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient from volume of displacement |
| 13 | **Midship section coefficient** | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Ratio of midship area to its circumscribing rectangle |
| 14 | **Prismatic coefficient** | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$ | Indicates fullness of ends |
| 15 | **Wetted surface area (wetted)** | $S \approx \nabla^{2/3} \cdot (1.7 L_{BP} + 2.0\sqrt{\nabla / L_{BP}})$ | m² (approximate, for resistance estimates) |
| 16 | **LBP to LWL correction** | $L_{WL} \approx L_{BP} \cdot (1.005 \text{ to } 1.020)$ | WL slightly longer than BP due to stem rake |
| 17 | **Trim correction to draft** | $T_{actual} = T_{mid} \pm \dfrac{L \cdot \text{trim}}{2 L_{BP}}$ | Draft at AP and FP from trim |
| 18 | **Bonjean curve input** | $A_i(Z)$ at each station as function of Z | Tabulated; gives sectional area at any waterline |

> [!TIP]
> **Simpson's Rule Reminder:** Simpson's 1st Rule applies when the number of **intervals is EVEN** (number of ordinates is ODD). For 20 intervals between 21 stations, the multipliers 1-4-2-4-…-4-1 apply. If the data has an odd number of intervals, use the **Trapezoidal rule** or add a dummy ordinate zero. KTU examiners love to test this.

## 2.4 Why is the Lines Plan Important? — Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Lines Plan is not an artistic drawing. It is the **engineering database of the hull form**. Its utilities include:

1. **Hydrostatic Calculations:** From sectional areas $A_i$, we integrate to get **displacement volume $\nabla$**, **center of buoyancy (LCB, VCB)**, **moment to trim 1 cm (MT1cm)**, and **longitudinal metacentre (L-BML)**.
2. **Stability Analysis:** The **Bonjean curves** (area of each station as a function of draft) are derived from the lines. These are the heart of damaged and intact stability.
3. **Resistance & Powering:** The **wetted surface area** is integrated from the offsets. Form factors ($C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$) are read off.
4. **Mould Loft Work:** The Lines Plan is enlarged in the mould loft to **full scale**, and templates are cut for **plate nesting** and **frame bending**.
5. **Hull Construction Tolerances:** Modern numerically controlled (NC) cutting machines read the lines plan directly via CAD/CAM.
6. **Lines Fairing and Optimization:** Iterative form modifications (e.g., bulbous bow addition, stern reshaping) start with the lines plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Fairing" the Lines:** A line is "fair" if it has **continuous curvature** with no kinks, inflections, or sudden slope changes. Fairing is the iterative process of adjusting offsets so that the three views agree and every line is smooth. Historically this was a 3-month manual task; today, software like **Maxsurf, Rhino, NAPA, and AVEVA** does it in hours, but the principles remain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing Sectional Area $A_i$ at a Station from Offsets

Each station (transverse section) is a 2D closed curve in the (Y, Z) plane. Its area is given by:

$$A_i = \int_0^{D} 2 Y_i(Z)\, dZ$$

**Discretization over $n+1$ waterlines (WL0 at baseline, WLn at deck) with even spacing $\Delta Z$**:

$$A_i = \int_{Z_0}^{Z_n} 2 Y_i(Z)\, dZ \approx \int_{Z_0}^{Z_n} 2Y_i\, dZ$$

**Step 1 — Discretize the integral as a sum over waterline ordinates:**

$$A_i = 2 \sum_{j=0}^{n} w_j \cdot Y_{i,j} \cdot \Delta Z$$

where $w_j$ are Simpson's weights.

**Step 2 — Apply Simpson's 1st Rule weights for n even:**

$$A_i = \dfrac{2 \Delta Z}{3}\left[Y_{i,0} + Y_{i,n} + 4\sum_{j\ \text{odd}} Y_{i,j} + 2\sum_{j\ \text{even},\ j\ne 0,n} Y_{i,j}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (10 waterlines, so n=10 intervals, $n+1=11$ ordinates):**

Take a single station with half-breadths (m) at WL0 through WL10:

$$Y = \{0,\ 0.8,\ 1.5,\ 2.1,\ 2.6,\ 3.0,\ 3.3,\ 3.5,\ 3.6,\ 3.6,\ 3.6\}$$

with $\Delta Z = 1.0$ m.

**Sub-step 3a — Identify the Simpson multipliers for n=10 intervals (11 ordinates):**

$$\text{Multipliers: } 1, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 1$$

**Sub-step 3b — Compute weighted sum $\sum w_j Y_j$:**

| $j$ | $Y_j$ (m) | $w_j$ | $w_j Y_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 1 | 0.0 |
| 1 | 0.8 | 4 | 3.2 |
| 2 | 1.5 | 2 | 3.0 |
| 3 | 2.1 | 4 | 8.4 |
| 4 | 2.6 | 2 | 5.2 |
| 5 | 3.0 | 4 | 12.0 |
| 6 | 3.3 | 2 | 6.6 |
| 7 | 3.5 | 4 | 14.0 |
| 8 | 3.6 | 2 | 7.2 |
| 9 | 3.6 | 4 | 14.4 |
| 10 | 3.6 | 1 | 3.6 |
| **Sum** | | **20+1=21** check | **77.6** |

**Sub-step 3c — Substitute into Simpson's formula:**

$$A_i = \dfrac{2 \times 1.0}{3} \times 77.6 = \dfrac{155.2}{3} = 51.73\ \text{m}^2$$

**Result:** Sectional area at this station is **51.73 m²**.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of 2 in front converts half-breadths to full breadths. The full beam at WL5 is $2 \times 3.0 = 6.0$ m, which is consistent with a station of a small ship.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Displacement Volume $\nabla$ from Sectional Areas

Once we have sectional areas $A_0, A_1, \ldots, A_N$ at each station, the volume of displacement is:

$$\nabla = \int_0^{L_{BP}} A(X)\, dX$$

**Discretize along the length** with $N$ intervals (so $N+1$ stations, $N$ even for Simpson's 1st Rule):

$$\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta L}{3}\left[A_0 + A_N + 4 \sum_{k\ \text{odd}} A_k + 2 \sum_{k\ \text{even},\ k\ne 0,N} A_k\right]$$

**Worked Example — 20 intervals, 21 stations:**

Suppose the sectional areas (m²) computed from the offsets are:

$$A = \{0,\ 5,\ 12,\ 22,\ 34,\ 45,\ 53,\ 57,\ 58,\ 56,\ 51,\ 43,\ 33,\ 22,\ 12,\ 5,\ 2,\ 0.5,\ 0.1,\ 0.02,\ 0\}$$

with $L_{BP} = 100$ m, so $\Delta L = 100/20 = 5.0$ m.

**Step 1 — Apply multipliers 1-4-2-4-…-4-1:**

$$\sum w_k A_k = (1)(0) + (4)(5) + (2)(12) + (4)(22) + (2)(34) + (4)(45) + (2)(53) + (4)(57) + (2)(58) + (4)(56) + (2)(51) + (4)(43) + (2)(33) + (4)(22) + (2)(12) + (4)(5) + (2)(2) + (4)(0.5) + (2)(0.1) + (4)(0.02) + (1)(0)$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

| $k$ | $A_k$ | $w_k$ | $w_k A_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0.0 |
| 1 | 5 | 4 | 20.0 |
| 2 | 12 | 2 | 24.0 |
| 3 | 22 | 4 | 88.0 |
| 4 | 34 | 2 | 68.0 |
| 5 | 45 | 4 | 180.0 |
| 6 | 53 | 2 | 106.0 |
| 7 | 57 | 4 | 228.0 |
| 8 | 58 | 2 | 116.0 |
| 9 | 56 | 4 | 224.0 |
| 10 | 51 | 2 | 102.0 |
| 11 | 43 | 4 | 172.0 |
| 12 | 33 | 2 | 66.0 |
| 13 | 22 | 4 | 88.0 |
| 14 | 12 | 2 | 24.0 |
| 15 | 5 | 4 | 20.0 |
| 16 | 2 | 2 | 4.0 |
| 17 | 0.5 | 4 | 2.0 |
| 18 | 0.1 | 2 | 0.2 |
| 19 | 0.02 | 4 | 0.08 |
| 20 | 0 | 1 | 0.0 |
| **Sum** | | | **1530.28** |

**Step 3 — Apply Simpson's formula:**

$$\nabla = \dfrac{5.0}{3} \times 1530.28 = 2550.47\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check using block coefficient:**

If $B = 14$ m, $T = 6$ m, $L_{BP} = 100$ m:

$$C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} = \dfrac{2550.47}{100 \times 14 \times 6} = \dfrac{2550.47}{8400} = 0.304$$

This is a typical $C_B$ for a **fine form** (e.g., a naval frigate or fast container ship). ✓ Reasonable.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Longitudinal Center of Buoyancy (LCB)

The LCB is the longitudinal position of the center of buoyancy, measured from midship or AP. It is the first moment of volume about the transverse axis, divided by the volume.

**Definition (continuous form):**

$$LCB = \dfrac{\int_0^{L} X \cdot A(X)\, dX}{\int_0^{L} A(X)\, dX} = \dfrac{M_{LCB}}{\nabla}$$

where $M_{LCB}$ is the **longitudinal moment of the buoyancy volume**.

**Discretized form using Simpson's 1st Rule on both numerator and denominator:**

$$LCB = \dfrac{\dfrac{\Delta L}{3} \sum w_k \cdot X_k \cdot A_k}{\dfrac{\Delta L}{3} \sum w_k \cdot A_k} = \dfrac{\sum w_k \cdot X_k \cdot A_k}{\sum w_k \cdot A_k}$$

**Worked Example using the same data as §3.2, with $X$ measured from Station 0 (AP):**

Station $X_k = k \cdot \Delta L = 5k$ m for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, 20$.

**Step 1 — Compute $w_k \cdot X_k \cdot A_k$:**

| $k$ | $X_k$ | $A_k$ | $w_k$ | $w_k X_k A_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 1 | 5 | 5 | 4 | 100 |
| 2 | 10 | 12 | 2 | 240 |
| 3 | 15 | 22 | 4 | 1320 |
| 4 | 20 | 34 | 2 | 1360 |
| 5 | 25 | 45 | 4 | 4500 |
| 6 | 30 | 53 | 2 | 3180 |
| 7 | 35 | 57 | 4 | 7980 |
| 8 | 40 | 58 | 2 | 4640 |
| 9 | 45 | 56 | 4 | 10080 |
| 10 | 50 | 51 | 2 | 5100 |
| 11 | 55 | 43 | 4 | 9460 |
| 12 | 60 | 33 | 2 | 3960 |
| 13 | 65 | 22 | 4 | 5720 |
| 14 | 70 | 12 | 2 | 1680 |
| 15 | 75 | 5 | 4 | 1500 |
| 16 | 80 | 2 | 2 | 320 |
| 17 | 85 | 0.5 | 4 | 170 |
| 18 | 90 | 0.1 | 2 | 18 |
| 19 | 95 | 0.02 | 4 | 7.6 |
| 20 | 100 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **Sum** | | | | **61337.6** |

**Step 2 — Compute LCB from AP:**

$$LCB_{AP} = \dfrac{61337.6}{1530.28} = 40.08\ \text{m from AP}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to LCB from midship (Station 10, $X = 50$ m):**

$$LCB_{midship} = 40.08 - 50.0 = -9.92\ \text{m from midship}$$

The **negative sign** indicates the center of buoyancy is **aft of midship** by 9.92 m, consistent with a full stern and fine bow.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Curvature and Fairing Condition (Analytical)

A **fair curve** must have continuous first and second derivatives. For a station curve parameterized by Z:

$$Y_i = Y_i(Z)$$

The **slope** is $\dfrac{dY}{dZ}$ and the **curvature** is:

$$\kappa_i = \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2 Y_i}{dZ^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dY_i}{dZ}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

**Fairing criterion:** $\kappa_i$ must change **smoothly and monotonically** along the curve, with no inflection points unless the design requires them (e.g., a knuckle).

**Discrete fairness check** (the "diagonal offset" method): Draw a diagonal plane at 45° intersecting the hull. The resulting diagonal curve, when projected onto the half-breadth plan, must also be smooth. If the diagonal has a kink while the WL and station do not, the form is **unfair** and must be re-corrected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why diagonals?** A station, a waterline, and a buttock all pass through the same hull point. If two of them are smooth and the third is kinked, the diagonal check will reveal the error. This is the classical **"three-view fairness check"** used in mould lofts since the 18th century.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Computation Steps

| Step | Computation | Required Inputs | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Read offsets from Lines Plan | Table of Offsets | $(X, Y, Z)$ triples |
| **2** | Compute sectional areas $A_i$ | Half-breadths at each station vs. Z | $A_0, A_1, \ldots, A_N$ (m²) |
| **3** | Apply Simpson's 1st Rule along length | $A_i$ values, station spacing $\Delta L$ | $\nabla$ (m³) — displacement volume |
| **4** | Apply Simpson's 1st Rule for moment | $X_k \cdot A_k$ weighted sum | LCB (m) |
| **5** | Compute $A_M$ (midship area) | From $A_{N/2}$ | Midship coefficient $C_M$ |
| **6** | Form coefficients | $\nabla, A_M, L_{BP}, B, T$ | $C_B, C_P, C_M$ |
| **7** | Bonjean curves | $A_i$ vs. $Z$ at each station | Tabulated areas for stability |
| **8** | Wetted surface | Numerical integration of $S$ | $S$ (m²) for resistance |

This diagram shows how the abstract 3D hull form is reduced to the three 2D views and the offset table.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    planeStation["Family of Transverse Planes<br/>Stations 0 to N"]
    planeWater["Family of Horizontal Planes<br/>Waterlines WL0 to WLn"]
    planeButtock["Family of Longitudinal Planes<br/>Buttocks B0 to Bn"]
    
    interStation["Intersection Curves<br/>21 Transverse Sections"]
    interWater["Intersection Curves<br/>11 Waterline Curves"]
    interButtock["Intersection Curves<br/>9 Buttock Curves"]
    
    bodyPlan["BODY PLAN<br/>Transverse View<br/>Forward right, Aft left"]
    sheerPlan["SHEER PLAN<br/>Profile View<br/>Side Elevation"]
    halfBreadth["HALF-BREADTH PLAN<br/>Plan View<br/>Port Side Only"]
    
    offsetTable["TABLE OF OFFSETS<br/>Numerical X Y Z matrix"]
    
    mouldLoft["MOULD LOFT<br/>Full scale templates"]
    hydroCalc["HYDROSTATIC CALC<br/>Displacement, LCB, VCB"]
    stability["STABILITY<br/>Bonjean Curves, GZ"]
    resistance["RESISTANCE POWER<br/>Form factors, WSA"]
    
    hull3D --> planeStation
    hull3D --> planeWater
    hull3D --> planeButtock
    
    planeStation --> interStation
    planeWater --> interWater
    planeButtock --> interButtock
    
    interStation --> bodyPlan
    interStation --> sheerPlan
    interWater --> halfBreadth
    interButtock --> halfBreadth
    
    bodyPlan --> offsetTable
    sheerPlan --> offsetTable
    halfBreadth --> offsetTable
    
    offsetTable --> mouldLoft
    offsetTable --> hydroCalc
    offsetTable --> stability
    offsetTable --> resistance
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Lines Plan Layout Convention (Mould Loft Schematic)

This block diagram shows the standard physical layout of a Lines Plan drawing sheet, as it would appear on the drawing board in a design office.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layout["STANDARD LINES PLAN LAYOUT<br/>Single Drawing Sheet"]

    layout --> middleLeft["MIDDLE LEFT: BODY PLAN<br/>Forward stations right of CL<br/>Aft stations left of CL<br/>Transverse Y vs Z"]
    layout --> middleRight["MIDDLE RIGHT: HALF BREADTH PLAN<br/>Plan view<br/>Length along X axis, Half breadth along Y axis<br/>Waterlines from WL0 at bottom to DWL at top"]
    layout --> bottom["BOTTOM ZONE: TABLE OF OFFSETS<br/>Tabulated X Y Z values<br/>Rows: Stations 0 to 20<br/>Columns: Waterlines WL0 to WLn<br/>Subcolumns: Half breadth and Height"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Offset Reading Logic for a Single Station

This block diagram shows how a designer reads three values (X, Y, Z) for a single hull point, by tracing from one view to another.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Select a hull point P<br/>on the hull surface"]
    start --> step1["Step 1: Identify Station i<br/>in Body Plan<br/>Transverse plane index"]
    step1 --> step2["Step 2: Read X coordinate<br/>Length of station i from AP<br/>visible in Sheer Plan or Half Breadth Plan"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Identify Waterline j<br/>horizontal plane index"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Read Z coordinate<br/>Height above baseline<br/>visible in Sheer Plan or Body Plan"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: Read Y coordinate<br/>Half breadth at station i and waterline j<br/>from Body Plan or Half Breadth Plan"]
    step5 --> endPoint["RESULT: P = X Y Z<br/>Three-dimensional hull point<br/>Logged in Table of Offsets"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Lines Plan in Exams:** When asked "what is the half-breadth at Station 8 on WL3?", the answer comes from the **Body Plan** (right side, since Station 8 is forward of midship). Trace horizontally from the centerline to where Station 8 curve crosses WL3, then read the Y-value. The corresponding Z is 3·ΔZ and the X is 8·ΔL.
