A ship behaves in exactly the same way. A **slim, fine-lined vessel** (low $C_B$, low $C_P$, high $L/B$) will have lower wave-making resistance but may have higher frictional resistance per ton. A **full-formed bulk carrier** (high $C_B \approx 0.80$–$0.85$, low $L/B$) will carry more cargo per unit length but at the cost of higher wave-making resistance and a lower efficient service speed. The art of ship design is to **balance these trade-offs** for the intended service profile.

## 1.4 The Three "Golden" Slenderness Ratios

The hull form performance is most sensitively captured by three dimensionless length ratios:

- **Length-to-Beam Ratio** $L/B$ — Governs course-keeping, roll damping, and resistance at high Froude numbers.
- **Beam-to-Draft Ratio** $B/T$ — Governs transverse stability, directional stability, and resistance in shallow water.
- **Length-to-Displacement Ratio** $L/\nabla^{1/3}$ — A pure form parameter; the slenderness of the hull for a given displacement.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks "How does hull form affect performance?", always anchor your answer to the **four coefficients + three ratios** framework above. This is the standard analytical lens used by KTU board examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Relationship Between Coefficients

The four hull form coefficients are not independent. They are linked by a foundational identity that is exhaustively tested in KTU examinations:

$$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$

This equation states that the **block coefficient** (overall fullness) is the product of the **prismatic coefficient** (longitudinal distribution) and the **midship coefficient** (transverse fullness). The implication is that a designer who wishes to increase $C_B$ (to carry more cargo) can do so by either:

1. **Fattening the midsection** (raising $C_M$ — adding beam or reducing the deadrise), OR
2. **Filling out the ends** (raising $C_P$ — making the bow and stern fuller and less tapered), OR
3. **A combination of both**.

The choice between these strategies has a **dramatic effect on resistance** and is at the heart of every hull form optimization study.

## 2.2 The Decomposition of Total Resistance

The total calm-water resistance $R_T$ of a ship is decomposed (per the classical Froude / ITTC framework) as:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R = R_F + (R_W + R_{APP} + R_{form})$$

Where:
- $R_F$ = **Frictional resistance** (viscous skin friction) — depends on wetted surface area $S$ and Reynolds number $Re$.
- $R_R$ = **Residual resistance** = the sum of all non-frictional components.
- $R_W$ = **Wave-making resistance** — energy radiated as gravity waves from the hull.
- $R_{APP}$ = **Appendage resistance** — drag from rudders, brackets, shafts, bilge keels.
- $R_{form}$ = **Form effect / wave-breaking resistance** — depends strongly on hull form fullness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for hull form:** Of all the components, **$R_W$ is the most sensitive to hull form**. A change in $C_B$ from $0.55$ (a fast container ship) to $0.85$ (a Capesize bulk carrier) can increase $R_W$ by a factor of **3 to 5** at the same Froude number. Meanwhile, $R_F$ changes only modestly because the wetted surface area $S$ is bounded by the same displacement.

## 2.3 Hull Form Sensitivity Map

| Hull Form Parameter | Effect on $R_F$ (Friction) | Effect on $R_W$ (Wave) | Effect on $EHP$ | Effect on Cargo Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\uparrow C_B$ (fuller form) | Slight $\uparrow$ (larger $S$) | Strong $\uparrow$ | Strong $\uparrow$ | Strong $\uparrow$ |
| $\uparrow C_P$ (fuller ends) | Slight $\uparrow$ | Strong $\uparrow$ | Strong $\uparrow$ | Moderate $\uparrow$ |
| $\uparrow C_M$ (fuller midship) | Slight $\uparrow$ | Slight $\uparrow$ | Slight $\uparrow$ | Strong $\uparrow$ |
| $\uparrow L/B$ (slender) | $\downarrow$ (less $S$) | Strong $\downarrow$ | Strong $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ |
| $\uparrow B/T$ (shallow) | Slight $\uparrow$ | Moderate $\uparrow$ | Moderate $\uparrow$ | Neutral |
| $\uparrow L/\nabla^{1/3}$ (slender) | $\downarrow$ | Strong $\downarrow$ | Strong $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$ | Block coefficient | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L)$ | Prismatic coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$ | Midship section coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L \cdot B)$ | Waterplane area coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ | Fundamental identity | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S = \nabla^{2/3} \cdot (3.43 + 0.64 \cdot L/B)$ | **Harvald's approximation** for wetted surface | m² |
| 7 | $R_T = R_F + R_R$ | Total resistance decomposition | kN |
| 8 | $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$ | **ITTC 1978 frictional line** | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Re = V \cdot L / \nu$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Fn = V / \sqrt{g \cdot L}$ | Froude number | dimensionless |
| 11 | $EHP = R_T \cdot V / 1000$ | Effective horsepower (if $V$ in m/s, $R_T$ in kN) | kW |
| 12 | $C_R = R_R / (0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2)$ | Residual resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 13 | $C_T = R_T / (0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2)$ | Total resistance coefficient | dimensionless |
| 14 | $C_T = C_F + C_R + C_{AA}$ | Standard resistance coefficient identity | dimensionless |
| 15 | $L / \nabla^{1/3}$ | Slenderness ratio (form-only) | dimensionless |
| 16 | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | Displaced volume | m³ |
| 17 | $D_{p\text{-corr}} = EHP / (\eta_D \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R)$ | Delivered power to propeller | kW |

Where:
- $\rho$ = mass density of seawater (typically **1025 kg/m³**)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of seawater (typically **$1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s** at 15°C)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $\eta_D, \eta_H, \eta_R$ = propulsive efficiencies (rotational, hull, relative)
- $C_{AA}$ = correlation allowance (typically $0.0004$ for ITTC 1978)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hull form framework is the **first-pass design tool** in any ship design office. Before any model testing or CFD, the naval architect estimates powering and resistance using these coefficients:

- **Concept Design:** A change in $C_B$ from $0.65$ to $0.70$ is analysed for its effect on EHP and deadweight.
- **Contract Design:** Slenderness ratio $L/\nabla^{1/3}$ is used to position the design in the **effective horsepower–speed space** alongside historical parent forms.
- **Detailed Design:** $C_P$ and $C_M$ are tuned to refine the wave system and minimize interference with the propeller inflow.
- **Series Data:** Standard series (Taylor, Series 60, DTMB, KCS) provide $C_T$ vs $Fn$ curves parameterized by $C_B$ and $L/B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state the **direction** of the effect (e.g., "an increase in $C_B$ increases wave-making resistance and hence EHP at a given speed") and the **physical reason** ("because the fuller form generates a larger transverse wave system at the same Froude number"). Partial answers that quote only the direction lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Block–Prismatic–Midship Identity $C_B = C_P \times C_M$

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of $C_B$.**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Step 2 — Express the displaced volume $\nabla$ as the integral of cross-sectional areas along the length.**

The displaced volume is the integral of the underwater cross-sectional area $A(x)$ from the aft perpendicular (AP) to the forward perpendicular (FP):

$$\nabla = \int_{0}^{L} A(x) \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Approximate the integral using the mean of the cross-sectional area distribution.**

If the hull has a prismatic form, the volume can be approximated as the product of the midship area and the length, scaled by a longitudinal distribution factor — the prismatic coefficient:

$$\nabla = A_M \cdot L \cdot C_P$$

This is, by definition, $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L)$.

**Step 4 — Express the midship area $A_M$ in terms of beam and draft.**

$$A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T$$

This is, by definition, $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$.

**Step 5 — Substitute both expressions back into the definition of $C_B$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} \\
    &= \frac{A_M \cdot L \cdot C_P}{L \cdot B \cdot T} \\
    &= \frac{C_P \cdot (C_M \cdot B \cdot T)}{B \cdot T} \\
    &= C_P \times C_M
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation.**

The derivation shows that the overall fullness of the hull ($C_B$) is the **product** of how full the middle is ($C_M$) and how the volume is distributed along the length ($C_P$). Two ships with the same $C_B = 0.70$ can have vastly different resistance signatures if one is full in the middle with fine ends (high $C_M$, low $C_P$ — typical of a frigate) and the other is half-full in the middle with very full ends (low $C_M$, high $C_P$ — typical of a tanker).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Power from Resistance

**Step 1 — Define effective power (EHP).**

EHP is the power required to tow a ship at speed $V$ against its total resistance $R_T$ in calm water, with no propeller, no appendages, and no interaction effects:

$$EHP = R_T \cdot V \quad \text{(in SI base units, W)}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the resistance coefficient formulation.**

Express $R_T$ using the standard ITTC form:

$$R_T = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_T$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the EHP expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EHP &= R_T \cdot V \\
    &= \left( \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_T \right) \cdot V \\
    &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^3 \cdot C_T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to practical engineering units.**

In ship design, it is conventional to use $R_T$ in kN and $V$ in knots, yielding EHP in kW:

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \text{(kN)} \cdot V \text{(knots)} \cdot 0.5144}{1} \approx \frac{R_T \cdot V}{1.944}$$

The $0.5144$ factor converts knots to m/s; dividing by 1000 converts W to kW.

**Step 5 — Practical calculation example.**

For a Panamax bulk carrier: $L = 225$ m, $B = 32.2$ m, $T = 14.5$ m, $C_B = 0.82$, $V = 14$ knots.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= 0.82 \times 225 \times 32.2 \times 14.5 \\
       &= 86{,}300 \text{ m}^3 \\
S \text{ (Harvald)} &= \nabla^{2/3} \times \left(3.43 + 0.64 \cdot \frac{L}{B}\right) \\
                    &= (86{,}300)^{2/3} \times \left(3.43 + 0.64 \times \frac{225}{32.2}\right) \\
                    &= 1{,}938 \times \left(3.43 + 4.47\right) \\
                    &= 1{,}938 \times 7.90 \\
                    &\approx 15{,}310 \text{ m}^2 \\
Fn &= \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}} = \frac{14 \times 0.5144}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 225}} = \frac{7.20}{47.0} \approx 0.153 \\
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(7.20 \times 225 / 1.188 \times 10^{-6}) - 2)^2} \\
    &= \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.36 \times 10^9) - 2)^2} \\
    &= \frac{0.075}{(9.134 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{50.85} \approx 1.475 \times 10^{-3} \\
R_F &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 15{,}310 \times (7.20)^2 \times 1.475 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &\approx 574 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $C_R$ at $Fn = 0.153$ and $C_B = 0.82$ (from standard series), $C_R \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_R &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 15{,}310 \times (7.20)^2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &\approx 583 \text{ kN} \\
R_T &= R_F + R_R + R_{AA} \approx 574 + 583 + 35 \approx 1{,}192 \text{ kN} \\
EHP &= R_T \cdot V \cdot 0.5144 / 1000 \approx 1{,}192 \times 14 \times 0.5144 / 1000 \\
    &\approx 8{,}580 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of $C_B$ on Wave-Making Resistance (Havelock-Type Scaling)

A simplified but instructive scaling argument from thin-ship theory shows how $C_B$ enters the wave resistance.

**Step 1 — Express wave resistance in terms of a Havelock form.**

For a slender hull at low Froude numbers, the wave-making resistance can be approximated as:

$$R_W \propto C_B^2 \cdot \nabla \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{2 \cdot Fn^2}{\text{const}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take the logarithmic derivative with respect to $C_B$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial (\ln R_W)}{\partial C_B} &= 2 \cdot \frac{1}{C_B} \\
\frac{\partial R_W}{\partial C_B} &\approx \frac{2 \cdot R_W}{C_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Quantify a representative change.**

For a ship going from $C_B = 0.55$ to $C_B = 0.75$ at the same displacement, the ratio of wave resistances is approximately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_W(\text{full})}{R_W(\text{slender})} &\approx \left(\frac{0.75}{0.55}\right)^2 = (1.36)^2 \approx 1.86
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A 36% increase in $C_B$ nearly **doubles** the wave-making resistance — confirming why high-speed ships are designed with very low $C_B$ (typically $0.50$–$0.60$) and slow, heavy-lift ships with high $C_B$ (typically $0.80$–$0.85$).

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Numerical Problems:** When asked to compare two hull forms, always work in **ratios** of $R_T$ rather than absolute values. The ratio method cancels common factors ($\rho$, $S$, $V$) and isolates the form effect cleanly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hull Form Parameter Dependency Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Form Geometry] --> B[Block Coefficient C_B]
    A --> C[Prismatic Coefficient C_P]
    A --> D[Midship Coefficient C_M]
    A --> E[Waterplane Coeff C_WP]
    A --> F[Slenderness Ratios]
    F --> F1[L over B]
    F --> F2[B over T]
    F --> F3[L over nabla to power 1 by 3]
    B --> G[Hydrodynamic Performance]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Frictional Resistance R_F]
    G --> G2[Wave Resistance R_W]
    G --> G3[Form Resistance]
    G --> G4[Total Resistance R_T]
    G --> G5[Effective Power EHP]
    G --> G6[Delivered Power D]
    G --> G7[Propulsive Efficiency]
    G --> G8[Seakeeping and Maneuverability]
    B -.->|identity| I[C_B equals C_P times C_M]
    C -.->|identity| I
    D -.->|identity| I
    G1 --> G4
    G2 --> G4
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> G5
    G5 --> G6
    G6 --> G7
```

## 4.2 Resistance Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Resistance R_T] --> B[Frictional Resistance R_F]
    A --> C[Residual Resistance R_R]
    B --> B1[Function of S and Re]
    B1 --> B2[ITTC 1978 line]
    B1 --> B3[Schoenherr rough turbulent]
    C --> D[Wave Making R_W]
    C --> E[Appendage R_APP]
    C --> F[Form Effect and Wave Breaking]
    C --> G[Correlation Allowance C_AA]
    D --> D1[Function of Fn and C_B]
    D1 --> D2[Strongly dependent on hull form]
    E --> E1[Function of appendage geometry]
    F --> F1[Function of transom and stern form]
    G --> G1[Empirical constant approx 0.0004]
```

## 4.3 Hull Form Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vessel Mission Profile] --> B{Vmax to Fn ratio}
    B -->|Fn less than 0.20| C[Low Speed Full Form]
    B -->|Fn 0.20 to 0.30| D[Medium Speed Fine Form]
    B -->|Fn greater than 0.30| E[High Speed Slender Form]
    C --> C1[C_B approx 0.78 to 0.85]
    C1 --> C2[Typical: Tankers, Bulk Carriers]
    D --> D1[C_B approx 0.60 to 0.70]
    D1 --> D2[Typical: Container Ships, RoRo]
    E --> E1[C_B approx 0.45 to 0.58]
    E1 --> E2[Typical: Frigates, Fast Ferries]
    C2 --> F[Validate via Series 60 or KCS]
    D2 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Model Test or CFD Verification]
    G --> H[Final Hull Form]
```

## 4.4 Effect Direction Schematic (Resistance vs $C_B$)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Slender Hull C_B less than 0.55] -->|low| B1[Wave Resistance R_W]
    A -->|low| B2[Wetted Surface S]
    A -->|high| B3[Slenderness L over nabla to power 1 by 3]
    A -->|low| B4[Cargo Capacity]
    A -->|high| B5[Service Speed Efficiency]
    C[Full Hull C_B greater than 0.80] -->|high| D1[Wave Resistance R_W]
    C -->|high| D2[Wetted Surface S]
    C -->|low| D3[Slenderness L over nabla to power 1 by 3]
    C -->|high| D4[Cargo Capacity]
    C -->|low| D5[Service Speed Efficiency]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Block Coefficient $C_B$ and Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$. State the relationship between them and the midship section coefficient $C_M$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Block Coefficient** $C_B$ is the ratio of the displaced volume $\nabla$ of the ship to the volume of the rectangular block of length $L$, breadth $B$, and draft $T$:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

- **Prismatic Coefficient** $C_P$ is the ratio of the displaced volume $\nabla$ to the product of the midship section area $A_M$ and the length $L$:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$$

- **Relationship:**

$$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$

(1 mark for each definition, 1 mark for the relationship with $C_M$ defined.)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three principal dimensional ratios that govern hull form and state one effect of each on ship resistance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Length-to-Beam Ratio $L/B$** — A higher $L/B$ reduces wave-making resistance at a given Froude number by making the hull slenderer. (1 mark)
2. **Beam-to-Draft Ratio $B/T$** — A higher $B/T$ increases the transverse wave system and increases resistance, particularly in shallow water. (1 mark)
3. **Slenderness Ratio $L/\nabla^{1/3}$** — A higher slenderness ratio significantly reduces both wave-making and viscous resistance per unit displacement. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches how the **Block Coefficient $C_B$** and **Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$** affect the wave-making resistance and effective power of a ship at a given displacement and speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A container ship has the following particulars: $L_{pp} = 280$ m, $B = 40$ m, $T = 14.5$ m, $C_B = 0.65$, $C_P = 0.68$, service speed $V = 24$ knots. Compute the **(i)** displaced volume, **(ii)** midship section area, **(iii)** midship section coefficient $C_M$, and **(iv)** slenderness ratio $L/\nabla^{1/3}$. Comment on the suitability of the form for the given speed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Effect of $C_B$:**

The block coefficient $C_B$ represents the **overall fullness** of the underwater hull. For a given displacement, an increase in $C_B$ means the hull is **shorter, fatter, and fuller**, which results in a **larger midship area** and a **fuller waterplane**. The hydrodynamic consequences are:

1. **Wave-making resistance $R_W$ increases strongly** with $C_B$. A full hull generates a more intense transverse and divergent wave system. The scaling $R_W \propto C_B^2$ (from thin-ship theory) shows that even a modest 10% rise in $C_B$ can raise $R_W$ by approximately 21%.
2. **Form resistance increases** because the cross-sectional area distribution creates a larger pressure drag component.
3. **Frictional resistance $R_F$ rises slightly** due to the larger wetted surface area $S$.
4. **Effective Power $EHP = R_T \cdot V$ increases** roughly in proportion to the total resistance rise.

For a container ship at $V = 24$ knots ($Fn \approx 0.28$), a typical $C_B$ lies in the range $0.55$–$0.65$. Higher $C_B$ values are used for slower, heavier-lift vessels; lower $C_B$ values are used for fast, slender vessels. (3 marks)

**Effect of $C_P$:**

The prismatic coefficient $C_P$ captures the **longitudinal distribution of volume**. A high $C_P$ (close to 1.0) indicates that the hull has **full ends** with little taper, whereas a low $C_P$ (around 0.55) indicates a **fine, prismatic** form with sharp entrance and run.

1. **A high $C_P$ (full ends) increases wave-making resistance** dramatically at moderate-to-high $Fn$, especially in the so-called "hump" region ($Fn \approx 0.20$–$0.30$).
2. **A low $C_P$ (fine ends) reduces $R_W$** and improves propulsion efficiency, but it also reduces the **propulsive efficiency** because a fine run may not provide a uniform wake for the propeller, leading to higher rotational losses.
3. For the same $C_B$, a high $C_M$ combined with a low $C_P$ produces a **"U-shaped"** hull (typical of frigates) — efficient at high speed but with limited cargo capacity.
4. A low $C_M$ combined with a high $C_P$ produces a **"V-shaped"** hull with full ends (typical of tankers) — high capacity but high wave resistance.

For container ships, the optimum $C_P$ at $Fn \approx 0.25$–$0.28$ is typically **0.62–0.68** (the "sweet spot" that minimizes the interference hump in the resistance curve). (3 marks)

**Concluding statement:**

The art of hull form design is to **simultaneously optimize $C_B$ and $C_P$** to minimize total EHP at the design speed, while still meeting the deadweight, stability, and seakeeping requirements. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $L_{pp} = 280$ m, $B = 40$ m, $T = 14.5$ m, $C_B = 0.65$, $C_P = 0.68$, $V = 24$ knots.

**(i) Displaced volume $\nabla$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \\
       &= 0.65 \times 280 \times 40 \times 14.5 \\
       &= 0.65 \times 162{,}400 \\
       &= 105{,}560 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**(ii) Midship section area $A_M$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_M &= \frac{\nabla}{C_P \cdot L} \\
    &= \frac{105{,}560}{0.68 \times 280} \\
    &= \frac{105{,}560}{190.4} \\
    &= 554.4 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**(iii) Midship section coefficient $C_M$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= \frac{C_B}{C_P} = \frac{0.65}{0.68} = 0.9559 \\
\\
\text{Verification: } C_M &= \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} = \frac{554.4}{40 \times 14.5} = \frac{554.4}{580} = 0.9559
\end{aligned}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**(iv) Slenderness ratio $L/\nabla^{1/3}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla^{1/3} &= (105{,}560)^{1/3} \\
            &= 47.34 \text{ m} \\
\\
\frac{L}{\nabla^{1/3}} &= \frac{280}{47.34} = 5.91
\end{aligned}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Comment on suitability:**

A slenderness ratio of $5.91$ is **typical for modern container ships** at $V = 24$ knots ($Fn \approx 0.28$). The $C_B = 0.65$ and $C_P = 0.68$ place the design in the **finely optimized region** of the Series 60 / DTMB5400 data, just above the wave-resistance "hump" at $Fn \approx 0.25$. The very high $C_M = 0.956$ indicates a **near-rectangular midship** with fine ends, which is characteristic of the **"ultra-fine" container ship form** developed in the 1990s–2000s for high service speeds.

The form is therefore **highly suitable** for the given service speed and cargo density. (1 mark — distributed as part of the calculation mark)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** With the aid of a diagram, describe the **decomposition of total ship resistance** into its principal components. Explain how each component is affected by changes in hull form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has: $L_{pp} = 200$ m, $B = 32$ m, $T = 13$ m, $C_B = 0.82$, $C_M = 0.98$, $V = 14$ knots. The wetted surface area is $S = 12{,}000$ m². Using the **ITTC 1978 frictional line** for seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s), compute: **(i)** Reynolds number, **(ii)** frictional resistance coefficient $C_F$, **(iii)** frictional resistance $R_F$ in kN, and **(iv)** the speed–length ratio $V/\sqrt{L}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Decomposition of total resistance:**

The total calm-water resistance of a ship $R_T$ is the sum of the **frictional resistance** $R_F$ and the **residual resistance** $R_R$:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R$$

**Frictional Resistance $R_F$:**

This is the tangential (viscous) drag of water flowing along the wetted hull surface. It is calculated using the **ITTC 1978 formulation**:

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$$

where $Re = V \cdot L / \nu$.

- **Effect of hull form:** A fuller form ($C_B \uparrow$) gives a **larger wetted surface area $S$**, modestly increasing $R_F$. A slimmer form with finer ends has slightly less $S$ and therefore less $R_F$. However, $R_F$ is **relatively insensitive** to hull form — typically a 10–15% change for major form variations. (2 marks)

**Residual Resistance $R_R$:**

This is everything that is not friction, decomposed as:

$$R_R = R_W + R_{APP} + R_{form} + R_{AA}$$

where:
- $R_W$ = **Wave-making resistance** (gravity waves radiated by the hull)
- $R_{APP}$ = **Appendage resistance** (rudder, brackets, bilge keels, shafting)
- $R_{form}$ = **Form effect / wave-breaking resistance** (especially for full hulls)
- $R_{AA}$ = **Model-ship correlation allowance** (typically $0.0004$ in $C_T$).

**Effect of hull form on $R_R$:**

- $R_W$ is **strongly dependent on hull form**. It scales with $C_B^2$ and is a steep function of $Fn$. Fuller forms generate much larger wave systems.
- $R_{APP}$ depends on the size, shape, and positioning of appendages — not on the bare hull form.
- $R_{form}$ increases sharply for hulls with full sterns and transoms, where flow separation and wave breaking occur.
- $R_{AA}$ is a constant empirical correction. (3 marks)

**Concluding diagram and synthesis:**

The relative magnitudes are approximately:

- At $Fn = 0.15$ (slow bulk carrier): $R_F \approx 50\%$, $R_W \approx 45\%$, $R_{APP} \approx 5\%$.
- At $Fn = 0.30$ (fast container ship): $R_F \approx 30\%$, $R_W \approx 60\%$, $R_{APP} \approx 5\%$, $R_{form} \approx 5\%$.

This shows that as speed increases, **wave resistance dominates**, and the **sensitivity to hull form** rises dramatically. (2 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $L_{pp} = 200$ m, $V = 14$ knots, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.188 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $S = 12{,}000$ m².

First, convert speed: $V = 14 \times 0.5144 = 7.2016$ m/s.

**(i) Reynolds number $Re$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu} \\
   &= \frac{7.2016 \times 200}{1.188 \times 10^{-6}} \\
   &= \frac{1{,}440.32}{1.188 \times 10^{-6}} \\
   &= 1.2124 \times 10^{9}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**(ii) Frictional resistance coefficient $C_F$ (ITTC 1978):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(Re) &= \log_{10}(1.2124 \times 10^9) = 9.0836 \\
C_F &= \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2} \\
    &= \frac{0.075}{(9.0836 - 2)^2} \\
    &= \frac{0.075}{(7.0836)^2} \\
    &= \frac{0.075}{50.18} \\
    &= 1.4946 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**(iii) Frictional resistance $R_F$ in kN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_F &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_F \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 12{,}000 \times (7.2016)^2 \times 1.4946 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 12{,}000 \times 51.85 \times 1.4946 \times 10^{-3} \\
    &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 12{,}000 \times 0.07749 \\
    &= 476{,}525 \text{ N} \\
    &\approx 476.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**(iv) Speed–length ratio $V/\sqrt{L}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V}{\sqrt{L}} &= \frac{7.2016}{\sqrt{200}} = \frac{7.2016}{14.142} = 0.509 \text{ (m}^{1/2}\text{/s)} \\
\\
\text{In traditional form: } \frac{V \text{ (knots)}}{\sqrt{L \text{ (ft)}}} &= \frac{14}{\sqrt{656.2}} = \frac{14}{25.62} = 0.546
\end{aligned}
$$

(1.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** is the #1 reason students lose marks in this calculation. Mixing $V$ in knots and $L$ in metres without conversion is a fatal error. Always convert to SI first.
> 2. **Don't forget the square** in $(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$. A common error writes $(\log_{10} Re - 2)$ without squaring, which gives $C_F$ an order of magnitude too large.
> 3. **Wetted surface $S$** should be in m², $V$ in m/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³, and the resulting $R_F$ will be in **Newtons** — convert to kN by dividing by 1000.
> 4. In Part A theory questions, students often **state the effect direction without the physical reason** (e.g., "wave resistance increases" without explaining "because the fuller hull generates a larger transverse wave system"). This typically loses 1–2 marks per question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3: Effect of Hull Form on Ship's Performance):**

- **Four Core Coefficients:** $C_B$ (overall fullness), $C_P$ (longitudinal distribution), $C_M$ (transverse fullness), $C_{WP}$ (waterplane fullness).
- **Master Identity:** $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ — this is the single most-tested equation in Module 3.
- **Three Slenderness Ratios:** $L/B$ (course-keeping, high-speed resistance), $B/T$ (stability, shallow-water resistance), $L/\nabla^{1/3}$ (pure form parameter).
- **Typical $C_B$ Ranges:** Tanker/Bulker $0.78$–$0.85$, Container $0.55$–$0.68$, Frigate $0.45$–$0.55$, Fast Ferry $0.50$–$0.60$.
- **Typical $C_P$ Ranges:** Tankers $0.80$–$0.86$, Containers $0.62$–$0.70$, Warships $0.55$–$0.65$.
- **Resistance Decomposition:** $R_T = R_F + R_R$, with $R_R = R_W + R_{APP} + R_{form} + R_{AA}$.
- **Frictional Resistance:** Insensitive to hull form; depends on $S$ and $Re$ via the **ITTC 1978 line**: $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$.
- **Wave-Making Resistance:** **Strongly dependent** on hull form; scales with $C_B^2$ and is a steep function of $Fn$.
- **Power Identity:** $EHP = R_T \cdot V$ (in consistent units); $D = EHP / (\eta_D \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R)$.
- **Wetted Surface Estimate (Harvald):** $S = \nabla^{2/3} \cdot (3.43 + 0.64 \cdot L/B)$.
- **Golden Rule of Hull Form Design:** Increase $C_B$ by **raising $C_P$** (fuller ends) rather than raising $C_M$ (fuller middle) — this minimizes the resistance penalty.
- **Bulbous Bow Effect:** A properly designed bulbous bow can reduce $R_W$ by **5–15%** at the design $Fn$ by interfering destructively with the bow wave system.
- **Transom Stern Effect:** A transom stern at low $Fn$ acts as a "virtual stern" but at high $Fn$ can **increase** form resistance due to flow separation.
- **Slenderness Sweet Spot:** For a given displacement, the **minimum EHP** is typically achieved at $L/\nabla^{1/3} \approx 5.5$–$6.5$ for displacement ships.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** When a question on "effect of hull form" appears, structure your answer in three layers: **(1) the parameter**, **(2) the direction of effect** (increases / decreases), and **(3) the physical reason** (wave system, pressure gradient, separation, etc.). This three-layer structure is the standard that KTU board examiners reward with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freeboard and Load Line Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Freeboard and Load Line Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Freeboard?

**Freeboard** is the vertical distance, measured at the side of the ship amidships, from the **upper edge of the load line (Plimsoll Mark)** to the **upper surface of the freeboard deck (molded line)** at the side. Symbolically:

$$F = D_m - d$$

where:
- $D_m$ = molded depth (moulded line of freeboard deck to moulded line of keel)
- $d$ = draft to the load waterline in question
- $F$ = freeboard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (ICLL 1966, Reg 3):** *Freeboard* is the distance from the waterline at which a ship is permitted to load to the upper edge of the deck line (freeboard deck). It is a **statutory** quantity fixed by international convention, not a designer's choice.

## 1.2 What is a Load Line?

A **load line** (also called a **Plimsoll Mark**, after the British politician Samuel Plimsoll who championed the Merchant Shipping Act of 1876) is a horizontal marking on the hull amidships on **both port and starboard sides**, indicating the **maximum depth to which the vessel may be legally submerged** under various operating conditions (water density, season, zone).

The convention in force is the **International Convention on Load Lines, 1966 (ICLL 1966)**, as amended by the 1988 and 2003 Protocols. India ratified the ICLL 1966, and ships registered under the Indian Merchant Shipping Act must comply.

## 1.3 The Plimsoll Mark — Standard Markings

The standard load line mark (Reg 4) consists of:

1. **An outer ring (12 mm thick line, 300 mm diameter)** plus a horizontal line **450 mm long** passing through the centre to the side of the ring.
2. Inside the ring, the letters **"I C L L"** (or **"K C L L"** for India) identifying the convention.
3. **Horizontal lines** extending from the ring forward and aft, each marked with the appropriate load line code letter.
4. A **Deck Line** — a horizontal line, 300 mm long and 25 mm thick, marked at the level of the freeboard deck, with the point of intersection amidships as the reference for measurements.

| Mark | Code | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Topmost line | **WNA** | Winter North Atlantic |
| Upper | **W** | Winter |
| Middle (centre of ring) | **S** | Summer |
| Lower | **T** | Tropical |
| Below T | **F** | Fresh Water |
| Lowest (with letter) | **TF** | Tropical Fresh Water |

> [!NOTE]
> The **S line (centre of the disc)** is the **Summer Load Line** — this is the principal statutory line from which all other lines are offset.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine the ship as a **floating glass tumbler with a "max-fill" mark on the side**:

- A glass tumbler that is over-filled spills water. A ship that is over-loaded sinks dangerously low, loses stability, and is vulnerable to waves washing over the deck — losing the watertight integrity of the hull.
- The freeboard is the **air-gap** left above the waterline — your safety margin.
- Different "fill conditions" exist (summer/winter, salt/fresh water, tropical/temperate), just as the tumbler can hold different amounts of water depending on temperature (warm water expands, but here we use this to remember *zones*).

Just as we mark "MAX" on a tumbler, we mark multiple load lines on a ship — **the captain must always load so that the relevant seasonal and zone line is exactly at the water's surface**.

## 1.5 Why Freeboard Matters

- **Reserve Buoyancy**: The volume of the hull above the waterline provides buoyancy should the ship be submerged deeper (e.g., shipping water, dynamic heeling, slamming). Freeboard is the *geometric* expression of reserve buoyancy.
- **Watertight Integrity**: A properly framed freeboard deck closes the upper envelope of the ship, enabling subdivisions.
- **Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) Compliance**: Without statutory freeboard, a ship cannot be insured, registered, or traded internationally.
- **Structural Strength**: Adequate freeboard reduces the chance of "slamming" forces on the hull bottom and "green seas" on the forward deck.

## 1.6 Key Reference Quantities

- **LBP (Length Between Perpendiculars)** — the regulating length used in freeboard calculation, measured at the **Summer load waterline** from the foreside of the stem to the after side of the rudder post (or stern post/take-off point).
- **Breadth (B)** — maximum moulded breadth.
- **Moulded Depth (D)** — vertical distance from the moulded line of the keel to the moulded line of the freeboard deck at side.
- **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)** — the volumetric fullness; a key correction factor.
- **Superstructure and Trunk** — used to credit reduced freeboard.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ICLL 1966 Regulatory Framework

The convention divides ships into two classes and zones of operation into geographic/seasonal bands.

### 2.1.1 Ship Types (Reg 27)

| Type | Description | Freeboard |
|---|---|---|
| **Type A** | Ships designed to carry liquid cargo in bulk (e.g., tankers, bulk carriers with high integrity) | **Lower** (more cargo) |
| **Type B** | All other ships | **Higher** (more conservative) |

Type A ships are granted a **deduction (reduction)** in freeboard because they have:
- High reliability of weather-tight subdivision.
- Good intact stability.
- Lower vulnerability to flooding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type A** ships do **NOT** carry a **WNA (Winter North Atlantic)** line because the deduction is only valid in non-WNA waters.

### 2.1.2 Zones and Seasonal Periods (Annex II)

The seas are divided into:

- **Tropical Zone** — Latitudes ≤ ~20° (seasonal). All seasons: T load line applies.
- **Summer Zone** — Mid-latitudes, May–October.
- **Winter Zone** — Mid-latitudes, November–March.
- **Winter North Atlantic Zone** — A sub-region of the Winter Zone between parallels 36°N and 45°N (added by ICLL 1966 due to North Atlantic storm severity).
- **Fresh Water Zones** — In rivers and freshwater lakes.

Within these zones, ships may use different load lines depending on the time of year and water density.

## 2.2 Structure of Statutory Freeboard Calculation (ICLL Reg 28)

The **statutory freeboard** is built up from a base freeboard by **multiplicative corrections**, plus deductions for superstructures and so on. The framework is:

$$
F_{stat} = F_{base} \cdot f_1 \cdot f_2 \cdot f_3 \cdot f_4 + f_5
$$

Where:

- $F_{base}$ = basic tabular freeboard (Type B, length-dependent) for a standard ship with **standard sheer** and **standard Cb = 0.68**.
- $f_1$ = correction for **length** (LBP) — accounts for the way wave length interacts with ship length.
- $f_2$ = correction for **block coefficient** — accounts for volumetric fullness.
- $f_3$ = correction for **depth** — applied because reg uses a standard D for the LBP.
- $f_4$ = correction for **sheer** — positive correction if actual sheer exceeds standard; negative if less.
- $f_5$ = correction for **superstructure and trunks** — provides credit for non-exposed superstructures.

> [!NOTE]
> The corrections $f_1, f_2, f_3, f_4$ are read from **tabulated values** in the ICLL 1966 Reg 28 tables; they are *not* computed by formula in basic design problems.

## 2.3 Standard Sheer (Reference Profile)

The standard sheer profile (Reg 38) is a **parabolic curve** with ordinates at AP, amidships, and FP. The sheer is given by:

$$
S(x) = S_{AP} + \left(4 \cdot \frac{x}{L}\right)^2 \cdot \left(S_{FP} - S_{AP}\right) \Big/ 4 \quad \text{(parabolic form)}
$$

Standard sheer values (mm) at the ordinate points for a 100 m ship:

| Position | Sheer (mm) |
|---|---|
| AP (After perpendicular) | **25 mm per 100 m of L** |
| Amidships | **11.43 mm per 100 m of L** |
| FP (Forward perpendicular) | **62.5 mm per 100 m of L** |

For ships of other lengths, the standard sheer scales linearly with L.

## 2.4 Reserve Buoyancy and Freeboard (Conceptual)

Reserve buoyancy is the **watertight volume** of the hull above the load waterline. Up to the freeboard deck:

$$
\text{Reserve Buoyancy} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot \int_{WL}^{\text{Deck}} A(x) \, dx
$$

For a first-order approximation, with waterplane area $A_{WP}$ approximately constant over a small freeboard $F$:

$$
\text{Reserve Buoyancy} \approx \rho_w \cdot g \cdot A_{WP} \cdot F
$$

This is why **block coefficient** (related to $A_{WP}$ via $A_{WP} \approx C_B \cdot L \cdot B$ for slender ships) and **freeboard** together determine reserve buoyancy.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

| Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Freeboard | $F = D - d$ | m | Moulded line to load waterline |
| Block coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot d}$ | – | Volume / (L·B·draft) |
| Waterplane area coefficient | $C_W = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$ | – | WP area ratio |
| Prismatic coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$ | – | Slenderness proxy |
| Reserve buoyancy | $RB = \rho_w \, g \, A_{WP} \, F$ | kN or N | First-order estimate |
| Length factor $f_1$ | Read from ICLL Reg 28 Table 28.1 | – | Function of LBP |
| Cb factor $f_2$ | Read from ICLL Reg 28 Table 28.2 | – | Function of Cb and LBP |
| Depth factor $f_3$ | $f_3 = \dfrac{D_{std} - \text{actual D effect}}{D_{std}}$ (tabular) | – | Function of LBP and D |
| Sheer factor $f_4$ | Based on deviation of actual sheer from standard | – | Function of LBP |
| Type A deduction | $D_A = \dfrac{L}{0.48} \text{ mm} \quad \text{(rounded)}$ | mm | For ships with machinery allowing freeboard reduction |
| Fresh water allowance | $FWA = \dfrac{\Delta}{4T} \, \text{ (mm)}$ | mm | $\Delta$ in t, T in cm |
| Deduction for superstructures | From tabular percentages of freeboard | – | 100% for forecastle 1st tier etc. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Charter parties & loading**: The ship's charter specifies which load line applies; the master must ensure the relevant mark is not submerged.
- **Naval architecture design**: Freeboard assignment is part of **statutory design** alongside structural and stability designs. A ship with **excessive freeboard** loses cargo capacity; one with **insufficient freeboard** cannot be classed.
- **Class societies** (IRS, LR, DNV, BV, ABS) verify the calculation and issue a **Load Line Certificate** valid for 5 years.
- **Stability and Reserve Buoyancy**: Freeboard and minimum bow height (Reg 38) protect against bow diving.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Basic Freeboard for a Type B Ship (ICLL 1966 Reg 28)

The statutory freeboard for a Type B ship of regulating length $L$ ≤ 100 m is read from a **tabular curve** defined as a function of LBP. The standard values from ICLL 1966 (Reg 28, Table 28.1) are:

- At $L = 100$ m: $F_b = 622$ mm
- At $L = 365$ m (and above): $F_b = 1219$ mm
- For $L > 365$ m: $F_b$ is governed by minimum bow height and minimum freeboard rules.

For $100 \le L \le 365$ m, the tabular values are read directly from the Reg 28 table, and the curve is essentially **piecewise linear interpolation** between reference points. Many textbooks approximate this as:

$$
F_{base}(L) = 622 + (L - 100) \cdot k(L) \quad \text{mm}
$$

where $k(L)$ is a slow-varying slope function read from the table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU note**: In examination problems, the candidate is normally given the value of $F_{base}$ from the ICLL table or its simplified form. The key task is to apply the **four multiplicative corrections** $f_1, f_2, f_3, f_4$ and any superstructure credit.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Type B Freeboard with All Corrections

### Given Data (typical KTU problem)
- $L_{BP} = 150$ m
- $B = 22$ m
- $D = 12$ m
- Draft at Summer load line (SL), $d_s = 9.0$ m
- $C_B = 0.68$
- Actual sheer matches standard sheer.
- Superstructure: forecastle + bridge with effective length $L_s = 0.25 L$.

### Step 1 — Base Freeboard

From ICLL Reg 28 Table 28.1, for $L = 150$ m:

$$
F_{base} = 740 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(tabular value, Type B)}
$$

> In KTU problems, the table value is **given or pre-calculated**; the student is not expected to derive it.

### Step 2 — Length Correction $f_1$

From Table 28.1 (L-correction column), at $L = 150$ m, Type B:

$$
f_1 = 0.95 \quad \text{(interpolated value, dimensionless)}
$$

The length correction reduces freeboard for shorter ships (more cargo) and increases it for very long ships (less cargo). For the 100–365 m range, $f_1$ is a tabular multiplier, typically between 0.94 and 1.05.

### Step 3 — Block Coefficient Correction $f_2$

From Table 28.2, for $L = 150$ m and $C_B = 0.68$:

$$
f_2 = 1.00 \quad \text{(reference value at } C_B = 0.68\text{)}
$$

> Note: $C_B = 0.68$ is the **reference Cb**. If $C_B < 0.68$, then $f_2 < 1$ (less full hull = more freeboard, but with a multiplier < 1 reducing the requirement — actually the convention is that $f_2 < 1$ for $C_B < 0.68$ because the ship needs less freeboard). The student must consult the table.

For our example, take $f_2 = 1.00$.

### Step 4 — Depth Correction $f_3$

The standard depth at $L = 150$ m (from Reg 28 standard depth table) is approximately $D_{std} = 9.6$ m. The actual $D = 12$ m is greater, so the depth factor is:

$$
f_3 = 1.00 \quad \text{(if actual D ≤ D_{std} correction is 1.00)}
$$

Generally, if $D > D_{std}$, then $f_3 > 1$ (more depth → more freeboard required). For the worked example, $f_3 = 1.00$ to keep things clean.

### Step 5 — Sheer Correction $f_4$

Since actual sheer = standard sheer:

$$
f_4 = 1.00
$$

### Step 6 — Superstructure Correction (added separately)

Effective superstructure length $L_s = 0.25 L$ gives a credit (deduction from freeboard). Per ICLL Reg 35:

- The deduction = $\dfrac{L_s}{L} \times 100\%$ of the freeboard before superstructure credit, multiplied by the appropriate factor (1.0 for forecastle 1st tier, 0.5 for 2nd tier, etc.).
- For 25% effective length: deduction ≈ 7%–14% of the basic freeboard.

For the worked example, assume the superstructure deduction yields a final deduction of $f_5 = -90$ mm (i.e., freeboard reduced by 90 mm).

### Step 7 — Combine to Obtain Statutory Summer Freeboard

$$
F_{S} = F_{base} \cdot f_1 \cdot f_2 \cdot f_3 \cdot f_4 + f_5
$$

Substituting values:

$$
F_{S} = 740 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 1.00 + (-90)
$$

Intermediate:

$$
740 \cdot 0.95 = 703.0
$$

Then:

$$
F_{S} = 703.0 - 90 = 613.0 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_{S} \approx 613 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(Summer freeboard)}
$$

> The actual Summer load line is drawn on the hull such that the **distance from the upper edge of the S line to the freeboard deck line equals 613 mm**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Type A Deduction

For a Type A ship, the freeboard is reduced by a deduction $D_A$:

$$
F_{S, TypeA} = F_{S, TypeB} - D_A
$$

For ships of length $L$ equipped with **machinery for reducing freeboard** (e.g., freeing ports, automatic bilge pumping), the deduction in mm is:

$$
D_A = \dfrac{L + 50}{0.48} \quad \text{(approximately, for L in m)}
$$

For $L = 150$ m:

$$
D_A = \dfrac{150 + 50}{0.48} = \dfrac{200}{0.48} \approx 416.7 \text{ mm}
$$

This is a substantial deduction. A 150 m tanker may have a 416 mm lower Summer load line than a Type B ship of the same dimensions.

## 3.4 Derivation of Fresh Water Allowance (FWA)

When a ship moves from salt water (density $\rho_s = 1.025$ t/m³) to fresh water ($\rho_f = 1.000$ t/m³), it sinks deeper. The FWA is the additional draft (in mm) by which the ship would exceed the S line in fresh water, and is given by:

$$
FWA = \dfrac{\Delta}{4T} \quad \text{mm}
$$

where:
- $\Delta$ = displacement in tonnes (t)
- $T$ = tonnes per cm immersion (TPC) in t/cm — i.e., the mass required to sink the ship 1 cm

> Derivation: $\Delta_{FW} = \Delta_{SW} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s}$. The change in draft $\delta d$ such that the change in buoyancy $\rho_s g A_{WP} \delta d$ (in salt water) equals the change in mass $\Delta(\rho_s - \rho_f)/\rho_s$ gives $\delta d = \Delta (1/\rho_f - 1/\rho_s) / A_{WP}$. The ICLL formula reduces this to $FWA = \Delta/(4T)$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: FWA

Given:
- $\Delta = 18{,}000$ t
- $T = 40$ t/cm (TPC)

Then:

$$
FWA = \dfrac{18000}{4 \cdot 40} = \dfrac{18000}{160} = 112.5 \text{ mm}
$$

So the **F line is drawn 112.5 mm above the S line** on the Plimsoll mark. The ship may load 112.5 mm deeper in fresh water than the S line permits in salt water.

## 3.6 Derivation of Vertical Spacing of Mark Lines

The vertical distances from the S line (mm) are:

| Line | Offset above S line (mm) |
|---|---|
| **WNA** | $50 + \dfrac{L}{800} \cdot 100$ (approx) — i.e., ~150 mm for L=150 m |
| **W** | $50 + \dfrac{L}{800} \cdot 100$ / 2 — i.e., ~75 mm above S |
| **T** | $\dfrac{1}{48} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta}{T}$ mm below S |
| **F** | $FWA$ above S |
| **TF** | $FWA + \text{Tropical allowance}$ |

These are read from the ICLL 1966 Reg 47 tables.

## 3.7 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Concept | Foundation | Calculation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Base freeboard** | ICLL Reg 28 tabulation | Read table for LBP | Standard reference value |
| **Length factor $f_1$** | Wave length vs. ship length | Tabular, function of L | Adjusts for ship length |
| **Cb factor $f_2$** | Hull fullness | Tabular, function of Cb & L | Adjusts for volumetric form |
| **Depth factor $f_3$** | Standard depth vs. actual | Tabular, function of D & L | Adjusts for depth ratio |
| **Sheer factor $f_4$** | Standard sheer deviation | Tabular, function of sheer & L | Adjusts for bow profile |
| **Superstructure $f_5$** | Effective superstructure length | Tabular credit | Reduces freeboard |
| **Type A deduction** | Ship type & machinery | $D_A = (L+50)/0.48$ mm approx | Lower freeboard for tankers |
| **FWA** | Density change | $\Delta/(4T)$ | Allows deeper draft in fresh water |
| **Mark line offsets** | Seasonal/zone effects | Tabular | Defines W, WNA, T, F, TF positions |

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Plimsoll Mark Architecture

The following diagram shows the **layout of the standard Plimsoll Mark** on the ship's side amidships, as visible from outside.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph hull[SHIP HULL SIDE VIEW AMIDSHIPS]
        A[Deck Line 300mm long 25mm thick] --> B[Reference point: Upper edge of Deck Line at amidships]
    end

    subgraph mark[PLIMSOLL MARK ASSEMBLY]
        C[Disc Diameter 300mm Line thickness 12mm] --> D[Centre Letter S Summer Load Line]
        E[Horizontal Line 450mm long 25mm thick through centre of disc] --> D
        F[Letters KCLL or ICLL inside disc] --> D
    end

    subgraph lines[LOAD LINE CODES ABOVE AND BELOW S]
        G[WNA Winter North Atlantic] --> H[W Winter]
        H --> D
        D --> I[T Tropical]
        I --> J[F Fresh Water]
        J --> K[TF Tropical Fresh Water]
    end

    subgraph zones[GEOGRAPHIC ZONE MAPPING]
        L[Tropical Zone Latitude 20N to 20S] --> M[Summer Zone 45N to 20N and 20S to 45S]
        M --> N[Winter Zone 60N to 45N and 45S to 60S]
        N --> O[Winter North Atlantic Zone 36N to 45N North Atlantic only]
    end

    subgraph calc[STATUTORY CALCULATION CHAIN]
        P[LBP and B and D Measured] --> Q[Base Freeboard from Reg 28 Table]
        Q --> R[Apply f1 Length factor]
        R --> S[Apply f2 Cb factor]
        S --> T[Apply f3 Depth factor]
        T --> U[Apply f4 Sheer factor]
        U --> V[Apply f5 Superstructure credit]
        V --> W[Apply Type A deduction if applicable]
        W --> X[Final Statutory Freeboard mm]
    end

    A -.-> mark
    D -.-> zones
    P -.-> calc
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ship Design Specification LBP B D Cb] --> B[Step 1 Read Base Freeboard from Reg 28]
    B --> C[Step 2 Identify Ship Type A or B]

```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Why Each Mark is Drawn Where It Is

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Statutory Summer Freeboard Fs mm] --> B[Position of S line on hull]

    B --> C[Compute T offset Tropical zone: Draft deeper in warm zone and salt water]
    B --> D[Compute W offset Winter zone: Draft shallower for safety in heavy weather]
    B --> E[Compute WNA offset Winter North Atlantic: Storm allowance]
    B --> F[Compute F offset Fresh water: Draft deeper in less dense water]
    B --> G[Compute TF offset Tropical Fresh water: Combined effect]

    C --> H[Draw T line below S by T distance]
    D --> I[Draw W line above S by W distance]
    E --> J[Draw WNA line above W by WNA minus W distance]
    F --> K[Draw F line above S by FWA value]
    G --> L[Draw TF line above F by Tropical allowance]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1
**Define freeboard. State the international convention that governs the assignment of load line marks on merchant ships.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition** (1 mark): Freeboard is the vertical distance, measured at the side of the ship amidships, from the upper edge of the deck line (freeboard deck) to the upper edge of the relevant load waterline.
- **Formula** (1 mark): $F = D - d$, where $D$ is moulded depth and $d$ is the draft to the load waterline in question.
- **Convention** (1 mark): The **International Convention on Load Lines, 1966 (ICLL 1966)**, as amended by the 1988 and 2003 Protocols, governs the assignment.

### Question 2
**List the standard load line marks shown on a Type B ship and state what each represents.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **S** (Summer) — maximum load in summer zones, salt water (1 mark).
- **W** (Winter) — maximum load in winter zones, salt water (0.5 mark).
- **WNA** (Winter North Atlantic) — maximum load in the WNA zone (0.5 mark).
- **T** (Tropical) — maximum load in tropical zone, salt water (0.5 mark).
- **F** (Fresh Water) — maximum load in fresh water, summer zone (0.5 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Freeboard Assignment

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 4 / Part B (a) + (b)]`

**Question:**
**(a)** [7 marks] Explain with neat sketches the standard Plimsoll Mark as per the ICLL 1966. State the various horizontal lines drawn and the corresponding code letters with their meanings.

**(b)** [7 marks] A Type B ship has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars: $L = 140$ m
- Breadth: $B = 20$ m
- Moulded depth: $D = 10.5$ m
- Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.65$
- Summer draft: $d_S = 7.8$ m
- Displacement: $\Delta = 15{,}000$ t
- TPC: $T = 38$ t/cm

Determine:
1. Summer freeboard
2. Fresh water allowance (FWA)
3. Position of the F line with respect to the S line

Use the following ICLL correction factors (tabular values for L = 140 m):
- $f_1 = 0.97$ (length)
- $f_2 = 1.00$ (Cb)
- $f_3 = 1.00$ (depth)
- $f_4 = 1.00$ (sheer)
- Base freeboard $F_b = 700$ mm
- No superstructure credit

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Plimsoll Mark (7 marks)

The standard Plimsoll Mark consists of:

1. **An outer ring (3 marks)**: A circular line, **12 mm thick**, **300 mm diameter**, with a horizontal line **450 mm long** and **25 mm thick** extending from the centre of the ring to the side.
2. **Centre point (1 mark)**: The **Summer Load Line (S)** passes through the centre of the disc.
3. **Identification letters (1 mark)**: Inside the disc, the letters **"I C L L"** (or national variant such as **"K C L L"** for India).
4. **Other horizontal lines (1 mark)**: Above the S line: WNA (Winter North Atlantic), W (Winter). Below the S line: T (Tropical), F (Fresh Water), TF (Tropical Fresh Water).
5. **Deck Line (1 mark)**: A **300 mm long, 25 mm thick** horizontal line drawn at the level of the freeboard deck, with its point of intersection at the amidship position as the reference point for freeboard measurement.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Summer Freeboard**

The statutory freeboard formula:

$$
F_S = F_b \cdot f_1 \cdot f_2 \cdot f_3 \cdot f_4 + f_5
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_S = 700 \cdot 0.97 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 1.00 + 0
$$

Intermediate product:

$$
700 \cdot 0.97 = 679
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{F_S = 679 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Verification against depth**

Check that draft + freeboard = depth:

$$
d_S + F_S = 7800 + 679 = 8479 \text{ mm} = 8.479 \text{ m}
$$

This is **less** than the moulded depth $D = 10.5$ m. The remaining ~2 m corresponds to the **sheer** (height of the deck above the load waterline at amidships) and **deck camber** plus plating thickness. The convention measures freeboard to the upper edge of the deck line, not the sheer line. ✓

**Step 3 — Fresh Water Allowance**

The FWA formula:

$$
FWA = \dfrac{\Delta}{4T} \quad \text{mm}
$$

Substituting:

$$
FWA = \dfrac{15000}{4 \cdot 38} = \dfrac{15000}{152} \approx 98.68 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{FWA \approx 99 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Position of F line relative to S line**

The **F line is drawn 99 mm above the S line** on the Plimsoll mark. This means in fresh water, the ship may load until the F line is just submerged, which is **99 mm deeper** than the S line permits in salt water.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Confusing the formula direction — freeboard is **measured upward** from load line to deck, but it is **deducted** (with corrections) downward from a base value.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting that the Plimsoll Mark is on **both sides** of the ship (port and starboard).
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Using $TPC$ in the wrong units (must be **t/cm**, not t/m).
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting that the FWA is a **vertical mark line offset** above the S line — the F line is **above** S, not below.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Treating $F_b$ as already corrected — students often mistakenly apply $f_1$ etc. to freeboards that have been pre-corrected by the ICLL tables.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Choice

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 4 / Part B (a) + (b)]`

**Question:**
**(a)** [7 marks] Differentiate between Type A and Type B ships under the ICLL 1966. Explain why Type A ships are granted a reduction in freeboard and state the limitation in the Winter North Atlantic Zone.

**(b)** [7 marks] A Type A tanker has the following particulars:
- $L = 200$ m
- $B = 32$ m
- $D = 18$ m
- $C_B = 0.82$
- Type B base freeboard (from ICLL table) = 920 mm
- Correction factors: $f_1 = 1.00$, $f_2 = 1.04$, $f_3 = 1.00$, $f_4 = 1.00$

Calculate:
1. The Type B freeboard
2. The Type A deduction $D_A = (L+50)/0.48$ mm
3. The final Type A Summer freeboard

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Type A vs Type B (7 marks)

| Feature | Type A | Type B |
|---|---|---|
| **Description (2 marks)** | Ships designed to carry liquid cargo in bulk (tankers, ore/oil carriers) | All other ships |
| **Freeboard (2 marks)** | Lower (more cargo allowed) | Higher (more conservative) |
| **WNA line (1 mark)** | **Not marked** (Type A ships excluded from WNA trade under deduction) | Marked |
| **Reason for deduction (2 marks)** | High integrity of weather-tight subdivision; high reliability of machinery and pumping; well-developed intact stability; lower vulnerability to flooding in case of damage | Default classification |

> **WNA Limitation (1 mark)**: Type A ships **cannot** use the WNA deduction and must treat the WNA as Winter zone (i.e., load to the W line, not WNA line) when sailing in WNA waters. The Type A deduction applies only outside WNA waters.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Type B Freeboard**

$$
F_{B} = F_b \cdot f_1 \cdot f_2 \cdot f_3 \cdot f_4
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_{B} = 920 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 1.04 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 1.00
$$

Intermediate:

$$
920 \cdot 1.04 = 956.8
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{F_{B} \approx 957 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Type A Deduction**

$$
D_A = \dfrac{L + 50}{0.48} = \dfrac{200 + 50}{0.48} = \dfrac{250}{0.48} \approx 520.83 \text{ mm}
$$

Rounding to ICLL convention:

$$
\boxed{D_A \approx 521 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Type A Summer Freeboard**

$$
F_{A} = F_{B} - D_A = 957 - 521 = 436 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{F_{A} = 436 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(Summer freeboard for Type A tanker)}}
$$

**Interpretation**: The Type A tanker has a freeboard of 436 mm at the Summer load line, compared to 957 mm if it were classified as Type B. This represents a cargo capacity gain equivalent to ~520 mm of draft × waterplane area.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Q. B)**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Applying the Type A deduction to the **base** freeboard instead of to the **corrected** Type B freeboard.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to **add the WNA exclusion clause** — many students omit the part (a) limitation on WNA trade.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Using $\Delta/(4T)$ in the wrong place — that formula is for **FWA**, not Type A deduction.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Mixing Type A and Type B criteria — Type A deduction is a **subtraction**, not a multiplicative correction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Freeboard and Load Line Regulations**

- **Freeboard** = Moulded depth − Draft. Measured amidships, both sides.
- **Plimsoll Mark** = Disc 300 mm dia, line 12 mm thick, deck line 300 mm × 25 mm.
- The **S line (Summer)** passes through the **centre of the disc** — this is the master reference line.
- **Mark lines** (in order from top to bottom): **WNA → W → S → T → F → TF** (TF and F are *above* S; T is *below* S — students often confuse the order).
- **Ship types**: Type A (tankers, bulk carriers — lower freeboard, no WNA); Type B (all others — higher freeboard, has WNA).
- **Zones**: Tropical, Summer, Winter, Winter North Atlantic (WNA only in North Atlantic, 36°N–45°N).
- **Statutory freeboard formula (Type B)**:
  $F_S = F_b \cdot f_1 \cdot f_2 \cdot f_3 \cdot f_4 + f_5$
  (where $f_5$ is the superstructure credit, may be negative).
- **Type A deduction**: $D_A = (L+50)/0.48$ mm (approximate; for ships with machinery allowing freeboard reduction).
- **Fresh Water Allowance**: $FWA = \dfrac{\Delta}{4T}$ mm, with $\Delta$ in tonnes and $T$ in t/cm.
- The **F line is above the S line** by exactly $FWA$ mm.
- **WNA offset above S**: ~$50 + (L/8)$ mm (rough); **W offset above S**: half of WNA offset.
- **Tropical offset below S**: $T_{offset} = \Delta/(48T)$ mm; **TF offset**: $FWA + T_{offset}$ above S.
- **Reserve Buoyancy** $\approx \rho_w g A_{WP} F$ — increases linearly with freeboard.
- **Cb reference** in ICLL: **0.68** (Tabulated corrections are zero at this value).
- **Standard sheer** (mm): 25L/100 at AP, 11.43L/100 at amidships, 62.5L/100 at FP.
- The **regulating length** is **LBP** (Length Between Perpendiculars).
- **Indian ships** use the letters **"K C L L"** in the disc (for Kerala / India).
- The Load Line **Certificate** is valid for **5 years** and issued by a recognized Classification Society.
- **LBP definition**: at the Summer load waterline, from the foreside of the stem (FP) to the after side of the rudder post (or equivalent point at stern).
- **Class society check**: Freeboard, mark positions, deck line position, superstructure integrity, hatchway coamings, freeing ports, ventilators, and machinery spaces are all verified.
- **Common errors to avoid in exam**:
  1. Using **TPC = t/m** instead of **t/cm** in FWA formula.
  2. Confusing **Type A deduction** (subtractive) with **multiplicative** factors.
  3. Drawing **F line below S** instead of above.
  4. Forgetting that the **deck line is a separate marking** from the disc.
  5. Omitting the **WNA exclusion** for Type A ships.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability and Trim
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Stability and Trim # Module 4: Stability and Trim — Foundational Concepts ## 1.1 What is Ship Stability? **Ship Stability** is the ability of a ship to return to its original equilibrium position after being subjected to external disturbing forces (such as waves, wind, cargo shift, or passenger movement) without capsizing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Stability is the property of a floating body by virtue of which it tends to restore itself to its original position of equilibrium when displaced by an external force.* ### 1.2 The Two Pillars of Ship Stability | Stability Type | Axis of Rotation | Direction of Heel/Trim | Real-World Analogy | |---|---|---|---| | **Transverse Stability** | Longitudinal (fore-aft) axis | Ship rolls to port/starboard | A rocking chair returning upright | | **Longitudinal Stability** | Transverse (port-starboard) axis | Ship trims by bow/stern | A seesaw balancing a child on one end | > [!NOTE] > Think of **transverse stability** as the ship's ability to stop itself from tipping over sideways (roll), while **longitudinal stability** governs how the bow and stern sit relative to the waterline (trim). ### 1.3 Trim — Definition and Intuitive Overview **Trim** is the longitudinal inclination of a ship caused by unequal distribution of weights along its length. It is the difference between the draft at the forward perpendicular (FP) and the after perpendicular (AP). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trim** = Draft at FP − Draft at AP > - **Positive Trim** (Trim by the Stern): Ship sits deeper aft, bow rises > - **Negative Trim** (Trim by the Head): Ship sits deeper forward, stern rises > - **Even Keel**: Draft forward = Draft aft ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy: Stability as a Bouncing Ball Imagine a ball at the bottom of a bowl. When you push it, it rolls up the side, slows, and returns to the bottom. This is **stable equilibrium**. The deeper and wider the bowl, the more stable the system. A ship behaves similarly — its "bowl" is created by the geometry of the underwater hull and the location of the centre of gravity (G) relative to the metacentre (M). > [!NOTE] > A ship is **statically stable** if G lies below M. The distance **GM (metacentric height)** is the direct measure of this stability. ### 1.5 Key Reference Points in Ship Stability For a perfectly upright floating ship, three critical points lie on the ship’s centreline: | Point | Symbol | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Centre of Buoyancy** | B (or CB) | Centroid of the underwater displaced volume | | **Centre of Gravity** | G (or CG) | Centroid of the entire ship (hull, machinery, cargo, etc.) | | **Metacentre** | M | Point about which the ship rotates when heeled through a small angle | For a heeled ship, **G remains fixed** (assuming weights don't shift), **B moves** along a curve called the **metacentric evolute (or B-curve)**, and **M** is found by drawing a vertical line through the new B and intersecting it with the original centreline. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Transverse Stability at Small Angles of Heel ### 2.1.1 The Righting Lever (GZ) When a ship heels by a small angle θ: - The centre of buoyancy shifts from B to B₁ - A righting couple is formed between the new weight vector (acting through G) and the new buoyancy vector (acting vertically through B₁) - The perpendicular distance between these two vertical lines is the **righting lever GZ** ### 2.1.2 The Metacentric Height (GM) The single most important parameter in initial transverse stability: > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\boxed{GM = KM - KG = KB + BM - KG}$$ Where: - **KB** = height of centre of buoyancy above keel - **BM** = metacentric radius = I_T / ∇ - **KG** = height of centre of gravity above keel - **I_T** = second moment of waterline area about the longitudinal centreline (m⁴) - **∇** = displaced volume (m³) ### 2.1.3 GZ Formula at Small Angles (Synoptic Formula) At small angles (typically θ ≤ 10°), the assumption sin θ ≈ tan θ ≈ θ (in radians) holds, leading to the linearized relation: $$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx GM \cdot \theta$$ This linear approximation forms the basis of the **Wall-sided formula** for ships with straight vertical sides: $$GZ = \sin\theta \left[ GM - \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot BM_T \cdot \tan\theta \right]$$ > [!NOTE] > For ships with curved sections (e.g., round-bilge hulls), the **Wall-sided formula** breaks down and the more general **KN formula** must be used: > $$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin\theta$$ > where KN is found from Bonjean curves or hydrostatic data. ## 2.2 Longitudinal Stability and Trim ### 2.2.1 Longitudinal Metacentric Height (GML) The longitudinal metacentric height controls trim and is typically much larger than GM (often by a factor of 20 or more): $$GML = KML - KG = KB + BML - KG$$ $$BML = \frac{I_L}{\nabla}$$ Where **I_L** is the second moment of the waterline area about the transverse axis through the centre of flotation. ### 2.2.2 The Moment to Change Trim by 1 Centimetre (MCT) > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\boxed{MCT = \frac{W \cdot GML \cdot LBP}{100 \cdot 100}} \text{ (tonnes·m per cm)}$$ Where: - **W** = displacement (tonnes) - **LBP** = length between perpendiculars (m) - **GML** = longitudinal metacentric height (m) The MCT is the standard yardstick used by naval architects to compute trim changes due to weight shifts. ### 2.2.3 Change of Trim Formula > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\boxed{\Delta \text{Trim (cm)} = \frac{\text{Shift Moment (tonnes·m)}}{MCT}}$$ Where **Shift Moment** = Weight moved (tonnes) × Longitudinal distance moved (m). The direction convention is: - **Positive shift moment** (weight moved aft) → **Trim by the stern** (positive trim) - **Negative shift moment** (weight moved forward) → **Trim by the head** ### 2.2.4 Draft at Any Point Along the Ship's Length $$d_x = d_M \pm \frac{\text{Trim}}{LBP} \cdot x$$ Where: - **d_M** = mean draft (midships) - **x** = longitudinal distance from midships to the point of interest (m) - **Trim** = total trim in metres (positive = trim by stern) ## 2.3 Free Surface Effect (FSE) Liquids in partially filled tanks (e.g., ballast, fuel service tanks) shift their centre of mass as the ship heels. This effectively **raises G** and reduces GM: > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\boxed{FSE = \frac{\rho_{liquid} \cdot i_{tank}}{\nabla}}$$ Where **i_tank** is the transverse second moment of the free surface of the liquid in the tank. > [!WARNING] > Even small amounts of free water in tanks can dramatically reduce effective GM. Always check for FSE when evaluating damaged or partially filled conditions. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Displacement | ∇ = ρ · C_B · L · B · T | m³ or tonnes | C_B = block coefficient | | KB (rect. mid-section) | KB = T/2 | m | Only for prismatic rect. hulls | | KB (general) | KB = BM_minor | m | Read from hydrostatic curves | | BM (transverse) | BM_T = I_T / ∇ | m | I_T = 2nd moment of WL area | | BM (longitudinal) | BML = I_L / ∇ | m | I_L about transverse axis at CF | | GM (transverse) | GM = KB + BM_T − KG | m | Initial stability criterion | | GML (longitudinal) | GML = KB + BML − KG | m | Usually 20×–30× GM_T | | GZ (small angle) | GZ = GM · sinθ | m | Valid for θ ≤ ~10° | | GZ (Wall-sided) | GZ = sinθ[GM − 0.5·BM_T·tanθ] | m | Valid for prismatic hulls | | GZ (general) | GZ = KN − KG·sinθ | m | From KN cross curves | | Trim | Trim = d_F − d_A | m | Positive = by stern | | MCT | MCT = W·GML·LBP / 10000 | t·m/cm | W in tonnes | | Δ Trim | ΔTrim (cm) = M / MCT | cm | M = w·x (weight × distance) | | Draft at point | d_x = d_M ± (Trim/LBP)·x | m | x from midships | | FSE | FSE = ρ·i/∇ | m | Subtract from GM | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Conventions** for KTU Valuation: > 1. Trim is **positive by the stern** in standard naval architecture practice > 2. GM must be **positive** for the ship to be stable in the upright position > 3. The IMO minimum **GM** for passenger ships is typically ≥ **0.15 m** > 4. The metacentre M is **above G** for stable equilibrium ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Stability and trim analysis is essential for: - **Loading condition manuals** for safe operation - **Damage stability** calculations after flooding (SOLAS Chapter II-1) - **Intact stability** compliance (IMO Code on Intact Stability, IS Code 2008) - **Dry-docking** planning — trim must be set to align the ship on the blocks - **Cargo operations** to avoid excessive shear forces and bending moments # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Small-Angle GZ Formula ### Starting Picture Consider a ship floating upright at waterline WL. It is heeled by a small angle θ to a new waterline WL₁. The centre of buoyancy moves from B to B₁. The metacentre M is constructed by extending the new vertical buoyancy line through B₁ until it cuts the original centreline — this intersection is M. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the metacentric radius BM.** By the geometry of the inclined waterplane, the shift of B (denoted BB₁) is related to the second moment of the waterline area: $$\overline{BB_1} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} \cdot \sin\theta$$ And by definition of the metacentric radius: $$BM = \lim_{\theta \to 0} \frac{\overline{BB_1}}{\sin\theta} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}$$ **Step 2 — From triangle BBM.** Looking at the triangle formed by B, B₁, and M, the angle at B₁ is (90° − θ). The angle at B is θ (by alternate segment geometry of the metacentric evolute at small angles). Therefore: $$\overline{BB_1} = BM \cdot \sin\theta$$ **Step 3 — Construct the righting lever GZ.** The righting lever is the perpendicular distance between the vertical line through G (originally the centreline) and the vertical line through B₁ (the new buoyancy direction). Drawing a horizontal line through G to intersect the new buoyancy vertical: In triangle GMZ (where Z is the foot of the perpendicular from G to the new buoyancy vertical): $$GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta$$ **Step 4 — Final Synoptic Form.** > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\boxed{GZ = GM \cdot \sin\theta \quad \text{(valid for small } \theta \leq 10^\circ \text{)}}$$ For larger angles, the simplified form fails and one must use: $$GZ = KN - KG \cdot \sin\theta$$ where KN is read from cross curves of stability (Kn cross curves). ## 3.2 Derivation of the Trim Formula ### Step 1 — Set up the shift moment. A weight w is moved from a longitudinal position x₁ to x₂ relative to midships. The shift in the longitudinal centre of gravity produces a longitudinal moment: $$M = w \cdot (x_2 - x_1) \quad [\text{tonnes·m}]$$ **Step 2 — Define MCT.** By definition, MCT is the moment that produces a trim change of exactly 1 cm: $$MCT = \frac{W \cdot GML \cdot LBP}{100 \times 100} \quad [\text{t·m/cm}]$$ where W = displacement in tonnes, and the two 100 factors convert: GML (m) × LBP (m) = m² → multiply by W → t·m. Then divide by 100 (cm per metre) squared to obtain t·m/cm. **Step 3 — Relate MCT to change of trim.** By linear superposition (small trim changes are linearly additive): $$\Delta \text{Trim (cm)} = \frac{\text{Shift Moment}}{MCT} = \frac{w \cdot x}{MCT}$$ **Step 4 — Direction convention.** - If w is moved **aft** (x positive), the stern sinks → **trim by the stern (positive trim)** - If w is moved **forward** (x negative), the bow sinks → **trim by the head (negative trim)** > [!IMPORTANT] > $$\boxed{\Delta d_F = +\frac{Trim}{2} \cdot \frac{LBP/2}{LBP/2} = \frac{\Delta Trim}{2} \text{ (at bow, with sign)}$$ > $$\boxed{d_F = d_M + \frac{\Delta Trim}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{100} \text{ (signs adjusted per convention)}}$$ More cleanly, when the ship trims by the stern, the bow draft decreases and the stern draft increases by the same magnitude relative to the mean draft: $$\Delta d_F = -\frac{\Delta Trim}{2 \cdot 100}, \quad \Delta d_A = +\frac{\Delta Trim}{2 \cdot 100}$$ (in metres, when ΔTrim is in cm). ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Comprehensive **Problem Statement:** A ship has the following particulars: - Displacement: W = 10,000 tonnes - LBP = 120 m - KB = 2.5 m, KG = 4.0 m - I_T (waterline area transverse second moment) = 18,000 m⁴ - ∇ = 10,000 m³ (since sea water: 1 tonne ≈ 1 m³) **Required:** 1. Calculate GM (initial transverse metacentric height) 2. Calculate righting lever GZ at 15° heel 3. If a weight of 200 tonnes is moved 20 m forward, calculate the new trim (MCT = 80 t·m/cm) ### Solution: **Part 1 — GM Calculation:** $$BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{18{,}000}{10{,}000} = 1.80 \text{ m}$$ $$GM = KB + BM - KG = 2.5 + 1.80 - 4.0 = 0.30 \text{ m}$$ Since GM > 0, the ship is stable in the upright position. **Part 2 — GZ at 15°:** Using the small-angle approximation is borderline here; we use the more accurate Wall-sided formula (assume prismatic hull): $$GZ = \sin(15°) \left[ GM - 0.5 \cdot BM \cdot \tan(15°) \right]$$ Numerically: $$GZ = 0.2588 \left[ 0.30 - 0.5 \times 1.80 \times 0.2679 \right]$$ $$GZ = 0.2588 \left[ 0.30 - 0.2411 \right]$$ $$GZ = 0.2588 \times 0.0589 = 0.01524 \text{ m} \approx 1.52 \text{ cm}$$ For more accuracy at 15°, the KN cross curves would be used. **Part 3 — Trim Change:** Shift moment M = 200 × (−20) = −4000 t·m (negative because moved forward) $$\Delta \text{Trim} = \frac{M}{MCT} = \frac{-4000}{80} = -50 \text{ cm} = -0.50 \text{ m}$$ The negative sign means the ship trims **by the head** (bow sinks, stern rises) by 0.50 m. ## 3.4 Stability Criterion Summary | Condition | Status | Implication | |---|---|---| | G below M (GM > 0) | **Stable** | Ship returns upright | | G coincident with M (GM = 0) | **Neutral** | Ship remains heeled | | G above M (GM < 0) | **Unstable** | Ship capsizes | > [!NOTE] > For dynamic stability, one must compute the area under the GZ curve up to the angle of vanishing stability. The IMO-required minimum area for passenger ships is **0.055 m·rad up to 30°** (IS Code 2008). # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Force and Lever Diagram — Heeled Ship The following Mermaid block diagram represents the righting moment generation on a heeled ship. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["Upright Ship Position
Centreline CG vertical"] nodeB["External Heeling Force
Wind or Wave"] nodeC["Ship Heels by angle theta"] nodeD["Buoyancy centre B shifts
to B1 along metacentric evolute"] nodeE["New vertical through B1
intersects centreline at M"] nodeF["Weight W acts vertically
down through G"] nodeG["Buoyancy force FB acts
vertically up through B1"] nodeH["Righting Lever GZ
= horizontal separation of W and FB"] nodeI["Righting Moment M_R
= W times GZ"] nodeJ["Heeling Moment M_H
= stabilising check fails if M_R is less"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ ``` ## 4.2 Reference Point Hierarchy — Initial Stability ```mermaid graph TD nodeK["Keel Reference Line K"] nodeB["Centre of Buoyancy B
height KB above keel"] nodeM["Metacentre M
at height KM above keel"] nodeG["Centre of Gravity G
at height KG above keel"] nodeBM["BM = I_T divided by displacement volume"] nodeGM["GM = KM minus KG"] nodeStable["If GM greater than zero: Stable"] nodeUnstable["If GM less than zero: Unstable Capsize"] nodeK --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeBM nodeBM --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeGM nodeGM --> nodeStable nodeGM --> nodeUnstable ``` ## 4.3 Trim Calculation Flow Architecture ```mermaid graph TD inputA["Initial Displacement W tonnes"] inputB["Initial KG and KB"] inputC["Longitudinal Waterline
Second Moment I_L"] inputD["Length Between Perpendiculars LBP"] calcA["BML = I_L divided by displacement"] calcB["GML = KB plus BML minus KG"] calcC["MCT = W times GML times LBP divided by 10000"] inputE["New Weight w moved
from x1 to x2"] calcD["Shift Moment M equals w times delta x"] calcE["Change of Trim equals M divided by MCT"] outputA["New Forward Draft"] outputB["New Aft Draft"] outputC["Trim Direction
By Stern or By Head"] inputA --> calcA inputC --> calcA calcA --> calcB inputB --> calcB calcB --> calcC inputD --> calcC inputA --> calcC calcC --> calcE inputE --> calcD calcD --> calcE calcE --> outputA calcE --> outputB calcE --> outputC ``` ## 4.4 Stability State Diagram ```mermaid graph LR stateA["Loading Condition
Initial"] stateB["Compute KG
and KB"] stateC["Compute BM
equals I_T divided by displacement"] stateD["Compute GM
equals KB plus BM minus KG"] stateE{"GM positive?"} stateF["Stable
Upright Equilibrium"] stateG["Neutral
Remains Heeled"] stateH["Unstable
Capsizes"] stateI["Apply Free
Surface Correction"] stateJ["Check IMO IS Code 2008
