Area under GZ curve"] stateA --> stateB stateB --> stateC stateC --> stateD stateD --> stateE stateE -->|Yes| stateF stateE -->|Equal Zero| stateG stateE -->|No| stateH stateF --> stateI stateI --> stateJ ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define metacentric height. Explain its significance in ship stability.** **Model Answer:** The metacentric height (GM) is the vertical distance between the ship's centre of gravity (G) and the metacentre (M), measured along the ship's centreline. **Significance:** 1. It is the direct measure of initial transverse stability — a positive GM indicates the ship will return upright when heeled. 2. A larger GM gives a faster, more "stiff" roll (overly stiff ships are uncomfortable in seaways). 3. A smaller GM gives a slower, "tender" roll (more comfortable but less safe). 4. The IMO regulations specify minimum GM values (typically 0.15 m for passenger ships) to ensure adequate safety. 5. GM is calculated as: **GM = KB + BM − KG** *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks significance points)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **What is meant by trim of a ship? Explain 'trim by the stern' and 'trim by the head'.** **Model Answer:** **Trim** is the longitudinal inclination of a ship, defined as the difference between the forward and aft drafts measured at the perpendiculars: > **Trim = Draft at FP − Draft at AP** - **Trim by the Stern (Positive Trim):** When the draft at the stern is greater than at the bow, the ship is said to be trimmed by the stern. This occurs when weights are concentrated aft, or when weight is removed forward. - **Trim by the Head (Negative Trim):** When the draft at the bow exceeds the draft at the stern, the ship is trimmed by the head. This results from weights concentrated forward or weight removal aft. The magnitude of trim is computed using the **MCT** (Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm) formula. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation of two cases)* ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) ### Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **A ship has the following particulars:** - Length between perpendiculars: LBP = 150 m - Displacement: ∇ = 15,000 tonnes - KG = 5.5 m, KB = 3.0 m - Transverse BM = 2.2 m - Longitudinal BM = 105 m - A weight of 300 tonnes is moved 25 m forward from midships. **(a)** Calculate the initial metacentric height GM and comment on the stability. (7 marks) **(b)** Calculate the new trim and the resulting drafts at FP and AP if the mean draft before movement was 8.0 m. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — GM Calculation Given: - KB = 3.0 m - BM_T = 2.2 m - KG = 5.5 m **Formula:** $$GM = KB + BM_T - KG$$ **Substitution:** $$GM = 3.0 + 2.2 - 5.5 = -0.3 \text{ m}$$ **Result:** GM = **−0.3 m** (negative) **Comment on Stability:** Since GM is negative, the centre of gravity G lies **above** the metacentre M. This means the ship is in an **unstable upright equilibrium**. Any external disturbance (e.g., a slight roll) will cause the ship to continue rolling and ultimately capsize. **Recommendation:** The cargo must be redistributed to lower KG, or ballast must be taken in to lower G, until GM becomes positive (preferably ≥ 0.15 m for IMO compliance). *(7 marks: 2 marks formula, 2 marks calculation, 3 marks interpretation and recommendation)* #### Part (b) — Trim Calculation **Step 1 — Calculate Longitudinal Metacentric Height (GML):** $$GML = KB + BML - KG = 3.0 + 105 - 5.5 = 102.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate MCT:** $$MCT = \frac{W \cdot GML \cdot LBP}{100 \times 100}$$ $$MCT = \frac{15{,}000 \times 102.5 \times 150}{10{,}000}$$ $$MCT = \frac{230{,}625{,}000}{10{,}000} = 23{,}062.5 \text{ t·m/cm}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate Shift Moment:** The weight is moved 25 m forward, so x = −25 m (forward is negative in our convention): $$M = w \cdot x = 300 \times (-25) = -7{,}500 \text{ t·m}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate Change in Trim:** $$\Delta \text{Trim (cm)} = \frac{M}{MCT} = \frac{-7{,}500}{23{,}062.5} = -0.3253 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to metres and interpret:** $$\Delta \text{Trim} = -0.003253 \text{ m} \approx -3.25 \text{ mm}$$ The negative sign indicates **trim by the head** (bow sinks further). **Step 6 — Compute New Drafts:** Trim change at bow and aft (each by half of total trim, opposite signs): $$\Delta d_F = +\frac{|\Delta \text{Trim}|}{2} = +0.00163 \text{ m} \approx +1.6 \text{ mm (bow sinks)}$$ $$\Delta d_A = -\frac{|\Delta \text{Trim}|}{2} = -0.00163 \text{ m} \approx -1.6 \text{ mm (stern rises)}$$ **New Drafts:** $$d_F^{new} = 8.0 + 0.00163 \approx 8.0016 \text{ m}$$ $$d_A^{new} = 8.0 - 0.00163 \approx 7.9984 \text{ m}$$ *(7 marks: 2 marks GML+MCT, 2 marks shift moment, 2 marks trim change, 1 mark final drafts)* --- ### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] **(a)** Derive the formula for the righting lever GZ for a wall-sided ship at an angle of heel θ. (7 marks) **(b)** A box-shaped vessel has L = 100 m, B = 15 m, T = 5 m, floating in seawater (ρ = 1.025 t/m³). A weight of 100 tonnes is placed on deck 6 m above the keel at the centreline. Calculate the new GM. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Derivation of Wall-Sided GZ Formula **Setup:** For a wall-sided ship (vertical sides between WL and WL₁), the centre of buoyancy B moves to B₁ when heeled by θ. The metacentre M is at height KM = KB + BM above the keel. **Geometric Relation:** From triangle BBM, with BM as the hypotenuse-like side: $$\overline{BB_1} = BM \cdot \sin\theta$$ **Volume of the Emerged Wedge (or Immersed Wedge):** For a wall-sided ship, the emerged wedge volume ν is: $$\nu = \frac{1}{2} \cdot b \cdot \overline{GG_1} \cdot L \text{ (approx., using mean breadth)}$$ Better expressed using the half-angle of inclination: $$\nu = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \nabla \cdot \tan\theta \cdot \frac{B}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{something}}$$ More rigorously, the horizontal shift of the centre of buoyancy is: $$\overline{BB_1} = \frac{\nabla \tan\theta \cdot (\text{cg of wedge})}{...}$$ The standard Wall-sided derivation gives the shift in B as: $$\overline{BG_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{B}{2} \tan\theta \right) \cdot \frac{B}{3} = \frac{B^2 \tan\theta}{12} \cdot \text{(per unit length)}$$ After integration across the length and simplification: $$\overline{BB_1} = \frac{BM \cdot \sin\theta}{1}$$ The GZ lever comes from: $$GZ = \sin\theta \left[ GM - \frac{1}{2} BM \tan\theta \right]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Wall-Sided Formula:** > $$\boxed{GZ = \sin\theta \cdot \left[ GM - \frac{BM}{2} \tan\theta \right]}$$ This reduces to **GZ = GM · sin θ** when θ is small (since tan θ → 0). *(7 marks: 2 marks setup, 3 marks derivation steps, 2 marks final formula)* #### Part (b) — Box-Ship GM Calculation **Given:** - L = 100 m, B = 15 m, T = 5 m - ρ = 1.025 t/m³ - Weight added: w = 100 t at height 6 m above keel (on deck, at centreline — no shift in transverse/longitudinal CG) **Step 1 — Initial Displacement:** $$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T = 100 \times 15 \times 5 = 7{,}500 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W_{initial} = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 7{,}500 = 7{,}687.5 \text{ t}$$ **Step 2 — New Displacement after Adding 100 t:** $$W_{new} = 7{,}687.5 + 100 = 7{,}787.5 \text{ t}$$ New draft T' from displacement (assuming constant L, B, ρ): $$T' = \frac{W_{new}}{\rho \cdot L \cdot B} = \frac{7{,}787.5}{1.025 \times 100 \times 15} = \frac{7{,}787.5}{1{,}537.5} = 5.065 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — KB for Box-Ship:** $$KB = \frac{T'}{2} = \frac{5.065}{2} = 2.533 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — BM for Box-Ship (Waterline is Rectangle):** $$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 15^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 3{,}375}{12} = 28{,}125 \text{ m}^4$$ $$\nabla_{new} = \frac{W_{new}}{\rho} = \frac{7{,}787.5}{1.025} = 7{,}597.6 \text{ m}^3$$ $$BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla_{new}} = \frac{28{,}125}{7{,}597.6} = 3.702 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — New KG (using parallel axis theorem):** Let z = 6 m (height of added weight above keel), and z_initial = T/2 = 2.5 m (initial KG of box-ship = T/2): $$KG_{new} = \frac{W_{initial} \cdot KG_{initial} + w \cdot z}{W_{new}}$$ $$KG_{new} = \frac{7{,}687.5 \times 2.5 + 100 \times 6}{7{,}787.5} = \frac{19{,}218.75 + 600}{7{,}787.5}$$ $$KG_{new} = \frac{19{,}818.75}{7{,}787.5} = 2.545 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — New GM:** $$GM_{new} = KB + BM - KG_{new} = 2.533 + 3.702 - 2.545 = 3.690 \text{ m}$$ **Result:** **GM = 3.69 m** > [!NOTE] > This is a very high GM — the ship would be extremely "stiff" and uncomfortable in a seaway. The IMO minimum is 0.15 m, but typical cargo ships have GM ≈ 0.5–1.5 m. *(7 marks: 2 marks displacement & draft, 2 marks KB+BM, 2 marks new KG, 1 mark final GM)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign Errors in Trim Convention:** Always state clearly whether trim is by the stern or by the head, and ensure draft changes have consistent signs. > 2. **Forgetting ρ in MCT:** If displacement is in tonnes (mass), do not multiply by ρ again. If displacement is in m³, multiply by ρ to get tonnes. > 3. **Confusing BM and GM:** BM is purely a geometric/hydrostatic quantity (I_T/∇), while GM depends on the loading condition (KG). > 4. **Using Small-Angle GZ at Large Heel:** GZ = GM·sinθ is **only valid for small angles** (θ ≤ 10°). At larger angles, use the Wall-sided formula or KN cross curves. > 5. **Forgetting Free Surface Effect:** Free liquids in tanks reduce effective GM. Always check for FSE in real stability calculations. > 6. **Wrong Direction in Shift Moment:** Moving weight forward gives **negative** shift moment, which produces **trim by the head**. Sign conventions must be strictly followed. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Stability** = ability to return to original equilibrium after disturbance (transverse = roll, longitudinal = trim). - **Transverse stability** is governed by **GM**; **longitudinal stability** is governed by **GML**. - **GM = KB + BM − KG** is the master formula for initial transverse stability. - **BM = I_T / ∇**, where I_T is the second moment of the waterline area about the longitudinal centreline. - **GZ** at small angle = **GM·sinθ**; for larger angles, use **GZ = KN − KG·sinθ** or Wall-sided formula. - **Trim = d_F − d_A** (positive = by stern). - **MCT = W·GML·LBP / 10000** (t·m/cm). - **ΔTrim = Shift Moment / MCT**. - **Draft at any point** = d_M ± (Trim/LBP)·x, where x is distance from midships. - **Free Surface Effect** reduces GM by ρ·i/∇ per tank with slack liquid. - **Stable condition:** GM > 0; **Unstable:** GM < 0. - **For passenger ships:** IMO requires GM ≥ 0.15 m and GZ area ≥ 0.055 m·rad up to 30°. - **GML is typically 20–30 times GM**, so longitudinal stability is rarely a concern for trim issues (trim is more often governed by MCT). - **Box-ship shortcuts:** KB = T/2, KG = T/2 (empty), I_T = LB³/12, I_L = BL³/12.

topics.title: Watertight Integrity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Watertight Integrity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU Definition

**Watertight Integrity** in ship design refers to the structural and design measures adopted to ensure that a vessel, when subjected to damage (collision, grounding, or any unintended breaching of the hull), can retain a defined level of buoyancy and stability by limiting flooding to a controllable number of compartments through the use of transverse and longitudinal **watertight bulkheads**, **doors**, **sills**, and **closing appliances** that meet prescribed pressure and leak-rate standards.

The entire system is governed by the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS) Chapter II-1**, Part B-1 through Part B-4, which prescribes the rules for subdivision, damage stability, and the integrity of the hull below the waterline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition for Examinations:**
> Watertight integrity = the ability of a ship's hull, when one or more adjacent compartments are flooded, to satisfy the residual stability criteria as laid down in SOLAS, provided the ship is loaded to its **subdivision load line**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Watermelon Subway**
> Imagine a long passenger submarine made of a watermelon. If a shark bites a hole in one section, the entire submarine sinks — just like the Titanic scenario we want to avoid.
> Now, instead of one large hollow space, we install **watertight partitions** (bulkheads) every few meters, dividing the watermelon into smaller "chambers." If a shark now bites a hole, only the bitten chamber floods, and the remaining dry chambers keep the submarine afloat.
> Each bulkhead is like a sealed "firewall" — strong enough to resist water pressure from a fully flooded adjacent compartment, and fitted with a watertight door that can be closed in seconds.

This is the **compartmentation principle** that governs every passenger ship, naval vessel, and even modern container ships.

### The Three Pillars of Watertight Integrity

| Pillar | What it Controls | SOLAS Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Compartmentation** | Physical division of the hull by watertight bulkheads | Reg. 4–7, II-1/B |
| **Subdivision Load Line** | The maximum service draft defining the worst flooding case | Reg. 6, II-1/B |
| **Margin Line** | The reference line above which no progressive flooding can occur through openings | Reg. 2.8, II-1/B |

### Standard Metrics (KTU Highlight Values)

- **Margin Line**: A line drawn on the shell plating **76 mm (3 inches)** below the upper edge of the bulkhead deck (for passenger ships).
- **Subdivision Load Line (SDL)**: The waterline corresponding to the deepest authorized subdivision draft, usually 5–10% below the deepest load line.
- **Permeability (μ)**: A dimensionless factor representing the volume of water that can flood a compartment, ranging typically between **0.95** (empty) and **0.60** (machinery spaces).
- **Factor of Subdivision (F)**: A function of ship length and service, ranging from 0.33 to 1.0.
- **Required Subdivision Index (R)**: Sum of contributions from each compartment/criterion.
- **Attained Subdivision Index (A)**: Calculated from the subdivision arrangement; **A ≥ R** for compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Watertight Subdivision is Necessary

1. **Residual Buoyancy**: When a compartment is breached, the ship must retain enough intact volume to stay afloat.
2. **Residual Stability**: The righting lever (GZ) curve post-flooding must remain positive across a sufficient range of heel angles (typically 0° to 20° at minimum, with positive range to ≥ 20°).
3. **Limiting Flooding**: Progressive flooding through unprotected openings must not occur — this is controlled by the **margin line** requirement.
4. **Survivability**: SOLAS prescribes the "**1-compartment**" or "**2-compartment**" flooding standard depending on length and number of passengers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Point:** The "**n-compartment criterion**" means the ship must stay afloat and stable with **any one (or any two)** adjacent main compartments flooded.

### 2.2 SOLAS Damage Stability Criteria (Passenger Ships)

The residual stability after damage must satisfy **ALL** the following:

1. **Heel Angle** (final equilibrium) ≤ **15°** in general; ≤ **7°** for special cases.
2. **Positive GZ range** ≥ **20°** beyond the equilibrium angle.
3. **Maximum GZ** ≥ **0.1 m**, occurring within the range **20° ≤ angle ≤ 50°**.
4. **No progressive flooding** through openings below the **margin line**.

### 2.3 Factor of Subdivision (F)

The factor of subdivision depends on ship length and the attained subdivision index:

$$
F = \left( \dfrac{L}{3} \right)^{0.5} \cdot K
$$

Where:
- $L$ = Rule length in metres
- $K$ = constant depending on ship service (passenger, cargo)

The **attained subdivision index A** is computed as:

$$
A = \sum_{i} \left[ p_i \cdot s_i \right]
$$

Where:
- $p_i$ = probability of flooding compartment $i$ (sum of all $p_i = 1$)
- $s_i$ = survival factor (0, 0.5, or 1) depending on whether the ship survives flooding of compartment $i$

The **required subdivision index R** is calculated using F, ship length, and a summation formula over compartments and criteria.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{flood} = \int_{0}^{L} \mu(x) \cdot b(x) \cdot dx$ | **Floodable length** — max length of damage that can be flooded without submerging the margin line | metres (m) |
| 2 | $L_{perm} = f(F, R, \text{machinery})$ | **Permissible length** of a single compartment derived from SOLAS curves | metres (m) |
| 3 | $p_i = \dfrac{l_i \cdot (b_i + L_{total})}{\sum (l_j \cdot b_j + \cdots)}$ | **Probability of flooding** compartment $i$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | $\mu = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{compartment}}$ | **Permeability** — ratio of water absorbed to gross volume | 0.60 (engine) – 0.95 (empty) |
| 5 | $A = \sum p_i \cdot s_i$ | **Attained Subdivision Index** | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R = \sum (C_i \cdot s_i \cdot p_i)$ | **Required Subdivision Index** | dimensionless |
| 7 | $s_i \in \{0, 0.5, 1\}$ | **Survival factor** | based on residual GZ meeting criteria |
| 8 | $F = 0.5 \cdot \left[ 1 + \left(\dfrac{L}{200}\right) \right]$ for L > 200 m | **Factor of Subdivision** (cargo ships) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta_{flooded} = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{compartment} \cdot \mu$ | **Lost buoyancy** when compartment floods | Newtons (N) |
| 10 | $h_{WL,red} = \dfrac{\Delta_{flooded}}{A_W \cdot \rho}$ | **Sinkage** due to flooding (parallel rise approximation) | metres (m) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Passenger Ship Safety**: Watertight integrity is the reason modern ferries and cruise ships can survive the loss of entire machinery spaces and stay afloat.
- **Warship Survivability**: Naval architects use similar compartmentation plus a "**damage control box**" (DC box) with pumped counter-flooding.
- **Submarines**: Outer pressure hull and inner watertight compartments with **double bottoms** and **ballast tanks** are extreme applications of the same principle.
- **Offshore Structures**: FPSOs and semi-submersibles are subdivided following a similar "**1-compartment damage**" philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Floodable Length Formula

The **floodable length** at a given point along the ship's length is the maximum length of a symmetrical damage that, if flooded, would just bring the waterline up to the margin line without submerging it.

Let us consider a small elementary segment $dx$ of the ship at longitudinal position $x$ from the aft perpendicular.

**Step 1**: The volume of the watertight envelope in this segment is:

$$
dV = A_{section}(x) \cdot dx
$$

**Step 2**: Due to damage of length $dl$ symmetric about position $x$, the ship will lose buoyancy in a region of length $dl$ on both sides (or just the full length on one side, depending on the assumption). For SOLAS calculation, the **floodable length** at $x$ assumes the whole ship is treated as a 2D problem of adding weight of water in damaged region and finding the resulting waterline.

**Step 3**: The weight of floodwater (considering permeability) in the damaged region of length $l$ centred at $x$:

$$
W_{flood} = \rho \cdot g \cdot \mu \cdot \int_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2} A_{section}(t) \cdot dt
$$

**Step 4**: To keep the margin line just dry, the additional draft $\Delta T$ caused by adding this weight must equal the available freeboard $FB_{margin}$ to the margin line:

$$
\rho \cdot g \cdot \mu \cdot \int_{x - l/2}^{x + l/2} A_{section}(t) \cdot dt = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_{W} \cdot (T_{actual} - T_{margin})
$$

**Step 5**: Solving for $l$, the **floodable length** at position $x$ is:

$$
l_{flood}(x) = \dfrac{A_W \cdot (T_{actual} - T_{margin})}{\mu \cdot \bar{A}_{section}}
$$

Where $\bar{A}_{section}$ is the mean section area over the damaged length.

For a more rigorous SOLAS formulation:

$$
l_{flood} = \dfrac{L \cdot A_W}{\sum_{i \in damage} \mu_i \cdot V_i} \cdot \left( T_{margin} - T_{draught} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Derivation Insight:** The floodable length is inversely proportional to permeability — meaning machinery spaces (μ = 0.60) have a **larger** permissible damaged length than empty cargo holds (μ = 0.95) because they absorb less water per unit volume.

### 3.2 Derivation: Attained Subdivision Index (A)

The probability of flooding a compartment $i$ (whose length is $l_i$ and mean breadth is $b_i$) is assumed proportional to its length and average breadth:

$$
p_i = \dfrac{l_i \cdot (b_i + \sum b_{adj,j})}{\sum_j l_j \cdot (b_j + \sum b_{adj,k})}
$$

In the simplified 1-D SOLAS formulation:

$$
p_i = \dfrac{l_i}{\sum l_{all}}
$$

Each compartment is assigned a **survival factor** $s_i$:

$$
s_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if ship survives in upright position} \\ 0.5 & \text{if survives only in heeled condition} \\ 0 & \text{if ship sinks} \end{cases}
$$

The attained subdivision index is then:

$$
A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot s_i
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

A passenger ship of length $L = 120$ m has 8 main watertight compartments with the following data:

| Comp. | $l_i$ (m) | $\mu_i$ | Status after flooding |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 12 | 0.85 | Survives upright (s = 1) |
| C2 | 14 | 0.80 | Survives heeled (s = 0.5) |
| C3 | 16 | 0.70 | Sinks (s = 0) |
| C4 | 15 | 0.90 | Survives upright (s = 1) |
| C5 | 18 | 0.60 | Survives upright (s = 1) |
| C6 | 14 | 0.85 | Survives heeled (s = 0.5) |
| C7 | 16 | 0.95 | Sinks (s = 0) |
| C8 | 15 | 0.90 | Survives upright (s = 1) |

**Step 1**: Sum of lengths

$$
\sum l_i = 12 + 14 + 16 + 15 + 18 + 14 + 16 + 15 = 120 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2**: Probabilities (assuming uniform $p_i = l_i / L$)

$$
p_1 = \frac{12}{120} = 0.10, \quad p_2 = 0.1167, \quad p_3 = 0.1333, \quad p_4 = 0.125
$$

$$
p_5 = 0.15, \quad p_6 = 0.1167, \quad p_7 = 0.1333, \quad p_8 = 0.125
$$

**Step 3**: Attained Subdivision Index

$$
A = (0.10)(1) + (0.1167)(0.5) + (0.1333)(0) + (0.125)(1)
$$
$$
+ (0.15)(1) + (0.1167)(0.5) + (0.1333)(0) + (0.125)(1)
$$

$$
A = 0.10 + 0.0583 + 0 + 0.125 + 0.15 + 0.0583 + 0 + 0.125
$$

$$
A = 0.6166 \approx 0.617
$$

This value of $A$ must exceed the **required subdivision index $R$** for SOLAS compliance.

### 3.3 Sinkage and Heel Calculation (Cross-Flooding Case)

When two adjacent compartments are asymmetrically flooded (e.g., a side compartment), the ship will **list** in addition to sinking.

**Step 1**: Lost buoyancy

$$
\Delta W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \mu_{flood} \cdot V_{flood}
$$

**Step 2**: Parallel sinkage

$$
\Delta T = \dfrac{\Delta W}{A_W \cdot \rho \cdot g}
$$

**Step 3**: Heel angle (using lost buoyancy method)

$$
\tan \phi = \dfrac{\Delta W \cdot e}{GM \cdot \Delta}
$$

Where:
- $e$ = transverse distance of the lost buoyancy centroid from the centreline
- $GM$ = metacentric height in the flooded condition
- $\Delta$ = displacement

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** When a passenger ship heels past 15°, the survival factor drops from 1 to 0.5. Examiners love asking "What if the heel exceeds 20°? — Then $s = 0$."

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Watertight Subdivision — General Arrangement Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hull Shell Plating] --> B[Double Bottom]
    A --> C[Side Shell]
    A --> D[Weather Deck]
    B --> E[Forepeak Bulkhead]
    B --> F[Engine Room Bulkhead]
    B --> G[Aftpeak Bulkhead]
    B --> H[Cross Bulkheads]
    H --> I[Main Watertight Compartments]
    I --> J[Compartment 1 - Forepeak]
    I --> K[Compartment 2 - Hold 1]
    I --> L[Compartment 3 - Hold 2]
    I --> M[Compartment 4 - Engine Room]
    I --> N[Compartment 5 - Hold 3]
    I --> O[Compartment 6 - Aftpeak]
    F --> P[Watertight Doors]
    H --> P
    P --> Q[Quick-Closing Mechanisms]
    P --> R[Power Operated Slides]
    P --> S[Hand-Operated Sliding Doors]
    E --> T[Collision Bulkhead]
    T --> U[Stepped Above Load Line]
    M --> V[Engine Room Bilge]
    V --> W[Cross Flooding Valves]
    H --> X[Sounding Pipes]
    H --> Y[Ventilation Closures]
```

### 4.2 Subdivision Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start - Ship Particulars] --> S2[Define Rule Length L]
    S2 --> S3[Determine Service Type]
    S3 --> S4{N > 12 Passengers?}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[SOLAS Passenger Rules Apply]
    S4 -->|No| S6[SOLAS Cargo Rules Apply]
    S5 --> S7[Calculate Factor of Subdivision F]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Calculate Required Index R]
    S8 --> S9[Propose Compartment Layout]
    S9 --> S10[Calculate Permissible Lengths]
    S10 --> S11[Compare L_compartment with L_permissible]
    S11 --> S12{Compliant?}
    S12 -->|No| S13[Add or Reposition Bulkheads]
    S13 --> S9
    S12 -->|Yes| S14[Calculate Attained Index A]
    S14 --> S15{A >= R?}
    S15 -->|No| S13
    S15 -->|Yes| S16[Design Approved - Issue Subdivision Plan]
```

### 4.3 Damage Scenario Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Damage Event] --> D2{Compartment Flooded?}
    D2 -->|Yes| D3[Check Survival Factor s]
    D3 --> D4{Critical Heel > 25 deg?}
    D4 -->|Yes| D5[s = 0 Ship Sinks]
    D4 -->|No| D6{Heel 15-25 deg?}
    D6 -->|Yes| D7[s = 0.5 Heeled Survival]
    D6 -->|No| D8{Heel less than 15 deg?}
    D8 -->|Yes| D9{s = 1 Upright Survival}
    D8 -->|No| D10[Re-evaluate Stability]
    D9 --> D11[Add to Attained Index A]
    D7 --> D11
    D5 --> D12[Subtract from A]
    D11 --> D13[Sum all contributions to A]
    D12 --> D13
    D13 --> D14{Is A greater than R?}
    D14 -->|Yes| D15[SOLAS Compliance Achieved]
    D14 -->|No| D16[Redesign Required]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of the Margin Line and Subdivision Load Line

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SHIP[CROSS-SECTION VIEW OF SHIP]
        direction TB
        SDA[Upper Deck - Bulkhead Deck]
        ML[Margin Line - 76 mm below upper edge of bulkhead deck]
        WLS[Service Waterline]
        SDL[Subdivision Load Line - Slightly above Service WL]
        WLA[Actual Loaded Waterline]
        DBL[Double Bottom]
        BT[Side Bulkhead]
        WB[Watertight Bulkhead]
        OP[Unprotected Opening]
    end
    SDA -.->|76 mm| ML
    ML -.-> OP
    WLS -.->|small margin| SDL
    SDL -.-> WLA
    WB --> DBL
    BT --> DBL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every opening (door, vent, air pipe) below the **margin line** must be fitted with a **weathertight or watertight closing device** that can be closed in the time required by SOLAS Reg. 20–22.

### 4.5 Sequence of Closing Watertight Openings

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Damage Event
    participant Detection System
    participant Bridge Officer
    participant Crew
    participant Door Mechanism
    participant Stability Computer

    Damage Event->>Detection System: Bilge alarm triggered
    Detection System->>Bridge Officer: Alarm + Compartment ID
    Bridge Officer->>Stability Computer: Request damage assessment
    Stability Computer->>Bridge Officer: Heel, sinkage, GZ curve
    Bridge Officer->>Crew: "Close watertight doors in zone X"
    Crew->>Door Mechanism: Activate power/hand closure
    Door Mechanism->>Door Mechanism: Doors close in < 60 seconds
    Door Mechanism->>Bridge Officer: All doors closed - status OK
    Bridge Officer->>Stability Computer: Re-assess final stability
    Stability Computer->>Bridge Officer: SOLAS criteria satisfied
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "Watertight Integrity" of a ship. State the significance of the margin line.**

**Model Answer:**

Watertight integrity is the ability of a ship's hull and its subdivision arrangement to prevent progressive flooding beyond a defined number of compartments when the hull is breached, thereby ensuring residual buoyancy and stability.

The **margin line** is a reference line drawn **76 mm (3 inches)** below the upper edge of the bulkhead deck on the shell plating. Its significance is that:

1. It defines the **safety boundary** for progressive flooding.
2. Any unprotected opening below the margin line must be fitted with weathertight/watertight closing devices.
3. The damage stability calculation assumes that progressive flooding is **NOT** allowed beyond the margin line.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the margin line with the sheer line or the freeboard deck line. The margin line is **NOT** the same as the load line. It is a virtual line on the shell plating used **only** for damage stability calculations.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is "Permeability" of a compartment? State typical values for (i) an empty cargo hold, (ii) machinery spaces, and (iii) a passenger accommodation space.**

**Model Answer:**

Permeability ($\mu$) is defined as the ratio of the volume of water that can flood a compartment to its gross volume, assuming all openings are sealed.

$$
\mu = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{compartment}}
$$

Typical values as per SOLAS:

| Compartment Type | Permeability (μ) |
|---|---|
| Empty cargo hold / dry stores | **0.95** |
| Machinery spaces (engine room) | **0.60** |
| Passenger accommodation / cabins | **0.95** |
| Chain lockers, storerooms | **0.60 – 0.70** |
| Cargo holds with cargo | **0.60 – 0.80** depending on stowage |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternative questions (A and B) are offered per module. **Attempt any ONE full question.**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2]`

**Q (A):**
**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **different types of watertight bulkheads** used in a passenger ship. Mention the SOLAS requirements for the **collision bulkhead** and the **aft peak bulkhead**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A passenger ship of rule length **L = 150 m** has a factor of subdivision **F = 0.50**. The ship is divided into 5 main watertight compartments with the following data:

| Comp. | Length $l_i$ (m) | Survival Factor $s_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| C1 | 24 | 1.0 |
| C2 | 30 | 0.5 |
| C3 | 32 | 1.0 |
| C4 | 34 | 0.0 |
| C5 | 30 | 1.0 |

Compute the **Attained Subdivision Index (A)**. Given the Required Subdivision Index **R = 0.65**, state whether the ship is SOLAS compliant. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q(A)

#### Solution to (a) — Types of Watertight Bulkheads

A passenger ship uses the following watertight transverse bulkheads (see diagram below):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stem] --> B[Forepeak Bulkhead - Collision Bulkhead]
    B --> C[Chain Locker Bulkhead]
    C --> D[First Transverse Bulkhead]
    D --> E[Cross Bulkheads between Holds]
    E --> F[Engine Room Bulkhead]
    F --> G[Engine Room Bulkhead Aft]
    G --> H[Aftpeak Bulkhead]
    H --> I[Stern Post]
```

**Types and Functions:**

1. **Collision Bulkhead (Fore Peak Bulkhead)**:
   - Located at a distance of **0.05 L to 0.08 L** (typically around 5–8% of L) abaft the forward perpendicular.
   - Must be **watertight up to the bulkhead deck**.
   - No doors, no manholes, no openings allowed below the bulkhead deck.
   - Must be welded construction (per SOLAS Reg. 11).

2. **Aft Peak Bulkhead**:
   - Located at the forward end of the stern frame.
   - Watertight up to the bulkhead deck.
   - May have a manhole fitted with a watertight cover for emergency access.

3. **Engine Room Bulkheads (Forward and Aft)**:
   - These are **double-skinned** in tankers and chemical carriers.
   - They form a cofferdam with the cargo tanks in oil tankers.

4. **Cross Bulkheads**:
   - Divide the cargo region into discrete holds.
   - Spaced at distances ≤ the **permissible length** for the ship.

5. **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead**:
   - Vertical bulkhead closing the shaft tunnel from the engine room.

**SOLAS Requirements (Reg. 11–14, II-1/B):**
- All bulkheads must be of steel or equivalent.
- They must be tested by hose test or hydrostatic test.
- Watertight doors in passenger ships are limited in number.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Calculation of Attained Subdivision Index

**Step 1**: Sum of compartment lengths

$$
\sum l_i = 24 + 30 + 32 + 34 + 30 = 150 \text{ m}
$$

This equals the rule length $L$ as expected.

**Step 2**: Calculate probability of flooding for each compartment

$$
p_i = \dfrac{l_i}{\sum l_j} = \dfrac{l_i}{150}
$$

| Comp. | $l_i$ (m) | $p_i$ | $s_i$ | $p_i \cdot s_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 24 | 0.1600 | 1.0 | 0.1600 |
| C2 | 30 | 0.2000 | 0.5 | 0.1000 |
| C3 | 32 | 0.2133 | 1.0 | 0.2133 |
| C4 | 34 | 0.2267 | 0.0 | 0.0000 |
| C5 | 30 | 0.2000 | 1.0 | 0.2000 |

**Step 3**: Sum to get the Attained Subdivision Index

$$
A = \sum p_i \cdot s_i = 0.1600 + 0.1000 + 0.2133 + 0.0000 + 0.2000
$$

$$
A = 0.6733 \approx 0.67
$$

**Step 4**: Compare with Required Subdivision Index

$$
A = 0.67 \geq R = 0.65 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{COMPLIANT}
$$

**Conclusion:** The ship satisfies the SOLAS subdivision criterion, although compartment C4 is critical (s = 0). The design margin is small (A − R = 0.02), so designers may consider either shortening C4 or increasing its permeability assumptions.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 1]`

**Q (B):**
**(a)** Define **floodable length** and **permissible length** of a compartment. Explain how they are used to determine the bulkhead spacing in a passenger ship. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier of rule length **L = 180 m** has the waterplane area **$A_W = 2400$ m²** and mean section area **$\bar{A}_{section} = 140$ m²**. The permeability of a hold is **0.85** and the available margin from current waterline to the margin line is **0.45 m**. Calculate the **floodable length** at the amidship region. If the permissible length of the same hold is **18 m**, comment on whether the bulkhead spacing is adequate. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q(B)

#### Solution to (a) — Floodable Length vs Permissible Length

**Floodable Length ($l_{flood}$):**
The floodable length at a given point along the ship's length is the maximum extent of a damage which, if flooded, would submerge the margin line at that point.

$$
l_{flood} = \dfrac{A_W \cdot (T_{actual} - T_{margin})}{\mu \cdot \bar{A}_{section}}
$$

**Permissible Length ($l_{perm}$):**
The permissible length is the maximum length of a single compartment, as derived from SOLAS curves, for the relevant factor of subdivision and ship service.

**Use in Bulkhead Spacing:**

1. The designer first establishes the **floodable length curve** along the ship's length.
2. From SOLAS curves, the **permissible length** for the relevant $F$ and service is read off.
3. The actual compartment length $l_{comp}$ must satisfy:

$$
l_{comp} \leq l_{perm} \quad \text{at every longitudinal position}
$$

4. If $l_{comp} > l_{perm}$, an additional bulkhead must be inserted, or the hold must be subdivided.

**Practical Bulkhead Spacing Rule of Thumb:**

- Fore 1/4 length: Tighter spacing due to smaller floodable length.
- Amidships: Maximum permissible lengths.
- Aft 1/4 length: Tighter again as the ship narrows.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Calculation of Floodable Length

**Given Data:**
- $A_W = 2400 \text{ m}^2$
- $\bar{A}_{section} = 140 \text{ m}^2$
- $\mu = 0.85$
- $T_{actual} - T_{margin} = 0.45 \text{ m}$ (allowable sinkage)

**Step 1**: Apply the floodable length formula

$$
l_{flood} = \dfrac{A_W \cdot (T_{actual} - T_{margin})}{\mu \cdot \bar{A}_{section}}
$$

$$
l_{flood} = \dfrac{2400 \times 0.45}{0.85 \times 140}
$$

$$
l_{flood} = \dfrac{1080}{119}
$$

$$
l_{flood} = 9.08 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2**: Compare with permissible length

$$
l_{perm} = 18 \text{ m} \quad \text{(given)}
$$

**Step 3**: Check bulkhead adequacy

Since $l_{perm} (18 \text{ m}) > l_{flood} (9.08 \text{ m})$, the permissible length is **greater** than the floodable length. This indicates that:

- The compartment is too long to satisfy the floodable length criterion at amidships.
- The bulkhead spacing of 18 m is **INADEQUATE**.
- Either the compartment must be subdivided into two holds of approximately **9 m each**, OR the margin line must be raised by loading the ship to a shallower draft.

**Conclusion:** The current design does NOT satisfy the SOLAS floodable length criterion. Designers must insert an additional bulkhead.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the formula's numerator and denominator** — A high waterplane area $A_W$ INCREASES floodable length, while high permeability $\mu$ DECREASES it.
> 2. **Sign convention** — Always use the absolute value of the difference $(T_{actual} - T_{margin})$. A negative value means the margin line is already submerged.
> 3. **Permeability range** — Students often use $\mu = 0.95$ for machinery spaces, but SOLAS uses **0.60** for engine rooms. Check the type of compartment.
> 4. **Forgetting to sum probabilities** — The attained subdivision index requires $p_i$ to be normalised; the sum $\sum p_i$ must equal 1.
> 5. **Heel angle limit confusion** — 15° is the upper limit for upright survival; above 15° but below 25° is heeled survival ($s = 0.5$); above 25° is non-survival ($s = 0$).

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Watertight Integrity**

- ✅ **Watertight integrity** = controlled flooding via compartmentation.
- ✅ **SOLAS II-1, Part B** is the governing regulation.
- ✅ **Margin line** = 76 mm below upper edge of bulkhead deck.
- ✅ **Subdivision load line** = the deepest service draft used for damage calculation.
- ✅ **Collision bulkhead** = at 5–8% of L abaft FP, no openings, no doors.
- ✅ **Aft peak bulkhead** = may have one manhole with watertight cover.
- ✅ **Permeability (μ)**: Machinery = 0.60, Holds = 0.95, Accommodation = 0.95.
- ✅ **Floodable length** $\propto A_W / (\mu \cdot \bar{A}_{section})$.
- ✅ **Permissible length** is read from SOLAS curves for given $F$ and service.
- ✅ **Attained Index A** = $\sum p_i \cdot s_i$.
- ✅ **Required Index R** = function of $F$, $L$, and ship service.
- ✅ **SOLAS Compliance**: $A \geq R$.
- ✅ **Survival factors**: $s = 1$ (heel ≤ 15°), $s = 0.5$ (15° < heel ≤ 25°), $s = 0$ (sinks).
- ✅ **Damage stability criteria**: Heel ≤ 15°, +GZ range ≥ 20°, GZ max ≥ 0.1 m.
- ✅ **No progressive flooding** allowed below the margin line.
- ✅ **Watertight doors** must close in ≤ 60 seconds (power) or ≤ 90 seconds (hand).
- ✅ **Factor of subdivision F** increases with ship length (longer ships need finer subdivision).
- ✅ **Aft peak bulkhead** test pressure = 0.25 m water column above deck (SOLAS).
- ✅ **Twin-screw ships** must still satisfy the "two-compartment" flooding criterion (where required).
- ✅ **Cross-flooding** is only allowed in symmetrical compartments; otherwise, list is unavoidable.
- ✅ **KTU Favourite Question Type**: "Calculate A and compare with R" — practice this problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IMO Regulations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IMO Regulations

# MODULE 4: IMO REGULATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of IMO and its Regulatory Framework

The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** is a specialized agency of the United Nations, established in 1948 (Geneva Convention) and operational since 1958, headquartered in **London, United Kingdom**. IMO is the global standard-setting authority for the safety, security, environmental performance, and legal matters of international shipping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** IMO regulations are codified international treaties, conventions, codes, and resolutions that establish mandatory minimum requirements for the design, construction, equipment, operation, and disposal of seagoing vessels, ensuring maritime safety, prevention of pollution, and uniformity of design practice worldwide.

### 1.2 Major IMO Conventions Affecting Ship Design

The following are the principal conventions that directly influence ship design parameters:

| Convention / Code | Year | Primary Focus for Ship Design |
|---|---|---|
| SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) | 1974 | Hull structural integrity, subdivision, stability, fire safety |
| MARPOL (Marine Pollution) | 73/78 | Pollution prevention from ships (oil, chemicals, sewage, air) |
| Load Lines Convention (LL) | 1966 | Minimum freeboard, hull strength, weather-tight integrity |
| Tonnage Convention | 1969 | Measurement of gross/net tonnage |
| STCW | 1978 | Training standards (indirectly affects bridge/control design) |
| COLREG | 1972 | Navigation rules (steering gear requirements) |
| ISPS Code | 2002 | Ship and port security |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Ship Design I Module 4, the focus is on **SOLAS, Load Lines, MARPOL (Annex I), and Tonnage Measurement** as they directly impose geometric and structural design constraints.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: IMO as the "Traffic Rule Book for Ship Designers"

Imagine designing a commercial building in a city. The municipal building code dictates: how many floors it can have, where fire exits go, how strong the foundation must be, how much glass is allowed, and even how much rain it must withstand. You, as the architect, do not invent these rules; you follow them to get an "Occupancy Certificate."

IMO plays an identical role for a naval architect:
- The **shipowner** is the "client" wanting a ship to carry cargo from Mumbai to Rotterdam.
- The **naval architect** is the "architect" who must produce safe, compliant lines and structural drawings.
- **Classification Societies** (like Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS) are the "building inspectors" who verify compliance.
- **Flag State Administrations** are the "city government" that ultimately issues the operational certificate.

So, when a naval architect draws the midship section, places watertight bulkheads, or sizes the double bottom, every dimension is being checked mentally (or via software) against an **IMO rule number** — much like an architect checks every drawing against IS 456 or NBC codes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** IMO conventions are **goal-based** in newer revisions (e.g., SOLAS Reg. II-1/3-10 for oil tankers and bulk carriers) but were traditionally **prescriptive** (specifying exact formulae and dimensions). The 2024 KTU syllabus addresses both approaches.

### 1.4 SOLAS Convention – The Bedrock of Ship Design Safety

The **SOLAS Convention 1974** is the most important international treaty concerning the safety of merchant ships. Its Chapter II-1 (Construction – Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations) and Chapter II-2 (Fire Protection, Fire Detection, Fire Extinction) directly govern hull form design and internal arrangement.

**Critical SOLAS Chapters for the Naval Architect:**
- **Chapter II-1**: Subdivision, damage stability, machinery installation.
- **Chapter II-2**: Fire protection structural requirements.
- **Chapter III**: Life-saving appliances (LSA) — affects deck arrangement.
- **Chapter V**: Safety of navigation (steering gear).
- **Chapter VII**: Carriage of dangerous goods (IMDG Code).
- **Chapter IX**: ISM Code (Safety Management).
- **Chapter XI-1**: Special measures to enhance maritime safety.
- **Chapter XII**: Additional safety measures for **bulk carriers**.

### 1.5 Load Lines Convention – The Sailor's "Plimsoll Mark"

The **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), 1966**, sets the minimum freeboard that must be maintained on a ship. The "load line" or **Plimsoll Mark** is a marking on the ship's hull that indicates the maximum depth to which the vessel may be loaded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Metric:** The Convention uses the **Summer Load Line (S)** as the reference; the **Fresh Water Allowance (FWA)** is computed as $\text{FWA} = \frac{\Delta}{4T}$ (mm) where $\Delta$ is the displacement in tonnes and $T$ is the TPC in tonnes per cm immersion.

### 1.6 MARPOL Convention – The Anti-Pollution Mandate

The **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL 73/78)** is the main international convention covering prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships from operational or accidental causes.

**Six Annexes of MARPOL:**
- **Annex I**: Prevention of pollution by oil (double hull requirements for tankers).
- **Annex II**: Control of pollution by noxious liquid substances (NLS).
- **Annex III**: Prevention of pollution by harmful substances in packaged form.
- **Annex IV**: Pollution by sewage from ships.
- **Annex V**: Pollution by garbage from ships.
- **Annex VI**: Air pollution from ships (SOx, NOx, EEDI for new ships).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Design Impact:** Annex I Regulation 19 mandates **double hull construction** for oil tankers of 5,000 DWT and above delivered after 1996, fundamentally altering the midship section geometry.

### 1.7 The 1969 Tonnage Convention

The **International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969**, was adopted by IMO to establish a universal system for measuring the tonnage of ships engaged in international voyages. It replaced the earlier heterogeneous national rules.

**Two Key Definitions:**
- **Gross Tonnage (GT)**: A measure of the ship's total internal volume, applied to all enclosed spaces.
- **Net Tonnage (NT)**: A measure of the ship's useful volume for cargo and passengers.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024:** Students must memorize the formula: $GT = K_1 \cdot V$ where $V$ is the total volume of all enclosed spaces in m³, and $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 SOLAS Chapter II-1: Subdivision and Damage Stability

The cornerstone of SOLAS for the naval architect is the **probabilistic damage stability** approach (introduced in 1991 amendments, applicable from 1998 onwards as the "SOLAS 90" standard). It replaced the deterministic "any one compartment" rule for passenger ships and is the basis for modern damage stability calculations.

#### 2.1.1 The Subdivision Index (A)

The **Attained Subdivision Index $A$** is calculated as:

$$A = \sum (p_i \cdot s_i)$$

Where:
- $p_i$ = probability of survival after flooding compartment $i$.
- $s_i$ = probability of the occurrence of the flooding of compartment $i$ only.

The **Required Subdivision Index $R$** is a function of the ship size and number of persons on board:

$$R = 1 - \frac{5000}{N} \quad \text{where } N = N_1 + 2N_2$$

Where:
- $N_1$ = number of persons for whom lifeboats are provided.
- $N_2$ = number of additional persons the ship is certified to carry.
- $R \geq 0.5$ for ships with $N \geq 1000$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The condition $A \geq R$ is a PASS criterion for damage stability. The numerical value of $R$ is frequently asked as a 3-mark question.

#### 2.1.2 Permeability $\mu$

The permeability of a space is the ratio of the volume that can be occupied by water to the total volume of that space. Standard SOLAS values:

| Space | Permeability ($\mu$) |
|---|---|
| Stores, cargo spaces, mail rooms, baggage rooms | **0.60** |
| Accommodation spaces | **0.95** |
| Machinery spaces | **0.85** |
| Light cargo spaces | **0.60** |
| Empty spaces (voids) | **0.00** |

### 2.2 Load Lines Convention: Freeboard Calculation

The minimum freeboard is determined by the **Type of Ship** (A, B, C, D) under the Convention. The basic formula is:

$$F_{min} = F_0 \cdot \text{(Correction factors)} + \text{(Sheer correction)} + \text{(Bow height correction)}$$

The basic freeboard $F_0$ for Type 'B' ship (most common for general cargo) is tabulated based on **Length $L$** in the Convention:

$$F_0 = f(L) \text{ from Table 1 of ICLL}$$

Where $L$ is the length in metres corresponding to **96% of the total waterline length** ($L_{96}$), or the length from the fore side of the stem to the axis of the rudder stock on the waterline if that is greater.

#### 2.2.1 Freeboard Correction Factors

The corrections applied to $F_0$ are:

$$F_{min} = F_0 \cdot \left( \frac{L}{100} \right)^{-0.215} \cdot \left( \frac{C_B}{0.65} \right)^{0.5} \cdot \left( \frac{100 \cdot SD}{L} \right)^{0.5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These corrections account for block coefficient ($C_B$) deviations from standard 0.65 and superstructure/deckhouse length ($SD$). The principal dimension designed is the **Moulded Depth $D$** since the freeboard is measured from the upper edge of the deck line.

### 2.3 MARPOL Annex I: Double Hull Tanker Geometry

For oil tankers, the most impactful design regulation is **MARPOL Annex I Regulation 19** which prescribes:

- **Double hull width** $b$: $b \geq \frac{B}{15}$ (m), minimum 1.0 m.
- **Double bottom height** $h$: $h \geq \frac{B}{15}$ (m), minimum 0.76 m, OR $h \geq 2.0$ m for ships of 20,000+ DWT if 0.76 m is impractical.
- **Wing tank width** at any cross-section: $b \geq 1.0$ m.
- **Slop tanks** capacity: minimum 3% of oil cargo capacity.

Where $B$ is the **moulded breadth**.

### 2.4 The 1969 Tonnage Convention: Formula Sheet

#### 2.4.1 Gross Tonnage (GT)

The gross tonnage is calculated as:

$$GT = K_1 \cdot V$$

$$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V) \quad \text{or} \quad K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V_c)$$

Where:
- $V$ = total volume of all enclosed spaces (m³).
- $V_c$ = total volume of cargo spaces (m³).

The function $K_1$ is **monotonically increasing but with decreasing rate** (logarithmic).

| Total Volume $V$ (m³) | $K_1$ |
|---|---|
| 1,000 | 0.2600 |
| 10,000 | 0.2800 |
| 100,000 | 0.3000 |
| 1,000,000 | 0.3200 |

#### 2.4.2 Net Tonnage (NT)

$$NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \left( \frac{4d}{3D} \right)^2 + K_3 \cdot (N_1 + N_2)$$

$$K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V_c)$$

$$K_3 = 1.25 \cdot \frac{GT + 10,000}{10,000}$$

Where:
- $d$ = moulded draught (m).
- $D$ = moulded depth to bulkhead deck (m).
- $N_1$ = number of passengers in cabins with not more than 8 berths.
- $N_2$ = number of other passengers.
- $\left( \frac{4d}{3D} \right)^2 \leq 1.0$

### 2.5 SOLAS Damage Stability Formula Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Cheat Sheet for Damage Stability:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Source |
|---|---|---|
| Required Subdivision Index | $R$ | $R = 1 - \frac{5000}{N}$ |
| Attained Subdivision Index | $A$ | $A = \sum p_i \cdot s_i$ |
| Survival Factor | $p_i$ | Function of residual stability (GZ curve area) |
| Probability of flooding | $s_i$ | Tabulated in SOLAS based on compartment length |
| Permeability (cargo) | $\mu$ | 0.60 |
| Permeability (accom.) | $\mu$ | 0.95 |
| Permeability (machinery) | $\mu$ | 0.85 |

The **range of positive stability** after damage for passenger ships must be at least **15° beyond the angle of vanishing stability**. The **residual GZ** must be at least **0.1 m** at the angle of maximum GZ.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of IMO Regulations

1. **Shipyard Contracts**: All shipbuilding contracts specify "Class and Flag" requirements. IMO regulations form the legal backbone.
2. **Hull Form Optimization**: The block coefficient $C_B$ and prismatic coefficient $C_P$ are tuned to pass freeboard regulations efficiently.
3. **Subdivision Layout**: Number and placement of watertight bulkheads (collision, engine room, cargo hold) follow SOLAS flooding boundaries.
4. **Midship Section Design**: Double hull requirements (for tankers) and scantling requirements (for bulk carriers under SOLAS Ch. XII) dictate the structural geometry.
5. **Statutory Dues**: Port State Control, Canal dues (Panama, Suez), and insurance premiums are based on GT and NT — making tonnage measurement a financial as well as technical parameter.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Subdivision Index $R$ for a Ship

**Problem:** A passenger ship has $N_1 = 1800$ passengers accommodated in lifeboats and $N_2 = 400$ additional passengers in liferafts. Calculate the Required Subdivision Index $R$.

**Step 1: Define the formula**

$$R = 1 - \frac{5000}{N}$$

**Step 2: Identify the values of $N_1$ and $N_2$**

From the problem:
- $N_1 = 1800$ (lifeboat passengers)
- $N_2 = 400$ (additional liferaft passengers)

**Step 3: Compute $N$**

$$N = N_1 + 2N_2$$

$$N = 1800 + 2 \times 400$$

$$N = 1800 + 800$$

$$N = 2600$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the formula**

$$R = 1 - \frac{5000}{2600}$$

$$R = 1 - 1.9231$$

$$R = -0.9231$$

**Step 5: Apply the minimum bound**

The SOLAS regulation states that $R$ must be at least 0.5 for ships with $N \geq 1000$, and $R$ is capped between 0.5 and 0.925.

$$\boxed{R_{required} = 0.5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Most modern large passenger ships (cruise liners) are designed to $R = 0.5$ as a minimum, but designers often aim for $A \geq R + 0.05$ to provide a safety margin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gross Tonnage Calculation

**Problem:** A general cargo ship has the following volume data:
- Total volume of all enclosed spaces: $V = 50{,}000$ m³.
- Volume of cargo holds: $V_c = 30{,}000$ m³.
- Moulded draught: $d = 9.5$ m.
- Moulded depth to bulkhead deck: $D = 12.0$ m.
- Passengers in cabins: $N_1 = 0$ (cargo ship).
- Other passengers: $N_2 = 0$.

Calculate GT and NT.

**Step 1: Calculate $K_1$**

$$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)$$

$$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(50{,}000)$$

$$\log_{10}(50{,}000) = \log_{10}(5 \times 10^4) = 4.6990$$

$$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.6990$$

$$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.09398$$

$$\boxed{K_1 = 0.29398}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Gross Tonnage**

$$GT = K_1 \cdot V$$

$$GT = 0.29398 \times 50{,}000$$

$$\boxed{GT = 14{,}699}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $K_2$**

$$K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V_c)$$

$$K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(30{,}000)$$

$$\log_{10}(30{,}000) = 4.4771$$

$$K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.4771$$

$$K_2 = 0.2 + 0.08954$$

$$\boxed{K_2 = 0.28954}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the draught-depth ratio term**

$$\left( \frac{4d}{3D} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{4 \times 9.5}{3 \times 12} \right)^2$$

$$= \left( \frac{38}{36} \right)^2 = (1.0556)^2$$

$$= 1.1142$$

Since this exceeds 1.0, the term is **capped at 1.0**:

$$\left( \frac{4d}{3D} \right)^2 = 1.0$$

**Step 5: Calculate $K_3$**

$$K_3 = 1.25 \cdot \frac{GT + 10{,}000}{10{,}000}$$

$$K_3 = 1.25 \cdot \frac{14{,}699 + 10{,}000}{10{,}000}$$

$$K_3 = 1.25 \cdot \frac{24{,}699}{10{,}000}$$

$$K_3 = 1.25 \times 2.4699$$

$$\boxed{K_3 = 3.0874}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Net Tonnage**

$$NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \left( \frac{4d}{3D} \right)^2 + K_3 \cdot (N_1 + N_2)$$

$$NT = 0.28954 \times 30{,}000 \times 1.0 + 3.0874 \times (0 + 0)$$

$$NT = 8686.2 + 0$$

$$\boxed{NT = 8686.2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** For a 50,000 m³ enclosed volume cargo ship, the GT ≈ 14,699 and NT ≈ 8,686. The ratio NT/GT ≈ 0.59, which is typical for dry cargo ships.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Double Hull Dimensions for an Oil Tanker

**Problem:** A 30,000 DWT product tanker has moulded breadth $B = 28$ m. Determine the minimum permissible double hull width $b$ and double bottom height $h$ as per MARPOL Annex I Reg. 19.

**Step 1: State the regulations**

According to MARPOL Annex I Regulation 19:
- Double hull width: $b \geq \max\left( \frac{B}{15},\ 1.0 \text{ m} \right)$.
- Double bottom height: $h \geq \max\left( \frac{B}{15},\ 0.76 \text{ m} \right)$ (with conditions for larger ships).

**Step 2: Calculate $b$**

$$b_{calc} = \frac{B}{15} = \frac{28}{15}$$

$$b_{calc} = 1.867 \text{ m}$$

Compare with the minimum of 1.0 m:
$$b_{calc} = 1.867 > 1.0$$

$$\boxed{b = 1.867 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $h$**

$$h_{calc} = \frac{B}{15} = \frac{28}{15}$$

$$h_{calc} = 1.867 \text{ m}$$

Compare with the minimum of 0.76 m:
$$h_{calc} = 1.867 > 0.76$$

$$\boxed{h = 1.867 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the design impact**

The cargo tank section is reduced from the full breadth $B = 28$ m to:
$$B_{cargo} = B - 2b = 28 - 2 \times 1.867 = 24.266 \text{ m}$$

The depth from baseline to tank top is reduced by $h = 1.867$ m compared to a single-hull design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Trade-off:** The double hull increases steel weight and reduces cargo volume, but provides substantial pollution protection. Approximately **20-25%** of the midship section is "lost" to the double hull void space.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Basic Freeboard from ICLL Tables

**Problem:** A Type 'B' ship has length $L = 130$ m. The block coefficient $C_B = 0.68$. The superstructure length $SD = 50$ m. Determine the basic freeboard correction.

**Step 1: Look up the basic freeboard $F_0$**

For Type 'B' ship with $L = 130$ m (from ICLL 1966 Annex II Table 1):

$$F_0 = 1.420 \text{ m (typical for this range)}$$

*(Note: Students should consult the official table for exact values; this is a representative value.)*

**Step 2: Apply the block coefficient correction**

$$F_{C_B} = F_0 \cdot \left( \frac{C_B}{0.65} \right)^{0.5}$$

$$F_{C_B} = 1.420 \cdot \left( \frac{0.68}{0.65} \right)^{0.5}$$

$$= 1.420 \cdot \sqrt{1.0462}$$

$$= 1.420 \times 1.0228$$

$$\boxed{F_{C_B} = 1.452 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Apply the length correction**

$$F_L = F_0 \cdot \left( \frac{L}{100} \right)^{-0.215}$$

$$F_L = 1.420 \cdot \left( \frac{130}{100} \right)^{-0.215}$$

$$= 1.420 \cdot (1.3)^{-0.215}$$

$$\log_{10}(1.3) = 0.1139$$

$$(1.3)^{-0.215} = 10^{-0.215 \times 0.1139} = 10^{-0.0245} = 0.9454$$

$$F_L = 1.420 \times 0.9454 = 1.342 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Final adjusted freeboard**

The smallest of the corrections governs:

$$\boxed{F_{min} = \min(F_L,\ F_{C_B}) = 1.342 \text{ m}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Regulation-by-Compartment Mapping

| IMO Regulation | Hull Geometry Affected | Structural Component | Design Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| SOLAS II-1 Subdivision | Number of bulkheads | Watertight bulkhead | Damage stability index $A \geq R$ |
| SOLAS II-2 Fire Safety | Decks, bulkheads | Fire divisions | A-60, A-30, A-0 class divisions |
| SOLAS XII Bulk Carriers | Double side skin in cargo holds | Side shell frames | Strength against hull girder bending |
| LL 1966 Load Lines | Moulded depth $D$ | Freeboard marking | Minimum summer freeboard $F_{min}$ |
| MARPOL Annex I | Double hull | Side shell + bottom plating | $b \geq B/15$ for tankers |
| MARPOL Annex VI | Engine room venting | Funnel height, scrubber | SOx emission, EEDI compliance |
| Tonnage 1969 | Enclosed spaces | Deckhouses, superstructures | Spaces $<4\%$ of $V$ excluded |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
