**Problem:** A box-shaped vessel has $L = 100$ m, $B = 20$ m, $T = 5$ m. KG = 3.5 m. Compute GM and GZ at 15° heel. ($\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³)

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= L \cdot B \cdot T = 100 \times 20 \times 5 = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^{3} \\[6pt]
\Delta &= 1.025 \times 10{,}000 = 10{,}250 \text{ t} \\[6pt]
I_T &= \dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 20^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 8000}{12} = 66{,}667 \text{ m}^{4} \\[6pt]
BM &= \dfrac{I_T}{V} = \dfrac{66{,}667}{10{,}000} = 6.667 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
KB &= \dfrac{T}{2} = 2.5 \text{ m} \quad (\text{rect. hull}) \\[6pt]
KM &= KB + BM = 2.5 + 6.667 = 9.167 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
GM &= KM - KG = 9.167 - 3.5 = 5.667 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
GZ_{15°} &= GM \cdot \sin(15°) = 5.667 \times 0.2588 = 1.467 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **KTU Examiner Tip:** Even though $GZ = GM\sin\phi$ is technically only valid for small angles, the *initial slope* of the GZ curve equals GM. For small-enough angles it gives a useful first-order value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of the Stability Booklet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SB[Stability Booklet] --> GP[1 General Particulars]
    SB --> HD[2 Hydrostatic Data]
    SB --> CC[3 Cross Curves KN]
    SB --> GZ[4 GZ Statical Curves]
    SB --> FSE[5 Free Surface Data]
    SB --> KGL[6 KG Limiting Curves]
    SB --> LC[7 Loading Conditions]
    SB --> CR[8 IMO Criteria Check]
    SB --> OP[9 Operating Limits]

    HD --> HD1[KB vs Draught]
    HD --> HD2[BM vs Draught]
    HD --> HD3[TPC vs Draught]
    HD --> HD4[MCTC vs Draught]

    CC --> CC1[KN at 0 to 90 deg]
    CC --> CC2[Multiple displacements]

    GZ --> GZ1[GZ vs Heel phi]
    GZ --> GZ2[Righting moment RM]

    CR --> CR1[Area 0 to 30 deg]
    CR --> CR2[Area 30 to 40 deg]
    CR --> CR3[GM greater than 0.15 m]
    CR --> CR4[Angle of max GZ]

    OP --> OP1[Max KG per draught]
    OP --> OP2[Tank filling rules]
    OP --> OP3[Cargo distribution guide]

    style SB fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style OP fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How the Master Uses the Booklet at Sea

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Master Receives Cargo and Bunker Figures] --> B[Compute Displacement and Draught]
    B --> C[Compute Vertical Centre of Gravity KG]
    C --> D[Read KM from Hydrostatic Table]
    D --> E[Compute GM equals KM minus KG]
    E --> F[Compute Free Surface Effect for Slack Tanks]
    F --> G[GM effective equals GM minus FSE]
    G --> H{Compare with KG Limit Curve}
    H -- OK --> I[Loading Approved]
    H -- Exceed --> J[Adjust Load or Ballast]
    J --> C
    I --> K[Plot GZ Curve for Condition]
    K --> L[Verify IMO Five Criteria]
    L --> M[Voyage Commences]

    style H fill:#ffd966
    style I fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Schematic: GZ Curve Annotated with IMO Criteria

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z[GZ Curve Phases] --> P1[Phase 1: Initial Stability GM sin phi]
    Z --> P2[Phase 2: Stability Peak GZ max at 25 to 40 deg]
    Z --> P3[Phase 3: Range up to Angle of Vanishing Stability]
    Z --> P4[Phase 4: Negative beyond AVS Capsize Region]

    P1 --> C1[Criterion Initial GZ at 30 deg GE 0.20 m]
    P2 --> C2[Criterion Angle of GZ max GE 25 deg]
    P3 --> C3[Criterion Area 0 to 30 deg GE 0.055 m rad]
    P3 --> C4[Criterion Area 30 to 40 deg GE 0.030 m rad]
    P3 --> C5[Criterion Weather Heeling Lever]

    style P4 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style C5 fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Information Flow During Booklet Preparation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Moulded Lines Plan] --> M2[Hull Form Coefficients]
    M2 --> M3[Hydrostatic Computation Module]
    M3 --> M4[Cross Curve Generator]
    M4 --> M5[KG Database from Loading Manual]
    M5 --> M6[GZ Plotter]
    M6 --> M7[IMO Criterion Validator]
    M7 --> M8[Approved Stability Booklet PDF]
    M8 --> M9[Flag State and Class Society Approval]
    M9 --> M10[Issued to Ship with Loading Manual]

    style M8 fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style M10 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*What is a Stability Booklet? List any four essential contents.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Stability Booklet** is a ship-specific, Class-approved document prepared in accordance with **IMO Resolution A.749(18)** that contains all data, curves, and operational limits required by the Master to ensure that the vessel maintains **adequate intact stability** under every loading condition throughout her service life.

Four essential contents:
1. **General particulars** (L, B, D, T, block coefficient).
2. **Hydrostatic particulars** (KB, BM, TPC, MCTC vs. draught).
3. **Cross curves of stability (KN)** at multiple displacements.
4. **KG limiting curves** versus displacement (or draught).
5. *(Any one more)* Free surface correction tables and IMO intact-stability check sheet.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series-2]** 
*Why is the Free Surface Effect (FSE) important in a Stability Booklet?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Free Surface Effect (FSE)** is the virtual rise of the ship's centre of gravity $G$ caused by a slack (partially filled) liquid tank whose surface remains horizontal as the ship heels. It is included in the Stability Booklet because:

1. **It reduces effective GM** by $FSE = \rho \cdot i_T / \Delta$, which can turn a *stable* ship into an *unstable* one if neglected.
2. It is **dynamic** — it changes with filling percentage and tank breadth.
3. It is a **major contributor to naval architecture casualties** (e.g., HMHS *Cap Trafalgar*, 1918; tankers in ballast).
4. The Booklet provides **pre-calculated FSE values** for every tank, allowing the Master to combine them as $\sum FSE_i$ instantly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the pair. Each Part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**.*

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *Define the Stability Booklet. With a neat block diagram, explain its hierarchical structure and information flow from moulded lines to the final approved document.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The Stability Booklet is the ship-specific, Class- and Flag-approved compendium of stability data, curves, criteria, and operating limits required by **IMO Res. A.749(18)** and **MSC.1/Circ.1200**, ensuring that the Master can verify intact stability for any loaded condition.

**Hierarchical Block Diagram (refer §4.1 & §4.4 above):**

| Stage | Input | Output |
|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | Moulded lines plan (offset table) | Hull form coefficients Cb, Cp |
| 2 | Form coefficients | Hydrostatic data (KB, BM, TPC) |
| 3 | Hydrostatic data | Cross curves of stability KN(φ, Δ) |
| 4 | KN curves + Loading manual KG | GZ curves GZ(φ) |
| 5 | GZ curves + FSE tables | IMO criteria check |
| 6 | All above | **KG limit curves** + Approved booklet |

**Information flow:** Moulded Lines → Hydrostatics → KN → GZ → FSE → Criteria → KG Limits → Approval → Issued to Ship.

**Conclusion:** Each stage feeds the next; without lower-stage accuracy, upper-stage conclusions become unsafe.

---

**(b)** *A box-shaped vessel has length L = 120 m, breadth B = 18 m, draught T = 6 m, KG = 4.0 m. Density of seawater = 1.025 t/m³. Calculate: (i) Displacement Δ, (ii) BM, (iii) KM, (iv) GM, (v) Righting lever GZ at 12° heel using the exact formula, and (vi) Righting moment RM in kN·m. Take KN at 12° = 1.40 m.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= L \cdot B \cdot T = 120 \times 18 \times 6 = 12{,}960 \text{ m}^{3} \\[6pt]
\Delta &= \rho \cdot V = 1.025 \times 12{,}960 = 13{,}284 \text{ t} \\[6pt]
I_T &= \dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{120 \times 18^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{120 \times 5832}{12} = 58{,}320 \text{ m}^{4} \\[6pt]
BM &= \dfrac{I_T}{V} = \dfrac{58{,}320}{12{,}960} = 4.500 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
KB &= \dfrac{T}{2} = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3.0 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
KM &= KB + BM = 3.0 + 4.5 = 7.5 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
GM &= KM - KG = 7.5 - 4.0 = 3.5 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
GZ_{12°} &= KN - KG \cdot \sin(12°) = 1.40 - 4.0 \times 0.2079 = 1.40 - 0.832 = 0.568 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
RM &= \Delta \cdot GZ \cdot g = 13{,}284 \times 0.568 \times 9.81 \text{ kN} \\[6pt]
RM &= 73{,}997 \text{ kN·m} \approx 74{,}000 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Note:** $RM = \Delta \cdot GZ$ in tonnes·m; multiply by $g = 9.81$ m/s² to obtain **kN·m**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** *State and explain the five mandatory IMO intact-stability criteria for a cargo ship as per Resolution A.749(18). Show them graphically on a representative GZ curve.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Criterion 1:** Area under GZ curve from 0° to 30° $\geq$ **0.055 m·rad**.
*Why:* Ensures the ship resists moderate rolling without losing stability early.

**Criterion 2:** Area under GZ curve from 0° to 40° $\geq$ **0.090 m·rad**.
*Why:* Provides reserve righting energy for severe wind/wave heeling.

**Criterion 3:** Area under GZ curve from 30° to 40° $\geq$ **0.030 m·rad**.
*Why:* Guarantees that stability does not collapse abruptly between 30° and 40° (deck-edge immersion protection).

**Criterion 4:** Righting lever $GZ_{max}$ occurs at an angle $\phi \geq$ **25°**.
*Why:* Avoids the dangerous "stiff-to-flinch" curve where GZ max appears before 25°.

**Criterion 5:** Initial metacentric height $GM \geq$ **0.15 m**.
*Why:* Prevents dangerously slow initial restoring in everyday seaways.

**Graphical Representation** (refer §4.3 schematic above). All five regions/criteria are marked on the GZ-vs-φ plot.

---

**(b)** *A rectangular service tank 8 m × 6 m × 4 m (filled to 50%) and a rectangular double-bottom tank 20 m × 12 m × 1.2 m (filled to 80%) both contain seawater. The ship displacement is 25,000 t and GM = 1.8 m. Compute the effective GM after applying FSE for both tanks.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Tank 1 (Service tank) — breadth in transverse direction = 6 m, length = 8 m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{T1} &= \dfrac{l \cdot b^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{8 \times 6^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{8 \times 216}{12} = 144 \text{ m}^{4} \\[6pt]
FSE_1 &= \dfrac{\rho \cdot i_{T1}}{\Delta} = \dfrac{1.025 \times 144}{25{,}000} = \dfrac{147.6}{25{,}000} = 0.00590 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: Filling percentage does **not** enter the small-angle formula directly; it only matters if the filling is so low that the surface touches the tank boundaries — assumed sufficient here.)*

**Tank 2 (Double-bottom tank) — breadth = 12 m, length = 20 m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{T2} &= \dfrac{20 \times 12^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{20 \times 1728}{12} = 2880 \text{ m}^{4} \\[6pt]
FSE_2 &= \dfrac{1.025 \times 2880}{25{,}000} = \dfrac{2952}{25{,}000} = 0.1181 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total correction and effective GM:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSE_{total} &= FSE_1 + FSE_2 = 0.00590 + 0.1181 = 0.124 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
GM_{eff} &= GM_{rigid} - FSE_{total} = 1.80 - 0.124 = 1.676 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The double-bottom tank dominates the loss because its transverse breadth (12 m) is large, and $i_T \propto b^3$.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in $\rho$** — Many students forget that $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³ for seawater (not 1000 kg/m³ without conversion). Always verify $\Delta$ is in **tonnes** to match $FSE$ in metres.
> 2. **Filling percentage in FSE** — In *small-heel theory*, $i_T$ is the full waterplane moment of the tank; partial filling reduces $i_T$ only when the free surface area shrinks (e.g., tank length or breadth is restricted by tank geometry). Do **not** simply multiply by 0.5 or 0.8 unless explicitly asked to use the "effective" $i_T$.
> 3. **Mixing up breadth and length** — In $i_T = Lb^3/12$, **b is the transverse dimension** (perpendicular to ship's centerline). KTU evaluators deduct full 1 mark for swapping L and B.
> 4. **Forgetting to sum FSEs** — If a ship has 5 slack tanks, **all five** must be summed: $\sum FSE_i$.
> 5. **GM must remain ≥ 0.15 m after correction** — If your computed $GM_{eff}$ falls below 0.15 m, the loading condition **fails IMO Criterion 5** and is unsafe.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Read before exam)**

- ✅ **Stability Booklet = ship-specific, IMO-compliant, Class-approved** — defined under Res. A.749(18) and MSC.1/Circ.1200.
- ✅ **Core purpose:** provide Master with all data needed to verify intact stability for *every* loading condition.
- ✅ **Mandatory contents:** General particulars, Hydrostatics, KN curves, GZ curves, FSE data, KG limits, Loading conditions, IMO criteria, Operating limits.
- ✅ **Master equations to memorise:**
  $BM = I_T / V$, $GM = KM - KG$, $GZ = KN - KG\sin\phi$, $FSE = \rho i_T / \Delta$.
- ✅ **IMO Five Criteria (cargo ships):** Areas 0–30° ≥ 0.055, 0–40° ≥ 0.090, 30–40° ≥ 0.030 m·rad, $\phi_{GZ_{max}} \geq 25°$, $GM \geq 0.15$ m.
- ✅ **KN curve is ship-form-dependent; GZ curve is condition-dependent.**
- ✅ **Free Surface Effect always reduces GM** — must be subtracted for **every** slack tank.
- ✅ **Small-angle approximation** $GZ = GM\sin\phi$ is valid **only up to ~10° heel**.
- ✅ **Righting Moment** $RM = \Delta \cdot GZ$ in t·m; multiply by $g$ for kN·m.
- ✅ **Rectangular waterplane simplification:** $I_T = L \cdot B^{3}/12$, $KB = T/2$.
- ✅ **KTU marker psychology:** Draw the **GZ curve with all 5 criteria marked** — examiners award full 7 marks when graph is present even if arithmetic has minor slips.
- ✅ **Booklet is a living document** — must be updated after any structural modification or major conversion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tonnage Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Tonnage Regulations # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tonnage** is a dimensionless numerical measure of a ship's volumetric capacity, regulated by international conventions to provide a uniform basis for port dues, safety regulations, registration, and statutory compliance. It is **NOT** a measure of weight or displacement, despite its name. The principal regulatory framework adopted globally is the **IMO International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969 (ITC 1969)**, which came into force on **18 July 1982** and applies to all new ships of **24 m** and above engaged on international voyages. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course explicitly covers the 1969 Tonnage Convention formulae, the KTM (Koeze–Todd–Malone) system, and special measurement systems used by the Suez and Panama Canals. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Apartment Size" Intuition Imagine two houses: - One is a **huge bungalow** with 12 bedrooms but only a small family living inside. - The other is a **small flat** with 2 rooms, but 8 people living inside. If a municipal tax depended on size, you'd use **Gross Area** for the bungalow (lots of enclosed space). If tax depended on how many people actually benefit from the space, you'd use a **Net Occupied Area** for the flat. **Ships work the same way:** - **Gross Tonnage (GT)** = the "total enclosed volume" of all spaces — like the bungalow's full floor plan. The tax authority cares about the size of your "structure." - **Net Tonnage (NT)** = the "useful earning volume" — cargo + passengers. The harbour master cares about how much *business* your ship does. > [!TIP] > Think of GT as the *building* and NT as the *rentable office space*. The lobby, walls, stairs (machinery, fuel tanks, crew quarters not generating revenue) shrink the NT relative to GT. ### 1.3 Why Tonnage is Volumetric, Not Weight-Based Historically, tonnage was an *approximation of weight* (based on a tun of wine ≈ 100 ft³). Modern tonnage is **purely volumetric** because: 1. Port fees scale with the **space occupied at the berth**, not the weight of cargo. 2. Safety regulations (e.g., life-saving appliances) scale with **number of people on board**, derivable from volume. 3. A single dimensionless number simplifies **international legal interoperability** across 150+ IMO member states. ### 1.4 Categories of Tonnage — At a Glance | Category | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | Gross Tonnage | **GT** | Total enclosed volume (scaled) | Registration, safety rules | | Net Tonnage | **NT** | Revenue-generating volume | Port dues, canal fees | | Deadweight Tonnage | **DWT** | Weight of cargo + stores + crew | Commercial cargo capacity | | Displacement | **Δ** | Weight of water displaced | Naval architecture, stability | | Suez Canal Gross | **SCGT** | Special volume measure | Suez Canal transit fee | | Panama Canal | **PC/UMS** | Universal Measurement System | Panama Canal transit fee | | KTM Tonnage | **KTM GT/NT** | Pre-1969 empirical formula | Legacy tonnage comparison | > [!WARNING] > KTU Common Mistake: Never use tonnes (mass units) for GT or NT. They are **dimensionless numbers**. DWT *is* expressed in tonnes (mass). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 1969 IMO Tonnage Convention — Logic Flow The convention defines tonnage in **three explicit stages** to ensure unambiguity: 1. **Stage 1 — Measure all enclosed volume** (V) in m³ below the tonnage deck. 2. **Stage 2 — Apply a non-linear "K" coefficient** to obtain GT. 3. **Stage 3 — Subtract non-revenue volumes** to obtain NT, with draft correction. The "K" coefficient is a clever logarithmic scale that approximates the **pre-1969 empirical Moorsom system** while remaining dimensionally cleaner. > [!NOTE] > **Why logarithmic?** The convention designers wanted GT to grow *sub-linearly* with volume so that doubling the ship did not double the regulatory burden disproportionately. This rewards economy of scale. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Computation Logic for GT ### Step 1 — Define Enclosed Volume (V) Sum of volumes of all enclosed spaces **below the tonnage deck** (in m³) plus spaces **above** the tonnage deck that are fully enclosed. $$V = \sum_{i} V_i \quad [\text{m}^3]$$ where $V_i$ is the volume of the $i$-th enclosed compartment, calculated using standard moulded dimensions. ### Step 2 — Apply the K₁ Coefficient $$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)$$ ### Step 3 — Compute GT $$GT = K_1 \cdot V$$ GT is **rounded down** to the nearest integer and must not be less than **0.30 GT** for any measured ship. ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Computation Logic for NT ### Step 1 — Identify V_c and V_s - $V_c$ = total volume of **cargo holds** below the tonnage deck (m³) - $V_s$ = total volume of **passenger and crew spaces** (m³) ### Step 2 — Apply Draft Correction Factor (D / d) - $D$ = moulded depth amidships (m) - $d$ = moulded draft at the **assigned load line** (m) - The factor is $\left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2$ - If $d < D/2$, then $d = D/2$ is used (lower bound for safety) - If $d > D$, then $d = D$ is used (upper bound) ### Step 3 — Apply the K₃ Factor $$K_3 = 1.25 - \frac{GT}{10{,}000}$$ For ships with **GT < 100**, $K_3 = 1.25$ (clamped). ### Step 4 — Compute NT $$NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 \cdot K_3 + K_2 \cdot V_s \cdot \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 \cdot K_4$$ Where: $$K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(K_3 \cdot V_c + V_s)$$ For ships with **GT < 100**, $K_2 = 1.0$ (simplified). ### Step 5 — NT Final Constraints - $NT \geq 0.30 \cdot GT$ (minimum 30% rule) - For pure passenger ships: $K_4 = \left(\dfrac{N_1 + 10 N_2}{10000}\right)^{1/2}$, where $N_1$ = number of passengers in cabins ≤ 8 berths, $N_2$ = number of passengers in other spaces. > [!NOTE] > **NT cannot be less than 0.30 × GT.** This is a regulatory floor ensuring that even pure tankers (no passengers, minimal crew volume) still pay a meaningful "structural" component. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V)$ | GT coefficient | dimensionless | | 2 | $GT = K_1 \cdot V$ | Gross Tonnage | dimensionless | | 3 | $K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(K_3 V_c + V_s)$ | NT coefficient | dimensionless | | 4 | $K_3 = 1.25 - GT/10000$ | Mixed-use factor | dimensionless | | 5 | $K_4 = \left(\dfrac{N_1+10N_2}{10000}\right)^{1/2}$ | Passenger factor | dimensionless | | 6 | $Draft Factor = \left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2$ | Draft correction | dimensionless | | 7 | $NT = K_2 V_c (4d/3D)^2 K_3 + K_2 V_s (4d/3D)^2 K_4$ | Net Tonnage | dimensionless | | 8 | $NT \geq 0.30 \cdot GT$ | Regulatory minimum | dimensionless | | 9 | $GT \geq 0.30$ | GT floor | dimensionless | | 10 | $DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$ | Deadweight Tonnage | tonnes (mass) | | 11 | $PC/UMS = V_{cm} \cdot F$ | Panama Universal Measurement | tonnes | | 12 | $SCNT = V_{cm} \cdot 0.8 \cdot (1 - 0.04/LCB)$ | Suez Net Tonnage (legacy) | tonnes | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU exam, when you see $\vert x \vert$ in a tonnage formula (e.g., $\vert GT - V \vert$ style cross-checks), always write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ in your answer sheet to avoid losing formatting marks. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Port Dues** (e.g., Rotterdam, Singapore) scale with GT. - **Canal Transit Fees** (Suez, Panama) use SCNT / PC/UMS respectively. - **Safety Rules** (life jackets, lifeboat capacity) scale with NT because NT ≈ number of persons aboard. - **Registration & Insurance** are tied to GT. - **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) applicability** triggers at **GT ≥ 500** for most regulations. - **MARPOL pollution limits** (e.g., sewage treatment for ≥ 400 GT) use GT thresholds. > [!TIP] > Memorize the **threshold values**: SOLAS passenger ship = 500 GT, oil tanker double-hull = 600 DWT, sewage discharge = 400 GT. These are favourite KTU short-answer questions. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — From First Principles to GT/NT ### 3.1.1 Deriving the K₁ Function The IMO 1969 Convention needed a formula that would **smoothly approach** the pre-existing Moorsom tonnage curve. Empirically, the relationship between gross register tonnage (GRT) and volume $V$ in m³ was found to be approximately: $$GRT_{Moorsom} \approx 0.591 \cdot V^{0.968}$$ To simplify to a closed form, IMO engineers fitted a **piecewise-linear approximation in log–log space** that, when reduced, yielded: $$GT = (0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V) \cdot V$$ Let us verify the consistency. Define $K_1$ such that $GT = K_1 V$: $$K_1 = \frac{GT}{V} = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V$$ **Verification of monotonicity:** $$\frac{dK_1}{dV} = 0.02 \cdot \frac{1}{V \ln 10} > 0 \quad \text{for all } V > 0$$ So $K_1$ increases slowly with $V$, ensuring GT grows slightly faster than linear. > [!NOTE] > For $V = 100$ m³: $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot 2 = 0.24$, so $GT = 24$. > For $V = 10{,}000$ m³: $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot 4 = 0.28$, so $GT = 2{,}800$. > The increase from 100 m³ → 10,000 m³ (100× volume) gives a 24 → 2,800 jump (117×), confirming slight super-linearity. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Bulk Carrier ### Given Data - Total enclosed volume: $V = 50{,}000$ m³ - Volume of cargo holds: $V_c = 30{,}000$ m³ - Volume of crew/passenger spaces: $V_s = 1{,}500$ m³ - Moulded draft at load line: $d = 12.0$ m - Moulded depth amidships: $D = 16.0$ m ### Step 1 — Calculate $K_1$ $$\log_{10}(50{,}000) = \log_{10}(5 \times 10^4) = 4 + \log_{10}(5) = 4.69897$$ $$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.69897 = 0.2 + 0.09398 = 0.29398$$ ### Step 2 — Calculate GT $$GT = K_1 \cdot V = 0.29398 \times 50{,}000 = 14{,}699$$ Rounding **down** to integer: $GT = 14{,}699$. ### Step 3 — Calculate Draft Factor $$\left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4 \times 12.0}{3 \times 16.0}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{48}{48}\right)^2 = 1.00$$ > [!TIP] > When $d = 0.75 D$, the draft factor is exactly 1.0 — a useful shortcut for KTU exam problems. ### Step 4 — Calculate $K_3$ $$K_3 = 1.25 - \frac{GT}{10{,}000} = 1.25 - \frac{14{,}699}{10{,}000} = 1.25 - 1.4699 = -0.2199$$ > [!WARNING] > Negative $K_3$ is impossible per IMO rules. This is a **trap question** in the KTU exam! Per the convention, $K_3$ has a **lower bound of 0.25**, so $K_3 = \max(1.25 - GT/10000,\, 0.25)$. So: $$K_3 = 0.25 \quad \text{(clamped)}$$ ### Step 5 — Calculate $K_2$ $$K_3 \cdot V_c + V_s = 0.25 \times 30{,}000 + 1{,}500 = 7{,}500 + 1{,}500 = 9{,}000$$ $$\log_{10}(9{,}000) = 3 + \log_{10}(9) = 3 + 0.95424 = 3.95424$$ $$K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \times 3.95424 = 0.2 + 0.07908 = 0.27908$$ ### Step 6 — Calculate NT Since this is not a passenger ship, $K_4 = 1.0$: $$NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 \cdot K_3 + K_2 \cdot V_s \cdot \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 \cdot K_4$$ $$NT = 0.27908 \times 30{,}000 \times 1.00 \times 0.25 + 0.27908 \times 1{,}500 \times 1.00 \times 1.0$$ $$NT = 2{,}093.10 + 418.62 = 2{,}511.72$$ Rounding down: $NT = 2{,}511$. ### Step 7 — Verify the 30% Rule $$0.30 \times GT = 0.30 \times 14{,}699 = 4{,}409.7$$ Our $NT = 2{,}511 < 4{,}409.7$! This **violates** the 30% floor. Therefore: $$NT_{final} = 4{,}410 \quad \text{(forced to the regulatory minimum)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a classic KTU-style numerical trap. The 30% floor is **not optional** and is checked at the very end. Always apply it. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem Classification | Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula | Common Error | |---|---|---|---|---| | Type I: Pure GT | V | GT | $GT = K_1 V$ | Forgetting $K_1$ | | Type II: Pure NT | V_c, V_s, d, D, GT | NT | 7-step process | Forgetting $K_3$ clamp | | Type III: Mixed | All above | GT and NT | Combined | Ignoring 30% floor | | Type IV: Reverse | GT, NT | V or V_c | Inverse log | Log base confusion | | Type V: Canal | Dimensional data | SCNT, PC/UMS | Special formulas | Wrong coefficient | ## 3.4 Derivation of the 30% NT Floor (KTU Conceptual) The 30% floor exists because **pure tankers** (very low $V_c$ relative to $V$) would otherwise have $NT \to 0$, making port fees negligible. The IMO justified the floor as a **fair-share contribution to port infrastructure** proportional to the ship's structural footprint. Mathematically, the convention explicitly states: $$\text{if } NT_{calculated} < 0.30 \cdot GT \text{ then } NT = 0.30 \cdot GT$$ This was introduced as **Regulation 7(2)(b)** of Annex I of ITC 1969. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Tonnage Computation Hierarchy Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ship Hull Geometry] --> B[Measure All Enclosed Volumes Vi] B --> C[Total Volume V in m cubed] C --> D[Compute K1 = 0.2 + 0.02 log V] D --> E[GT = K1 times V] E --> F{GT less than 100?} F -- Yes --> G[Use Simplified Rules K2 = 1.0] F -- No --> H[Compute K3 = max 1.25 minus GT/10000, 0.25] H --> I[Identify Vc cargo and Vs service] I --> J[Compute Draft Factor 4d over 3D squared] J --> K[Compute K2 = 0.2 + 0.02 log of K3 Vc + Vs] K --> L[Compute NT raw] L --> M{NT less than 0.30 GT?} M -- Yes --> N[Set NT = 0.30 GT floor] M -- No --> O[Accept NT] G --> O N --> P[Final GT and NT] O --> P ``` ## 4.2 Tonnage Type Comparison Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IMO 1969 Convention] --> B[Gross Tonnage GT] A --> C[Net Tonnage NT] D[KTM Koeze Todd Malone] --> E[Pre 1969 Empirical] F[Suez Canal] --> G[SCGT and SCNT] H[Panama Canal] --> I[PC UMS Universal] J[Moorsom System] --> D B --> K[Port Dues and SOLAS] C --> L[Canal Fees and Crew Rules] G --> M[Suez Transit Fee] I --> N[Panama Transit Fee] E --> O[Legacy Insurance Records] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Data Flow for NT Computation ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Input Block] --> A1[Total Volume V] A0 --> A2[Volume of Cargo Vc] A0 --> A3[Volume of Service Vs] A0 --> A4[Draft d] A0 --> A5[Depth D] A1 --> B1[Block K1 Calculator] B1 --> B2[Block GT Output] B2 --> C1[Block K3 Calculator] A2 --> C2[Block Volume Mixer] A3 --> C2 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3[Block K2 Calculator] A4 --> D1[Block Draft Ratio] A5 --> D1 D1 --> D2[Block Draft Factor] C3 --> E1[Block NT Assembly] D2 --> E1 E1 --> E2[Block 30 Percent Floor Check] E2 --> E3[Final NT] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree — 30% Floor Application ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Compute NT raw] --> compare{NT raw less than
0.30 times GT?} compare -- Yes --> apply[Apply Floor
NT = 0.30 times GT] compare -- No --> accept[Accept NT raw] apply --> end1[Output Final NT] accept --> end1 ``` ## 4.5 Schematic — Volume Regions on a Ship ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Tonnage_Deck_Above A1[Enclosed Superstructure] A2[Passenger Lounges Vs] end subgraph Tonnage_Deck_Below B1[Cargo Holds Vc] B2[Machinery Spaces] B3[Ballast Tanks] B4[Stores and Crew] end A1 --> TotalV[Total Enclosed Volume V] A2 --> TotalV B1 --> TotalV B2 --> TotalV B3 --> TotalV B4 --> TotalV TotalV --> GT[GT Calculation] B1 --> NT[NT Calculation] A2 --> NT ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the terms Gross Tonnage (GT) and Net Tonnage (NT) as per the IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention. State the units and their primary uses.** **Model Answer:** > **Gross Tonnage (GT):** A dimensionless measure of the total internal volume of a ship, calculated as $GT = K_1 \cdot V$, where $V$ is the total volume of all enclosed spaces in m³ and $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V)$. > **Net Tonnage (NT):** A dimensionless measure of the *useful earning volume* of a ship (cargo + passenger + crew volumes), adjusted for draft and ship type. Used for calculating port dues, canal fees, and crew-related safety regulations. > **Primary uses:** > - **GT** → Registration, SOLAS applicability (≥ 500 GT), life-saving equipment. > - **NT** → Harbour dues, canal tolls (Suez/Panama), manning scales. > [!IMPORTANT] > Both GT and NT are **dimensionless** (no tonnes of mass). KTU expects the exact formula $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V$. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the KTM Tonnage System? Why is the IMO 1969 Convention considered superior to KTM?** **Model Answer:** > **KTM (Koeze–Todd–Malone) System** was developed in **1939** by J.J. Koeze, F.H. Todd, and P.K. Malone at the University of Glasgow as an empirical alternative to the old Moorsom system. It uses coefficients $A$ and $B$ that depend on hull form, length, breadth, and depth, fitted to a database of contemporary merchant ships. > **Why IMO 1969 is superior:** > 1. **Universal applicability** — KTM coefficients were fitted to 1930s ship forms and became inaccurate for modern hulls. > 2. **Simplicity** — KTM required hull-form measurements; IMO uses only total enclosed volume. > 3. **Legal uniformity** — Adopted by **150+ countries**; KTM was a national British/European standard. > 4. **Handles oddities better** — KTM over-penalized slender or unusual ships. ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] A bulk carrier has the following particulars: - Total enclosed volume $V = 60{,}000$ m³ - Volume of cargo holds $V_c = 36{,}000$ m³ - Volume of passenger and crew spaces $V_s = 1{,}800$ m³ - Moulded draft at the assigned load line $d = 12.6$ m - Moulded depth amidships $D = 16.8$ m **(a)** Calculate the **Gross Tonnage (GT)** of the vessel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the **Net Tonnage (NT)** of the vessel and verify the 30% minimum rule. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):** **Step 1:** Compute $\log_{10}(V) = \log_{10}(60{,}000) = 4 + \log_{10}(6) = 4.77815$. **Step 2:** Compute $K_1$: $$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.77815 = 0.2 + 0.09556 = 0.29556$$ **Step 3:** Compute $GT$: $$GT = K_1 \times V = 0.29556 \times 60{,}000 = 17{,}733.6$$ **Step 4:** Round **down** to integer: $GT = 17{,}733$. > The "round down" rule is per **Regulation 4(1)** of ITC 1969. --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):** **Step 1:** Compute the draft factor: $$\frac{4d}{3D} = \frac{4 \times 12.6}{3 \times 16.8} = \frac{50.4}{50.4} = 1.00$$ $$\left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 = 1.00$$ > Convenient! $d = 0.75 D$ is the "neutral" loading condition. **Step 2:** Compute $K_3$: $$K_3 = 1.25 - \frac{GT}{10{,}000} = 1.25 - 1.7733 = -0.5233$$ > [!WARNING] > Negative $K_3$ — **clamp to 0.25** per Regulation 6(2). $$K_3 = 0.25$$ **Step 3:** Compute the combined revenue-volume term: $$K_3 V_c + V_s = 0.25 \times 36{,}000 + 1{,}800 = 9{,}000 + 1{,}800 = 10{,}800$$ **Step 4:** Compute $K_2$: $$\log_{10}(10{,}800) = 4 + \log_{10}(1.08) = 4 + 0.03342 = 4.03342$$ $$K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.03342 = 0.2 + 0.08067 = 0.28067$$ **Step 5:** Compute NT (no passengers with cabins, so $K_4 = 1.0$): $$NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 \cdot K_3 + K_2 \cdot V_s \cdot \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 \cdot K_4$$ $$NT = 0.28067 \times 36{,}000 \times 1.00 \times 0.25 + 0.28067 \times 1{,}800 \times 1.00 \times 1.0$$ $$NT = 2{,}526.03 + 505.21 = 3{,}031.24$$ Round down: $NT_{raw} = 3{,}031$. **Step 6:** Verify the 30% floor: $$0.30 \times GT = 0.30 \times 17{,}733 = 5{,}319.9$$ Since $3{,}031 < 5{,}319.9$, the floor applies: $$\boxed{NT_{final} = 5{,}320}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the **Suez Canal Net Tonnage (SCNT)** and **Suez Canal Gross Tonnage (SCGT)** formulae. Why does the Suez Canal use a special measurement system? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A container ship has GT = 25,000 and NT = 12,500. Its total enclosed volume is 90,000 m³. Verify whether the given values are internally consistent. If inconsistent, identify the discrepancy. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):** **Suez Canal Special Tonnage:** The Suez Canal Authority (SCA) uses a unique formula based on the **Moorsom System modified by R. Lloyd's Register in 1872** and updated periodically. Unlike the IMO system, Suez tonnage **is in tonnes (mass units)** because it dates from a time when tonnage implied weight. > **Suez Canal Net Tonnage (SCNT):** > $$SCNT = V_{cm} \cdot \left( 0.8 + 0.04 \cdot \frac{LCB_{aft}}{L_{bp}} \right)$$ > where $V_{cm}$ is the total volume of cargo and passenger spaces (in m³) and $LCB_{aft}$ is the longitudinal centre of buoyancy measured from the aft perpendicular. > **Suez Canal Gross Tonnage (SCGT):** > $$SCGT = V_{L} \cdot 0.85$$ > where $V_L$ is the total volume of all enclosed spaces. > **Why a special system?** > 1. **Historical precedent** — Adopted in 1872, before any international convention. > 2. **Revenue protection** — The SCNT formula is calibrated to yield ~10–15% higher NT than IMO NT for the same ship, increasing transit fees. > 3. **No IMO binding** — Canals are sovereign and can set their own fee basis. > 4. **Recognizes ship form** — The $LCB$ term penalises unbalanced ships. --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):** **Step 1:** Recompute GT from the given V: $$\log_{10}(90{,}000) = 4 + \log_{10}(9) = 4.95424$$ $$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.95424 = 0.2 + 0.09908 = 0.29908$$ $$GT_{calc} = 0.29908 \times 90{,}000 = 26{,}917.2$$ Round down: $GT_{calc} = 26{,}917$. **Step 2:** Compare with given GT = 25,000. > **Discrepancy: GT is given as 25,000 but calculated as 26,917.** This is a difference of 1,917 units (≈ 7.7% error). **Step 3:** Likely cause: - The given $V = 90{,}000$ m³ was **overstated** by the problem-setter, or - The given $GT = 25{,}000$ is **from the KTM legacy system** (older ships), not IMO 1969. **Step 4:** Verify the NT/GT ratio: $$\frac{NT}{GT} = \frac{12{,}500}{25{,}000} = 0.50$$ This passes the 30% floor check (since $0.50 > 0.30$), so NT is internally valid for the stated GT. > **Conclusion:** GT and NT are mutually consistent (50% ratio, 30% floor satisfied), but the **GT is inconsistent with the stated volume $V$**. Either the volume is wrong, or a non-IMO measurement basis is being used. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Top 5):** > 1. **Forgetting to clamp $K_3$ at 0.25** — loses 1 mark silently when GT > 12,500. > 2. **Forgetting the 30% NT floor** — silent 1–2 mark loss on the final verification step. > 3. **Rounding GT/NT up instead of down** — convention says always **round down**. > 4. **Using wrong logarithm base** — convention uses $\log_{10}$, NOT $\ln$ (natural log). > 5. **Forgetting the $K_4$ passenger factor** — relevant only for passenger ships; for cargo ships, $K_4 = 1.0$ (don't compute it). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - [ ] **Tonnage is dimensionless**, not mass — common KTU trap. - [ ] **GT = $K_1 V$** where $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V$. - [ ] **NT** uses $V_c$, $V_s$, $K_2$, $K_3$, $K_4$, and draft factor $(4d/3D)^2$. - [ ] **$K_3$ is clamped** to a minimum of **0.25**. - [ ] **$K_2$ becomes 1.0** for ships with GT < 100 (simplified regime). - [ ] **30% floor:** $NT \geq 0.30 \times GT$ (always verify last). - [ ] **Rounding direction:** Always **round DOWN** for both GT and NT. - [ ] **Draft factor = 1.0** when $d = 0.75 D$ (neutral condition — useful shortcut). - [ ] **1969 Convention applies** to all ships ≥ 24 m engaged on international voyages. - [ ] **KTM** is the pre-1969 empirical system, largely obsolete for new builds. - [ ] **Suez uses SCNT/SCGT** (mass-based, includes LCB term). - [ ] **Panama uses PC/UMS** (Universal Measurement System). - [ ] **DWT ≠ GT/NT** — DWT is mass (tonnes), GT/NT are volume-related (dimensionless). - [ ] **SOLAS** trigger threshold = **500 GT** for passenger ships. - [ ] **MARPOL sewage** trigger = **400 GT**. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Memory Hook:** *"K-one times V gives you G-T; K-two times Vc and Vs gives you N-T; K-three clamps at quarter; thirty percent is the floor you must deliver."*

topics.title: Gross Tonnage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gross Tonnage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Gross Tonnage (GT)

**Gross Tonnage (GT)** is the unitless measure of the *overall internal volumetric capacity of a ship*, calculated strictly under the **International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969 (IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention)**, which came into force globally on **18 July 1994**. GT is not a unit of weight — it is a pure volumetric index expressed as a function of the total volume of all enclosed spaces of a vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBNST504 — Module 4):**
> Gross Tonnage is a statutory, regulation-driven index. It governs **SOLAS applicability, IMO safety rules, manning levels, registration fees, port dues, and classification society charges**. Every commercial vessel above **24 m** in length must hold a valid International Tonnage Certificate (ITC 1969).

The official IMO definition states:

$$
\text{GT} = K_{1} \cdot V
$$

where:
- **V** = Total volume of **all enclosed spaces** of the ship (in cubic metres, m³)
- **K₁** = A dimensionless coefficient derived from V

$$
K_{1} = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)
$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for the Student

Imagine you have a **transparent, hollowed-out scale model of the ship** — like a giant sealed glass bottle. Now imagine **pouring water into it until every internal nook, cabin, corridor, engine room, and tank** is filled, but you *stop the water at the uppermost continuous deck level*. The volume of that water (in m³) is **V**.

Gross Tonnage does **not** equal that volume directly. Instead, it applies a **logarithmic scaling factor (K₁)**. Think of **K₁** as a *currency exchange rate* between raw cubic metres and a "regulation-friendly" tonnage number — so that a giant oil tanker doesn't end up with an astronomically large GT that becomes unmanageable for paperwork. The logarithmic taper *compresses* the growth, keeping GT values in a practical numeric range across vessel sizes.

| Vessel Size | Volume (m³) | K₁ (approx) | GT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small Fishing Boat | ~50 | 0.252 | ~12.6 |
| Handysize Bulker | ~30,000 | 0.337 | ~10,110 |
| VLCC Tanker | ~300,000 | 0.373 | ~111,900 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why log₁₀?** Because as ships grew bigger in the 20th century, the old Gross Register Tonnage (GRT) formula (a purely linear volume measure) became unwieldy. The IMO 1969 convention intentionally **dampened GT growth at large sizes** by introducing the log term, so that regulatory thresholds remained meaningful.

---

## 1.3 What GT Measures vs. What it Doesn't

GT counts **every enclosed space** of the ship below the *tonnage deck* and above it, including:
- Cargo holds
- Machinery spaces
- Crew accommodations
- Bridge and superstructure
- Ballast tanks (when part of hull structure)
- Funnel and mast enclosures (if covered)

GT **excludes**:
- Open deck spaces (no overhead deck above)
- Water in open channels
- Spaces specifically marked as *appendices* of the hull

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do not confuse GT with **Displacement Tonnage** (which is in tonnes and is a measure of *weight of water displaced*) or **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** (which is *cargo-carrying capacity* in tonnes). GT is purely a **volumetric regulatory index**, dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — The IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention

Prior to 1969, ships were measured using **Moorsom's system** (1854) and a heterogeneous mix of national rules. This created disputes over port fees and safety thresholds. The **IMO** (then IMCO) standardized the system. The new GT replaced:
- **Gross Register Tonnage (GRT)** — the legacy British volumetric measure
- **Net Register Tonnage (NRT)** — used for cargo-only spaces

### 2.1.1 Key Statutory Thresholds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Ships built **before 18 July 1982** → measured under old GRT/NRT system
> - Ships built **between 18 July 1982 and 17 July 1994** → transitional dual measurement (both GRT and GT shown on certificate)
> - Ships built **after 18 July 1994** → **ONLY GT** is shown. GRT is no longer legally valid.

## 2.2 The Two-Coefficient System (K₁ and K₂)

The IMO 1969 convention uses **two logarithmic coefficients**:

**K₁** — for Gross Tonnage:

$$
K_{1} = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)
$$

**K₂** — for Net Tonnage (used in NT formula):

$$
K_{2} = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}\left(V_{c} \cdot \frac{N}{V}\right)
$$

where **V_c** = cargo volume, **N** = number of passengers, and the bracketed term is bounded such that $\left(V_{c} \cdot \frac{N}{V}\right) \geq 0.25$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Relevant)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table for Module 4 questions — these equations are tested in **ESE (End Semester Exam)** regularly.

| **Formula** | **Definition** | **Applicability / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $GT = K_{1} \cdot V$ | Gross Tonnage | Dimensionless, $V \geq 10 \text{ m}^3$ |
| $K_{1} = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)$ | Coefficient for GT | Dimensionless |
| $V$ | Total volume of all enclosed spaces | m³ |
| $NT = K_{2} \cdot V_{c} \cdot (4d/3D)^2 + K_{2} \cdot \text{Passenger Spaces}$ | Net Tonnage | Dimensionless |
| $K_{2} = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V_{c} \cdot N/V)$ | Coefficient for NT | Dimensionless |
| $DWT = \text{Displacement}_{\text{loaded}} - \text{Lightweight}$ | Deadweight Tonnage | Tonnes (t) |
| $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement | Tonnes, $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ (seawater) |
| $\text{Lightweight} = \text{Steel} + \text{Outfit} + \text{Machinery}$ | Light Ship Weight | Tonnes |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of GT

- **SOLAS Regulation Threshold:** Cargo ships ≥ 500 GT require specific structural and life-saving appliance rules
- **MARPOL Annex VI:** Applies to ships ≥ 400 GT for air pollution control
- **Manning Rules (STCW):** Number of certified officers required depends on GT
- **Load Line Convention:** Some thresholds use GT
- **Port State Control:** Inspections, certificates all keyed to GT

## 2.5 Why the Logarithmic Coefficient Matters

The factor $0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)$ provides **sub-linear growth**. For very small V, K₁ ≈ 0.20. For very large V (say V = 1,000,000 m³), K₁ ≈ 0.40. Thus GT grows at roughly **half the rate** of raw volume. This keeps regulatory thresholds, which are written as fixed numbers (e.g., "ships over 500 GT"), in a sensible operational range.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the K₁ Coefficient (Logical Origin)

The IMO 1969 convention did not derive K₁ from first principles — it was a **calibrated empirical fit** chosen to ensure continuity with the legacy GRT system at typical mid-sized vessels, while smoothing growth at the extremes. However, the **logical structure** can be reconstructed as follows.

### 3.1.1 Continuity Constraint

At $V = 100 \text{ m}^3$, the old GRT (based on $100 \text{ ft}^3 = 1 \text{ ton}$) gave a reference tonnage. The IMO selected K₁ such that GT matched GRT *in the small-to-medium ship regime*. This gave the constant **0.2** in the formula.

$$
K_{1} = 0.2 + f(V)
$$

### 3.1.2 Growth-Damping Term

To prevent GT from rising linearly with V (which would make 500 GT thresholds meaningless as ships got larger), a logarithmic correction $0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)$ was added:

$$
K_{1} = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)
$$

### 3.1.3 Boundary Behaviour

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } V = 1 \text{ m}^3 &: \quad K_{1} = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(1) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot 0 \\
&= 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } V = 10,000 \text{ m}^3 &: \quad K_{1} = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(10{,}000) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot 4 \\
&= 0.2 + 0.08 = 0.28
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } V = 1,000,000 \text{ m}^3 &: \quad K_{1} = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(10^6) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot 6 \\
&= 0.2 + 0.12 = 0.32
\end{aligned}
$$

So K₁ stays bounded between **0.20 and ~0.40** for any practical ship, making GT well-behaved.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — GT Calculation (KTU-Style Numerical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exact numerical pattern** asked in KTU ESE questions under Module 4.

**Problem Statement:**

A Panamax bulk carrier has the following enclosed space volumes, calculated from the general arrangement plan:

| Space | Volume (m³) |
|---|---|
| Cargo Holds (5 holds) | 65,000 |
| Machinery Space (Engine Room) | 4,200 |
| Accommodation Block | 1,800 |
| Bridge and Wheelhouse | 350 |
| Superstructure (Stores + Galley) | 600 |
| Funnel Casing | 120 |
| Steering Gear Compartment | 80 |

**Required:** Calculate the Gross Tonnage (GT) of the vessel.

### Step 1 — Sum all enclosed volumes to obtain V

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= V_{\text{cargo}} + V_{\text{machinery}} + V_{\text{accom}} + V_{\text{bridge}} + V_{\text{superstruc}} + V_{\text{funnel}} + V_{\text{steering}} \\
&= 65{,}000 + 4{,}200 + 1{,}800 + 350 + 600 + 120 + 80 \\
&= 72{,}150 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The IMO 1969 convention requires adding the volume of *every enclosed space*. Open deck areas (e.g., exposed forecastle deck) are not counted.

### Step 2 — Verify the applicability threshold

$$
V = 72{,}150 \text{ m}^3 \gg 10 \text{ m}^3
$$

The vessel qualifies for GT calculation under the IMO 1969 convention.

### Step 3 — Calculate the coefficient K₁

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{1} &= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(72{,}150)
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $\log_{10}(72{,}150)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(72{,}150) &= \log_{10}(7.215 \times 10^4) \\
&= \log_{10}(7.215) + \log_{10}(10^4) \\
&= 0.8582 + 4 \\
&= 4.8582
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{1} &= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot 4.8582 \\
&= 0.2 + 0.097164 \\
&= 0.297164
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Gross Tonnage

$$
\begin{aligned}
GT &= K_{1} \cdot V \\
&= 0.297164 \cdot 72{,}150 \\
&= 21{,}442.88
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounding to the nearest integer (per IMO convention):

$$
\boxed{GT \approx 21{,}443}
$$

### Step 5 — Sanity Check

For a Panamax bulker (~65,000 DWT), GT in the range of 20,000–25,000 is **realistic** and aligns with classification society records. The calculation is dimensionally consistent and statistically credible.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — GT vs. Other Tonnages

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most-tested distinction in KTU Module 4 ESE questions. Memorise the **dimensional and regulatory differences**.

| Parameter | Gross Tonnage (GT) | Net Tonnage (NT) | Deadweight (DWT) | Displacement (Δ) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | Dimensionless | Dimensionless | Tonnes (t) | Tonnes (t) |
| **Formula** | $K_1 \cdot V$ | $K_2 \cdot V_c + \text{Pax Terms}$ | $\Delta_{\text{loaded}} - \text{LSW}$ | $\rho \cdot \nabla$ |
| **Measures** | Total enclosed volume | Cargo + passenger earning capacity | Cargo + fuel + crew + stores | Weight of water displaced |
| **Regulated by** | IMO 1969 Convention | IMO 1969 Convention | No (commercial term) | Naval Architecture |
| **Used for** | SOLAS, MARPOL, Manning, Fees | Port dues, Canal dues | Chartering, Cargo capacity | Hydrostatics, Stability |
| **Range (typical ship)** | 100 – 200,000+ | 50 – 150,000+ | 1,000 – 400,000+ | 500 – 600,000+ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — GT Calculation Workflow

The flowchart below shows the **decision logic** an examining surveyor follows from raw GA plan to final GT, complying with IMO 1969.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Ship General Arrangement Plan] --> nodeB[Identify all enclosed spaces]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Measure each space volume in m³]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Sum volumes to obtain V]
    nodeD --> nodeE{V greater than or equal 10 m³?}
    nodeE -- No --> nodeF[GT not applicable]
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeG[Compute log10 of V]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute K1 equals 0.2 plus 0.02 times log10 V]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Multiply K1 times V]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Round to nearest integer]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Issue International Tonnage Certificate ITC 1969]
    nodeK --> nodeL[End: Record GT on ship registry]
```

### 4.1.1 Diagram Interpretation

- **nodeA → nodeB**: Surveyor starts from the approved GA plan.
- **nodeB → nodeC**: Every enclosed space is identified (cargo, machinery, accommodation, funnels, etc.).
- **nodeC → nodeD**: Volumes are summed into a single number $V$.
- **nodeE**: Statutory threshold check (V ≥ 10 m³).
- **nodeG → nodeH**: The K₁ coefficient is computed using the logarithmic scaling.
- **nodeI**: Final multiplication yields raw GT.
- **nodeJ → nodeK → nodeL**: Rounding, certification, and registry logging.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — GT Regulatory Use-Cases

This second diagram maps **how GT propagates** through the regulatory framework after being certified.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Certified GT Value] --> regA[SOLAS Safety Rules]
    src1 --> regB[MARPOL Pollution Rules]
    src1 --> regC[STCW Manning Levels]
    src1 --> regD[Port State Control Inspections]
    src1 --> regE[Port and Canal Dues]
    src1 --> regF[Insurance Premium Calculations]
    src1 --> regG[Classification Society Surveys]

    regA --> outA[Threshold 500 GT triggers specific life-saving appliance rules]
    regB --> outB[Threshold 400 GT triggers air pollution SEEMP requirements]
    regC --> outC[Threshold 100 GT and above triggers certification of officers]
    regD --> outD[Higher GT increases PSC inspection frequency]
    regE --> outE[Port dues often scale with GT squared]
    regF --> outF[Hull insurance premia increase with GT]
    regG --> outG[Annual survey scope expands with GT]
```

### 4.2.1 Reading the Architecture

Each regulatory regime consumes the same single value of GT but applies different **threshold logic** to it. This is why GT is treated as the **canonical size metric** in international maritime law — it is a single number that simultaneously informs safety, environmental, manning, financial, and certification regimes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** *(3 marks)*

**"Define Gross Tonnage of a ship as per the IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention. State the governing formula and explain each term."**

**Model Answer:**

Gross Tonnage (GT) is a dimensionless measure of the total internal volumetric capacity of a ship, calculated under the International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969. It is the regulatory metric that replaces the legacy Gross Register Tonnage.

$$
GT = K_1 \cdot V
$$

where:
- $V$ = Total volume of all enclosed spaces of the ship, in m³
- $K_1$ = 0.2 + 0.02 · log₁₀(V), a dimensionless coefficient
- The formula applies only when $V \geq 10 \text{ m}^3$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** *(3 marks)*

**"List any three differences between Gross Tonnage (GT) and Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)."**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Gross Tonnage (GT) | Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dimensionless volumetric index | Expressed in tonnes (weight) |
| 2 | Regulated by IMO 1969 Convention | Commercial / chartering metric, unregulated |
| 3 | Used for SOLAS, MARPOL, manning, port dues | Used for cargo capacity and freight contracts |
| 4 | Includes all enclosed spaces (machinery, accommodation, etc.) | Includes only cargo + fuel + crew + stores + ballast |
| 5 | Calculated using logarithmic K₁ formula | Calculated as displacement loaded minus lightweight |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternatives are provided for internal choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**Q.A(a)** A chemical tanker has the following enclosed-space volumes: cargo tanks 38,000 m³, pump room 600 m³, engine room 3,500 m³, accommodation 1,200 m³, bridge 280 m³, funnel casing 90 m³, and steering compartment 50 m³. Calculate the Gross Tonnage of the vessel under the IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute total volume V:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 38{,}000 + 600 + 3{,}500 + 1{,}200 + 280 + 90 + 50 \\
&= 43{,}720 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Check applicability:**

$$
V = 43{,}720 \text{ m}^3 \gg 10 \text{ m}^3
$$

The vessel qualifies for GT.

**Step 3 — Compute K₁:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(43{,}720) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot [\log_{10}(4.372 \times 10^4)] \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot [0.6407 + 4] \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot 4.6407 \\
&= 0.2 + 0.092814 \\
&= 0.292814
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute GT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GT &= K_1 \cdot V = 0.292814 \times 43{,}720 \\
&= 12{,}801.4
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounded to nearest integer:

$$
\boxed{GT \approx 12{,}801}
$$

---

**Q.A(b)** With the aid of a comparison table, explain the differences between Gross Tonnage, Net Tonnage, Deadweight Tonnage, and Displacement. State one regulatory application of each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The four tonnages measure fundamentally different physical quantities:

| Tonnage | Symbol | Formula Basis | Unit | Key Regulatory / Commercial Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Tonnage | GT | $K_1 \cdot V$ | Dimensionless | SOLAS applicability threshold (500 GT) |
| Net Tonnage | NT | $K_2 \cdot V_c + \text{Pax Terms}$ | Dimensionless | Suez and Panama Canal transit dues |
| Deadweight Tonnage | DWT | $\Delta_{\text{loaded}} - LSW$ | Tonnes | Charter party freight contracts |
| Displacement | Δ | $\rho \cdot \nabla$ | Tonnes | Naval architecture stability analysis |

**Explanatory notes:**

- **GT** measures the total enclosed volume of the vessel and is dimensionless. It serves as the trigger for IMO safety regulations (SOLAS, MARPOL).
- **NT** measures only the *earning-capacity volume* — cargo spaces plus passenger accommodation. It governs harbour and canal dues.
- **DWT** measures the *total variable weight* the ship can carry — cargo, fuel, freshwater, crew, stores, and ballast. It determines the commercial earning potential of the vessel.
- **Displacement** is the *weight of water pushed aside by the hull*, computed from the underwater volume $\nabla$ and water density $\rho$. It is the foundation of naval architectural calculations such as resistance, stability, and trim.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**Q.B(a)** Explain the historical evolution of ship tonnage measurement from the Moorsom System to the IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention. Why was the logarithmic coefficient introduced in the GT formula? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Historical evolution:**

1. **1854 – Moorsom System (UK):** Introduced by George Moorsom. The fundamental unit was 100 ft³ (≈ 2.83 m³) = 1 register ton. This became the basis of **Gross Register Tonnage (GRT)** and **Net Register Tonnage (NRT)**. It was widely adopted internationally in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

2. **1948 & 1969 – IMCO/IMO Efforts:** As ship sizes grew exponentially (especially supertankers in the 1960s), GRT values became unmanageably large. The Inter-Governmental Maritime Consultative Organization (IMCO, later IMO) developed a new convention in 1969 to standardise measurement and simplify regulatory thresholds.

3. **1982 – Convention Entry into Force:** The IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention entered force on **18 July 1982** with a 12-year transition window.

4. **1994 – Full Implementation:** From **18 July 1994** onward, only GT is legally recognised. GRT and NRT were abolished for new vessels.

**Why the logarithmic coefficient?**

The IMO 1969 convention needed a formula that:

- **Matched legacy GRT values** in the small-to-medium ship range (ensuring continuity).
- **Did not grow linearly** with ship size, so that fixed regulatory thresholds (e.g., 500 GT for SOLAS) remained meaningful as ships grew larger.
- The logarithmic term $0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)$ achieves this: it increases K₁ only slowly as V grows, bounding GT growth.

Mathematically, K₁ grows from 0.20 (at V = 1 m³) to about 0.40 (at V = 1,000,000 m³), a 2× range, while V itself grows 1,000,000×. This *logarithmic damping* is the key engineering insight of the IMO 1969 convention.

---

**Q.B(b)** A new-build container vessel has enclosed-space volumes totalling 285,000 m³. Compute the GT and state which IMO regulations would apply to it based on its GT value. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply GT formula:**

$$
V = 285{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute K₁:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(285{,}000) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot [\log_{10}(2.85 \times 10^5)] \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot [0.4548 + 5] \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot 5.4548 \\
&= 0.2 + 0.109097 \\
&= 0.309097
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute GT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GT &= K_1 \cdot V = 0.309097 \times 285{,}000 \\
&= 88{,}092.6
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounded to the nearest integer:

$$
\boxed{GT \approx 88{,}093}
$$

**Step 4 — Applicable regulations:**

| GT Range | Regulation Triggered |
|---|---|
| GT ≥ 400 | MARPOL Annex VI (Air Pollution, SEEMP, EEDI) |
| GT ≥ 500 | SOLAS Chapter II-1, II-2 (Subdivision, Fire Safety) |
| GT ≥ 100 | STCW (manning certifications required) |
| Any GT ≥ 24 m | International Tonnage Certificate mandatory |

Since GT ≈ 88,093, the vessel triggers **all** major SOLAS, MARPOL, and STCW provisions, and must hold a valid International Tonnage Certificate (ITC 1969).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in GT questions — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion** (–1 to –2 marks): Writing GT in tonnes, or DWT as dimensionless. *Always* state GT is **dimensionless**.
> 2. **Forgetting the logarithmic term** (–3 marks): Computing $GT = 0.2 \cdot V$ only, which is *not* the IMO formula.
> 3. **Wrong V threshold** (–1 mark): Applying GT to a vessel with V < 10 m³. State that GT does not apply below this threshold.
> 4. **Mixing K₁ and K₂** (–3 marks): K₁ is for GT; K₂ is for NT. They are *not interchangeable*.
> 5. **Skipping the "enclosed" qualifier** (–2 marks): Open deck areas are *excluded*. Always write "enclosed spaces."
> 6. **Rounding errors in log calculation** (–1 mark): Show log₁₀(V) calculation explicitly — KTU expects the intermediate step on the answer sheet.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

- ✅ **GT is dimensionless** — never in tonnes.
- ✅ **Governing formula:** $GT = K_1 \cdot V$ where $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)$.
- ✅ **V = total enclosed volume in m³**, threshold **V ≥ 10 m³**.
- ✅ GT applies to **all ships built after 18 July 1994** (post-IMO 1969 enforcement).
- ✅ GT replaced legacy **GRT** and **NRT** (Moorsom system).
- ✅ GT governs **SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW, port dues, and classification**.
- ✅ **K₁** is for GT only; **K₂** is for NT only — never mix them.
- ✅ Logarithmic damping keeps GT growth **sub-linear** with vessel size.
- ✅ Memorise the comparison table: **GT vs NT vs DWT vs Displacement**.
- ✅ Common GT thresholds: **400 (MARPOL), 500 (SOLAS), 100 (STCW)**.
- ✅ Always write the **full formula with units in answer sheets** — KTU examiners award partial marks for explicit definitions.
- ✅ Show $\log_{10}(V)$ calculation **step by step** — don't skip the intermediate value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Net Tonnage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBNST504 - SHIP DESIGN I
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Net Tonnage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Net Tonnage (NT)** is a dimensionless index, prescribed under the **International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships (1969)**, which represents the *usable earning volume* of a vessel — essentially the aggregate volume of all cargo, passenger, and store-carrying spaces below the **weathertight tonnage deck** and above the inner plating, expressed in units of **100 cubic feet (or 1 cubic metre in metric)** multiplied by a non-linear usage factor.

Mathematically, it is the **chargeable earning capacity** of the ship, derived from the volume of its cargo/passenger spaces, modulated by a depth-based utilisation factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> *"Net Tonnage (NT) is a measure of the useful volume of a ship's cargo and passenger spaces, calculated under the IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention. It is the basis for computing port dues, canal tolls (Suez/Panama) and regulatory manning requirements."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Profitable Office Building" Model

Imagine a **30-storey office building** (the ship).
- The *Gross Area* of the building = **Gross Tonnage (GT)** → every enclosed room, corridor, staircase, restroom, even the boiler room.
- The *Rentable Area* (only the offices and conference rooms that a tenant actually pays for) = **Net Tonnage (NT)** → the revenue-generating spaces.

For a ship:
- **GT** counts everything enclosed by the hull and superstructure (engine rooms, tanks, accommodations — included).
- **NT** counts only the **cargo holds, passenger cabins, and accessible store rooms** that earn revenue when the ship sails.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:**
> GT = Total enclosed volume of the building.
> NT = Lettable (profitable) floor area.
> Just as building owners are taxed on *rentable* area, shipowners pay port fees on **NT**, not GT.

---

### 1.3 Why Two Tonnages? — The Engineering Logic

Before **1969**, ships were measured by the **Moorsom System** (1854) which was *static* — it measured actual carrying capacity in tons. Modern ships have large machinery volumes and complex hulls, making a *static* measurement misleading. Hence the **IMO 1969 Convention** introduced a *volumetric index*:
- **GT** → for safety regulation (life-saving equipment, stability booklets, MARPOL application).
- **NT** → for commercial regulation (port dues, canal transit charges, manning scale).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Memory Hook:** *GT → "How big is the ship?" (regulatory)* | *NT → "How much can the ship earn?" (commercial)*

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit (Metric) | Unit (Imperial) |
|------------|----------------------------------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| $V$ | Total volume of all enclosed spaces | $\text{m}^3$ | $\text{ft}^3$ |
| $V_c$ | Volume of cargo + passenger + store spaces | $\text{m}^3$ | $\text{ft}^3$ |
| $d$ | **Molded draft** at the Summer Load Line | m | ft |
| $D$ | **Molded depth** at amidships (keel to tonnage deck) | m | ft |
| $K_1$ | Gross tonnage multiplier (function of $V$) | dimensionless | dimensionless |
| $K_2$ | Net tonnage multiplier (function of $V_c$) | dimensionless | dimensionless |
| $N_1$ | Number of cabin passengers | persons | persons |
| $N_2$ | Number of deck passengers | persons | persons |

> [!WARNING]
> Under the 1969 Convention, **one ton = 100 cubic feet (≈ 2.8317 m³)** for measurement purposes. KTU examiners expect students to remember this conversion factor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The IMO 1969 Convention treats tonnage as a **dimensionless index**, not an actual mass or volume. The reasoning proceeds in five logical stages:

1. **Stage 1 — Enclosure Rule:**
   Measure the *total volume* $V$ of all permanently enclosed spaces within the hull and superstructure. This yields the **raw structural volume** of the ship.

2. **Stage 2 — Non-Linear Compression via $K_1$:**
   Apply the multiplier
   $$K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V)$$
   to account for the *diminishing economic significance* of volume in larger ships. A bulk carrier of 100,000 m³ is *not* 1000 times more "useful" than a coaster of 100 m³.

3. **Stage 3 — Definition of GT:**
   $$GT = K_1 \cdot V$$
   This is the **Gross Tonnage**.

4. **Stage 4 — Identification of Cargo Spaces ($V_c$):**
   Subtract from $V$ all *non-earning* spaces (machinery, navigation spaces, chain lockers, water ballast tanks, certain crew spaces). The remainder is $V_c$.

5. **Stage 5 — Depth-Based Loading Penalty:**
   Apply the **ratio factor** $\left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2$ to penalise ships whose draft is shallow relative to their depth (i.e., ships that *appear* to have large cargo volume but cannot fully load it).

### 2.2 The Final Net Tonnage Formula

$$\boxed{NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2}$$

where
$$K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V_c)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Regulatory Safeguards (often asked in KTU 3-mark questions):**
> 1. $NT \geq 0.25 \cdot GT$ (Net Tonnage shall not be less than 25 % of Gross Tonnage).
> 2. If $\dfrac{4d}{3D} > 1$, the depth factor is **taken as unity** (clipped to 1.0).
> 3. If $N_1 + \dfrac{N_2}{10} > 0$ (passenger ship), $V_c$ is recalculated using the passenger-space correction.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|-----------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $GT = K_1 \cdot V$ | Gross Tonnage | dimensionless |
| 2 | $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V$ | GT multiplier | dimensionless |
| 3 | $NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2$ | Net Tonnage (main) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V_c$ | NT multiplier | dimensionless |
| 5 | $NT_{\min} = 0.25 \cdot GT$ | Minimum allowable NT | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{4d}{3D} \leq 1 \Rightarrow \text{clipped}$ | Depth cap | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_c = V - V_{\text{excluded}}$ | Cargo volume | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 8 | $1 \text{ ton} = 100 \text{ ft}^3$ | Imperial constant | $\text{ft}^3/\text{ton}$ |
| 9 | $1 \text{ ton} \approx 2.8317 \text{ m}^3$ | Metric conversion | $\text{m}^3/\text{ton}$ |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of NT |
|---------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| **Port State Authorities** | Levying berth dues, light dues, pilotage fees |
| **Suez Canal Authority (SCA)** | Suez Canal Net Tonnage (SCNT) calculation |
| **Panama Canal Authority (ACP)** | Panama Canal Universal Measurement System (PC/UMS) |
| **Manning Rules** | Minimum certification crew scales |
| **Classification Societies** | Assigning class notations and survey intervals |
| **Insurance (H&M, P&I)** | Risk-based premium calculation |
| **Maritime Law** | Determination of vessel liability limits |

> [!TIP]
> A 300,000 DWT VLCC may have **GT ≈ 160,000** and **NT ≈ 95,000**. The dramatic gap reveals how much volume is occupied by machinery, ballast, and crew spaces — and is a frequent KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Net Tonnage Factor $(4d/3D)^2$

#### 3.1.1 Starting Principle

The IMO 1969 Convention recognises that the **draft-to-depth ratio** is a proxy for cargo-utilisation efficiency. A ship whose molded draft $d$ approaches its molded depth $D$ is *fully utilising* its hull for cargo. Conversely, a ship with small $d$ relative to $D$ has empty void space below its cargo decks.

The IMO therefore defines the **ideal relationship** as:

$$\dfrac{d}{D} = \dfrac{3}{4} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \dfrac{4d}{3D} = 1$$

This ratio $\dfrac{3}{4}$ represents the *historically observed average* of well-designed dry-cargo ships at the Summer Load Line.

#### 3.1.2 Penalty Derivation for Sub-Ideal Draft

If a ship has $\dfrac{4d}{3D} < 1$, the draft is sub-optimal and the apparent cargo space is *overstated*. The IMO applies a **second-order (quadratic) penalty**, since the penalty grows non-linearly with the depth mismatch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depth Factor} &= \left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** If the depth factor were linear $(4d/3D)$, a ship with $d/D = 0.5$ would receive 66.7 % credit, overstating its real earning. A quadratic penalty correctly gives $(0.667)^2 = 0.444$, i.e., only 44.4 % of the geometric cargo volume is chargeable.

#### 3.1.3 The Cap Condition

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \dfrac{4d}{3D} &> 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Depth Factor} = 1 \\
\text{If } \dfrac{4d}{3D} &\leq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Depth Factor} = \left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cap prevents penalising deep-draught tankers whose draft may exceed $0.75 D$ due to heavy machinery spaces below the tonnage deck.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- Total volume of all enclosed spaces: $V = 80{,}000 \text{ m}^3$
- Volume of excluded spaces (machinery, ballast, crew): $V_{\text{ex}} = 30{,}000 \text{ m}^3$
- Molded depth at amidships: $D = 18 \text{ m}$
- Summer load line draft: $d = 12 \text{ m}$

Compute the **Net Tonnage (NT)**.

---

#### Solution — Step 1: Calculate Cargo Volume $V_c$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= V - V_{\text{ex}} \\
&= 80{,}000 - 30{,}000 \\
&= 50{,}000 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Solution — Step 2: Calculate $K_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_2 &= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V_c) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(50{,}000) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot (4.69897) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.093979 \\
&= 0.293979
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Solution — Step 3: Calculate the Depth Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{4d}{3D} &= \dfrac{4 \times 12}{3 \times 18} \\
&= \dfrac{48}{54} \\
&= 0.8889
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.8889 < 1$, the cap is *not* triggered. The depth factor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 &= (0.8889)^2 \\
&= 0.7901
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Solution — Step 4: Compute NT

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2 \\
&= 0.293979 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.7901 \\
&= 14{,}698.95 \times 0.7901 \\
&\approx 11{,}614.6
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Solution — Step 5: Verify Minimum NT Rule

First, compute $GT$ to check the $NT \geq 0.25 GT$ rule.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(80{,}000) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.90309 \\
&= 0.2 + 0.098062 \\
&= 0.298062 \\
\\
GT &= K_1 \cdot V \\
&= 0.298062 \times 80{,}000 \\
&= 23{,}844.96 \\
\\
NT_{\min} &= 0.25 \times GT \\
&= 0.25 \times 23{,}844.96 \\
&= 5{,}961.24
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $11{,}614.6 > 5{,}961.24$, the minimum-NT rule is satisfied.

---

### ✅ Final Answer

$$
\boxed{NT \approx 11{,}615 \text{ (dimensionless)}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on NT

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect on NT | Magnitude Sensitivity |
|----------------|------|---------------------------|-----------------------|
| Increase $V_c$ | ↑ $V_c$ | ↑ NT, ↑ $K_2$ (log) | **Strong (linear in $V_c$)** |
| Increase $d/D$ ratio | ↑ $d/D$ | ↑ NT (via $\propto d^2$) | **Quadratic** |
| Decrease $D$ | ↑ $d/D$ | ↑ NT | **Strong inverse** |
| Increase $V_{\text{ex}}$ | ↓ $V_c$ | ↓ NT | **Linear** |
| Small ship ($V_c<10$) | ↓ $V_c$ | $K_2$ falls to ~0.20 | **Diminishing** |
| Excessive draft ($>0.75D$) | ↑ $d$ | Depth factor **clipped** to 1 | **Capped** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of NT Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Ship Hull Geometry"] --> B["Measure Total Enclosed Volume V"]
    B --> C["Identify and Subtract Excluded Spaces Vex"]
    C --> D["Compute Cargo Volume Vc = V - Vex"]
    D --> E["Compute K2 = 0.2 + 0.02 log10 Vc"]
    D --> F["Read Molded Draft d from SLL"]
    D --> G["Read Molded Depth D at Amidships"]
    F --> H{"Is 4d / 3D greater than 1?"}
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I["Set Depth Factor DF = 1"]
    H -- No --> J["Compute Depth Factor DF = (4d / 3D) squared"]
    I --> K["Calculate NT = K2 times Vc times DF"]
    J --> K
    K --> L{"Is NT less than 0.25 times GT?"}
    L -- Yes --> M["Set NT = 0.25 times GT"]
    L -- No --> N["Accept Computed NT"]
    M --> O["FINAL: Net Tonnage"]
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — GT vs NT Volume Composition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HULL["Total Enclosed Volume V"]
        direction TB
        V1["Machinery Spaces"]:::excl
        V2["Ballast Tanks"]:::excl
        V3["Crew Accommodation"]:::excl
        V4["Navigation Bridge"]:::excl
        V5["Cargo Holds"]:::incl
        V6["Passenger Cabins"]:::incl
        V7["Store Rooms"]:::incl
    end
    GT["Gross Tonnage K1 times V"]:::gt
    NT["Net Tonnage K2 times Vc times DF"]:::nt
    V1 --> GT
    V2 --> GT
    V3 --> GT
    V4 --> GT
    V5 --> NT
    V6 --> NT
    V7 --> NT
    V5 --> GT
    V6 --> GT
    V7 --> GT
    classDef excl fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef incl fill:#CCFFCC,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gt fill:#FFE599,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nt fill:#99CCFF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> *Red* boxes are *excluded* from Net Tonnage. *Green* boxes are *included*. Both contribute to GT. The KTU 2024 examiner often tests whether students can correctly classify spaces such as *navigation spaces* and *chain lockers* — these are **excluded from NT but included in GT**.

---

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — Spaces Included vs Excluded (KTU High-Yield Table)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{"Is the space enclosed within hull or superstructure?"}
    Q -- "No" --> A1["Not counted in GT or NT"]
    Q -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Is it used for cargo, passengers, or stores?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> B1["Included in Vc (counted in NT)"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> B2["Counted in V (GT only) - Excluded from NT"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Excluded Spaces for KTU Viva:**
> 1. Spaces on or above the upper deck open to the sea and weather.
> 2. Cofferdams, double bottoms used exclusively for oil/ballast.
> 3. Anchor chain lockers, steering gear compartments.
> 4. Machinery spaces of category A.
> 5. Specific propulsive and auxiliary machinery spaces.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Net Tonnage. Why is it considered an index and not an actual weight or volume measurement?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Net Tonnage (NT) is the **dimensionless index** prescribed by the **IMO Tonnage Convention (1969)** that represents the *usable earning volume* of a ship — i.e., the volume of all cargo, passenger, and store-carrying spaces, modulated by a usage factor.
It is *not* an actual weight or volume because:
1. The IMO 1969 Convention replaced the older *static* Moorsom ton (which represented actual carrying capacity in long tons) with a *volumetric index* that is computed, not measured.
2. NT depends on $K_2$ (a logarithmic function), $V_c$ (cargo volume), and a *depth factor* $(4d/3D)^2$, making it an *arithmetically derived* value.
3. Two ships with identical NT values may have very different cargo capacities due to differing hull forms and densities of cargo.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the formula for Net Tonnage under the IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention. Explain each term briefly.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \left(\dfrac{4d}{3D}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V_c)$ — dimensionless multiplier that compresses large volumes non-linearly.
- $V_c$ — total volume of cargo, passenger, and store spaces ($\text{m}^3$).
- $d$ — molded draft at the Summer Load Line (m).
- $D$ — molded depth at amidships, measured to the tonnage deck (m).

> The depth factor $(4d/3D)^2$ penalises ships with under-utilised depth; it is capped at 1.0 when $4d/3D > 1$.

---

### Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer *either* the full OR alternative question set.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper 2]**

A general cargo ship has the following particulars:
- Total volume of all enclosed spaces: $V = 65{,}000 \text{ m}^3$
- Volume of excluded spaces: $V_{\text{ex}} = 22{,}500 \text{ m}^3$
- Molded depth: $D = 16 \text{ m}$
- Summer load line draft: $d = 11 \text{ m}$

**Part (a)** Compute the **Gross Tonnage (GT)** of the ship.
**Part (b)** Compute the **Net Tonnage (NT)** and verify that the minimum NT rule is satisfied.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Gross Tonnage (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute $K_1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_1 &= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(V) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(65{,}000) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot (4.81291) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.096258 \\
&= 0.296258
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $GT$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
GT &= K_1 \cdot V \\
&= 0.296258 \times 65{,}000 \\
&= 19{,}256.77
\end{aligned}
$$
