$$\boxed{GT \approx 19{,}257}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Net Tonnage (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute $V_c$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= V - V_{\text{ex}} \\
&= 65{,}000 - 22{,}500 \\
&= 42{,}500 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $K_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_2 &= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(42{,}500) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot (4.62839) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.092568 \\
&= 0.292568
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute depth factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{4d}{3D} &= \dfrac{4 \times 11}{3 \times 16} = \dfrac{44}{48} = 0.9167
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.9167 < 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depth Factor} &= (0.9167)^2 = 0.8403
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $NT$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \text{Depth Factor} \\
&= 0.292568 \times 42{,}500 \times 0.8403 \\
&= 12{,}434.14 \times 0.8403 \\
&\approx 10{,}448.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Verify the minimum NT rule.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_{\min} &= 0.25 \times GT \\
&= 0.25 \times 19{,}256.77 \\
&= 4{,}814.19
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $10{,}448.4 > 4{,}814.19$, the minimum-NT rule is **satisfied**.

$$\boxed{NT \approx 10{,}448}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 1]**

**Part (a) (7 marks):**
Compare and contrast **Gross Tonnage (GT)** and **Net Tonnage (NT)** under the IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention. Tabulate the differences across at least **six** parameters.

**Part (b) (7 marks):**
A small chemical tanker has:
- $V = 9{,}500 \text{ m}^3$
- $V_c = 5{,}200 \text{ m}^3$
- $D = 9 \text{ m}$, $d = 6.5 \text{ m}$

Calculate the **NT** and explain why port authorities worldwide prefer NT over GT for charging berth dues.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| # | Parameter | Gross Tonnage (GT) | Net Tonnage (NT) |
|---|-----------------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1 | Formula | $GT = K_1 \cdot V$ | $NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot (4d/3D)^2$ |
| 2 | Volume basis | *Total enclosed* volume $V$ | *Cargo/passenger* volume $V_c$ only |
| 3 | Spaces counted | All enclosed spaces (machinery, ballast, accommodation) | Excludes machinery, navigation, ballast, chain lockers |
| 4 | Multiplier | $K_1 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V$ | $K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V_c$ |
| 5 | Depth factor | None | $(4d/3D)^2$, clipped at 1.0 |
| 6 | Primary use | Safety regulation (life-saving, MARPOL, class) | Commercial (port dues, canal tolls, manning) |
| 7 | Minimum bound | None (but $GT \geq 0$) | $NT \geq 0.25 \cdot GT$ |

---

#### Part (b) — Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute $K_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_2 &= 0.2 + 0.02 \cdot \log_{10}(5{,}200) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.02 \times (3.71600) \\
&= 0.2 + 0.07432 \\
&= 0.27432
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute depth factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{4d}{3D} &= \dfrac{4 \times 6.5}{3 \times 9} = \dfrac{26}{27} = 0.9630
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depth Factor} &= (0.9630)^2 = 0.9273
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $NT$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot \text{Depth Factor} \\
&= 0.27432 \times 5{,}200 \times 0.9273 \\
&= 1{,}426.46 \times 0.9273 \\
&\approx 1{,}322.74
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{NT \approx 1{,}323}$$

**Why Port Authorities Prefer NT for Berth Dues:**

1. NT represents the **cargo-carrying earning capacity** — the metric most aligned with the *commercial throughput* of the port.
2. It **excludes machinery volume**, ensuring a tanker (heavy machinery) is not unfairly over-charged versus a bulk carrier of identical GT.
3. The **depth factor** $(4d/3D)^2$ rewards well-utilised designs and discourages under-loaded vessels.
4. The IMO Convention mandates a **uniform formula worldwide**, ensuring legal certainty for shipping lines.
5. NT correlates better with the **demand on port resources** (cranes, labour, storage) than GT.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.25 GT minimum rule** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you do not verify $NT \geq 0.25 \cdot GT$.
> 2. **Mixing up $K_1$ and $K_2$** — $K_1$ uses $V$ (total volume); $K_2$ uses $V_c$ (cargo volume). A common slip is using $V$ for both.
> 3. **Not clipping the depth factor** — when $4d/3D > 1$, students often leave it uncapped and compute $(1.05)^2 = 1.1025$, which is **wrong** (should be 1.0).
> 4. **Wrong units in log** — $\log_{10}$ argument must be in the same volume units as $V$ and $V_c$. Do not mix m³ and ft³.
> 5. **Counting excluded spaces in $V_c$** — anchor chain lockers, machinery spaces, and cofferdams are NOT part of $V_c$.
> 6. **Wrong number of decimal places in $K_1$, $K_2$** — typically 4–5 decimal places are expected in KTU answer scripts.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NT** is a **dimensionless index**, not an actual weight or volume.
- The governing regulation is the **IMO International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969**.
- **Master formula:** $NT = K_2 \cdot V_c \cdot (4d/3D)^2$, with $K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V_c$.
- **Two key safeguards:** $NT \geq 0.25 GT$ **and** depth factor $\leq 1$.
- $V_c$ = volume of **cargo + passenger + store rooms** only (machinery excluded).
- $d$ = molded draft at **Summer Load Line**; $D$ = molded depth at amidships to **tonnage deck**.
- NT is used for **commercial purposes** — port dues, Suez/Panama tolls, manning scales.
- GT is used for **safety/regulatory purposes** — life-saving equipment, MARPOL, classification.
- 1 ton (1969 Convention) = **100 ft³ ≈ 2.8317 m³**.
- Always **clip** the depth factor at 1.0 — never let $NT$ exceed $K_2 \cdot V_c$.
- For **passenger ships** ($N_1 + N_2/10 > 0$), $V_c$ requires the passenger-space correction — consult IMO Reg. 4.
- KTU frequently tests the **comparison table** between GT and NT (3–7 marks).
- Numerical problems expect answers in **whole numbers**, with $K_1$, $K_2$ quoted to **4 decimals**.
- The depth factor is **quadratic**, so a small error in $d/D$ compounds significantly.
